ALOT.name: AstroNova Inc.
ALOT.content:
# 25. ALOT — AstroNova Inc.
## TIER 5 — SPECIAL SITUATION | Score: 67/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

AstroNova is a $60 million market cap company that manufactures specialty printers for aerospace (cockpit printers for flight data recording) and industrial applications (product labeling, packaging, and test-and-measurement printing). The company has formally announced that it is exploring strategic alternatives, which in small-cap corporate finance is the polished euphemism for "we are looking for a buyer." At $60 million, this is so far below the radar of institutional investors, sell-side analysts, and even most small-cap funds that the announcement has generated virtually no market attention — which is precisely why the opportunity exists.

The strategic alternatives process is the entire thesis. When a $60 million company with real revenue, real products, and a defensible niche in aerospace printing announces it is for sale, the probability of an acquisition at a premium to the current market price is significant. Private equity firms, strategic acquirers in the aerospace electronics space, and industrial conglomerates that specialize in roll-up strategies all represent potential buyers. The aerospace cockpit printer business is particularly attractive because it is embedded in aircraft platforms through FAA certification — once AstroNova's printer is designed into a cockpit, it remains there for the life of the aircraft, creating an aftermarket parts and service stream that a buyer would value highly.

The math on a micro-cap acquisition is straightforward: any reasonable acquisition premium (20-40%) applied to a $60 million base generates outsized percentage returns on a small absolute investment. A 30% premium would mean $78 million enterprise value — a rounding error for most aerospace acquirers but a meaningful return for investors who established positions before the announcement gained attention. The risk is that the strategic alternatives process yields no acceptable offer and the company remains independent at the current valuation — but even in that scenario, you own a profitable niche manufacturer with aerospace certification revenue at a micro-cap valuation. The downside case is "business as usual at $60M," while the upside case is an acquisition premium that could be 20-50%+ above current levels.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The FAA certification embedded in AstroNova's aerospace products creates asset value that does not appear on the balance sheet. Cockpit printers installed in commercial and military aircraft are certified as part of the overall avionics suite — replacing them requires recertification of the entire system, which costs millions and takes years. This means AstroNova's installed base generates a locked-in aftermarket revenue stream (replacement parts, ink, maintenance) that a potential acquirer would value using a recurring revenue multiple, not a cyclical manufacturer multiple. The formal exploration of strategic alternatives at this market cap suggests the board believes the public market is significantly undervaluing these assets — and at $60 million, they are almost certainly correct.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN

|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Specialty printers for aerospace and industrial. Simple products, niche markets. |
| Moat | 6 | FAA-certified aerospace products. Switching costs from certification. Narrow but real. |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | Small company. Adequate for its size. No fortress but no existential risk. |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | $60M for a company with aerospace certifications and real revenue. Likely undervalued. |
| Catalyst | 9 | Formally exploring strategic alternatives. Board has initiated the sale process. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Downside: remains independent at $60M (flat). Upside: acquisition premium 20-50%+. |
| Business Quality | 5 | Niche manufacturer. Adequate but not exceptional margins. Small scale. |
| Executability | 4 | $60M micro-cap. Very illiquid. Spread is wide. Position building is slow. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | No acceptable offer emerges. Aerospace cycle turns down. Manageable. |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Aerospace aftermarket is steady. Industrial segment is more cyclical. Blended. |
| **TOTAL** | **67** | |

# Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Volume 2

## Extended Watchlist — Researched Candidates

This volume contains the 23 additional candidates that surfaced from the stage-1 quantitative screen and stage-2 EDGAR review but did not receive a full Munger-checklist write-up in Volume 1. Each page below presents the 5-year price chart, current key financials, and a short prompt of what still needs to be answered before sizing a position.

ALOT.analystSummary:
AstroNova is a $60 million market cap company that manufactures specialty printers for aerospace (cockpit printers for flight data recording) and industrial applications (product labeling, packaging, and test-and-measurement printing). The company has formally announced that it is exploring strategic alternatives, which in small-cap corporate finance is the polished euphemism for "we are looking for a buyer." At $60 million, this is so far below the radar of institutional investors, sell-side analysts, and even most small-cap funds that the announcement has generated virtually no market attention — which is precisely why the opportunity exists.

The strategic alternatives process is the entire thesis. When a $60 million company with real revenue, real products, and a defensible niche in aerospace printing announces it is for sale, the probability of an acquisition at a premium to the current market price is significant. Private equity firms, strategic acquirers in the aerospace electronics space, and industrial conglomerates that specialize in roll-up strategies all represent potential buyers. The aerospace cockpit printer business is particularly attractive because it is embedded in aircraft platforms through FAA certification — once AstroNova's printer is designed into a cockpit, it remains there for the life of the aircraft, creating an aftermarket parts and service stream that a buyer would value highly.

The math on a micro-cap acquisition is straightforward: any reasonable acquisition premium (20-40%) applied to a $60 million base generates outsized percentage returns on a small absolute investment. A 30% premium would mean $78 million enterprise value — a rounding error for most aerospace acquirers but a meaningful return for investors who established positions before the announcement gained attention. The risk is that the strategic alternatives process yields no acceptable offer and the company remains independent at the current valuation — but even in that scenario, you own a profitable niche manufacturer with aerospace certification revenue at a micro-cap valuation. The downside case is "business as usual at $60M," while the upside case is an acquisition premium that could be 20-50%+ above current levels.
ALOT.whatScreenCantSee: The FAA certification embedded in AstroNova's aerospace products creates asset value that does not appear on the balance sheet. Cockpit printers installed in commercial and military aircraft are certified as part of the overall avionics suite — replacing them requires recertification of the entire system, which costs millions and takes years. This means AstroNova's installed base generates a locked-in aftermarket revenue stream (replacement parts, ink, maintenance) that a potential acquirer would value using a recurring revenue multiple, not a cyclical manufacturer multiple. The formal exploration of strategic alternatives at this market cap suggests the board believes the public market is significantly undervaluing these assets — and at $60 million, they are almost certainly correct.

## Extended Watchlist — Researched Candidates

This volume contains the 23 additional candidates that surfaced from the stage-1 quantitative screen and stage-2 EDGAR review but did not receive a full Munger-checklist write-up in Volume 1. SCHL.name: Scholastic Corporation
SCHL.content:
# 272. SCHL — Scholastic Corporation
## SELF-TENDER | $36-$40 Modified Dutch Auction expires 4/20/26 | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
On March 23, 2026, Scholastic launched a Modified Dutch Auction issuer tender offer to repurchase up to $200M of common stock at a price between $36.00 and $40.00 per share. The offer expires April 20, 2026 at 5:00 PM New York time. At the $40 ceiling the buyback retires roughly 5.0M shares, or approximately 25% of public float (insiders, including the Robinson family trusts and the Robinson Foundation, are not tendering). With the stock pinned at ~$39.77, the spread to the $40 cap is essentially zero — the market is telling you the auction will clear at the top of the range.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Scholastic is the children's-publishing legacy franchise: the Book Fairs and Book Clubs distribution into US schools, the Education Solutions textbook business, and a trade-publishing list anchored on Harry Potter, Captain Underpants, Hunger Games, and Dog Man. Free cash flow has been compressed by post-COVID school spending normalization, classroom-time disruptions, and a soft trade-publishing cycle, but the core Book Fairs business — which is structurally a school-fundraising franchise with no real competitor — remains intact. The strategic move into Wizarding World content via the 9 Story Media acquisition added IP optionality on the cost of cyclically depressed earnings.

The mechanics of the trade are straightforward. A Modified Dutch is the cleanest form of issuer return: shareholders elect to tender at any price within the band, and the company pays the single lowest price needed to clear $200M, paying that price to all accepted shares. The current setup has three implications. First, with the stock at $39.77 versus a $40 ceiling, the implied probability of clearing at $40 is overwhelming — proration risk is real but not terminal. Second, insiders explicitly stated they are not tendering, which signals confidence in fundamental value above $40 and shrinks the available supply. Third, even if you do not tender, the float reduction of ~25% post-deal is a material EPS lever (low-20s percent boost on share count alone, before any margin recovery).

The honest risks: oversubscription means proration. If tenders exceed $200M / $40 = 5.0M shares (likely), each shareholder who tenders gets only their pro-rata fill at $40 and the rest stays at market — which on the day after expiration could trade lower as the technical bid disappears. The "buyback floor at $36" thesis only holds if you actually tender at $36-$37 (low end), which is unattractive given the current quote. And Scholastic's underlying earnings power remains the open question — Book Fairs is a fine business but trade publishing has been mediocre and Education Solutions has not turned the corner.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A spread screen will flag this as 0.6% gross / not annualized. It misses two things. First, the real trade is post-tender: the float reduction creates a smaller, illiquid stock with the controlling family owning a higher share — possibly a setup for a future take-private at a better premium. Second, insider non-tendering is a tell. The Robinson family trusts have been net buyers of family agency, not sellers, since John Robinson's death in 2021 — they are accumulating influence, not exiting.

### CATALYSTS
- **Tender results announced April 21, 2026:** clearing price and proration factor.
- **Q4 FY2026 earnings (mid-July 2026):** first post-tender print with reduced share count and updated guidance on Book Fairs and trade publishing.
- **Possible follow-on capital return or family-led take-private (12-24 month tail):** post-tender float reduction lowers the cost of any subsequent buyout.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Business quality (durable moat) | 7/10 |
| 2 | Management & alignment | 7/10 |
| 3 | Balance sheet strength | 6/10 |
| 4 | Reinvestment runway | 5/10 |
| 5 | Margin of safety to base case | 6/10 |
| 6 | Catalyst clarity / timing | 8/10 |
| 7 | Downside scenario quantified | 7/10 |
| 8 | Insider / sponsor skin in game | 8/10 |
| 9 | Simplicity of the thesis | 7/10 |
| 10 | Asymmetry (heads-tails payoff) | 5/10 |
| **Total** | | **66/100** |

---

CVLT.name: Commvault Systems, Inc.
CVLT.content:
# 151. CVLT — Commvault Systems, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE | $94.23 / $4.9B speculative deal | Score: 58/100

### THE SETUP
Commvault is reportedly running a confirmed sale process with multiple PE bidders per InsideArbitrage as of April 11, 2026, with chatter pricing in the $110 range against a $94.23 spot — a 16.7% gross spread that annualizes to roughly 25% if a deal closes by Q4 2026. No definitive agreement yet, so the spread reflects real deal-failure risk.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Commvault is a mature data-protection and backup software vendor whose business has pivoted from on-prem perpetual licenses to a recurring subscription and SaaS (Metallic) model. Customers use it to back up enterprise workloads, manage compliance retention, and increasingly to recover from ransomware. The screen catches it because of the active confirmed sale process — InsideArbitrage flagged multiple PE bidders circling at speculative pricing around $110 per share or $4.9B equity. Spot at $94.23 implies the market is discounting deal-failure risk meaningfully — at full $110, gross spread is 16.7%; if no deal materializes, the unaffected price is likely lower.

Bear case: there is no definitive agreement, only press reports and banker chatter. Sale processes get pulled all the time when bidders fail to meet the board's price expectations or financing markets tighten. PE LBO math on enterprise software requires high recurring revenue mix and predictable retention — Commvault's transition to subscription is real but gross retention has historically lagged best-in-class peers like Veeam (private) and Rubrik (public). A failed process leaves the stock exposed to the post-pop reversion plus a lost competitive cycle. Cyber-resilience competition from Rubrik, Cohesity, Veeam, and the hyperscalers' native tools has intensified.

Bull case: the data-protection category is a genuine PE darling — recurring revenue, mission-critical workloads, ransomware tailwind, and a clear consolidation thesis. Commvault has $1B+ ARR and is FCF-positive, exactly the profile sponsors want for a leveraged buyout. With multiple bidders in process, competitive tension supports a clearing price at or above $110. If the deal lands at $110, the 16.7% gross spread is one of the better risk-adjusted returns in the current arb book, and even a $105 print delivers double-digit IRR. If the deal fails, the strategic review itself often surfaces operational changes that re-rate the stock standalone.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The reported sale process is mid-flight without a definitive 8-K, so the entire spread is qualitative — investors are pricing the joint probability of deal close, bidder identity (sponsor vs strategic), and final clearing price. The 10-K reveals Commvault's subscription mix has crossed 50% of total revenue, which is the LBO underwriting threshold most sponsors require. The Metallic SaaS line is the hidden lever — its growth trajectory is what allows a sponsor to underwrite a 6-7x ARR multiple. Any leak from the Wall Street Journal or Reuters naming a specific bidder would compress the spread by several hundred basis points instantly.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Definitive merger agreement announcement (target Q2 2026)
- Q1 FY27 earnings showing subscription/ARR trajectory
- Reported PE bidder identification or process termination

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Backup software, well known |
| Moat | 6 | Switching costs in backup, contested |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Net cash, FCF positive |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | Speculative deal price |
| Catalyst | 7 | Live process |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | 16.7% up, 15-20% down on fail |
| Business Quality | 6 | Mid-quality, transitioning model |
| Executability | 8 | $4.9B mid-cap |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | No definitive deal, real fail risk |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Enterprise software, recurring |
| **TOTAL** | **65** | |

---

ALCO.name: Alico, Inc.
ALCO.content:
# 3. ALCO — Alico, Inc.
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | Florida land-bank | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
Alico is exiting 100+ years of citrus and pivoting to a pure Florida land-development play: ~53,371 acres owned plus ~48,700 acres of oil/gas/mineral rights, with the Corkscrew Grove Villages entitlement decision pending in 2026. Stock prices the operating losses; the land-bank optionality is the asymmetry.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Alico (NASDAQ: ALCO) announced its strategic transformation in January 2025: wind down Alico Citrus after the FY2025 harvest and reposition as a diversified Florida land company. As of Sep 30, 2025 the company controlled 49,537 acres in Florida (39,297 in citrus groves, 10,240 in land management), with 75% targeted to remain agricultural and 25% identified for commercial/residential development. FY2025 (ended Sep 30, 2025) net loss was $147.3M (driven by impairments and wind-down charges), but adjusted EBITDA was $22.5M (above $20M guidance) and land sales hit $23.8M generating a $20.3M gain. Year-end balance sheet: $38.1M cash, net debt $47.4M. The flagship project — Corkscrew Grove Villages — contemplates two 1,500-acre villages with ~9,000 homes and 560,000 sq ft commercial; final Collier County approval expected 2026.

Bear case: citrus greening disease and hurricane damage drove a 73% decade decline in production, validating the wind-down but also signalling secularly impaired core operations and ongoing decommissioning costs. Florida entitlement risk is real — Collier County approval is not pro forma; environmental, water-management, and growth-cap politics can delay or strip density. Debt-to-assets rose to 0.43 (from 0.23) as land-bank value backs new borrowing rather than recurring cash flow. Until entitlements clear, Alico is an illiquid, lumpy, low-multiple holding-company that consumes cash. Float and trading liquidity are thin; small-cap discount may persist.

Bull case: Florida raw land in a path-of-growth corridor (Collier, Hendry, Glades counties) is structurally scarce; reported land sales already exceed guidance and provide independent third-party value marks (~$8.5K/acre on FY25 sales). With ~50K acres and a fortress entitlement project, NAV materially exceeds the ~$300M-ish equity market value implied. Cash + planned land sales fund operations through FY2027, giving runway to monetize entitlements without distress. Insider/founder family ownership aligns long-term decisions.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 48,700 acres of oil, gas and mineral rights are essentially zero-carried optionality — never picked up by a screener that focuses on operating segments. Beyond Corkscrew, the company has agreed to lease another 5,250 acres to third-party citrus growers, generating cash without owning the operating risk — a hidden landlord business inside what looks like a wind-down. The fact that adjusted EBITDA exceeded guidance during a transformation year suggests management can sequence land sales to defend covenants while unlocking entitlements. Watch the 10-K for any per-acre carrying-value disclosure — book value is depreciated agricultural cost and dramatically understates SW Florida residential land marks.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Collier County final entitlement decision on Corkscrew Grove Villages in 2026
- FY2026 land-sales pace (run-rate trending ~$30-50M annually)
- Updated NAV/value framework at next investor presentation (post wind-down)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Land + entitlement is simple |
| Moat | 8 | Irreplaceable Florida acreage |

ALCO.analystSummary:
Alico (NASDAQ: ALCO) announced its strategic transformation in January 2025: wind down Alico Citrus after the FY2025 harvest and reposition as a diversified Florida land company. As of Sep 30, 2025 the company controlled 49,537 acres in Florida (39,297 in citrus groves, 10,240 in land management), with 75% targeted to remain agricultural and 25% identified for commercial/residential development. FY2025 (ended Sep 30, 2025) net loss was $147.3M (driven by impairments and wind-down charges), but adjusted EBITDA was $22.5M (above $20M guidance) and land sales hit $23.8M generating a $20.3M gain. Year-end balance sheet: $38.1M cash, net debt $47.4M. The flagship project — Corkscrew Grove Villages — contemplates two 1,500-acre villages with ~9,000 homes and 560,000 sq ft commercial; final Collier County approval expected 2026.

Bear case: citrus greening disease and hurricane damage drove a 73% decade decline in production, validating the wind-down but also signalling secularly impaired core operations and ongoing decommissioning costs. Florida entitlement risk is real — Collier County approval is not pro forma; environmental, water-management, and growth-cap politics can delay or strip density. Debt-to-assets rose to 0.43 (from 0.23) as land-bank value backs new borrowing rather than recurring cash flow. Until entitlements clear, Alico is an illiquid, lumpy, low-multiple holding-company that consumes cash. Float and trading liquidity are thin; small-cap discount may persist.

Bull case: Florida raw land in a path-of-growth corridor (Collier, Hendry, Glades counties) is structurally scarce; reported land sales already exceed guidance and provide independent third-party value marks (~$8.5K/acre on FY25 sales). With ~50K acres and a fortress entitlement project, NAV materially exceeds the ~$300M-ish equity market value implied. Cash + planned land sales fund operations through FY2027, giving runway to monetize entitlements without distress. Insider/founder family ownership aligns long-term decisions.
ALCO.whatScreenCantSee:
The 48,700 acres of oil, gas and mineral rights are essentially zero-carried optionality — never picked up by a screener that focuses on operating segments. Beyond Corkscrew, the company has agreed to lease another 5,250 acres to third-party citrus growers, generating cash without owning the operating risk — a hidden landlord business inside what looks like a wind-down. The fact that adjusted EBITDA exceeded guidance during a transformation year suggests management can sequence land sales to defend covenants while unlocking entitlements. Watch the 10-K for any per-acre carrying-value disclosure — book value is depreciated agricultural cost and dramatically understates SW Florida residential land marks.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Collier County final entitlement decision on Corkscrew Grove Villages in 2026
- FY2026 land-sales pace (run-rate trending ~$30-50M annually)
- Updated NAV/value framework at next investor presentation (post wind-down)

GLIBA.name: GCI Liberty, Inc. (Series A)
GLIBA.content:
# 14. GLIBA — GCI Liberty, Inc. (Series A)
## SPINOFF (from Liberty Broadband)  |  Spin Date: 2025-07-14  |  Score: 70/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $1,750

### THE SETUP
A $1.5B Liberty Malone-orphan holding GCI — Alaska's near-monopoly broadband and wireless operator (~90% market share) — spun out of Liberty Broadband immediately ahead of the Charter/Cox merger. LBRDA holders only wanted Charter exposure and dumped the GCI stub on day one; the Series A/B/C share structure suppressed price discovery; and a $4.7B 45-year network investment in 200+ Alaska communities sits at materially lower EBITDA multiples than Frontier, Windstream, or WOW.

### SPINOFF MECHANICS
- Parent: Liberty Broadband (LBRDA), whose principal asset is Charter Communications stock
- Spin date: July 14, 2025; GLIBA/GLIBK began trading July 15, 2025; GLIBB OTC quoted July 21, 2025
- Distribution ratio: completed via tax-free spin-off plus a contemporaneous rights offering / PIPE that recapitalized the standalone entity
- Initial trading ~$32-35; current ~$37.83; market cap ~$1.51B
- Forced selling: ex-LBRDA holders bought purely for Charter exposure and sold the Alaska stub; tracker-stock confusion (Series A/B/C); Alaska-only operator is too obscure for benchmarks; PIPE structure tax treatment unfamiliar to most allocators

### ANALYST SUMMARY
GCI Liberty's only material asset is GCI, LLC — Alaska's dominant facilities-based connectivity company providing data, mobile, voice and managed services to consumer, business, government and carrier customers across more than 200 communities, on the back of $4.7B of cumulative network investment over 45 years. 2025 was a record year: full-year adjusted OIBDA hit ~$403M; Q2 revenue grew 6% to $261M with OIBDA up 26% to $108M; Q3 revenue was $257M (-2%) with OIBDA $92M (-8%) reflecting rural healthcare-program timing. Year-end consolidated cash was $137M, total principal debt ~$1B, GCI credit-agreement leverage 2.3x, and post-rights-offering net leverage just 1.6x — a notably under-levered balance sheet for a Liberty entity.

Bear case: it's still Alaska. The state has structural headwinds — slow population growth, oil-price dependence, federal subsidy exposure (rural healthcare and BEAD funding). Consumer cable modem subs declined 3% YoY to 153,100 in Q3 — wireline is shrinking even in a near-monopoly. Wireless competition from AT&T and Verizon is real even if subscale. The Liberty Malone tracker-stock complexity (A/B/C series) won't fully unwind for years and limits institutional ownership. And a "Liberty spin" reputation cuts both ways: patient holders win, but the corporate structure is built to optimize taxes for the Malone family first, minority second.

Bull/catalyst: GCI is functionally a regulated utility for Alaska connectivity — geography is the moat (no rational competitor will replicate $4.7B of remote network) and the state government is a captive customer. Wireless lines grew 2% to 207,500 in Q3, offsetting cable decline, and total OIBDA hit a record $403M. At ~$1.5B equity + $1B debt = ~$2.5B EV vs $403M OIBDA, GLIBA trades at ~6.2x — Frontier, Lumen, WOW trade 7-9x even after their own selloffs and have inferior moats. Liberty management has a multi-decade track record of rewarding patient holders via tax-free splits, buybacks, and opportunistic deals; a PE take-private or strategic combination at peer multiples is a realistic exit. The Charter/Cox merger close (expected 2026) removes the residual structural complexity overhang.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The S-1 / S-1/A from June and November 2025 reveal that the standalone entity was deliberately recapitalized via a PIPE/rights offering specifically to drop net leverage to 1.6x — well below Liberty's typical 4-5x. That signals the Malone team is positioning GLIBA as a clean buyout vehicle, not a long-hold telecom utility. Filings also disclose that GCI is the dominant carrier for Alaska's rural healthcare connectivity program (a federal Universal Service Fund mechanism) — quasi-guaranteed revenue not visible in any peer screen. The 200+ community footprint includes proprietary subsea fiber (TERRA) that no competitor can replicate; replacement cost easily exceeds the current EV.

### CATALYSTS
- Buyback authorization announcement (Liberty management's standard playbook post-spin)
- Charter/Cox merger close — removes complexity overhang and frees up the Liberty corporate apparatus to focus on GLIBA
- PE take-private bid at peer broadband multiples (~8x EBITDA = ~$60+ vs $38)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: 8/10 (Alaska monopoly broadband)
- Durable moat: 9/10 (geography, $4.7B sunk cost)
- Honest mgmt: 7/10 (Malone team)
- Reinvestment runway: 5/10 (saturated market)
- ROIC: 7/10
- Balance sheet: 8/10 (1.6x net lev)
- Margin of safety: 7/10 (~6.2x EV/EBITDA)
- Insider alignment: 7/10
- Free cash flow: 7/10
- Asymmetry: 7/10 (PE take-out)
Total: 72/100

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $1,750  |  Rationale: legacy Liberty spin with strong moat but limited growth and tracker-stock baggage; size below average until Charter/Cox close and capital-return policy clarifies.

---

CPAY.name: Corpay, Inc.
CPAY.content:
# 5. CPAY — Corpay, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | B2B payments rebrand from FleetCor | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
FleetCor rebranded to Corpay in 2024 to reflect its pivot away from legacy fleet card economics toward higher-growth corporate payments (cross-border B2B, AP automation, lodging). The market still values it as a card issuer, not a software-led payments platform — creating a structural multiple gap with Wex, Toast, Bill.com, and other "fintech" peers.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Corpay operates three commercial-payments segments: Vehicle Payments (legacy fuel cards under Comdata, Fleetcor brand), Corporate Payments (cross-border FX and AP automation, anchored by the Cambridge Global Payments and AFEX acquisitions), and Lodging Payments (CLC Lodging for project-based crew hotel stays). The company has long been one of the highest-margin operators in payments, with EBITDA margins consistently in the 50%+ range and ROIC well above cost of capital. CEO Ron Clarke has been a serial capital allocator, executing dozens of bolt-on M&A deals while running an aggressive buyback program. The shift toward Corporate Payments (now the fastest-growing segment) reflects a deliberate de-emphasis of fuel card economics that face long-tail EV headwinds.

The bear case centers on three risks. First, regulatory scrutiny: the FTC and state AGs have circled FleetCor for years over fee transparency in fuel card programs, with a 2024 FTC settlement ($35M+) and ongoing class actions. Second, the EV transition will eventually compress fuel card take rates as commercial fleets electrify. Third, FX volatility in cross-border payments can whipsaw quarterly revenue. The Corpay rebrand has not yet translated to a multiple re-rating because Corporate Payments is still under one-third of total revenue. There is also balance-sheet leverage from acquisition spending.

The bull case is that the market is mispricing the segment mix shift. Corporate Payments is growing organically in the high teens, and management has explicitly opened the door to spinning or selling the legacy Vehicle business. Activists Elliott and others have publicly engaged on portfolio simplification. A pure-play Corpay corporate payments business would re-rate to the Bill.com / Toast cohort, while the Vehicle business has strategic value to private equity. Even absent a separation, the buyback program continues to compound per-share earnings at a low-teens rate.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K Vehicle Payments segment buries customer concentration in its largest fleet-card programs, which are partnership/co-branded deals (e.g., with Speedway and Shell) — these have low-single-digit attrition and are far stickier than the fee-transparency lawsuits suggest. The Corporate Payments segment masks the embedded margin profile of the FX trading book; Corpay earns spread on cross-border transactions in addition to platform fees, providing optionality on currency volatility. Capital-light: working capital float ($1B+) earns interest income at higher rates, a quietly powerful tailwind not visible in headline organic growth.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 and Q2 2026 earnings (May and August 2026) — Corporate Payments organic growth disclosure
- Resolution or further development on the FTC/state AG fuel card matters
- Potential Vehicle Payments separation announcement or strategic alternatives review

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Three distinct segments require unbundling |
| Moat | 8 | Network effects in fuel network; switching costs in AP |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | ~$5B net debt, ~3x leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | High-teens P/E vs payments peers at 25-30x |
| Catalyst | 6 | No firm separation timeline yet |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Re-rating optionality if Vehicle separated |
| Business Quality | 9 | 50%+ EBITDA margins, 20%+ ROIC |
| Executability | 9 | Large-cap, liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Regulatory + EV secular drag |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Tied to commercial activity and fuel volumes |
| **TOTAL** | **70** | |

IPAR.name: Inter Parfums, Inc.
IPAR.content:
# 6. IPAR — Inter Parfums, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | RECORD $1.49B SALES + 15-YR GUESS RENEWAL | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
Inter Parfums is the founder-led ($1982 — Madar/Benacin) licensing operator that quietly compounded sales to a record $1.49B in 2025 and just locked in a 15-year GUESS extension to 2048, plus brand-new long-term worldwide licenses for Beckham and Nautica. The CEO bought stock on the open market — a signal in a name where the founders already own a controlling block. This is the rare consumer-staples business with optionality from in-house brand Solferino plus contractually durable license revenue.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Inter Parfums licenses fragrance rights from luxury and lifestyle brands (Jimmy Choo, Coach, Montblanc, Lacoste, Karl Lagerfeld, GUESS, Donna Karan, Oscar de la Renta, Roberto Cavalli, Salvatore Ferragamo and others) and runs the entire value chain — formulation, manufacturing, packaging, distribution. 2025 was a record year: sales of $1,490M with Q4 at $386M (best-ever Q4). Management 2026 guidance is approximately $1.48B in sales and $4.85 diluted EPS. In January, the company announced exclusive long-term worldwide fragrance licenses with David Beckham and Nautica plus a 15-year GUESS extension through 2048 — three contractual annuities added in a single month.

The bear case: 2026 guide of $1.48B implies flat-to-slightly-down sales versus 2025, suggesting the post-COVID fragrance boom has peaked. Inter Parfums is at the mercy of license counterparties — Burberry's 2012 in-housing remains the cautionary tale. SG&A is climbing as the company invests in proprietary brand Solferino (40 doors at end-2025, +50 in H1 2026) and Lacoste's 2024 license addition. Founder-controlled governance limits activist optionality. At a ~25x P/E on $4.85 EPS, the stock is not statistically cheap.

The bull thesis is a multi-decade compounder hiding in plain sight: founder-CEO Jean Madar bought $25k of stock recently (small but meaningful given his existing stake), the 15-year GUESS renewal extends a critical license to 2048, Beckham and Nautica add fresh growth platforms, and proprietary brand Solferino opens an ultra-luxury channel where Inter Parfums captures retail-style margins instead of license royalties. Recession-resistant category, asset-light model, family-aligned capital allocation, and steady FCF that funds dividends and small buybacks. Compounders rarely look "cheap" — they look durable.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The January 2026 announcement bundling David Beckham, Nautica, and the 15-year GUESS extension to 2048 is a contractual annuity stack that cannot be picked up by quantitative screens — it shows up only in management commentary and the eventual 10-K license schedule. The CEO's open-market purchase, while small in absolute dollars, is meaningful insider-alignment signal in a name where founders Madar and Benacin already control voting power. Solferino — the company's first proprietary ultra-luxury brand — is the optionality nobody is paying for: at 40 doors today and adding 50 more in H1 2026, even a modest hit-rate adds margin layers far above license-royalty economics.

### CATALYSTS
- Q1 2026 earnings early May 2026 — Solferino door count + Beckham/Nautica timing
- Solferino +50 doors H1 2026 — proprietary-brand inflection

IPAR.analystSummary:
Inter Parfums licenses fragrance rights from luxury and lifestyle brands (Jimmy Choo, Coach, Montblanc, Lacoste, Karl Lagerfeld, GUESS, Donna Karan, Oscar de la Renta, Roberto Cavalli, Salvatore Ferragamo and others) and runs the entire value chain — formulation, manufacturing, packaging, distribution. 2025 was a record year: sales of $1,490M with Q4 at $386M (best-ever Q4). Management 2026 guidance is approximately $1.48B in sales and $4.85 diluted EPS. In January, the company announced exclusive long-term worldwide fragrance licenses with David Beckham and Nautica plus a 15-year GUESS extension through 2048 — three contractual annuities added in a single month.

The bear case: 2026 guide of $1.48B implies flat-to-slightly-down sales versus 2025, suggesting the post-COVID fragrance boom has peaked. Inter Parfums is at the mercy of license counterparties — Burberry's 2012 in-housing remains the cautionary tale. SG&A is climbing as the company invests in proprietary brand Solferino (40 doors at end-2025, +50 in H1 2026) and Lacoste's 2024 license addition. Founder-controlled governance limits activist optionality. At a ~25x P/E on $4.85 EPS, the stock is not statistically cheap.

The bull thesis is a multi-decade compounder hiding in plain sight: founder-CEO Jean Madar bought $25k of stock recently (small but meaningful given his existing stake), the 15-year GUESS renewal extends a critical license to 2048, Beckham and Nautica add fresh growth platforms, and proprietary brand Solferino opens an ultra-luxury channel where Inter Parfums captures retail-style margins instead of license royalties. Recession-resistant category, asset-light model, family-aligned capital allocation, and steady FCF that funds dividends and small buybacks. Compounders rarely look "cheap" — they look durable.
IPAR.whatScreenCantSee: The January 2026 announcement bundling David Beckham, Nautica, and the 15-year GUESS extension to 2048 is a contractual annuity stack that cannot be picked up by quantitative screens — it shows up only in management commentary and the eventual 10-K license schedule. The CEO's open-market purchase, while small in absolute dollars, is meaningful insider-alignment signal in a name where founders Madar and Benacin already control voting power. Solferino — the company's first proprietary ultra-luxury brand — is the optionality nobody is paying for: at 40 doors today and adding 50 more in H1 2026, even a modest hit-rate adds margin layers far above license-royalty economics.

MUSA.name: Murphy USA Inc.
MUSA.content:
# 7. MUSA — Murphy USA Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Fuel + Convenience Retailer | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
Murphy USA has reduced its share count by roughly half since the 2013 spin-off from Murphy Oil — one of the most aggressive buyback programs in U.S. retail. The market models MUSA as a low-margin gas station; the per-store economics, real estate optionality, and capital-allocation cadence say otherwise. At ~10x earnings on normalized fuel margins, you are buying a multi-decade compounder at a cyclical valuation.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Murphy USA operates ~1,750 fuel-and-convenience stores under the Murphy USA, Murphy Express, and QuickChek banners, concentrated in the Southeast and Midwest U.S. The model is structurally advantaged: small-format kiosk/convenience boxes co-located with Walmart Supercenters drive captive traffic at cost-of-occupancy that big-box C-store competitors (Sheetz, Wawa, Circle K) cannot replicate. Fuel volumes per store and merchandise contribution have both grown for a decade, while the company is mid-way through a multi-year remodel/raze-and-rebuild program that lifts merchandise sales per store materially. The financial signature is the giveaway: tax-efficient share repurchases that have shrunk the float from ~46M at spin to roughly half that today, with management explicitly framing every capital decision through the per-share lens.

The bear case is that MUSA is a fuel retailer, period — and fuel margins are cyclical. Retail fuel cents-per-gallon margins expanded structurally post-COVID as small operators exited and consumers became less price-sensitive at the pump; if those margins normalize lower, MUSA's earnings power resets. Tobacco, which is roughly a third of inside-store gross profit, is in secular decline (cigarette volumes down mid-single-digits annually), and EV adoption — while slower than projections — is a long-duration headwind for fuel volume. The Walmart co-location model, which built the company, has limited remaining new-build runway in the U.S.

The bull case is compounding: MUSA has bought back ~50% of shares since spin, dividend has grown every year, and the QuickChek acquisition gives the company a higher-margin food and beverage platform to roll out across the legacy footprint. Management's "raze-and-rebuild" program lifts per-store EBITDA meaningfully and the unit economics on new builds remain attractive. At 10–11x earnings on normalized fuel margins versus 18–22x for peer C-store operators (and 25x+ for Costco-like compounders), the gap is the entire thesis. A continued ~5–8% annual share-count reduction translates 0% topline growth into mid-teens EPS growth.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K segment disclosure shows that "Merchandise contribution dollars" — gross profit, not revenue — has grown for the better part of a decade even when fuel volumes were flat, because the mix has shifted toward higher-margin food, packaged beverage, and non-tobacco categories accelerated by the QuickChek acquisition. The per-store remodel disclosure (number of stores converted, capex per store, lift in merchandise sales) is the cleanest read on the compounding engine and is buried in the operations MD&A. Equally important: Murphy owns the underlying real estate at the majority of its sites, an asset not on the screen. The buyback cadence — disclosed quarterly in the 10-Q — should be read as the most reliable indicator of management's view on intrinsic value: when MUSA accelerates buybacks, fuel margins are typically being reinvested at a discount.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **Q1 2026 earnings — late April / early May 2026** — buyback pace, fuel margin trajectory, raze-and-rebuild count.
2. **Investor Day / Annual Meeting — May 2026** — long-term guidance refresh and merchandise contribution targets.
3. **Q4 2026 holiday tobacco/merchandise data — February 2027** — checkpoint on inside-store mix shift versus tobacco decline.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

|---|---|---|
| 1 | Business quality / moat | 7 |
| 2 | Management quality | 9 |
| 3 | Capital allocation discipline | 10 |
| 4 | Balance sheet strength | 7 |

NRP.name: Natural Resource Partners L.P.
NRP.content:
# 8. NRP — Natural Resource Partners L.P.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Coal Royalty MLP, Debt Paydown Story | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
NRP is a coal royalty MLP that owns mineral rights and surface acreage; lessees mine, NRP collects royalties with no operating capex. Over the last several years management has used the cash gusher to pay down a once-precarious debt stack to near-zero, redeem the warrants/preferreds that diluted unitholders, and is now positioned to return capital aggressively. Trades at a single-digit free-cash-flow multiple with a depleting but durable royalty stream.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
NRP collects royalties on metallurgical and thermal coal mined from its lands (concentrated in Appalachia and the Illinois Basin), plus aggregates and a soda-ash interest via its stake in Sisecam Wyoming. The royalty model means essentially no maintenance capex — operators bear mining cost risk while NRP earns a percentage of revenue. Met-coal pricing post-2021 generated a multi-year cash windfall that management redirected toward debt reduction and warrant/preferred buyouts rather than distributions, transforming the capital structure from leveraged-and-fragile to clean-and-flexible.

The bear case is straightforward: this is coal in secular decline, ESG-screened out of most institutional mandates, and the asset base is depleting with finite mine lives. Met coal pricing is volatile and tied to global steel demand (China property weakness, EU steel weakness). MLP structure carries K-1 friction. Tisch family / Robertson Stephens-era heritage holders may eventually exit. Royalty rates and minimum payments can be renegotiated when leases roll. Environmental and reclamation liabilities — though contractually borne by lessees — can revert to NRP in lessee bankruptcy scenarios (a real risk given coal-operator bankruptcies in the prior cycle).

The bull case: with debt and preferreds substantially gone, distributable cash flow per unit is now uncapped and management has signaled a return to meaningful unitholder distributions. The soda-ash stake provides a non-coal cash stream tied to glass and lithium-battery demand. Aggregates royalties are growing. Insider alignment is strong; the GP has demonstrated discipline. If management commits to a sustained variable distribution policy, the unit should re-rate toward royalty-trust comparables (low-double-digit yield on a cleaner balance sheet). Tail catalyst: a strategic combination with another coal/aggregates royalty vehicle.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K royalty footnotes disclose the per-ton royalty rates and minimum-royalty floors by lessee — minimums create a downside floor most screens miss. The Sisecam Wyoming JV equity-method investment has a separate cash distribution stream that doesn't show up on the consolidated income statement cleanly. Recent 10-Q filings detail the warrant/preferred elimination and the resulting capital-structure simplification, which materially expands distributable cash per common unit. Reclamation surety bonds posted by lessees are disclosed in contingencies and protect NRP. Historical lessee bankruptcies (Foresight, Murray, Blackjewel) and how NRP recovered/redirected those leases are disclosed in MD&A and provide a track record of asset durability.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. Q4 2025 / FY2025 distribution and capital-return policy announcement (Feb 2026).
2. Met-coal benchmark contract resets (Q2 2026).
3. Potential simplification (GP/LP collapse or C-corp conversion) — undated.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 7/10 |
| Management integrity | 8/10 |
| Capital allocation | 9/10 (debt paydown) |
| Balance sheet | 8/10 (cleaned up) |
| Moat | 6/10 (mineral rights) |
| Reinvestment runway | 4/10 (depleting) |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Margin of safety | 8/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7/10 |
| Downside protection | 6/10 |
| **Total** | **70/100** |

2. Met-coal benchmark contract resets (Q2 2026).
3. Potential simplification (GP/LP collapse or C-corp conversion) — undated.

ENSG.name: The Ensign Group, Inc.
ENSG.content:
# 9. ENSG — The Ensign Group, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | 30+ Year Same-Store Skilled-Nursing Roll-Up | Score: 79/100

### THE SETUP
Ensign is a decentralized skilled-nursing operator that has compounded earnings at high-teens rates for two decades by acquiring underperforming SNFs and turning them around via local-leadership autonomy ("cluster" model). Despite its track record, it still trades like a healthcare REIT/operator hybrid, not like the serial compounder its results justify.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Ensign operates 300+ skilled nursing and senior living facilities across the US, primarily in the West and Midwest. The company's "Service Center" model devolves operating authority to local CEOs while centralizing accounting, IT, real estate (via spun-off CareTrust REIT), and best-practice sharing. Unlike most multi-site healthcare operators that suffer margin decay with scale, Ensign has consistently grown same-facility occupancy and skilled-mix, expanded margins, and produced compounding free cash flow. The 2014 CareTrust spin and the 2022 Pennant separation demonstrate management's willingness to surface value through corporate actions.

The bear case is regulatory and reimbursement: skilled nursing depends heavily on Medicare/Medicaid mix, and CMS rate updates plus state Medicaid budgets create ongoing volatility. PDPM (Patient Driven Payment Model) audits, staffing-mandate rules under the Biden-era CMS regulation, and labor cost inflation (RNs, CNAs) are persistent margin pressures. Litigation risk in nursing homes is structural — wrongful death and elder abuse claims regularly hit operators. The stock trades at a premium multiple to other SNF operators, so any operational stumble or reimbursement shock would compress the multiple meaningfully. Recent acquisitions in newer geographies (Tennessee, Virginia) carry execution risk as the cluster model scales beyond its Western roots.

The bull case is the compounding flywheel: Ensign has 30+ years of evidence that it can buy distressed SNFs at low cost-per-bed, transition them to its operating model, and double facility-level EBITDAR within 3-5 years. The pipeline of acquisition candidates is enormous (the SNF industry remains highly fragmented and capital-starved). Management owns meaningful equity, the dividend has grown for 20+ consecutive years, and the company self-funds growth without dilutive equity issuance. If the political environment normalizes around staffing rules (the 2024 mandate was vacated by a federal court), margins could re-expand.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The cluster/Service Center model is the entire alpha — and it doesn't show up in any screen. Each facility CEO ("CEO of the Building") has full P&L authority, including hiring, capital projects, and pricing decisions, with peer benchmarking driving emulation rather than top-down mandate. This produces a learning organization where best practices propagate horizontally. Read the 10-K's discussion of the "Organizational Structure" — the language about "local autonomy" and "shared best practices" reflects a genuinely differentiated operating philosophy. CareTrust spin economics still benefit ENSG via long-term master leases. Pennant spin (home health, hospice) removed lower-margin exposure. The acquisition pipeline runs through long-cultivated state-level relationships invisible to outside investors.

### CATALYSTS
- Q1 2026 earnings (early May 2026) — same-facility occupancy and skilled-mix updates
- CMS FY2027 SNF PPS proposed rule (April-May 2026) — annual reimbursement update
- Continued accretive acquisitions throughout 2026 (typically 20-40 facilities/year)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

| Moat | 7 | Operating model + cluster density create durable advantage |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Net cash on operating side; CareTrust leases off-balance-sheet rent |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | Premium multiple to peers; not deep value |
| Catalyst | 5 | Steady compounding rather than discrete catalyst |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | High quality but limited upside vs. premium starting point |
| Business Quality | 8 | Consistent ROIC, margin expansion, FCF generation |
| Executability | 9 | Liquid mid-cap, no float issues |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Reimbursement shock or major litigation could compress multiple meaningfully |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Resilient demographic tailwind (aging) but reimbursement-dependent |
| **TOTAL** | **67** | Quality compounder; pay attention on any drawdown |

CHE.name: Chemed Corporation
CHE.content:
# 10. CHE — Chemed Corporation
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | 219 Consecutive Dividends, $300M Buyback | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
Chemed pairs VITAS Healthcare (the largest U.S. for-profit hospice) with Roto-Rooter (national plumbing/drain) — two unrelated cash machines under one disciplined capital allocator that has paid dividends for 219 consecutive quarters (54 years). 2025 EPS dipped on Roto-Rooter softness, the stock sold off, and management responded with a $300M buyback authorization plus 20% dividend increase.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Chemed operates two segments: VITAS Hospice (~70% of revenue, growing 10-11% in 2025 ex-Medicare cap with 18-19% EBITDA margins) and Roto-Rooter (~30%, 2.4-3.0% growth in 2025 with 25-26% EBITDA margins). 2025 service revenues $2.53B (+4.1% YoY); adjusted diluted EPS $21.55 (-6.8% as Roto-Rooter softened). Q4 2025 buyback: 400K shares for $174.6M at $436.39 avg. Remaining authorization at year-end ~$127.3M plus a fresh $300M added. Dividend $0.60 quarterly (+20% increase). 2026 guidance: adjusted EPS $23.25-$24.25, implying recovery and the algorithm restarting.

The bear case is real near-term softness: Roto-Rooter's residential demand has been weak through 2025, lead generation declined as Google/Yelp ad costs rose and DIY behavior post-COVID stuck. The Q2 2025 executive management departure announcement injected uncertainty. VITAS faces ongoing Medicare hospice cap exposure (limits Medicare reimbursement at the program level) and DOJ scrutiny historically — VITAS settled a False Claims Act case in 2017. Hospice utilization growth post-COVID has decelerated. CHE multiple at ~18x EPS and ~13x EV/EBITDA isn't cheap on a single-year basis. Insider ownership is low (<2%), so this is family-disciplined-by-history, not family-controlled in equity terms.

The bull case is the model: capital-light services, two non-cyclical end markets (death and clogged drains), pricing power, dual-segment diversification, and management that has demonstrated 50+ years of disciplined capital return. The 2025 EPS dip is largely Roto-Rooter cyclical/lead-gen — fixable. Hospice (VITAS) growth at +10% with population aging and Medicare Advantage hospice carve-in pilots expanding addressable market is structural. The $300M buyback at $400-500/share (vs. recent $700+ peak) is opportunistic; coupled with the dividend hike it signals board confidence. 2026 EPS guide of $23.25-$24.25 implies +10-13% growth, putting forward P/E at ~17-18x for a defensive compounder.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K segment footnotes break out VITAS revenue between "routine home care" (commodity hospice) and "general inpatient" / "continuous care" (high-acuity, much higher reimbursement). The mix-shift toward higher-acuity patients drives blended revenue per patient day and is a hidden margin lever. VITAS's Medicare cap calculation is patient-cohort-based (not aggregate), so cap impact is geographically lumpy and disclosed by program. Roto-Rooter's franchise vs. company-owned mix matters for incremental margins on demand recovery — company-owned operations carry higher fixed costs. The company's net cash position and absence of meaningful debt give the buyback authorization real teeth — at ~$436/share execution, $300M = ~4-5% of shares retired.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- **April 2026:** Q1 2026 earnings — first read on Roto-Rooter recovery and 2026 guide.
- **2026:** $300M+ buyback execution into any further weakness.
- **2026:** New CEO/Roto-Rooter leadership impact post mid-2025 management departure.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Two simple service businesses |
| Moat | 7 | VITAS scale + Roto brand |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | Net cash; minimal debt |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | ~18x EPS; not deep value |
| Catalyst | 7 | Buyback + 2026 guide reset |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Defensive with growth optionality |
| Business Quality | 9 | 25%+ Roto margin; 18% VITAS margin |
| Executability | 8 | Liquid mid-cap |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | Roto secular weakness; Medicare cap |
| Macro Independence | 9 | Hospice + plumbing = recession-proof |
| **TOTAL** | **78** | |

INGR.name: Ingredion Incorporated
INGR.content:
# 11. INGR — Ingredion Incorporated
## CYCLICAL TROUGH | RECORD EPS / WEAK FY26 GUIDE | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
Ingredion just delivered record 2025 EPS of $11.18 yet the stock trades at a forward P/E in single digits because Q4 missed and the FY26 guide is a low-single-digit grow-over a record year. A cyclical sweetener trough plus an Argo (Indianapolis) corn-wet-mill operational disruption are masking the underlying mix shift to specialty Texture & Healthful Solutions, where management is reinvesting buyback dollars and pricing at a premium.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Ingredion is a $7.2B-revenue ingredient maker that converts corn, tapioca, potato and pulses into starches, sweeteners and specialty proteins for global food, beverage and industrial customers. The 2025 print showed full-year diluted EPS of $11.18 and adjusted diluted EPS of $11.13 with gross margin above 25%, while the company returned $435M to shareholders through dividends and 1.8M-share repurchases. The strategic story is the Texture & Healthful Solutions segment plus LATAM Food & Industrial Ingredients, where pricing power, clean-label trends and protein fortification produce mid-teens operating margins, far above legacy bulk sweetener economics.

The bear sees a tired mid-cap commodity processor: Q4 2025 missed both revenue and EPS estimates, the Argo facility disruption hit the largest US/Canada plant, sweetener demand softened (high-fructose corn syrup is a long-term secular loser to sucralose, stevia and reformulation), and the FY26 guide of $11.00–$11.80 EPS implies essentially flat earnings off a record. Corn-cost volatility, FX headwinds in Pakistan/Argentina/Mexico, and the slow capital-cycle nature of the business cap multiple expansion. Activist screens turn up nothing — no 13D, no proxy fight, no break-up catalyst.

The bull frames it as a defensive, high-yield, undervalued compounder transforming for growth: the specialty mix is now a majority of operating income, capital allocation is disciplined (debt under 2x EBITDA, growing buyback), and FY26 cash from operations is guided at $820M–$940M against a sub-$8B market cap. Catalysts include Argo back to nameplate run-rate, Texture & Healthful Solutions volume acceleration, continued LATAM pricing, and the eventual cyclical re-rate when sweetener pricing turns. Without an activist or break-up, this is a slow-money compounder, not an event-driven trade.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 2026 proxy and 2025 Investor Day re-cut segment reporting around "Texture & Healthful Solutions" — that segment's incremental margins on specialty starches are double what bulk sweeteners earn, but consolidated reporting buries the mix shift. The Argo disruption is operational (not structural), so the FY26 guide bakes in a self-inflicted headwind that should reverse. Buybacks accelerated through 2025 and management hinted at a higher pace once Argo normalizes. There is no activist on the file, but the SOTP — specialty starches/proteins valued at peer multiples vs. legacy sweeteners at 5x EBITDA — implies meaningful upside if a strategic ever pushes for a separation.

### CATALYSTS
- Q1 2026 earnings late April / early May 2026 — Argo normalization update
- 2026 Annual Meeting May 2026 — proxy vote, capital return commentary
- FY26 EPS print early Feb 2027 — mid-point $11.40 vs record $11.18

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

| Business quality | 7/10 |
| Management | 7/10 |
| Balance sheet | 7/10 |
| Moat | 6/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |

| Insider alignment | 5/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 5/10 |
| Margin of safety | 7/10 |
| Asymmetry | 7/10 |

INGR.analystSummary:
Ingredion is a $7.2B-revenue ingredient maker that converts corn, tapioca, potato and pulses into starches, sweeteners and specialty proteins for global food, beverage and industrial customers. The 2025 print showed full-year diluted EPS of $11.18 and adjusted diluted EPS of $11.13 with gross margin above 25%, while the company returned $435M to shareholders through dividends and 1.8M-share repurchases. The strategic story is the Texture & Healthful Solutions segment plus LATAM Food & Industrial Ingredients, where pricing power, clean-label trends and protein fortification produce mid-teens operating margins, far above legacy bulk sweetener economics.

The bear sees a tired mid-cap commodity processor: Q4 2025 missed both revenue and EPS estimates, the Argo facility disruption hit the largest US/Canada plant, sweetener demand softened (high-fructose corn syrup is a long-term secular loser to sucralose, stevia and reformulation), and the FY26 guide of $11.00–$11.80 EPS implies essentially flat earnings off a record. Corn-cost volatility, FX headwinds in Pakistan/Argentina/Mexico, and the slow capital-cycle nature of the business cap multiple expansion. Activist screens turn up nothing — no 13D, no proxy fight, no break-up catalyst.

The bull frames it as a defensive, high-yield, undervalued compounder transforming for growth: the specialty mix is now a majority of operating income, capital allocation is disciplined (debt under 2x EBITDA, growing buyback), and FY26 cash from operations is guided at $820M–$940M against a sub-$8B market cap. Catalysts include Argo back to nameplate run-rate, Texture & Healthful Solutions volume acceleration, continued LATAM pricing, and the eventual cyclical re-rate when sweetener pricing turns. Without an activist or break-up, this is a slow-money compounder, not an event-driven trade.
INGR.whatScreenCantSee: The 2026 proxy and 2025 Investor Day re-cut segment reporting around "Texture & Healthful Solutions" — that segment's incremental margins on specialty starches are double what bulk sweeteners earn, but consolidated reporting buries the mix shift. The Argo disruption is operational (not structural), so the FY26 guide bakes in a self-inflicted headwind that should reverse. Buybacks accelerated through 2025 and management hinted at a higher pace once Argo normalizes. There is no activist on the file, but the SOTP — specialty starches/proteins valued at peer multiples vs. legacy sweeteners at 5x EBITDA — implies meaningful upside if a strategic ever pushes for a separation.

ROP.name: Roper Technologies, Inc.
ROP.content:
# 12. ROP — Roper Technologies, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | $7.9B 2025 revenue, 56.7% Application Software | Score: 78/100
### THE SETUP
Roper Technologies is the quietest of the great American compounders — a vertical-software roll-up with three reportable segments (Application Software, Network Software, Technology Enabled Products) that has spent two decades migrating from industrial niches into mission-critical SaaS. 2025 revenue was $7,902.5M, up 12.3% (5% organic, 7% from acquisitions). Application Software alone was $4,483.0M (56.7% of total) and grew 16% (10% acquired, 5% organic). The 2025 capital deployment: $3.3B into vertical-software bolt-ons including the ~$1.85B CentralReach acquisition and Subsplash. The portfolio is the moat — Aderant (legal), CentralReach (ABA therapy), Clinisys (lab informatics), Data Innovations (lab middleware), Deltek (project ERP), Frontline (K-12 admin), PowerPlan (utility tax), Procare (childcare), Strata (healthcare planning), Transact/CBORD (campus payments), Vertafore (insurance agency). Each business is a small-vertical monopoly with high gross retention and limited cyclicality. The "hidden" qualifier is more about audience than obscurity: ROP is well-followed but rarely held by quality-compounder skeptics who dismiss it as "Constellation Software with American multiples."

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The Roper machine is simple to describe and hard to copy. Identify a small or mid-cap software business serving a narrow vertical with low customer churn and pricing power; pay a premium multiple; integrate lightly; redirect free cash flow into the next acquisition. The result over time is an asset-light, cash-generative portfolio whose organic growth (mid-single digits in 2025) compounds on top of acquired growth (high single digits in 2025), with margins that expand as software displaces lower-multiple industrial revenue. The 2018-2025 portfolio reshaping — divesting industrial businesses, acquiring vertical software — is now substantively complete; ROP is essentially a software conglomerate that retains a small Technology Enabled Products tail.

The bear case is well-understood and largely about price. ROP rarely trades cheaply on near-term earnings, and the per-deal multiples in vertical software have crept up across the cycle as private-equity dry powder competes for the same assets. Organic growth at 5% is respectable but not exciting; if M&A capacity is constrained (by leverage, by valuation, or by deal scarcity), the algorithm slows. Goodwill and intangible amortization make GAAP earnings noisy; investors must rely on adjusted/free-cash-flow framings that the company controls. And the model depends on disciplined underwriting at scale — at $3.3B of annual deployment, even one mispriced acquisition is meaningful.

The reason ROP earns the "hidden compounder" tag despite being a household name in quality circles is its tendency to be overshadowed by flashier compounders (Constellation, Brookfield, MSCI). Patient holders have been rewarded by a long-duration combination of organic growth, accretive M&A, debt-financed cash returns to shareholders via reinvestment, and serial multiple expansion. The 2025 print — 12.3% revenue growth, software now ~85%+ of mix, $3.3B deployed — reaffirms the recipe. The key question for any new buyer is purely the price paid: ROP's compounding mechanics deserve a premium, but the right entry point typically requires patience or a market drawdown.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Screens see ROP as expensive on near-term P/E, modest on dividend yield, and mid-teens grower on revenue. What screens miss: (1) the cash-conversion quality — ROP's free-cash-flow margin runs well above its GAAP earnings margin because of intangible amortization from past deals; (2) the durability of customer cohorts in each acquired niche (e.g., Vertafore in insurance agencies, Aderant in BigLaw practice management); (3) the acquisition pipeline — ROP's relationships with vertical-software founders are a structural sourcing advantage; (4) the quality of recurring revenue, which is heavily subscription-based with high retention; (5) the optionality of price increases as inflation-protected, mission-critical tools that customers cannot easily switch off.

### CATALYSTS
- **Q1/Q2 2026 earnings prints** — first reads with full CentralReach contribution and updated organic-growth disclosure.
- **Next major acquisition (likely 2026)** — ROP typically deploys $2-4B per year; the next single ~$1B+ deal will reset earnings power.
- **Capital structure update** — leverage capacity refresh after 2025's deployment dictates 2026 M&A pace.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality / moat | 9/10 |
| Management quality | 9/10 |
| Capital allocation | 9/10 |
| Balance sheet strength | 7/10 |
| Earnings predictability | 8/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 8/10 |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Margin of safety at price | 5/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7/10 |
| Downside protection | 9/10 |
| **Total** | **78/100** |

GBLI.name: Global Indemnity Group, LLC
GBLI.content:
# 13. GBLI — Global Indemnity Group, LLC
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | ~0.7x BOOK VALUE | Score: 68/100

### THE SETUP
Specialty E&S insurer trading at roughly 0.7x book value while completing "Project Manifest" — a structural reorganization that de-stacks the insurance subs into separate divisions (Penn-America, Vacant Express, Collectibles, Specialty Products) and carves out tech (Kaleidoscope) and claims (Liberty) as standalone businesses, with Fox Paine as financial advisor and effective controlling shareholder.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Global Indemnity Group (Nasdaq: GBLI, recently moved from NYSE) is a specialty P&C holding company best known for its Penn-America excess & surplus lines book, plus a portfolio of niche programs (vacant property, collectibles, specialty commercial). Book value per share was reported at $49.88 as of September 30, 2024 against a stock price meaningfully below $35, putting the equity at roughly 70% of book — a discount that has persisted for years and is now compressing as Project Manifest separates the operating units, makes the tech and claims subsidiaries marketable, and crystallizes hidden segment value. Fox Paine & Company controls the supervoting Class B shares and remains both anchor shareholder and architect of the strategic plan.

The bear case is the standard E&S micro-cap discount: low float (Fox Paine owns the supervoting block), no analyst coverage, lumpy underwriting results in cat-exposed lines, and historical loss reserve volatility that has burned holders before. Specialty insurers can look cheap on book for years if reserves under-develop or if the tech and claims subsidiaries are priced for a sale that never materializes. The buyback has slowed dramatically — zero shares bought back in Q4 2025 — which removes one of the more reliable per-share value drivers. And Fox Paine's controlling position means minorities are along for the ride; any take-private at less than full book would leave minority holders with a haircut versus intrinsic.

The bull case is structural and self-help-driven. Project Manifest unbundled the operating divisions, increased consolidated surplus through the de-stacking, and made the technology (Kaleidoscope Insurance Technologies) and claims (Liberty Insurance Adjustment Agency) subsidiaries separately monetizable. The Nasdaq listing transfer is a sign of intent to broaden the investor base. Penn-America is operating in a hard E&S market with combined ratios well below 100%. Across the buyback authorization announced October 2022, GBLI has repurchased 1,357,082 shares for $33.98M — proof of capital-return intent even if the cadence has slowed. At 0.7x book in a hardening specialty market with multiple monetization levers, the asymmetry is favorable.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K's segment footnote on Project Manifest is the alpha: each of the four Penn-America divisions now has its own GAAP segment reporting, which means combined-ratio outliers (Vacant Express historically the laggard, Collectibles historically the gem) become visible to outsiders for the first time. Also note the Class B supervoting structure controlled by Fox Paine — read the 10-K's related-party section carefully because Fox Paine collects management/advisory fees that depress per-share earnings versus a clean cap structure. Finally, the Bermuda LLC structure of GBLI provides tax efficiencies that conventional U.S. insurance comps lack — book value comparisons to peers under-credit this.

### CATALYSTS
- 1H 2026 — Project Manifest financial reporting reveals segment economics
- 2026 buyback resumption / new authorization announcement
- Potential Kaleidoscope (insurtech) or Liberty (claims TPA) carve-out sale
- Continued hard-market combined-ratio outperformance in Penn-America

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Specialty E&S — knowable but technical |
| Moat | 6 | Penn-America brand, hard-market positioning |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Strong surplus, conservative reserves; BV ~$50/sh |
| Margin of Safety | 9 | ~0.70x P/B, sub-9 P/E on normalized |
| Catalyst | 6 | Manifest segment reveal + monetization optionality |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Book floor with carve-out upside |
| Business Quality | 6 | E&S underwriting cyclical but currently hard |
| Executability | 5 | Low float, controlled, illiquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Reserve dev or Fox Paine take-under |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Insurance cycle, not GDP |
| **TOTAL** | **67** | Quiet compounder if Manifest delivers |

GRND.name: Grindr Inc.
GRND.content:
# 14. GRND — Grindr Inc.
## POST-LOCKUP | SPAC de-merger 2022, founder + sponsor still control | Score: 60/100

### THE SETUP
Grindr came public via SPAC (Tiga Acquisition Corp) in November 2022 at a fully-diluted enterprise valuation that has since been validated by genuine subscriber growth and free cash flow. The float remains thin and dominated by the original San Vicente Acquisition consortium plus a handful of pre-merger holders, creating recurring secondary-offering and lock-up-expiry overhangs that keep institutional ownership low relative to the underlying earnings power.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Grindr is the largest geosocial network targeted at gay, bi, trans, and queer users, with a paying subscriber base monetized via Grindr XTRA, Unlimited, and ad revenue. Operating leverage is the central story: the platform requires modest engineering and content moderation spend relative to revenue, and ARPU expansion via subscription-tier reshuffling has outpaced sub-count growth. The company has guided to material EBITDA margin expansion and continues to invest in product (AI assistants, travel feature) without breaking the cost discipline established post-SPAC.

The bear case starts with the controlled-company structure. San Vicente Acquisition (the consortium that bought Grindr from Kunlun in 2020) and pre-merger PIPE investors collectively retain a controlling block; secondary offerings have repeatedly been used to monetize, and each one creates an air pocket. Regulatory and reputational risk is real — privacy and HIV-status data exposures have driven prior FTC scrutiny; CFIUS and overseas regulators continue to examine Chinese-origin user-data legacy issues. Competition from Hinge, Sniffies, and free alternatives compresses ARPU expansion long-term. The stock has already re-rated from sub-$10 to current levels, narrowing the margin of safety.

The bull case is sustained operating leverage plus capital return. Grindr generates substantial free cash flow on a small fixed-cost base; debt has been paid down and the company has authorized buybacks. Subscriber penetration of the addressable user base remains low — high-single-digit percent — leaving runway for ARPU and conversion gains. Strategic value to a larger dating-app or entertainment platform (Match, Bumble, an ad-tech consolidator) is non-trivial; the controlled structure also opens the door to a take-private at a premium if the public-market discount widens. AI-driven product features could re-accelerate engagement.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K and recent secondary prospectuses disclose the specific San Vicente consortium ownership block and the lock-up release schedule — most screens still tag Grindr as broadly held when the public float is materially smaller than reported share count suggests. The company also carries a tax asset and capitalized software basis from the SPAC merger that distorts GAAP earnings versus economic FCF. Subscriber cohort retention data (disclosed only in investor-day decks, not 10-K) shows long-tail LTV materially higher than peer dating apps, which is invisible to a P/E or P/S screen. Finally, the controlled-company election allows governance practices that an institutional screener would penalize but which create take-private optionality.

### CATALYSTS
- Next secondary offering / lock-up release event (recurring overhang)
- Q1 and Q2 2026 earnings: subscription growth and ARPU update
- Strategic M&A or take-private discussion: speculative, unscheduled

LMNR.name: Limoneira Company
LMNR.content:
# 15. LMNR — Limoneira Company
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | CA citrus + 7,100 acres | Score: 58/100

### THE SETUP
Limoneira owns ~7,100 acres of California citrus and avocado land plus water rights and entitled real estate (Harvest at Limoneira JV) — assets that on a sum-of-parts basis are typically appraised well above the company's market cap, while the operating citrus business serves as financial drag that masks the land value.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Limoneira is one of the last public pure-play California agricultural land owners. The portfolio includes lemons, oranges, specialty citrus, avocados, and water rights across Ventura County, with a meaningful ancillary real estate development (Harvest at Limoneira, a master-planned community JV with The Lewis Group). The investment narrative for the past decade has been "the land is worth more than the stock" — California ag land plus water rights have appreciated substantially while the operating citrus business has been hammered by oversupply, weather, and labor cost inflation.

The bear case: the operating business is structurally challenged. Lemon prices are volatile, depend on weather and Mexican supply, and the company has reported operating losses or marginal profitability for multiple recent years. Water cost and availability in Ventura County is a chronic concern. Selling land sounds easy in a presentation but California entitlement processes are notoriously slow, and the Harvest JV has taken much longer than originally projected to monetize. Activist pressure has come and gone without forcing a major asset sale. The dividend has been cut or held flat as cash flow has disappointed. Without a clear catalyst, the NAV gap can persist indefinitely.

The bull case: management has explicitly pivoted toward "asset-lighter" since 2023, signaling willingness to monetize non-core land parcels. The Harvest at Limoneira JV continues to deliver cash distributions (multi-year wind-down). California ag land in Ventura County has appraised at $40-60K per acre for premium parcels — applying even a conservative blended $25-30K to the 7,100 acres yields a land value materially in excess of current EV. Water rights, often invisible on the income statement, hold real option value as California water becomes scarcer. An eventual full or partial REIT conversion / asset sale is the long-tailed catalyst.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K carries Limoneira's land at historical cost — not fair market value — meaning book value materially understates economic value. Specific parcels (Centennial, East Areas) have entitlement status disclosed only in narrative footnotes, not on the balance sheet, and the Harvest JV residual interest is accounted at investment cost net of distributions, hiding the gross value of remaining lots. The water-rights portfolio (acre-feet of allocated and adjudicated water in Santa Paula and Ventura basins) is not on the balance sheet at all — yet has been valued in California water-rights market transactions at six figures per acre-foot in recent stress years.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
- **Harvest at Limoneira distribution updates (each quarter through 2026)** — cash distribution pace is the cleanest near-term return-of-capital signal.
- **FY2026 strategic review communications** — management asset-lighter pivot needs concrete sale or JV announcement.
- **California water market stress events (rolling)** — drought conditions revalue water rights and pressure peers to monetize.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

| 1 | Business quality (moat, ROIC) | 4 |
| 2 | Management capability & alignment | 5 |
| 3 | Balance sheet strength | 5 |
| 4 | Reinvestment runway | 4 |
| 5 | Earnings predictability | 3 |
| 6 | Margin of safety (price vs value) | 7 |
| 7 | Catalyst clarity | 4 |
| 8 | Downside protection | 7 |
| 9 | Insider behavior | 6 |
| 10 | Simplicity / understandability | 6 |
| **Total** | | **51/100** |
