EEFT.name: Euronet Worldwide, Inc.
EEFT.content:
# 91. EEFT — Euronet Worldwide, Inc.
## ACTIVIST — OPERATIONAL TURN | Voss 4.2%, $115–140 PT vs. $74 | Score: 75/100

### THE SETUP
Voss Capital's March 4, 2026 open letter to the Euronet board lays out a sale/spin thesis with a $115–140 price target — 55–90% above the $74.40 quote. The stock has de-rated from 15–20x to 6.5x earnings while peers like NCR Atleos demonstrate the standalone value of a focused ATM business; Voss wants strategic alternatives now.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Euronet is a $3.1B-market-cap global payments operator across three segments: EFT (ATM network, including Euronet's massive global independent ATM footprint), epay (prepaid/digital top-up distribution), and Money Transfer (Ria/Xe remittance). The stock is down ~54% over five years vs. the S&P +81%. Voss Capital, which holds approximately 4.2%, issued a March 4, 2026 open letter calling for an immediate strategic-alternatives review, halt to "investor forum" placeholder communications, meaningful capital return, and explicitly highlighting the ATM business as a sale candidate to NCR Atleos or private equity. Voss frames an SOTP at $115–140 per share.

The bear case rests on management's track record of capital allocation defending the conglomerate structure. CEO Mike Brown is also the largest individual shareholder and has historically resisted divestiture pressure, treating the three segments as cross-sell synergistic. Travel-volume normalization post-COVID has been disappointing in the EFT segment, with revenue per ATM transaction lower than pre-pandemic peaks. The Money Transfer business faces structural margin compression from digital-native remittance competitors (Wise, Remitly). The 6.5x earnings multiple may simply reflect appropriate value for a mid-cycle, low-growth payments roll-up. With only ~4.2% Voss stake, board pressure absent ISS support is limited.

The bull case has clear catalysts. NCR Atleos — the spun-off ATM-as-a-service business — trades as a clean comp suggesting Euronet's EFT segment alone could be worth $4–5B if separated. That is more than Euronet's entire current $3.1B market cap, with the epay and Money Transfer segments a free option. Voss' $115–140 per share PT translates to 55–90% upside. The cancelled investor day cited in Voss' letter is real evidence of management evasiveness. Either the board commences a formal strategic review (likely after Q1/Q2 2026 earnings) or a third party (Atleos, KKR-style PE) bids for the ATM business directly. With 6.5x earnings as the floor, downside is bounded.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A momentum screen flags EEFT as broken; a value screen sees the 6.5x P/E and stops there. The Voss letter is the alpha: it explicitly named NCR Atleos as a strategic acquirer for the EFT segment — Atleos has publicly stated it wants to grow inorganically in independent ATM, and Euronet's global footprint is its single most attractive M&A target. The letter also cites a cancelled investor day, which is the kind of soft governance signal screens entirely miss but that ISS weighs heavily in proxy fights. CEO Mike Brown's personal ownership creates the unusual dynamic where the largest holder is also the entrenchment risk — but it also means a $115–140 outcome is enormously personally lucrative for him, so a settlement is plausible.

### CATALYSTS
- Strategic alternatives announcement (any quarter)
- Q1 2026 earnings — late April / early May 2026
- 2026 annual meeting (late spring)
- Potential NCR Atleos or PE bid for ATM segment

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Three-segment payments roll-up; segments easy to value |
| Moat | 6 | EFT network density real; Money Transfer faces digital threat |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | ~$3.1B mkt cap; modest leverage; cash-generative |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | 6.5x earnings; 55–90% upside to Voss SOTP |
| Catalyst | 6 | Activist-driven; no hard deadline yet |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | $115–140 vs $74; downside cushioned by low multiple |
| Business Quality | 6 | Decent ROIC in EFT; mid-teens overall margins |
| Executability | 8 | $3.1B mkt cap; deep liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Brown entrenches, no review, stock dead-money for years |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Travel volumes drive EFT; macro-sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **68** | Activist-led re-rating with SOTP cushion; opportunity score 75 |

---

GLRE.name: Greenlight Capital Re Ltd.
GLRE.content:
# 128. GLRE — Greenlight Capital Re Ltd.
## HOLDCO | 0.73x book, BVPS +13.8% in 2025 | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
Greenlight Re trades at ~$14.95 against fully-diluted year-end 2025 BVPS of $20.43 — a 27% discount to book. Book value per share grew 13.8% in 2025 (8.1% in Q4 alone), well above cost of equity, while the company keeps repurchasing shares meaningfully below book. David Einhorn's investment vehicle plus a turning underwriting cycle is being priced as if neither will compound.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Greenlight Capital Re is a Cayman-domiciled property/casualty reinsurer focused on event-driven and specialty lines. Its differentiated feature is that the investment portfolio is managed by Einhorn's Greenlight Capital — a long/short equity strategy that has delivered strong post-2022 performance after a difficult 2018-2021 stretch. Underwriting has been repositioned away from legacy 2018-2020 capacity into more disciplined specialty programs, and the Innovations Unit holds minority equity stakes in insurance fintechs (Hippo, Cytora, Galileo) that are periodically marked up. Q4 2025 BVPS growth of 8.1% in a single quarter shows both legs working.

The bear case is the persistent "Einhorn discount." Long/short equity volatility is hard to underwrite, and Cayman domicile narrows institutional ownership. Float is small (~$480M market cap), making the stock illiquid for funds. Combined ratio improvement is real but reinsurance pricing — after several years of hardening — is starting to soften; a single catastrophe season could wipe out a year of underwriting gains. Innovations Unit private marks are model-driven and could reverse in a venture downturn. The discount to book has held for years; there is no obvious mechanism to close it absent a take-private.

The bull case is mathematical. BVPS compounding at 14% with shares purchased below book means intrinsic value per share grows faster than reported book. Continued buybacks at $14 against $20.43 book are immediately accretive. A take-private by Einhorn — long-rumored, never executed — at any price between current book and 1.0x book would deliver a 35%+ uplift overnight. Even absent that, Innovations Unit step-ups, a 2026 reinsurance pricing rebound after the recent Atlantic season, or simply a P/B re-rating to 0.85x represents 15%+ upside on top of underlying compounding.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Q4 2025 release confirms BVPS of $20.43 and notes continued buyback execution — but screens use stale annual book and miss the 8.1% single-quarter step-up. The Innovations Unit carries equity stakes that are NOT marked-to-market each quarter; realized gains on a Hippo/Cytora monetization could surprise to the upside. Einhorn's own Greenlight Capital LP holds a meaningful GLRE position, creating a structural floor — any take-private would require him to negotiate against himself, which historically has produced fair-to-favorable terms for minorities (see Loral, Compaq precedents). The Cayman domicile means dividends to U.S. holders avoid foreign withholding.

### CATALYSTS
- Q1 2026 results (early May 2026) — BVPS update and buyback pace
- 2026 mid-year reinsurance renewals (June/July) — pricing direction
- Potential take-private announcement by Einhorn — speculative, no timeline

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 6/10 |
| Management integrity | 8/10 |
| Capital allocation | 8/10 |
| Balance sheet | 7/10 |
| Moat durability | 5/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 7/10 |
| Insider alignment | 9/10 |
| Margin of safety | 8/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 6/10 |
| Asymmetry | 6/10 |
| **Total** | **70/100** |

---

Underwriting has been repositioned away from legacy 2018-2020 capacity into more disciplined specialty programs, and the Innovations Unit holds minority equity stakes in insurance fintechs (Hippo, Cytora, Galileo) that are periodically marked up. Q4 2025 BVPS growth of 8.1% in a single quarter shows both legs working.

The bear case is the persistent "Einhorn discount." Long/short equity volatility is hard to underwrite, and Cayman domicile narrows institutional ownership. Float is small (~$480M market cap), making the stock illiquid for funds. Combined ratio improvement is real but reinsurance pricing — after several years of hardening — is starting to soften; a single catastrophe season could wipe out a year of underwriting gains. Innovations Unit private marks are model-driven and could reverse in a venture downturn. The discount to book has held for years; there is no obvious mechanism to close it absent a take-private.

The bull case is mathematical. BVPS compounding at 14% with shares purchased below book means intrinsic value per share grows faster than reported book. Continued buybacks at $14 against $20.43 book are immediately accretive. A take-private by Einhorn — long-rumored, never executed — at any price between current book and 1.0x book would deliver a 35%+ uplift overnight. Even absent that, Innovations Unit step-ups, a 2026 reinsurance pricing rebound after the recent Atlantic season, or simply a P/B re-rating to 0.85x represents 15%+ upside on top of underlying compounding.
GLRE.whatScreenCantSee: The Q4 2025 release confirms BVPS of $20.43 and notes continued buyback execution — but screens use stale annual book and miss the 8.1% single-quarter step-up. The Innovations Unit carries equity stakes that are NOT marked-to-market each quarter; realized gains on a Hippo/Cytora monetization could surprise to the upside. Einhorn's own Greenlight Capital LP holds a meaningful GLRE position, creating a structural floor — any take-private would require him to negotiate against himself, which historically has produced fair-to-favorable terms for minorities (see Loral, Compaq precedents). The Cayman domicile means dividends to U.S. holders avoid foreign withholding.

ING.name: ING Groep N.V.
ING.content:
# 18. ING — ING Groep N.V.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Capital Return Story | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
ING Groep is a Dutch banking giant trading at a persistent discount to US peers despite cleaner capital ratios, an above-15% CET1 buffer, and an aggressive multi-year buyback-plus-dividend program. Not a sub-cash play (deposits are liabilities, not free cash) — but an under-owned European compounder distributing roughly its earnings yield back to holders annually.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
ING Groep N.V. is the Netherlands' largest bank, with a sprawling European retail and wholesale franchise spanning the Benelux core, Germany (consumer/digital), Poland, and selective wholesale banking globally. The post-financial-crisis reorganization shed insurance (NN Group spinoff, 2014) and US banking (ING Direct sold to Capital One) to leave a focused European universal bank. Capital ratios sit comfortably above ECB minima, with CET1 typically running 14-15% — well above the binding requirement — funding an explicit "distribute excess capital" framework via dividends plus serial buybacks. EUR retail franchise generates stable net interest income, with some duration sensitivity to ECB rate path.

The bear case is European bank structural: NIM compression as ECB cuts proceed through 2026, persistent regulatory drag (DGS contributions, single resolution fund levies, the Basel IV phase-in), and chronically below-cost-of-equity returns on the wholesale book. Dutch and German residential mortgage exposure carries housing-market sensitivity. Geopolitical risk is real — ING has notable wholesale exposure to commodity trade finance and emerging markets where credit cycles can turn quickly. The stock's persistent discount to US peers reflects genuine return-on-tangible-equity gap (ING typically 12-13% vs. JPM/BAC mid-teens), not just sentiment. Anti-money-laundering history (2018 Dutch settlement) is a tail risk that occasionally resurfaces.

The bull case is total-shareholder-yield arithmetic. ING's framework targets distribution of excess capital above a CET1 buffer — every year that earnings exceed buffer demands, the difference flows back via dividends and buybacks. At trough European bank multiples (~7-8x earnings, ~0.9-1.1x tangible book), the implied capital-return yield exceeds 10% annually for a credible multi-year horizon. ECB rate cuts hurt asset-side yield but help fee income and reduce funding costs; net NIM impact is modest and offset by volume. The German consumer franchise (ING-DiBa) is a secularly growing digital bank with sticky deposits. M&A optionality exists for any consolidation in European retail banking — ING is plausibly both predator and prey.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The capital framework specifics matter: ING's "CET1 ratio above ~12.5% target" creates a mechanical excess-capital release, and management has been disciplined about returning rather than reinvesting. Read the quarterly capital ratio walk to see the math. The TCFD/climate disclosure includes loan-book carbon intensity that increasingly drives both regulatory capital and reputational risk. Key footnote: ING's structural rate hedges (replicating portfolio) smooth NIM through cycles — actual NIM sensitivity to ECB rates is much lower than naive duration-of-equity calculations suggest. Also note: Dutch withholding tax on dividends affects net yield for US holders (15% treaty rate) — relevant for after-tax math.

### CATALYSTS
- Q4 2025 capital return announcement (typically buyback + dividend)
- ECB rate-cut path and NIM guidance updates
- Possible EU bank consolidation activity in 2026

| **Total** | **64/100** |

BLKB.name: Blackbaud, Inc.
BLKB.content:
# 19. BLKB — Blackbaud, Inc.
## ACTIVIST — OPERATIONAL TURN | Clearlake 20.45% / Rule of 40 = 41.4 | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
Nonprofit/social-good SaaS leader with Clearlake Capital sitting on a 20.45% stake (entered 2022), now post-EVERFI divestiture and showing organic recurring revenue +5.8% with 35.9% adjusted EBITDA margins in FY25. The company has crossed Rule of 40 and is guiding 2026 revenue $1.17-1.18B with EBITDA $430-438M and FCF $280-290M. Clearlake is the largest holder; a sale or take-private is a non-trivial outcome given the financials and PE-friendly recurring revenue profile.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Blackbaud is the dominant SaaS provider to nonprofits, foundations, K-12 private schools, faith communities, and corporate social responsibility programs (Raiser's Edge, Financial Edge, Blackbaud CRM, Blackbaud Tuition Management). Per the Feb 18, 2026 10-K and Q4 2025 release: FY25 GAAP revenue ~$1.1B (down YoY due to EVERFI divestiture), non-GAAP organic revenue +5.5%, organic recurring revenue +5.8%, non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA $405.3M at 35.9% margin, Rule of 40 = 41.4, returning to GAAP profitability. 2026 guide: revenue $1.173-1.179B, adjusted EBITDA $430-438M, non-GAAP EPS $5.15-5.25, FCF $280-290M. Clearlake Capital owns ~20.45% — entered with a rejected $71/share take-private bid in 2022; never sold down.

Bear case: Blackbaud's organic growth at mid-single-digits trails best-in-class vertical SaaS peers — nonprofit software has TAM constraints, customer budgets are donation-dependent and rate-sensitive, and competition from generalist CRM/ERP (Salesforce nonprofit cloud, Microsoft Dynamics) is intensifying. The 2020 ransomware breach and resulting class actions still drive litigation accruals years later. EVERFI was sold at a write-down — capital allocation history is mixed. Clearlake has held since 2022 without forcing a deal — that's a 4-year stalemate with no formal proxy process.

Bull case: at $61-65/share area (research note didn't include current price; stock has traded $60-75 range in 2025-2026), the company trades at roughly 14-15x 2026 EBITDA — modest for a 35%+ EBITDA-margin recurring SaaS. PE comps for vertical SaaS (Veeva, Tyler Tech segments) are 18-25x. Clearlake's 20% stake gives it veto power on any deal and standing to push for a sale. Blackbaud's 2026 guide is the cleanest in years post-EVERFI; FCF yield ~7-8% at current cap. Cushion: even without an M&A outcome, $280-290M FCF supports an aggressive buyback that compounds EPS at high single digits.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Clearlake stake plus a refreshed board (post-2022 cooperation agreement added Clearlake-aligned directors) means BLKB is governed like a quasi-private. The 2022 rejected $71 bid is the public floor — any new offer would have to top that, and 2026 EBITDA is materially higher than 2022 baseline. Blackbaud's nonprofit moat is real: switching costs in donor management software are extreme (data migrations, gift records spanning decades, IRS-required donor receipts) — Raiser's Edge has ~80%+ retention in its core customer base. The Rule of 40 = 41.4 inflection is the type of fundamental that PE buyers (Vista, Thoma Bravo) target for take-privates with debt-funded LBOs.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 and Q2 2026 earnings: trajectory toward 2026 guide upper end
- Possible refreshed Clearlake bid or strategic review announcement
- Buyback execution (BLKB has been aggressive on repurchases)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Vertical SaaS, well-disclosed |
| Moat | 8 | Switching costs in donor data |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | Net debt manageable; strong FCF |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | ~14x EBITDA vs. 18-25x comps |
| Catalyst | 7 | Clearlake 20% + bid history |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Rule of 40 inflection |
| Business Quality | 8 | 36% EBITDA, recurring |
| Executability | 8 | $3B+ market cap |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | TAM, breach overhang |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Donation cyclicality |
| **TOTAL** | **70** | |

NXST.name: Nexstar Media Group, Inc.
NXST.content:
# 20. NXST — Nexstar Media Group, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Largest U.S. TV Station Owner | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
Nexstar owns/operates 200+ local TV stations covering ~70% of U.S. households — the largest broadcaster by reach — plus The CW Network and a 31% stake in TV Food Network. Trading at a single-digit free cash flow multiple in the trough of the political-ad / retransmission-renegotiation cycle, with FCC ownership-cap reform actively being discussed in Washington as a structural catalyst.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Nexstar is the consolidator-of-last-resort in U.S. local broadcast television. CEO Perry Sook built the company through serial acquisitions (Tribune in 2019, Media General before that), and the model is brutally simple: own as many Big-4 affiliated stations as possible, harvest dual revenue streams from political/local advertising and retransmission consent fees from cable/satellite/vMVPD distributors, and return ~100% of free cash flow to shareholders via buybacks and dividends. The company generates billions in free cash flow in even-year (election + Olympics) cycles and meaningfully less in odd years. Even-year FCF is highly visible and recurring.

The bear case is the secular cord-cutting story: traditional pay-TV subscribers continue to decline 6-8% annually, which compresses the retransmission-fee base over time. The CW Network acquisition has been a cash drain. Political ad spend is lumpy and reform efforts (small-donor caps, digital substitution) could erode the local-broadcast political moat. Streaming bypass means networks like NBC and CBS can theoretically go direct-to-consumer and disintermediate the affiliate. The recently completed FCC ownership-cap rules at 39% national reach are technically a binding constraint, though Nexstar has used UHF discounts and structural workarounds. Leverage is meaningful (~3x net debt/EBITDA on trailing eight-quarter average), and refinancing risk exists if rates stay elevated.

The bull case is that Nexstar is the cheapest distressed-mid-teens-FCF-yield asset in media-land, with an actively pro-deregulation FCC under the current administration likely to lift or modify the 39% cap — which would unlock immediate accretive M&A (Tegna, Sinclair, Scripps stations all in play). Sook has compounded book value/share at mid-teens for 20 years. The 2024 election cycle generated record political ad revenue, and 2026 midterms set up another. The 31% stake in Food Network (TV Food) is unconsolidated and worth several billion in a separation. Buybacks at current FCF yields are accretive double-digits.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Food Network stake (TV Food, LLC) is carried at a tiny GAAP basis on the balance sheet but sources estimate fair value at $1.5-2.5B based on Warner Bros. Discovery's implied multiple — that's $50-80/share of hidden NAV that does not show up in screen-level multiples. Also invisible: Nexstar's retransmission contracts are staggered with multi-year renewal cycles; the company has historically extracted 8-15% step-ups at each renewal because its station portfolio is must-carry for any vMVPD launching nationally (YouTube TV, Hulu Live, Fubo all need local Big-4 affiliates). The political-revenue cyclicality means screens computing TTM FCF in Q1 of an odd year systematically understate normalized cash generation by 25-40%.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **2026 midterms (Nov 2026)** — Political ad cycle peak; expected $500M+ incremental revenue.
2. **2026 FCC ownership-cap review** — Potential cap lift unlocks Tegna/Sinclair-style M&A.
3. **Food Network strategic review** — WBD has signaled openness to monetize Food/HGTV; Nexstar's 31% stake is a contingent catalyst.

| Management integrity | 8 |

NXST.analystSummary:
Nexstar is the consolidator-of-last-resort in U.S. local broadcast television. CEO Perry Sook built the company through serial acquisitions (Tribune in 2019, Media General before that), and the model is brutally simple: own as many Big-4 affiliated stations as possible, harvest dual revenue streams from political/local advertising and retransmission consent fees from cable/satellite/vMVPD distributors, and return ~100% of free cash flow to shareholders via buybacks and dividends. The company generates billions in free cash flow in even-year (election + Olympics) cycles and meaningfully less in odd years. Even-year FCF is highly visible and recurring.

The bear case is the secular cord-cutting story: traditional pay-TV subscribers continue to decline 6-8% annually, which compresses the retransmission-fee base over time. The CW Network acquisition has been a cash drain. Political ad spend is lumpy and reform efforts (small-donor caps, digital substitution) could erode the local-broadcast political moat. Streaming bypass means networks like NBC and CBS can theoretically go direct-to-consumer and disintermediate the affiliate. The recently completed FCC ownership-cap rules at 39% national reach are technically a binding constraint, though Nexstar has used UHF discounts and structural workarounds. Leverage is meaningful (~3x net debt/EBITDA on trailing eight-quarter average), and refinancing risk exists if rates stay elevated.

The bull case is that Nexstar is the cheapest distressed-mid-teens-FCF-yield asset in media-land, with an actively pro-deregulation FCC under the current administration likely to lift or modify the 39% cap — which would unlock immediate accretive M&A (Tegna, Sinclair, Scripps stations all in play). Sook has compounded book value/share at mid-teens for 20 years. The 2024 election cycle generated record political ad revenue, and 2026 midterms set up another. The 31% stake in Food Network (TV Food) is unconsolidated and worth several billion in a separation. Buybacks at current FCF yields are accretive double-digits.
NXST.whatScreenCantSee:
The Food Network stake (TV Food, LLC) is carried at a tiny GAAP basis on the balance sheet but sources estimate fair value at $1.5-2.5B based on Warner Bros. Discovery's implied multiple — that's $50-80/share of hidden NAV that does not show up in screen-level multiples. Also invisible: Nexstar's retransmission contracts are staggered with multi-year renewal cycles; the company has historically extracted 8-15% step-ups at each renewal because its station portfolio is must-carry for any vMVPD launching nationally (YouTube TV, Hulu Live, Fubo all need local Big-4 affiliates). The political-revenue cyclicality means screens computing TTM FCF in Q1 of an odd year systematically understate normalized cash generation by 25-40%.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **2026 midterms (Nov 2026)** — Political ad cycle peak; expected $500M+ incremental revenue.
2. **2026 FCC ownership-cap review** — Potential cap lift unlocks Tegna/Sinclair-style M&A.
3. **Food Network strategic review** — WBD has signaled openness to monetize Food/HGTV; Nexstar's 31% stake is a contingent catalyst.

NPK.name: National Presto Industries, Inc.
NPK.content:
# 21. NPK — National Presto Industries, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Defense Backlog + Net Cash | Score: 72/100

### THE SETUP
National Presto is a quirky, family-controlled (Cohen family) micro-conglomerate combining defense ammunition manufacturing (AMTEC subsidiary, principally 40mm grenade rounds for the U.S. Army) with a legacy Presto-branded housewares business and a small adult-incontinence products unit. It carries large net cash, pays a giant variable special dividend most years, has near-zero sell-side coverage, and trades like the housewares brand even though defense is the main earnings engine.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
NPK's defense segment manufactures medium-caliber ammunition under multi-year programs of record with the U.S. Department of Defense. Backlog disclosed in the 10-K has expanded materially in the post-Ukraine restocking cycle, and the company has been awarded follow-on 40mm contracts that extend visibility several years out. The housewares segment (pressure cookers, fryers, small appliances) is mature and seasonal but throws off cash; the absorbent products segment is small. Capital structure is pristine — essentially debt-free with a meaningful cash hoard relative to the ~$700M-class market cap, which funds the annual special dividend that has been the headline shareholder-return mechanism for decades.

The bear case centers on lumpy defense revenue, single-customer concentration (the Army), and the housewares segment's secular decline as Instant Pot/competitor brands have eroded share. Margins in defense are subject to government cost-plus and FFP pricing dynamics that compress when input costs spike. Family control (Cohen heirs and trust) means there will be no activist-driven balance-sheet optimization, no buybacks at scale, and no spin-off of the defense unit despite its arguably much higher standalone multiple. Disclosure quality is famously thin — the company files plain-vanilla 10-Ks with limited segment color and does not host earnings calls.

The bull case: defense backlog growth in a multi-year DoD restocking environment, plus 40mm being a NATO-standard round with allied export demand, supports a multi-year earnings ramp. The "look-through" sum-of-parts (defense at peer multiples + housewares at scrap + cash) implies fair value materially above the current quote. The annual special dividend creates a natural total-return floor, and the family's track record of returning cash (rather than empire-building) is investor-friendly even without buybacks. Catalyst: any large new ammunition award or a rare strategic action.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K segment footnote shows defense-segment operating margin expansion that screens miss because they aggregate it with declining housewares. The pension footnote discloses an overfunded pension that flatters reported equity. Critically, the company holds a long-running litigation/indemnification reserve related to environmental remediation at AMTEC sites (Janesville, WI legacy) — the contingencies note is small but recurring and worth tracking. Insider ownership concentration via the Cohen family trusts is structural; proxy filings show consistent voting control with no dissent. The variable special dividend is announced in the Q4/annual release and not screen-visible until declared.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. Annual special dividend declaration (typically Feb/Mar 2026 with the 10-K release).
2. New 40mm or medium-caliber DoD contract announcements (rolling through FY2026).
3. FY2025 10-K filing — backlog and defense-segment margin disclosure.

HQI.name: HireQuest, Inc.
HQI.content:
# 22. HQI — HireQuest, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Franchise Royalty Model + $20M Buyback | Score: 73/100

### THE SETUP
HireQuest is a 413-office staffing franchisor that booked $30.6M of royalty/license/interest revenue on $500M of system-wide franchisee sales in 2025 — a capital-light, high-margin franchise model wearing a low-multiple "staffing company" disguise. December 2025 brought a $20M buyback authorization plus the partial divestiture of executive-search brand MRINetwork, simplifying the story right as franchise temp-labor demand bottoms.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
HireQuest is the parent franchisor of HireQuest Direct (direct-dispatch industrial labor), Snelling, DriverQuest, and HireQuest Health staffing brands, plus historically MRINetwork executive search. The business model is materially different from operating staffing companies like Kelly Services or ManpowerGroup — HireQuest does not employ the temps; its 413 franchisee-owned offices employ ~75,000 temporary workers system-wide and remit royalties and license fees to HQI. That structure means HireQuest captures a low-double-digit percentage of system sales as recurring royalty revenue with corporate operating margins materially above operating-staffing peers. Capital intensity is near zero; the recurring revenue base scales with franchisee count and system sales rather than W-2 employee growth.

The bear case is cyclical and structural. Light-industrial temp-labor demand is sensitive to construction starts, manufacturing PMI, and warehouse activity — all of which softened through 2024-2025. Franchisees bear the front-line economic risk, but if franchisees fail or close, HQI's royalty stream contracts. The MRINetwork divestiture (January 1, 2026, contributing most non-contract staffing assets in exchange for 40% of MRI Operations) acknowledges that executive search franchising under the HQI umbrella underperformed and required restructuring. Acquisition history — Snelling, TEC Staffing, Ready Temporary Services — shows growth via tuck-in M&A; mis-priced deals or integration friction would compress returns. Float is small and liquidity is limited.

The bull case is the franchise economics finally being recognized. The $20M buyback (announced December 17, 2025) against a sub-$200M market cap is a large repurchase commitment and signals management confidence at depressed levels. As cyclical temp-labor demand recovers through 2026, royalty leverage (mostly fixed corporate cost base) drives operating-leverage EPS upside. Continued tuck-in acquisitions of small regional staffing brands at distressed multiples convert into franchised offices that compound the royalty base. The pure-franchisor multiple (think MTN, RCI Hospitality, Driven Brands) sits well above where HQI trades.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 2025 10-K reveals that revenue is "primarily royalties, license fees, and interest from franchisees" on system-wide sales of $500.2M — a structurally different financial profile from operating-staffing peers that screens lump it with. The MRI restructuring removes a low-margin, low-fit brand from the consolidated P&L while retaining 40% economics — accretive to corporate margins immediately. Workers' compensation reserves are a critical staffing-company tail liability; the 10-K's WC reserve commentary is the load-bearing footnote investors should pressure-test. The $20M buyback against a small float is a meaningful percentage of public float, mechanically supportive.

### CATALYSTS
- Q1 2026 earnings (first clean post-MRI quarter)
- Buyback program execution pace through 2026
- Franchisee count and system-sales metrics in next 10-K
- Possible additional tuck-in M&A announcements

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 8/10 |
| Management integrity | 7/10 |
| Capital allocation | 8/10 |
| Balance sheet | 7/10 |
| Moat durability | 7/10 |

| Insider alignment | 8/10 |

| Optionality | 7/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7/10 |
| **Total** | **73/100** |

UTMD.name: Utah Medical Products, Inc.
UTMD.content:
# 15. UTMD — Utah Medical Products, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER  |  Score: 77/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $3,000 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
A boring, micro-cap niche medical-device maker with 57%+ gross margins, $85.8M cash on a $223M market cap (38%), zero debt, no meaningful stock-based compensation, a CEO-Chairman who has run the place since 1992, and a buyback machine that retired ~4.5% of shares in 2025. Revenue and earnings dipped in 2025 — the screen entry point. Quality you can't usually buy at this multiple.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
UTMD makes specialty obstetric and gynecologic medical devices: intrauterine pressure catheters (IUPCs), the Filshie Clip female sterilization device, neonatal blood-pressure monitoring (Deltran), electrosurgical disposables, vacuum-assisted delivery systems, and obstetric vacuum cups. Manufacturing is in Utah, Ireland, England and Australia. Screen flagged it for $85.5M net cash (38% of mcap), TTM net income ~$11.3M, and profitability with low SBC. FY2025 revenue $38.5M (-5.8%), GAAP EPS $3.483 (-12.1%), gross margin maintained above 57%. In 2025 the company repurchased 148,935 shares for $8.36M (avg $56.10) — about 4.5% of shares — and paid $4.0M in dividends. The 2025 weakness was driven by a PendoTECH OEM customer slowdown and a Chinese distributor cancellation that produced a written-off receivable.

The bear case is concentration and growth. PendoTECH (a single OEM) and a now-burnt Chinese distributor relationship caused the 2025 revenue dip. A $38.5M-revenue medical device company has limited operating leverage, no high-growth product line, and faces gradual ASP/competitive erosion in legacy categories like Filshie Clip (which itself faced FDA-related labeling controversy historically). Kevin Cornwell is 80+ and has been CEO since 1992 — there is real key-person and succession risk; the 1998 change-of-control agreement is a tell. The float is tiny (~$220M mcap, low daily volume), making both entry and exit costly. International exposure (Ireland, UK, Australia, China) creates FX drag. There is no realistic 2x re-rating catalyst; this is a "compound at high single digits + buyback" story, not a multibagger.

The bull case: this is a textbook Munger company hiding in a screen. 57%+ gross margin specialty medical, family-controlled (Cornwell ~5.2%, ~190K shares ~$11M+ insider stake), zero debt, $85.8M cash growing despite buybacks, ~zero stock-based compensation (almost unheard of), and a 30+ year CEO who treats shareholders like partners. Strip the cash and you pay ~$137M for ~$11M of net income — ~12x earnings on a 57%-GM annuity that has paid uninterrupted dividends and bought back stock for years. The 2025 dip is cyclical (one OEM, one distributor) not structural — a normalization in 2026 plus the smaller share count creates EPS upside.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The screen sees "P/E ~20" and gets bored. The 10-K reveals one of the rarest combinations in micro-cap healthcare: a CEO who has been in the seat since 1992, owns ~5% of the float, takes a $152K base salary, no stock options awarded, and runs the cash like a private owner — buying back 4.5% of shares in a single year ($8.4M) at $56.10 average while the company simultaneously paid $4M in dividends and grew the cash balance. There is no SBC dilution silently eating returns. The filings also show that 2025 weakness is identifiable and idiosyncratic (one OEM + one distributor write-off), not a market-share story. Manufacturing across four countries (Utah, Ireland, UK, Australia) creates FX drag but also tariff insulation. The Filshie Clip and IUPC franchises throw off cash with minimal reinvestment.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- 2026 normalization off the PendoTECH/Chinese distributor trough (easy comps)
- Continued buyback at depressed prices (4.5% retired in 2025; pace likely continues)
- Special dividend or accelerated buyback if cash hits $90M+ on a $223M cap

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Niche OB/GYN devices, manufacturing-driven |
| Moat | 7 | FDA-cleared niche products, sticky OEM relationships |
| Balance Sheet | 10 | $85.8M cash, no debt, 38% of mcap |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | ~12x ex-cash earnings on a 57%-GM annuity |
| Catalyst | 6 | Easy 2026 comps + buyback compounding |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Hard cash floor + buyback shrinking float |
| Business Quality | 9 | 57%+ GM, no SBC, owner-operator, no debt |
| Executability | 5 | Tiny float; need limit orders |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | Worst case is dead money, not capital loss |
| Macro Independence | 9 | Birth rates / sterilization demand non-cyclical |
| **TOTAL** | **77** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $3,000 (of $100K)
Rationale: This is exactly the kind of boring, owner-operated, cash-rich micro-cap this manual was designed to surface. High conviction on quality and capital allocation; sized at the high-conviction tier despite small float.

---

# B. RECENT SPINOFFS (LAST 12-18 MONTHS)
## 15 stocks  |  ranked by recency + asymmetry

Stocks created by parent-company separations within the last 12-18 months. Forced selling by parent-company indexers, complexity discounts, and mismatched investor bases create the classic Greenblatt setup. Each entry details the spin mechanics and what's still mispriced.

---
UTMD.analystSummary:
UTMD makes specialty obstetric and gynecologic medical devices: intrauterine pressure catheters (IUPCs), the Filshie Clip female sterilization device, neonatal blood-pressure monitoring (Deltran), electrosurgical disposables, vacuum-assisted delivery systems, and obstetric vacuum cups. Manufacturing is in Utah, Ireland, England and Australia. Screen flagged it for $85.5M net cash (38% of mcap), TTM net income ~$11.3M, and profitability with low SBC. FY2025 revenue $38.5M (-5.8%), GAAP EPS $3.483 (-12.1%), gross margin maintained above 57%. In 2025 the company repurchased 148,935 shares for $8.36M (avg $56.10) — about 4.5% of shares — and paid $4.0M in dividends. The 2025 weakness was driven by a PendoTECH OEM customer slowdown and a Chinese distributor cancellation that produced a written-off receivable.

The bear case is concentration and growth. PendoTECH (a single OEM) and a now-burnt Chinese distributor relationship caused the 2025 revenue dip. A $38.5M-revenue medical device company has limited operating leverage, no high-growth product line, and faces gradual ASP/competitive erosion in legacy categories like Filshie Clip (which itself faced FDA-related labeling controversy historically). Kevin Cornwell is 80+ and has been CEO since 1992 — there is real key-person and succession risk; the 1998 change-of-control agreement is a tell. The float is tiny (~$220M mcap, low daily volume), making both entry and exit costly. International exposure (Ireland, UK, Australia, China) creates FX drag. There is no realistic 2x re-rating catalyst; this is a "compound at high single digits + buyback" story, not a multibagger.

The bull case: this is a textbook Munger company hiding in a screen. 57%+ gross margin specialty medical, family-controlled (Cornwell ~5.2%, ~190K shares ~$11M+ insider stake), zero debt, $85.8M cash growing despite buybacks, ~zero stock-based compensation (almost unheard of), and a 30+ year CEO who treats shareholders like partners. Strip the cash and you pay ~$137M for ~$11M of net income — ~12x earnings on a 57%-GM annuity that has paid uninterrupted dividends and bought back stock for years. The 2025 dip is cyclical (one OEM, one distributor) not structural — a normalization in 2026 plus the smaller share count creates EPS upside.
UTMD.whatScreenCantSee:
The screen sees "P/E ~20" and gets bored. The 10-K reveals one of the rarest combinations in micro-cap healthcare: a CEO who has been in the seat since 1992, owns ~5% of the float, takes a $152K base salary, no stock options awarded, and runs the cash like a private owner — buying back 4.5% of shares in a single year ($8.4M) at $56.10 average while the company simultaneously paid $4M in dividends and grew the cash balance. There is no SBC dilution silently eating returns. The filings also show that 2025 weakness is identifiable and idiosyncratic (one OEM + one distributor write-off), not a market-share story. Manufacturing across four countries (Utah, Ireland, UK, Australia) creates FX drag but also tariff insulation. The Filshie Clip and IUPC franchises throw off cash with minimal reinvestment.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- 2026 normalization off the PendoTECH/Chinese distributor trough (easy comps)
- Continued buyback at depressed prices (4.5% retired in 2025; pace likely continues)
- Special dividend or accelerated buyback if cash hits $90M+ on a $223M cap

IMPP.name: Imperial Petroleum Inc.
IMPP.content:
# 1. IMPP — Imperial Petroleum Inc.
## SUB-CASH OPERATING  |  Score: 67/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $3,000 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
A debt-free Greek tanker/drybulk owner trading at roughly book value of cash. Imperial Petroleum closed FY25 with $179.1M of cash plus time deposits, zero bank debt, and just declared a $10M open-market buyback in February 2026 — all on a market cap that has at times sat *below* the cash line. The catalyst stack: a 26-vessel fleet on the water by year-end 2026, no covenants to choke capital returns, and a controlling shareholder who has finally pivoted from dilution to repurchase.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Imperial Petroleum (NASDAQ: IMPP), an Athens-based ship-owning company controlled by CEO Harry Vafias, transports petroleum products, crude oil and dry bulk cargo. The fleet stood at 19 vessels by year-end 2025 (seven MR product tankers, two Suezmax tankers, and ten drybulk carriers) with one Post Panamax delivery in January 2026 lifting the count to 20 — and a contracted pipeline to reach 26 vessels by year-end. FY25 produced revenue of $161.0M (up 9.2% YoY), net income of $50.0M, EBITDA of $71.0M and operating cash flow of $80.8M. The screen flags net cash > market cap (~$179M vs ~$158M, ratio 1.13x). At a P/E of ~3.4x reported earnings and ~3.0x adjusted, IMPP is among the cheapest profitable cash-rich names in the public market — even after a brutal 2024–25 share dilution that took weighted-average count from 30M to 38M.

The bear case is real and well-documented in the 6-K. Tanker rates are violently cyclical and "ongoing attacks by Houthis in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden" remain a live risk explicitly named by management. Vafias-affiliated entities provide the company's commercial management, vessel management and ship brokering — and the press release shows $5.9M of related-party voyage/operating/management fees in FY25, with another related-party "Investment in related party" of $13.0M sitting on the balance sheet. The float is dual-class with Series B preferred granting outsized control to Vafias; minority holders have been diluted repeatedly via warrants, ATM offerings and a $60M registered direct in 2025. The drybulk pivot also added depreciation, lifting D&A from $17.0M to $25.9M and pulling reported net income flat YoY despite the revenue growth.

The bull case rests on three pillars. First: $179M cash + zero debt = downside protection that is genuinely hard to vaporize even in a tanker downturn. Second: management explicitly disclosed *current* cash of "about $198 million" as of the March 6, 2026 press release — i.e., cash is *building*, not bleeding. Third: capital allocation finally turned shareholder-friendly. The $10M buyback (251,625 shares already repurchased for $0.9M as of disclosure) plus declared 8.75% Series A preferred dividends signal that further common dividends or buybacks are plausible if the share price keeps trading at a discount to NAV. Six more vessel deliveries in 2026 = scaled earnings power into a tanker market that, per the CEO, was "firm particularly in the second half of 2025."

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The "cash" line is now mostly time deposits ($173.3M) — only $5.8M is actual cash and equivalents — because management has been laddering proceeds to harvest interest income ($7.3M in FY25). That's positive for return-on-cash but means the company is one bad rate cycle from giving back its non-operating earnings buffer. Second hidden item: the $13.0M "Investment in related party" generates a $760K dividend stream and is *not* truly liquid. Third — and the genuine alpha for filing-readers — Vafias's earlier vessel acquisitions used *seller financing* booked at imputed-interest discounts, which is why FY25 has a $2.3M "Interest expense – related party" line despite zero bank debt. All of this was repaid in 2025; going forward, the income statement should look cleaner. Finally, the buyback authorization is small ($10M) relative to the cash pile — it is a price-support gesture, not a recapitalization.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Six contracted vessel deliveries through 2026 lifting earnings power on the existing fleet utilization base of ~91.8% in Q4 25.
- Continued execution of the $10M repurchase program (only $0.9M used as of March 6, 2026 disclosure).
- Q1 2026 6-K — first quarter as a fully-debt-free 20+ vessel operator; tanker rates and any incremental dividend declaration the key items.
- Geopolitical wild card: any escalation/de-escalation in Red Sea routing impacts ton-mile demand directly.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Plain-vanilla shipping, audited fleet table, no SaaS hand-waving |
| Moat | 3 | None — shipping is commoditized, scale advantage modest |
| Balance Sheet | 10 | $179M cash, $0 debt, $198M cash as of March 6, 2026 disclosure |
| Margin of Safety | 9 | ~3.4x P/E, market cap ~ cash, vessels are essentially "free" |
| Catalyst | 7 | Buyback live, fleet additions, possible common dividend |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Downside cushioned by cash; upside if cycle holds |
| Business Quality | 5 | EBITDA margin ~44% but cyclical, ROE ~10% on inflated equity |
| Executability | 8 | ~$30M ADV, easy in/out at $2-5K |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Vafias dilution history; controlled-co minority abuse risk |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Tanker rates + Suez routing = macro-sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **67** | Cash-rich cyclical with newly aligned capital allocation |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $3,000 (of $100K)
Rationale: Liquid float, balance-sheet downside protection, and a sub-NAV entry justify above-average sizing; size capped because the controlled-company structure means minority holders depend on Vafias' goodwill, and dilution history is a real overhang.

---

CAAS.name: China Automotive Systems, Inc.
CAAS.content:
# 12. CAAS — China Automotive Systems, Inc.
## SUB-CASH OPERATING  |  Score: 72/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $2,500 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
A profitable, growing Chinese auto-parts maker trading at a P/E of ~3.9 with 45% of its market cap in net cash, on track to a record $730M revenue year — yet the market values the entire equity at $129M because "China + auto + small cap" is three strikes. Q3 2025 EPS grew 77.8% YoY. The company already paid a $0.80 special dividend in 2024, proving the cash is real and repatriable.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
CAAS is a Hubei-based supplier of power steering systems (hydraulic, electric, and the hybrid "iRCB" intelligent electro-hydraulic circulating ball steering for heavy vehicles) operating through eight Sino-foreign joint ventures. Customers include FAW, Dongfeng, BYD, Beiqi Foton, Chery, Stellantis and Ford. Screen flagged it for net cash of ~$58M against a $129M market cap (45%), TTM net income of $33.5M and a P/E under 4. Q3 2025 net sales were $193.2M (+17.7% YoY); nine-month sales hit $536.5M (+16.1%) and management raised full-year 2025 guidance to $730M. Cash and short-term investments were $167.3M (~$5.54/share) at quarter-end. North America +77.3%, Brazil +30.5%. By April 22, 2026 the company files audited 2025 numbers.

The bear case is the China discount, which exists for reasons: VIE-adjacent corporate structure (Sino-foreign JVs with minority partners on the operating subsidiaries); RMB-to-USD repatriation risk via PRC capital controls; opaque governance for US holders; auditing/PCAOB overhang on US-listed China names; customer concentration in a viciously competitive Chinese auto market where local OEMs compete on price; EV transition risk to legacy hydraulic steering content; and geopolitical risk of US-China tariffs/delistings. Sales to Chery dropped in 2025 even as the rest of the book grew. The dividend was a one-off "special," not recurring. A $25M dividend is a small thank-you on a $58M net cash hoard sitting in PRC subsidiaries.

The bull case: at ~3.9x earnings, the equity is essentially priced to liquidate. Strip the cash and the operating business trades at ~2x earnings on a growing book. Management is executing the EV transition in real time — winning a first R-EPS (rack-assist electric power steering) order from a major European OEM with annual sales expected to exceed $100M starting 2027, and bringing a 20 N·m eRCB intelligent steering motor to mass production in mid-December 2025. Brazil and North America growth diversifies away from China. The 2024 special dividend proved cash can leave China. Catalyst: full-year 2025 audited results April 22, 2026 — a clean print + repeat special dividend would force a re-rating.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The screen sees "China auto P/E 4" and reflexively assumes value trap. The filings show something more interesting: this isn't a melting hydraulic steering company; it's actively winning EV-platform business at premium European OEMs (R-EPS deal, Stellantis-driven Brazil growth) and just raised guidance — most "value trap" autos are guiding down. The eight-JV structure also matters: it means CAAS doesn't 100% own the operating cash, but minority partners are mostly Chinese SOEs with aligned interests, and the parent has an established mechanism (the 2024 special dividend) to upstream capital. The 77.8% Q3 EPS growth is being ignored because investors stopped reading Chinese small caps in 2021.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- April 22, 2026 audited FY2025 results — guidance was raised, expect a clean print
- Repeat special cash dividend (precedent: $0.80 in 2024 = $25M)
- Mass production ramp of 20 N·m eRCB and first R-EPS shipments to European OEM

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Steering systems — straightforward, JV structure adds complexity |
| Moat | 5 | Commodity-ish auto parts; some EV product differentiation |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | $167M cash & ST investments vs. $129M mcap; no meaningful debt |
| Margin of Safety | 10 | ~3.9x P/E, ~2x ex-cash; deep discount |
| Catalyst | 7 | April 2026 audited results + potential dividend |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Trough multiple + growth = optionality |
| Business Quality | 6 | 17% gross margin, EPS growing 77% in Q3 |
| Executability | 6 | Small float, China-stock liquidity constraints |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Delisting / capital control / VIE-style risks are real |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Tied to China auto cycle and US-China relations |
| **TOTAL** | **72** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $2,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Statistically cheap with hard cash and a real catalyst (April 2026 audited print), but China-listing risk and JV structure cap conviction. Above-average size for the optionality, below high-conviction tier for the geopolitical tail.

---

DLNG.name: Dynagas LNG Partners LP
DLNG.content:
# 3. DLNG — Dynagas LNG Partners LP
## BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 60/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Dynagas LNG Partners is a Marshall Islands MLP that owns six ice-class LNG carriers, all on multi-year time charters to investment-grade-tier counterparties. Per the FY2025 20-F (filed April 2026), 2025 net income was $61.6 million on adjusted EBITDA of $109.2 million; charters are with SEFE Marketing & Trading (the German entity formerly Gazprom Marketing & Trading), Equinor, Yamal Trade, and Rio Grande LNG. The Arctic Aurora Equinor charter expires September 2026, with a follow-on seven-year charter already contracted. Three vessels serve the Yamal LNG project on long-tenor charters extending into 2028. Cash distributions resumed at modest levels — common $0.05/quarter (paid Feb 2026) and the 42nd sequential Series A 8.75% preferred distribution of $0.5625/unit. With a $145M equity cap against book value of roughly $400M+ and a young, fully contracted fleet, the partnership trades at 0.36x book and roughly 2.9x earnings.

Why is it cheap? Three structural overhangs. First, charterer concentration: SEFE/Yamal exposure means a Russian-related-flag risk premium even though SEFE itself was nationalized by Germany and Yamal cargoes flow legally. Second, MLP structure with a controlling general partner held by the Prokopiou family ("Sponsor") — minority unitholders have no governance levers, the GP receives incentive distribution rights, and the Series A preferreds sit ahead of common in the cash waterfall. Third, refinancing/maturity risk: the senior credit facility was extensively refinanced in 2025 (interest expense recognized on a sponsor facility from June 27 to July 25, 2025), and unitholders bear the credit-spread mark on the post-refi term loan. Fourth-tier worry: U.S.-China port-fee retaliation suspended through November 2026 but unresolved, potentially affecting LNG carrier port economics.

The bull case is "fully contracted asset at half book." All six vessels carry forward charter cover into 2026–2028, generating predictable cash. The newly extended $10M common buyback (through November 2026) signals capital-return intent — 148,933 units repurchased in Q4 2025 alone — and the common distribution though tiny is sequentially restored. LNG shipping rates remain structurally supported by the global LNG export build-out (Plaquemines, Rio Grande, Corpus Christi Stage III all coming online). Ice-class capability is a moat for Arctic loading windows. To close the gap an investor needs (a) the Arctic Aurora to roll cleanly to its contracted Equinor seven-year follow-on, (b) no defaults or sanctions hit on Yamal-trade counterparties, and (c) common distribution to keep stepping back up. Even at current $0.20 annualized common distribution the yield exceeds 5%, with significant room to grow as the term loan amortizes.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 20-F discloses that the partnership relied on a Sponsor (Prokopiou-family) bridge facility between June 27 and July 25, 2025, recognizing $902.3 thousand of interest expense to the related party during a 28-day window — a clear sign that the senior debt refinancing executed in mid-2025 had a tight closing. That kind of related-party bridge is not a long-term financing; it tells you the public capital markets remained shut to DLNG until the refi closed and that the GP family is the lender of last resort. Two read-throughs: (1) the GP has every incentive to keep the partnership solvent and pay the preferreds because it's also a creditor, but (2) the same family controls the GP, the manager, and now apparently the bridge financing — minority unitholders are riding entirely on Prokopiou-family alignment, which has been historically conservative on common distributions but has at least stopped issuing equity at the bottom.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Six vessels, six charters, simple to model. |
| Moat | 6 | Ice-class is a real technical moat; only ~30 ice-class LNGCs in the world. |
| Balance Sheet | 5 | Newly refinanced term loan, $61.6M net income covers debt service comfortably. |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | 0.36x book on a fully contracted fleet with ~2.9x earnings multiple. |
| Catalyst | 6 | Buyback extended to Nov 2026; Arctic Aurora rollover Sep 2026; common distribution rising. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Asymmetric — preferreds anchor fair value; common is the option. |
| Business Quality | 6 | Long contracts, IG-tier counterparties, but commoditized post-charter. |
| Executability | 5 | Thin float, MLP K-1 deters U.S. retail; institutional adoption muted. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Yamal sanctions step-up could strand 3 of 6 vessels; refinancing-risk concentration. |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Charter cover insulates near-term but residual values ride on global LNG. |
| **TOTAL** | **60** | |

---

DLNG.whatScreenCantSee: The 20-F discloses that the partnership relied on a Sponsor (Prokopiou-family) bridge facility between June 27 and July 25, 2025, recognizing $902.3 thousand of interest expense to the related party during a 28-day window — a clear sign that the senior debt refinancing executed in mid-2025 had a tight closing. That kind of related-party bridge is not a long-term financing; it tells you the public capital markets remained shut to DLNG until the refi closed and that the GP family is the lender of last resort. Two read-throughs: (1) the GP has every incentive to keep the partnership solvent and pay the preferreds because it's also a creditor, but (2) the same family controls the GP, the manager, and now apparently the bridge financing — minority unitholders are riding entirely on Prokopiou-family alignment, which has been historically conservative on common distributions but has at least stopped issuing equity at the bottom.

|-----------|-------|------|

| Moat | 6 | Ice-class is a real technical moat; only ~30 ice-class LNGCs in the world. |
| Balance Sheet | 5 | Newly refinanced term loan, $61.6M net income covers debt service comfortably. |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | 0.36x book on a fully contracted fleet with ~2.9x earnings multiple. |

ACCO.name: ACCO Brands Corporation
ACCO.content:
# 4. ACCO — ACCO Brands Corporation
## VALUE TRAP RISK | Score: 51/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

ACCO Brands is a portfolio of legacy office-products and consumer-brand businesses — Five Star, Mead, Swingline, Quartet, GBC, Kensington, Leitz, Marbig, NOBO, Rapid, Rexel, Tilibra, plus the higher-growth PowerA gaming-accessories label. Per the FY2025 10-K (filed March 2026), the company reported net sales of $1,524.7 million (down 8.5% from $1,666.2M in 2024), a 32.8% gross margin, and net income of $41.3 million ($0.44/share) — a swing back to profit after the prior year's $101.6M net loss that was driven by goodwill impairment charges. Two reportable segments: ACCO Brands Americas (~59% of sales) and ACCO Brands International (~41%). Operating cash flow was $68.7M in 2025 (down from $148.2M in 2024 on working-capital timing). At a $299M cap and 0.44x book, the screen flags it as cheap; FY2026 guidance calls for sales flat to +3% with adjusted EPS of $0.84–$0.89 and adjusted FCF of $75–$85M.

Why is it cheap? The market is pricing secular decline in office and education products. Top-customer concentration with Amazon, Walmart, and the big-box channel creates margin pressure as private-label and direct-import alternatives proliferate. Total debt of $840.9M against trailing EBITDA leaves consolidated leverage at ~4.1x at year-end 2025 — well above the 3.0x range investors typically want for this kind of mature business, with a target of 3.7x–3.9x by year-end 2026. Brazilian operations (Tilibra) carry a long-running tax assessment ($3.0M near-term plus ongoing dispute). Management has just consummated the EPOS acquisition, which adds another integration burden ($7M of restructuring planned in 2026 for $15M targeted synergies). And the Americas reporting unit took a goodwill impairment in Q4 2023 — a reminder that the carrying value of these legacy brands is fragile.

The bull case is "self-help compounder at a low multiple." Management has delivered $35M of cost savings in 2025 and is on track for $100M cumulative by year-end 2026. Capital allocation is rational: prioritize debt paydown, sustain a covered quarterly dividend, opportunistic M&A. No debt maturities until 2029. The $467.5M revolving credit facility ($164.6M drawn) plus ~$292M available capacity gives ample liquidity. FCF guide of $75–$85M against $299M cap is roughly 25%+ FCF yield. PowerA gaming accessories provide a credible growth offset to declining school-and-office staples. To close the gap an investor needs (a) leverage to drift below 3.7x without forcing a dividend cut, (b) PowerA mix to keep growing through 2026 console refresh cycles, and (c) the EPOS integration to deliver the targeted synergies on schedule. A re-rating to 9–10x EPS would be a double.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K discloses Approximately 200 U.S. manufacturing/distribution employees covered by collective bargaining agreements, plus government-mandated collective bargaining in multiple international jurisdictions — a meaningful operational drag in any cost-rationalization program. More importantly, the supplemental disclosures show $96.7M of operating-lease obligations and $116.9M of purchase obligations in addition to the $840.9M of nominal debt, bringing total contractual obligations to roughly $1,268M. Against shareholders' equity in the $675M zone (implied by a 0.44x P/B at ~$299M cap), the off-balance-sheet items add another ~30% to the effective leverage that does not appear in the simple debt/equity ratio screens use. The restructuring-charge line has been a recurring annual item, not a one-off — readers who normalize earnings by adding back restructuring are likely overstating run-rate profitability by $20–30M per year.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Mature consumer brands portfolio, transparent segments. |
| Moat | 5 | Brand strength in slowly-shrinking categories; gaming accessories have switching costs. |
| Balance Sheet | 4 | $840.9M total debt, 4.1x leverage — workable but tight; no maturities until 2029. |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | 0.44x book and ~25% FCF yield on guidance midpoint. |
| Catalyst | 6 | $100M cost program by YE26; deleveraging glidepath; EPOS synergies. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Upside if deleveraging works; downside if office products decline accelerates. |
| Business Quality | 4 | Mid-single-digit operating margins on declining top line; 32.8% gross margin OK. |
| Executability | 7 | Liquid float, NYSE listing, no controlling shareholder. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Recurring restructuring charges and goodwill impairment risk; debt covenants tighten if EBITDA dips. |
| Macro Independence | 1 | Direct retail-cycle, school-spend, and back-to-office exposure. |
| **TOTAL** | **51** | |

AMPY.name: Amplify Energy Corp.
AMPY.content:
# 5. AMPY — Amplify Energy Corp.
## CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 56/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Amplify Energy is a small upstream E&P that, after a sweeping 2025 portfolio cleanup, owns just two core asset packages: the Bairoil CO2-flood waterflood in Wyoming and the Beta field in federal waters offshore Southern California (the asset behind the 2021 San Pedro Bay pipeline incident). Per the FY2025 10-K (filed March 2026), the company divested its non-operated Eagle Ford position (July 2025), East Texas/North Louisiana assets (December 2025), and Oklahoma assets (December 2025). Total revenues fell to $263.4M (from $294.7M) reflecting both lower volumes and a $42.5M impairment charge. Net income was reported but cash flow was the headline: divestiture proceeds of roughly $250M were used to retire all credit-facility borrowings, leaving the company with no debt and approximately $61M of cash at year-end 2025. PV-10 of remaining proved reserves was $376.4M; standardized measure $335.1M. At a $227M cap that's a 0.49x P/B and 5.3x earnings on a now-debt-free, simplified portfolio.

Why is it cheap? Beta is the controversial asset. The 2021 oil spill from the San Pedro Bay pipeline (operated by Amplify subsidiary San Pedro Bay Pipeline Company, ~17.5 miles) created multi-year litigation, ongoing regulatory scrutiny from BSEE, and reputational baggage that depresses the multiple. A contemplated January 2025 merger with Juniper Capital portfolio companies (NPOG and COG, which would have added Rockies acreage) was terminated mid-2025 — leaving the company without the diversified scale management originally pursued. Bairoil is mature with declining production. Capital intensity is meaningful — 2026 guidance is $45–$65M of capex, more than 95% concentrated at Beta on 5–8 D-Sand wells, against guided adjusted EBITDA of only $20–$45M. Executive transition: former CEO Willsher transitioned to "Special Advisor" effective July 22, 2025.

The bull case is "debt-free single-asset levered call on Beta drilling success." With zero debt and $61M cash on a $227M cap, downside is genuinely cushioned. The remaining proved reserves' standardized measure of $335M plus cash exceeds market cap before crediting Beta drilling upside. Beta's D-Sand interval is highly economic at strip prices and the 2026 program is the first real capital cycle since the 2021 incident. Bairoil offers optionality on CCUS / Section 45Q monetization — one of the few existing CO2-flood operators well-positioned for tax-credit-backed expansion. To close the gap an investor needs (a) the 2026 Beta D-Sand wells to deliver type-curve results, (b) no new Beta operational incidents, and (c) Section 45Q clarity to monetize Bairoil CO2 infrastructure. The terminated merger is in the rearview; capital allocation has visibly turned conservative and asset-focused.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Beta pipeline incident liability machinery still runs in the background even though the cash settlements have largely been booked. The 10-K discloses the BetaPipelineIncident as a tagged accounting line — meaning ongoing accruals, indemnity coverage analysis, and regulatory-monitoring costs are still flowing through G&A. More substantively: 65% of total proved reserves are concentrated in (presumably) Beta — a level of single-asset concentration that's unusual for a small-cap E&P and is the direct consequence of the 2025 divestiture program. Translation: a single regulatory event at Beta — a permit revocation, a BSEE production curtailment, a fresh leak — would disproportionately impair the entire reserve report. Counterbalancing this, the divestitures eliminated the corresponding G&A and lease-operating overhead, so the residual cost structure is much more variable than legacy Amplify. The G&A line did spike to $52.1M in 2025 (from $35.9M in 2024), reflecting transaction costs, executive separation, and merger-termination expenses — most of which should not recur in 2026.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Two asset packages, fully disclosed reserve report — easy to model. |
| Moat | 3 | Beta has barrier-to-entry from offshore CA permitting, but no commercial moat. |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | Zero debt, $61M cash post-divestitures — strongest balance sheet in company history. |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | PV-10 $376M + cash exceeds $227M market cap before crediting Beta drilling. |
| Catalyst | 7 | 2026 Beta D-Sand drilling program (5–8 wells); CCUS optionality at Bairoil. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Asymmetric to Beta well results; downside cushioned by net cash. |
| Business Quality | 5 | Mature E&P, mediocre realizations, but zero leverage rare in the space. |
| Executability | 6 | Reasonable float and trading liquidity; no controlling shareholder. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Single-asset concentration risk at Beta; another incident could be terminal. |
| Macro Independence | 2 | Pure WTI/HH price taker. |
| **TOTAL** | **56** | |

---

DSX.name: Diana Shipping Inc.
DSX.content:
# 6. DSX — Diana Shipping Inc.
## CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 49/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Diana Shipping is a Marshall Islands-domiciled, Greek-managed dry bulk owner that operates 36 vessels in service plus two Kamsarmax newbuilds for delivery in 2027 and 2028, with combined deadweight capacity around 4.1 million tons and a weighted-average fleet age of 12.3 years. The fleet spans Ultramax through Newcastlemax classes and is chartered out on staggered time charters to counterparties including Cargill, Stone Shipping, NYK and others. FY2025 time-charter revenues were $213.5M, down from $228.2M in 2024 and $262.1M in 2023 as average TCE slipped to $15,454/day. Net income was $17.8M and net income to common $12.1M after $5.8M of Series B preferred dividends. The company trades around 0.55x book on a $309M market cap with $502M of common equity, fitting the textbook below-book shipping profile.

The bear case is the dry bulk cycle. Substantially all revenue comes from one charter market and the 10-K candidly flags "cyclicality of the dry bulk shipping industry" and "volatility in charter hire rates" as the primary structural risk. Costs are a problem too — interest expense and finance costs were $43.0M in 2025 against $42.2M of operating income, meaning the equity earns very little once leverage is paid. Around 30% of operating expenses and half of G&A are denominated in Euros while revenue is in dollars, so a weaker dollar squeezes the cost base. The 8.875% Series B Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred sits ahead of common holders and will keep absorbing $5.8M annually regardless of common dividends, which were token at $0.04 per common share in 2025.

The bull case is a Capesize-led tightening into 2026–2027 paired with disciplined capital return. Recent disclosures show DSX has fixed a Capesize at $21,650/day for March 2026–April 2027 and locked in roughly 50% of 2026 revenue days at attractive rates, with implied $17,102/day on unfixed days using FFA references. The early-2025 $23M tender at $2.00/share retired 11.4M shares, the $100M repurchase authorization remains unused, and management put $0.04/share through the year while the Series B yields 8.875%. With NAV well above market, every up-cycle vessel sale (gain on sale of vessels was $3.7M in 2025, $5.8M in 2024) directly proves the discount.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Two structural items dilute the apparent earnings power. First, DSX is a Marshall Islands "C-corp" that pays out via 1099-DIV-equivalent foreign distributions through the Diana Wilhelmsen Management 50/50 JV with Wilhelmsen Ship Management — equity-method losses from JVs took $2.8M off net income in 2025 and the Bergen and other JV/newbuild capex commitments will keep pressuring cash conversion. Second, FY2025 net income was flattered by $14.7M of mark-to-market gains on equity securities (related to a position acquired during the year that drove a $118.2M working-capital increase from "investments in equity securities") — back that out and core operating earnings to common were thin. Investors taking the headline P/E of 22.7 should know it embeds the equity-securities gain.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Single-segment dry bulk owner; chartering structure is transparent. |
| Moat | 3 | Commodity shipping; no pricing power outside scale and counterparty relationships. |
| Balance Sheet | 5 | $1.17B assets, $502M common equity, ~$155M working capital after long-term debt and finance liabilities. |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | 0.55x P/B on tangible vessels; P/E 22.7 inflated by securities mark. |
| Catalyst | 6 | Capesize fixtures rolling onto higher rates; $100M buyback authorization untouched. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Series B preferred and high interest expense cap upside leverage. |
| Business Quality | 4 | TCE $15,454/day versus $43M interest expense leaves thin per-vessel margin. |
| Executability | 7 | $309M cap, listed common, listed preferred, listed warrants — liquid enough. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Charter rates roll over and Series B cumulates while common gets $0.04. |
| Macro Independence | 2 | Pure proxy for Chinese steel demand and BDI. |
| **TOTAL** | **49** | |

---

SEVN.name: Seven Hills Realty Trust
SEVN.content:
# 7. SEVN — Seven Hills Realty Trust
## BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 53/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Seven Hills Realty Trust is an externally managed commercial-mortgage REIT whose entire portfolio is 24 floating-rate first-mortgage loans on transitional middle-market CRE properties, with aggregate loan commitments of $724.5M, a weighted-average all-in yield of 7.92% and weighted-average maximum maturity of 2.6 years. As of December 31, 2025 total assets were $820.9M, loans held for investment net of $8.8M CECL reserve were $676.9M, secured financing facilities totaled $487.7M and shareholders' equity was $328.7M against 22.6M shares outstanding (book value ~$14.55). FY2025 net income was $15.4M ($1.00 per share) on $26.9M of net interest income from loans plus $2.5M of REO income. The REIT distributed $1.26 per share in 2025 and recently set the Q1 2026 dividend at $0.28 ($1.12 annualized), a 22% step down following the December 2025 rights offering.

The bear case is in the watch list. Of 24 loans, four (17% of portfolio amortized cost, $115.5M) are risk-rated 4 — meaning higher than expected risk of loss — and three of those four are Office collateral in Dallas, Plano and Carlsbad with 72%–78% LTVs and 2026 maturities. Office is also the second-largest property type at 19% of the book. The CRE bridge lending book is by definition a "transitional CRE" book, which the 10-K defines as properties undergoing redevelopment that may or may not stabilize on schedule. Fee leakage to RMR/Tremont (base management $4.4M plus incentive $0.6M plus reimbursed shared services $2.0M = $7.0M, or ~24% of total revenue) is a permanent drag relative to internally managed peers.

The bull case is the recapitalized balance sheet at a discount to book. The fully-backstopped December 2025 rights offering raised $61.4M in net proceeds at $8.65, lifted share count from 14.9M to 22.6M, and equipped Tremont with capacity to deploy more than $200M of new loans into a market where competitors are wounded. Cash on hand jumped from $70.8M to $123.5M. Adam Portnoy/ABP Trust and Tremont participated in the offering, signaling sponsor alignment. With shares trading around $8.40 versus book of $14.55, you pay 0.58x book for a portfolio yielding nearly 8% with $36.9M of unfunded commitments to redeploy as rates and credit spreads grind.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
SEVN is small and concentrated to a degree the screen does not display: 24 loans means a single risk-4 office defaulting at, say, 25% loss-given-default would consume roughly two years of net income at a stroke. The Dallas Office loan ($46.8M / $44.2M funded, 72% LTV, August 2026 maturity) alone is 6.5% of the portfolio. The CECL reserve at $8.8M (1.28% of loans) is thinner than peers carrying meaningful office exposure. The external manager structure also has a real-money cost: the November 2025 RMR Group transaction shows the REIT acquired loans from an affiliated transaction, demonstrating the related-party flow of paper between SEVN and the manager. That dependency has historically helped origination volume but is a governance item the screen would never flag.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Pure-play floating-rate first mortgage CRE bridge lender — easy mental model. |
| Moat | 3 | None; competes on origination relationships through Tremont/RMR. |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | $820.9M assets, $328.7M equity, $487.7M secured financing, $123.5M cash post-rights. |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | 0.58x P/B against a marked-to-market loan book. |
| Catalyst | 6 | $200M+ deployable capacity post-offering at peak credit-spread environment. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Office watch-list could erase a year of EPS; upside capped by REIT payout. |
| Business Quality | 4 | External manager fees take ~24% of revenue; ROE ~5%. |
| Executability | 6 | $190M cap, daily liquidity adequate but thin. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Two of the four office watch-list loans hit nonperforming; book mark hits NAV. |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Sensitive to short rates (floating-rate spread) and CRE credit conditions. |
| **TOTAL** | **53** | |
