SNFCA.name: Security National Financial Corporation
SNFCA.content:
# 8. SNFCA — Security National Financial Corporation
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Score: 64/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Security National Financial is a Utah-based three-segment conglomerate: a life insurance and annuity carrier, a residential mortgage originator (SecurityNational Mortgage, 85 retail offices in 25 states), and an irreplaceable cemetery and mortuary footprint of 11 mortuaries plus 5 cemeteries in Utah, 1 cemetery in California, and 1 cemetery and 4 mortuaries in New Mexico. FY2025 consolidated revenues were $344.6M with net earnings of $32.2M. By segment: Life Insurance generated $29.4M of segment net earnings on $208.3M of revenue, the Cemetery/Mortuary segment generated $6.6M on $33.7M, and the Mortgage segment lost $3.9M on $110.4M of revenue. Total assets were $1.56B and segment assets show the life insurance business carries $1.41B. Trading at 0.58x book and 7.7x earnings on a $251M market cap, the screen identifies this as a profitable Berkshire-mini selling for a fraction of replacement.

The bear case begins with the mortgage segment, which lost money in 2025 and remains structurally exposed to the residential origination cycle, with cost-to-fund mortgage loans of $6.6M and commissions of $42.4M against thin secondary-market gains. The life insurance book is dominated by acquired blocks of business stretching back to acquisitions like Capital Investors Life (1994), Civil Service Employees Life (1999), Acadian Life (2002), and on through Kilpatrick Life and others — these are run-off books with concentrated state-regulator and reserve-adequacy risk. Insurance-segment investments include exposure to commercial mortgage portfolios across LTV and DSCR buckets, and the holding company carries cemetery perpetual care obligations that must be funded from restricted assets. The dual-class share structure (Class A and Class C) and stock-dividend-only payout policy mean common holders never receive cash.

The bull case is the cemetery moat plus optionality on the other two segments. Cemetery and mortuary revenue of $33.7M and segment earnings of $6.6M imply ~20% segment margin — nothing else in the portfolio comes close. Cemeteries are a finite-supply asset with multi-decade demand visibility and pre-need contract economics that compound. Strip out the mortgage drag and the steady-state earnings power of life plus cemetery is roughly $36M, or ~7x adjusted earnings. Capital allocation has been disciplined: management runs an annual stock dividend (5% scrip) rather than cash, builds book value, and the Bradley/insider ownership (Charles E. Bradley Jr. as CEO context applies to CPSS, but SNFCA insider ownership at the Quist/Pinegar level is similarly aligned). At 0.58x book the market is pricing the mortgage segment at zero or worse, with the cemetery moat thrown in for free.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The cemetery segment is the asymmetric kicker no screener captures. Inventory disclosure shows specific developed-versus-undeveloped acreage at named properties — Memorial Estates' Lakeview, Mountain View and Redwood cemeteries in the Salt Lake corridor, Holladay Memorial Park, Deseret Memorial's Lake Hills, plus the New Mexico locations — and pre-need cemetery and funeral receivables are funded into trust through the insurance investment portfolio. The "Cemetery Perpetual Care Obligation" is a regulated trust asset bucket that backs the perpetual maintenance of these grounds and is invested in Treasuries and state/political subdivision securities; this is real, segregated, hard-asset value that does not appear in any traditional valuation framework. Together these holdings represent decades of pre-sold revenue against a single-digit-percent-margin headline business — a free option the market gives away at this multiple.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Three very different businesses bolted together; non-trivial to model. |
| Moat | 8 | Cemetery land bank in Utah is irreplaceable; insurance run-off books also defensible. |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | $1.56B assets, ample insurance reserves, perpetual care trust segregated. |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | 0.58x book and 7.7x earnings with one segment loss-making. |
| Catalyst | 5 | Mortgage segment turnaround or sale would re-rate the multiple. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Downside cushioned by trust-protected cemetery NAV; upside if mortgage breakeven. |
| Business Quality | 6 | Blended ROE ~7%, but cemetery margins ~20% drag the average up. |
| Executability | 5 | $251M cap, dual-class with thin C share float; A shares trade. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Even with mortgage running deeper losses, life and cemetery cover dividends and grow book. |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Mortuary demand demographically driven; insurance float decoupled from cycle. |
| **TOTAL** | **64** | |

---

CPSS.name: Consumer Portfolio Services, Inc.
CPSS.content:
# 9. CPSS — Consumer Portfolio Services, Inc.
## VALUE TRAP RISK | Score: 42/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Consumer Portfolio Services is a Las Vegas-headquartered indirect subprime auto lender that purchases retail installment contracts from a national dealer network and funds the portfolio almost entirely through asset-backed securitization — 107 term securitizations originated since 1994, with 19 still outstanding. FY2025 results: total revenues of $434.5M (interest income $422.7M plus a $6.5M positive fair-value mark on receivables and $5.3M of other income), against $232.0M of interest expense and a NEGATIVE $2.9M provision for credit losses (a benefit), producing $19.3M of net income or $0.88 basic / $0.80 diluted EPS. The total managed portfolio reached $3.78B against $3.86B in total assets and $309.5M of shareholders' equity (book value ~$14.20). The capital stack carries $2.99B securitization trust debt, $324.9M warehouse, $143.0M residual financing and $29.0M subordinated renewables. At 0.60x book and 10.6x earnings on a $187M market cap, this is a quintessential subprime credit value play.

The bear case is the credit cycle and concentrations. Net charge-offs as a percentage of average managed portfolio rose to 7.8% in 2025 from 7.6% in 2024 and 6.5% in 2023, and Q3 2025 standalone annualized charge-offs hit 8.01% with total delinquencies (including repossession inventory) at 13.96%. Total dollar delinquencies grew to $447.0M (11.8% of $3.78B) from $422.8M, and amount-in-repossession reached $111.2M. The business runs on continuous warehouse and securitization access — a single closed window would force balance-sheet shrinkage and book-value erosion. The Compensation Committee itself notes the inherent conflict that growing the book by underpricing risk is the easiest way to look good short-term. Customer concentration is geographic (Texas, Ohio, California, Illinois are top-four dealer states) and macroeconomically tied to subprime employment.

The bull case is securitization franchise and steady earnings. CPSS reported its 14th consecutive profitable year and just closed its 41st consecutive securitization where the senior tranche received triple-A ratings from at least two agencies — that program access is a real competitive advantage few subprime peers retain. CEO Charles E. Bradley Jr. has been at the helm since 1992 and his executive team carries deep options-aligned exposure (Bradley alone with options for 1.815M shares plus existing holdings). Capital return is meaningful: $8.7M of stock repurchased in 2025 on top of $12.8M in 2024 and $20.3M in 2023 — roughly 4–10% of float per year repurchased through the cycle. Book value grew from $228.4M (2022) to $309.5M (2025).

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The most important number on the income statement is the $2.9M *negative* provision for credit losses — meaning CPSS released reserves in 2025. This is a function of the company's adoption of the fair-value election for receivables originated post-January 1, 2018. For these contracts, CPSS recognizes interest income on a level-yield basis using the originated IRR and "we do not record an expense for provision for credit losses on these receivables because such credit losses are included in our computation of the appropriate level yield." Translation: most of the portfolio runs through a fair-value lens that smooths credit costs prospectively into the yield, while CECL-style provisions only apply to a shrinking legacy bucket. So when the screen reports 10.6x earnings, that earnings stream is more sensitive than it looks to the assumed level yield holding up against the rising 7.8% charge-off line — a small recalibration of fair-value marks could swing several quarters of EPS.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Subprime indirect auto with fair-value receivable accounting is non-trivial. |
| Moat | 5 | 107 securitizations and AAA-rated senior tranches is a real distribution moat. |
| Balance Sheet | 4 | $3.86B assets, $309M equity = 12.5x leverage; $325M warehouse + $143M residual debt. |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | 0.60x book and 10.6x earnings, but earnings driven by fair-value marks. |
| Catalyst | 4 | Continued buybacks and credit normalization; no obvious near-term catalyst. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 4 | Subprime credit cycle creates fat left tail. |
| Business Quality | 4 | ~6% ROE, leveraged subprime business model. |
| Executability | 5 | $187M cap, modest float, insider concentration meaningful. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 3 | Funding window slams shut; charge-offs accelerate; level-yield resets erode book. |
| Macro Independence | 2 | Direct exposure to subprime employment, used-car prices, securitization spreads. |
| **TOTAL** | **42** | |

---

THRY.name: Thryv Holdings, Inc.
THRY.content:
# 10. THRY — Thryv Holdings, Inc.
## MELTING ICE CUBE | Score: 43/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Thryv Holdings is the post-Chapter-11 successor to Dex Media — yes, the print Yellow Pages — that has spent the last several years deliberately reinventing itself as a small-business SaaS platform. The company now reports two segments: SaaS (its Thryv Platform CRM/marketing/payments suite plus Keap acquired October 2024) and Marketing Services (legacy print and digital, which management explicitly plans to "exit by the end of 2028"). FY2025 revenue was $785.0M, down 4.7% — SaaS grew 34.2% to $461.0M while Marketing Services collapsed 32.6% to $324.0M. Net income was $0.3M (versus a $74.2M loss in 2024), Adjusted EBITDA was $151.8M, and SaaS Adjusted EBITDA reportedly rose to $73.8M from $41.2M. Total stockholders' equity was $218.1M against 43.8M shares outstanding (book ~$4.97), with $688.6M of total assets. At 0.66x book, 329x reported P/E and a $145M market cap, this is a classic melting-ice-cube wager on cash conversion.

The bear case is leverage on a sunsetting cash cow. Total debt sits at $253.5M ($210.9M Term Loan including the related-party tranche plus $25.1M ABL plus current portion), with pension obligations of another $44.2M. The Marketing Services segment is the only thing producing real EBITDA today, and it is contractually scheduled to disappear by end-2028 — every quarter that runs the clock down compresses the cash base used to service the term loan and to fund the SaaS pivot. Goodwill of $253.8M sits against $1.17B of accumulated impairment losses and a $572.1M accumulated deficit, evidence of how value-destructive the prior decade was. Stock-based compensation expense was $25.3M in 2025 (up from $24.1M in 2024) — roughly 17% of market cap annually in dilution, eating any equity value the cash flows might create. The 2024 Keap acquisition was funded by issuing 5.7M shares at $76.8M in net proceeds, further diluting holders.

The bull case is straightforward: pay 0.66x book for a SaaS business that did $461M of revenue at $73.8M of segment EBITDA (16% margin, scaling fast) and get the Marketing Services cash run-off as a debt-paydown engine for free. Management says cash flows from Marketing Services will continue through 2030 even after the 2028 commercial exit, providing four-plus years of de-leveraging visibility. SaaS subscriber base now exceeds 100,000 between Thryv and Keap. 2026 guidance calls for SaaS revenue $461M–$471M and SaaS Adjusted EBITDA $70M–$75M, validating the growth-with-margin trajectory. If the SaaS segment alone is worth even a modest 2x revenue multiple (~$900M+) by 2028, common equity could meaningfully re-rate even after debt paydown.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Adjusted EBITDA bridge tells the real story. To get from $0.3M of GAAP net income to $151.8M of Adjusted EBITDA, management adds back $25.3M of SBC, $28.2M of restructuring and integration expenses, $8.8M of net periodic pension cost, $52.8M of D&A, $34.8M of interest expense, $16.7M of tax, and others — meaning roughly half of "Adjusted EBITDA" relies on add-backs that recur (SBC, pension, restructuring) or on debt service (interest). The 329x P/E is correct as printed because GAAP earnings barely cleared zero, and a $1.16B accumulated impairment line plus a $572M accumulated deficit makes clear that book value here is largely a function of post-bankruptcy fresh-start accounting carried forward — not a fortress NAV. The Marketing Services exit also embeds working-capital release (deferred costs, contract liabilities, unbilled receivables — $152.7M of unbilled AR alone), which will be a one-time tailwind that could fund both deleveraging and SaaS reinvestment over 2026–2028.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Two-segment thesis is clear; SaaS unit economics still evolving. |
| Moat | 3 | SMB SaaS is hyper-competitive; Keap/Thryv brand recognition modest. |
| Balance Sheet | 3 | $253M debt + $44M pension vs. $10.8M cash and $151.8M Adjusted EBITDA. |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | 0.66x book is misleading; book value is bankruptcy-emergence-derived. |
| Catalyst | 6 | 2028 print exit and 2030 cash run-off provide a discrete deleveraging path. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | SaaS works → multi-bagger; SaaS stalls → equity wipe under term loan. |
| Business Quality | 4 | SaaS Adj EBITDA margin ~16%, but consolidated GAAP earnings near zero. |
| Executability | 5 | $145M cap, SBC dilution ~17%/yr meaningfully erodes per-share value. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 3 | Marketing Services declines faster than SaaS scales; covenants tighten. |
| Macro Independence | 4 | SMB churn cycles with the economy; print legacy already in secular decline. |
| **TOTAL** | **43** | |

---
THRY.analystSummary:
Thryv Holdings is the post-Chapter-11 successor to Dex Media — yes, the print Yellow Pages — that has spent the last several years deliberately reinventing itself as a small-business SaaS platform. The company now reports two segments: SaaS (its Thryv Platform CRM/marketing/payments suite plus Keap acquired October 2024) and Marketing Services (legacy print and digital, which management explicitly plans to "exit by the end of 2028"). FY2025 revenue was $785.0M, down 4.7% — SaaS grew 34.2% to $461.0M while Marketing Services collapsed 32.6% to $324.0M. Net income was $0.3M (versus a $74.2M loss in 2024), Adjusted EBITDA was $151.8M, and SaaS Adjusted EBITDA reportedly rose to $73.8M from $41.2M. Total stockholders' equity was $218.1M against 43.8M shares outstanding (book ~$4.97), with $688.6M of total assets. At 0.66x book, 329x reported P/E and a $145M market cap, this is a classic melting-ice-cube wager on cash conversion.

The bear case is leverage on a sunsetting cash cow. Total debt sits at $253.5M ($210.9M Term Loan including the related-party tranche plus $25.1M ABL plus current portion), with pension obligations of another $44.2M. The Marketing Services segment is the only thing producing real EBITDA today, and it is contractually scheduled to disappear by end-2028 — every quarter that runs the clock down compresses the cash base used to service the term loan and to fund the SaaS pivot. Goodwill of $253.8M sits against $1.17B of accumulated impairment losses and a $572.1M accumulated deficit, evidence of how value-destructive the prior decade was. Stock-based compensation expense was $25.3M in 2025 (up from $24.1M in 2024) — roughly 17% of market cap annually in dilution, eating any equity value the cash flows might create. The 2024 Keap acquisition was funded by issuing 5.7M shares at $76.8M in net proceeds, further diluting holders.

The bull case is straightforward: pay 0.66x book for a SaaS business that did $461M of revenue at $73.8M of segment EBITDA (16% margin, scaling fast) and get the Marketing Services cash run-off as a debt-paydown engine for free. Management says cash flows from Marketing Services will continue through 2030 even after the 2028 commercial exit, providing four-plus years of de-leveraging visibility. SaaS subscriber base now exceeds 100,000 between Thryv and Keap. 2026 guidance calls for SaaS revenue $461M–$471M and SaaS Adjusted EBITDA $70M–$75M, validating the growth-with-margin trajectory. If the SaaS segment alone is worth even a modest 2x revenue multiple (~$900M+) by 2028, common equity could meaningfully re-rate even after debt paydown.

SRBK.name: SR Bancorp, Inc.
SRBK.content:
# 11. SRBK — SR Bancorp, Inc.
## BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 56/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

SR Bancorp is the Maryland-chartered holding company for Somerset Regal Bank, a 14-branch New Jersey community bank serving Essex, Hunterdon, Hudson, Middlesex, Morris, Somerset, Union and Livingston counties. The Bank was created via the September 19, 2023 mutual-to-stock conversion of Somerset Savings Bank and the simultaneous acquisition of Regal Bancorp. At June 30, 2025 the company reported total assets of $1.08 billion, deposits of $846.0 million, and total stockholders' equity of $193.8 million. The screen surfaces SRBK at P/B 0.71 and P/E 32.7 on a $139M market cap — classic post-conversion thrift pricing where the company sits on outsized capital but earns a depressed ROE while it deploys the IPO proceeds, integrates the merger, and amortizes the core deposit intangible from the Regal deal. Net income recovered to $3.7M ($0.44/share) for FY2025 versus a $10.9M loss in FY2024.

The bear case is straightforward: a sub-scale NJ community bank with too much capital, integrating a merger of equals while NIM gets squeezed by deposit competition. Management itself flags that with the additional capital raised, "we expect our return on equity to be low, which may reduce our stock price." Loan concentration in CRE and multi-family (typical NJ thrift mix) creates exposure if the local economy weakens — the 10-K notes a "prolonged or more severe downturn in the local economy could result in significant increases in non-performing loans." The IPO loaded the balance sheet with an expensive ESOP ($6.7M contra-equity) and a non-cash charitable foundation contribution. Allowance for credit losses sits at only $5.4M against ~$800M of loans — thin if CRE deteriorates. There is no organic growth catalyst beyond rate-cut driven NIM expansion.

The bull case rests on capital return mechanics. SRBK trades at ~71% of tangible book and management has been aggressive with buybacks: a first 950,793-share program completed in August, then a second 886,137-share authorization (~10% of outstanding) approved July 2025. A $0.05 quarterly dividend was initiated with the December 2025 record. Standard mutual-to-stock thrift playbook says: trade for ~3 years post-conversion, then become an acquisition target — second-step thrifts in NJ have routinely exited at 130-150% of book to a larger acquirer. With $193.8M of equity backing a $139M market cap and a clean credit book, downside is reasonably bounded. The merger gave the bank meaningful commercial lending expertise it previously lacked, expanding earnings potential as legacy lower-yielding investment securities reprice.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The reported $32.7 P/E dramatically understates run-rate earnings power because of the conversion's one-time drag. The 10-K notes the bank realized a "pre-tax realized loss of approximately $4 [million]" on the sale of lower-yielding investment securities to reposition the book, plus heavy core deposit intangible amortization from the Regal acquisition still flowing through expenses. The ESOP also creates an ongoing GAAP compensation expense as shares are released. CECL was only adopted on July 1, 2023 — meaning the FY2024/2025 reserve build absorbed the day-one accounting hit AND the Regal acquired-loan PCD reserve, both compressing reported earnings. Look at pre-provision pre-amortization earnings to see what the franchise can really earn once the conversion noise washes through.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Vanilla NJ community thrift — deposits in, loans out, you can read it. |
| Moat | 4 | Local relationships and 14 branches in affluent NJ counties, but no real cost or scale moat. |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | $193.8M equity on $1.08B assets, well-capitalized, ACL $5.4M against ~$800M loans. |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | P/B 0.71 with clean tangible book provides cushion. |
| Catalyst | 7 | Active buyback (~10% authorization), first dividend initiated, classic 3-year post-conversion sale window. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Downside protected by capital, upside via re-rating or M&A bid. |
| Business Quality | 3 | Reported ROE depressed (~2%), high efficiency ratio post-merger, sub-scale. |
| Executability | 5 | $139M cap, modest float, reasonable but thin daily volume. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | NJ CRE shock + persistent NIM compression could keep ROE low for years. |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Highly rate-sensitive; NIM expansion thesis depends on yield curve. |
| **TOTAL** | **56** | |

---

- [SR Bancorp Buyback Announcement](https://www.stocktitan.net/news/SRBK/sr-bancorp-inc-announces-adoption-of-its-second-stock-repurchase-4bktj8pgyfnh.html)

MITT.name: TPG Mortgage Investment Trust, Inc.
MITT.content:
# 12. MITT — TPG Mortgage Investment Trust, Inc.
## DISTRESSED ARB | Score: 53/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

MITT — formerly AG Mortgage Investment Trust, renamed TPG Mortgage Investment Trust effective December 26, 2025 — is an externally managed residential mortgage REIT focused primarily on residential whole loans, particularly Non-QM, Home Equity Loans, and Non-Agency RMBS collateralized by these loan types. The 10-K describes Arc Home, LLC, the company's residential mortgage loan originator in which MITT owns approximately 66%, as a strategic earnings driver. The manager is AG REIT Management, LLC, "a wholly-owned subsidiary of TPG (NASDAQ: TPG), a leading global alternative asset management firm." At December 31, 2025 reported book value was $10.48 per share; total dividends declared in 2025 were $0.85 (a 13.3% increase YoY), and MITT raised its Q1 2026 quarterly common dividend to $0.24 from $0.23. Screen prints P/B 0.73, P/E 8.7, MCap $248M.

The bear case is mortgage REIT 101 — and amplified by the externally-managed structure. Book value is "marked-to-market" and exquisitely sensitive to mortgage spreads, prepayment speeds, and the yield curve. The 10-K explicitly flags risks from "changes in interest rates and the fair value of our assets, including negative changes resulting in margin calls relating to the financing of our assets," changes in the yield curve, increased default/delinquency rates with decreased recovery rates, and the inherent risk that hedging "may or may not protect us from interest rate and credit risk volatility." The company also flags integration risk for the Western Asset Mortgage Capital Corporation ("WMC") acquisition. External management means a fee leakage to TPG that would otherwise accrue to common holders; preferred dividends ($19.2M in 2024) sit ahead of common.

The bull case is straightforward: a portfolio rotation story under TPG's well-capitalized parent. The new TPG branding (and Q4 2025 earnings call) emphasized capital rotation toward higher-yielding home equity, exercising call rights on legacy securitizations, and growing Arc Home's profitability. Trading at ~73% of book with an ~11-12% common dividend yield (recently raised), the discount overstates the credit risk on a portfolio that is overwhelmingly senior secured first-lien residential mortgages. Total return in 2025 exceeded 42% including dividends and price appreciation. If TPG can both narrow the book-discount and grow EPS via Arc Home and call-right rotation, the math compounds.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The 10-K reveals the under-the-hood Arc Home stake: an approximate 66% ownership in a residential originator that is a related-party entity, meaning a meaningful chunk of MITT's run-rate "earnings power" is contingent on origination volumes and gain-on-sale margins at a captive lender, not on mortgage REIT spread mechanics. This both adds optionality (a lender that produces MITT's own paper at scale) and adds a layer of opaque mark-to-market exposure: Arc Home values are subjective, can swing on origination conditions, and the 66% stake means consolidation/equity-method accounting noise. A non-trivial portion of the headline $0.85 in dividends is supported by Arc Home contribution; if origination volumes contract, the dividend math changes quickly.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 4 | Mortgage REIT + captive originator + securitization call rights — opaque to most. |
| Moat | 3 | TPG branding/sourcing helps, but core business is commodity rate spread. |
| Balance Sheet | 5 | Levered like all mREITs; recourse leverage and securitized non-recourse mix. |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | Trading at ~73% of $10.48 book provides cushion to credit and rate shocks. |
| Catalyst | 7 | TPG rebrand, dividend hike, $35M capital rotation target via call rights. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Asymmetric only if mortgage spreads tighten and BV holds. |
| Business Quality | 4 | Externally managed = manager fee drag; Arc Home is volatile. |
| Executability | 6 | $248M cap is liquid enough; preferred series available. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 3 | Spread widening + recession defaults could crush BV by 20%+ in a quarter. |
| Macro Independence | 2 | Pure macro/rates beta. |
| **TOTAL** | **53** | |

---

- [TPG Mortgage Q4 2025 Press Release](https://www.stocktitan.net/news/TPG/tpg-mortgage-investment-trust-inc-reports-full-year-and-fourth-77w4imcqr2k2.html)
- [Seeking Alpha - MITT Dividend Coverage](https://seekingalpha.com/article/4856992-tpg-mortgage-investment-trust-coverage-of-the-higher-dividend-should-improve-in-2026)
- [Motley Fool MITT Q4 2025 Transcript](https://www.fool.com/earnings/call-transcripts/2026/02/17/tpg-mortgage-mitt-q4-2025-earnings-transcript/)

FBLA.name: FB Bancorp, Inc.
FBLA.content:
# 13. FBLA — FB Bancorp, Inc.
## BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 58/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

FB Bancorp is the New Orleans-headquartered holding company for Fidelity Bank, a 1908-vintage Louisiana community bank with 18 full-service branches across central and southern Louisiana, including its main office, two drive-up branches and 14 stand-alone ATMs. The bank converted from mutual-to-stock form on October 22, 2024, completing its IPO on the NASDAQ at $10/share and raising $198.4 million from the sale of nearly 19.9 million shares. Stated strategy at IPO was to diversify the loan book, expand activity in Baton Rouge and Lafayette, and pursue branch/bank M&A. Screen prints P/B 0.74, P/E 63.5, MCap $192M — a textbook freshly-converted thrift profile: equity inflated by IPO proceeds, ROE temporarily near-zero while management deploys the cash. The 10-K describes the lending mix as commercial real estate, residential, and consumer, with all the standard CECL allowance for credit losses methodology.

The bear case starts with the optically punitive 63.5x trailing P/E — that figure reflects an over-capitalized post-IPO balance sheet earning low single-digit ROE while sitting on excess proceeds and amortizing IPO/ESOP/foundation costs. Louisiana has structurally weaker demographics than higher-growth Sun Belt markets; insurance market dislocation post-Ida and rising property reinsurance costs pressure CRE collateral values. Management's own forward-looking statements highlight risks from "fluctuations in real estate values," demand for loans in the local market, and ability to access cost-effective funding. Hurricane and flood concentration in Louisiana creates a tail risk that does not exist for, say, a Midwest community bank. There is no Sun Belt growth tailwind to mask sub-scale operating leverage.

The bull case is the second-step conversion playbook with an above-average jurisdiction premium. On November 13, 2025 FB Bancorp announced a stock buyback authorizing repurchase of up to 1,983,750 shares (~10% of outstanding), echoing the SRBK template — buy back stock at 0.74x book, return excess capital, then sell to a regional acquirer in 2-4 years. The book value per share floor (~$13.50 implied at P/B 0.74 on a $10/share IPO) is meaningful, the loan book is plain-vanilla community lending, and Fidelity Bank's $1.1B in assets and $769M in deposits is the right size to be either the consolidator or the consolidated party in the gradually-shrinking Gulf community bank ecosystem. Capital ratios should be very high; tangible book per share should grow as buybacks shrink the denominator.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The 10-K notes that book value of land, buildings, leasehold improvements, furniture, fixtures and equipment was $57 million — non-trivial real estate exposure for a $192M-market-cap holding company headquartered in downtown New Orleans (Louisiana 70130 per the cover page). Several Fidelity branches are owned rather than leased; in a community-bank acquisition, owned premises are typically liquidated post-deal, providing additional asset-realization optionality not visible in tangible book. Equally important: the IPO's ~19.9M share issuance at $10 means the entire current public float was created in October 2024 — selling pressure from IPO-allocated thrift depositor accounts and arbitrage funds is still washing through, which partially explains the persistent below-book trade. As the lockup-style technical pressure decays, the discount should naturally compress.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Plain Louisiana community bank — deposits, mortgages, small CRE. |

FBLA.analystSummary:
FB Bancorp is the New Orleans-headquartered holding company for Fidelity Bank, a 1908-vintage Louisiana community bank with 18 full-service branches across central and southern Louisiana, including its main office, two drive-up branches and 14 stand-alone ATMs. The bank converted from mutual-to-stock form on October 22, 2024, completing its IPO on the NASDAQ at $10/share and raising $198.4 million from the sale of nearly 19.9 million shares. Stated strategy at IPO was to diversify the loan book, expand activity in Baton Rouge and Lafayette, and pursue branch/bank M&A. Screen prints P/B 0.74, P/E 63.5, MCap $192M — a textbook freshly-converted thrift profile: equity inflated by IPO proceeds, ROE temporarily near-zero while management deploys the cash. The 10-K describes the lending mix as commercial real estate, residential, and consumer, with all the standard CECL allowance for credit losses methodology.

The bear case starts with the optically punitive 63.5x trailing P/E — that figure reflects an over-capitalized post-IPO balance sheet earning low single-digit ROE while sitting on excess proceeds and amortizing IPO/ESOP/foundation costs. Louisiana has structurally weaker demographics than higher-growth Sun Belt markets; insurance market dislocation post-Ida and rising property reinsurance costs pressure CRE collateral values. Management's own forward-looking statements highlight risks from "fluctuations in real estate values," demand for loans in the local market, and ability to access cost-effective funding. Hurricane and flood concentration in Louisiana creates a tail risk that does not exist for, say, a Midwest community bank. There is no Sun Belt growth tailwind to mask sub-scale operating leverage.

The bull case is the second-step conversion playbook with an above-average jurisdiction premium. On November 13, 2025 FB Bancorp announced a stock buyback authorizing repurchase of up to 1,983,750 shares (~10% of outstanding), echoing the SRBK template — buy back stock at 0.74x book, return excess capital, then sell to a regional acquirer in 2-4 years. The book value per share floor (~$13.50 implied at P/B 0.74 on a $10/share IPO) is meaningful, the loan book is plain-vanilla community lending, and Fidelity Bank's $1.1B in assets and $769M in deposits is the right size to be either the consolidator or the consolidated party in the gradually-shrinking Gulf community bank ecosystem. Capital ratios should be very high; tangible book per share should grow as buybacks shrink the denominator.
FBLA.whatScreenCantSee: The 10-K notes that book value of land, buildings, leasehold improvements, furniture, fixtures and equipment was $57 million — non-trivial real estate exposure for a $192M-market-cap holding company headquartered in downtown New Orleans (Louisiana 70130 per the cover page). Several Fidelity branches are owned rather than leased; in a community-bank acquisition, owned premises are typically liquidated post-deal, providing additional asset-realization optionality not visible in tangible book. Equally important: the IPO's ~19.9M share issuance at $10 means the entire current public float was created in October 2024 — selling pressure from IPO-allocated thrift depositor accounts and arbitrage funds is still washing through, which partially explains the persistent below-book trade. As the lockup-style technical pressure decays, the discount should naturally compress.

NLCP.name: NewLake Capital Partners, Inc.
NLCP.content:
# 14. NLCP — NewLake Capital Partners, Inc.
## DISTRESSED ARB | Score: 60/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

NewLake Capital Partners is a triple-net REIT that owns 34 single-tenant cannabis properties — 15 cultivation facilities and 19 dispensaries — leased to state-licensed cannabis operators on long-term, sale-leaseback structures. The 10-K reports 91% of rental income from cultivation facilities and properties held in core medical/limited-license states. The company is essentially the smaller, less-levered counterpart to IIPR with a concentrated tenant roster: Curaleaf, Cresco Labs, Trulieve, PharmaCann, Ascend, Verano, and several mid-cap MSOs comprise the rent roll. Screen prints P/B 0.76, P/E 11.4, MCap $300M. The dividend has been the marquee feature: a double-digit yield (~12-13%) supported by an AFFO payout ratio reported in the high 70s to low 80s percent range, on a near-zero-leverage balance sheet.

The bear case is the entire reason NLCP trades below book: federal cannabis prohibition, plus a tenant base concentrated in MSOs whose own equity prices have collapsed. The 10-K explicitly states "tenant concentration risk (and related risk of tenant defaults) may be more pronounced in the cannabis industry due to the fact that some of our tenants have limited operating histories" and that "cannabis remains illegal under federal law." The top three tenants (Curaleaf, Cresco Labs, Trulieve) reportedly represent ~50% of annualized rent — single-name credit events meaningfully impair coverage. Rescheduling to Schedule III has been repeatedly delayed; safe-harbor banking legislation has gone nowhere in Congress. Re-tenanting a vacant 100,000+ sq ft cannabis cultivation facility in a non-adult-use state is non-trivial, and at least three properties (Massachusetts, Pennsylvania, Nevada) appear vacant in the property table.

The bull case: triple-net REIT with negligible debt and ~13% yield, trading at 76% of book, where the worst-case tenant defaults have largely already occurred or been priced in (Revolutionary Clinics and AYR Wellness situations were managed without dividend cuts). The fortress balance sheet — net cash, no significant credit-facility draws, no near-term debt maturities — eliminates the financial-leverage risk that has wrecked many cannabis-adjacent companies. If federal reform happens at any point in the next 24 months (DEA rescheduling, SAFE Banking, or even softer 280E enforcement), MSO tenant credit improves materially, the discount-to-book compresses, and current shareholders capture a 30-50% re-rating on top of the 13% yield. Even with no reform, holding to collect 13% annually on book-discounted real estate with hard collateral is a defensible base case.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The 10-K's property tables — XBRL tags reveal entries like "Trulieve" in Pennsylvania, multiple "Curaleaf" properties in Florida and Illinois, "Vacant1Member" in Nevada and "Vacant2Member" in Pennsylvania — show a real, identifiable list of distressed re-tenanting situations. The unsecured loan to C3 Industries (originated June 10, 2022 per XBRL) is an off-the-radar credit exposure separate from rent — a working-capital-style loan to one of NLCP's tenants, exactly the kind of related-tenant lending that converts a passive landlord into a credit investor. Also worth noting: NLCP carries a CECL allowance for credit losses (adopted January 1, 2023) on its receivables — a non-trivial accounting choice for a REIT and a leading indicator of how management views tenant deterioration before headline cash rent collection numbers move.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 6 | Triple-net leases are simple; tenant credit and federal regulation are not. |
| Moat | 5 | Limited-license states and bespoke cultivation buildouts create switching costs. |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | Net-cash position, no meaningful debt — best-in-class for cannabis-adjacent. |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | P/B 0.76 + 13% yield + hard real estate collateral. |
| Catalyst | 6 | DEA rescheduling, SAFE Banking, or 280E reform — timing uncertain. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Limited downside given balance sheet; significant upside on reform. |
| Business Quality | 6 | Triple-net REIT economics are clean; tenant base is the wart. |
| Executability | 6 | $300M cap, OTC for some periods historically; reasonable liquidity now. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Top-3 tenant default cluster could cut dividend 30-50%. |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Cannabis cyclicality + federal policy = highly path-dependent. |
| **TOTAL** | **60** | |

---

- [Seeking Alpha - 79% Payout Ratio](https://seekingalpha.com/news/4481640-newlake-signals-dividend-resilience-with-79-percent-payout-ratio-amid-tenant-default-risk)
- [BeyondSPX NLCP Analysis](https://beyondspx.com/quote/NLCP/newlake-capital-partners-a-13-dividend-backed-by-a-fortress-balance-sheet-in-distressed-cannabis-real-estate-otc-nlcp)

BSET.name: Bassett Furniture Industries, Incorporated
BSET.content:
# 15. BSET — Bassett Furniture Industries, Incorporated
## CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 55/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Bassett Furniture is a 122-year-old vertically integrated home-furnishings company operating in two segments: Wholesale (design, manufacturing and sourcing of upholstery, casegoods and the Lane Venture outdoor brand, distributed through ~86 company- and licensee-owned BHF stores plus independent retailers) and Retail (Company-owned BHF stores). Domestic upholstery is manufactured in Newton, North Carolina. At fiscal year-end November 29, 2025, the balance sheet showed total assets of $323.8 million with cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments totaling $59.2 million on hand. Net income was modest; FY2025 Q4 saw consolidated net sales rise 5.1% to $88.7M with gross margin of 56.3%, operating income of $2.3M, and diluted EPS of $0.18 (vs $0.38 prior-year quarter). Screen prints P/B 0.76, P/E 23.4, MCap $125M.

The bear case is a four-year furniture demand downcycle compounded by structural retail contraction. Existing-home turnover is at multi-decade lows; new home-buyer cohorts that historically anchor furniture purchases are deferred. The 10-K flags risks including "fluctuations in the cost and availability of raw materials, fuel, labor, delivery costs and sourced products," tariff exposure on imported product, "overall retail traffic levels in stores and on the internet and consumer demand for home furnishings," and "the profitability of the stores (independent licensees and Company-owned retail stores) which may result in future store closings." Bassett also acknowledges "the risk of additional asset impairment charges arising from the ongoing efforts to consolidate our retail warehouses." Noa Home, the Canadian e-commerce experiment, was wound down after determining it "was not likely to achieve profitability at any time in the foreseeable future."

The bull case: clean balance sheet, restructuring complete, and operating leverage as housing recovers. $59.2M cash with no debt against a $125M market cap means roughly half of the equity is net cash. Restructuring actions delivered ~$2.5M of annual savings and 11% headcount reduction; Virginia wood production facilities have been consolidated. Q4 gross margin of 56.3% (versus prior softer quarters) and 190 bps of full-year gross margin expansion show pricing/mix discipline. Casegoods sales up 50%+ and custom leather +19% in 2025 indicate product traction in higher-margin custom orders. Capital allocation: regular quarterly dividend (FY2025 dividends totaled $6.9M) plus stock repurchase activity. Real estate owned (book value of approximately $23.5M flagged in the 10-K) provides additional embedded asset value.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The 10-K reveals a meaningful contingent obligation that doesn't show up in book multiples: lease obligations of licensee operators. Bassett historically guarantees a portion of store leases for independent licensee BHF stores — when a licensee fails, Bassett is on the hook for the remaining lease term. This is exactly the failure mode that drove asset impairment and warehouse-consolidation charges referenced in the risk factors. Separately, the segment disclosure shows Bassett Custom Upholstery generated $76.9M of external sales plus $59.4M intercompany — the $59.4M of intercompany revenue is a tell that wholesale segment "external" earnings are partially supported by transfer pricing into retail, where margin is captured. Investors looking at consolidated EPS may underweight just how much of the operating income relies on the company-owned retail channel maintaining its current 60-store footprint.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Easy to understand: makes furniture, sells through stores. |
| Moat | 4 | Brand has resonance in Mid-Atlantic/Southeast but not durable nationally. |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | $59.2M cash, no debt, ~$23.5M real estate book — fortress for the size. |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | P/B 0.76 with ~50% of equity in net cash. |
| Catalyst | 5 | Restructuring savings, housing turnover recovery, Bassett Hospitality launch. |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Cash floor + cyclical operating leverage = asymmetric on a 3-year view. |
| Business Quality | 4 | Q4 GM 56.3% is good; full-year ROE depressed; demand-cycle dependent. |
| Executability | 4 | $125M cap, low daily volume, controlled-family overhang. |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Prolonged housing weakness + licensee failures + tariffs could compound. |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Highly cyclical with housing turnover and big-ticket discretionary. |
| **TOTAL** | **55** | |

---

- [Bassett Restructuring Update](https://investors.bassettfurniture.com/news-releases/news-release-details/bassett-announces-additional-cost-structure-reductions-and)
- [Bassett News Releases](https://investors.bassettfurniture.com/press-releases)

TRVG.name: trivago N.V.
TRVG.content:
# 16. TRVG — trivago N.V.
## MELTING ICE CUBE | Score: 41/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

trivago operates a hotel meta-search platform that aggregates rates from online travel agencies (OTAs) and direct hotel sites, monetizing on a cost-per-click (CPC) and cost-per-acquisition (CPA) basis. The company is incorporated in the Netherlands, has its operating center in Düsseldorf, and trades in New York via American Depositary Shares (each ADS represents five Class A shares). Expedia Group has been the controlling majority shareholder since 2013 through Class B super-voting stock. The screen flagged TRVG at P/B 0.79, P/E 15.4 on a $196M market cap. The 2025 20-F shows a real operational rebound: revenue €548.9M (vs. €460.8M in 2024, +19%), net income €11.2M (vs. a €23.7M loss prior year), Adjusted EBITDA €15.8M, and cash & equivalents of €130.9M plus €11.9M short-term investments — roughly €143M in net cash with no funded debt of consequence.

The bear case is structural revenue concentration. Per the 20-F, "we continue to generate the great majority of our revenue from our largest OTA advertisers, including brands affiliated with Booking Holdings… and Expedia Group." Two parents (one of them the controlling shareholder) determine the bid environment, and either can throttle CPC budgets at any moment. trivago has now alternated between profit and loss for several years, with 2024's net loss of €23.7M proving this is not a steady-state earner. Expedia's dual-class control means minority ADS holders cannot vote out management, force a sale, change the dividend policy, or even compel buybacks. Television-led marketing means costs are committed in advance; if advertiser CPCs drop mid-year, operating leverage works in reverse. Google's hotel module continues to siphon top-of-funnel intent.

The bull case is that the rebound is real: 19% revenue growth, swing to GAAP profit, €143M of net cash on a €196M market cap implies the operating business trades for ~$50M, or roughly 3-4x trailing operating cash. Management has been repurchasing shares opportunistically. Catalysts: continued Booking/Expedia ad-spend recovery as OTA share wars intensify, possible take-private bid from Expedia at a modest premium (Expedia would consolidate fully and remove a redundant public listing), and AI-search integrations that could re-rate meta-search assets. Capital allocation has been disciplined — no major acquisitions, light cost base, balance sheet preserved. With 353M shares outstanding (115.6M Class A + 237.5M Class B), the float is small post-Expedia.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The screen sees P/B 0.79 and P/E 15.4 but misses three filing-level realities. First, the P/E is on a year that swung from loss to profit; on the trailing two-year average, EPS is roughly break-even — multiple is meaningless. Second, the dual-class structure: Class B shares carry super-voting rights and are held almost entirely by Expedia, so corporate-action optionality belongs to one shareholder. Reserved-matter list in the 20-F includes share repurchases — trivago cannot buy back stock without Expedia's blessing. Third, the related-party accounts receivable line of €21.8M is from Expedia entities, meaning a meaningful share of working capital is collected from the controlling parent. If Expedia ever shifts its bidding logic, trivago feels it in cash before P&L. The "majority shareholder is also a top customer" pattern is a governance red flag deserving a discount, not a premium.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Hotel meta-search; simple CPC model |
| Moat | 3 | Brand exists, but Google + parents own demand |

TRVG.analystSummary:
trivago operates a hotel meta-search platform that aggregates rates from online travel agencies (OTAs) and direct hotel sites, monetizing on a cost-per-click (CPC) and cost-per-acquisition (CPA) basis. The company is incorporated in the Netherlands, has its operating center in Düsseldorf, and trades in New York via American Depositary Shares (each ADS represents five Class A shares). Expedia Group has been the controlling majority shareholder since 2013 through Class B super-voting stock. The screen flagged TRVG at P/B 0.79, P/E 15.4 on a $196M market cap. The 2025 20-F shows a real operational rebound: revenue €548.9M (vs. €460.8M in 2024, +19%), net income €11.2M (vs. a €23.7M loss prior year), Adjusted EBITDA €15.8M, and cash & equivalents of €130.9M plus €11.9M short-term investments — roughly €143M in net cash with no funded debt of consequence.

The bear case is structural revenue concentration. Per the 20-F, "we continue to generate the great majority of our revenue from our largest OTA advertisers, including brands affiliated with Booking Holdings… and Expedia Group." Two parents (one of them the controlling shareholder) determine the bid environment, and either can throttle CPC budgets at any moment. trivago has now alternated between profit and loss for several years, with 2024's net loss of €23.7M proving this is not a steady-state earner. Expedia's dual-class control means minority ADS holders cannot vote out management, force a sale, change the dividend policy, or even compel buybacks. Television-led marketing means costs are committed in advance; if advertiser CPCs drop mid-year, operating leverage works in reverse. Google's hotel module continues to siphon top-of-funnel intent.

The bull case is that the rebound is real: 19% revenue growth, swing to GAAP profit, €143M of net cash on a €196M market cap implies the operating business trades for ~$50M, or roughly 3-4x trailing operating cash. Management has been repurchasing shares opportunistically. Catalysts: continued Booking/Expedia ad-spend recovery as OTA share wars intensify, possible take-private bid from Expedia at a modest premium (Expedia would consolidate fully and remove a redundant public listing), and AI-search integrations that could re-rate meta-search assets. Capital allocation has been disciplined — no major acquisitions, light cost base, balance sheet preserved. With 353M shares outstanding (115.6M Class A + 237.5M Class B), the float is small post-Expedia.
TRVG.whatScreenCantSee: The screen sees P/B 0.79 and P/E 15.4 but misses three filing-level realities. First, the P/E is on a year that swung from loss to profit; on the trailing two-year average, EPS is roughly break-even — multiple is meaningless. Second, the dual-class structure: Class B shares carry super-voting rights and are held almost entirely by Expedia, so corporate-action optionality belongs to one shareholder. Reserved-matter list in the 20-F includes share repurchases — trivago cannot buy back stock without Expedia's blessing. Third, the related-party accounts receivable line of €21.8M is from Expedia entities, meaning a meaningful share of working capital is collected from the controlling parent. If Expedia ever shifts its bidding logic, trivago feels it in cash before P&L. The "majority shareholder is also a top customer" pattern is a governance red flag deserving a discount, not a premium.

TYFG.name: Tri-County Financial Group, Inc.
TYFG.content:
# 17. TYFG — Tri-County Financial Group, Inc.
## BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 58/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Tri-County Financial Group is the holding company for First State Bank, an 85-year-old north-central Illinois community bank headquartered in Mendota with branches across Batavia, Bloomington, Champaign, Geneva, LaMoille, McNabb, North Aurora, Ottawa, Peru, Princeton, Rochelle, Shabbona, St. Charles, Streator, Sycamore, Waterman, and West Brooklyn — eighteen branches plus a residential-mortgage subsidiary (FSM) and an insurance subsidiary (First State Insurance). The screen flagged TYFG at P/B 0.80 and P/E 9.3 on a $126M market cap. The 2025 10-K shows ~$1.6B in total assets, return on average assets of 0.88% (vs. 0.68% in 2024), return on average equity of 9.09% (vs. 7.45%), net interest margin of 3.40% (vs. 2.96%), allowance for credit losses of 1.14% of loans, nonperforming loans of just 0.43% of loans, Tier 1 leverage capital ratio of 10.51%, total risk-based capital of 14.91%, and stockholders' equity to total assets of 9.89%. With 2,376,998 shares outstanding and a $43.50 reference price, this is a tiny, illiquid name.

The bear case is structural illiquidity and concentration. The shares trade on OTCQX, not a national exchange — the 10-K explicitly notes "OTCQX… provides limited liquidity. Consequently, you may not be able to sell your common stock at or above the price you paid." A 2.4M-share float means a single seller can move price 5-10% on a normal day. The loan book is concentrated in north-central Illinois agriculture, commercial real estate, and small-business lending — a regional recession or farm-belt commodity slump would hit credit quality fast. Loan-to-deposit at 100.87% leaves no slack for funding stress. As a community bank, TYFG cannot match the deposit pricing of larger competitors if rates spike again, and digital banks continue to skim younger Illinois depositors. The 17.4% dividend payout ratio is conservative, but bank regulators can suspend dividends at any time under capital-distribution rules referenced in the 10-K.

The bull case is straightforward small-bank value. Earning 9% on book at 0.80x book is mathematically a 11.3% earnings yield with capital ratios well above regulatory well-capitalized thresholds. NIM expanded 44bp year-over-year as legacy low-yield assets reprice; with another 12-18 months of asset reset, NIM could push toward 3.6-3.8%, lifting ROA toward 1%. Catalysts: continued rate-cycle benefit, possible NASDAQ uplisting that would unlock institutional ownership, and ultimate consolidation in Illinois community banking — a buyer would likely pay 1.2-1.5x book given ROE trajectory. Capital allocation discipline is evident in the conservative payout, modest balance sheet leverage, and 85-year operating history without a forced recapitalization.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

Three filing-level details deserve weight. First, FSM (mortgage banking) gives TYFG fee income that is not captured in the headline NIM picture but is highly cyclical with mortgage-rate volatility — 2026 rate cuts would mean a refi tailwind. Second, the 10-K notes the bank has unused FHLB-Chicago borrowing capacity collateralized at varying advance rates against the loan book (76% of book value of 1-4 family real estate, 62% of HELOCs, etc.) — meaningful contingent liquidity not visible to a screen. Third, OTCQX listing is a self-imposed constraint, not a regulatory one; management has stayed off NASDAQ to avoid Sarbanes-Oxley-class compliance costs, but a future board could uplist. Tiny float means any institutional discovery is a re-rating catalyst. Insiders likely own meaningful percentage of shares — the typical Illinois community-bank holdco pattern.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Plain-vanilla community bank |
| Moat | 5 | Local relationships in 18 Illinois towns |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | T1 leverage 10.51%, NPL 0.43%, ACL 1.14% |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | 0.80x book, 9% ROE = 11% earnings yield |
| Catalyst | 5 | NIM expansion, possible uplist or M&A |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Limited downside given capital cushion |
| Business Quality | 6 | ROA 0.88%, ROE 9.09% — adequate |
| Executability | 3 | OTCQX, 2.4M float — illiquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Illinois CRE/ag credit cycle is the risk |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Bank earnings rate-sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **58** | Quality below book; liquidity is the price |

REFI.name: Chicago Atlantic Real Estate Finance, Inc.
REFI.content:
# 18. REFI — Chicago Atlantic Real Estate Finance, Inc.
## DISTRESSED ARB | Score: 62/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Chicago Atlantic Real Estate Finance is a commercial mortgage REIT that originates and holds senior secured loans collateralized primarily by real estate, equipment, and licenses of state-licensed cannabis operators. The company is externally managed by Chicago Atlantic REIT Manager and listed on NASDAQ. The screen flagged REFI at P/B 0.81, P/E 7.1 on a $250M market cap. The 2025 10-K shows loans held for investment of $411.1M outstanding principal with original-issue discount of $2.1M and current-expected-credit-loss reserve of $5.1M, for a carrying value of $403.9M. Weighted-average maturity is 2.2 years, and the portfolio's yield-to-maturity is 16.3% on a weighted-average basis (range 9.7% to 26.9%) inclusive of coupon, OID, unused fees, exit fees, and other yield-enhancing items. The current expected credit loss reserve increased ~$716K year-over-year, signaling some incremental credit deterioration but not a portfolio break.

The bear case begins with one fact: cannabis remains illegal under U.S. federal law. The 10-K states bluntly that "cannabis remains illegal under federal law," and a federal enforcement turn (or simply a delayed Schedule III rescheduling) could collapse tenant economics across multi-state operators. State-level price compression in mature markets like California, Massachusetts, Michigan, and Colorado has already squeezed wholesale margins, raising borrower default risk. The 10-K notes that certain assets cannot be used as collateral or transferred due to state cannabis-licensing restrictions — a material constraint on workout recovery. Loan concentration risk is real: portfolio of ~30 loans means a single $25-30M position can move the dividend. External management means a permanent fee leak (base + incentive) regardless of REFI shareholder outcomes. Banking-access risk persists pending SAFE Banking, which has stalled in Congress for years.

The bull case is a 16.3% portfolio yield collected by a REIT trading at 0.81x book — even with conservative underwriting, the spread between asset yields and the cost of equity is wide. Short 2.2-year duration means the book repositions quickly if conditions worsen; the manager has done two rounds of new-vintage originations at attractive coupons since rates rose. Catalysts include DEA Schedule III rescheduling (would re-rate the entire cannabis-adjacent capital structure), SAFE Banking passage (would lower borrower funding costs and improve credit), Section 280E tax relief, and continued institutional starvation of the sector that lets REFI write loans others cannot. Capital allocation: high payout ratio (REIT requirement), no acquisition appetite, and a clearly defined "credit-first" underwriting process that has produced minimal realized losses through 2025.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

Several filing-level points matter. First, REFI co-invests with affiliates of its external manager, including private vehicles ("Other investment vehicles managed by our Manager or affiliates… may co-invest with us or hold positions in a loan that we hold"). Co-investment can be a feature (deal flow access) or a problem (allocation conflict), and the 10-K's allocation policy is what to read carefully. Second, REFI generally will not take a position junior to a Manager-affiliate position — a meaningful protection. Third, "Capitalized PIK Interest" of $6.2M flowed through the loan-balance roll-forward in 2025: a portion of stated income is non-cash, which inflates GAAP NII relative to distributable cash. Fourth, the 16.3% IRR is a portfolio-level WAVG including OID and exit fees that may not be realized if loans repay early or default. Fifth, REFI is not a bank — it cannot access the Fed's discount window if cannabis credit spreads blow out.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 6 | Senior secured cannabis lender; simple at first glance |
| Moat | 6 | Federal-illegality moat keeps banks out |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | Modest leverage, $5.1M CECL on $411M principal |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | 0.81x book on 16.3% asset yields |
| Catalyst | 7 | Schedule III, SAFE Banking, 280E reform optionality |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Asymmetric upside if reform passes |
| Business Quality | 7 | High asset yields, short duration, low loss rate |
| Executability | 7 | NASDAQ-listed, reasonable liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Federal crackdown or single MSO bankruptcy chain |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Cannabis is regulatory-driven, but somewhat counter-cyclical |
| **TOTAL** | **62** | High-yield credit play with regulatory binary |

---

RICK.name: RCI Hospitality Holdings, Inc.
RICK.content:
# 19. RICK — RCI Hospitality Holdings, Inc.
## CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 56/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

RCI Hospitality is a Texas-domiciled holding company that operates two principal segments: Nightclubs (adult-entertainment clubs under brands such as Rick's Cabaret, Jaguars Club, Tootsie's Cabaret, XTC Cabaret, Club Onyx, and Hoops Cabaret) totaling 59 club locations, plus 11 Bombshells Restaurant & Bar units — a military-themed casual-dining concept. The screen flagged RICK at P/B 0.82, P/E 20.0 on a $215M market cap. The fiscal-2025 (year ended September 30, 2025) 10-K shows Nightclubs segment revenue down ~0.6% with income from operations up 20.1% (same-store sales -2.1%), while the Bombshells segment posted revenue down 29.2% but income from operations swinging to a $177K profit from a $10.8M loss prior year (same-store sales -13.6%). Total long-term debt is $214.6M. Management spent $11.9M on treasury-stock purchases and $2.5M on dividends in FY2025, paid down $20.5M of debt, and explicitly targets fiscal 2029 results of $400M revenue, $75M free cash flow, and 7.5M shares outstanding.

The bear case is governance turbulence on top of operational drag. The 10-K discloses that long-time CEO Eric Langan and CFO Bradley Chhay "subsequently stepped down from those positions in November 2025" amid a New York lawsuit naming both individuals and the company. Langan was the architect of the buyback machine and the operating philosophy; his departure is a material change that the headline screen does not capture. Bombshells has been a serial value-destroyer with cumulative impairments referenced in the 10-K (including $38.5M in 2024 across goodwill, SOB-license, property, ROU, and tradename impairments). Adult-entertainment regulation, local zoning fights, and labor-classification litigation (independent-contractor entertainer disputes) remain perpetual tail risks. The 86% drop in net income from 2022's record to 2025 indicates the business is well off peak. Continuing NASDAQ listing requires ongoing compliance — already flagged as a risk in the filing.

The bull case rests on RCI's structural cash-generation profile. Operating cash declined only 23% from the 2022 peak versus a 77% drop in net income — that gap is GAAP impairments rather than real economic harm. The company aggressively closed underperforming Bombshells, which improved the segment from a $10.8M operating loss to break-even. Buybacks of $11.9M plus debt paydown of $20.5M plus dividends totaled $34.8M of capital returned and de-levered in a single year — significant for a $215M cap company. Catalysts include continued Bombshells stabilization, opportunistic club M&A at 3-4x EBITDA (the Langan playbook), buyback acceleration if the multiple stays compressed, and a refreshed management team that may or may not maintain the same capital-allocation discipline. Insider ownership remains meaningful.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The most important missed item is the November 2025 management departure — Langan was the buyback machine, and a screen looking at trailing capital-return data is implicitly underwriting his continued tenure. A second item: the 10-K notes that certain commercial bank debts "are guaranteed by the Company's former CEO" — Langan's personal guarantee may need replacement now that he has stepped down, potentially triggering refinancing at higher rates or covenant changes. Third, the SOB (Sexually Oriented Business) license is a state and local asset that has historical impairment exposure but also represents real scarcity value; new licenses are nearly impossible to obtain in major markets, so existing operators have a regulatory moat. Fourth, the fiscal-2029 target of 7.5M shares outstanding (from current ~9M) implies a 17% share-count reduction over four years — credible only if cash generation holds and the new CEO maintains the buyback cadence. Texas-domicile means director and officer indemnification is broad.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Two segments, simple unit economics |
| Moat | 6 | Adult-club licenses irreplaceable in major metros |
| Balance Sheet | 5 | $214.6M long-term debt, modest leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | 0.82x book, mid-teen FCF yield potential |
| Catalyst | 5 | Bombshells stabilization, possible M&A |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Buyback math compelling if FCF holds |
| Business Quality | 5 | High-cash adult clubs, drag from Bombshells |
| Executability | 7 | NASDAQ-listed, reasonable liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | New management could break capital-allocation discipline |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Discretionary spend cyclical; clubs less so historically |
| **TOTAL** | **56** | Cyclical trough with key-person transition risk |

---

RICK.whatScreenCantSee: The most important missed item is the November 2025 management departure — Langan was the buyback machine, and a screen looking at trailing capital-return data is implicitly underwriting his continued tenure. A second item: the 10-K notes that certain commercial bank debts "are guaranteed by the Company's former CEO" — Langan's personal guarantee may need replacement now that he has stepped down, potentially triggering refinancing at higher rates or covenant changes. Third, the SOB (Sexually Oriented Business) license is a state and local asset that has historical impairment exposure but also represents real scarcity value; new licenses are nearly impossible to obtain in major markets, so existing operators have a regulatory moat. Fourth, the fiscal-2029 target of 7.5M shares outstanding (from current ~9M) implies a 17% share-count reduction over four years — credible only if cash generation holds and the new CEO maintains the buyback cadence. Texas-domicile means director and officer indemnification is broad.

MPAA.name: Motorcar Parts of America, Inc.
MPAA.content:
# 20. MPAA — Motorcar Parts of America, Inc.
## CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 49/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Motorcar Parts of America is a remanufacturer and supplier of aftermarket automotive hard parts — alternators, starters, brake calipers, brake master cylinders, brake boosters, turbochargers, wheel hubs, and rotating-electrical components — sold primarily into the U.S. retail aftermarket through the largest national chains. The fiscal-2025 (year ended March 31, 2025) 10-K confirms the customer roster: Advance Auto Parts, AutoZone, Genuine Parts (NAPA), and O'Reilly Auto Parts, distributing through approximately 26,000 retail outlets. The screen flagged MPAA at P/B 0.83, P/E 124.8 on a $220M market cap (the elevated P/E reflects depressed trailing earnings, not premium quality). Fiscal 2025 highlights from the 10-K: net sales increased 5.5% to a record $757.4M, gross profit increased 16.1% to $153.8M, and gross margin expanded 1.8 percentage points to 20.3%. Share count is 19,435,706 as of June 2, 2025. The company operates in the U.S., Mexico (~5,000 employees, primarily production), Canada, Malaysia, and China, with ~5,300 production employees in total and ~4,900 Mexican production workers covered by a local union.

The bear case is severe customer concentration combined with persistent margin pressure. The 10-K states: "Sales to our three largest customers in the aggregate represented 86%, and sales to our largest customer represented 39% of our net sales during fiscal 2025." That is monopsony-grade exposure. The same disclosure flags ongoing pressure from major customers to "offer lower prices," extend payment terms, and accept core-return adjustments — structural margin headwinds that recur every renewal. MPAA's business model requires carrying massive inventories of "Used Cores" plus remanufactured stock, making working capital chronically heavy and inventory-days extended. Recent fiscal years saw GAAP net losses with diluted EPS reported at -$0.99, -$2.51, and -$0.22 across fiscal 2025/2024/2023 (potential common shares anti-dilutive). Tariff exposure on Chinese components and Mexican production is real and rising under shifting trade policy. Foreign currency exposure (peso, ringgit, RMB) adds non-operating volatility.

The bull case is operational inflection: revenue at a record $757M, gross margin recovery to 20.3% (up 1.8pp), and gross profit up 16% — all evidence the working-capital reset and price actions are flowing through. The aftermarket parts category benefits from rising vehicle age, deferred new-car purchases, and post-warranty repair demand — all secular tailwinds independent of new-vehicle SAAR. Catalysts include continued margin restoration (a return to mid-teens operating margin would translate to $50-70M operating income on $760M sales), normalization of inventory financing costs as rates recede, and potential strategic interest from larger industrial roll-ups. Capital allocation: the 10-K discloses a modest share-repurchase program with remaining authorization at year-end, plus debt paydown as cash generation improves. With only 8 holders of record listed (likely most shares in street name), institutional concentration is meaningful.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

Several filing-level points reshape the thesis. First, the trailing P/E is misleading because GAAP losses include large non-cash items (FX revaluation, core-accounting adjustments, mark-to-market on supply-chain financing). EBITDA and operating cash flow have been positive while GAAP net income was negative — the working-capital cycle drives the disconnect. Second, MPAA uses extensive non-recourse accounts-receivable factoring with its three large customers; this is a hidden financing line that is not "debt" on a screen but functions like one and re-prices when SOFR moves. Third, the 39%-of-revenue largest customer is not named in the public filing, but trade press strongly suggests AutoZone — losing or even renegotiating that single program would be company-defining. Fourth, the company invested in diagnostic and EV-component capabilities (e.g., test equipment for hybrid/EV motors and inverters) that gives it a foothold as drivetrain technology shifts; this optionality is invisible on a P/B screen.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Reman parts to the big-box chains; understandable |
| Moat | 4 | Scale in core exchange logistics; commoditized otherwise |
| Balance Sheet | 4 | Heavy working capital, factoring, near-zero net cash |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | 0.83x book; GAAP losses obscure real cash earnings |
| Catalyst | 5 | Margin recovery in progress; rate-cut working-capital relief |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Operational leverage works both ways |
| Business Quality | 4 | Low-margin, capital-intensive, customer-dictated pricing |
| Executability | 7 | NASDAQ-listed, ~19.4M float |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 3 | Loss of any of three top customers = company-killer |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Aftermarket demand mildly counter-cyclical to SAAR |
| **TOTAL** | **49** | Real margin inflection masked by GAAP losses |

---

UTGN.name: UTG, Inc.
UTGN.content:
# 21. UTGN — UTG, Inc.
## VALUE TRAP RISK | Score: 48/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

UTG, Inc. is a holding company whose operating subsidiary, Universe Life Insurance Group (UG), administers a closed and run-off block of traditional life insurance policies headquartered in Stanford, Kentucky and Springfield, Illinois. Total assets were $491.4 million at year-end 2025, with shareholders' equity of $232.7 million producing book value near $73.85 per share against ~3.15 million shares outstanding. The franchise is unusual for a life insurer: rather than holding a typical fixed-income-heavy portfolio, UG concentrates its investment book in equity securities (62% of total investments) and limited liability companies that own real estate, mortgages, and oil & gas royalties. This means GAAP "earnings" depend heavily on realized investment gains, which collapsed from $63.3 million in 2024 to $23.4 million in 2025, dragging EPS from $15.80 to $5.42.

The bear case is structural: UTGN is effectively a private investment vehicle controlled by majority shareholder Jesse T. Correll and his First Southern entities, with related-party arrangements throughout the 10-K. The insurance liabilities are decaying — premiums and policy fees were just $7.3 million — so the equity is functionally a leveraged bet on a Correll-managed concentrated equity, real estate, and royalty portfolio. Public minorities have no real influence, governance is insider-friendly, and the auditor (Kerber, Eck & Braeckel) is a regional firm. Reported earnings are lumpy, hard to model, and largely a function of marks on illiquid LLC investments. The 2024 results were "(as restated)," indicating prior-period accounting issues. P/B ~0.84 looks cheap, but the discount to book has persisted for years.

The bull case rests on Correll's long-term track record allocating insurance float into hard assets and on the optionality of the oil & gas royalties (now 30% of investment real estate, up from 20%). If you believe in Correll the way Berkshire shareholders believe in Buffett, UTGN is a private compounder trading at 0.84x reported book, with thinly-traded shares, modest dividends, and a buyback program funded out of philanthropy-adjusted retained earnings. The company is overcapitalized, regulators leave it alone, and an eventual liquidation or going-private transaction at or near book would deliver a clean ~20% pop. Small float means even modest accumulation can move shares.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The 10-K explicitly defines a "related party" group around Correll and First Southern Funding/Bancorp (which itself owns 95% of First Southern National Bank). UTG owns common stock and other interests in these affiliates, and the LLCs that hold real estate are nested under UG to "provide additional protection to the policyholders." Park Avenue Life Insurance Company (PALIC) coinsures substantially all of UTG's pre-1996 paid-up life book. The investment strategy — Level 3 LLCs at $89.2 million fair value, equity securities at $265.7 million, oil & gas royalties — is essentially impossible for an outside analyst to mark. The 2024 figures are restated. This is a "trust the operator" stock, not a screenable bank.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 4 | Closed-block insurer with concentrated equity/RE/royalty book |
| Moat | 4 | No franchise; just legacy reserves and Correll's allocation skill |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | $232.7M equity vs $491.4M assets; well-capitalized statutorily |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | P/B 0.84, P/E 11.4 on lumpy gains |
| Catalyst | 3 | None visible; no buyback urgency; no break-up |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Limited downside if marks hold; limited upside without catalyst |
| Business Quality | 4 | Premiums shrinking; earnings = mark-to-market |
| Executability | 3 | ~$195M cap, illiquid, controlled-company |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | LLC marks down 25% would erase the discount |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Equity-heavy book ties earnings to S&P-like exposure |
| **TOTAL** | **45** | Adjusted: 48/100 with insider-skin-in-game credit |

---
