LCNB.name: LCNB Corp.
LCNB.content:
# 22. LCNB — LCNB Corp.
## BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 64/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

LCNB Corp. is the holding company for LCNB National Bank, a Lebanon, Ohio-headquartered community bank with roughly $2.27 billion in average assets, $1.84 billion in deposits, and a wealth management division providing trust, fiduciary, and investment management services. Net interest income for 2025 was $70.4 million, producing a tax-equivalent NIM of 3.50% — a meaningful expansion from 2.91% in 2024 as fixed-rate assets repriced and deposit costs stabilized. EPS rebounded to $1.63 diluted on 14.09 million weighted average shares, against book value supporting a P/B near 0.86. Capital is healthy, with the company falling under the Basel III framework as a covered bank holding company. Net charge-offs were just $273 thousand in 2025 — extraordinarily clean credit.

The bear case starts with deposit composition: non-interest-bearing demand deposits averaged $468 million versus $1,374 million interest-bearing — only ~25% NIB, well below sticky-deposit franchises like Glacier or Hingham. Long-term debt of $104.4 million (down from $155 million) still represents real wholesale funding dependence. The 2024 acquisition of Eagle Financial Bancorp added complexity and goodwill that pressures tangible book. NIM expansion is rate-cycle dependent — if the Fed cuts further and asset yields lag, the 3.50% margin compresses. Ohio community banking is competitive (Park National, Civista, First Financial Bancorp), and LCNB has no obvious moat in deposit gathering. The wealth management line is meaningful but not disclosed at AUM-scale specificity that would let an analyst value it as a separate asset.

The bull case is the cleanest credit in the batch combined with a real franchise. NCOs of $273K on a ~$1.6 billion loan book is a 0.02% loss rate. ACL/loans rose to 0.80% from 0.70%, building cushion. Capital ratios remain well-capitalized with CET1 well above regulatory floors. The dividend has been maintained, the buyback program is in place, and the wealth management division provides fee income that smooths cyclicality. At 0.86x book and 10.2x earnings — with NIM still expanding off a low base and credit pristine — this is the kind of "boring quality" community bank that re-rates to 1.0–1.1x book in any normalization of regional bank sentiment. Eagle Financial integration should produce cost synergies in 2026.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

LCNB's 10-K reports the "ratio of allowance for credit losses to total non-accrual loans" at 763.88% — meaning non-accruals are essentially de minimis relative to the reserve. That's a tell that the credit book is exceptionally clean and management is reserving conservatively. The Wealth Management Division offers full trust administration and IRA management through LCNB National Bank, providing a fee-income stream rare among $2 billion community banks. Related-party loans to directors and officers are disclosed as ordinary-course with normal collectability — not the red-flag pattern of self-dealing seen at some smaller controlled banks. The Federal Reserve has authority to block dividends if capital is impaired; LCNB management explicitly references maintaining a target Tier 1 ratio before paying dividends.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Plain-vanilla Ohio community bank + trust |
| Moat | 5 | Local relationships; wealth mgmt sticky; deposits average |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | Well-capitalized; $104M long-term debt; CET1 ample |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | P/B 0.86, P/E 10.2 with NIM expanding |
| Catalyst | 6 | NIM expansion; Eagle synergies; wealth mgmt scale |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Downside protected by clean credit |
| Business Quality | 6 | NCO 0.02%; ROA modest; ROE building |
| Executability | 6 | $236M cap; reasonable liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Rate cuts compress NIM; CRE shock manageable |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Diversified Ohio markets; trust fees buffer |
| **TOTAL** | **64** | |

---

FNWD.name: Finward Bancorp
FNWD.content:
# 23. FNWD — Finward Bancorp
## VALUE TRAP RISK | Score: 52/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Finward Bancorp is the Munster, Indiana-based holding company for Peoples Bank, an Indiana community bank serving Lake, Porter, and surrounding counties along with adjacent Cook County, Illinois. The Wealth Management Group oversaw $416.9 million in assets under management at year-end 2025, up $24.0 million year-over-year. Stockholders' equity totaled $174.7 million, or 8.6% of total assets, producing a book value per share of $40.37 against ~4.3 million shares outstanding. Net income for 2025 was $8.1 million ($1.88 diluted EPS) on average assets of roughly $2.05 billion. Net interest margin expanded meaningfully from 2.54% to 3.00%. ROA was a soft 0.39% and ROE just 5.10% — well below community-bank averages and the source of the persistent discount to book.

The bear case is that returns on capital are subscale. ROA below 0.4% and ROE near 5% mean the bank earns about its cost of equity in good quarters and below in bad ones — the textbook definition of a "destroying value" community bank. The 10-K discloses a memorandum of understanding requiring prior regulatory approval before paying cash dividends — a meaningful red flag on regulatory standing and a constraint on capital return. ACL to nonperforming loans of 156.84% (vs. 123.10% prior year) is fine but not pristine. Total deposits were essentially flat at $1.755 billion, suggesting a stagnant deposit franchise in a competitive Chicago-suburb market. The 18.4x P/E reflects depressed earnings, not a premium.

The bull case is straightforward NIM normalization plus regulatory cleanup. NIM jumped 46 basis points to 3.00% in a single year — if it can climb another 50–75 basis points toward peer averages, EPS roughly doubles purely on operating leverage. Wealth Management at $416.9 million AUM provides fee income and optionality. Net charge-offs collapsed to just $187 thousand in 2025 from $2.06 million in 2024 — a 91% improvement signaling that the legacy credit issues that landed FNWD in regulatory crosshairs are clearing. At 0.86x book, the market is pricing continued mediocrity; if the MOU is lifted and the dividend resumes meaningfully, a re-rate to 1.0–1.1x book delivers 15–30% upside on top of NIM-driven EPS growth. The 2014 buyback authorization (50,000 shares) was tiny but symbolic.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The single most important disclosure is the regulatory memorandum of understanding requiring prior approval to pay cash dividends — a holding-company-level constraint that signals supervisory concerns and sharply limits capital return optionality. The bank's geographic footprint includes Cook County, Illinois (Chicago suburbs), exposing it to higher-risk CRE than a pure-Indiana lender. The drop in net charge-offs from $2.06 million to $187 thousand and the $782 thousand provision (versus $199 thousand prior year) suggests management is rebuilding reserves while losses normalize — a classic pre-recovery setup, but only if the macro cooperates.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Standard community bank + small wealth mgmt |

FNWD.analystSummary:
Finward Bancorp is the Munster, Indiana-based holding company for Peoples Bank, an Indiana community bank serving Lake, Porter, and surrounding counties along with adjacent Cook County, Illinois. The Wealth Management Group oversaw $416.9 million in assets under management at year-end 2025, up $24.0 million year-over-year. Stockholders' equity totaled $174.7 million, or 8.6% of total assets, producing a book value per share of $40.37 against ~4.3 million shares outstanding. Net income for 2025 was $8.1 million ($1.88 diluted EPS) on average assets of roughly $2.05 billion. Net interest margin expanded meaningfully from 2.54% to 3.00%. ROA was a soft 0.39% and ROE just 5.10% — well below community-bank averages and the source of the persistent discount to book.

The bear case is that returns on capital are subscale. ROA below 0.4% and ROE near 5% mean the bank earns about its cost of equity in good quarters and below in bad ones — the textbook definition of a "destroying value" community bank. The 10-K discloses a memorandum of understanding requiring prior regulatory approval before paying cash dividends — a meaningful red flag on regulatory standing and a constraint on capital return. ACL to nonperforming loans of 156.84% (vs. 123.10% prior year) is fine but not pristine. Total deposits were essentially flat at $1.755 billion, suggesting a stagnant deposit franchise in a competitive Chicago-suburb market. The 18.4x P/E reflects depressed earnings, not a premium.

The bull case is straightforward NIM normalization plus regulatory cleanup. NIM jumped 46 basis points to 3.00% in a single year — if it can climb another 50–75 basis points toward peer averages, EPS roughly doubles purely on operating leverage. Wealth Management at $416.9 million AUM provides fee income and optionality. Net charge-offs collapsed to just $187 thousand in 2025 from $2.06 million in 2024 — a 91% improvement signaling that the legacy credit issues that landed FNWD in regulatory crosshairs are clearing. At 0.86x book, the market is pricing continued mediocrity; if the MOU is lifted and the dividend resumes meaningfully, a re-rate to 1.0–1.1x book delivers 15–30% upside on top of NIM-driven EPS growth. The 2014 buyback authorization (50,000 shares) was tiny but symbolic.
FNWD.whatScreenCantSee: The single most important disclosure is the regulatory memorandum of understanding requiring prior approval to pay cash dividends — a holding-company-level constraint that signals supervisory concerns and sharply limits capital return optionality. The bank's geographic footprint includes Cook County, Illinois (Chicago suburbs), exposing it to higher-risk CRE than a pure-Indiana lender. The drop in net charge-offs from $2.06 million to $187 thousand and the $782 thousand provision (versus $199 thousand prior year) suggests management is rebuilding reserves while losses normalize — a classic pre-recovery setup, but only if the macro cooperates.

ENBP.name: ENB Financial Corp
ENBP.content:
# 24. ENBP — ENB Financial Corp
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Score: 72/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

ENB Financial Corp is the holding company for Ephrata National Bank, an OCC-chartered national bank founded April 11, 1881 and headquartered in Ephrata, Lancaster County, Pennsylvania. The bank serves the heart of Pennsylvania Dutch country with a customer base anchored by the Amish, Mennonite, and broader Plain communities — a rare deposit franchise built on multi-generational trust, low loan-to-deposit utilization, and exceptionally sticky non-interest-bearing demand accounts. As of December 31, 2025, total deposits stood at $1.873 billion, with non-interest-bearing demand at $649 million (~35% of deposits) — meaningfully better than most $2 billion peers. Book value supports ~5.69 million shares outstanding held by approximately 850 shareholders. Net interest margin expanded 32 basis points to 3.19% in 2025, and net income reached $21.6 million.

The bear case starts with the December 2024 acquisition of Cecil Bancorp (Maryland), which added $218.7 million in assets, $153.5 million in loans, and $187.4 million in deposits — meaningful integration risk for a bank with no recent M&A track record, and dilutive to the famously homogeneous Lancaster franchise. ENBP trades on the OTCQX (limited-volume), creating real liquidity friction for institutional buyers. The 2025 P/E of ~6.5 is partly distorted by a single-year boost from NIM expansion and a lower provision; normalized earnings sustain a higher multiple. Average non-interest-bearing deposit growth of just 3.6% suggests the deposit franchise is mature, not growing. Pennsylvania community banking faces headwinds from regional consolidation by Mid-Penn, Univest, and Penns Woods.

The bull case is one of the strongest deposit franchises in the small-bank universe trading at 0.87x book. Lancaster County's Amish/Mennonite community has the lowest loan demand and highest demand-deposit balances of any U.S. demographic — ENBP's 35% NIB ratio reflects this structural advantage. Net charge-offs were a trivial $92 thousand on a roughly $1+ billion loan book, indicating the credit culture is intact. With Cecil acquisition synergies still ahead and NIM having room to expand toward 3.4–3.5%, EPS could grow meaningfully into 2026. Dividends of $4.08 million on $21.6 million of net income (~19% payout) leave significant capital for buybacks under existing authorization. ENBP is the textbook below-book community bank with a real moat that screening data underweights.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The Lancaster County, PA customer base is genuinely differentiated. The Amish and Mennonite communities operate on cash-basis principles, hold large checking balances for farm and business operations, and transact through a small network of trusted local institutions — with Ephrata National being one of the dominant relationship banks for nearly 145 years. The bank also operates ENB Insurance, a full-service insurance agency offering commercial and personal lines, providing stable fee income. Average non-interest-bearing demand accounts grew $21.5 million in 2025, described as "the Corporation's cheapest source of funding for balance sheet growth." The Cecil acquisition extends the franchise into Maryland's Cecil County, geographically adjacent to Lancaster's southeast border.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Pure Lancaster County community bank with insurance |
| Moat | 8 | Amish/Mennonite deposit franchise; 144-year history |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Net income $21.6M; small bank holding company exempt from consolidated capital rules |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | P/B 0.87, P/E 6.5 |
| Catalyst | 7 | Cecil integration + NIM expansion |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Limited downside; clear upside |
| Business Quality | 8 | NCO $92K; 35% NIB deposits; ROE building |
| Executability | 5 | OTCQX limited liquidity; ~850 shareholders |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | Cecil integration stumble is the main risk |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Sticky deposits insulate from rate cycle |
| **TOTAL** | **76** | Adjusted: 72/100 for OTCQX liquidity friction |

MNSB.name: MainStreet Bancshares
MNSB.content:
# 25. MNSB — MainStreet Bancshares
## CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 65/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY

MainStreet Bancshares is the Fairfax, Virginia-headquartered holding company for MainStreet Bank, operating seven branches in the affluent Northern Virginia / Washington D.C. metro (Herndon, Fairfax, McLean, Clarendon, Leesburg, Middleburg, plus a leased D.C. branch). At year-end 2025 the company had $2.21 billion in total assets, $1.8 billion in net loans, $1.9 billion in deposits, and stockholders' equity of $218.6 million. Tangible book value per common share rose to $25.52 (from $23.77) against a market price of $20.36 — a 20% discount to tangible book. EPS rebounded sharply to $1.76 from a $(1.60) loss in 2024, as NIM expanded from 3.13% to 3.46% and the company began winding down its money-losing Avenu Banking-as-a-Service platform.

The bear case is that Avenu's pivot is incomplete and expensive. The 2025 financial technology segment expenses remain elevated due to "shutting down the segment, such as canceling contracts." Non-performing loans of $31.5 million plus $1.7 million OREO equate to 1.50% NPAs/total assets — meaningfully above peer averages (LCNB and ENBP run near 0.1-0.2%). The efficiency ratio of 74.15%, while improved from 110.85% in 2024, remains well above the ~55-60% community-bank benchmark. The bank operates only 6 offices with 171 FTE — a small operating base for a $2.2 billion balance sheet, suggesting the Avenu wind-down hasn't yet produced peer-level cost efficiency. Northern Virginia commercial real estate — particularly office near D.C. — faces secular headwinds from federal-workforce reductions and remote work.

The bull case is a clean cyclical-trough setup. Bank capital ratios are robust: Tier 1 leverage 13.28%, CET1 15.05%, total risk-based 16.08%. With Avenu shut down, 2026 reported earnings should reflect the underlying community bank only — a much cleaner story trading at 0.80x tangible book. NIM expanded 33 basis points in 2025 with further runway as the wholesale deposit mix rolls. Net charge-offs were 0.00% in 2025 versus 0.25% in 2024 — credit is healing. The $0.40 annualized dividend has been maintained through the loss year. Northern Virginia remains one of the wealthiest U.S. metros, and MainStreet's commercial banking franchise is genuinely differentiated. If NPAs work out and efficiency drops below 65%, 2026-2027 EPS could reach $2.50-3.00, supporting a re-rate well above tangible book.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The single most important narrative in MNSB's 10-K is the explicit decision to "pivot away from operating certain BaaS services." Avenu was MainStreet's strategic bet on Banking-as-a-Service — the segment burned cash, drove the 2024 loss, and ballooned the efficiency ratio. The 10-K confirms management is incurring one-time costs to cancel contracts and accelerate exit, meaning 2025 reported earnings still understate the run-rate of the core community bank. The company's geographic concentration in McLean/Fairfax/Leesburg places it adjacent to wealthy federal-contractor clients and Loudoun County data-center growth — both meaningful tailwinds offsetting D.C. office weakness. With FTEs already cut from 204 to 171, operating leverage on revenue growth into 2026 should be substantial.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | NoVA community bank + winding-down BaaS segment |
| Moat | 5 | Wealthy market; small deposit share vs Atlantic Union/Burke |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | TCE/TA 9.88%; CET1 15.05%; well-capitalized |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | P/TBV 0.80, P/E 13.2 on transitional earnings |
| Catalyst | 8 | Avenu shutdown completes; NIM expansion; NPA workout |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Discount embeds the bad year; upside on normalization |
| Business Quality | 5 | Efficiency 74%; NPAs 1.50%; improving fast |
| Executability | 6 | $171M cap; reasonable trading liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | NoVA CRE shock + Avenu wind-down overruns |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Tied to federal-workforce CRE dynamics |
| **TOTAL** | **64** | Adjusted: 65/100 for clean catalyst path |

FLX.name: BingEx Limited (FlashEx / Flash Express)
FLX.content:
# 26. FLX — BingEx Limited (FlashEx / Flash Express)
## CASH FORTRESS | Score: 56/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
BingEx, the China on-demand intracity courier branded "FlashEx," is a 2024 IPO that completed its float just as Chinese ADR sentiment cratered. The model is dedicated point-to-point delivery — one rider, one order, average completion in roughly 27 minutes — competing with Meituan Instashopping, Dada/JD Now, and SF Intracity for time-critical parcels (documents, gifts, groceries, restaurant supplies, business B2B runs). FY2024 revenue was RMB4,468.2 million (US$612.1 million) on 277.2 million orders fulfilled, up from 213.4 million in 2022. The screen flag — net cash of roughly US$929M against a US$183M market cap — is the headline; almost all of the IPO proceeds remain on the balance sheet, with consolidated cash plus short-term investments well in excess of current liabilities.

The bear case is straightforward: this is a low-margin gig-economy business in a fiercely contested segment where giants (Meituan, JD, Alibaba's Ele.me) are willing to subsidize delivery indefinitely as a loss-leader for higher-margin commerce. Gross margin sits in the low 10s, FY2024 swung to a small net loss after a brief 2023 profit, and volume growth has decelerated to mid-single-digit. Add the standard China ADR overhang — VIE contracts (the VIE contributed 18.5% of 2024 revenue, but more critically holds the operating licenses), Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act inspection risk, capital controls preventing offshore distribution of mainland cash — and the gap between book cash and shareholder economic value widens materially.

The bull case rests on the cash itself plus a credible management response. The board authorized a US$30M repurchase that was extended in March 2026; roughly US$9.2M had been deployed by mid-March 2026, ~2.8M ADSs retired. FY2025 returned to GAAP profitability (net income RMB109.4M, non-GAAP RMB199.4M — third straight year of non-GAAP profit) on RMB3,992.1M revenue, with gross margin expanding to 10.8% in Q4. If management accelerates buybacks, declares a special dividend out of offshore cash, or executes a strategic transaction, the discount can close. Until then, you own a cash pile with a real (if structurally challenged) operating business attached.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 20-F discloses cash mechanics that screens miss. Of consolidated cash, only the WFOE/VIE pools sit in mainland RMB; significant balances reside at the Cayman parent and Hong Kong sub from IPO proceeds — those are the dollars actually returnable to shareholders without SAFE approval. The filing also notes that "96.9%, 23.2%, and 18.5% of our revenues were contributed by the VIE" in 2022/23/24 respectively — the company restructured to reduce VIE revenue dependence, but the VIE still holds the China courier operating licenses, so the legal contracts remain load-bearing for compliance, not cash flow. The riders are independent contractors, not employees — labor-cost reclassification by PRC authorities is a tail risk that could compress margins overnight, similar to what happened to US gig peers post-AB5.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Simple intracity courier; metric (orders × ticket) clean |
| Moat | 3 | Network density vs. Meituan/JD; commodity price competition |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | NC ~US$929M vs MCap US$183M; ~5x cash coverage |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | EV is deeply negative on stated cash |
| Catalyst | 6 | $30M buyback extended; capital return is the unlock |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Downside = cash trap; upside = re-rating to book |
| Business Quality | 4 | 10% gross margin, slim net margin, decelerating |
| Executability | 5 | ADS float thin; some liquidity post-IPO |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | China subsidy war + VIE unwind = permanent discount |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Tied to China consumption + regulatory cycle |
| **TOTAL** | **56** | Cash dominates the thesis; quality trails |

---

HERE.name: Here Group Limited (formerly QuantaSing Group)
HERE.content:
# 27. HERE — Here Group Limited (formerly QuantaSing Group)
## CASH FORTRESS | Score: 51/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
HERE is the rebranded shell of QuantaSing Group, a Chinese online adult-learning company that has explicitly pivoted out of education and into pop toys. The 20-F is candid: "QuantaSing Group Limited is a pop toy company," operating the WAKUKU and other IP under the "HERE奇梦岛" brand following the March 2025 acquisition of Shenzhen Letsvan (Yiqi Culture). The company terminated its VIE contractual arrangements during 2025 and exited individual online learning. Ticker changed from QSG to HERE on Nasdaq in November 2025. FY2025 (June year-end) total revenue was RMB2,725.6 million (~US$380.5M), with FY2026 pop-toy revenue guided to RMB750–800M. The screen flag — net cash near US$700M against a sub-US$200M market cap — reflects accumulated education-era profits plus IPO proceeds, with FY2025 net income of RMB356.6M and a US$0.067/share special dividend already paid.

The bear case: the entire investment case is a category pivot from regulated ed-tech (with collapsing economics) into trend-driven plush collectibles (WAKUKU vs. Pop Mart's Labubu). Trend toy IP is fashion — high gross margins one cycle, unsold inventory the next. The reported cash is consolidated, but a substantial chunk sits inside the now-unwound VIE structure and at mainland subsidiaries; offshore distribution requires PRC SAFE approvals. The Letsvan deal added goodwill and minority interests; the legacy education business is being deconsolidated, so reported FY2025 numbers are not "normalized." HFCAA inspection risk remains. And consumer-facing pop toys give zero recurring revenue base — every quarter is an IP-by-IP execution test.

The bull case: the company has already returned cash (the FY2025 cash flow statement shows "cash paid for repurchase of the ADSs of RMB25.8 million... and cash paid for a special dividends of RMB78.1 million"). FY2026 pop-toy revenue guidance of RMB750–800M (vs. the consolidated 2025 base) implies the new business alone could approximate the market cap if margins normalize toward Pop Mart's. WAKUKU has reportedly crossed 1 billion organic digital impressions. With ~US$700M of cash and a clean exit from regulated learning, management has optionality — buyback, dividend, or M&A — that the share price doesn't credit.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 20-F notes the company "began to operate in 2025 along with the acquisition of equity interests in Shenzhen Letsvan" and that prior-period statements are "not directly comparable with or indicative of the performance of our current business." Translation: every historical line — revenue mix, gross margin, ROE, even the "ed-tech VIE" risk factor — is being rewritten. The 20-F also discloses that net cash used in financing for FY2025 was RMB105.6 million driven by ADS repurchases and the special cash dividend funded "by surplus cash and to be paid" — meaning offshore cash is reachable, the company has used it, and the precedent matters more than future capital allocation promises. The pop-toy P&L is also new, so revenue concentration in WAKUKU and 14 other IPs is a hidden Herfindahl risk.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 4 | Mid-pivot from ed-tech to pop toys; comp set unstable |
| Moat | 3 | IP-led trend toys; replicable, fashion-driven |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | NC ~US$700M vs MCap ~US$190M; EV/EBITDA ~0.9x |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | EV negative; pop toy upside optionality free |
| Catalyst | 6 | Letsvan ramp + capital return precedent |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Downside protected by cash, fashion risk on upside |
| Business Quality | 4 | Pop Mart-like gross margin if successful, fashion cyclicality |
| Executability | 5 | Float ok; rebrand may have shaken out passive holders |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Ed-tech regulation killed comp; toys could face the same |
| Macro Independence | 3 | China discretionary consumer; tariff / sentiment exposed |
| **TOTAL** | **51** | Cash + optionality, but identity crisis |

XYF.name: X Financial
XYF.content:
# 28. XYF — X Financial
## VALUE TRAP RISK | Score: 47/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
X Financial (Xiaoying) is a Shenzhen-based online consumer micro-lender that facilitates small-ticket unsecured loans (the "Xiaoying Card Loan" product) to underbanked Chinese borrowers via institutional funding partners. Loan facilitation amount grew from RMB73.7B in 2022 to RMB104.9B in 2024 to RMB130.6B in 2025. Disclosed FY2024 operating margin was 31.9%, net income margin 26.2%, ROE 24.1% — exceptional in absolute terms, which is why the screen flags a P/E of 0.9x and P/B of 0.16x. The company pays a dividend (a US$0.28/ADS semi-annual was declared with Q4 2025 results, payable May 2026) and has been buying back stock — share repurchase payable to a major shareholder ballooned in late 2024. On the surface this is the cheapest bank-like asset on a US exchange.

The bear case is rapidly worsening credit. Q4 2025 results showed delinquency rates 31–60 days past due rising to 2.90% from 1.17% a year earlier; 91–180 day delinquencies jumped to 6.31% from 2.48%. Net income dropped 85% YoY in Q4 to RMB57.2M as provisions absorbed the higher charge-offs. Total Q4 revenue fell 14% YoY and 25% sequentially. China's online consumer credit cycle has clearly turned, and XYF's borrower base — already at the lower-prime/subprime end — is exposed first. Regulatory overhang is also constant: PRC has been re-tightening online lending rules, capping rates, mandating institutional funding, and there is no shortage of headlines about defunct peer platforms. Valuation cheapness reflects regulatory tail risk and a credit-quality unknown — the standard "cheap because the earnings are about to disappear" trap.

The bull case rests on the management team continuing to actually return cash, plus the possibility that 2025 was the worst quarter of a normal cycle. The 20-F shows persistent ROE 18–24% over 2022–2024 even through tightening, the dividend is real, and management has incurred buybacks. If FY2026 delinquencies stabilize and the new conservative provisioning is sufficient, P/B 0.16x is absurd — even half of a normalized ROE on tangible book would imply multi-bagger upside. But this is the kind of name where being early is the same as being wrong, and you must underwrite the credit cycle, not the multiple.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 20-F discloses that "in 2016, 0.9% of our total loan facilitation amount were Xiaoying Card Loan, while in 2022, 2023 and 2024, such proportion was 99.8%, 100%, and 100%" — single-product concentration is total. The filing also flags institutional-funding dependence: virtually all loans are now off-balance-sheet, with funding partners taking the credit risk economically but XYF wearing reputational/regulatory risk of high delinquencies. The recent Q4 prints show provisions running ahead of delinquencies, which can be read two ways: prudent management or admitting that the back-book is impaired. Important: the company carries restricted cash of ~RMB749M (US$93M) as of FY2024 — that is risk-reserve money, not freely available for distribution.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Loan facilitation model; off-balance-sheet originator |
| Moat | 3 | Funding partner relationships, no real franchise |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | Net cash positive but restricted cash large |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | P/E 0.9x, P/B 0.16x absurd if earnings persist |
| Catalyst | 5 | Dividend + buyback continuing |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Cheap, but credit cycle accelerating against it |
| Business Quality | 4 | High ROE in 2024, but credit-cycle dependent |
| Executability | 5 | Liquid ADR, normal trading |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 3 | Delinquencies +2.5x YoY signal pre-impairment regime |
| Macro Independence | 4 | 100% tied to China consumer credit + regulation |
| **TOTAL** | **47** | Looks cheapest, may be cheap for fundamental reasons |

---

CGEN.name: Compugen Ltd.
CGEN.content:
# 10. CGEN — Compugen Ltd.
## BIOTECH BINARY  |  Score: 64/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $1,500 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
Compugen monetized a portion of its rilvegostomig royalty stream to AstraZeneca for $65M upfront (+$25M on BLA acceptance) in December 2025, extending cash runway into 2029 — and retained the majority of the royalty interest plus up to $195M in remaining milestones. With $145.6M cash on a $274M market cap, enterprise value is ~$129M for a partnered TIGIT discovery platform whose lead asset is in 11 ongoing Phase 3 trials run by AstraZeneca. The asymmetry: meaningful Phase 3 readouts are 2026–2027 events the market hasn't priced.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Compugen is an Israeli computational antibody discovery platform — its proprietary algorithms predict drug targets from genomic data, and the company validates and out-licenses them. The crown jewel is COM701/rilvegostomig: a TIGIT/PD-1 bispecific antibody licensed to AstraZeneca, now the most advanced anti-TIGIT asset in industry, with 11 Phase 3 trials across NSCLC, GI, and endometrial cancers. A second asset, COM902 (anti-TIGIT mAb), is partnered with Gilead. The screen captures Compugen on $142.7M net cash / 52% of market cap and a 7.6 P/E — the GAAP profitability comes from the December 2025 royalty monetization with HealthCare Royalty, not recurring operations. As of December 31, 2025, cash was $145.6M and management guides runway into 2029.

The bear case: TIGIT as a target has been a graveyard. Roche's tiragolumab failed multiple Phase 3s; Merck's vibostolimab data has been mixed; Gilead's domvanalimab is ongoing but watched with skepticism. AstraZeneca's rilvegostomig is the bispecific bet, but the broader class signal-to-noise is poor and a high-profile Phase 3 miss would compress the platform multiple immediately. Secondary: Compugen has limited control over partner-driven Phase 3 timing, the royalty stream Compugen retained is now diluted (sold a portion), and the company is a foreign private issuer (Israel) with semi-annual reporting cadence under the 20-F regime, plus regional geopolitical overlay.

The bull case is structurally compelling. The $65M HCRx payment is non-dilutive and validates the rilvegostomig royalty stream's NPV at well above current EV. Enterprise value of ~$129M values the residual royalty (mid-single-digit tiered), retained $195M milestone potential, the entire discovery platform, and the Gilead-partnered COM902 program at less than the price of a single Phase 3 monetization tranche. The 11 Phase 3 trials create a portfolio of binary readouts in 2026–2028; even one positive registrational outcome (especially in biliary tract or NSCLC) would re-rate the equity meaningfully. Cash to 2029 removes financing overhang, a critical de-risker for small biotech.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 7.6 P/E is entirely from the December 2025 royalty monetization upfront — no recurring product revenue exists. The $195M of retained milestones from AstraZeneca is contingent and not on the balance sheet, but represents the real value driver. The screen also can't see that Compugen is a foreign private issuer reporting under IFRS — the cash figure and "net cash" calculation differ slightly from US GAAP screen conventions. Most importantly, the rilvegostomig royalty rate is mid-single-digit tiered (Compugen sold a portion to HCRx but retained "the majority"); the precise royalty rate Compugen still owns is not fully disclosed, which is the single most important undisclosed number for valuation.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- AstraZeneca rilvegostomig Phase 3 interim or topline data (multiple trials, 2026–2027)
- Potential $25M HealthCare Royalty milestone on rilvegostomig BLA acceptance
- COM902 (Gilead-partnered) Phase 1/2 updates
- New AstraZeneca / BMS partnership extensions or additional discovery deals

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Computational discovery + TIGIT — specialized |
| Moat | 6 | Proprietary discovery platform; AZ + Gilead validators |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | $146M cash, runway into 2029, no material debt |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | EV ~$129M for partnered Ph3 + platform |
| Catalyst | 6 | Phase 3 readouts span 2026-2028 |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Cash floor + multi-shot Ph3 portfolio |
| Business Quality | 4 | Pre-revenue platform, partner-dependent |
| Executability | 7 | $274M cap, reasonable ADV |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | TIGIT class graveyard precedent |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Trial readouts dominate, regional overlay |
| **TOTAL** | **64** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $1,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Standard biotech binary weight nudged up for cash-runway-to-2029 (removes financing overhang) and the multi-shot Phase 3 portfolio. Capped below average given TIGIT class skepticism and partner-driven readout timing.

---

ABEO.name: Abeona Therapeutics Inc.
ABEO.content:
# 9. ABEO — Abeona Therapeutics Inc.
## BIOTECH BINARY  |  Score: 60/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $1,500 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
ZEVASKYN (pz-cel) FDA-approved April 28, 2025 — first and only autologous gene-modified cell therapy for RDEB wounds. Abeona ended 2025 with $191M cash (incl. short-term investments) on a $307M market cap, profitable for the year on the back of a $155M PRV sale ($152M net gain). The asymmetry: market is pricing in essentially zero commercial value while a real, approved orphan therapy is just starting its launch ramp.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Abeona is now a commercial-stage gene therapy company. The lead asset, ZEVASKYN (prademagene zamikeracel, "pz-cel"), is an autologous, gene-modified cell therapy for recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa (RDEB) — an ultra-rare, devastating skin-blistering genetic disease. The FDA approved ZEVASKYN on April 28, 2025; commercial launch began in Q4 2025 with the first commercial patient treated in December. Total 2025 revenue was $5.8M ($3.4M license + $2.4M product), reflecting only one commercial patient. The screen captures the name on $166M net cash / 54% of market cap and a 5.4 TTM P/E driven entirely by the $152.4M PRV sale gain — not recurring operations. Underlying operating burn remained meaningful (mid-double-digit millions annually).

The bear case: ZEVASKYN is a complex autologous therapy requiring biopsy, gene modification ex vivo, and graft application at qualified treatment centers. Each treatment is logistics-heavy and surgical — slow ramp is the base case. The addressable US RDEB population is roughly 750–1,000 patients, with maybe 200–300 with severe enough wounds to warrant the procedure (only ~30 patients were initially identified by the company). Reimbursement at the multi-million-dollar list price requires payer education for what is a one-time-per-wound therapy. Competition exists — Krystal Bio's Vyjuvek (a topical gene therapy approved 2023) addresses the same disease with very different administration. Real underlying loss without PRV is ~$80M annually; cash funds operations into 2027 base case.

The bull case: the entire enterprise value is roughly $140M ($307M cap minus $166M net cash) for an FDA-approved gene therapy in an ultra-rare disease with multi-million-dollar pricing power. If Abeona treats just 20–30 patients per year at peak, run-rate revenue would be $50–100M+ — and the launch infrastructure (treatment center activations) is the gating item, not approval or efficacy. Beyond ZEVASKYN, the AAV204 capsid platform offers preclinical optionality. The PRV sale already de-risked cash through the launch ramp window; another 1–2 years of treatment center activations and patient throughput data could justify a multi-hundred-million dollar revaluation if the launch executes.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 5.4 P/E is non-recurring — it's the PRV sale. Strip out the $152.4M one-time gain and Abeona is structurally loss-making until ZEVASKYN reaches commercial scale. The screen also doesn't show that "approved gene therapy with $191M cash" is fundamentally different from a Phase 2 binary: the binary is now operational (can Abeona activate treatment centers and execute the surgical workflow at scale?) rather than scientific (does the therapy work?). Treatment center activation pace and Q1/Q2 2026 patient counts are the actual leading indicators — not press releases. Underlying cash burn (~$80M/yr) means runway is roughly 24 months absent further milestones; that's the real clock.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 2026 earnings (May 2026) — first full quarter of commercial ZEVASKYN sales
- Treatment center activations and patient throughput updates
- Potential ex-US partnership announcements (EU regulatory path)
- AAV204 capsid platform preclinical / IND-enabling progress

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Gene therapy — knowable but technically complex |
| Moat | 7 | First-in-class FDA-approved RDEB cell therapy |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | $191M cash, no meaningful debt, ~24 mo runway |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | EV ~$140M for approved orphan therapy |
| Catalyst | 7 | Quarterly launch metrics, hard catalysts |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Cash floor + commercial upside |
| Business Quality | 4 | Pre-scale launch, large operating losses ex-PRV |
| Executability | 7 | $307M cap, decent volume |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Slow launch + Vyjuvek competition |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Disease-specific, not macro-driven |
| **TOTAL** | **65** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $1,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Approved-product biotech merits a half-step above the $1K binary minimum, but slow launch trajectory and competing Vyjuvek therapy keep it below the $2K average. Cash cushion supports the position size.

---

The bull case: the entire enterprise value is roughly $140M ($307M cap minus $166M net cash) for an FDA-approved gene therapy in an ultra-rare disease with multi-million-dollar pricing power. If Abeona treats just 20–30 patients per year at peak, run-rate revenue would be $50–100M+ — and the launch infrastructure (treatment center activations) is the gating item, not approval or efficacy. Beyond ZEVASKYN, the AAV204 capsid platform offers preclinical optionality. The PRV sale already de-risked cash through the launch ramp window; another 1–2 years of treatment center activations and patient throughput data could justify a multi-hundred-million dollar revaluation if the launch executes.
ABEO.whatScreenCantSee:
The 5.4 P/E is non-recurring — it's the PRV sale. Strip out the $152.4M one-time gain and Abeona is structurally loss-making until ZEVASKYN reaches commercial scale. The screen also doesn't show that "approved gene therapy with $191M cash" is fundamentally different from a Phase 2 binary: the binary is now operational (can Abeona activate treatment centers and execute the surgical workflow at scale?) rather than scientific (does the therapy work?). Treatment center activation pace and Q1/Q2 2026 patient counts are the actual leading indicators — not press releases. Underlying cash burn (~$80M/yr) means runway is roughly 24 months absent further milestones; that's the real clock.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 2026 earnings (May 2026) — first full quarter of commercial ZEVASKYN sales
- Treatment center activations and patient throughput updates
- Potential ex-US partnership announcements (EU regulatory path)
- AAV204 capsid platform preclinical / IND-enabling progress

ORMP.name: Oramed Pharmaceuticals Inc.
ORMP.content:
# 3. ORMP — Oramed Pharmaceuticals Inc.
## SUB-CASH OPERATING  |  Score: 58/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $2,000 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
A failed-Phase-3 oral-insulin biotech that has quietly transformed into a specialty financier. Oramed's "profitability" is an artifact: $89M of FY25 financial income from its Scilex notes, warrants and royalty deal — not from drug development. Net cash is ~80% of market cap, the company just paid a special dividend funded by Scilex repayments, and it is actively returning capital while shareholders still treat it as a binary biotech.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Oramed Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ: ORMP) is an Israeli holding company with two distinct lives. Drug-development life: oral insulin candidate ORMD-0801 missed both primary and secondary endpoints in the ORA-D-013-1 Phase 3 trial. The company plans a 60-patient U.S. trial in H2 2026 to validate "responder" subpopulations and has handed clinical management to a JV (with HTIT, though HTIT failed initial closing conditions) and to Lifeward (POD platform transferred January 2026). Investment-vehicle life: in 2023 Oramed lent $101.875M (Tranche A) to Scilex Holding; later added $25M Tranche B plus warrants and a 4% royalty on ZTLido. As of March 26, 2026, Scilex had repaid $69.2M of Tranche A and $13.0M of Tranche B; per company communications cumulative returns reached approximately $118M on the original $99.5M cost basis. FY25 revenue of $2.0M (versus zero in FY24), operating loss of $14.1M, but financial income of $89.5M produced reported net income of $64.0M.

The bear case is direct. The drug pipeline failed and is being de-prioritized in favor of small responder-subgroup trials; per the 10-K, "we expect to discontinue oral insulin clinical activities for T2D." The Scilex creditor exposure is concentrated and risky — Scilex itself has disclosed "substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern" in its filings. The 10-K explicitly warns "Scilex is unable to service its obligations under the Note and defaults on such Note, it could have a material adverse effect on our business." Operating cash burn of $9.1M in FY25 will continue. Working capital fell from $137.5M to $114.2M YoY — the cash pile is shrinking even as the company harvests Scilex repayments, because of the dividend payable and continued investments in Alpha Tau, Lifeward, and real-estate ventures (Hapisga, Profit Sharing Loan).

The bull case rests on the realisation pattern. Scilex has *actually paid* — $118M on $99.5M cost basis already, and Oramed retains roughly $27M of remaining notes and a $12M convertible note (convertible at $36/share at Oramed's option), plus the 4% ZTLido royalty perpetuity, plus warrants. That residual stack is essentially "free" if you're paying market cap below cash. The company has aggressively repurchased shares: 899,609 in 2025 at $2.39 average and a separate 1,155,367 from HTIT at $2.23 — both at significant discounts to NAV. Board-authorized $20M Buy-Back Program was extended to June 2026. A January 2026 cash dividend was announced after the Scilex Option Agreement payoff.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K's "Investments at fair value" section is where the alpha lives. Oramed elected the fair-value option for the Scilex notes "in order to reduce operational complexity of bifurcating embedded derivatives" — meaning the carrying value of those notes already reflects management's expected repayment scenarios, weighted across multiple paths. Translated: the $89M financial income line is part real cash interest and part mark-to-fair-value re-rating as Scilex actually pays. The company has also become a hidden real-estate financier — September 2024 Profit Sharing Loan Agreement and March 2025 Hapisga loan are property project finance, not biotech. April 2025 Alpha Tau equity stake and November 2025 $3M Lifeward secured note are further capital allocations away from the original drug thesis. Insider activity: a flurry of Form 4 filings in March 2026 (Section 16 transactions on March 16, 18 and 31, 2026) signals active trading by officers/directors — review these before sizing.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Remaining Scilex Tranche B amortization payments through October 2026 — additional ~$30M of expected cash inflow if paid on schedule.
- Special-dividend follow-on: management has shown willingness to declare cash distributions when Scilex repays.
- 60-patient responder-subgroup U.S. trial start in H2 2026 (binary biotech optionality, but *the cash already pays you to wait*).
- Continued buyback execution under the $20M program (extended to June 2026).

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Hybrid biotech/specialty finance is hard to model |
| Moat | 2 | None — failed Phase 3 erased the original moat |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | $114M working capital, no traditional debt, plus Scilex residual |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | ~80% cash to mcap; receivables stack on top |
| Catalyst | 7 | Remaining Scilex repayments, dividend policy, buyback |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Cash floor + free Scilex tail + free trial readout |
| Business Quality | 3 | Operating business loses money; this is a portfolio bet |
| Executability | 7 | Liquid enough for $2-3K position |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Scilex going-concern risk = creditor stress; cash burn continues |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Insulated from rates cycle but Scilex's distress is firm-specific |
| **TOTAL** | **58** | A liquidation-style biotech with collected receivables |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $2,000 (of $100K)
Rationale: Cash and Scilex-receivable cover protect downside, but ORMP is essentially a managed wind-down/repositioning — no compounding engine, just realization of existing assets. Sized to average position; speculative biotech component capped.

---

SITC.name: SITE Centers Corp.
SITC.content:
# 32. SITC — SITE Centers Corp.
## DISTRESSED ARB | Score: 58/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
SITE Centers is the residual real estate vehicle left over after the October 1, 2024 spin-off of Curbline Properties (CURB), which received the company's 79 convenience retail properties — the highest-quality, lowest-cap-rate slice of the portfolio. What remains at SITC is a thin and shrinking collection of larger open-air shopping centers, predominantly community/power-center format, in active disposition mode. The 10-K describes the entity as essentially executing a wholly-owned wind-down: management is "currently in various stages of marketing several wholly-owned assets," and the company expressly references the "anticipated winding up of its operations." Aggregate occupancy was 85.9% on a pro rata basis at 12/31/2025, down from 90.6% the year prior — a meaningful step-down that reflects both portfolio mix and tenant attrition.

The bear case is that the 1.6x P/E and 0.85x P/B are accounting artifacts, not fundamental value signals. Earnings have been inflated by gains on disposition; book value will continue to be eroded by any disposition prices below carrying value, plus continued G&A drag during the wind-down. The remaining centers are the harder-to-sell, lower-quality power-center boxes that institutional buyers are walking away from. The Mortgage Facility imposes ongoing financing friction, and the company has paid out substantial special dividends ($227.7 million increase in dividends in 2025), shrinking the equity base further. There is real risk that the residual NAV falls short of the current market quote once dispositions clear at realized prices, particularly if cap rates expand for off-mall power centers.

The bull case is a classic liquidation arbitrage: the market is mis-pricing a vehicle whose stated business is to monetize its remaining assets and return capital. If the disposition program clears at or near carrying value, the stub equity should distribute meaningfully more in special dividends than the current market cap implies. The 10-K confirms management is actively marketing assets and the Mortgage Facility provided liquidity to retire senior unsecured notes, simplifying the capital structure. Class A Preferred shares were redeemed for $175.0 million in 2024, which removes a senior claim. For a patient distressed-arb investor with a 12-24 month horizon willing to underwrite real-estate broker quotes, this is exactly the type of vehicle that screens hate but settles cleanly.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The post-spin SITC is structurally not a going-concern REIT — the 10-K openly references the "anticipated winding up of its operations," which most data providers do not flag. The remaining unencumbered asset pool is shrinking (the Mortgage Facility encumbers many of the better assets), so timing of dispositions matters more than reported FFO. The Shared Services Agreement with Curbline means a meaningful portion of the cost base is contractually tied to a separate company's operating tempo. Combined GLA, occupancy step-down to 85.9%, and ABR of $22.61/sf are below peer open-air averages, confirming the lower-quality residual. Dividend cadence going forward should be lumpy special distributions, not a steady REIT yield.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 6 | Wind-down REIT — straightforward but lumpy |
| Moat | 2 | Power centers — commoditized, weak pricing power |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | Mortgage Facility live; preferreds redeemed |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | 0.85x P/B with disposition catalyst |
| Catalyst | 8 | Active marketing of remaining assets; special divs |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Defined liquidation; downside = NAV haircut |
| Business Quality | 3 | 85.9% occupancy and falling — operationally weak |
| Executability | 7 | Liquid name; mid-cap REIT trading depth |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Disposition prices below carrying value = NAV gap |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Cap-rate and tenant-credit sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **58** | Stub-equity wind-down with measurable downside |

---

BDTX.name: Black Diamond Therapeutics, Inc.
BDTX.content:
# 6. BDTX — Black Diamond Therapeutics, Inc.
## BIOTECH BINARY  |  Score: 58/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $1,500 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
Net cash of $109.9M against a $167M market cap — the market is pricing BDTX-1535 (the only meaningful asset post-restructuring) at roughly $57M of enterprise value, with a Phase 2 readout in NSCLC already showing 60% ORR / 86% CNS ORR in 43 frontline patients. Cash runway extended into 2H 2028 after the Servier BDTX-4933 license. Asymmetry comes from the gap between in-line enterprise value and the addressable EGFR-mutant NSCLC market.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Black Diamond is a clinical-stage precision oncology company built around its MasterKey allosteric kinase inhibitor platform. After an October 2024 restructuring that cut headcount and refocused the pipeline, the entire investment case is now BDTX-1535 — a fourth-generation EGFR inhibitor designed for non-classical EGFR mutations and osimertinib-resistant disease. The screen flags it because the GAAP TTM net income (~$22M) is non-operating: it reflects upfront and milestone recognition from the Servier BDTX-4933 deal, not commercial revenue. The $109.9M net cash represents two-thirds of market cap, and the company guided runway into 2H 2028 as of the Q3 2025 print ($135.5M cash, equivalents, and investments).

The bear case is straightforward biotech binary risk. BDTX-1535 has shown encouraging early Phase 2 numbers, but durability of response (DOR) and progression-free survival (PFS) — the data that actually drive registration — won't read out until Q2 2026. The EGFR-mutant NSCLC space is crowded: J&J's Rybrevant (amivantamab) is approved and combined with lazertinib is the new standard of care challenger; Takeda's Exkivity was withdrawn from market in 2023 (a sobering precedent); sunvozertinib and furmonertinib are advancing as well. A miss on durability or competitive labeling tilts the equity toward cash value with optionality discount.

The bull case rests on three pillars. First, BDTX-1535 attacks a differentiated niche — non-classical / compound EGFR mutations and osimertinib-resistance — where current therapies underperform. Second, FDA feedback in Q4 2025 on a pivotal path could unlock a registrational design in 1H 2026, accelerating the value-recognition timeline. Third, downside is mathematically protected: $110M net cash on a $167M cap = $1.66/share of cash floor, so a clean clinical wash leaves ~30% downside before management discretion erodes it. The Servier BDTX-4933 milestone tail provides additional non-dilutive optionality.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 7.3 P/E is a mirage. TTM net income is dominated by Servier BDTX-4933 license revenue recognition — there is no recurring product revenue and operating cash burn is meaningful (the runway-extending impact of the Servier deal pushed cash from "into Q4 2026" guidance to "into 2H 2028"). The screen also misses that BDTX-1535 is NOT positioned in EGFR exon 20 insertion (where Rybrevant and the now-withdrawn Exkivity sit) — the company explicitly targets PACC/compound mutations and osimertinib-resistance, a smaller but less crowded niche. Finally, the restructuring shed the rest of the pipeline, so a single Phase 2 readout dictates equity outcome.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- BDTX-1535 frontline NSCLC updated DOR/PFS data — Q2 2026 medical meeting
- Initial Phase 2 data in newly-diagnosed non-classical EGFRm NSCLC — disclosed Q4 2025 (per company)
- FDA feedback on pivotal design and pivotal trial initiation — 1H 2026
- Potential Servier BDTX-4933 milestone payments (not disclosed by amount)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | EGFR oncology — knowable but specialized |
| Moat | 4 | MasterKey platform, but crowded EGFR space |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | $109.9M net cash, no debt, runway into 2H 2028 |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | EV ~$57M for de-risked Ph2 asset |
| Catalyst | 7 | DOR/PFS data Q2 2026 — clear date |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Cash floor + optionality |
| Business Quality | 3 | Clinical-stage, no product revenue |
| Executability | 6 | $167M cap, decent volume for $1.5K |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Single-asset binary, Exkivity withdrawal precedent |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Trial readouts dominate |
| **TOTAL** | **60** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $1,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Standard binary biotech weight. The cash cushion warrants a half-step above the $1K minimum, but the single-asset concentration and Q2 2026 readout binary keep it below the $2K average.

---

KINS.name: Kingstone Companies, Inc.
KINS.content:
# 34. KINS — Kingstone Companies, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Score: 71/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Kingstone is a small specialty insurance holding company whose operating subsidiary, Kingstone Insurance Company (KICO), writes personal-lines (predominantly homeowners) and commercial auto coverage primarily in New York, where it ranked the 12th largest writer of homeowners insurance in 2024. The defining catalyst is the Change in Market Dynamics: as Allstate, State Farm and other national carriers materially withdrew from the New York personal-lines market, Kingstone has been one of the primary admitted-market beneficiaries, picking up replacement policies at significantly improved pricing. The 2025 10-K reports net income of $40.8 million, compared with $18.4 million in 2024 — a $22.4 million improvement that reflects both rate adequacy and favorable market share dynamics. On April 14, 2025 KICO entered an agreement to offer replacement quotes to selected policyholders of a withdrawing competitor under a New York DFS-approved Withdrawal Plan.

The bear case is centered on tail risk and reserve adequacy. KICO's geographic concentration is acute: 98% of direct written premiums in 2025 came from New York policies (up from 96% in 2024), and Downstate New York exposes the book to coastal hurricane and noreaster losses that have historically blown up Kingstone's combined ratio. The reinsurance program (Swiss Re, Hanover Rück, Lancashire, SCOR, others) is high-quality but expensive, and pricing has hardened across all reinsurance towers in 2024-2025. Reserves on the legacy book of business — including the run-off of unprofitable policies that drove the 2022-2023 losses — could still develop adversely. Personal-lines insurance is also a regulated business; rate-increase approvals and regulatory scrutiny of the post-Withdrawal Plan growth could compress underwriting margins precisely when the reported numbers look best.

The bull case is a textbook insurance hard-market compounder. Combined ratio has been driven under 100, ROE is 43% on accounting numbers but trending toward a sustainable mid-teens to low-20s range as the new-business cohort earns out. The Change in Market Dynamics is a multi-year tailwind: dislocated competitors do not return overnight to a market they exited, and Kingstone retains the regulatory relationships, agent network, and underwriting infrastructure to absorb growth. The 12th-largest position in NY homeowners is meaningful share with room to expand. At 6.4x earnings and 2.18x book — high P/B for an insurer but justified by the ROE — and with policy-count growth running, this is the cleanest "right time, right place" thesis on the screen.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K specifically describes the Withdrawal Plan transaction with a competitor, which most equity screens will not capture as a discrete catalyst — this is regulator-blessed market-share transfer, not organic competition. The sale of the company's headquarters building to Ulster County (announced February 2025) generates one-time cash but also signals an asset-light operating model going forward. The reinsurance panel is concentrated among top-tier global reinsurers, which is positive for credit quality but means renewal pricing flows directly to underwriting margins. The Cosi Agency multi-state licensing creates an embedded option to extend the model beyond New York if and when the company chooses to redeploy capital geographically.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Simple monoline P&C insurer; readable book |
| Moat | 6 | Local NY underwriting expertise; agent network |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | Reinsured tower; reserve risk modest post-reset |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | 6.4x earnings against 43% ROE |
| Catalyst | 9 | NY Withdrawal Plan; Allstate/State Farm pullback |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Multi-year tailwind vs. single-storm tail risk |
| Business Quality | 7 | CR sub-100; ROE durably elevated |
| Executability | 7 | Small-cap liquid; institutional ownership rising |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Major hurricane = single-quarter loss event |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Cycle is hard market — independent of broader macro |
| **TOTAL** | **71** | Cleanest hard-market insurance compounder on list |

KINS.analystSummary:
Kingstone is a small specialty insurance holding company whose operating subsidiary, Kingstone Insurance Company (KICO), writes personal-lines (predominantly homeowners) and commercial auto coverage primarily in New York, where it ranked the 12th largest writer of homeowners insurance in 2024. The defining catalyst is the Change in Market Dynamics: as Allstate, State Farm and other national carriers materially withdrew from the New York personal-lines market, Kingstone has been one of the primary admitted-market beneficiaries, picking up replacement policies at significantly improved pricing. The 2025 10-K reports net income of $40.8 million, compared with $18.4 million in 2024 — a $22.4 million improvement that reflects both rate adequacy and favorable market share dynamics. On April 14, 2025 KICO entered an agreement to offer replacement quotes to selected policyholders of a withdrawing competitor under a New York DFS-approved Withdrawal Plan.

The bear case is centered on tail risk and reserve adequacy. KICO's geographic concentration is acute: 98% of direct written premiums in 2025 came from New York policies (up from 96% in 2024), and Downstate New York exposes the book to coastal hurricane and noreaster losses that have historically blown up Kingstone's combined ratio. The reinsurance program (Swiss Re, Hanover Rück, Lancashire, SCOR, others) is high-quality but expensive, and pricing has hardened across all reinsurance towers in 2024-2025. Reserves on the legacy book of business — including the run-off of unprofitable policies that drove the 2022-2023 losses — could still develop adversely. Personal-lines insurance is also a regulated business; rate-increase approvals and regulatory scrutiny of the post-Withdrawal Plan growth could compress underwriting margins precisely when the reported numbers look best.

The bull case is a textbook insurance hard-market compounder. Combined ratio has been driven under 100, ROE is 43% on accounting numbers but trending toward a sustainable mid-teens to low-20s range as the new-business cohort earns out. The Change in Market Dynamics is a multi-year tailwind: dislocated competitors do not return overnight to a market they exited, and Kingstone retains the regulatory relationships, agent network, and underwriting infrastructure to absorb growth. The 12th-largest position in NY homeowners is meaningful share with room to expand. At 6.4x earnings and 2.18x book — high P/B for an insurer but justified by the ROE — and with policy-count growth running, this is the cleanest "right time, right place" thesis on the screen.
KINS.whatScreenCantSee: The 10-K specifically describes the Withdrawal Plan transaction with a competitor, which most equity screens will not capture as a discrete catalyst — this is regulator-blessed market-share transfer, not organic competition. The sale of the company's headquarters building to Ulster County (announced February 2025) generates one-time cash but also signals an asset-light operating model going forward. The reinsurance panel is concentrated among top-tier global reinsurers, which is positive for credit quality but means renewal pricing flows directly to underwriting margins. The Cosi Agency multi-state licensing creates an embedded option to extend the model beyond New York if and when the company chooses to redeploy capital geographically.

OMSE.name: OMS Energy Technologies Inc.
OMSE.content:
# 8. OMSE — OMS Energy Technologies Inc.
## SUB-CASH OPERATING  |  Score: 70/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $2,500 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
Recent IPO (March 2025), a Singapore-domiciled oilfield equipment maker selling specialty connectors and surface wellhead systems primarily into Saudi Aramco under a 10-year supply agreement. $128.7M cash at end of H1 FY26 vs. $190M market cap = 67% cash, plus the company is profitably generating operating cash ($26.4M in H1 FY26). The underfollowed micro-cap discount is the asymmetry — institutional analysts haven't picked it up post-IPO.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
OMS Energy Technologies designs and manufactures premium connector systems and surface wellhead equipment for the upstream oil and gas industry. The company operates manufacturing in Singapore, Indonesia, and Saudi Arabia, and is qualified under Saudi Aramco's IKTVA (In-Kingdom Total Value Add) localization program — a structural moat that only a few non-Saudi suppliers have earned. The screen captures it via $119M net cash on a $190M cap (63% cash), a TTM P/E of 6.5, and $29M of TTM net income. H1 FY2026 (six months to September 30, 2025) revenue was $82.8M with $14.6M net profit. The H1 number declined vs. unusually strong prior-year comp driven by exceptional Saudi call-off volumes in H1 FY25.

The bear case starts and ends with customer concentration: Saudi Aramco accounted for 67% of FY25 revenue, and the long-term supply agreement allows termination on 30 days' notice. That is an extreme single-customer dependency in a region where geopolitical risk is non-trivial. Secondary risks: oil & gas capex cyclicality, lumpy call-off orders that complicate revenue forecasting (already visible in the H1 normalization), recent-IPO insider lockup expirations creating supply, and the small-float / illiquid trading that makes both entry and exit harder. As a foreign private issuer (20-F filer), disclosure cadence is also lighter than US issuers — only semi-annual financials.

The bull case: cash is nearly two-thirds of market cap and growing, the company is profitable, the Saudi Aramco relationship is structurally hard to replicate (IKTVA qualification took years), and a March 2026 $11M call-off order signals continued spending under the umbrella agreement. With $128.7M cash plus $61M of trailing operating leverage, the entire enterprise value is roughly $61M for a business throwing off $25–30M of net income. That is a sub-3x EV/earnings on a profitable, IKTVA-qualified specialty oilfield supplier with multi-year contractual visibility. Even with a haircut for customer concentration, this is a structurally cheap micro-cap.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three filing-level points the screen misses. First, the IKTVA qualification (and Indonesia's TKDN equivalent) is a regulatory moat — Aramco's localization program rewards qualified suppliers with preferred bidding rights, and de-qualification would require a multi-year unwind. Second, the H1 FY26 net cash growth came partly from $28.9M of IPO proceeds — strip those and operating cash generation is still positive but the runway extension is structural, not one-off. Third, OMS reports under IFRS as a foreign private issuer; "net cash" and "EBITDA" are calculated differently than US GAAP screens assume. Backlog disclosure is qualitative ("sizeable") rather than quantified — a notable gap.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- FY2026 full-year results (20-F) — expected July 2026
- Additional Saudi Aramco call-off orders under 10-year agreement (one already booked March 2026)
- Indonesia / Southeast Asia contract wins via TKDN qualification
- Potential first sell-side coverage initiation post-IPO seasoning

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Oilfield equipment — knowable |
| Moat | 7 | IKTVA qualification, multi-year Aramco SLA |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | $119M net cash, growing, no material debt |
| Margin of Safety | 9 | EV ~3x earnings ex-cash |
| Catalyst | 5 | No dated catalyst, just compounding |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Cash floor, profitable optionality |
| Business Quality | 6 | Decent margins; cyclical end-market |
| Executability | 4 | Thin volume, recent IPO; $2.5K fine but slip likely |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | 67% Aramco concentration is real |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Tied to oil capex cycle |
| **TOTAL** | **63** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $2,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Above average size on quantitative cheapness (sub-3x EV/earnings) and cash cushion, but trimmed from a higher allocation due to single-customer concentration risk and post-IPO float liquidity issues.

---

JILL.name: J.Jill, Inc.
JILL.content:
# 36. JILL — J.Jill, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Score: 67/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
J.Jill is a 256-store omnichannel women's apparel chain targeting an affluent customer aged 45+, with a heritage in relaxed, easy-to-wear fits and a roughly 50/50 split between brick-and-mortar and direct (catalog plus e-commerce). Fiscal 2025 (ended January 31, 2026) revenue was approximately $611M, essentially flat versus FY24, with reported net income near $39.5M and a net margin of about 6.5%. The company has just under 14.9M shares outstanding and trades at a single-digit P/E with a 25% reported ROE — figures that imply the market views the business as a melting catalog asset rather than a durable specialty retailer. Management has raised the quarterly dividend 12.5% to $0.09 and continued to invest in store openings and the New Hampshire distribution and contact center.

The bear case is that J.Jill's customer is loyal but old, the catalog channel is structurally declining, and apparel is a brutally cyclical, fashion-risk business with limited pricing power once promotional pressure resumes. Comps were essentially flat in FY25; capex jumped 33% to $14.3M, suggesting the easy free-cash-flow harvesting period of the post-2020 restructuring is ending. The 25% ROE flatters a business whose return on invested capital is lower once you strip out the depleted equity base and adjust for operating leases. A modest macro shock, a return to mall traffic decline, or a single bad season of inventory could compress margins by 200-300 bps and turn the P/E from 6.7x into a value trap.

The bull case is that JILL is a tightly run, niche specialty retailer with a defined customer, a high direct mix that gives it 1P data, and a balance sheet that has been deleveraged dramatically since the 2020 sale-leaseback restructuring. At ~$200-300M market cap on roughly $40M of net income, the stock is priced for terminal decline; any combination of stable comps, modest store-count growth, and continued buyback/dividend capacity unlocks a re-rating to 9-10x earnings. Tax assets remaining from the restructuring shield cash taxes, boosting reported FCF conversion. If management continues the dividend hike pattern and adds a buyback, total shareholder yield could reach 6-8%.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 520,000-sqft New Hampshire distribution and contact center is a single point of failure — it processed 28M units in FY25 (13M retail, 15M direct) and any disruption (storm, system outage, labor) would hit both channels simultaneously. The 14.9M share count is unusually small for a public retailer and creates real liquidity friction; a single index reconstitution or one large holder's exit could move the stock 10%. JILL also still carries a meaningful deferred tax asset position from the post-COVID restructuring that is not visible in headline P/E. Watch the gap between reported net income, cash taxes paid, and SBC — operating cash flow is what matters, not GAAP earnings. Lease liabilities (256 stores) are the real "debt."

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Specialty apparel retail — straightforward business model |
| Moat | 4 | Customer loyalty in 45+ niche; no structural moat |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | Deleveraged post-restructuring; lease liabilities remain |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | P/E 6.7, P/B 1.49 on 25% ROE — clearly cheap on earnings |
| Catalyst | 6 | Dividend hike, potential buyback, possible take-private |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Downside ~30%, upside re-rate to 10x = 50%+ |
| Business Quality | 6 | 6.5% net margin, double-digit ROIC ex-leases |
| Executability | 4 | ~$300M cap, 14.9M float — thin liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Aging customer + catalog decline = secular pressure |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Discretionary apparel highly cycle-sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **67** | |

FOA.name: Finance of America Companies Inc.
FOA.content:
# 37. FOA — Finance of America Companies Inc.
## DISTRESSED ARB | Score: 62/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Finance of America is the dominant U.S. reverse mortgage originator and servicer, with roughly 30% HECM (Home Equity Conversion Mortgage) issuance market share in 2025. The company emerged from a 2024 debt restructuring and is now profitable: 2025 net income of approximately $103M on revenue of about $497M, a sharp swing from prior-year losses. Adjusted net income of about $74M was up 429% year over year and adjusted EBITDA grew 138% to roughly $143M. The Retirement Solutions segment — proprietary and HECM reverse mortgages plus the emerging Home Equity Investment (HEI) product — is the entire forward thesis. Cash and equivalents grew to $90M from $47M in 2024. In late 2025 FOA announced an agreement to acquire PHH Mortgage's reverse mortgage servicing portfolio from Onity, and a $2.5B strategic partnership with Blue Owl that included a $50M equity investment. Blackstone's residual equity stake was repurchased in February 2026.

The bear case is that this is still a fair-value-accounted balance-sheet business whose reported earnings are extremely sensitive to interest-rate assumptions, prepayment speeds, and home-price appreciation embedded in the retained interests on securitized HECMs. A reverse mortgage originator carries massive gross balance-sheet exposure (HMBS-related obligations are tens of billions) against a tiny equity sliver — book value near $0.54x of equity reflects that the market does not fully trust the marks. The HEI product (shared appreciation in exchange for lump-sum cash) is unproven through a downturn; if U.S. home prices roll over, both retained HECM interests and HEI valuations re-mark down simultaneously. Consumer reverse-mortgage origination is regulator-sensitive and reputationally fragile.

The bull case is straightforward: post-restructuring, FOA is a now-profitable monopoly-like player in a structurally growing demographic market (aging boomers with home equity), and Blue Owl's $2.5B commitment gives it both capital and a credibility stamp. Trading near book at $188M market cap on $103M of GAAP earnings (P/E ~5.6x, P/B ~0.54), it is priced as if 2024's distress will recur. If reverse-mortgage volumes hold and HEI scales to meaningful size, the stock should clear book — implying ~85% upside before any earnings re-rating.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
GAAP earnings here are not cash. The retained beneficial interests in HMBS securitizations are Level-3 fair-value assets — the income statement gain on origination is partly an unrealized mark, and discount-rate or HPA-assumption changes can swing reported earnings sharply quarter-to-quarter. Read the 10-K's sensitivity tables on discount rates and prepayment assumptions. The Blue Owl partnership terms (warrants, preferred features, consent rights) materially affect common equity value and warrant close reading. The Blackstone equity buyback removed a structural overhang but used cash. Finally, HECM origination is dependent on FHA program continuity — any policy change to the HECM program (loan limits, mortgage insurance premiums, principal-limit factors) is a binary risk.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 4 | Reverse mortgage accounting is genuinely hard |
| Moat | 7 | ~30% HECM share, scale servicing platform |
| Balance Sheet | 5 | $90M cash but huge gross securitized obligations |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | P/B 0.54 with positive earnings |
| Catalyst | 8 | Blue Owl $2.5B partnership; PHH portfolio acquisition |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | 80%+ to book, downside if HPA rolls over |
| Business Quality | 5 | Cyclical, mark-sensitive, capital-intensive |
| Executability | 6 | Small float, post-restructuring overhang |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Housing downturn + FHA policy = double hit |
| Macro Independence | 3 | Rate- and home-price-sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **62** | |

---
