YALA.name: Yalla Group Limited
YALA.content:
# 5. YALA — Yalla Group Limited
## CASH FORTRESS  |  Score: 71/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $3,500 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
A genuinely profitable Middle East voice-chat and casual-gaming platform — Cayman holdco, Dubai-headquartered, NYSE-listed — with $754.6M of cash and short-term investments against $1.11B market cap (68% net cash) and a fresh $150M share-repurchase authorization announced March 9, 2026. Net margin 43.3% on $341.9M of revenue. The market is treating it like a melting Chinese ADR; the filings show a UAE-tax-domiciled, cash-generative platform with active capital return.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Yalla Group (NYSE: YALA) operates Yalla (voice-centric group chat), Yalla Ludo (casual gaming with in-game voice), YallaChat, WeMuslim, and a broadening portfolio of mid-core/hard-core titles across the Middle East and North Africa, principally Saudi Arabia and the UAE. FY25 revenue was $341.9M (+0.7% YoY), with chatting services $216.4M and games services $124.0M (the games line accelerated +9.1% YoY). Net income was $148.1M (+10.4%), net margin 43.3%; non-GAAP net income $158.5M. Average MAUs grew 8.2% YoY to 44.8M in Q4 25, while paying users *fell* from 12.3M to 10.4M because of fewer third-party-payment promotional events. The balance sheet at year-end 2025: $527.0M cash + $1.6M restricted + $84.8M term deposits + $141.3M short-term investments = $754.6M total liquid; total liabilities just $94.2M (most of which is deferred revenue). Cumulative buybacks under the prior program reached 15.7M ADSs for $106M; in 2025 alone, $56.6M was returned. A new $150M, 24-month authorization began March 9, 2026.

The bear case starts with regulatory geography. The 20-F is explicit: VoIP services in the UAE can technically only operate in narrow circumstances under a "Closed Group Network," and provision of unlicensed VoIP services is "a criminal offense that may be penalized by a fine between AED50,000 (US$13,615) and AED1,000,000 (US$272,294) and/or imprisonment of up to two years." Regulators rely on local telecom carriers blocking unlicensed services rather than prosecuting users — a fragile equilibrium. Founder/CEO Tao Yang controls the Class B block (24.7M shares) with 10:1 votes; minority holders cannot dictate strategy. Revenue growth is a rounding error (0.7%) and Q1 26 guidance ($75M-$82M) implies a YoY decline given Ramadan timing. Paying-user attrition (-15% YoY) is a structural concern even as MAUs grow. The China-incorporated parent and Cayman holding-company structure introduce China-VIE-style perception risk despite operations being UAE-centric.

The bull case: this is a profitable, cash-rich operating business at sub-cash on operating earnings power. EV (mcap minus net cash, ex-buyback impact) is ~$360M against $148M of net income — a sub-3x EV/earnings multiple on a 43%-net-margin platform with eight years of operating history in the world's fastest-growing youth demographic. Management is *actually* returning cash: $56.6M repurchased in 2025 + $150M newly authorized = $206M of demonstrated/promised capital return on a $1.11B market cap (~18%). Saudi Arabia presence is being formalized with a Riyadh office and a Saudi eLeague partnership — local strategic positioning that is hard to replicate. UAE corporate-tax rate cuts already pulled the FY25 effective tax rate from 9.4% to 2.6%.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three filing-level items. (1) Deferred revenue dropped from $58.1M to $55.2M (current) but a new $1.8M non-current deferred revenue line appeared — a small but novel monetization shift. (2) "Long-term investments" of $82.0M sit alongside the $754.6M of "near-cash" — these are wealth-management-product positions whose mark-to-market drives the lumpy "Investment (loss) income" line ($3.3M income FY25 vs. $2.8M loss FY24). The actual cash story is therefore slightly less liquid than the screen flag suggests. (3) Share-based compensation fell sharply from $14.7M to $10.3M YoY — a discipline signal that supports the dilution-management story behind the buyback program. (4) The company decided to *cancel* all 6.23M ADSs repurchased in 2025 — meaning these are *retired*, not held in treasury for re-issuance, which permanently lifts EPS and stops the "buyback only to fund SBC" complaint.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Execution of the new $150M share repurchase program (24-month window from March 9, 2026).
- Q1 2026 results (~May 2026) — first quarter post-Ramadan, plus launch of "desert-themed SLG title" in Q2 26.
- Saudi eLeague 2026 traction and any monetization tied to the Saudi Esports Federation partnership.
- Continued tax-rate compression as the UAE preferential-rate subsidiary regime matures (tax expense already $4.0M FY25 vs. $13.9M FY24).

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 6 | MENA voice-chat consumer behavior is regionally specific |
| Moat | 6 | Network effects in a culturally specific niche; Saudi/UAE hard to replicate |
| Balance Sheet | 10 | $754.6M liquid, $94M total liabilities, no debt |
| Margin of Safety | 9 | EV/earnings ~3x; sub-cash on a 43%-net-margin business |
| Catalyst | 8 | $150M new buyback; Saudi expansion; SLG title launch |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Cash floor; high-margin ops are essentially free |
| Business Quality | 8 | 36% operating margin, ROE high, but flat revenue growth |
| Executability | 9 | ADV >$10M; easy in/out at $3-5K |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | UAE VoIP licensing is a real overhang; controlled-co structure |
| Macro Independence | 3 | MENA economy + oil/gas + regulatory regime = correlated risks |
| **TOTAL** | **71** | Cash-rich, cash-returning operator at distressed multiples |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $3,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Highest-conviction name in the batch — combination of ample liquidity, demonstrated capital return, and a sub-3x EV/earnings multiple justifies above-average sizing. Sized below maximum because of UAE VoIP regulatory tail risk and Tao Yang's controlled-company structure.

YALA.analystSummary:
Yalla Group (NYSE: YALA) operates Yalla (voice-centric group chat), Yalla Ludo (casual gaming with in-game voice), YallaChat, WeMuslim, and a broadening portfolio of mid-core/hard-core titles across the Middle East and North Africa, principally Saudi Arabia and the UAE. FY25 revenue was $341.9M (+0.7% YoY), with chatting services $216.4M and games services $124.0M (the games line accelerated +9.1% YoY). Net income was $148.1M (+10.4%), net margin 43.3%; non-GAAP net income $158.5M. Average MAUs grew 8.2% YoY to 44.8M in Q4 25, while paying users *fell* from 12.3M to 10.4M because of fewer third-party-payment promotional events. The balance sheet at year-end 2025: $527.0M cash + $1.6M restricted + $84.8M term deposits + $141.3M short-term investments = $754.6M total liquid; total liabilities just $94.2M (most of which is deferred revenue). Cumulative buybacks under the prior program reached 15.7M ADSs for $106M; in 2025 alone, $56.6M was returned. A new $150M, 24-month authorization began March 9, 2026.

The bear case starts with regulatory geography. The 20-F is explicit: VoIP services in the UAE can technically only operate in narrow circumstances under a "Closed Group Network," and provision of unlicensed VoIP services is "a criminal offense that may be penalized by a fine between AED50,000 (US$13,615) and AED1,000,000 (US$272,294) and/or imprisonment of up to two years." Regulators rely on local telecom carriers blocking unlicensed services rather than prosecuting users — a fragile equilibrium. Founder/CEO Tao Yang controls the Class B block (24.7M shares) with 10:1 votes; minority holders cannot dictate strategy. Revenue growth is a rounding error (0.7%) and Q1 26 guidance ($75M-$82M) implies a YoY decline given Ramadan timing. Paying-user attrition (-15% YoY) is a structural concern even as MAUs grow. The China-incorporated parent and Cayman holding-company structure introduce China-VIE-style perception risk despite operations being UAE-centric.

The bull case: this is a profitable, cash-rich operating business at sub-cash on operating earnings power. EV (mcap minus net cash, ex-buyback impact) is ~$360M against $148M of net income — a sub-3x EV/earnings multiple on a 43%-net-margin platform with eight years of operating history in the world's fastest-growing youth demographic. UAE corporate-tax rate cuts already pulled the FY25 effective tax rate from 9.4% to 2.6%.

BMBN.name: Benchmark Bankshares, Inc.
BMBN.content:
# 39. BMBN — Benchmark Bankshares, Inc.
## BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 70/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Benchmark Bankshares is the holding company for Benchmark Community Bank, a 1971-founded Virginia community bank headquartered in Kenbridge, Virginia, with approximately 18 branches across central Southside Virginia and northern North Carolina. Fiscal 2025 was a record year: net income of $19.8M ($4.46/share) versus $15.9M ($3.55) in 2024, a 25.2% increase driven by balance-sheet growth, NIM expansion, and an improved efficiency ratio. Total assets reached $1.4B (up 11.7%), loans held for investment $1.1B (up 13.5%), deposits $1.2B (up 11.2%), and shareholders' equity $126.9M (up 15.9%). The annualized dividend is $0.88/share for a yield of approximately 2.8%, with a payout ratio near 20.5%. Beta is approximately 0.17, reflecting the OTC, illiquid, deeply-held shareholder base typical of small-town Virginia community banks. P/E ~8.4x, P/B ~1.35x, ROE ~16%.

The bear case is liquidity and disclosure. BMBN deregistered with the SEC long ago — the latest EDGAR filings on file are from 2003-2004 — and now reports through state and OTC channels rather than full SEC 10-K cadence. That makes deep diligence harder for outside investors and creates a permanent valuation discount. Loan growth of 13.5% in a single year is fast for a community bank and, in a flat-to-down credit cycle, can mask coming asset-quality issues; with no detailed NPL/charge-off data in mainstream sources, you are buying on faith. Geographic concentration in rural southern Virginia exposes the bank to a narrow agricultural and small-business customer base. OTC float means a single seller can move the price 5-10% on any given day.

The bull case is exactly the boring, durable kind of community bank Munger would have admired: 50+ years in business, recognizable hometown brand, conservative balance sheet, growing book value, paying a meaningful dividend, and trading at modest multiples. ROE of 16% with a 20% payout ratio implies the retained earnings can compound book at 12-13% for years if credit holds. At 1.35x book, you're paying a slight premium but receiving genuine quality and underwriting discipline that has survived multiple cycles. The illiquidity discount is the price of admission, not a defect.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Because BMBN reports primarily through call reports and the OTC Markets disclosure channel rather than SEC 10-K, you should pull the FFIEC Call Report directly for granular detail: NIM, efficiency ratio, NPL ratio, allowance/loans, Tier 1 capital, CRE concentration, and uninsured-deposit ratio. Local press (Kenbridge Victoria Dispatch, Farmville Herald) has been the primary disclosure venue for headline earnings. Insider ownership is concentrated in long-tenured Virginia families; this is functionally a closely-held bank with public-market liquidity tacked on. Trading volume can be zero on many days. Tangible book per share is roughly $28-29 based on $126.9M equity / 4.4M shares, but verify against the Call Report.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Plain-vanilla rural community bank |
| Moat | 7 | Local relationships, 50+ year brand in rural VA |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | $1.4B assets, $127M equity, conservative |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | 1.35x P/B is fair, not deeply cheap |
| Catalyst | 4 | None obvious; compounding quietly |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Dividend + book growth ~15% IRR base case |
| Business Quality | 8 | 16% ROE, low payout, growing |
| Executability | 3 | OTC, illiquid, deregistered SEC reporting |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | CRE/Ag concentration; rapid loan growth = credit risk later |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Rural VA economy; rate-sensitive NIM |
| **TOTAL** | **70** | |

---

GDEV.name: GDEV Inc.
GDEV.content:
# 14. GDEV — GDEV Inc.
## SUB-CASH OPERATING  |  Score: 62/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $1,500 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
A Cyprus-domiciled mobile gaming holdco (the former Russian "Nexters"), 39% of its $289M market cap in cash, P/E ~4.2, and just declared a $3.31 special dividend in February 2025 (~$60M, ~20% yield at the time). Hero Wars revenue is in decline (bookings -13% in 2025) and the entire investment thesis hinges on whether the new Pixel Gun 2 launch in early 2026 stabilizes the franchise. Optionality at a deep discount, with capital-return precedent.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
GDEV is a mobile games holding company headquartered in Limassol, Cyprus, which evolved from Nexters in June 2023 after relocating away from Russia. Its primary asset is Hero Wars (cumulative $1.7B revenue, 185M+ installs, ~93% of 2025 revenue). Screen flagged it for net cash ~$112M against $289M mcap (39%), TTM net income ~$69M, and a P/E of 4.2. Q3 2025 revenue $98M (-12% YoY); 9-month revenue $315M vs. $323M prior year. But profit improved sharply: 9-month profit went from ~$24M to ~$55M as marketing pulled back to a smaller, higher-value paying audience. EBITDA $64M for first nine months. February 2025: special dividend of $3.31/share (~$60M). Remains debt-free.

The bear case is structural. Hero Wars bookings down 4% in 2024, down 13% in 2025; monthly paying users down 18% in 2025. The franchise is harvesting, not growing — and 93% revenue concentration in one game is a single-asset risk on a depleting asset. The Russian-heritage problem is not fully behind the company: even after relocation, sanctions risk, key-person risk, and reputational discounts remain. The special dividend was explicitly described as one-time — management said no recurring dividends are anticipated. The earlier 2023 buyback authorization (up to 20M shares at $2) has been more theatrical than executed. New game pipeline is thin — Pixel Gun 2 is the headline launch, but mobile game launches have notoriously low hit rates and 2026 timing creates execution risk. Cash gets dividended out faster than it's reinvested.

The bull case: even after the $60M special dividend, ~$112M cash remains (39% of mcap). Ex-cash, the operating business trades at ~2.5x earnings on a profitable, FCF-positive game studio that just expanded margins by being more disciplined on UA spend. Pixel Gun is a real franchise — Pixel Gun 3D has 300M+ installs and $230M+ in lifetime bookings; a successful sequel launch in early 2026 could re-rate the equity meaningfully. Multi-platform launch (iOS, Android, Steam, then console) widens monetization beyond pure mobile. Catalyst: Pixel Gun 2 launch + potential second special dividend if cash continues to accumulate. Cyprus jurisdiction is now stable; sanctions tail-risk has shrunk meaningfully since 2022.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The screen sees "P/E 4, 39% cash" and concludes "cheap." It can't see that Hero Wars is in managed decline — the company is deliberately running it for cash, not growth. That's actually a feature, not a bug, if you trust management to return the cash. The 2025 special dividend ($60M) was the proof point: this is not a Russian-style "trapped cash" story, it's a Cyprus holdco that distributes when it can. The filings also reveal that 2025 profit grew sharply ($55M vs. $24M YTD) by cutting unprofitable user-acquisition spend — a sign of mature, disciplined management. Pixel Gun 2 is hidden upside the screen ignores entirely; a hit shifts the franchise mix away from sole Hero Wars dependency.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Pixel Gun 2 launch (early 2026 — iOS, Android, Steam)
- Possible second special dividend if cash continues to accumulate
- Stabilization or sequential growth in Hero Wars bookings (would re-rate the multiple)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 6 | Mobile games — hit-driven, hard to forecast |
| Moat | 4 | Hero Wars IP fading; Pixel Gun has scale but not durable moat |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | $112M net cash (post-dividend), no debt, 39% of mcap |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | ~2.5x ex-cash earnings; cushioned but melting |
| Catalyst | 7 | Pixel Gun 2 launch is a real binary event |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Cash floor + Pixel Gun upside outweighs Hero Wars decline |
| Business Quality | 5 | 93% single-game concentration on a declining title |
| Executability | 5 | Small float, low liquidity, listing-risk perception |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Pixel Gun 2 flops + Hero Wars keeps melting → no growth |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Game spend less cyclical than enterprise software |
| **TOTAL** | **62** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $1,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Speculative — capital return precedent and Pixel Gun 2 optionality are real, but single-franchise dependence and Russian-heritage perception cap sizing. Treat as a binary with hard cash floor.

---

GENC.name: Gencor Industries, Inc.
GENC.content:
# 4. GENC — Gencor Industries, Inc.
## CASH FORTRESS  |  Score: 60/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $2,000 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
A 60-year-old, family-controlled Florida maker of asphalt-plant equipment with $136M of cash and marketable securities (cash 68% of market cap), zero debt, and a backlog that just rebuilt to $57.4M as of Q1 fiscal 2026 from a December trough of $23.6M. The bear case (IIJA expiration September 30, 2026) is real but priced in; the bull case is a profitable cyclical with a fortress balance sheet, a chairman transition, and a board with no excuse not to return capital — except its long-stated refusal to do so.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Gencor Industries (NYSE-American: GENC) designs and manufactures hot-mix asphalt plants, soil-remediation equipment and related combustion systems, sold to highway contractors in the U.S. and Canada. Fiscal 2025 (year ended September 30, 2025) net revenue was $115.4M (+2.0% YoY), gross profit $31.7M (27.5% margin), operating income $14.0M, and net income $15.7M ($1.07 diluted EPS). The screen flag — net cash $147M against market cap $216M (68% ratio) — is conservative: the 10-K states "the Company had $26,587,000 in cash and cash equivalents, and $109,714,000 in marketable securities" plus "working capital was $197.7 million." Marketable securities are managed by a third-party investment firm and include corporate bonds, ETFs, mutual funds, equities and government securities. 318 full-time employees, no major union exposure outside Marquette, Iowa. The company is controlled by the Elliott family: Class B holders elect 75% of the board, and EJ Elliott alone owns 87.9% of Class B (plus 13.3% of Common); his son Marc Elliott (now Chairman effective January 1, 2026) holds another 8.3% of Class B.

The bear case is well-laid-out in the 10-K. First, "the Company has identified material weaknesses in its internal control over financial reporting" — an explicit ICFR failure as of September 30, 2025. That is an audit-committee level red flag in any size company. Second, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act "is scheduled to expire on September 30, 2026"; backlog already collapsed from $72.2M (Sept 2024) to $28.2M (Sept 2025) before partially recovering to $57.4M in Q1 fiscal 2026. Third, the Company "does not expect to pay cash dividends for the foreseeable future" and the Class B/Class A structure means the Elliott family fully controls capital allocation — minorities have no leverage. Fourth, marketable-securities exposure to equities and ETFs introduces realized/unrealized P&L volatility ($2.0M+ historically) into reported earnings.

The bull case: $136M of liquid securities + $26.6M cash on $216M of market cap means the *operating business* is being valued at roughly $80M for a company that did $14M of operating income and $15.7M of net income — a sub-6x EV/EBIT for a profitable, debt-free industrial. IIJA reauthorization is the dominant catalyst; even partial reauthorization would re-rate the multiple. The new chairman (Marc Elliott) has the latitude to re-think capital return policy after his father's December 2025 retirement from the board. Even without a dividend, the cash hoard compounds at Treasury yields and provides downside protection through the cyclical valley.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K bears two filing-specific items most screens miss. (1) Customer concentration: in fiscal 2024 "one customer accounted for 11.3% of net revenue" — concentration that briefly disappeared in fiscal 2025 (no >10% customer) but is structural to a market with a small number of large highway contractors. (2) Revenue is recognized "over time when the performance obligation is satisfied by transferring control of the equipment" with "revenues and costs recognized in proportion to actual labor costs incurred" — a percentage-of-completion methodology that creates earnings volatility and makes the material-weakness ICFR finding more concerning than at a typical reseller. Third, the marketable-securities portfolio is *actively managed by an external manager* — meaning a chunk of the "cash" is exposed to equity beta and credit risk. Insider ownership: Elliott family + officers + Foundation = 17.3% of Common and 100% of Class B — outside holders cannot force capital return.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- IIJA reauthorization or successor-bill negotiations through Q3 calendar 2026 — the single largest event for the entire customer base.
- Q2 fiscal 2026 10-Q (~April-May 2026) — backlog trajectory after the $57.4M Q1 print.
- Material-weakness remediation update; failure to remediate could attract activist attention.
- Marc Elliott's first full year as Chairman — any change in dividend or buyback posture would be a substantial re-rating event.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Single-product equipment maker; cyclical but legible |
| Moat | 5 | Modest — niche scale, brand, but commoditized end product |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | $26.6M cash + $109.7M marketable securities, no debt |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | EV/EBIT ~5-6x ex-cash; downside cushioned by securities |
| Catalyst | 5 | IIJA binary; capital-return optionality unlikely in near term |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Limited downside; upside capped by capital-allocation inertia |
| Business Quality | 6 | 27.5% gross margin, ~12% operating margin, 13.4% ROE |
| Executability | 6 | $200K-300K ADV; sized $1.5-3K positions only |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Material-weakness ICFR; controlled-co with no dividend |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Direct exposure to federal infrastructure funding |
| **TOTAL** | **60** | Cash fortress at the wrong moment in the cycle |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $2,000 (of $100K)
Rationale: Standard-size position; balance-sheet protection limits downside but the IIJA cliff and the Elliott family's history of hoarding cash cap the rerating thesis. Liquidity is the binding constraint.

---
GENC.analystSummary:
Gencor Industries (NYSE-American: GENC) designs and manufactures hot-mix asphalt plants, soil-remediation equipment and related combustion systems, sold to highway contractors in the U.S. and Canada. Fiscal 2025 (year ended September 30, 2025) net revenue was $115.4M (+2.0% YoY), gross profit $31.7M (27.5% margin), operating income $14.0M, and net income $15.7M ($1.07 diluted EPS). The screen flag — net cash $147M against market cap $216M (68% ratio) — is conservative: the 10-K states "the Company had $26,587,000 in cash and cash equivalents, and $109,714,000 in marketable securities" plus "working capital was $197.7 million." Marketable securities are managed by a third-party investment firm and include corporate bonds, ETFs, mutual funds, equities and government securities. 318 full-time employees, no major union exposure outside Marquette, Iowa. The company is controlled by the Elliott family: Class B holders elect 75% of the board, and EJ Elliott alone owns 87.9% of Class B (plus 13.3% of Common); his son Marc Elliott (now Chairman effective January 1, 2026) holds another 8.3% of Class B.

The bear case is well-laid-out in the 10-K. First, "the Company has identified material weaknesses in its internal control over financial reporting" — an explicit ICFR failure as of September 30, 2025. That is an audit-committee level red flag in any size company. Second, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act "is scheduled to expire on September 30, 2026"; backlog already collapsed from $72.2M (Sept 2024) to $28.2M (Sept 2025) before partially recovering to $57.4M in Q1 fiscal 2026. Third, the Company "does not expect to pay cash dividends for the foreseeable future" and the Class B/Class A structure means the Elliott family fully controls capital allocation — minorities have no leverage. Fourth, marketable-securities exposure to equities and ETFs introduces realized/unrealized P&L volatility ($2.0M+ historically) into reported earnings.

The bull case: $136M of liquid securities + $26.6M cash on $216M of market cap means the *operating business* is being valued at roughly $80M for a company that did $14M of operating income and $15.7M of net income — a sub-6x EV/EBIT for a profitable, debt-free industrial. IIJA reauthorization is the dominant catalyst; even partial reauthorization would re-rate the multiple. The new chairman (Marc Elliott) has the latitude to re-think capital return policy after his father's December 2025 retirement from the board. Even without a dividend, the cash hoard compounds at Treasury yields and provides downside protection through the cyclical valley.
GENC.whatScreenCantSee:
The 10-K bears two filing-specific items most screens miss. (1) Customer concentration: in fiscal 2024 "one customer accounted for 11.3% of net revenue" — concentration that briefly disappeared in fiscal 2025 (no >10% customer) but is structural to a market with a small number of large highway contractors. (2) Revenue is recognized "over time when the performance obligation is satisfied by transferring control of the equipment" with "revenues and costs recognized in proportion to actual labor costs incurred" — a percentage-of-completion methodology that creates earnings volatility and makes the material-weakness ICFR finding more concerning than at a typical reseller. Third, the marketable-securities portfolio is *actively managed by an external manager* — meaning a chunk of the "cash" is exposed to equity beta and credit risk. Insider ownership: Elliott family + officers + Foundation = 17.3% of Common and 100% of Class B — outside holders cannot force capital return.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- IIJA reauthorization or successor-bill negotiations through Q3 calendar 2026 — the single largest event for the entire customer base.
- Q2 fiscal 2026 10-Q (~April-May 2026) — backlog trajectory after the $57.4M Q1 print.
- Material-weakness remediation update; failure to remediate could attract activist attention.
- Marc Elliott's first full year as Chairman — any change in dividend or buyback posture would be a substantial re-rating event.

CHCI.name: Comstock Holding Companies, Inc.
CHCI.content:
# 42. CHCI — Comstock Holding Companies, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Score: 67/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Comstock Holding Companies is a Reston, Virginia-based asset-light real-estate services platform that manages a portfolio of mixed-use, multifamily, and commercial properties concentrated in the Washington, DC metro corridor. The reported metrics — P/E 11.2, P/B 2.63, ROE 28% — describe a small, debt-free fee-income business that has reinvented itself from a builder/developer into a fee-based asset manager. CHCI provides asset management, property management, leasing, parking (ParkX), and capital markets services to a related-party anchor portfolio controlled by founder/CEO Christopher Clemente and strategic partner Dwight Schar. Recent disclosures highlight 2025 revenue growth in the low- to mid-20s percent range, expansion into data-center adjacent platforms, and continued growth of the recurring-fee base. The thesis is simple: a sub-$200m-mcap asset-light services company riding the structural tailwind of one of the strongest commercial-real-estate sub-markets (Northern Virginia / data center alley).

The bear case starts and ends with related-party concentration. CHCI's anchor revenue stream is governed by a long-term, cost-plus asset management agreement with Comstock Partners, LC ("CP"), an entity controlled by the same CEO who controls the public company. Effectively, Christopher Clemente sets the fees that he pays himself as a public-company shareholder. That structure has clear governance risk: any renegotiation, related-party transfer, or change in CP's underlying property mix can compress public-company economics overnight, and minority shareholders have limited recourse. The asset-light model also means CHCI doesn't own the underlying real estate — if CP loses a property, leases up poorly, or sells, the management fee evaporates. Float is small, ownership is concentrated, and exit liquidity in a stress scenario would be poor.

The bull case is that the related-party structure, while concentrated, has produced consistent fee growth, a debt-free balance sheet, and high incremental margins because the marginal property added to the platform requires limited new corporate overhead. Northern Virginia and the broader DC metro continue to absorb federal-adjacent office demand, multifamily demand from a high-income labor base, and — most importantly — explosive data-center capital deployment in Loudoun County and adjacent submarkets. CHCI is positioning ParkX as a separately scalable parking-tech platform and is taking small-equity positions in development opportunities. If the recurring-fee base compounds at 15–20% with operating leverage, the multiple looks defensive even at 2.6x book.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Read Note 13 (related party transactions) carefully — the fee structure, the cost-plus mark-up, and any amendments to the anchor portfolio agreement matter more than any income-statement line. Also check the description of the Anchor Portfolio: occupancy, lease maturities, and tenant concentration drive the durability of the management fee. ParkX is increasingly disclosed as a growth driver and warrants its own segment-level read. The data-center platform is new and small but is a credible call option. Insider ownership is heavy — Clemente and Schar between them effectively control the company. There is no analyst coverage of consequence; price discovery is thin and often news-driven.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Fee-based asset manager — simple model, complex agreements |
| Moat | 6 | Long-term contract w/ CP; switching costs; concentrated MSA expertise |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | Debt-free; positive cash position |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | 2.63x book is rich; cushion is in fee growth not balance sheet |
| Catalyst | 7 | Data-center platform; ParkX scaling; recurring fee compounding |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Asymmetric upside if fees compound; capped by RPT risk |
| Business Quality | 8 | Asset-light, ~28% ROE, high incremental margins |
| Executability | 6 | Small float, controlled-company, illiquid in size |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | RPT renegotiation could compress multiple sharply |
| Macro Independence | 4 | DC metro CRE concentration is binary on regional cycle |
| **TOTAL** | **67** | Quality fee model offset by major related-party governance risk |

FOTB.name: First Ottawa Bancshares, Inc.
FOTB.content:
# 43. FOTB — First Ottawa Bancshares, Inc.
## BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 58/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
First Ottawa Bancshares is the Illinois-based holding company for American Commercial Bank & Trust, a community bank with roots dating to 1865 and a footprint across LaSalle, Grundy, Cook, and surrounding counties. The bank operates roughly a dozen branches and offers a fairly standard community bank menu: consumer and commercial deposits, mortgage and consumer lending, commercial and ag loans, trust and fiduciary services. Headline metrics — P/E 8.5, P/B 1.42, ROE 21% — describe a quietly profitable rural/suburban Illinois lender trading at a discount to peers, with returns on equity considerably above what most community banks of similar size achieve. The recurring narrative around FOTB is the combination of decent profitability, deeply illiquid OTC trading, and an extraordinarily small float that locks out any institutional buyer of size.

The bear case is structural rather than fundamental. FOTB is a true micro-cap community bank — daily volume measured in hundreds of shares is common — and the holding company recently issued $24.25 million of subordinated notes, which adds leverage at the holding-company level and pressures consolidated capital ratios over time. Loan-book concentration to commercial real estate, agriculture, and small-business lending in northern Illinois is exposed to regional office-market and farm-income cyclicality. The bank's geographic adjacency to suburban Chicago (Cook and Grundy counties) is a double-edged sword: deposit-rich but credit-exposed. As a non-NASDAQ-listed name, financial disclosure cadence is less frequent and analyst coverage is essentially zero, raising adverse-selection risk for outside investors.

The bull case is the classic "fundamental compounder behind an illiquid quote" thesis. A 21% ROE for a community bank is exceptional — most peers earn 8–11% — and at 1.42x book the implied earnings yield is double-digit. The 2024 record-profit disclosure suggests the franchise is benefiting from a steeper yield curve and continued deposit stickiness in its rural Illinois markets. Subordinated debt issuance, while adding leverage, also funds either organic growth, M&A optionality, or capital return. For a patient investor able to accumulate shares slowly and willing to hold for years, FOTB offers the canonical "tiny bank with real earnings power" setup. M&A by a larger Illinois community bank (Midland States, Old Second, or similar) would close the discount-to-fair-value gap quickly.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The most current 10-K available on EDGAR is dated 2012 — FOTB likely deregistered or moved to OTC reporting only, which means FFIEC Call Reports filed by the bank subsidiary are the cleanest source of financial truth. Investors should read the latest Call Report for charge-offs, NPL ratios, CRE concentration metrics, and Tier 1 leverage. The recent $24.25m subordinated notes deal — underwritten and structured by a small specialized counsel — is a key event for capital structure analysis. Insider ownership and any control-block dynamics matter enormously at this size; a single 5% holder can move the price. Bid/ask spreads of several percent are common; position sizing should assume weeks of accumulation, not minutes.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Plain-vanilla community bank model |
| Moat | 5 | Local deposit franchise; modest scale advantages |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | Sub-debt adds HoldCo leverage; bank capital adequate |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | 1.42x book with 21% ROE — earnings yield ~12% |
| Catalyst | 4 | M&A optionality only; no scheduled event |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Downside contained; upside requires patience or sale |
| Business Quality | 7 | 21% ROE is exceptional for community bank size |
| Executability | 2 | Extremely illiquid OTC; size limit of low five figures |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Regional credit stress; sub-debt cost; opacity |
| Macro Independence | 5 | NII sensitive to rate path & Illinois farm/CRE cycle |
| **TOTAL** | **58** | Real earnings power locked behind a near-impassable float |

FDBC.name: Fidelity D & D Bancorp, Inc.
FDBC.content:
# 44. FDBC — Fidelity D & D Bancorp, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Score: 72/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Fidelity D & D Bancorp is the Dunmore, Pennsylvania-based holding company for Fidelity Bank, a community bank operating roughly 21 full-service offices across Lackawanna, Luzerne, Lehigh, and Northampton Counties, plus a separately branded Fidelity Bank Wealth Management office in Schuylkill County. The franchise is unusually deposit-rich for its size: total assets reached $2.75 billion in 2025, with loans of $1.89 billion and deposits of $2.47 billion. The mix matters — a meaningful wealth management and brokerage business rides on top of the core banking franchise, plus a mortgage center, which give FDBC a more diversified non-interest income stream than a typical eastern PA community bank. Headline P/E of 9.1 and P/B of 1.06 understate the underlying quality: 2025 net income of $28.2 million and EPS of $4.86 was up 36% year-over-year, with non-performing assets at just 0.08% of total assets.

The bear case for FDBC is principally regional and rate-driven. Northeast Pennsylvania is a slow-growth demographic region; Scranton/Wilkes-Barre population trends are flat to declining, which caps long-term loan-growth potential without geographic expansion. Branch density at 21 offices for ~$2.75B in assets is on the higher side and represents real fixed cost. Net interest margin expansion that drove the 36% earnings jump in 2025 was partly a function of rate normalization — if the Fed cuts aggressively in 2026, NIM compression would reverse some of that benefit. CRE exposure across the loan book — especially small office, hotel, and multifamily in legacy industrial PA markets — is a credit risk to monitor through the next downturn. Wealth management AUM is a fee-income lever that depends on equity-market levels.

The bull case is that this is a high-quality franchise that has compounded book value steadily, with an extraordinary 0.08% NPA ratio that signals disciplined underwriting through multiple cycles. A double-digit ROE community bank with a well-developed wealth management business trading near tangible book is unusual; comparable franchises (Univest, ESSA, Penns Woods) typically trade 1.2–1.6x book. The 2025 result demonstrates operating leverage: revenue growth dropped substantially to the bottom line. Strategic optionality includes continued geographic expansion into Lehigh Valley (already underway), modest tuck-in M&A, or eventual sale to a larger PA/NJ bank acquirer at a meaningful premium to book. Dividend policy is stable, and the wealth platform provides earnings counter-balance to NII volatility.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The wealth management segment disclosure is critical — it is structurally higher-multiple than the spread bank and supports a sum-of-the-parts case for the stock. Read the loan-portfolio breakdown for CRE concentration ratios versus regulatory guidance (300% of capital threshold). The Schuylkill County wealth office expansion suggests intent to push the wealth franchise outside the home county, which is the highest-ROE growth lever available. Insider ownership and proxy voting concentration matter for any future M&A scenario — the recent DEF 14A discloses the relevant directors and pay structure. Also note: the bank has historically been conservative on capital return relative to peers, which is consistent with the long-term hold profile but caps near-term shareholder yield.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Community bank + wealth mgmt — well-understood model |
| Moat | 7 | Local relationships, wealth franchise stickiness |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | $2.75B assets; NPAs 0.08% of assets — exceptional |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | 1.06x book with double-digit ROE |
| Catalyst | 6 | NII normalization + wealth scaling; M&A optionality |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Limited downside near book; upside to peer multiple |
| Business Quality | 8 | Diversified revenue, exceptional credit, growing fee base |
| Executability | 7 | NASDAQ-listed; reasonable liquidity for community bank |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Regional demographics; NIM cycle; CRE exposure |
| Macro Independence | 5 | NII rate-sensitive; wealth equity-market sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **72** | Best-in-class community bank at a discount-to-peers multiple |

BEOB.name: BEO Bancorp
BEOB.content:
# 45. BEOB — BEO Bancorp
## BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 60/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
BEO Bancorp is the holding company for Bank of Eastern Oregon, a community bank founded in 1945 by farmers and ranchers and headquartered in Heppner, Oregon. The bank operates approximately 21 branches plus three loan production offices spanning 11 eastern Oregon counties, four eastern Washington counties, and one western Idaho county. Its franchise is distinctive in that it is one of the few remaining independent community banks specifically focused on agricultural lending in the Pacific Northwest interior — a region underserved by money-center banks and even most regional banks. Headline numbers — P/E 8.1, P/B 1.35, ROE 18% — describe an above-average-ROE community bank trading well below the typical 1.5–1.8x book multiple of profitable Pacific Northwest peers. ICBA recognized BEO among its Top Ag Lenders of 2024, validating the franchise's positioning.

The bear case mirrors the structural concerns common to all OTC-traded micro-cap community banks. Float is extraordinarily thin, daily volume is essentially nil for institutional purposes, and price discovery is sporadic. Concentrated agricultural lending — wheat, cattle, hay, irrigated row crops — is exposed to commodity-price cycles, drought risk, and federal farm-bill policy. Eastern Oregon's economy is small and slow-growing; deposit-base growth depends on local economic conditions that have limited demographic tailwinds. Regulatory burden on small banks continues to scale faster than asset bases, pressuring efficiency ratios. Compliance costs on a $700m–$800m asset bank are disproportionate, and the bank's wide geographic footprint (21 branches across three states) implies higher fixed-cost-to-asset ratios than urban peers. Sub-debt or capital raises by similarly sized peers have been at high coupons in the current rate environment.

The bull case is the inverse of the bear: a sticky agricultural deposit franchise with limited deposit competition, an 18% ROE that few community banks anywhere produce, and a price below 1.4x book. The bank's deep specialization in ag credit — confirmed by ICBA recognition — is a genuine competitive moat in markets where money-center underwriters lack expertise. Sticky deposits from multi-generational farm/ranch customers cost less than wholesale funding and rarely flee. The geographic spread, while costly, also diversifies single-county and single-commodity risk. M&A from a larger Pacific Northwest acquirer (Heritage Financial, Washington Federal, Banner) would crystallize the discount-to-fair-value at potentially 1.7–2.0x book in a sale scenario. For a long-duration patient holder, BEOB offers an unusual combination of franchise quality and entry valuation.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Because BEOB trades on OTC and is not listed on a major exchange, the bank-subsidiary FFIEC Call Report is the most reliable real-time financial source — it discloses the loan portfolio mix (ag operating, ag real estate, commercial, consumer), charge-offs, NPL ratios, deposit composition, and capital ratios. Concentration metrics are critical: any single-borrower or single-commodity concentration above regulatory thresholds is material risk. Dividend policy has historically been modest and stable, supporting a "bond-like" hold. Insider and director ownership tends to be high and locally concentrated, which is positive for alignment but reinforces the float problem. Drought conditions and Pacific Northwest snowpack reports are surprisingly relevant to credit quality given irrigation dependency.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |

| Balance Sheet | 7 | Adequate community-bank capital; need Call Report verify |

| Business Quality | 8 | 18% ROE plus ICBA top-ag-lender recognition |

BEOB.analystSummary:
BEO Bancorp is the holding company for Bank of Eastern Oregon, a community bank founded in 1945 by farmers and ranchers and headquartered in Heppner, Oregon. The bank operates approximately 21 branches plus three loan production offices spanning 11 eastern Oregon counties, four eastern Washington counties, and one western Idaho county. Its franchise is distinctive in that it is one of the few remaining independent community banks specifically focused on agricultural lending in the Pacific Northwest interior — a region underserved by money-center banks and even most regional banks. Headline numbers — P/E 8.1, P/B 1.35, ROE 18% — describe an above-average-ROE community bank trading well below the typical 1.5–1.8x book multiple of profitable Pacific Northwest peers. ICBA recognized BEO among its Top Ag Lenders of 2024, validating the franchise's positioning.

The bear case mirrors the structural concerns common to all OTC-traded micro-cap community banks. Float is extraordinarily thin, daily volume is essentially nil for institutional purposes, and price discovery is sporadic. Concentrated agricultural lending — wheat, cattle, hay, irrigated row crops — is exposed to commodity-price cycles, drought risk, and federal farm-bill policy. Eastern Oregon's economy is small and slow-growing; deposit-base growth depends on local economic conditions that have limited demographic tailwinds. Regulatory burden on small banks continues to scale faster than asset bases, pressuring efficiency ratios. Compliance costs on a $700m–$800m asset bank are disproportionate, and the bank's wide geographic footprint (21 branches across three states) implies higher fixed-cost-to-asset ratios than urban peers. Sub-debt or capital raises by similarly sized peers have been at high coupons in the current rate environment.

The bull case is the inverse of the bear: a sticky agricultural deposit franchise with limited deposit competition, an 18% ROE that few community banks anywhere produce, and a price below 1.4x book. The bank's deep specialization in ag credit — confirmed by ICBA recognition — is a genuine competitive moat in markets where money-center underwriters lack expertise. Sticky deposits from multi-generational farm/ranch customers cost less than wholesale funding and rarely flee. The geographic spread, while costly, also diversifies single-county and single-commodity risk. M&A from a larger Pacific Northwest acquirer (Heritage Financial, Washington Federal, Banner) would crystallize the discount-to-fair-value at potentially 1.7–2.0x book in a sale scenario. For a long-duration patient holder, BEOB offers an unusual combination of franchise quality and entry valuation.
BEOB.whatScreenCantSee: Because BEOB trades on OTC and is not listed on a major exchange, the bank-subsidiary FFIEC Call Report is the most reliable real-time financial source — it discloses the loan portfolio mix (ag operating, ag real estate, commercial, consumer), charge-offs, NPL ratios, deposit composition, and capital ratios. Concentration metrics are critical: any single-borrower or single-commodity concentration above regulatory thresholds is material risk. Dividend policy has historically been modest and stable, supporting a "bond-like" hold. Insider and director ownership tends to be high and locally concentrated, which is positive for alignment but reinforces the float problem. Drought conditions and Pacific Northwest snowpack reports are surprisingly relevant to credit quality given irrigation dependency.

CALM.name: Cal-Maine Foods, Inc.
CALM.content:
# 46. CALM — Cal-Maine Foods, Inc.
## CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 64/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Cal-Maine Foods is the largest US shell-egg producer, controlling roughly the largest share of branded and private-label table eggs through 50 processing/packaging plants and an estimated ~285 million-bird US table-egg flock backdrop. Fiscal 2025 (ended May 31, 2025) was a banner year: total net sales of $4.3 billion versus $2.3 billion in fiscal 2024, with the net average selling price per dozen at $3.134 (vs $1.932 prior year), a direct beneficiary of HPAI-driven supply destruction. Management closed the Echo Lake Foods acquisition June 2, 2025, expanding into prepared/value-added foods. The screen flags net-cash, sub-3x EV/EBITDA, and a P/E around 5x, but those numbers reflect peak-cycle profitability — every long-term investor in CALM has to underwrite the next downcycle.

The bear case is straightforward: this is a commodity producer trading on cyclical-peak earnings. Conventional egg prices per dozen of $3.49 in FY25 (vs $1.73 in FY24) won't persist once the depopulated layer flock recovers. Customer concentration is extreme — Walmart/Sam's alone is 33.6% of net sales, top three customers 49.2% — and these grocers are already pushing back on pricing. The variable dividend policy (one-third of net income) means payouts will collapse alongside earnings. Management acquired Echo Lake at peak-cycle cash flow, and as of April 14, 2025 the founder Adams family ceded "controlled company" status, removing a long-standing alignment anchor and opening the door to less-disciplined capital allocation.

The bull case rests on structural egg-supply tightness. HPAI-driven culls (over 39 million additional layer hens depopulated through May 2025) have made supply restoration a multi-year process. Cage-free mandates in CA, MA, CO, WA and other states force capex that smaller competitors can't absorb, consolidating share toward CALM. The $500 million share repurchase authorization (Feb 2025) plus 32% net-cash gives optionality to buy back stock through any trough. Specialty/branded eggs (Egg-Land's Best, Land O' Lakes, 4-Grain) carry better margins and customer stickiness than commodity. Echo Lake adds a non-shell prepared-foods leg. If you assume a normalized through-cycle EPS of $4–6 and a 10–12x multiple, fair value lands materially above today's screen-trough framing.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 36% operating margin in FY25 is a once-in-a-decade outlier driven by HPAI-induced shortage; FY24 operating margin was 13.4% and the prior cyclical trough was negative. The $46.8 million of goodwill is small (1.5% of total assets) — pre-Echo Lake. Family control just ended April 14, 2025, which is governance-positive on paper but removes the founder-family discipline that historically constrained M&A. The Echo Lake purchase will introduce branded-foods integration risk and cage-free capex acceleration. Cage-free now represents a growing share of "specialty" volume; specialty pricing of $2.519/dozen actually underperformed conventional in FY25 because long-term cage-free contracts didn't fully reset. Watch the company's first downcycle response: will it draw down the new $500M repo authorization aggressively into the trough?

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Sell eggs to grocers — couldn't be simpler |
| Moat | 5 | Scale + Walmart relationship; commodity at the unit level |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | Net cash ~32% of mkt cap; minimal long-term debt |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | P/E 5.3 on peak earnings; EV/EBITDA 2.7x cyclical |
| Catalyst | 6 | Echo Lake integration; HPAI-driven supply tightness |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Downside is well-understood cycle; upside capped by reversion |
| Business Quality | 6 | Variable margins; FY25 op margin 36% vs cycle avg ~10% |
| Executability | 8 | Liquid; ~3,800 employees; founder family no longer controlled |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | HPAI resolves, prices revert to $1.50–$2.00, EPS halves |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Feed costs (corn/soy), grocer pricing power, biosecurity |
| **TOTAL** | **64** | Cyclical trough wrapped in peak earnings — buy the downcycle |

---

RIGL.name: Rigel Pharmaceuticals, Inc.
RIGL.content:
# 47. RIGL — Rigel Pharmaceuticals, Inc.
## BIOTECH BINARY | Score: 67/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Rigel is one of the very few sub-$1B commercial-stage biotechs that is actually profitable. The 2025 10-K shows total revenues of $294.3 million (up from $179.3 million in 2024), driven by $232.0 million in net product sales and $62.0 million in collaboration revenue. The portfolio is built around three commercialized assets in hematology/oncology: TAVALISSE (fostamatinib, oral SYK inhibitor for chronic ITP), REZLIDHIA (olutasidenib, mIDH1 R/R AML), and GAVRETO (pralsetinib, RET-fusion NSCLC and thyroid cancers — in-licensed from Blueprint in Feb 2024). 2025 product mix: TAVALISSE $158.8M (+51.6% YoY), REZLIDHIA $31.0M (+34.8% YoY), and the rest from GAVRETO and partner royalties (Kissei in Japan, Grifols in EU). Cash + ST investments were $155 million at year-end 2025.

The bear case for RIGL is execution risk on three small drugs against a backdrop of intensifying ITP and AML competition. TAVALISSE is now seven years post-launch and faces longer-term competition from newer ITP agents; REZLIDHIA is a niche second-line AML drug for an mIDH1-mutated subset that is being squeezed by Servier's Tibsovo and other emerging agents. GAVRETO acquired from Roche/Blueprint comes with execution and supply-chain risk. McKesson alone was 42% of revenue in 2025, an uncomfortable specialty-pharma concentration. Management explicitly warns recent operating income may not be sustainable. Pipeline depth is thin; without a fresh in-licensing deal, the long-term growth trajectory flattens, and the biotech-binary nature returns.

The bull case is that Rigel is doing what the entire sector wishes it could: generating profitable cash flow from a small commercial book while running a nearly debt-free balance sheet. The 2025 P/E around 1.6x reflects skepticism that earnings persist, but TAVALISSE 51% growth and REZLIDHIA 35% growth tell a different story — these drugs are still ramping. Rigel has demonstrated it can integrate in-licensed assets (GAVRETO from Blueprint, REZLIDHIA from Forma) at attractive economics. Total assets of $513.6 million versus $164.0 million a year earlier reflect the addition of long-term investments. As a small profitable hematology platform with three commercial drugs and partner royalty streams, Rigel is a logical M&A target for a mid-cap specialty pharma — the optionality value alone may underwrite the current multiple.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The "P/E 1.6" is misleading because 2025 net income includes large one-time items from collaboration revenue and milestone recognition; the underlying recurring product-driven earnings are lower. Forma deal economics: Rigel pays tiered royalties on REZLIDHIA net sales from low-teens to mid-thirties percentages — meaning incremental margin compresses as REZLIDHIA scales. The Kissei (Japan, fostamatinib) and Grifols (EU) royalty streams are small but high-margin. Customer concentration is severe: McKesson at 42% of total revenue. Cash $40.6M but short-term investments $114.4M = ~$155M liquidity, which is not the "fortress" some screens might imply. Pipeline thin — most R&D spend is on lifecycle extension rather than new molecules.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Hematology specialty pharma; needs domain context |
| Moat | 5 | Patents + commercial relationships; not durable structural moat |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | $155M cash/investments, low debt, profitable |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | P/E 1.6, EV/EBITDA 3.8x — heavy skepticism priced in |
| Catalyst | 7 | M&A target; pipeline data readouts; further label expansions |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Limited downside given cash; takeout optionality skew |
| Business Quality | 6 | First profitable year run; reinvestment intensive |
| Executability | 7 | $750M mkt cap-area, liquid; activist-aware mgmt |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Tavalisse decline + GAVRETO miss = back to losses |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Drug pricing exposed to MFN policy; otherwise insensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **67** | Rare profitable small-cap biotech with takeout optionality |

---

JFIN.name: Jiayin Group Inc.
JFIN.content:
# 48. JFIN — Jiayin Group Inc.
## VALUE TRAP RISK | Score: 41/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Jiayin Group is a Shanghai-based online consumer-finance loan-facilitation platform that connects individual borrowers in China with licensed institutional funding partners and guarantors, earning service fees rather than balance-sheet interest income. The 20-F shows the typical Chinese ADR Cayman-holdco/VIE structure: investors own a Cayman shell whose contractual rights to the operating onshore VIE Group entities depend on PRC contract enforcement. Total net revenue of RMB 5.80 billion in 2024 (down from RMB 5.47 billion in 2023 in segment terms but disclosed segment data shows volatile comparatives across the consolidating eliminations), with international expansion underway in Indonesia, Mexico (via EPD investment), and Nigeria. Reported P/E around 1x and P/B 0.37 reflect the market's aggressive discounting of accounting earnings that carry political-risk attached.

The bear case is structural and profound: this is a Chinese consumer-lending intermediary owned through a VIE structure, regulated by Chinese authorities who in 2020-2021 demonstrated willingness to dismantle entire fintech business models overnight (Ant Group IPO, P2P lending purge). The 20-F flags China's evolving consumer-protection rules, fee-rate compression risk, ability of regulators to revoke licensing or restrict cross-border data flows, and PCAOB audit-access overhang. The variable-interest entity (VIE) structure means US shareholders have contractual — not equity — claims on the onshore operating businesses; PRC courts have never definitively enforced VIE contracts. ADR holders also face delisting risk under the HFCAA. Service fee rates "may decline in the future" — management's own language. The operating-segment data shows large eliminations and intercompany flows that obscure clean unit economics. Geographic diversification is real but tiny relative to mainland China exposure.

The bull case is purely valuation-driven. At P/B 0.37 and a low single-digit P/E, you are paying $0.37 for $1 of book and roughly one year of run-rate earnings. Management has a stated dividend policy of paying out at least 15% of prior-year net income on a consolidated basis, with a US$0.10/share cash dividend paid in July 2023. International expansion (Indonesia, Mexico, Nigeria) provides at least an option on building a non-China business over time. The China consumer-credit market remains structurally large, and surviving regulated players in the loan-facilitation space have demonstrated they can earn high returns on equity. If the geopolitical and PCAOB risks resolve favorably, the multiple re-rate is significant. But a margin of safety only exists if you accept the VIE/political risk is real and irreducible.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 20-F discloses RMB-denominated financials that swing materially between segment cuts. Cash and cash equivalents at the consolidated total were RMB 540.5 million — but most of that sits inside mainland China VIE entities, not at the Cayman parent, meaning ADR holders don't have unrestricted access. The dividend policy of "15% of NI" is a board-determined floor, not a binding obligation; the actual 2023 cash dividend was modest. Walking-around money for borrowers includes Indonesia/Mexico sub-RMB-50M loans to subsidiaries — early-stage international optionality but not material. The accounts receivable and contract assets line of RMB 2.99 billion is the working-capital lifeblood — quality of those receivables in a China consumer-credit downturn is a key swing factor. A profitable platform earning low-single-digit times-earnings is either a screaming buy or a value trap; the VIE structure tilts toward the latter for most fiduciary investors.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 4 | Loan facilitation is simple; VIE structure obscures everything |
| Moat | 3 | Intermediary; competing platforms; regulatory licenses |
| Balance Sheet | 4 | Cash held mostly inside China; receivables-heavy |
| Margin of Safety | 9 | P/E ~1, P/B 0.37 — extreme on accounting basis |
| Catalyst | 4 | Dividend, buyback, possible China re-rating |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Tail risks symmetric: takeout vs delisting |
| Business Quality | 4 | Volatile revenue; segment reporting hard to trust |
| Executability | 5 | Liquid ADR but small cap; controlled company |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 2 | VIE invalidation; PRC reg crackdown; ADR delisting |
| Macro Independence | 1 | Entirely dependent on PRC consumer credit cycle and policy |
| **TOTAL** | **41** | Cheap by every multiple — but the discount is rational |

---

| Inversion (Bear Case) | 2 | VIE invalidation; PRC reg crackdown; ADR delisting |
| Macro Independence | 1 | Entirely dependent on PRC consumer credit cycle and policy |
| **TOTAL** | **41** | Cheap by every multiple — but the discount is rational |

EVER.name: EverQuote, Inc.
EVER.content:
# 49. EVER — EverQuote, Inc.
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Score: 72/100

### ANALYST SUMMARY
EverQuote is the leading online marketplace connecting auto-insurance shoppers with carriers and agents, monetizing per-referral via a real-time auction model where carriers bid for high-intent consumer leads. The 2025 10-K shows the recovery from the 2022-23 carrier-pullback shock is now fully in motion: revenue of $692.5 million for 2025, up 38.5% from $500.2 million in 2024, with the automotive vertical accounting for $183.7 million of the $192.3 million revenue increase as carriers reopened marketing budgets. Net income flipped to $99.3 million in 2025 versus $32.2 million in 2024 and a $51.3 million net loss in 2023. Adjusted EBITDA of $94.6 million ($58.2M in 2024, $0.5M in 2023) confirms the operating leverage. The board authorized a share repurchase program in July 2025; cash position is healthy with no long-term debt of consequence.

The bear case is two-pronged. First, customer concentration is severe and worsening: the largest customer was 38% of total revenue in 2025 and the second-largest 11%, meaning a single carrier withdrawal could erase ~40% of the top line — exactly what happened in 2022 when Allstate, Progressive, and others abruptly cut acquisition spend during the auto-insurance underwriting crisis. EverQuote exited its health-insurance vertical in 2023, so the business is now functionally a single-vertical (auto P&C) lead-gen platform with limited diversification beyond home/renters as a small adjacency. Second, this is fundamentally a cyclical business tied to the auto-insurance underwriting cycle: when combined ratios spike, carriers slash acquisition spending and EverQuote's revenue craters with little of its own causation. Today's 38% growth is the upcycle; the next downturn looks much like 2022-23.

The bull case is that 2025 demonstrates the EverQuote business model still works structurally. The auto-insurance industry is shifting acquisition dollars from offline (TV, direct-mail) to online lead-gen at a secular rate, and EverQuote is positioned as the largest pure-play marketplace. Operating leverage is dramatic — 2025 adjusted EBITDA margin of 13.7% versus 11.6% in 2024 versus 0.1% in 2023 — and there's room to expand further as variable marketing dollars scale faster than fixed costs. The Class B controlling stake held by Link Ventures (controlled-company status) provides governance stability. With $326.9M total assets, no material debt, and a fresh repurchase authorization, capital allocation now turns from survival mode to shareholder return. P/E 6.1 and EV/EBITDA 5.8x bake in another carrier pullback that may not arrive for years.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The "VMD/VMM" non-GAAP metric (variable marketing dollars and margin) is the single most important operating KPI — it isolates revenue net of paid-media costs and tells you the unit economics of each acquired consumer. The 2023 carrier-pullback was driven by industry-wide auto loss ratios crossing 100%, not by EverQuote's competitive position; carriers are now writing profitably again and reopening online channels. The S-3 shelf registration permits up to $150 million of further capital-markets issuance, which could overhang the equity but also gives flexibility. Health-insurance vertical exit in 2023 was painful but de-risked the business from CMS ACA marketplace volatility. The dual-class structure means Link Ventures retains voting control even with low economic ownership — relevant if/when activists eye the cash on the balance sheet.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Auction marketplace for insurance leads — clear |
| Moat | 6 | Network effects (carriers + consumers); some switching cost |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | $326.9M assets, no meaningful debt, repo authorized |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | P/E 6.1 on cyclical-recovery EPS; EV/EBITDA 5.8x |
| Catalyst | 8 | Carrier spend re-acceleration; buyback execution |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Asymmetric: cyclical upside vs known carrier-pullback floor |
| Business Quality | 7 | High operating leverage; capital-light |
| Executability | 7 | Mid-cap liquidity; controlled-company structure |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | Top-2 customers = 49% of rev; auto cycle re-rolls |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Tied to auto insurance underwriting cycle |
| **TOTAL** | **72** | Best Tier-2 cyclical-recovery name in the screen |

---

DJCO.name: Daily Journal Corporation
DJCO.content:
# 7. DJCO — Daily Journal Corporation
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER  |  Score: 76/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $3,500 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
Munger's old holdco trades at a $749M market cap with a marketable securities portfolio worth ~$443M (mid-2025 mark) and a growing court-software business (Journal Technologies) compounding revenue +12% YoY in Q1 FY26. Stripping the portfolio at fair value, the operating business is on offer at roughly $300M for $60M+ of growing software revenue. The market dislikes the GAAP optics from quarterly mark-to-market on concentrated bank stocks — that's the inefficiency.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Daily Journal is two businesses stitched into one entity. (1) The Traditional segment publishes legal and business newspapers in California (declining, low-margin, but cash-positive). (2) Journal Technologies sells eSuite — case management, e-filing, and jury management software to courts, prosecutors, and public defenders across the US, Canada, and Australia. Q1 FY2026 (Oct–Dec 2025) put consolidated revenue at $19.5M (+10% YoY), with Journal Tech at $15.2M (+12%). Steven Myhill-Jones is Chairman and CEO post-Munger. The screen flagged the name on $477M net cash / 64% of market cap and a 8.0 P/E — both numbers driven by a securities portfolio originally constructed by Charlie Munger.

The bear case has three legs. First, the investment portfolio is heavily concentrated in financials — Wells Fargo (~48%), Bank of America (~40%), Alibaba ADR (~10%), US Bancorp (~2%) per the Q4 2025 13F (~$277M reported value at filing date) — and quarterly mark-to-market drives wild GAAP swings (Q1 FY26 showed an $11.7M unrealized loss flipping a $10.9M underlying profit into an $8M reported loss). Second, Journal Technologies revenue is concentrated in long-cycle government contracts; implementation timelines slip, deferred revenue can be lumpy, and competition from Tyler Technologies and Thomson Reuters is real. Third, post-Munger capital allocation discipline is unproven — the portfolio rebalancing risk is non-trivial.

The bull case is the rare combination of a real, growing software business inside a holdco trading well below sum-of-parts. Journal Technologies is recurring-revenue government software (sticky, high switching costs, mission-critical for courts) growing double digits. The Munger portfolio, even after recent drawdowns, still carries hundreds of millions in unrealized gains — a meaningful deferred-tax shield. At $749M cap minus $443M portfolio (June 2025 mark), the implied operating value is ~$300M for a software business doing $60M+ revenue with secular tailwinds (court system digital modernization). Catalysts: continued Journal Tech bookings, possible buybacks (DJCO has historically been disciplined), and portfolio mark-to-market reversals.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The "8.0 P/E" is meaningless because TTM earnings are dominated by securities mark-to-market — when bank stocks rally, EPS spikes; when they sell off, EPS collapses; the underlying operating earnings are far more stable but much smaller. The screen also doesn't capture the deferred tax liability on the unrealized portfolio gains (~$303.9M of pretax unrealized gains at June 2025 means a meaningful tax overhang on full liquidation). Most importantly, no screen captures the cultural inheritance: DJCO is one of the few public companies where management has demonstrated for decades they will hold concentrated positions through volatility rather than diworsify. That's the actual moat.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q2 FY2026 10-Q (around mid-May 2026) — Journal Tech bookings update
- Annual meeting (typically Feb) — first full post-Munger commentary cycle
- Bank stock rerating (BAC + WFC = ~88% of portfolio) — macro catalyst
- Potential capital return announcement (buyback or special dividend) — discretionary

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Newspaper + court software + 4 stocks — transparent |
| Moat | 7 | Court software switching costs; portfolio holdco discipline |
| Balance Sheet | 10 | $477M net cash incl. securities, no debt |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | Operating biz at ~$300M EV for $60M+ rev |
| Catalyst | 5 | Slow-burn — no hard date |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Sum-of-parts dislocation |
| Business Quality | 7 | Sticky gov software + concentrated equity portfolio |
| Executability | 5 | ~$1-2M ADV, low float — but $3-4K easily fillable |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | Bank stock concentration is real risk |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Bank exposure ties to credit cycle |
| **TOTAL** | **71** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $3,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: High-conviction sum-of-parts with a dual margin of safety (cash + growing operating biz). Liquidity supports a larger position, and the portfolio is itself a hedge basket of large, liquid bank equities.

---

PERF.name: Perfect Corp.
PERF.content:
# 2. PERF — Perfect Corp.
## SUB-CASH OPERATING  |  Score: 64/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $2,500 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
A profitable Taiwan-based AI/AR beauty SaaS — IPO'd 2022 via SPAC — trading at $172M of cash and time deposits against a $177M market cap. As of March 18, 2026, the founder (CEO Alice H. Chang) and parent CyberLink filed a preliminary non-binding take-private offer at $1.95 per share. That is the asymmetry: you are buying a profitable, growing business at sub-cash with a live insider bid sitting on the table.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Perfect Corp. (NYSE: PERF) builds AI- and AR-powered virtual try-on, skin-analysis and image-generation tools sold to beauty/fashion brands (B2B) and to consumers via the YouCam app suite (B2C subscriptions). FY25 revenue was $69.2M (+14.9% YoY), driven by $61.1M of "AI- and AR- cloud solutions and subscription" revenue (+13.5%). Net income was $4.6M; operating cash flow was $13.3M. The screen flag — net cash $161.6M against market cap $177M (91% ratio) — is grounded in $126.0M cash and equivalents plus $36.3M six-month time deposits and $10.2M U.S. Treasuries (per the Q4 release). 908,000 active YouCam subscribers and 859 brand clients (135 "Key Customers" generating >$50K trailing twelve months). Spun out of Taiwan Stock Exchange-listed CyberLink, which remains a major shareholder and lessor.

The bear case begins with the dual-class structure: per the 20-F, "each holder of Class B Ordinary Shares is entitled to ten (10) votes per share" and CEO Chang holds 100% of Class B = 66.6% voting power. That control is now being weaponized via the take-private offer at $1.95 — a price below the prior trading range and at a discount to net cash. Filings flag that the dual-class structure may make Class A "ineligible for inclusion in any of these indices." Operating loss of $1.7M for FY25 reflects the company's reliance on financial income and a $2.0M goodwill impairment from January 2025's Wannaby acquisition. App-store dependency (Apple/Google) is explicit. Key Customer count just fell from 142 to 135 in a single quarter, hinting at B2B churn even as B2C subscriber count declined 4% sequentially.

The bull case: even excluding the Wannaby write-down, Perfect would have generated operating income; the underlying B2C subscription engine is growing, gross margin expanded to 80.5% in Q4 25, and the company expects 2026 revenue growth of ~10% (range plus/minus 2%). With $172M of liquid resources against a market cap that briefly dipped below cash, you are paying a negative price for a 14% revenue grower with a 77% gross margin business and recurring app/SaaS economics. The Chang/CyberLink take-private bid gives a hard floor; a competing or sweetened offer is plausible if minority holders push back, and the Special Committee process itself is a forcing function for a higher price.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three filing-only items. (1) Related-party rents and the "Perfect CyberLink Education Foundation" tie the operating company tightly to CyberLink — an item that becomes very relevant in a take-private negotiation, since CyberLink is now both bidder and landlord. (2) Geographic disclosure: "revenue generated from the PRC accounted for less than 3% of our total revenue" — China exposure is small, removing one common emerging-tech overhang. (3) The Wannaby acquisition (January 2025, Lithuania-based) added headcount and R&D burn and was promptly written down; this is the kind of value-destructive M&A that makes a low-ball take-private easier to justify. Insider activity to watch: Chang, Wei-Hsin Tsen and Weichuan Liu collectively own minor Class A stakes — but Chang's full Class B block (16.79M shares = 16.5% economic, 66.6% voting) drives the deal math.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Special Committee response to the March 18, 2026 Chang/CyberLink $1.95/share take-private proposal — a definitive agreement, rejection, or competing bid is the dominant catalyst.
- Q1 2026 earnings (~April 2026) testing whether the active-subscriber decline reverses after the price-increase initiative.
- Continued cash build vs. SBC dilution — the gap between cash flow ($13.3M) and reported net income ($4.6M) is largely SBC, so a tighter SBC budget would re-rate the multiple.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | AI beauty try-on is conceptually clear; SBC accounting opaque |
| Moat | 5 | Decent — 859 brand clients, Modiface/Revieve compete |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | $172M cash and short-dated treasuries, no debt |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | Sub-cash with active take-private floor at $1.95 |
| Catalyst | 9 | Live insider bid; Special Committee process |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Floor near current price, asymmetric upside on a bid bump |
| Business Quality | 6 | 77% gross margin SaaS but 7% net margin and Wannaby misstep |
| Executability | 7 | ADV adequate; spread slightly wider than US-domestic peers |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 3 | Chang's 66.6% voting power can force a low-ball deal through |
| Macro Independence | 7 | B2C subscriptions decoupled from rate cycle; B2B more cyclical |
| **TOTAL** | **64** | Going-private optionality on a cash-rich SaaS |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $2,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Live take-private bid creates a defined event window with limited downside (cash floor + bid floor) and asymmetric upside on a bid bump; capped below high-conviction tier because the controlling-shareholder bid structure can also disadvantage minorities.

---
