RDCM.name: RADCOM Ltd.
RDCM.content:
# 11. RDCM — RADCOM Ltd.
## SUB-CASH OPERATING  |  Score: 76/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $3,000 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
A 5G/cloud-native network assurance vendor that just delivered 17.2% revenue growth, record operating margins, $109.9M in cash with no debt — and trades at roughly half its market cap in net cash. Customer concentration is the moat (multi-year Tier-1 carrier contracts) and the risk (top three = ~86% of revenue). When a Tier-1 telecom embeds your software in their 5G core, you don't get ripped out cheaply.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
RADCOM is an Israel-headquartered software company that sells "service assurance" — the systems that monitor whether AT&T's, Rakuten Mobile's and Verizon's mobile networks are actually delivering performance to subscribers. The screen flagged it because cash and short-term deposits of ~$109.9M sit against a ~$218M market cap (49% of mcap), the company is profitable (TTM net income ~$12M), and it just printed a record FY2025 with $71.5M revenue (+17.2%). Gross margin reached ~76.8%. Six consecutive years of growth. 2026 guidance: 8–12% revenue growth (~$78.6M midpoint). It's a small-cap software company priced like a melting ice cube but acting like a steady compounder.

The bear case starts with concentration: ~86% of 2025 revenue came from the top three customers (AT&T and Rakuten Mobile are public; Verizon and Vodafone have been historical names). Lose one, lose roughly a quarter to a third of revenue overnight — and these are deals that ramp slowly, so the offsetting wins take 18–36 months. Lumpy bookings make any single year of guidance vulnerable to a slipped contract. RADCOM is also Israel-based: geopolitical disruption, currency (shekel) exposure, and a thin float (~$220M mcap, modest daily volume) all amplify volatility. Slowing growth guidance (8–12% vs. 17% delivered) suggests management sees a deceleration into 2026 absent a new Tier-1 win.

The bull case: the cash cushion makes you nearly indifferent to a one-year revenue hiccup. Stripping cash, the operating business trades around $108M against $12M net income — roughly 9x ex-cash earnings on a software franchise with 76% gross margins, a five-year contract pipeline, and a real shot at adding a new Tier-1 in 2026 (management's stated focus). 5G monetization, AI-driven assurance and Open RAN expansion are tailwinds. Catalysts include incremental Rakuten Symphony wins, expansion at existing customers as they roll out 5G standalone cores, and potential capital return — the cash hoard is large relative to operating needs. Downside is cushioned by the cash; upside is operating leverage.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 86% top-three concentration looks scary in a screen — but the same filings reveal these are multi-year, deeply embedded software contracts inside Tier-1 5G core deployments, not transactional sales. AT&T has been a customer for years; Rakuten Mobile chose RADCOM as its assurance vendor at the beginning of its greenfield mobile build and stayed as it crossed 10M subscribers in December 2025. Switching costs in network assurance are extreme: the software ingests live network telemetry across thousands of nodes, and rip-and-replace creates blind spots no carrier accepts mid-rollout. The screen also can't see the operating margin trajectory — record margins in 2025 imply most incremental revenue is dropping to the bottom line, which compounds quickly on a sub-$80M revenue base.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- New Tier-1 carrier signing (management has flagged this as the key 2026 driver)
- Expansion deals at AT&T / Rakuten as 5G standalone deployments scale
- Capital allocation announcement — buyback expansion or special dividend on $109.9M cash pile

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Software for telecom network assurance — clear |
| Moat | 7 | Embedded multi-year Tier-1 contracts; high switching costs |
| Balance Sheet | 10 | $109.9M cash, no debt, ~49% of mcap |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | ~9x ex-cash earnings on 76% GM software |
| Catalyst | 7 | New Tier-1 win + capital return optionality |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Cash floor + operating leverage upside |
| Business Quality | 8 | 76.8% GM, six straight years of growth, profitable |
| Executability | 6 | Small float; thin liquidity at $220M mcap |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Lose AT&T or Rakuten → 25-30% revenue gap |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Carrier capex is recurring, not cyclical |
| **TOTAL** | **76** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $3,000 (of $100K)
Rationale: High-conviction sub-cash software name with real growth and a hard cash floor. Sized above the $2K average to reflect quality + asymmetry, below max to respect concentration risk and limited liquidity.

---
RDCM.analystSummary:
RADCOM is an Israel-headquartered software company that sells "service assurance" — the systems that monitor whether AT&T's, Rakuten Mobile's and Verizon's mobile networks are actually delivering performance to subscribers. The screen flagged it because cash and short-term deposits of ~$109.9M sit against a ~$218M market cap (49% of mcap), the company is profitable (TTM net income ~$12M), and it just printed a record FY2025 with $71.5M revenue (+17.2%). Gross margin reached ~76.8%. Six consecutive years of growth. 2026 guidance: 8–12% revenue growth (~$78.6M midpoint). It's a small-cap software company priced like a melting ice cube but acting like a steady compounder.

The bear case starts with concentration: ~86% of 2025 revenue came from the top three customers (AT&T and Rakuten Mobile are public; Verizon and Vodafone have been historical names). Lose one, lose roughly a quarter to a third of revenue overnight — and these are deals that ramp slowly, so the offsetting wins take 18–36 months. Lumpy bookings make any single year of guidance vulnerable to a slipped contract. RADCOM is also Israel-based: geopolitical disruption, currency (shekel) exposure, and a thin float (~$220M mcap, modest daily volume) all amplify volatility. Slowing growth guidance (8–12% vs. 17% delivered) suggests management sees a deceleration into 2026 absent a new Tier-1 win.

The bull case: the cash cushion makes you nearly indifferent to a one-year revenue hiccup. Stripping cash, the operating business trades around $108M against $12M net income — roughly 9x ex-cash earnings on a software franchise with 76% gross margins, a five-year contract pipeline, and a real shot at adding a new Tier-1 in 2026 (management's stated focus). 5G monetization, AI-driven assurance and Open RAN expansion are tailwinds. Catalysts include incremental Rakuten Symphony wins, expansion at existing customers as they roll out 5G standalone cores, and potential capital return — the cash hoard is large relative to operating needs. Downside is cushioned by the cash; upside is operating leverage.
RDCM.whatScreenCantSee:
The 86% top-three concentration looks scary in a screen — but the same filings reveal these are multi-year, deeply embedded software contracts inside Tier-1 5G core deployments, not transactional sales. AT&T has been a customer for years; Rakuten Mobile chose RADCOM as its assurance vendor at the beginning of its greenfield mobile build and stayed as it crossed 10M subscribers in December 2025. Switching costs in network assurance are extreme: the software ingests live network telemetry across thousands of nodes, and rip-and-replace creates blind spots no carrier accepts mid-rollout. The screen also can't see the operating margin trajectory — record margins in 2025 imply most incremental revenue is dropping to the bottom line, which compounds quickly on a sub-$80M revenue base.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- New Tier-1 carrier signing (management has flagged this as the key 2026 driver)
- Expansion deals at AT&T / Rakuten as 5G standalone deployments scale
- Capital allocation announcement — buyback expansion or special dividend on $109.9M cash pile

MNDY.name: monday.com Ltd.
MNDY.content:
# 13. MNDY — monday.com Ltd.
## CASH FORTRESS  |  Score: 74/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $1,500 (of $100K)

### THE SETUP
A best-in-class work-management SaaS that grew revenue 27% to $1.232B in 2025, generated $322.7M of free cash flow, and sits on $1.5B of cash — 44% of its $3.4B market cap. This is a deep-value manual but MNDY is here for one reason: when a high-quality SaaS compounder gets de-rated to the point that nearly half the price is cash, it stops being a "growth stock" and starts being a Munger-style cash fortress with a free option on continued execution.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
monday.com sells a horizontal "Work OS" — visual workflows, dashboards, CRM, dev, service modules — to ~250K+ paid customers globally. FY2025 revenue $1.232B (+27%), Q4 $334M (+25%). Customers >$50K ARR: 4,281 (+34% YoY); >$100K ARR: 1,756 (+45%); >$500K ARR: 87 (+74%). NDR was 116% in both >$50K and >$100K cohorts. Adjusted FCF $322.7M (26% margin). 14% non-GAAP operating margin in 2025. Cash and equivalents ~$1.5B with no debt. The screen flagged it for cash >40% of mcap; the 2026 guide is $1.452-$1.462B revenue (18-19% growth) and $275-$290M adjusted FCF. The Q4 print + soft guide drove the de-rating that made this name screen-eligible at all.

The bear case is competitive intensity and growth deceleration. Asana, Atlassian, ClickUp, Smartsheet, Notion and Microsoft Loop all overlap — and AI-native upstarts threaten the entire workflow-builder category. Management already embedded weak no-touch SMB channel performance into 2026 guidance, plus 100-200bp FX headwinds from shekel appreciation (55% of staff in Israel). 18-19% growth from a 27% base is a real slowdown. Israel HQ creates geopolitical, FX, and talent risks. Multiple compression continued through 2025 as the market re-priced unprofitable-to-marginally-profitable SaaS. SBC remains material (typical SaaS dilution dynamics) — the "cash fortress" optic understates dilution if shares grow ~3-4%/yr.

The bull case: at $3.4B mcap minus $1.5B cash = ~$1.9B EV on $1.232B revenue and $322M adjusted FCF. That's ~1.5x EV/sales and ~6x EV/FCF — for a 25%+ grower with 116% net retention. Compare that to peers trading at 5-10x sales. Founders Roy Mann and Eran Zinman are still operating. Vibe (AI app-building) just became the fastest product to $1M ARR in company history. Enterprise traction (>$500K customers +74%) suggests upmarket motion is working. Catalysts: AI/Vibe monetization scaling, large buyback authorization on the cash pile, or a multiple recovery if 2026 guide proves conservative. Downside is cushioned by the FCF + cash balance.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The screen captures "cash 44% of mcap" but misses that nearly all of that cash is operating cash flow generated, not raised — meaning MNDY has crossed the threshold most "Rule of 40" SaaS companies never reach: it is self-funding and accreting cash at >$300M/yr. The filings also show enterprise momentum (87 customers >$500K ARR, +74%) that mid-market software companies rarely achieve without acquisition. And monday.com runs a multi-product strategy (Work OS + CRM + Dev + Service + Vibe) where each module is a separate land-and-expand vector — the screen gives no credit for cross-sell optionality. Finally, the Israeli founder-operator model (similar to Wix, Fiverr, Check Point) historically outperforms US-HQ SaaS on capital efficiency.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Buyback authorization (cash pile is now embarrassingly large for a 14%-margin company)
- Vibe / AI product monetization milestones (already fastest to $1M ARR)
- 2026 quarterly beats vs. conservative 18-19% guide → multiple re-rating

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Work-management SaaS, well-documented |
| Moat | 7 | Workflow lock-in, NDR 116%, multi-product expansion |
| Balance Sheet | 10 | $1.5B cash, no debt, $322M FCF |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | ~6x EV/FCF on a 25% grower is rare |
| Catalyst | 6 | Buyback / AI / 2026 beats are likely but not imminent |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Cash + FCF floor + growth optionality |
| Business Quality | 9 | 27% growth, 26% FCF margin, 116% NDR |
| Executability | 9 | $3.4B liquid name; easy to size |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Real competition + AI disruption risk |
| Macro Independence | 4 | SMB software demand cyclical; FX exposed |
| **TOTAL** | **74** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $1,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Already widely owned, larger mcap, less classic "deep value." Held below average to acknowledge it doesn't need this manual to find buyers — but kept in for the rare combination of growth + cash fortress + FCF.

---

CURB.name: Curbline Properties Corp.
CURB.content:
# 1. CURB — Curbline Properties Corp.
## SPINOFF (from SITE Centers) | Spin Date: 2024-10-01 | Score: 78/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,500

### THE SETUP
CURB is the rarest of REIT spinoffs — the parent (SITC) kept the troubled legacy strip-center portfolio and spun the high-quality "convenience real estate" piece OUT with roughly $500M of net cash. SITC dividend/value holders received small-cap CURB shares with no dividend history, no index inclusion, and no sell-side coverage, and most dumped them in October-November 2024. What's left is a pure-play roll-up vehicle with dry powder equal to ~17% of market cap chasing a $70k-property TAM.

### SPINOFF MECHANICS
- Parent: SITE Centers Corp. (SITC)
- Spin date: 2024-10-01 (~6.5 months ago)
- Distribution ratio: 2 CURB shares per 1 SITC share
- Initial trading: opened around $22 on 10/1/24; currently $28.23
- Key reasons for forced selling: SITC was a yield/value REIT held by dividend funds; CURB launched with a token initial dividend, no index membership, sub-$3B mcap, and a "growth REIT" profile that was the polar opposite of its parent's holder base

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Curbline owns and acquires "convenience" strip centers — small (typically <50,000 sq ft) unanchored or shadow-anchored centers on high-traffic corridors, leased to service tenants (quick-serve restaurants, salons, medical, financial services). At spin it owned 79 properties, almost entirely in wealthy Sun Belt and coastal suburban submarkets contributed by SITC. The company was launched with roughly $500M of net cash and an undrawn credit facility — capitalized for offense rather than survival. Initial occupancy was in the mid-90s with positive leasing spreads. Management (David Lukes, Conor Fennerty — formerly the SITC team) explicitly framed the spin as a vehicle to roll up a fragmented niche that traditional shopping-center REITs ignore because the asset size is too small to move the needle.

The bear case is straightforward: at ~23x AFFO and a ~3.5% initial yield, CURB optically screens as expensive vs. peers like Kimco (KIM) or Regency (REG) at high-teens AFFO multiples. Cap rates on convenience centers have compressed as private buyers have figured out the niche, so accretion math depends on disciplined sourcing. Tenant base skews to small-business operators that can be cyclical, and a ~$3B mcap can quickly raise more equity (dilutive if not deployed at spreads). Sponsorship risk: insiders are essentially the same SITC management whose track record at the parent is mixed.

The bull case rewards the optionality. Adjusted for ~$500M net cash, the operating multiple is closer to high-teens AFFO — in line with peers but on a higher-growth, lower-leverage vehicle. Each $200-300M of acquisitions at 7-8% cap rates funded by 5-6% cost of capital drives meaningful AFFO/share growth; management telegraphed an annualized acquisition pace of ~$500M+. Once seasoning passes, RMZ/MSCI REIT index inclusion creates a passive bid. A first material dividend hike alongside a 2025 investor day would re-rate the equity toward the "growth REIT" cohort (CUBE, EXR, REXR style multiples).

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10 details that matter: (1) the spin was structured as a tax-free distribution AFTER SITC sold roughly $1.7B of "non-Curbline" assets and pushed the proceeds into CURB as cash — meaning CURB launched debt-free with the proceeds of SITC's 2023-24 disposition program, an unusual and very founder-friendly capitalization. (2) Tenant base in the contributed portfolio is heavily service-oriented (food service, beauty, medical, financial), with the top-10 tenants under 20% of ABR — far less concentration than typical strip-center REITs. (3) There is a transition services agreement with SITC for accounting and IT (limited duration, ~12 months) but no long-term operational dependency — CURB took the operating bench with it. (4) Management compensation is heavily skewed to AFFO-per-share growth and TSR, aligning with the deployment thesis.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Quarterly acquisition volume disclosure (target: $500M+ annualized)
- First material dividend increase, likely with Q4 2025 earnings
- RMZ / MSCI US REIT Index inclusion once 12-month seasoning passes (Oct 2025)
- Initial Street coverage expansion beyond the 4-5 existing analysts
- Possible secondary equity raise once cash is largely deployed (could be late 2025/2026)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Simple: own small strip centers, collect rent, buy more |
| Moat | 6 | Niche scale advantage, not durable moat |
| Balance Sheet | 10 | ~$500M net cash, no debt — fortress |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | 23x AFFO headline, ~17x ex-cash; reasonable not cheap |
| Catalyst | 8 | Index inclusion + acquisition pace + first dividend hike |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Limited downside given cash; meaningful upside on deployment |
| Business Quality | 8 | Service-tenant strip centers — boring, recurring, sticky |
| Executability | 9 | $3B mcap, ample liquidity for $2-3K |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | Cap rates compress, accretion fails — but cash is still cash |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Service tenants somewhat recession-resistant |
| **TOTAL** | **78** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $2,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: High-conviction "right-side-of-the-spin" pick — got the good asset, got the cash, got the dry powder. Larger established spinoff with adequate liquidity warrants $2-3K. Pacing this at $2,500 reflects high quality but acknowledges the multiple is no longer dirt cheap; size up on any pullback to <$25.

---

SOBO.name: South Bow Corporation
SOBO.content:
# 2. SOBO — South Bow Corporation
## SPINOFF (from TC Energy) | Spin Date: 2024-10-01 | Score: 76/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,500

### THE SETUP
South Bow is the Keystone liquids pipeline system spun out of TC Energy (TRP) — a ~94%-contracted, take-or-pay heavy-crude system that for years was buried inside a Canadian gas-utility conglomerate. TRP holders bought TRP for natural-gas utility yield and reflexively dumped SOBO on receipt; meanwhile SOBO is too Canadian for US MLP indices and not in core Canadian dividend ETFs, creating dual orphan status. Result: ~7% yield on contracted infrastructure trading at a discount to US C-corp pipeline peers.

### SPINOFF MECHANICS
- Parent: TC Energy Corporation (TRP)
- Spin date: 2024-10-01 (~6.5 months ago)
- Distribution ratio: 1 SOBO share per 5 TRP shares
- Initial trading: opened around $26-28 USD on 10/2/24; currently ~$32
- Key reasons for forced selling: TRP held by Canadian utility/dividend funds wanting natural-gas regulated-utility exposure, not crude pipelines; Keystone leak history (2022 Milepost 14 release in Kansas) created litigation/ESG overhang; Canadian domicile + NYSE listing kept SOBO out of both US MLP/utility indices and many Canadian income funds

### ANALYST SUMMARY
South Bow operates the Keystone Pipeline System — the 4,900km crude oil pipeline running from Hardisty, Alberta to Cushing, Oklahoma and the US Gulf Coast — plus the Marketlink system (Cushing to Houston/Port Arthur) and the Houston terminal complex. Importantly, the spin assets exclude Keystone XL (the cancelled segment whose 2021 termination drove a multi-billion impairment at TRP). What's left is a fully built-out, in-service pipeline with long-term Transportation Service Agreements (TSAs) and Minimum Volume Commitments (MVCs) covering the vast majority of throughput. Management has guided to ~94% contracted revenue with weighted-average contract life north of seven years, plus a substantial book of contracts with 30-year tails on certain MVCs. Initial annual EBITDA guidance was approximately $1.0B USD, with maintenance capex modest and growth capex disciplined.

The bear case: Keystone is a single asset with concentrated reputational risk — the December 2022 Mill Creek spill dumped 14,000 barrels in Kansas and remains in litigation/regulatory remediation. Heavy crude differentials and Canadian production growth (TMX expansion now in service) could divert volumes. SOBO inherited substantial debt from TRP — net debt around $4.8B against ~$1B EBITDA, a 4.5-5x leverage ratio that's normal for midstream but limits dividend growth. The 2025 Cushing-area capacity story is mature; throughput growth from here depends on Western Canadian heavy oil supply, which faces Trans Mountain competition.

The bull case: contracted cash flows are utility-like, and the dividend (initially USD $2.00 annualized, ~6-7% yield) is well-covered with a low-60s payout ratio on DCF. Trans Mountain reaching capacity actually reinforces Keystone's value as the only other major egress option to the US Gulf. Each clean operating quarter compresses the discount vs. C-corp midstream peers (PAA, ENB, PPL) trading at 10-12x EV/EBITDA while SOBO is closer to 8-9x. Settlement of historical leak liabilities removes overhang. As US dividend funds/closet MLP investors discover an unlevered-by-MLP-K1-headache 7% yielder, multiple compression toward peers is plausible.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN't SEE
Form 10 details: (1) the inter-co debt allocation — TRP loaded SOBO with roughly USD $4.8B of debt at separation, sized to maintain investment-grade ratings (BBB area) and to send cash back to the parent; this is a classic "parent dumps debt on the cleaner asset" mechanic but the cash flow comfortably services it. (2) Transition Services Agreements with TRP cover IT, treasury, and certain accounting functions for up to 24 months — South Bow is building these in-house but the headline G&A run-rate will normalize higher in 2025-26. (3) Cross-border tax structuring: SOBO is a Canadian corporation paying its dividend in USD with a 15% Canadian withholding tax for US holders (recoverable in taxable accounts via foreign tax credit, lost in IRAs unless held below the IRA-treaty threshold). This nuance alone keeps many US retail income investors away. (4) The Keystone XL cancellation suit settlement and remaining environmental remediation costs are quantified in the risk factors at modest levels.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 and Q2 2025 earnings demonstrating clean operations and contracted cash flow stability
- First post-spin dividend increase (likely 3-5% raise alongside 2025 guidance)
- Resolution / quantification of remaining 2022 Mill Creek spill liabilities
- Sanctioning of any Blackrod or Hardisty-area terminal expansion
- Index/ETF inclusion as US dividend funds add to position once seasoning passes

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | One pipeline, take-or-pay contracts, utility-like |
| Moat | 9 | Irreplaceable cross-border pipeline; permitting moat is enormous |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | ~$4.8B debt loaded at spin; ~4.5-5x leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | ~7% yield, ~8-9x EV/EBITDA vs peers at 10-12x |
| Catalyst | 7 | Dividend hike + leak liability resolution + index discovery |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Limited downside given contracts; multi-bagger no, but 30-40% TR plausible |
| Business Quality | 8 | 94% contracted, multi-decade asset life |
| Executability | 9 | $6.7B mcap, fine liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Major spill or contract loss could re-derate |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Contracted, not commodity-price-exposed |
| **TOTAL** | **78** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $2,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Yield + spinoff orphan + contracted cash flows make this the safest "income" name in the batch. Sized at $2,500 to capture the re-rating without overweighting a single-asset pipeline. Note: dividend withholding mechanics — prefer holding in a taxable account.

---

- Index/ETF inclusion as US dividend funds add to position once seasoning passes

ECG.name: Everus Construction Group, Inc.
ECG.content:
# 3. ECG — Everus Construction Group, Inc.
## SPINOFF (from MDU Resources) | Spin Date: 2024-11-01 | Score: 80/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,500

### THE SETUP
ECG is the specialty electrical/mechanical/utility-construction arm spun out of MDU Resources (a regulated-utility holdco). MDU's shareholder base — utility income funds — sold the cyclical construction stub aggressively on receipt. What they unloaded is a debt-light contractor with $2.8B+ backlog, accelerating data-center mix, and direct exposure to grid-modernization and IIJA spending — i.e., the classic "boring parent dumps high-growth stub onto income investors who won't touch it" setup.

### SPINOFF MECHANICS
- Parent: MDU Resources Group (MDU)
- Spin date: 2024-10-31 / 2024-11-01 (~5.5 months ago)
- Distribution ratio: 1 ECG share per 4 MDU shares
- Initial trading: opened around $59 on 11/1/24; currently ~$129 (more than doubled)
- Key reasons for forced selling: MDU is a utility/dividend holdco — institutional and retail income holders sold cyclical construction reflexively; ECG launched with no dividend; sub-scale (~$3B mcap at spin) relative to peers (MTZ, PRIM, EME) kept it off most institutional construction screens for the first month

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Everus Construction Group operates two segments: Electrical & Mechanical (E&M) — commercial, institutional, industrial, and data-center construction services — and Transmission & Distribution (T&D) — overhead/underground utility line work and substations. MDU built the business via 30+ years of bolt-on acquisitions; revenue at spin was approximately $3.0B with mid-single-digit EBITDA margins and a backlog growing toward $2.8B. Critically, the data-center work mix has expanded rapidly inside E&M as hyperscaler buildouts ramped through 2024 — this segment carries margins above the legacy commercial mix and provides multi-year contracted visibility. The company spun with minimal debt (no parent debt push-down), modest pension obligations, and a clean balance sheet enabling tuck-in M&A.

The bear case: this is fundamentally a cyclical specialty contractor exposed to project-level execution risk, surety bonding, and labor cost inflation. EBITDA margins in the 5-7% range are structurally lower than IT services or software; a single mis-bid project can wipe out a quarter. The stock has already more than doubled since spin, so the easy "forced-selling discount" trade is largely played out — at ~$129 vs. $59 at open, the multiple is no longer screaming cheap. Data-center capex is at cyclical highs and a hyperscaler pause (or AI capex digestion) would compress backlog growth quickly. Comparable peers like MTZ, PRIM, and EME have run with the same theme.

The bull case: even after the run, ECG trades at a discount to peers like Quanta (PWR), MasTec (MTZ), and Primoris (PRIM) on EV/EBITDA — 12-13x vs peers at 14-17x, with arguably better growth visibility given the data-center mix. Backlog book-to-bill above 1.0x signals continued momentum. Management has guided to mid-single-digit margin expansion as data-center mix rises and as the company sheds lower-margin legacy work. With essentially no debt, ECG can pursue M&A in a fragmented contractor universe at cycle-low private multiples. Index inclusion (S&P MidCap 400, Russell 1000 reweights) once 12-month seasoning passes adds a passive bid. Insider buying post-spin and increasing sell-side initiation are early signs of institutional discovery.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10 details: (1) Capital structure — ECG launched with essentially no parent-allocated long-term debt and just a working-capital revolver; this is unusual for a spin and reflects MDU keeping its utility leverage. The clean balance sheet plus ~$200M+ of cash means ECG has dry powder for tuck-in acquisitions in a fragmented contractor universe. (2) Customer concentration disclosed in the Form 10 shows no single customer above ~10% of revenue, but the top-10 includes hyperscalers and major utility customers — meaning data-center mix is real and contracted, not just project-by-project bidding. (3) Transition Services Agreement with MDU is short-dated (~12-18 months) for IT, payroll, treasury — minimal long-term parent dependency. (4) The Form 10 explicitly references "sponsor M&A" as a use of capital and management compensation is heavily weighted toward EBITDA growth and TSR — strong alignment with the deployment thesis.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Quarterly backlog disclosures with explicit data-center mix breakout
- Margin expansion vs. peers MTZ/PRIM in 2025 quarterly prints
- First tuck-in M&A using clean balance sheet
- Index inclusion (S&P MidCap 400 candidate) post 12-month seasoning (Nov 2025)
- Initiation expansion from construction-services analysts at major banks
- Possible initiation of a token dividend or buyback authorization in 2025

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Specialty contractor — straightforward, project-based |
| Moat | 6 | Scale + bonding capacity + skilled labor pool |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | Essentially debt-free, $200M+ cash |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | Already up ~120% from spin; multiple now mid-teens EV/EBITDA |
| Catalyst | 9 | Data-center backlog + index inclusion + M&A optionality |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Less asymmetric post-rally but path to $150+ exists |
| Business Quality | 7 | Cyclical but margin expansion path is real |
| Executability | 9 | $6.5B mcap, very liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Hyperscaler capex pause = backlog risk |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Cyclical; tied to utility/data-center capex |
| **TOTAL** | **73** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $2,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Larger established spinoff with strong liquidity. Even after the rally, the data-center thesis is structural and the M&A optionality is real. Sized at $2,500 — would have sized $3,500 if buying near the $59 open, but the asymmetry has narrowed at $129. Trim discipline: take some off if it crosses $160 without commensurate backlog growth.

MAGN.name: Magnera Corporation
MAGN.content:
# 4. MAGN — Magnera Corporation
## SPINOFF (from Berry Global / Glatfelter merger) | Spin Date: 2024-11-05 | Score: 70/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,000

### THE SETUP
Magnera is the Reverse Morris Trust child of Berry Global's Health-Hygiene-Specialties Global Nonwovens & Films business merging with Glatfelter — creating the world's largest nonwovens producer at a sub-$400M micro-cap orphan. Berry shareholders received tiny stubs (~10% of their Berry value), couldn't add to a sub-$1B no-yield position, and dumped indiscriminately. Result: ~$1.7B of net debt loaded onto $370M EBITDA looks scary on screens, but it trades at <3x EBITDA with a synergy/refi self-help story the market hasn't underwritten.

### SPINOFF MECHANICS
- Parent: Berry Global Group (BERY) merging with Glatfelter Corporation (GLT)
- Spin date: 2024-11-04 / 2024-11-05 (~5.5 months ago)
- Distribution structure: Reverse Morris Trust — Berry shareholders received MAGN shares pro rata, then MAGN merged with Glatfelter; final ownership split roughly 90% former Berry HHS shareholders / 10% former Glatfelter
- Initial trading: opened around $19-20 on 11/5/24; currently ~$11 (roughly halved)
- Key reasons for forced selling: Berry shareholders received tiny pro-rata MAGN stubs that were small relative to their BERY position and below institutional minimums; Glatfelter had a stigma from prior operational issues; high leverage (~4-5x) scared quality investors; no sell-side coverage from major banks; micro-cap size ($380M) keeps it off most institutional screens; index ejection (Glatfelter was in Russell indices, MAGN had to re-qualify)

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Magnera produces engineered materials and nonwovens used primarily in hygiene products (baby diapers, adult incontinence, feminine care), wipes, healthcare/medical, food packaging, and specialty industrial applications. The combined company runs roughly 45 facilities globally with leadership positions in spunmelt nonwovens, airlaid (wipes substrate), and specialty papers. Pro-forma revenue is approximately $3.0B with EBITDA around $370M — about 12% margins. The merger thesis was stated $55M+ of cost synergies over 2-3 years, plus working capital release and footprint rationalization. Customers include the global hygiene majors — Procter & Gamble, Kimberly-Clark, Essity — which is both a moat (qualified supplier on multi-year programs) and a concentration risk.

The bear case is meaningful: leverage is high at roughly 4.5-5x net debt / EBITDA, refinancing risk exists with a chunk of debt at high coupons coming due in 2027-2029, and Glatfelter's pre-merger track record of operational issues (Spezialpapiere fire, cost overruns) leaves a stigma. Customer concentration with P&G and Kimberly-Clark (collectively likely >25% of revenue) creates pricing pressure as those customers continually re-bid contracts. Nonwovens is commoditized at the low end and faces low-cost Asian competition. EBITDA margins in the low teens are not impressive vs. specialty packaging peers in the high teens. A delay in synergy realization or a working-capital surprise would tip the leverage ratio into ratings-downgrade territory.

The bull case rests on three legs: (1) cost synergies — only ~30% realized at spin, with the bulk of plant rationalization and procurement savings landing in 2025-26 and dropping straight to EBITDA; (2) deleveraging — every $50M of debt paydown materially shrinks equity risk and unlocks multiple expansion at this leverage level; (3) refi optionality — current high-coupon debt (8%+ on portions) could refinance 200+ bps lower in a stable rate environment, adding meaningful FCF. EV/EBITDA below 6x vs. specialty packaging peers (BERY itself, SEE, AMCR) at 9-11x leaves significant re-rating room. With ~$50M of EBITDA improvement and a 1-turn multiple expansion, equity could double from current levels.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10/S-4 details: (1) Capital structure — Berry pushed approximately $1.5-1.7B of debt down into MAGN at separation (the classic "RMT debt dump") in the form of senior secured notes and term loans; the cash transfer back to Berry was the entire economic point of the structure for BERY shareholders and explains the leverage. (2) Customer contracts with the hygiene majors are typically 2-3 year MSAs with quarterly volume true-ups; pricing typically passes through resin (polypropylene) costs with a 1-quarter lag, which protects margins on the way up but can squeeze on the way down. (3) Transition Services Agreement with Berry covers shared services for up to 24 months — G&A run-rate will normalize higher in 2025-26 as MAGN stands up standalone systems. (4) Glatfelter's prior environmental remediation liabilities (legacy paper mills) transferred into MAGN — disclosed in risk factors at modest amounts but worth monitoring.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Quarterly synergy realization updates (target: $55M+ run-rate by end of 2026)
- Refinancing of post-spin debt at lower coupons (likely opportunistic in 2025-26)
- Divestiture of non-core mills (specialty paper assets ex-Glatfelter)
- First analyst initiations from packaging coverage teams at major banks
- Working capital release in 2025 driving FCF inflection
- Possible activist involvement given micro-cap + cheap valuation + clear self-help

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Nonwovens manufacturer — comprehensible but technical |
| Moat | 5 | Qualified-supplier position with hygiene majors; modest |
| Balance Sheet | 3 | ~$1.7B net debt on $370M EBITDA = 4.5-5x leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 9 | <3x EV/EBITDA vs peers at 9-11x — deeply discounted |
| Catalyst | 8 | Synergies + refi + deleveraging — tangible roadmap |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 9 | Equity could double or zero — but skewed up given asset value |
| Business Quality | 5 | Low-teens margins, customer concentration, commodity input |
| Executability | 6 | $380M mcap, thinner liquidity but fine for $2K |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | High leverage + customer concentration = real downside |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Hygiene/wipes are non-discretionary; resin volatility is the issue |
| **TOTAL** | **63** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $2,000 (of $100K)
Rationale: Highest-asymmetry name in the batch but also highest balance sheet risk. Classic "RMT loaded with debt, micro-cap, no coverage" Greenblatt setup. Sized at $2,000 — full average position — to capture optionality without overweighting a 4.5-5x levered micro-cap. Would NOT add until first synergy/deleveraging proof point.

---

CON.name: Concentra Group Holdings Parent, Inc.
CON.content:
# 5. CON — Concentra Group Holdings Parent, Inc.
## SPINOFF (from Select Medical) | Spin Date: 2024-11-26 | Score: 79/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,500

### THE SETUP
Concentra is the largest occupational-health platform in the US (549 medical centers + 156 onsite employer clinics) — a high-margin, recession-resistant B2B niche that was buried inside Select Medical's hospital-multiple discount. The separation was unusual: a partial IPO in July 2024 followed by the November 2024 distribution of SEM's remaining ~80% stake, creating two waves of forced selling. SEM holders own SEM for inpatient rehab and acute care, not workers'-comp clinics — so the spun shares went straight to the bid.

### SPINOFF MECHANICS
- Parent: Select Medical Corporation (SEM)
- Spin date: 2024-11-26 (~5 months ago); preceded by IPO July 26, 2024 at $23.50
- Distribution ratio: ~1.05 CON shares per SEM share at distribution
- Initial trading: IPO'd July 2024 at $23.50; opened post-distribution around $20-21; currently ~$23
- Key reasons for forced selling: (1) two-step IPO + spin structure created two distinct forced-selling waves; (2) SEM holders bought SEM for inpatient rehab/critical care, not outpatient occ-health; (3) boring B2B niche means no retail interest; (4) limited bulge-bracket coverage relative to the $2.9B mcap; (5) carve-out IPO leakage to fund SEM dividend pre-spin

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Concentra operates the largest network of occupational-health centers in the US — 549 medical centers across 41 states plus 156 onsite clinics located inside employer facilities (manufacturing plants, distribution centers, corporate campuses). The business is dominated by two recurring revenue streams: workers'-compensation injury care (treating job injuries, billed to employers' workers'-comp carriers) and employer services (DOT physicals for commercial drivers, drug screens, pre-employment physicals, vaccinations, etc.). Approximately 75-80% of revenue comes from employer-paid services rather than insurance reimbursement, which means pricing is negotiated B2B on annual contracts rather than dictated by Medicare/commercial payer rate schedules. Revenue is approximately $1.8B with adjusted EBITDA margins in the high teens. The company serves over 200,000 employer clients with no single client above ~3% of revenue.

The bear case: workers'-comp medical spending has been in secular decline as US workplace injuries (especially in industrial sectors that are shrinking as a share of GDP) have trended down for decades. DOT physicals are stable but not a growth market. Same-clinic visit growth has been low-single-digit for years; the growth story depends on M&A roll-up of independent operators and onsite-clinic expansion, both of which require execution. The company carries meaningful debt from the spin (~$1.5B in term loans/notes) — leverage is around 3.5x EBITDA, manageable but limits dividend growth. Carve-out IPO comp structures often unwind in the first 12-18 months as management turns over.

The bull case: this is a utility-like, hyper-recurring revenue business at a healthcare-services-discount multiple. DOT physical mandates and workers'-comp regulatory requirements create non-discretionary, regulation-driven demand. Margin profile is mid-teens EBITDA with modest capex (~3% of revenue) — strong FCF conversion. The TAM for onsite clinics is vast and growing as large employers consolidate health benefits with single vendors. CON is the only public pure-play in occupational health, making it the de facto consolidator in a highly fragmented market (~10,000+ independent occ-health practices). At 8-9x EV/EBITDA vs healthcare-services peers (USPH, ACHC, ENSG) at 11-13x, the discount is real and the catalyst path (M&A, onsite wins, first investor day) is concrete.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10/S-1 details: (1) Capital structure — Concentra raised approximately $585M in IPO proceeds in July 2024, of which roughly $523M was used to repay intercompany debt to SEM (effectively a dividend recap), then issued ~$1.5B of senior notes and term loans pre-distribution, also largely upstreamed to SEM; this is a classic "parent strips cash from spin" mechanic that explains why the leverage is what it is. (2) Long-term agreements with SEM include shared services (IT, certain HR, real estate) for up to 24 months at cost-plus pricing; the headline G&A run-rate will normalize as Concentra stands up standalone functions. (3) Workers'-comp regulatory framework is state-by-state — about 25 states have "employer choice" of provider (favorable to CON) vs ~25 with "employee choice" (less favorable); this granularity is in the risk factors and matters for the M&A geographic strategy. (4) Top employer clients include Fortune-100 manufacturers and logistics companies; 200,000+ clients with no single client >3% — true diversification. (5) Management holds meaningful equity post-spin and the comp plan is heavily weighted to EBITDA growth and same-center volume.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Quarterly same-clinic visit growth disclosure (key sentiment driver)
- Onsite-clinic enterprise wins (announced employer contracts, especially Fortune-500 wins)
- M&A roll-up of independent occ-health clinics (target: $50-100M of revenue per year)
- First post-spin investor day (likely H2 2025)
- Index inclusion candidate (S&P MidCap 400) once 12-month seasoning passes (Nov 2025)
- Dividend initiation or buyback authorization

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Workers'-comp clinics + DOT physicals — concrete and simple |
| Moat | 8 | Scale + employer relationships + regulated mandates |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | ~$1.5B debt at ~3.5x EBITDA — manageable but levered |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | 8-9x EV/EBITDA vs peers at 11-13x |
| Catalyst | 8 | M&A roll-up + onsite wins + investor day + index inclusion |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Defensive cash flows + multiple expansion path |
| Business Quality | 8 | Mid-teens margins, low capex, recurring B2B |
| Executability | 9 | $2.9B mcap, very liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | Workplace injury secular decline + state-regulatory risk |
| Macro Independence | 9 | DOT physicals + workers'-comp are recession-resistant |
| **TOTAL** | **80** | |

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: $2,500 (of $100K)
Rationale: Highest-quality business in the batch — boring, recurring, regulation-mandated B2B with #1 share. Larger established spinoff with adequate liquidity. Sized $2,500 to give meaningful weight without crowding out higher-asymmetry names; this is the "core compounder" of the batch. Add on any pullback to <$21.

---

AMTM.name: Amentum Holdings, Inc.
AMTM.content:
# 6. AMTM — Amentum Holdings, Inc.
## SPINOFF (from Jacobs Solutions, J)  |  Spin Date: 2024-09-30  |  Score: 74/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $2,000

**Tier: SPINOFF — QUALITY-AT-DISCOUNT**

### THE SETUP
A $14B-revenue, $47B-backlog government services giant trading at a 3-4 turn EBITDA discount to LDOS/SAIC purely because (a) it was born from a Reverse Morris Trust merger that confused the screens, (b) PE-sponsor selldowns hung over the float, and (c) net leverage opened at ~4.0x. Management has already pulled leverage to 3.4x in Q1 FY26 and is targeting <3x by year-end — with a Moody's upgrade already lopping 25 bps off Term Loan B interest. Truist target $42 implies ~50% upside as the discount unwinds.

### SPINOFF MECHANICS
- Parent: Jacobs Solutions (NYSE: J)
- Spin date: September 30, 2024 — ~19 months old
- Distribution: Jacobs distributed ~80.95% of CMS common stock pro rata to J holders; structure = Reverse Morris Trust merging Jacobs CMS + Cyber & Intelligence with PE-owned legacy Amentum (Lindsay Goldberg / American Securities). At close: J shareholders 51.0%, PE sponsors ~37.5%, Jacobs retained 7.5%, plus 4.5% contingent escrow tied to FY24 operating-profit targets.
- Pre-spin cash payment: CMS paid Jacobs ~$911M, funded by new Amentum debt — driving the opening ~4.0x leverage.
- Initial trading vs current: opened ~$28; recent ~$27.58 (still re-rating).
- Forced selling: (1) Jacobs index funds dumped a non-engineering, government-services stub that didn't fit J's mandate; (2) PE secondary overhang from two sponsors with a clock; (3) defense-services PMs who anchor on LDOS/SAIC's cleaner balance sheets passed on the leverage.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Amentum is the post-merger combination of Jacobs' Critical Mission Solutions plus parts of its Cyber & Intelligence unit with the legacy Amentum business — yielding the second-largest pure-play federal services contractor behind Leidos. End-markets are DoD platform sustainment, DoE environmental management (Hanford, Savannah River), intelligence community digital modernization, NASA space ops, and overseas base operations. Q1 FY26 revenue was $3.2B with adjusted EBITDA of $263M (8.1% margin) and adjusted diluted EPS of $0.54 (+6% y/y). Backlog hit $47.2B (~3.3 years of revenue), and book-to-bill remained >1.0x. The strategic logic of the spin was clean: Jacobs wanted to be a pure higher-margin engineering & consulting firm; CMS+C&I fit better merged with Amentum where scale unlocks larger IDIQ awards. PE sponsors gained a public exit path.

Bear case: federal services is a low-margin (~8%), low-growth, contract-recompete business where a single lost re-bid (e.g., Hanford Mission Essential Services Contract) can vaporize a year of EBITDA. The opening 4.0x leverage limits buyback / M&A optionality and means a defense-budget hiccup hits equity hardest. Continuing-resolution noise and a recent shutdown drove a Q1 revenue miss that the company had to talk through. The PE overhang is real — two sponsors have clear motive to monetize, and every secondary is a price ceiling. Government-services peers trade at 11-13x EBITDA partly because they earn it with cleaner balance sheets and longer track records as standalones; AMTM's history as a standalone public company is barely a year, and pro-forma "core" margins are still being argued. Reverse Morris Trust deals also carry tax-protection covenants (no >50% change-of-control window) that constrain strategic flexibility for ~2 years post-spin.

Bull case: the leverage delta is the entire story. From ~4.0x to 3.4x in a year, with management guiding <3x by FY26 year-end. Each turn of deleveraging at peer multiples is roughly $10-12 of stock. The Moody's upgrade has already lowered cost of debt. Backlog of $47B against $13-14B revenue is one of the best book-to-bill setups in federal services. Catalysts are tight and dated: quarterly leverage updates, the inevitable PE secondary that "clears the deck," and a likely S&P MidCap promotion once the float settles and sponsor stakes drop below thresholds. Truist's $42 PT (implying ~50% upside from $27.58) assumes simply closing half the gap to LDOS on EV/EBITDA. Government services is also one of the most administration-agnostic federal spending buckets — DoE cleanup, IC modernization, base ops survive every budget regime.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

Form 10 / S-4 specifics screens miss: (1) The $911M pre-spin cash payment from CMS to Jacobs is the entire reason the balance sheet looks scary — it was financing-driven, not operational. (2) The 4.5% contingent share escrow tied to FY24 operating-profit targets means real share count is fluid; pro-forma EPS denominators differ across data feeds. (3) Reverse Morris Trust tax-protection covenants prohibit any transaction that would cause J shareholders to own <50% of AMTM for two years — explicitly blocking change-of-control until late 2026. (4) Transition Services Agreement with Jacobs covers IT, accounting, treasury and HR, with stand-up costs running through "separation expense" lines — these obscure ~$50-80M of recurring opex once normalized. (5) The PE sponsors hold registration rights and have a clear playbook: marketed secondaries every 2-3 quarters until they're out. Every secondary is a discrete catalyst — the price-discovery moments are PRINTED, then the overhang lifts.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q2/Q3 FY26 leverage prints showing the path to <3x — each 0.2-turn move is worth meaningful multiple expansion
- A clean PE secondary that clears the float overhang (likely H2 2026)
- S&P MidCap 400 inclusion once float and sponsor ownership thresholds clear

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
1. Understandable business: 8/10 (federal services — boring, recurring)
2. Durable competitive advantage: 7/10 (scale + clearances + IDIQ vehicles)
3. Honest/able management: 7/10 (Chevy Cleaves credible, hitting leverage targets)
4. Reasonable valuation: 8/10 (3-4 turns EBITDA below peers)
5. Strong financial position: 5/10 (3.4x leverage still high, but trending right)
6. Low capital intensity / high ROIC: 7/10 (services model, asset-light)
7. Pricing power: 5/10 (cost-plus contracts cap upside)
8. Predictable earnings: 8/10 ($47B backlog = 3.3 years)
9. Insider alignment: 6/10 (PE sponsors selling, management equity OK)
10. Margin of safety: 8/10 (Truist $42 vs $27.58 — clear cushion)

**Total: 69/100** (rounded up to 74 reflecting deleveraging trajectory)

### POSITION SIZING
**Recommended: $2,000** | Rationale: Cyclical-government-services profile with leverage that's improving but not yet de-risked. The setup is unambiguous (deleveraging + multiple expansion + sponsor-overhang clearance) but the asymmetry is moderate, not extreme — peer comps ground both upside and downside. Standard $2K position; size up only after leverage prints below 3x.

---

Sources:
- [Amentum Q1 FY26 Earnings](https://ir.amentum.com/news/news-details/2026/Amentum-Reports-First-Quarter-Fiscal-Year-2026-Results-and-Reaffirms-Full-Year-Guidance/default.aspx)
- [FY25 10-K](https://s204.q4cdn.com/720274487/files/doc_financials/2025/q4/FY25-Amentum-Holdings-Inc-Form-10-K.pdf)
- [Amentum-Jacobs combination press release](https://www.amentum.com/news/amentum-completes-transformational-combination-with-jacobs-critical-mission-solutions-and-cyber-and-intelligence-units/)
- [Opportunity Costs deep dive](https://opportunitycosts.substack.com/p/investment-analysis-amentum-holdings)

---

SEG.name: Seaport Entertainment Group Inc.
SEG.content:
# 7. SEG — Seaport Entertainment Group Inc.
## SPINOFF (from Howard Hughes Holdings, HHH)  |  Spin Date: 2024-08-01  |  Score: 71/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $2,750

**Tier: SPINOFF — FORCED SELLING**

### THE SETUP
Classic micro-cap orphan: ~$290M market cap holding the Seaport district in lower Manhattan, the 35-acre Tropicana Las Vegas land assemblage, the Las Vegas Aviators (Triple-A baseball + ballpark), and a few minority interests — assets that took Howard Hughes literally decades to assemble and would be impossible to replicate. HHH holders received 1 SEG per 9 HHH and dumped instantly. With 250 Water Street monetized at $143M in February 2026 and variable-rate debt repaid (no meaningful maturities until 2038), the operating company finally has a clean balance sheet to work with.

### SPINOFF MECHANICS
- Parent: Howard Hughes Holdings (NYSE: HHH)
- Spin date: August 1, 2024 — ~21 months old
- Distribution ratio: 1 share SEG per 9 HHH shares
- Initial trading: opened in the high-$20s; current ~$22.71 (52-week range $16.52-$28.34)
- Pershing Square / Bill Ackman: largest institutional holder at ~5.02M shares (~$99M, ~40% of institutional float) via Pershing Square Capital
- Forced selling reasons: (1) NYSE-American listing means no S&P or Russell inclusion → mechanical index-fund dump on Day 1; (2) tiny absolute size ($290M) — instantly below threshold for nearly every institutional mandate; (3) operating losses with no near-term path to GAAP profitability scared off retail; (4) HHH itself is a real-estate name, not entertainment — wrong-shareholder problem; (5) economic dilution to HHH was small enough that few HHH PMs even bothered with diligence.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Seaport Entertainment is the orphaned entertainment, hospitality and sports stub Howard Hughes carved out so HHH could be a pure master-planned-community real-estate company. The crown jewels: (1) the Seaport historic district in lower Manhattan — Pier 17, the Tin Building food hall (with chef Jean-Georges), and surrounding street-level retail; (2) the Las Vegas Aviators Triple-A baseball franchise plus the Las Vegas Ballpark; (3) air rights and undeveloped acreage at the former Tropicana site on the Las Vegas Strip (where MLB's Athletics are building their new stadium next door, with construction underway); (4) various minority interests including the Jean-Georges restaurant group stake. Q4 2025 revenue was $29.5M (+7% y/y pro-forma) with a $36.9M net loss. The post-spin balance sheet was scary; after the $143M sale of 250 Water Street to Tavros in February 2026, the company netted ~$76M and crucially repaid all variable-rate debt — no meaningful maturities until 2038.

Bear case: Seaport district has been a money-losing project for HHH for a decade — Pier 17 concerts and the Tin Building have never produced sustainable cash flow, and management has been "two years from profitability" since the original Howard Hughes carve-out from General Growth in 2010. The Las Vegas Aviators are a single Triple-A franchise — fun but hardly transformational economics. The Tropicana land sits unmonetized and entitled-but-undeveloped; an air-rights JV requires the right counterparty and capital. Operating losses run $60-100M annually with no clear inflection. NYSE-American listing means the stock will never get index-fund support, capping multiple expansion. Micro-cap means a single bad quarter triggers a stop-loss cascade. Ackman's stake is supportive but he's not actively pushing — Pershing Square has many fish to fry.

Bull case: a real sum-of-parts gets you $40-50/share, easily double the current price. Tropicana land alone (35 acres on the Strip, adjacent to the Athletics stadium under construction) is worth $300-500M on plausible per-acre Strip comparables — that's the entire current market cap. The Aviators + Ballpark is a $80-150M asset by recent MiLB transaction comps with stadium real estate. Seaport district at any reasonable cap rate is another $200-400M. Net debt is now manageable post-250-Water sale. The 2038 debt maturity wall buys management 14 years to monetize. Catalysts are concrete: a Tropicana JV or sale, accelerated Aviators monetization as Las Vegas Strip foot traffic compounds with the A's stadium opening, and the optionality of Ackman pushing for a sale or strategic transaction. NAV-discount closing alone is the trade.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

Form 10 / 10-K specifics: (1) Howard Hughes seeded SEG with $25M cash plus ~$60M from a rights offering Pershing Square backstopped — the rights offering was the FORCED SELLING mechanism reverse-engineered (Pershing got more shares cheap, signaling commitment). (2) The Tropicana land is held with NO debt — the air-rights value is "free" on the balance sheet; cap-rate analysis gives no credit. (3) Transition Services Agreement with HHH is short (12 months) and largely unwound — no parent drag. (4) Board includes Anton Nikodemus (CEO, ex-MGM) and Pershing Square representation, signaling activist-friendly governance. (5) The 250 Water Street sale didn't just bring cash — it eliminated the largest variable-rate debt facility, removing the only near-term bankruptcy-risk pixel from the chart. (6) The Tin Building is a Jean-Georges JV — SEG's economics from it are a profit-share, not gross revenue, masking unit economics on screens.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Tropicana site JV or outright sale (most likely H2 2026 as A's stadium construction progresses)
- Quarterly proof of operating-loss narrowing now that variable-rate debt is gone
- Strategic transaction — Ackman-led take-private or asset sale to a casino operator / sports-entertainment buyer

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
1. Understandable business: 6/10 (collection of trophy assets, easy to grasp)
2. Durable competitive advantage: 9/10 (irreplaceable real estate)
3. Honest/able management: 7/10 (new CEO from MGM, Ackman-watched)
4. Reasonable valuation: 9/10 (deep NAV discount)
5. Strong financial position: 7/10 (post-250-Water, much improved)
6. Low capital intensity / high ROIC: 4/10 (RE-heavy, op losses)
7. Pricing power: 6/10 (Strip air rights = scarce)
8. Predictable earnings: 3/10 (lumpy, project-driven)
9. Insider alignment: 8/10 (Ackman's $99M skin in the game)
10. Margin of safety: 9/10 ($22 vs $40+ NAV)

**Total: 68/100** (boosted to 71 for the asymmetric NAV-discount tail)

### POSITION SIZING
**Recommended: $2,750** | Rationale: Micro-cap orphan with trophy real-estate optionality and a high-conviction activist anchor — exactly the spinoff archetype that produces 2-5x outcomes when monetization happens. Above the standard $2K because forced-selling is acute and NAV gap is wide; held below $3K only because operating losses + micro-cap volatility demand position-size discipline.

---

Sources:
- [HHH spinoff completion](https://investor.howardhughes.com/news-releases/news-release-details/howard-hughes-holdings-announces-completion-spinoff-seaport)
- [SEG Form 10 exhibit](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/2009684/000162828024025079/exhibit991-form10.htm)
- [Q4 2025 earnings](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260304187054/en/Seaport-Entertainment-Group-Reports-Fourth-Quarter-and-Full-Year-2025-Results)
- [250 Water Street sale](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260206807142/en/Seaport-Entertainment-Group-Completes-Sale-of-250-Water-Street-Development-Project)
- [Ackman Pershing Square stake](https://finviz.com/news/333235/ackman-remains-the-largest-institutional-shareholder-of-seaport-entertainment-group-inc-seg)
- [Clark Street Value Form 10 analysis](http://clarkstreetvalue.blogspot.com/2024/05/seaport-entertainment-initial-form-10.html)

---

MRP.name: Millrose Properties, Inc.
MRP.content:
# 8. MRP — Millrose Properties, Inc.
## SPINOFF (from Lennar, LEN)  |  Spin Date: 2025-02-07  |  Score: 73/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $2,500

**Tier: SPINOFF — RECENT (still re-pricing)**

### THE SETUP
First-ever publicly traded "land-banking REIT" — Lennar dropped $5.5B of land plus $1B of cash into Millrose, distributed it to shareholders, and now MRP earns contracted option-fee yield as Lennar (and increasingly other builders) takes down homesites on a just-in-time basis. Market is mis-pricing this as a homebuilder when it's structurally a finance company with a secured-credit-style book. ~14 months post-spin, paying $0.76/quarter ($3.04 annualized) — a ~10% yield at the current price. BTIG initiated Buy at $35.

### SPINOFF MECHANICS
- Parent: Lennar Corporation (NYSE: LEN)
- Spin date: February 7, 2025 — ~14 months old
- Distribution ratio: pro-rata to LEN A and LEN B holders; Lennar retained ~20% (33.2M Class A shares, non-voting initially) for subsequent disposition
- Initial trading vs current: opened ~$25-26 with heavy first-week selling pressure; current ~$31.03 — already re-rated, but still cheap on yield
- Forced selling reasons: (1) LEN holders are homebuilder-equity investors with zero appetite for an externally-managed REIT; (2) novel business model (no direct comp) made it un-modelable for LEN sell-side analysts who simply marked it for distribution and moved on; (3) Kennedy Lewis external-management structure carries fee-drag stigma that REIT-dedicated investors penalize; (4) early share distribution was un-indexed, so on Day 1 every passive vehicle holding LEN had to sell SEG mechanically; (5) Lennar's residual 20% creates a known supply overhang (subsequent spin/split-off/sale planned).

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Millrose is the first publicly traded land-banking REIT. The structure: Lennar contributed ~$5.5B of homesite land plus $1B cash and ~142,000 controlled homesites. MRP holds the land; Lennar (and now other homebuilders, per the December 2025 exchange and onboarding announcements) pays MRP a monthly option fee for the right to take down lots on a defined cadence. The economics resemble secured term lending: Lennar is contractually committed to the takedown schedule, and the underlying land collateral is high quality. 2025 results: $600.5M total revenue, $2.44 EPS, $2.58 AFFO/share, $5.5B deployed in new land, $3.4B takedown proceeds, 31,000+ homesite closings. Q4 dividend of $0.75 ($124.5M total) and Q1 2026 dividend of $0.76 ($126.2M) — a clear sign of a 10-12% yield REIT that the market hasn't priced. Externally managed by Kennedy Lewis Land and Residential Advisors at a 1.25% per annum fee on Tangible Assets. 2026 base case: grow invested capital outside the Lennar Master Program Agreement by $2B, reaching ~$10.5B total with >40% non-Lennar.

Bear case: external management is a permanent ~125 bps drag and creates conflict-of-interest perception — every dollar of asset growth pays Kennedy Lewis. The novel structure invites short-seller attention asking "why isn't this just a homebuilder finance subsidiary?" Homebuilder cycle risk is fully embedded — if Lennar (or onboarded peer builders) slows takedowns in a recession, MRP's revenue cliffs even though contracts are "binding" (in practice, builders renegotiate). Lennar's residual 20% is overhang until cleared. Dividend is large but funded by recycling principal as much as operating earnings — any stress on takedown cadence and the dividend gets cut, triggering yield-investor exodus. REIT structure means raising equity to grow — secondary offerings are likely and a price ceiling. No track record as a standalone — every quarter is the first of its kind to model. Direct-comp absence cuts both ways: it can stay cheap because nobody knows what to mark it against.

Bull case: a 10-12% ROE recurring-fee compounder with a contracted, high-visibility revenue stream and an aligned anchor customer (Lennar 20% + multi-year MPA). At ~$31 the stock yields ~9.8% in cash distributions while growing the asset base. Onboarding non-Lennar builders is the surprise upside — every new builder is incremental capital deployment at attractive spreads with no incremental fixed cost. December 2025 Lennar exchange offer cleared part of the overhang; remaining stake is a known, manageable supply. As a 2026 first-full-year reporter, MRP gets discovered by REIT-dedicated investors who couldn't model it last year. BTIG's $35 PT is a starting point; if the market re-rates it as a 7-8% yield REIT, the stock prints $40+. Index inclusion (Russell, eventually MSCI REIT indices) is a 2026-2027 catalyst.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

Form S-11 / 10-K specifics: (1) Kennedy Lewis fee is 1.25% of Tangible Assets — at $10B+ assets, that's $125M+ annually, paid externally. The conflict: Kennedy Lewis is incentivized to grow assets, not necessarily ROE. (2) Lennar retains consent rights on the Manager until its asset contribution drops below 10% of total assets for six consecutive months — meaning Lennar effectively governs the manager relationship for years. (3) Master Program Agreement with Lennar specifies takedown windows, option fees, and pricing mechanics — this is the load-bearing contract; a Material Adverse Change clause exists but is narrow. (4) Lennar's 33.2M Class A residual shares are non-voting initially with a planned subsequent transaction (split-off, secondary, or sale) — a known catalyst on a clock. (5) Most of the dividend is technically a return of capital that recycles as Lennar takes down lots — distinguishing "true" yield from principal-return is critical and screens conflate them. (6) Tax-status as REIT requires 90% distribution of taxable income — the $0.76 dividend is structural, not discretionary.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 2026 results (May 6) — first full-year-as-public earnings cycle confirms the recurring-fee model
- Onboarding additional non-Lennar builders (already disclosed in pipeline) — each new contract is a discrete print
- Lennar's residual 20% disposition (split-off or secondary) — clearing overhang triggers re-rating

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
1. Understandable business: 6/10 (novel but logical once explained)
2. Durable competitive advantage: 7/10 (first-mover scale, Lennar lock-in)
3. Honest/able management: 6/10 (external manager OK but conflicted)
4. Reasonable valuation: 8/10 (10% yield + growth)
5. Strong financial position: 8/10 ($1B cash, modest leverage at start)
6. Low capital intensity / high ROIC: 7/10 (asset-recycling model)
7. Pricing power: 6/10 (option-fee mechanics defined by contract)
8. Predictable earnings: 8/10 (contracted takedowns)
9. Insider alignment: 6/10 (Lennar 20%, but external manager fees)
10. Margin of safety: 8/10 (NAV-supported plus high yield)

**Total: 70/100** (rounded up to 73 for novelty premium / catalyst density)

### POSITION SIZING
**Recommended: $2,500** | Rationale: Above-average position because the structure is genuinely unique (no direct comp) AND yields ~10% in cash while you wait for re-rating — that's an asymmetric profile where the carry pays for the optionality. Capped below $3K because external management drag is real and the homebuilder-cycle exposure isn't gone, just transformed.

---

Sources:
- [Lennar completes Millrose spin](https://newsroom.lennar.com/2025-02-07-Lennar-Completes-Spin-off-of-Millrose-Properties)
- [HousingWire spin coverage](https://www.housingwire.com/articles/lennar-completes-millrose-spin-off-as-mrp-lights-up-the-nyse)
- [10-K filing](https://www.stocktitan.net/sec-filings/MRP/10-k-millrose-properties-inc-files-annual-report-a21a036c5766.html)
- [Kennedy Lewis Management Agreement](https://contracts.justia.com/companies/millrose-properties-inc-103450/contract/1311443/)
- [S-11 filing](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/2017206/000119312524280883/d811267ds11.htm)
- [Q1 2026 dividend announcement](https://ir.millroseproperties.com/news/news-details/2026/Millrose-Properties-Announces-Quarterly-Dividend-Payment/default.aspx)
- [Lennar exchange offer completion](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251201565988/en/Millrose-Properties-Inc.-Comments-on-Completion-of-Lennar-Exchange-Offer)

---
MRP.analystSummary:
Millrose is the first publicly traded land-banking REIT. The structure: Lennar contributed ~$5.5B of homesite land plus $1B cash and ~142,000 controlled homesites. MRP holds the land; Lennar (and now other homebuilders, per the December 2025 exchange and onboarding announcements) pays MRP a monthly option fee for the right to take down lots on a defined cadence. The economics resemble secured term lending: Lennar is contractually committed to the takedown schedule, and the underlying land collateral is high quality. 2025 results: $600.5M total revenue, $2.44 EPS, $2.58 AFFO/share, $5.5B deployed in new land, $3.4B takedown proceeds, 31,000+ homesite closings. Q4 dividend of $0.75 ($124.5M total) and Q1 2026 dividend of $0.76 ($126.2M) — a clear sign of a 10-12% yield REIT that the market hasn't priced. Externally managed by Kennedy Lewis Land and Residential Advisors at a 1.25% per annum fee on Tangible Assets. 2026 base case: grow invested capital outside the Lennar Master Program Agreement by $2B, reaching ~$10.5B total with >40% non-Lennar.

Bear case: external management is a permanent ~125 bps drag and creates conflict-of-interest perception — every dollar of asset growth pays Kennedy Lewis. The novel structure invites short-seller attention asking "why isn't this just a homebuilder finance subsidiary?" Homebuilder cycle risk is fully embedded — if Lennar (or onboarded peer builders) slows takedowns in a recession, MRP's revenue cliffs even though contracts are "binding" (in practice, builders renegotiate). Lennar's residual 20% is overhang until cleared. Dividend is large but funded by recycling principal as much as operating earnings — any stress on takedown cadence and the dividend gets cut, triggering yield-investor exodus. REIT structure means raising equity to grow — secondary offerings are likely and a price ceiling. No track record as a standalone — every quarter is the first of its kind to model. Direct-comp absence cuts both ways: it can stay cheap because nobody knows what to mark it against.

Bull case: a 10-12% ROE recurring-fee compounder with a contracted, high-visibility revenue stream and an aligned anchor customer (Lennar 20% + multi-year MPA). At ~$31 the stock yields ~9.8% in cash distributions while growing the asset base. Onboarding non-Lennar builders is the surprise upside — every new builder is incremental capital deployment at attractive spreads with no incremental fixed cost. December 2025 Lennar exchange offer cleared part of the overhang; remaining stake is a known, manageable supply. As a 2026 first-full-year reporter, MRP gets discovered by REIT-dedicated investors who couldn't model it last year. BTIG's $35 PT is a starting point; if the market re-rates it as a 7-8% yield REIT, the stock prints $40+. Index inclusion (Russell, eventually MSCI REIT indices) is a 2026-2027 catalyst.
MRP.whatScreenCantSee:
Form S-11 / 10-K specifics: (1) Kennedy Lewis fee is 1.25% of Tangible Assets — at $10B+ assets, that's $125M+ annually, paid externally. The conflict: Kennedy Lewis is incentivized to grow assets, not necessarily ROE. (2) Lennar retains consent rights on the Manager until its asset contribution drops below 10% of total assets for six consecutive months — meaning Lennar effectively governs the manager relationship for years. (3) Master Program Agreement with Lennar specifies takedown windows, option fees, and pricing mechanics — this is the load-bearing contract; a Material Adverse Change clause exists but is narrow. (4) Lennar's 33.2M Class A residual shares are non-voting initially with a planned subsequent transaction (split-off, secondary, or sale) — a known catalyst on a clock. (5) Most of the dividend is technically a return of capital that recycles as Lennar takes down lots — distinguishing "true" yield from principal-return is critical and screens conflate them. (6) Tax-status as REIT requires 90% distribution of taxable income — the $0.76 dividend is structural, not discretionary.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 2026 results (May 6) — first full-year-as-public earnings cycle confirms the recurring-fee model
- Onboarding additional non-Lennar builders (already disclosed in pipeline) — each new contract is a discrete print
- Lennar's residual 20% disposition (split-off or secondary) — clearing overhang triggers re-rating

SNDK.name: Sandisk Corporation
SNDK.content:
# 9. SNDK — Sandisk Corporation
## SPINOFF (from Western Digital, WDC)  |  Spin Date: 2025-02-24  |  Score: 78/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $1,750

**Tier: SPINOFF — QUALITY-AT-DISCOUNT (post-rerate)**

### THE SETUP
Sandisk was carved out of Western Digital at the bottom of the NAND cycle in February 2025 at ~$28. The stock has since printed >$900 — a ~30x move — driven by the AI-demand-driven NAND supercycle (bit demand growing 20-22%, supply 15-17%) and the High Bandwidth Flash (HBF) co-development with SK Hynix. The forced-selling thesis is mostly over; what remains is a cyclical-supercycle thesis where forward EV/EBITDA still doesn't price a multi-year shortage extending to 2028. Position size cut deliberately because we're well past the easy money.

### SPINOFF MECHANICS
- Parent: Western Digital (NASDAQ: WDC)
- Spin date: February 24, 2025 (separation effective Feb 21) — ~14 months old
- Distribution ratio: 1/3 share of SNDK for every 1 WDC share held
- Initial trading vs current: opened ~$48; ran to a $27.89 low in April 2025 (forced-selling flush); now $920+ (~30x off the lows) — recent ~$921
- Forced selling reasons: (1) WDC had announced plans to retire ~28.8M SNDK shares via debt-for-equity exchanges, creating known overhang and incremental supply; (2) WDC index funds had to sell SNDK on Day 1 (different ticker, different sector classification); (3) NAND was at cycle bottom with negative gross margins memory in NAND 2024 — anyone who could sell did; (4) WDC fundamental holders are HDD-cycle investors, not NAND-cycle investors — completely different end-markets, customers, and pricing dynamics.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Sandisk is the only US-listed pure-play NAND flash memory company post-separation. The business: NAND wafer production via the long-running joint venture with Kioxia (Japan) — a structural cost-share arrangement that lets both partners stay at the leading edge with half the capex burden — packaged into consumer SSDs (the SanDisk and WD brands), enterprise/data-center SSDs, and embedded/automotive solutions. Q3 FY26 revenue guide is $4.4-$4.8B with gross margin guidance of 65-67% — versus 26% in the prior-year quarter and a 7.1% trough in FY23. The cycle has flipped: contract NAND prices were up 85-90% q/q, Goldman models +100-120% NAND pricing in 2026, and the demand-supply gap (22% vs 16-17%) implies persistent shortage through 2028 per multiple sell-side desks. Free cash flow generation in H2 2025 was $1.2B, used to pay down separation-inherited debt. The HBF (High Bandwidth Flash) co-development with SK Hynix targets 8-16x HBM capacity at comparable bandwidth — if it ships H2 2026 it directly competes for AI-accelerator memory sockets.

Bear case: NAND is the most cyclical commodity in semiconductors. The peak-to-trough on gross margins has historically been 50+ points. At 65%+ GM you are at-or-above prior cycle peaks; mean reversion alone is brutal. Capex discipline lasts only as long as everyone is profitable — Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron and Kioxia all add capacity into shortages, eventually flooding the market. The $1.165B installment payment to Kioxia (2026-2029) is a real cash outflow tied to JV restructuring. HBF is a technology bet against entrenched HBM (Samsung/SK Hynix DRAM stacking) — first-mover doesn't always win, and AI accelerators are tightly co-designed with specific memory architectures. The stock has 30x'd in 14 months — every "AI-adjacent" momentum holder is in, and any cycle wobble (China recession, Apple iPhone air pocket, hyperscaler capex pause) triggers a violent reversal. NAND prices have historically over- and under-shot by 50%+ — picking the cycle top here is a coin flip.

Bull case: even at $920, forward EV/EBITDA on FY26-FY27 numbers under sell-side cycle-peak assumptions is below 6x — meaning the market still implicitly assumes mean reversion within 18 months. If the structural AI shortage extends to 2028 (consensus among NAND-dedicated desks), three more years of peak earnings prints, with FCF used to delever and buy back stock. HBF is the genuine wild card — if it captures even 10% of what becomes a $50B+ AI memory market by 2028, that's a $5B/year revenue lever at gross margins north of 60%. S&P 500 inclusion is pending (already meets size and float thresholds) — a mechanical demand catalyst. Sandisk brand has consumer pricing power that pure-merchant NAND players (Micron NAND, SK Hynix Solidigm) lack. The Kioxia JV is the deepest moat — fab capacity that takes 3-5 years and $30B+ to replicate.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

Form 10 / S-1A / TSA specifics: (1) The Kioxia JV is the entire cost-of-goods structure — Sandisk doesn't own fabs outright, it shares them. Screens looking at "fab utilization" miss this. (2) The $1.165B installment payment to Kioxia (2026-2029) shows up as committed contractual cash obligations, not opex — it's an artifact of the separation/JV renewal. (3) Transition Services Agreement with WDC runs up to 18 months covering quality assurance, IT, procurement, logistics, finance, HR, engineering, marketing, operations, sales, manufacturing, R&D — broad scope means standalone-cost emergence is just unwinding now (2026). (4) WDC's debt-for-equity exchange plan with ~28.8M SNDK shares creates discrete overhang events — these are predictable and discountable. (5) Board includes legacy WDC and JV-relationship members; CEO David Goeckeler is a known semis operator. (6) HBF JDA with SK Hynix is structured as a joint development with ip-sharing on the architecture — economics are partner-friendly but not exclusive.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Quarterly contract NAND price prints (each one validates or breaks the supercycle thesis)
- HBF product roadmap milestones — sample shipments to AI-accelerator OEMs in H2 2026
- S&P 500 inclusion (mechanical buy-in event)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
1. Understandable business: 7/10 (memory is cyclical commodity but clear)
2. Durable competitive advantage: 7/10 (Kioxia JV + brand)
3. Honest/able management: 7/10 (Goeckeler proven)
4. Reasonable valuation: 5/10 (post-30x, less margin of safety)
5. Strong financial position: 7/10 (deleveraging fast on FCF)
6. Low capital intensity / high ROIC: 4/10 (semis fab capex burden)
7. Pricing power: 7/10 (cycle-peak now, brand premium)
8. Predictable earnings: 3/10 (most cyclical commodity in tech)
9. Insider alignment: 6/10 (standard equity comp, no founder)
10. Margin of safety: 6/10 (cycle-peak leaves less room)

**Total: 59/100** (boosted to 78 to reflect the still-mispriced supercycle duration / HBF optionality at sub-6x EV/EBITDA forward)

### POSITION SIZING
**Recommended: $1,750** | Rationale: Below-average position deliberately. The forced-selling thesis paid out spectacularly (30x), but we're now buying a cyclical at peak earnings. The bull case (extended supercycle + HBF) still has runway, but the asymmetry has compressed and downside in any cycle wobble is sharp. Large, well-covered name where excess return diminishes — keep position small and let the index-inclusion mechanical bid do the heavy lifting.

---

Sources:
- [Sandisk celebrates Nasdaq listing](https://www.sandisk.com/company/newsroom/press-releases/2025/sandisk-celebrates-nasdaq-listing-after-completing-separation)
- [Techi NAND supercycle analysis](https://www.techi.com/sandisk-sndk-stock-supercycle/)
- [Anand Capital deep dive](https://anandcapital.substack.com/p/sandisk-corporation-sndk-comprehensive)
- [WDC Information Statement](https://investor.wdc.com/static-files/32636b9d-b92b-4d18-b523-ca690e2046fc)
- [SNDK 10-Q](https://investor.sandisk.com/static-files/f47a3cf4-871a-43ef-8559-4ce27eb96b3d)
- [SNDK S-1/A](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/2023554/000119312525110314/d934557ds1a.htm)
- [Options.cafe Mar 2026 analysis](https://options.cafe/stock-analysis/sndk-analysis-march-2026/)

---
