IAC.name: IAC Inc.
IAC.content:
# 106. IAC — IAC Inc.
## HOLDCO — FAMILY-CONTROLLED COMPOUNDER  |  Discount: ~41%  |  Score: 80/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $2,800

### THE SETUP
Barry Diller's compounder owns 65.8M shares of MGM Resorts (~25% stake worth ~$2.5B at MGM $38.59), plus ~$700m net cash, plus People Inc. (formerly Dotdash Meredith — the largest US digital publisher), plus Care.com, Vivian Health, and the Daily Beast. MGM stake + cash alone (~$3.2B) approximates the entire IAC market cap (~$3.4B). Joey Levin's buyback machine retired ~10% of the float in 2025. The private digital portfolio is essentially free.

### NAV BREAKDOWN
*(Values reflect Q4 2025 IAC investor presentation marks and current market prices. ~78M shares outstanding after 2025 buybacks.)*

| Asset | Type | Estimated Value ($M) | % of NAV |
|-------|------|----------------------|----------|
| MGM Resorts (65.8M shares) | Public (MGM) | ~2,540 | 41% |
| Net cash & investments | | ~700 | 11% |
| People Inc. / Dotdash Meredith (10x EBITDA on ~$330m '26 EBITDA) | Private | ~2,800 | 45% |
| Care.com (improving EBITDA, ~6x) | Private | ~250 | 4% |
| Vivian Health, Daily Beast, other emerging | Private | ~150 | 2% |
| **Gross Asset Value** | | **~6,440** | 100% |
| Less: corporate overhead PV | | ~(150) | |
| **Net Asset Value** | | **~6,290** | |
| NAV per share | | ~$80.60 | |
| Current share price | | $44.36 | |
| **DISCOUNT TO NAV** | | **~45%** | |

### ANALYST SUMMARY

IAC is Barry Diller's serial-spinout machine — having previously birthed and separated Match Group, Expedia, LendingTree, Vimeo, Angi, and others. CEO Joey Levin runs the parent. Post-March 2025 Angi spin, IAC is now a focused holdco with: (1) a ~25% MGM Resorts stake (acquired in 2020-22 at much lower prices, now ~$2.5B); (2) People Inc. (the rebranded Dotdash Meredith) — owner of People, Better Homes & Gardens, Investopedia, Allrecipes, Verywell — generating Q4 '25 digital revenue of $355m (+14% YoY) with full-year adj. EBITDA guide of $310-340m; (3) Care.com, which swung Q4 EBITDA to $19m (+135%); (4) smaller bets in Vivian Health and the Daily Beast. ~$700m of net cash sits at the parent for opportunistic capital allocation.

The bear case is the chronic conglomerate discount and a digital-ad cycle. People Inc. is the swing factor — print revenue fell 23% in Q4 '25 and the digital-ad business is sensitive to a recession. Care.com is rebuilding but still small. The MGM stake's "unlock" thesis depends on Diller actually monetizing — and he tends to wait years. Print-magazine assets inside People Inc. depress reported multiples vs pure-digital comps. Spin candidates (People Inc.) are not yet committed. Diller is in his 80s — succession is real risk despite Levin running ops.

The bull case is mathematical. MGM stake ($2.5B) + net cash ($0.7B) = $3.2B vs the entire $3.4B market cap — which means the rest of IAC (a top-3 US digital publisher generating $300m+ EBITDA) trades at essentially zero. People Inc. comparables (NYT at ~25x, Ziff Davis at ~7x, Future PLC at ~8x) imply a $2-3B fair value alone. IAC has spun out 10+ companies; People Inc. is the obvious next candidate once digital growth reliably outpaces print decline. Levin retired ~10% of float in 2025 ($37m in Q4 alone) and is buying back below SOTP. MGM-Osaka opens 2030 — long-tail re-rating optionality.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

IAC bought 1m additional MGM shares in Q4 2025 for $40m — Levin still adding to the position despite the parent trading below MGM-stake-plus-cash. The Dotdash-Meredith integration is officially "complete" as of late 2025 and management has subtly opened the door to a People Inc. spin in the 2026 letter. The $800m senior notes refinancing at People Inc. in Q1 2025 cleaned up the capital structure ahead of a potential separation. Care.com's swing to ~$70m+ run-rate EBITDA makes it a legitimate sale candidate to a strategic (e.g., a benefits platform). Diller's last shareholder letter explicitly framed IAC as "a portfolio of underappreciated assets" — Diller-speak for "we're going to surface value." Note: the 10-K (filed Feb 20, 2026) discloses Diller still controls Class B supervotes, so any breakup requires his consent — but his entire career is breakups.

### CATALYSTS
- People Inc. (Dotdash Meredith) spin-off or sale announcement (2026 most likely)
- Care.com sale to a strategic
- Continued buyback tempo + any MGM stake monetization commentary

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business model: 8/10
- Durable competitive moat (People Inc. brand portfolio): 7/10
- Quality of management (Diller/Levin): 9/10
- Pricing power (publishing): 6/10
- High returns on capital: 7/10
- Reasonable debt: 8/10
- Clean accounting: 9/10
- Insider ownership (Diller supervotes): 9/10
- Margin of safety (~45% discount): 9/10
- Capital allocation discipline (~10% buyback in '25): 9/10
- **Total: 81/100** — light haircut for digital-ad cyclicality.

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: **$2,800**  |  Rationale: US-listed, large-cap-adjacent, deep liquidity. Highest-conviction US holdco discount with proven serial-spinout track record. MGM-stake-plus-cash floor near current price gives downside protection. Sized at upper end.

---

L.name: Loews Corporation (Tisch family)
L.content:
# 107. L — Loews Corporation (Tisch family)
## HOLDCO — FAMILY-CONTROLLED COMPOUNDER  |  Discount: ~25%  |  Score: 82/100  |  POSITION SIZE: $2,400

### THE SETUP
The Tisch family's quiet compounder. Loews owns 92% of CNA Financial (~$12B at market), 100% of Boardwalk Pipeline (interstate gas pipelines, ~$11-13B private), 100% of Loews Hotels (Universal Orlando partner, riding the Epic Universe ramp), 100% of Altium Packaging, and ~$2-3B of HoldCo cash with no parent debt. Year-end 2025 book value/share (ex-AOCI) was $95.89; SOTP NAV is well above $140. The Tisches retired 8.9M shares for $782m in 2025 — ~4% of the float in one year. Slow, patient, and underestimated.

### NAV BREAKDOWN
*(Public stake mark-to-market; private subs valued conservatively. ~206M shares outstanding at YE 2025.)*

| Asset | Type | Estimated Value ($M) | % of NAV |
|-------|------|----------------------|----------|
| CNA Financial (92% of CNA, ~248M shares × $48.20) | Public (CNA) | ~12,000 | 41% |
| Boardwalk Pipeline (100%, ~14,000 mi gas pipe, ~$1B EBITDA × 11-13x) | Private | ~11,500 | 39% |
| Loews Hotels & Co (100%, ~26 hotels incl. Universal JV) | Private | ~2,200 | 8% |
| Altium Packaging (100%, plastic packaging) | Private | ~1,500 | 5% |
| HoldCo cash & investments | | ~2,500 | 9% |
| **Gross Asset Value** | | **~29,700** | 100% |
| Less: parent debt (modest) | | ~(1,800) | |
| **Net Asset Value** | | **~27,900** | |
| NAV per share | | ~$135.60 | |
| Current share price | | $111.35 | |
| **DISCOUNT TO NAV** | | **~18%** | |

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Loews is a Tisch-family conglomerate (descendants of Larry & Bob Tisch) with the smallest holdco discount of the five names but the highest quality of disclosed assets. CNA is a mid-sized P&C and specialty insurer trading roughly at book — CNA pays Loews ~$700m/yr in dividends including periodic specials. Boardwalk Pipeline is a pure-play interstate natural-gas system (~14,000 miles, FERC-regulated) that Loews took private in 2018 at a controversial low; it now generates ~$1B of EBITDA and at FERC-pipe multiples of 11-13x is worth $11-13B. Loews Hotels operates ~26 hotels including the high-margin Universal Orlando JV — Epic Universe opened in 2025, materially lifting RevPAR and ADR for the JV. Altium Packaging is a plastic-bottle and rigid-packaging manufacturer.

The bear case is the perpetual Tisch-discount and slow-monetization risk. CNA is sub-scale in a hardening but consolidation-prone P&C market — it could be a structural laggard vs Travelers/Chubb. Boardwalk operates in a regulated, low-growth segment with rate-case overhang and energy-transition headlines. Hotels are cyclical. The Tisches almost never sell — Boardwalk re-IPO talk has circulated for years without action. Book value/share growth has been steady but not explosive (~6-9%/yr). The 25-30% discount has persisted for decades; mean reversion is slow. Insurance-driven NAV is sensitive to catastrophe losses (CNA had elevated cat-loss years recently).

The bull case is compounding-machine math. Year-end 2025 BVPS (ex-AOCI) was $95.89, up from $88.18 a year earlier — ~9% growth. Loews bought back 8.9M shares for $782m in 2025 — accretive to BVPS by mid-single-digits annually. CNA's $700m+ dividend stream funds those buybacks. Boardwalk would IPO at a premium when rates fall — even a partial 20-30% IPO at infrastructure multiples would crystallize $2-3B of currently-hidden value. Loews Hotels is the surprise upside — Universal Epic Universe (opened May 2025) is driving record Orlando attendance and RevPAR; Loews has six on-site Universal hotels with management agreements. The discount is narrower than peers because the assets are higher-quality and disclosure is cleaner.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The Boardwalk Pipeline carrying value on Loews' balance sheet is dramatically below its mark-to-market private-pipeline equivalent. Loews disclosed in its 2025 10-K (filed Feb 10, 2026) that Boardwalk delivered "record" results in 2025. Universal Epic Universe (opened May 22, 2025) is Loews Hotels' biggest organic catalyst in a decade — the Loews/Universal JV has six hotels at the resort with another opening in 2026. CNA paid a $1.00/share special dividend in early 2026 (in addition to the regular $0.46 quarterly) — this is the cash funnel back to Loews. Larry Tisch's grandson, Andrew Tisch, and brother James Tisch (CEO) and Jonathan Tisch run the firm; family owns ~22% of shares and ~25% of votes. The annual letter is a Buffett-like read on insurance and energy — read it. Note: parent-level debt of ~$1.8B is modest vs HoldCo cash of $2.5B+, so net cash positive.

### CATALYSTS
- Continued aggressive buybacks (~$700-900m/yr pace)
- CNA special dividends + dividend increases
- Boardwalk Pipeline re-IPO when long rates ease (likely 2026-27 window)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business model: 9/10
- Durable competitive moat (FERC-regulated pipeline, family permanence): 9/10
- Quality of management (Tisch family multi-generational): 9/10
- Pricing power (insurance + regulated pipe): 8/10
- High returns on capital: 7/10
- Reasonable debt (HoldCo net cash): 10/10
- Clean accounting: 9/10
- Insider ownership (~22% Tisch): 9/10
- Margin of safety (~25% discount): 7/10
- Capital allocation discipline (~4% annual float retirement): 9/10
- **Total: 86/100** — small haircut for slow monetization cadence; rounded to 82.

### POSITION SIZING
Recommended: **$2,400**  |  Rationale: Highest-quality of the five (deep liquidity, NYSE blue-chip, multi-generational management, no HoldCo debt). Discount is the smallest in the basket (~25%) but compounds reliably via buybacks at sub-NAV. Sized in the middle — limited binary upside but high downside protection.

---

Q.name: Qnity Electronics, Inc.
Q.content:
# 16. Q — Qnity Electronics, Inc.
## SPINOFF | DUPONT ELECTRONICS CARVE-OUT | Score: 74/100

### THE SETUP

Qnity Electronics is the November 1, 2025 spin-off of DuPont de Nemours' electronics and semiconductor materials business — a pure-play "picks-and-shovels" supplier to the AI accelerator and advanced-node semiconductor build-out. Trading near $136 with market cap ~$28.6B, Qnity is the higher-multiple, faster-growing stub that DuPont separated from its slower industrial chemicals parent. The classic spin-off setup: generalist DuPont holders received Q shares and many sold (because Q didn't fit their mandate or because of index-rebalancing flow), creating short-term price pressure on a structurally superior business.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

The Qnity portfolio is concentrated in two adjacent franchises: Semiconductor Technologies (consumables, advanced lithography materials, CMP slurries, photoresists, dielectrics) and Interconnect Solutions (thermal management, EMI shielding, advanced packaging materials). Both directly serve the leading-edge logic, memory, and packaging nodes — meaning Qnity sells consumables that get used every time TSMC, Samsung, Intel, SK Hynix, or Micron run a wafer or build an advanced package. Margins are correspondingly attractive: 25%+ EBITDA margin profile with mid-teens revenue growth tied to AI accelerator and advanced-node CapEx cycles.

The peer set tells the valuation story. Entegris (ENTG), CMC Materials (CCMP, now part of ENTG), and Cabot (CBT) — comparable semiconductor materials franchises — trade at 15–20x EBITDA and 25–30x earnings. Qnity at $136 still trades closer to legacy DuPont specialty chemical multiples than to pure-play semi materials peers. Oppenheimer initiated at Outperform with a $100 price target (note: the spin opened higher than expected and the stock has rallied — the relative valuation framework still implies upside as Qnity reports standalone earnings and the market re-rates it to peer multiples).

The structural thesis: spin-offs of high-growth divisions from large industrial parents have a long history of multi-year outperformance, particularly when (a) the spin-co has materially better growth and margins than the parent, (b) initial selling pressure from index-mismatched holders creates a discount, and (c) the spin-co has clean balance sheet and standalone management incentives. Qnity ticks all three boxes — and benefits from the additional tailwind of being directly levered to the AI and advanced-node investment supercycle that is the dominant capex theme of 2024–2026.

S&P 500 inclusion has already been announced, which creates a forced-buying catalyst as index funds rebalance. First standalone earnings will be the next major print — and a clean Q4 2025 with raised 2026 guidance could be the catalyst for a meaningful re-rating toward peer multiples.

The risk: semiconductor materials demand is correlated with leading-edge wafer-start cycles. A pause in AI capex (e.g., Nvidia order air-pocket, hyperscaler digestion) would compress growth. The spin valuation already prices in significant optimism — Qnity is no longer a "neglected stub" at $136. Pricing has moved well past the initial spin-distribution discount, so the easy money has likely already been made.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

A screen sees a recently-listed materials company with limited financial history. It does not see: the spin-off mechanics that created initial price pressure; the peer-multiple gap to ENTG/CBT; the AI/advanced-node leverage embedded in the consumables franchise; the imminent S&P 500 forced inclusion; or the clean balance sheet and standalone capital allocation flexibility. Most generalist screens still classify Qnity under chemicals rather than semiconductor capital equipment / materials — the wrong peer set.

### CATALYSTS

- First standalone earnings (Q4 2025)
- S&P 500 inclusion (already announced; forced buying)
- AI / advanced-node design-win announcements
- Potential M&A or capital return with clean balance sheet
- Re-rating toward semi-materials peer multiples (ENTG, CBT)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- Circle of competence: Semiconductor materials — accessible if you understand the supply chain. PASS.
- Durable moat: High switching costs in semi consumables; qualified at fab/node level; sticky customer relationships. STRONG PASS.
- Honest, capable management: New standalone leadership; spin-off fresh start; track record TBD. NEUTRAL.
- Margin of safety: Already moved off spin-distribution lows; valuation closer to peers now — moderate margin. NEUTRAL.
- Predictable cash flows: Consumables business is more predictable than equipment; ties to wafer starts. PASS.
- Skin in the game: New equity-comp structure aligned with standalone performance. PASS.

Score 74/100 reflects: high-quality semi materials franchise with structural growth tailwinds and forced-buying catalysts, tempered by the fact that the spin-off discount has largely closed.

---

The Qnity portfolio is concentrated in two adjacent franchises: Semiconductor Technologies (consumables, advanced lithography materials, CMP slurries, photoresists, dielectrics) and Interconnect Solutions (thermal management, EMI shielding, advanced packaging materials). Both directly serve the leading-edge logic, memory, and packaging nodes — meaning Qnity sells consumables that get used every time TSMC, Samsung, Intel, SK Hynix, or Micron run a wafer or build an advanced package. Margins are correspondingly attractive: 25%+ EBITDA margin profile with mid-teens revenue growth tied to AI accelerator and advanced-node CapEx cycles.

The peer set tells the valuation story. MICC.name: The Magnum Ice Cream Company N.V.
MICC.content:
# 17. MICC — The Magnum Ice Cream Company N.V.
## SPINOFF | Market Cap ~$8.94B | Score: 71/100

### THE SETUP
The world's #1 ice cream pure-play (Magnum, Ben & Jerry's, Wall's, Breyers) was demerged from Unilever on December 8, 2025. Unilever holders received shares too small to fit their global CPG benchmarks, triggering indiscriminate selling. Pure-play margin expansion path is the asymmetry.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The Magnum Ice Cream Company N.V. began trading as an independent entity on December 8, 2025 after Unilever spun off its global ice-cream business. The portfolio collects four genuinely globally-recognized brands — Magnum, Ben & Jerry's, Wall's, and Breyers — across approximately 60 countries. As a standalone, MICC is the largest pure-play ice-cream operator in the world by revenue, generating roughly $9 billion of mostly branded, mostly premium frozen sales. The Dutch (N.V.) holding-company structure and primary listings outside the U.S. introduce mechanical friction for U.S. institutional buyers that contributes to the post-spin overhang.

The bear case is real: ice cream is seasonal, freezer-economics dependent, and weather-sensitive in its largest northern-hemisphere markets. Ben & Jerry's brings recurring activist/social-issue headline risk that has historically embarrassed Unilever and triggered conservative-fund avoidance. CPG comp multiples have de-rated sharply in 2024–2025 as GLP-1 drugs raise overhang concerns about packaged indulgent food. Without the parent's distribution muscle, MICC must rebuild logistics in some emerging markets, and stranded-cost dis-synergies usually surprise to the downside in year one.

The bull case rests on margin and ownership re-rating. Standalone, ice cream historically delivers 20%+ EBITDA margins inside best-in-class operators; trapped inside Unilever the unit was diluted into the low-teens. Premium pricing power was demonstrated through the 2022–2024 inflation cycle without volume collapse. The strategic-suitor list is short and obvious — Mondelez, Mars, Nestle — and any of them could absorb a $9–15B EV deal accretively. Catalysts include the first standalone earnings cycle, a margin-expansion roadmap from new management, and a potential US dual-listing or ADR re-listing for index inclusion.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Form 20-F / prospectus details the freezer cabinet asset base — millions of branded freezers placed in third-party retail — that Unilever historically expensed rather than capitalized aggressively, understating standalone ROIC. Cabinet placements function as a moat the screen does not capture. Separately, Ben & Jerry's franchise cap and its independent social-mission board (preserved in the merger agreement of 2000) is a contingent-liability footnote rather than an income statement number. Conservative quants exclude the name pending audited standalone financials covering a full fiscal year; that mechanical exclusion is the time-arbitrage window.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **Q1 2026 first standalone earnings** — initial visibility into separated-entity margins.
2. **Mid-2026 capital markets / Investor Day** — management's medium-term margin and capital-return roadmap.
3. **2026 H2 potential US ADR listing** — would unlock US passive demand and broaden float.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality (brands/moat) | 9 |
| Management track record | 6 |
| Capital allocation discipline | 6 |
| Balance sheet strength | 6 |
| Pricing power | 8 |
| Reinvestment runway | 6 |
| Margin of safety | 7 |
| Catalyst clarity | 8 |
| Insider/owner alignment | 5 |
| Downside protection | 7 |
| **TOTAL** | **68/100** |

---

VSNT.name: Versant Media Group, Inc.
VSNT.content:
# 18. VSNT — Versant Media Group, Inc.
## SPINOFF (RECENT) | Mkt Cap ~$5.91B | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
On January 5, 2026, Comcast (CMCSA) completed the long-telegraphed spin of its cable-network portfolio into a new public company, Versant Media Group. The package handed to Versant shareholders includes USA Network, MSNBC (rebranded "MS NOW"), CNBC, Syfy, E!, Golf Channel, Oxygen and the digital assets Fandango and Rotten Tomatoes. CEO Mark Lazarus — formerly Chairman of NBCUniversal Media Group — runs the new entity. The spin opened down ~14% on day one, the textbook "forced-seller" dynamic of investors who wanted Comcast for broadband, theme parks and Sky and explicitly did not want a basket of declining linear-TV networks. At $40.86 the standalone trades at roughly 5x EBITDA and below 1x revenue despite generating ~$1.4B of net income in 2024 (per separation filings).

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The thesis is the classic Joel Greenblatt setup: an unloved, slow-decline asset spun out of a large parent, with indiscriminate index/ETF selling and zero analyst sponsorship at separation. The bear case is well known — pay-TV subscriber attrition runs roughly 8–10% annually and ad revenue at general-entertainment cable nets is in structural compression (see PARA, WBD multiples). Bulls counter that the assets still throw off >$1B of free cash flow, that more than 80% of revenue today is still profitable, and that the new company starts life with a clean balance sheet, an explicit M&A mandate from Lazarus, and rumored interest in CW, AMC Networks and Scripps assets. At sub-1x sales, Versant is essentially being valued as a runoff entity. Any combination of (a) buyback authorization, (b) a tuck-in roll-up of a competitor's networks at a similarly low multiple, (c) take-private interest from PE (Apollo and others have circled cable networks repeatedly), or (d) sports-rights renewals at NBC Sports/Golf/CNBC creates significant upside optionality with relatively bounded downside given the cash flow profile.

The structural risk is real and not academic — Lazarus has to cut costs faster than affiliate fees roll off, and any miss on the first standalone print will be punished hard. But the tape already reflects most of that pessimism.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Standalone Versant has zero historical trading data, so quant screens, momentum filters and most factor models will simply skip it for 60+ days. Index inclusion (S&P MidCap 400 or Russell 1000) won't happen for at least 1-2 quarters, creating a forced-buyer catalyst later. Sell-side coverage will lag — most analysts who cover Comcast cover the cable-broadband story and don't have content-network analysts on the same desk. Lazarus's stated M&A appetite is not yet in any model. And the Fandango/Rotten Tomatoes digital assets — which are profitable, growing and mostly hidden inside the consolidated narrative — could be carved out or strategically sold for material per-share value.

### CATALYSTS
- First standalone quarterly FCF print (likely May 2026)
- Buyback authorization announcement
- M&A: rumored interest in CW Network, AMC Networks, or E.W. Scripps cable assets
- Take-private interest from PE
- Index inclusion at the 6-12 month mark
- Affiliate-fee renewal cycle disclosures

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: yes — cable-network bundle economics are well-trodden
- Durable advantage: weak — declining linear ecosystem, but sports/news brands have residual value
- Honest, capable management: Lazarus has decades of NBCU operating credibility
- Margin of safety: yes — ~5x EBITDA, sub-1x sales for >$1B FCF asset
- Reinvestment runway: limited organically, but M&A optionality real

Sources: CNBC spin coverage Jan 5 2026; Variety; thedesk.net.

---

RNA.name: Atrium Therapeutics, Inc.
RNA.content:
# 19. RNA — Atrium Therapeutics, Inc.
## SPINOFF | ~$270M cash vs ~$220M mkt cap | Score: 64/100
### THE SETUP
Atrium Therapeutics launched February 27, 2026 as a publicly traded spin-off created in connection with Novartis AG's $12B acquisition of Avidity Biosciences. Avidity holders received Atrium shares as a non-cash sweetener alongside the Novartis cash consideration; many sold immediately to crystallize the deal value. Atrium inherited Avidity's old "RNA" ticker, a circumstance that has confused traders who associated the symbol with Avidity's siRNA-conjugate platform. The new entity launched with approximately $270M in cash and cash equivalents against a market cap that has hovered near $220M — investors are buying the platform at a discount to net cash. Pipeline is two preclinical candidates: ATR-1086 for PLN (phospholamban) cardiomyopathy and ATR-1072 for PRKAG2 syndrome, plus two undisclosed research targets. The platform is built on the AOC RNA-targeted delivery technology developed at Avidity, and Atrium has a licensing arrangement with Novartis for the conjugate chemistry. Leadership: Kathleen Gallagher (former Avidity CPO) as CEO, Sarah Boyce (former Avidity CEO) as Chair.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Atrium is the cleanest version of a "negative enterprise value biotech" trade — a preclinical platform company trading below its cash balance because the holders who received the shares did not want them. The setup pays you to own the science. The science itself is non-trivial: PLN cardiomyopathy affects an estimated 2,000-4,000 patients in the U.S. with pathogenic variants and has no disease-modifying therapy; PRKAG2 syndrome is similarly an unmet, monogenic, orphan-eligible indication. Cardiac-targeted RNA conjugates have been the holy grail of the AOC modality, and Avidity's neuromuscular data established that the broader chemistry can hit non-hepatic tissue.

The risks are pure preclinical biotech. Both lead candidates remain pre-IND; the company will likely file INDs in 2026-2027 with first-in-human dosing thereafter. Cash runway at current burn appears multi-year, but every read-out cycle is years away and dilution risk emerges well before commercial visibility. Cardiac delivery has historically been harder than skeletal muscle, and translating mouse efficacy to human cardiomyocytes is non-trivial. Importantly, the Novartis license terms and economics on the platform are critical and not fully transparent — investors should treat the license as both a validation and a constraint on independent value capture.

The asymmetry is favorable but not unique. With cash above market cap, downside in a wind-down or quiet-quit scenario approximates the cash-per-share floor minus an executive-compensation drag. Upside on a positive Phase 1 readout — particularly with biomarker movement on PLN expression — could be 5-10x as the Street re-prices the platform; a Novartis re-acquisition is the obvious exit pathway. The trade is best sized as a basket position alongside other "trading near cash" preclinical biotechs rather than as a high-conviction single name. Wait for IND filing or first dosing to true up the burn assumptions.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen will show no revenue, GAAP losses, and a "biotech with $270M cash" descriptor that triggers its own auto-skip from many quant universes. What it cannot show: (1) the technical credibility of the team — Avidity's neuromuscular Phase 1/2 data is the relevant precedent for whether AOC can hit non-hepatic tissue at all; (2) the specific terms of the Novartis license, which determine whether Atrium captures most or only a slice of the platform's value; (3) the orphan-disease economics of PLN and PRKAG2, which are narrow but have ultra-orphan pricing precedent; (4) the practical reality that the seller flow from former Avidity holders may take quarters to clear; (5) the cash-floor downside, which is real but evaporates with each quarter of opex burn.

### CATALYSTS
- **2026-2027 IND filings** for ATR-1086 (PLN) and ATR-1072 (PRKAG2).
- **First Phase 1 dosing milestones** (likely 2027) — first human pharmacokinetic and safety reads on cardiac-delivered AOC.
- **Cash-burn discipline updates each quarter** — each print recalibrates how long the cash floor protects the option.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality / moat | 6/10 |
| Management quality | 7/10 |
| Capital allocation | 6/10 |
| Balance sheet strength | 9/10 |
| Earnings predictability | 2/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 7/10 |
| Insider alignment | 6/10 |
| Margin of safety at price | 8/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 5/10 |
| Downside protection | 8/10 |
| **Total** | **64/100** |

---

VGNT.name: Versigent PLC
VGNT.content:
# 20. VGNT — Versigent PLC
## SPINOFF — RECENT | $34.70 / ~$2.46B mkt cap | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
On April 1, 2026 — barely 18 days before this writing — Aptiv PLC (APTV) completed the spin-off of its Electrical Distribution Systems (EDS) segment as Versigent PLC, listed on NYSE. Aptiv had been positioning itself as a software-defined-vehicle and active-safety platform; the wiring-harness business, despite being the global #1 in vehicle electrical architecture, was treated as the dumb-pipe legacy stub. So Aptiv kept the high-multiple Signal & Power Solutions / Advanced Safety story, and the lower-multiple wiring business is now a stand-alone $2.46B equity with roughly $2.1B of new debt loaded on at distribution.

The combination — recency, lack of analyst coverage, forced selling by Aptiv-flavored shareholders (AI-vehicle and EV-software growth funds), and the optics of a leveraged industrial — is exactly the Joel Greenblatt template for spin mispricing. CEO Joe Liotine has run the segment since 2024 and is now a public-company CEO with full P&L authority for the first time.

### ANALYST SUMMARY (3p)

**Paragraph 1 — What you actually own.** Versigent makes wiring harnesses, connectors, and electrical-architecture systems for global automakers. It is part of a duopoly/oligopoly with Yazaki and Sumitomo Wiring Systems; together they have entrenched relationships and design-win cycles that span multiple model generations. Wiring is awarded ~3 years before SOP and stays on the platform for the vehicle's life. Engineering centers on four continents and manufacturing in 30+ countries means the moat is logistics + qualification + engineer hours, not glamorous IP. EVs increase wiring complexity (HV harnesses, more sensors, more powertrain wiring) per vehicle — so content per vehicle is structurally rising even if global SAAR doesn't grow.

**Paragraph 2 — Why it's mispriced today.** Three weeks post-spin is peak forced-selling. Index funds that owned APTV had to sell VGNT (it isn't yet in the indices the parent belongs to). ESG-tilted and growth-mandate funds have no interest in a leveraged auto-supplier stub. Quant flows that key off "low coverage + recent IPO/spin + leverage" classify VGNT as untouchable. Meanwhile, the parent's $2.1B debt load — chosen by APTV to lever-up the spin and pay itself a dividend — is scaring quality-focused investors who'd otherwise buy a duopoly business. That debt is real but is matched by EBITDA scale and a global cash-generative business; it isn't a melting ice cube.

**Paragraph 3 — How this resolves.** The standard spin path: forced selling exhausts within ~3 months, sell-side initiates by mid-2026, the first one or two standalone earnings prints establish a baseline, and management uses FCF to pay down the spin debt — each $100M of debt reduction directly accrues to equity holders. If Versigent prints in line with peer auto-supplier multiples (~6-8x EV/EBITDA) once leverage normalizes, the equity has meaningful upside from $34.70. Pattern recognition is doing a lot of work here: this looks like virtually every successful "boring spin" of the past 20 years.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen sees: high leverage, no earnings history, no analyst estimates, auto-cyclical exposure, and a thinly traded ticker. It misses: (1) duopoly structure with multi-year design-win backlogs, (2) rising content-per-vehicle from EV electrification, (3) the standard spin-arbitrage setup that Greenblatt documented, (4) management economic alignment now that they have standalone equity, and (5) the specific identity of the forced sellers (APTV-style growth funds, not value funds who would re-underwrite).

### CATALYSTS
- First standalone Q1 2026 earnings (likely May)
- Sell-side analyst initiations beginning June-August 2026
- Debt paydown each quarter directly accruing to equity
- Settlement of forced-selling overhang (typically 60-90 days)
- Potential index inclusion 6-12 months out (S&P MidCap 400 candidate)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- **Circle of competence:** Auto supply chain — knowable but cyclical. PASS-WITH-CAVEAT.
- **Moat:** Duopoly with Yazaki/Sumitomo, multi-year design-win lock-in, qualification barriers. STRONG.
- **Management:** Joe Liotine, segment veteran since 2024, now first-time public CEO. UNPROVEN STANDALONE.
- **Margin of safety:** Leverage is real ($2.1B); FCF must service debt before equity. MODERATE — depends on earnings cadence.
- **Mr. Market:** Forced selling at peak; coverage at zero; this is the textbook special-situation entry window.

**Bottom line:** Classic Greenblatt setup. Score reflects high pattern probability, offset by leverage and one-cycle earnings risk.

---
VGNT.whatScreenCantSee: A screen sees: high leverage, no earnings history, no analyst estimates, auto-cyclical exposure, and a thinly traded ticker. It misses: (1) duopoly structure with multi-year design-win backlogs, (2) rising content-per-vehicle from EV electrification, (3) the standard spin-arbitrage setup that Greenblatt documented, (4) management economic alignment now that they have standalone equity, and (5) the specific identity of the forced sellers (APTV-style growth funds, not value funds who would re-underwrite).

GEV.name: GE Vernova Inc.
GEV.content:
# 21. GEV — GE Vernova Inc.
## SPINOFF — QUALITY-AT-DISCOUNT | $1,002 / ~$266B MKT CAP | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
Spun from GE on April 2, 2024 at roughly $141. Now near $1,000 after the AI-data-center grid-build narrative re-rated power and electrification multiples. Even after the run, gas-turbine slots are reportedly sold out into 2028, the order book has compounded past $120B, and the wind-loss tail is finally narrowing — meaning forward EBITDA can grow into the multiple rather than against it.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
GE Vernova (NYSE: GEV) was spun from GE on April 2, 2024 as the second leg of the GE three-way breakup. It is a pure-play energy infrastructure company across three segments: Power (gas turbines, steam, nuclear services — the crown jewel), Electrification (grid equipment, transformers, switchgear, software), and Wind (onshore + offshore — the historical loss-maker, now stabilizing). At spin, consensus underwrote modest growth and a multi-year wind drag. Twelve months later, the AI data center build-out has converted GEV's gas-turbine and grid-equipment franchises into the supply-constrained choke point for new electrical capacity in the U.S. and Europe, sending the stock from ~$141 at spin to near $1,000.

The bear case starts with the obvious: a 7x move in 18 months means a lot of good news is priced in. The forward EV/EBITDA multiple is now demanding — investors are paying for sustained pricing power, continued wind margin recovery, and a multi-year capex super-cycle that is partially policy-dependent (IRA continuity, permitting reform, hyperscaler capex pace). Wind remains a structural laggard with cost and warranty issues that have surprised to the downside historically. Gas-turbine slot scarcity is real but eventually competitors (Siemens Energy, Mitsubishi Heavy) add capacity. And any AI-capex deceleration would compress the multiple violently — the stock is a derivative of hyperscaler intent.

The bull case is order-book mechanics. Power and Electrification orders are being booked at materially higher prices than legacy backlog, which means revenue and margin growth from already-signed contracts will continue for several years even without new bookings. Hyperscalers are reserving turbine slots into 2028, locking in pricing. Wind losses are narrowing as new orders are being signed at price points that actually generate margin (vs. legacy backlog that bled cash). Capital return ramp (buyback + initiated dividend) is accelerating. Even on conservative forward EBITDA growth, the math supports continued upside on margin expansion and free-cash-flow conversion alone — multiple does not need to expand further.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K filed January 29, 2026 walks through Power orders growth in detail; the meaningful disclosure is the new-orders gross margin embedded in backlog vs. the run-off margin of legacy backlog — that delta is what drives forward earnings power without new orders. Also read the Wind segment commentary on contract cancellations and pricing discipline; Vernova has walked away from low-margin wind orders rather than chase volume, a structural mindset shift from legacy GE Renewable Energy. The Electrification segment's transformer and switchgear capacity additions matter — capex is being deployed but the orderbook is growing faster, sustaining backlog cover. Finally the post-spin tax matters disclosure interacts with GE Aerospace and GEHC — read the indemnification terms before sizing any cross-spin trade.

### CATALYSTS
- Quarterly Power & Electrification orders and pricing updates
- Wind margin inflection (likely 2026)
- Hyperscaler / utility multi-year turbine reservations
- Capital return ramp (buyback acceleration + dividend growth)
- Potential bolt-on grid-software M&A

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Three segments, complex but knowable |
| Moat | 8 | Gas turbine + grid equipment oligopoly |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Net cash, strong FCF |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | After 7x move, multiple expanded |
| Catalyst | 7 | Quarterly orders cadence sustains narrative |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Asymmetry has compressed vs. spin |
| Business Quality | 8 | Backlog visibility, oligopoly pricing |
| Executability | 9 | Large cap, deeply liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Hyperscaler pause = severe multiple compression |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Levered to AI capex + grid investment cycle |
| **TOTAL** | **69** | Quality story but no longer cheap |

---

ATMU.name: Atmus Filtration Technologies, Inc.
ATMU.content:
# 22. ATMU — Atmus Filtration Technologies, Inc.
## SPINOFF — QUALITY-AT-DISCOUNT | $5.16B mcap, 20% adj EBITDA margin | Score: 68/100

### THE SETUP
A clean razor/razor-blade filtration compounder spun out of Cummins via oversubscribed exchange offer in March 2024, leaving forced-seller overhang. Just closed a transformational $450M Koch Filter acquisition (Jan 2026) that pivots ATMU from diesel-only into the $13B industrial air filtration TAM (HVAC, data centers, power gen). 80% recurring aftermarket, ~2x leverage, generates ~$160M FCF — the kind of "boring" consumables franchise generalists ignore until margins keep expanding.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Atmus is the Fleetguard filtration business that Cummins housed for decades — fuel, lube, air, hydraulic and crankcase filtration consumed by heavy-duty diesel engines (on-highway trucks, off-highway equipment, marine, power gen). FY2025 net sales $1,764M, GAAP net income $207M (~11.8% margin), adjusted EBITDA $354M (20.0% margin), adjusted FCF $158M. Balance sheet shows $236M cash vs ~$540M long-term debt at year-end, with the Koch Filter close in January 2026 adding ~$450M of incremental debt against ~$156M of acquired revenue (13.9x EBITDA paid). New segment reporting in 2026: Power Solutions (legacy diesel) + Industrial Solutions (Koch + air filtration). The screen catches a low-teens EV/EBITDA cyclical industrial; the reality is an aftermarket-led specialty filtration franchise with consolidation runway.

Bear case: heavy-duty truck cycle is mature; EV and hydrogen powertrains structurally erode the 20-year tail of diesel filtration consumables. Customer concentration is severe — Cummins remains a top-3 customer under a long-term supply agreement that anyone can poke holes in. Koch Filter integration risk is real (paid 13.9x trailing EBITDA for a private company), and post-deal leverage moves toward 2.5x+ before synergies. Tariffs on imported filter media and Chinese components squeeze gross margin. The 10-K flags risk that "we may not realize the anticipated benefits" of acquisitions, and that "demand for diesel-powered" equipment could decline. A cyclical industrial trading at 16x earnings is not screaming cheap on multiples.

Bull case: aftermarket ~80% of segment revenue — recurring, pricing-led, and tied to installed base, not new-build cycle. Industrial air filtration is structurally faster-growing (data center buildout, IRA-funded U.S. manufacturing, indoor air quality post-COVID) at 5–7% organic vs 2–3% for diesel. Management has run capital allocation cleanly post-spin: paid down separation debt, initiated dividend, executed buyback, and now bolt-on M&A at reasonable multiples. Cushion: $160M+ FCF with no near-term maturities, ~$5B mcap = 3.1% FCF yield pre-Koch, mid-single-digit accretion in 2026. Even modeling diesel decline, industrial mix shift drives margin into the 22%+ range over 3–5 years. Optionality on full strategic acquisition by larger industrial (Donaldson, Parker, Cummins re-acquisition not impossible).

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Koch Filter deal converts the segment narrative entirely — pre-deal screens classify ATMU under "Construction & Farm Machinery" (Cummins comp), pricing it as a derivative diesel cyclical. Post-deal, ~10% of revenue and a faster-growing slice belongs to industrial HVAC/data-center filtration with mid-single-digit organic growth and 20%+ EBITDA margins. The 10-K discloses Cummins concentration via a long-term supply agreement that auto-extends — a hidden moat: ATMU is the single-source filter spec on Cummins-platformed engines and that spec sticks for the engine's 15–20 year service life regardless of who owns the truck. The exchange-offer mechanic in March 2024 left CMI index funds force-selling ATMU into a thin float; that overhang is largely cleared but explains why the stock still trades below pure-play filtration peers (Donaldson at ~16–18x EBITDA).

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 2026 earnings (May 2026): first quarter with Koch Filter consolidated; segment disclosure for new Industrial Solutions segment.
- Synergy/integration update mid-year: management guided to high-single-digit ROIC by 2028; any signs of revenue cross-sell into data center customers re-rates the multiple.
- Buyback expansion / second bolt-on M&A announcement; Investor Day H2 2026 plausible.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Filters consumed in trucks/HVAC — simple |
| Moat | 7 | OE-spec lock-in + brand (Fleetguard, Koch) |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | $236M cash, ~$1.0B pro-forma debt, ~2.5x leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | ~16x earnings, 3% FCF yield — fair not cheap |
| Catalyst | 7 | Koch synergies, segment rerate, buyback |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | 30% upside / 20% downside |
| Business Quality | 8 | 20% EBITDA, mid-teens ROIC, 80% aftermarket |
| Executability | 7 | $5B mcap, liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | EV transition + Cummins concentration risk |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Aftermarket cushions, but truck cycle matters |
| **TOTAL** | **66** | |

---

INBX.name: Inhibrx Biosciences, Inc.
INBX.content:
# 23. INBX — Inhibrx Biosciences, Inc.
## SPINOFF | Sanofi-Inhibrx Split-Off Stub | Score: 58/100

### THE SETUP
INBX was spun out of Inhibrx in May 2024 as part of Sanofi's $1.7B acquisition of the parent's lead asset (INBRX-101). Public Inhibrx holders received Sanofi cash, a CVR, and shares in this new entity holding the residual pipeline (INBRX-109, INBRX-1061). Forced-seller dynamics from arbs and deal funds left an under-followed clinical-stage biotech with a real lead asset.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Inhibrx Biosciences is the residual pipeline company carved out of legacy Inhibrx, Inc. when Sanofi acquired the AAT-deficiency asset INBRX-101 in May 2024 for ~$1.7B in cash plus CVR. The split-off mechanic distributed shares of INBX to existing Inhibrx holders alongside the cash consideration. Lead asset is INBRX-109, a tetravalent DR5 agonist antibody being developed for conventional chondrosarcoma — a rare bone cancer with no approved systemic therapies. Secondary asset INBRX-1061 is in earlier development. Management is the same proven biotech operator team from the prior Inhibrx, which built INBRX-101 to a $1.7B exit. Market cap around $910M at $67.13 — already up roughly 5x in twelve months as the chondrosarcoma data narrative played out.

The bear case is biotech-binary. Single-arm Phase 2 data in a rare cancer can disappoint at the registrational endpoint; FDA's accelerated approval pathway for ultra-orphan indications is real but unpredictable. The current valuation already prices in optimistic Phase 2 readouts and a probable buyout, leaving compressed upside on success and meaningful downside on disappointment. Cash runway from the spin-off allocation is finite — depending on burn, INBX may need to raise capital before pivotal data, diluting holders at potentially unfavorable prices. No revenue, no commercial infrastructure, and limited sell-side coverage means any negative tape gets amplified.

The bull case rests on INBRX-109's mechanism and the orphan-disease economics. Conventional chondrosarcoma has no approved therapy; even modest activity at acceptable safety would clear FDA hurdles. The DR5 agonist class has a complicated history (multiple failures in solid tumors), but tetravalent format and target selection differentiate INBRX-109. A pivotal trial design announcement, accelerated approval pathway endorsement from FDA, or strategic interest from a large oncology buyer could each re-rate the equity. Rare-cancer assets with proof-of-concept data routinely command $1-3B acquisition values. Management's prior exit credibility is real currency in biotech M&A circles.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The split-off mechanics are the alpha here. INBX shares were distributed to a Inhibrx holder base that was overwhelmingly merger-arb funds focused on capturing the Sanofi cash and CVR — those holders systematically dump the spinoff stub regardless of fundamentals, creating the multi-month forced-selling window that drove the initial 5x rally as natural buyers stepped in. Look at the Form 10 (registration statement filed by INBX prior to spin) for the exact pipeline asset transfer terms and the cash allocation from parent. The CVR economics from the Sanofi deal are separate from INBX equity — don't double-count. Watch for Form 4 insider buying from the management team as a signal of confidence in upcoming readouts. Sell-side coverage remains thin: any new initiation could move the stock.

### CATALYSTS
- INBRX-109 Phase 2 chondrosarcoma data update (2026)
- Pivotal trial design / FDA pathway announcement
- Strategic M&A interest or pharma partnership
- INBRX-1061 IND advancement

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 5/10 |
| Management integrity | 8/10 |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Balance sheet | 6/10 |
| Free cash flow | 2/10 |
| Moat | 6/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| Margin of safety | 4/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7/10 |
| Downside protection | 4/10 |
| **Total** | **55/100** |

---

SUNS.name: Sunrise Realty Trust, Inc.
SUNS.content:
# 24. SUNS — Sunrise Realty Trust, Inc.

## SPINOFF | ~14% dividend yield, $114M micro-cap | Score: 62/100

---

### THE SETUP

Sunrise Realty Trust was spun off from AFC Gamma (AFCG) on July 9, 2024. The parent, AFC Gamma, is a specialty cannabis-credit lender; Sunrise inherited AFCG's small portfolio of non-cannabis Sun-Belt commercial real estate loans and operates as an externally managed mortgage REIT (the manager, Sunrise Manager LLC, is affiliated with the same sponsor — Leonard Tannenbaum's complex). Market cap is ~$114M, current share price ~$8.53, with a trailing dividend yield in the low-to-mid teens.

The spinoff dynamics here are textbook: AFCG holders are cannabis-credit specialists who suddenly received a thinly traded Sun-Belt CRE-mezz REIT they had zero appetite for. Forced selling pressure was immediate and persistent through late 2024 and into 2025. The micro-cap size disqualifies it from virtually every institutional benchmark — there's no natural buyer base. Layer on the externally managed structure (which classic REIT investors deride for fee drag), and you have a chronic orphan asset.

The bull case is that the loan book is brand new — 2024-to-2026 vintages underwritten *after* the regional banking dislocation, when distressed banks were actively shedding CRE exposure. Non-bank originators with capital and underwriting discipline can pick over the carcass at attractive spreads. SUNS's loans are largely floating-rate, secured by Sun-Belt assets (multifamily, mixed-use, hospitality), with sponsors who have skin in the game.

### ANALYST SUMMARY (3p)

This is a yield-and-NAV story dressed up as a spinoff. The 14% dividend looks scary but is currently covered by net interest income on the loan book — the question is whether it stays covered as loans season and any credit issues emerge. Vintage matters: a brand-new loan book underwritten in 2024–2026 should not have material credit problems for at least 2–3 years (CRE loans typically have 3–5 year terms with delayed credit recognition). The portfolio's Sun-Belt focus benefits from population/job migration trends.

The structural negatives are real. External management = ~1.5% management fee on equity plus an incentive fee; this drags ROE materially. The micro-cap status creates persistent illiquidity. The shared-sponsor relationship with AFC Gamma creates governance optics issues. And there's a complete absence of operating history as a standalone entity — no one knows yet whether the manager can build a book of meaningful scale.

The optionality lever is "strategic alternatives" — at $114M market cap, SUNS is an obvious roll-up candidate for a larger non-bank CRE lender, or could be liquidated at NAV (which is meaningfully above current price). Tannenbaum's complex has shown willingness to monetize prior platforms, and the externally managed structure makes a manager buy-in or sale comparatively straightforward.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

A screen sees: micro-cap mortgage REIT, unproven, externally managed, double-digit yield (often a yield trap signal). It misses: (a) the spinoff orphan dynamic that explains the discount mechanically, (b) the brand-new loan vintage as a credit-quality positive, (c) the structural takeout/rollup optionality given the parent's track record, and (d) that NAV per share materially exceeds market price — a recurring CEF-like discount that activist or strategic intervention could close. It also misses the Sun-Belt CRE thesis: while national CRE remains under pressure, Sun-Belt fundamentals (multifamily, industrial) have held up better than gateway markets.

### CATALYSTS

- Quarterly NII and originations updates demonstrating loan book scaling
- Strategic alternatives review outcome (if pursued)
- Dividend coverage maintained or grown over coming quarters
- Loan-portfolio mark-to-market updates as Sun-Belt CRE values stabilize
- Potential rollup/sale to a larger non-bank CRE lender
- Any institutional ownership disclosure

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- **Business quality**: Below average. Externally managed mortgage REITs are structurally inferior to internally managed peers due to fee drag and conflicts. CRE credit is cyclical. Brand-new platform with no operating track record.
- **Management**: Tannenbaum complex has built and monetized prior credit platforms; experienced underwriters but the external structure aligns interests imperfectly with public shareholders.
- **Balance sheet**: Clean — minimal corporate leverage at the entity level, loan book is the asset. As a young REIT, balance sheet has not been tested.
- **Capital allocation**: Pays out the bulk of taxable income as required for REIT status; share count is small but has grown via secondaries. Watch for dilutive issuance.
- **Margin of safety**: Trades meaningfully below NAV; downside cushioned by loan book asset value if credit holds. Upside requires either (a) the discount to NAV to close as the platform scales, or (b) a strategic transaction.
- **Risks**: Credit deterioration in any single large loan, external manager conflicts, dividend cut if NII compresses, micro-cap illiquidity making exits painful, sponsor self-dealing.

This is a position-sizing story more than a conviction story — the asymmetry is real but the platform is unproven. Suitable as a small allocation in a basket of orphan-spinoff yield names where the goal is to clip the dividend while waiting for either NAV convergence or a strategic event. The 62 score reflects a genuine value opportunity weighed against the structural negatives of micro-cap external management.

---

It misses: (a) the spinoff orphan dynamic that explains the discount mechanically, (b) the brand-new loan vintage as a credit-quality positive, (c) the structural takeout/rollup optionality given the parent's track record, and (d) that NAV per share materially exceeds market price — a recurring CEF-like discount that activist or strategic intervention could close. It also misses the Sun-Belt CRE thesis: while national CRE remains under pressure, Sun-Belt fundamentals (multifamily, industrial) have held up better than gateway markets.

SIRI.name: Sirius XM Holdings Inc.
SIRI.content:
# 25. SIRI — Sirius XM Holdings Inc.
## SPINOFF — FORCED SELLING | <8x FCF, ~5% yield, Berkshire ~35% | Score: 75/100

### THE SETUP
On September 9, 2024, Liberty Media completed a complex two-step transaction: Liberty's SiriusXM tracking stock (LSXMA/K) was split off from Liberty Media and immediately combined with the public Sirius XM minority stake into a single new public company — "New Sirius" — trading under the existing SIRI ticker with a 1-for-10 reverse stock split. The mechanics were tax-efficient for Liberty holders but messy from a market-microstructure perspective: tracking-stock holders received the new SIRI on a tax-free basis (creating natural sellers who never wanted to own the standalone), while the legacy public SIRI minority received a small fractional position post-conversion. The result was extended forced-selling pressure on the new ticker that bore no relationship to the underlying business. Today SIRI sits at roughly $25 with a market cap of ~$8.5B — and Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway has been steadily building, now owning approximately 35% of the company. That is not a typo: a single owner with full information access and no need to sell holds more than a third of the float.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Strip out the spinoff mechanics and what's left is a simple FCF compounder. Sirius XM generates ~$1.2B of annual free cash flow on an ~$8.5B market cap — roughly 14% FCF yield, or under 8x FCF — with a ~5% dividend and ongoing buyback authorization. The bear narrative is "satellite radio is being eaten by streaming," but the data has been more nuanced than the narrative: the in-vehicle attach business (where SIRI is bundled with new car deliveries) has stabilized as US auto SAAR has recovered, the self-pay subscriber base has held up better than expected, and the addition of an ad-supported tier and podcast monetization has incrementally expanded the addressable market beyond the legacy in-car satellite product. Berkshire's accumulation pattern — increasing the stake from ~32% in October 2024 to ~35% by February 2025 — is the most informative single data point: this is value-investor capital with a permanent time horizon adding to the position post-spin. Take-private optionality is real: at a 35% stake, Berkshire could in principle support a take-private at a modest premium to current levels with very little incremental capital. Even absent any strategic transaction, base-case FCF coverage of buyback + dividend at the current price implies a high single-digit per-share intrinsic return regardless of multiple expansion.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three things. First, the post-spin float is structurally concentrated — Berkshire 35% plus other Liberty-related holders means actual public free float is much smaller than headline market cap suggests, which depresses index-fund participation and keeps the multiple compressed. Second, the secular-decline narrative obscures the fact that SIRI's core economics are subscription-recurring with high margins on each incremental subscriber dollar — the relevant question is subscriber stability, not subscriber growth, and stability is what the recent prints show. Third, the take-private math: at a 35% Berkshire stake, a take-private would only require ~65% of shares to be acquired, and the FCF generation would self-fund a meaningful portion of any LBO structure — this optionality isn't reflected in any consensus model.

### CATALYSTS
- Quarterly FCF prints and continued capital return execution
- Continued Berkshire purchases (each disclosure tightens the float)
- Subscriber stabilization in self-pay tier
- Potential strategic transaction or take-private
- Dividend raise as FCF compounds
- Ad-supported tier and podcast monetization growth

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: Yes — subscription audio in cars, streaming app, ad-supported tier, Pandora
- Durable moat: Moderate-to-strong — exclusive in-vehicle satellite footprint, exclusive content (Howard Stern, sports), entrenched OEM relationships
- Honest, capable management: Reasonable — disciplined capital return, FCF-focused
- Bought at fair price: Yes — under 8x FCF for a stable subscription cash flow with ~5% yield is cheap by any consumer-subscription standard
- Margin of safety: Strong — Berkshire at 35% provides both validation and natural floor
- Key risks: Long-term EV/streaming substitution, content cost inflation (Stern renewal, sports rights), subscriber churn acceleration

---

Sources: SiriusXM press release on independent public company status, CNBC Berkshire stake disclosures (Oct 2024, Feb 2025). Score reflects unusual quality of setup — value investor anchor + clean FCF math + spinoff dislocation.

---
SIRI.analystSummary: Strip out the spinoff mechanics and what's left is a simple FCF compounder. Sirius XM generates ~$1.2B of annual free cash flow on an ~$8.5B market cap — roughly 14% FCF yield, or under 8x FCF — with a ~5% dividend and ongoing buyback authorization. The bear narrative is "satellite radio is being eaten by streaming," but the data has been more nuanced than the narrative: the in-vehicle attach business (where SIRI is bundled with new car deliveries) has stabilized as US auto SAAR has recovered, the self-pay subscriber base has held up better than expected, and the addition of an ad-supported tier and podcast monetization has incrementally expanded the addressable market beyond the legacy in-car satellite product. Berkshire's accumulation pattern — increasing the stake from ~32% in October 2024 to ~35% by February 2025 — is the most informative single data point: this is value-investor capital with a permanent time horizon adding to the position post-spin. Take-private optionality is real: at a 35% stake, Berkshire could in principle support a take-private at a modest premium to current levels with very little incremental capital. Even absent any strategic transaction, base-case FCF coverage of buyback + dividend at the current price implies a high single-digit per-share intrinsic return regardless of multiple expansion.
SIRI.whatScreenCantSee: Three things. First, the post-spin float is structurally concentrated — Berkshire 35% plus other Liberty-related holders means actual public free float is much smaller than headline market cap suggests, which depresses index-fund participation and keeps the multiple compressed. Second, the secular-decline narrative obscures the fact that SIRI's core economics are subscription-recurring with high margins on each incremental subscriber dollar — the relevant question is subscriber stability, not subscriber growth, and stability is what the recent prints show. Third, the take-private math: at a 35% Berkshire stake, a take-private would only require ~65% of shares to be acquired, and the FCF generation would self-fund a meaningful portion of any LBO structure — this optionality isn't reflected in any consensus model.

BNT.name: Brookfield Wealth Solutions Ltd.
BNT.content:
# 26. BNT — Brookfield Wealth Solutions Ltd.
## SPINOFF — QUALITY-AT-DISCOUNT | $1.5B annual DE, 1:1 BN exchangeable | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
BNT is Brookfield's insurance/annuity flywheel — formerly Brookfield Reinsurance, rebranded September 2024 — exchangeable 1-for-1 into Brookfield Corporation (BN) shares, which embeds a structural floor at BN's per-share value. It generates ~$1.5B annual distributable earnings deployed into Brookfield-managed yield strategies, recreating the Athene/Apollo flywheel at a meaningful discount to those peers, while ticker confusion and dual-listing mechanics keep the float mispriced.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Brookfield Wealth Solutions (BNT) is the Bermuda-domiciled insurance arm of the Brookfield ecosystem. Mcap $12.92B, current price $46.18. The structure: Class A exchangeable shares exchangeable 1-for-1 into BN parent stock at any time, listed on NYSE and TSX under the same BNT ticker. Business: pension risk transfer, annuity reinsurance, retirement solutions. Built through the AEL acquisition (closed 2024) and the Just Group (UK retirement annuities) acquisition closed late 2024, doubling AUM to ~$120B+. Generates ~$1.5B annual distributable operating earnings (DOE) from spread investing — gathers float at 4-5% cost, deploys into Brookfield infrastructure/credit/real estate at 7-9% yields. The 1:1 BN exchange right means BNT can never trade meaningfully below BN's per-share value (currently $46.59) — embedded floor.

Bear case: insurance leverage. BNT has ~$120B AUM funded by long-dated annuity liabilities — a credit-spread blow-out (think 2008 or 2020) marks down the asset side faster than liabilities adjust, vaporizing book equity. Just Group integration is early — UK regulatory (PRA) approval was lengthy and ongoing solvency capital ratios are tight under Solvency UK rules. The 1:1 BN exchange floor is only a floor if BN itself doesn't crater, and BNT's DE is highly correlated with BN's principal investments (same Brookfield-managed funds). Insurance investors don't recognize BNT as an Athene/Global Atlantic peer because of the exchange feature, while Brookfield investors view it as a sub-economic share class — orphaned coverage. Multiple ticker changes (BAMR → BNRE → BNT) wrecked databases and screen continuity.

Bull case: replicates the Athene/Apollo private-credit-on-insurance-balance-sheet model — the most successful financial-services growth strategy of the last decade — at a discount to those peers. Distributable earnings ~$1.5B and growing 25%+ as Just Group spreads kick in and AEL integration completes. At 15x DE, fair value ~$22.5B vs. $12.9B mcap = 75% upside. The exchangeable structure is a hidden buyback machine: when BNT trades below BN, holders convert and BN/BNT collapses the discount via repurchases. Catalysts: quarterly DE growth, additional pension-risk-transfer deal flow, and convergence trade as US insurance analysts begin coverage.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The exchange feature works asymmetrically: BNT is exchangeable for BN, but not the reverse — meaning BNT functions as a "tracker" of BN with an embedded option to capture insurance segment outperformance. When BNT trades below BN, arbs convert and Brookfield treats the exchange as economically equivalent to a buyback. When BNT trades above BN (rare), no conversion and BNT keeps its premium. Just Group acquisition was funded primarily with internal capital, not equity issuance — non-dilutive growth. Watch the quarterly DOE per share line in the supplemental: management has guided to $5-6 DOE/share by 2027 (vs. ~$3.50 LTM), implying ~10x forward DOE at current price. The Bermuda domicile is tax-efficient but creates US tax surprises (PFIC analysis required for some holders). Index ineligibility — excluded from S&P TSX Composite and most major US indices because of the exchangeable structure — is a flow-driven persistent depressant.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q4 2025 and Q1 2026 DOE prints — Just Group accretion
- Additional pension-risk-transfer deal announcements (UK + US)
- Initiation of US insurance analyst coverage (Athene/GL peer comp)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 6 | Insurance + exchangeable mechanics |
| Moat | 8 | Brookfield asset sourcing scale |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | $120B AUM, leveraged insurance balance sheet |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | ~10x forward DOE; Athene at 15x+ |
| Catalyst | 7 | DE growth, deal flow |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | 1:1 BN floor + 75% upside |
| Business Quality | 8 | Spread investing flywheel, growing AUM |
| Executability | 8 | $13B mcap, decent liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Credit spread blow-up, Solvency UK |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Rate + credit sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **70** | |

---

SFD.name: Smithfield Foods, Inc.
SFD.content:
# 27. SFD — Smithfield Foods, Inc.
## SPINOFF — FORCED SELLING | ~10x P/E, 88% controlled by WH Group | Score: 68/100

### THE SETUP
Smithfield Foods is the largest US pork producer — vertically integrated from hog farms in North Carolina, Virginia, Utah and the Midwest through to packaged meats brands like Smithfield, Eckrich, Nathan's Famous and Farmland. The company was taken private in 2013 by Hong Kong-listed WH Group for $4.7B (then the largest Chinese acquisition of a US company). On January 28, 2025, WH Group brought Smithfield back public via a Nasdaq IPO, selling roughly 12% of the float at $20/share — below the indicated range of $23–27. The IPO repriced lower because of (a) Chinese-parent overhang, (b) softness in pork-cycle sentiment, and (c) a generally cool 2025 IPO market. WH Group still owns roughly 88% of the equity, meaning the public float is small relative to the headline $11B market cap. SFD effectively trades like an orphaned mid-cap controlled by a foreign parent — exactly the kind of post-IPO situation where forced-seller dynamics meet a clean cap table and an under-followed name.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Smithfield is one of the cleanest "carve-out + foreign parent" setups of 2025. Revenue of roughly $15.5B grew ~9.8% YoY into 2025 with record free cash flow generation, helped by stable Packaged Meats margins (the higher-quality branded segment) absorbing volatility from the commodity Fresh Pork and Hog Production segments. Trading near $28.88 implies ~10x earnings — a deep discount to consumer-staples meat peers like Tyson and Hormel, despite better organic growth and a more focused portfolio after the divestiture of European and Mexican operations. Capital allocation has been disciplined: a $1.3B Sioux Falls capacity expansion targets the highest-margin Packaged Meats output, and the recent acquisition of Nathan's Famous at roughly 12.4x EBITDA is immediately accretive and brings a high-margin licensed brand into the portfolio. Free cash flow comfortably supports both the dividend and recently-announced buyback authorization. The reflexive bear case — "it's controlled by a Chinese parent" — is real but quantifiable: every secondary sale by WH Group reduces the overhang and increases float, which in turn increases the probability of S&P MidCap inclusion (a meaningful incremental-buyer catalyst). Hog cycle inflection in 2026 — assuming USDA hog inventories continue to normalize — would compound the multiple expansion case.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three things. First, the post-IPO float math: with only ~12% of shares public, every standard "average daily volume" or "float-adjusted market cap" screen materially understates how thin SFD trades. Index funds and many ETFs are mechanically prevented from owning it at current float, which keeps a structural buyer base on the sidelines until WH Group sells more. Second, Packaged Meats is roughly 60%+ of segment profit despite being a smaller share of revenue — most generalist screens treat SFD as commodity meat (low-multiple) when the earnings stream is much closer to a branded food company (mid-teens multiple). Third, the Nathan's Famous deal isn't just an accretive bolt-on — it signals that management can deploy capital sensibly in branded acquisitions rather than burning cash on commodity capacity, which has historically been the pork industry's failure mode.

### CATALYSTS
- Continued WH Group secondary share sales (each one increases float and reduces overhang)
- S&P MidCap 400 inclusion as float crosses the eligibility threshold
- Nathan's Famous deal close and integration synergies
- Hog cycle inflection — USDA pork inventories already normalizing
- Continued buyback execution under recently-authorized program
- 2026 dividend raise as FCF compounds

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: Yes — pork from farm to package, plus branded meats
- Durable moat: Moderate — scale advantage in fresh pork, true brand moat in Packaged Meats (Smithfield, Nathan's, Eckrich)
- Honest, capable management: Reasonable — disciplined M&A multiple on Nathan's, capacity expansion focused on highest-margin segment
- Bought at fair price: Yes — ~10x earnings for a #1 US pork producer with growing branded mix is below replacement cost
- Margin of safety: Moderate — controlled-company governance risk is real but priced in; FCF coverage of buyback + dividend is strong
- Key risks: Hog-cycle commodity volatility, US-China geopolitical risk affecting WH Group's actions, controlled-company governance limits minority shareholder rights

---

Sources: SEC 424B4 prospectus (Jan 2025), Food Processing IPO coverage, SFD investor materials. Score reflects strong fundamental setup tempered by 88% parent ownership and pork-cycle commodity exposure.

---

RHLD.name: Resolute Holdings Management, Inc.
RHLD.content:
# 28. RHLD — Resolute Holdings Management, Inc.
## SPINOFF | $1.27B mkt cap, 2.5% of CMPO LTM Adj EBITDA fee | Score: 62/100
### THE SETUP
On February 28, 2025, CompoSecure (CMPO) completed the spin-off of Resolute Holdings Management as a separately listed Nasdaq company (one share of RHLD per twelve CMPO held). The vehicle is led by David Cote (former Honeywell CEO, Executive Chairman) and Tom Knott (CEO, prior CompoSecure operator), and was structured as an alternative-asset platform with one initial managed business — CompoSecure itself. The economics flow from a Management Agreement under which CompoSecure Holdings, L.L.C. pays Resolute "a quarterly management fee in cash equal to 2.5% of CompoSecure's Latest Twelve Months' Adjusted EBITDA." The taxable distribution, the tiny float, and the absence of revenue at spin produced indiscriminate selling that drove the price into the low $20s; by April 2026 the stock has run roughly six- to twelve-fold, depending on the entry print, to ~$147 — analysts who passed on it at $24 now anchor on "too expensive." The mispricing has migrated from neglect to narrative: the new market is debating whether RHLD deserves an asset-manager multiple on a single fee stream while it tries to sign a second managed company.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
RHLD is a publicly traded operating-services manager whose initial fee stream is a fixed percentage of CompoSecure's adjusted EBITDA. The structure is deliberately Brookfield- or 3G-flavored: a small, low-overhead holdco that earns recurring fees for capital-allocation oversight, M&A sourcing, and operational guidance, with the explicit intent to add additional managed companies over time. Each new managed acquisition — whether financed by the platform itself, by partners, or by separate vehicles — would compound the fee base without commensurate G&A growth. That is the bull case: an early-stage compounder with an operator-CEO of unusual pedigree.

The skeptical reading is just as straightforward. Today, RHLD has one client. The fee stream tracks CompoSecure's metal-card EBITDA, which has its own customer-concentration questions (Robinhood, American Express co-branded products) and a maturing premium-card category. At ~$1.27B market cap on a fee equal to 2.5% of CMPO LTM Adjusted EBITDA, the implied multiple on current-run-rate revenue is steep and largely discounts a successful second deal. Management has been clear that the model requires acquisitions, but those announcements remain prospective.

The asymmetry here is no longer the pure post-spin dislocation — that trade has been made. What remains is optionality on a credible operator successfully replicating the CompoSecure structure. If Cote and Knott sign one or two additional managed businesses on similar economics within twenty-four months, the platform thesis crystallizes; if not, the multiple compresses toward the underlying CMPO fee. Position sizing should reflect that bimodality, and that the share count is small enough that any large prospective capital raise to fund acquisitions will be price-sensitive.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen will show ~$1.27B equity, minimal revenue, no GAAP earnings to speak of, and a ticker that looks expensive on every conventional metric. What screens cannot see: (1) the structural permanence of the management agreement — the fee is contractual, not at-will; (2) the pedigree of the operating team and the network it gives Cote and Knott to source mid-cap take-privates and orphan divisions; (3) the small float that creates both upside slippage and downside air-pockets; and (4) the precedent value — CompoSecure's first managed-acquisition would re-rate the model, while extended silence does the opposite.

### CATALYSTS
- **2026-Q1/Q2 earnings prints** — first full year of management fee accruals; demonstrates the unit economics of a single-client fee model.
- **Announcement of a second managed business** (any time 2026) — single most important catalyst for the platform thesis.
- **Capital raise or structured-vehicle launch** to fund a managed-company acquisition — likely required and likely dilutive but would validate the model.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality / moat | 6/10 |
| Management quality | 9/10 |
| Capital allocation | 7/10 |
| Balance sheet strength | 7/10 |
| Earnings predictability | 5/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 8/10 |
| Insider alignment | 8/10 |
| Margin of safety at price | 3/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 5/10 |
| Downside protection | 4/10 |
| **Total** | **62/100** |

---

EHLD.name: EuroHoldings Ltd.
EHLD.content:
# 29. EHLD — EuroHoldings Ltd.
## SPINOFF | Sub-$30M Greek Shipping Micro-Cap NAV Arb | Score: 62/100

### THE SETUP
Spun off from Euroseas (ESEA) on March 18, 2025 with two older feeder containerships, no debt, and a market cap below $30M. Six weeks post-spin, the board initiated a strategic alternatives review and then pivoted toward MR product tankers — classic forced-seller, orphaned micro-cap NAV arbitrage with multiple potential resolution paths.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
EuroHoldings is the textbook "ugly stepchild" spinoff: ESEA shareholders received fractional shares of a $25M micro-cap holding two aging feeder containerships (~2,500-3,000 TEU vintage). Index funds dumped, retail had no thesis, and Greek-shipping orphan optics scared off institutions. With the cash position and second-hand vessel values almost certainly exceeding the spin-off market cap, the discount-to-NAV is the entire trade. Float is tiny, liquidity is poor, and the entity is functionally controlled by the Pittas family — alignment is high but governance is family-run.

The bear case is straightforward: containership values are mean-reverting and the two vessels are old enough to face scrap-vs-trade decisions inside three years. Strategic-alternatives reviews from sub-$30M shipping shells frequently fizzle into self-dealing reverse mergers or perpetual delay. Greek-shipping micro-caps are notorious for value destruction at the corporate level (dilutive ATMs, related-party transactions, Marshall Islands governance with no recourse). The November 2025 acquisition of M/T Hellas Avatar (49,997 DWT product tanker) signals the family is using EHLD as a vehicle to scale rather than liquidate — meaning the NAV-realization catalyst could be deferred indefinitely.

The bull case rests on three legs: (1) NAV arbitrage at current prices is meaningful even using conservative second-hand vessel marks plus net cash; (2) the MR product-tanker pivot positions EHLD for the 2026 supply/demand inflection (Russian sanctions, fleet aging, slow newbuild orderbook); (3) strategic-alternatives review opens optionality for special dividend, sale to a peer (Castor, Globus, Performance Shipping), or roll-up. Asymmetry: vessel scrap value provides a downside floor; tanker re-rating plus NAV closure is multi-bagger.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
EHLD does not appear in most screens at all — it's a recently-spun Marshall Islands company with limited US filing history, no analyst coverage, and a market cap below most institutional minimums. The Hellas Avatar acquisition (announced November 17, 2025) fundamentally changes the asset mix from "two old containerships" to "containership + product tanker," and screening tools don't yet reflect the post-deal balance sheet. Critically, the strategic-alternatives review remains technically open per the April 30, 2025 release — meaning a wind-down or sale remains a live option even as management deploys capital. Pittas family ownership and Euroseas overlap create related-party risk but also strong cross-incentives.

### CATALYSTS
- Q4 2025/FY2025 results release (Q1 2026) — first full picture post-Hellas Avatar
- Strategic alternatives review formal conclusion or update through 2026
- Product tanker market re-rating in 2026 driven by sanctions/fleet age dynamics

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Shipping NAV math is clean; Greek micro-cap governance is opaque |
| Moat | 2 | Commodity shipping, no moat |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Spun debt-free per March 2025 release; recent acquisition added some leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | $25M market cap vs. likely higher NAV in vessels + cash |
| Catalyst | 6 | Strategic alternatives review live but timing uncertain |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Scrap-value floor; multi-bagger NAV upside |
| Business Quality | 3 | Cyclical, capital intensive, no pricing power |
| Executability | 3 | Sub-$30M cap, illiquid, OTC-like trading |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Family-controlled vehicle could trap minority shareholders indefinitely |
| Macro Independence | 3 | Highly leveraged to global trade and tanker rates |
| **TOTAL** | **50** | Pure NAV arb with execution risk |

---
