EQIX.name: Equinix, Inc.
EQIX.content:
# 78. EQIX — Equinix, Inc.
## ACTIVIST | Elliott Engagement + AI Capacity Re-Rating | Score: 71/100

### THE SETUP
Elliott Investment Management disclosed an initial 0.15% Equinix position in Q1 2025 and materially increased the stake in Q2 2025 (per 13F). Public reporting (CNBC August 2025, Bloomberg July 2025) suggests collaborative engagement focused on amplifying EQIX's AI/HPC positioning given its industry-leading interconnection density. Board-seat invitation by the May 2026 AGM is the consensus expectation.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Equinix is the world's largest interconnection-focused data center REIT, operating 260+ International Business Exchange (IBX) facilities across 70+ metros. The franchise rests on the network effect of having more carriers, clouds, and enterprise tenants in close physical proximity than any competitor — measured by the "Equinix Fabric" software-defined interconnect platform and the recurring revenue from cross-connects (high-margin, near-monopoly). With AI workloads driving demand for low-latency colocation and hybrid-cloud stitching, EQIX should be a primary beneficiary, but the stock has lagged pure AI infrastructure plays.

The bear case is twofold. First, EQIX's growth model depends on continuous greenfield IBX construction, and capex intensity has stepped up materially as the company chases AI/HPC capacity. Funding via debt and equity has compressed FFO/share growth and weighed on the multiple. Second, hyperscaler self-build represents a structural threat — Microsoft, Google, AWS, and Meta increasingly build their own facilities and bypass colocation for primary compute. EQIX's interconnection franchise insulates it somewhat, but the narrative of "data center = AI" doesn't perfectly fit a company whose core asset is multi-tenant retail colocation. Short reports (Hindenburg, May 2024) raised questions about accounting and depreciation policies that, while contested by the company, lingered with some investors.

The bull case is the Elliott catalyst plus AI-driven demand acceleration. Elliott has data-center expertise from Cyxtera and CoreWeave engagements and is pushing for capital allocation discipline, faster monetization of AI-related power capacity, and improved per-share growth metrics. A board seat by May 2026 would catalyze announcements: AI-specific product offerings, accelerated capacity sales, possible JV structures with hyperscalers or AI labs, and potentially returning capital. Take-private rumors have circulated periodically (Brookfield, GIP, KKR all logical buyers), and Elliott's involvement increases the probability of a process.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The interconnection moat is the entire investment case and screens don't capture it. EQIX's 480,000+ cross-connects generate ~17-20% of revenue at near-100% incremental margin, and each new tenant ecosystem-effect-strengthens the existing one. Read the 10-K's discussion of "Magnetism" — the company's term for the network-effect dynamic where customers cluster with their counterparties. The AI thesis hinges on whether AI inference (latency-sensitive, distributed) requires the kind of metro-edge density EQIX provides, vs. AI training (large-scale, centralized) which favors purpose-built hyperscaler campuses. Elliott engagement is collaborative per CNBC reporting, suggesting management is open to incremental optimization rather than radical change. xScale joint venture (with GIC) is the off-balance-sheet vehicle for hyperscaler-scale builds — economics there matter materially.

### CATALYSTS
- May 2026 annual meeting — potential board seat / settlement announcement
- Q1 and Q2 2026 earnings — AI/HPC bookings and capacity commentary
- Possible private-market take-private rumors or strategic JV announcements

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Colocation REIT economics are clear; AI overlay still emerging |
| Moat | 9 | Best-in-class interconnection network effect |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | Investment-grade rated; rising debt to fund AI capex |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | Premium REIT multiple; not deep value |
| Catalyst | 7 | Defined Elliott timeline into May 2026 AGM |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Modest upside on activist win; downside if AI narrative cools |
| Business Quality | 8 | Recurring high-margin revenue with pricing power |
| Executability | 9 | $100B+ market cap, deeply liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Hyperscaler self-build + capex intensity could compress multiple |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Demand resilient but rate-sensitive REIT |
| **TOTAL** | **67** | Quality plus catalyst at fair-not-cheap valuation |

---
EQIX.analystSummary:
Equinix is the world's largest interconnection-focused data center REIT, operating 260+ International Business Exchange (IBX) facilities across 70+ metros. The franchise rests on the network effect of having more carriers, clouds, and enterprise tenants in close physical proximity than any competitor — measured by the "Equinix Fabric" software-defined interconnect platform and the recurring revenue from cross-connects (high-margin, near-monopoly). With AI workloads driving demand for low-latency colocation and hybrid-cloud stitching, EQIX should be a primary beneficiary, but the stock has lagged pure AI infrastructure plays.

The bear case is twofold. First, EQIX's growth model depends on continuous greenfield IBX construction, and capex intensity has stepped up materially as the company chases AI/HPC capacity. Funding via debt and equity has compressed FFO/share growth and weighed on the multiple. Second, hyperscaler self-build represents a structural threat — Microsoft, Google, AWS, and Meta increasingly build their own facilities and bypass colocation for primary compute. EQIX's interconnection franchise insulates it somewhat, but the narrative of "data center = AI" doesn't perfectly fit a company whose core asset is multi-tenant retail colocation. Short reports (Hindenburg, May 2024) raised questions about accounting and depreciation policies that, while contested by the company, lingered with some investors.

The bull case is the Elliott catalyst plus AI-driven demand acceleration. Elliott has data-center expertise from Cyxtera and CoreWeave engagements and is pushing for capital allocation discipline, faster monetization of AI-related power capacity, and improved per-share growth metrics. A board seat by May 2026 would catalyze announcements: AI-specific product offerings, accelerated capacity sales, possible JV structures with hyperscalers or AI labs, and potentially returning capital. Take-private rumors have circulated periodically (Brookfield, GIP, KKR all logical buyers), and Elliott's involvement increases the probability of a process.
EQIX.whatScreenCantSee: The interconnection moat is the entire investment case and screens don't capture it. EQIX's 480,000+ cross-connects generate ~17-20% of revenue at near-100% incremental margin, and each new tenant ecosystem-effect-strengthens the existing one. Read the 10-K's discussion of "Magnetism" — the company's term for the network-effect dynamic where customers cluster with their counterparties. The AI thesis hinges on whether AI inference (latency-sensitive, distributed) requires the kind of metro-edge density EQIX provides, vs. AI training (large-scale, centralized) which favors purpose-built hyperscaler campuses. Elliott engagement is collaborative per CNBC reporting, suggesting management is open to incremental optimization rather than radical change. xScale joint venture (with GIC) is the off-balance-sheet vehicle for hyperscaler-scale builds — economics there matter materially.

NCLH.name: Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd.
NCLH.content:
# 79. NCLH — Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd.
## ACTIVIST — OPERATIONAL TURN | Elliott 10%+ stake, 5 board seats Mar 2026 | Score: 76/100

### THE SETUP
Elliott Investment Management disclosed a >10% stake in NCLH on February 15, 2026 and reached a cooperation agreement on March 27, 2026 placing five new directors on a nine-seat board — including Steve Pagliuca (Bain Capital), Alex Cruz (ex-British Airways CEO), Kevin Lansberry (ex-Disney CFO), Brian MacDonald (CDK Global CEO), and Jonathan Cohen (Hepco CEO). CEO Harry Sommer's predecessor Frank Del Rio is gone, current CEO Joel Chidsey takes the chair, and four long-tenured directors stepped down by March 31. The cruise industry's #3 player is now in the middle of a Bain-touched operational reset while trading at a 30%+ EV/EBITDA discount to RCL.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
NCLH operates three cruise brands — Norwegian Cruise Line (contemporary), Oceania (premium), and Regent Seven Seas (luxury) — with ~32 ships and a forward-order book of new builds that will lift capacity meaningfully through 2028. Post-COVID, the cruise industry has run at extraordinary load factors (>105% double occupancy) with strong onboard spending and price/yield growth. NCLH has consistently underearned versus Royal Caribbean (RCL) and Carnival (CCL) on a per-berth basis — a multi-year margin gap that Elliott is now explicitly targeting. Equity at ~$21 against a market cap of ~$9.6B and a heavily leveraged balance sheet (still working off COVID-era debt) — but EBITDA is rising and free cash flow is turning positive at scale.

The bear case starts with the balance sheet: NCLH carries materially more debt per berth than RCL and the maturity wall through 2026–2028 is real. Cruise demand is cyclical and tied to consumer discretionary income; a U.S. consumer rollover (auto delinquencies, credit card balances) would compress yields and onboard spend. New-ship deliveries pressure free cash flow until they start earning. A lingering Middle East/Red Sea routing risk has forced repositioning of itineraries and added fuel cost. Most importantly, even if Elliott is right that operational gaps can be closed, RCL's brand power and Royal/Celebrity scale advantages mean some discount may persist permanently — the multiple may compress, not just expand.

The bull case is the cleanest activist setup of 2026: Elliott does not file 13Ds with five board seats unless they have a credible operational and capital-allocation roadmap. Pagliuca's involvement is the tell — Bain Capital is one of the few sponsors that could write the equity check for a take-private, and his presence on the board signals that path is on the menu. Even absent a deal, simply closing half of the ~30% EV/EBITDA gap to RCL is a 40–60% equity move at constant EBITDA. CEO compensation is being reset to performance metrics, capex discipline is on the table, and Q1/Q2 2026 earnings will reset depressed expectations.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The March 27, 2026 cooperation agreement (filed as exhibit to 8-K) is the document to read carefully — it specifies director-replacement mechanics, standstill terms, voting commitments, and any committee assignments. The newbuild order book in the 10-K capital commitments footnote shows multi-year capex of several billion through 2028; Elliott's most likely first move is to renegotiate or stretch some of these deliveries to free up FCF. Margin-gap math: comparing NCLH's per-berth EBITDA against RCL berth-by-berth (disclosed in supplemental schedules) shows the specific dollar opportunity — typically described as "$300–$500M of run-rate EBITDA" by sell-side analysts, which at a 9–10x multiple is the entire upside thesis. Steve Pagliuca's separate Bain affiliation also means any take-private process would route through a special committee — watch for the formation of one in 8-K disclosures.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **Q1 2026 earnings — early May 2026** — first read on margin-gap reset under refreshed board.
2. **2026 Annual Meeting — June 2026** — formal director election; ratification of cooperation framework.
3. **Q3 2026 earnings — November 2026** — full quarter under new compensation framework; potential strategic-review language.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Business quality / moat | 6 |
| 2 | Management quality | 7 |
| 3 | Capital allocation discipline | 7 |
| 4 | Balance sheet strength | 5 |
| 5 | Free cash flow conversion | 7 |
| 6 | Reinvestment runway | 8 |
| 7 | Pricing power | 8 |
| 8 | Insider ownership / alignment | 8 |
| 9 | Margin of safety vs intrinsic value | 8 |
| 10 | Catalyst clarity / timing | 9 |
| **Total** | | **76/100** |

---

KVUE.name: Kenvue Inc.
KVUE.content:
# 263. KVUE — Kenvue Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE / ACTIVIST | 19.3% gross spread | Score: 72/100

### THE SETUP
Starboard's Jeff Smith joined the Kenvue board in March 2025; eight months later, Kimberly-Clark agreed to acquire the consumer-health spinoff for ~$48.7B EV ($3.50 cash + 0.14625 KMB shares per Kenvue share). With shareholders already approved (Jan 29, 2026) and HSR cleared, the 19% spread is essentially priced regulatory wait + KMB stock decline — a true arb dressed as a defensive consumer name.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Kenvue is the J&J consumer-health spinoff (Tylenol, Listerine, Band-Aid, Neutrogena, Aveeno) that came public in May 2023. The business is high-quality but post-spin operating execution disappointed: market share losses in skin care, Tylenol category overhang from acetaminophen-autism press cycles, and a sluggish allergy season repeatedly missed Street numbers. Starboard built its position in late 2024, secured a board seat in March 2025, and pushed for a strategic alternatives review that culminated in the November 3, 2025 announcement of a sale to Kimberly-Clark at an implied ~$21/share. Shares closed at $17.61 on report date, leaving a 19.3% gross spread.

The bear case is collar/regulatory: the deal is a mixed cash-stock structure, so KMB's own equity drift drives implied value. KMB stock fell after announcement on integration skepticism (32% pro-forma leverage, $50B asset combination, channel concentration), and any further KMB weakness shrinks the math directly. EU/UK regulators are still working through filings on a global consumer-health combination, and a remedy package or extended timeline could push close into late Q4 2026. Talc litigation overhang technically sits with J&J under indemnity but headline risk lingers, and a deal break — however unlikely — would expose Kenvue to a re-rate toward standalone $14-15.

The bull case is that all the gating items are de-risked: shareholders voted yes, US antitrust cleared, no horizontal product overlap, and Kimberly-Clark's $4B+ synergy story gives the buyer maximum incentive to close. Annualized 51% spread over an ~9 month window is wide for a strategic buyer with cleared US antitrust. Even if KMB stock drops another 10%, breakeven is still positive at current Kenvue prices.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The merger agreement contains a fixed exchange ratio (0.14625 KMB) with no collar — meaning Kenvue holders bear 100% of KMB equity drift between signing and close. A pairs trade (long KVUE / short 0.14625 KMB per share) isolates the pure spread/timing arb at roughly the same 19% gross. Also buried: the $1.65B termination fee payable by KMB in an antitrust break, which functions as a floor on Kenvue downside in the antitrust-fail scenario. J&J's talc indemnity (carved out at separation) means the litigation headlines that drag KVUE in retail flow have effectively zero economic impact at Kenvue level.

### CATALYSTS
- EU/UK antitrust clearance — expected Q2-Q3 2026
- KMB Q1 2026 earnings (Apr/May 2026) — drives stock portion value
- Deal close — guided H2 2026 (Q3 base case)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Factor | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 8/10 |
| Management quality | 6/10 |
| Balance sheet | 6/10 |
| Moat / pricing power | 8/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 5/10 |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 9/10 |
| Downside protection | 7/10 |
| Margin of safety | 7/10 |
| Simplicity / understandability | 9/10 |
| **Total** | **72/100** |

---

PFE.name: Pfizer Inc.
PFE.content:
# 81. PFE — Pfizer Inc.
## ACTIVIST — OPERATIONAL TURN | $1B Starboard Stake | Score: 52/100

### THE SETUP

In October 2024, Starboard Value (Jeff Smith) disclosed an approximately $1 billion stake in Pfizer — about 0.5% of the $156 billion market cap. The thesis was straightforward: post-COVID Pfizer had overpaid for Seagen ($43B), under-executed on R&D capital allocation, and let its stock price drift from $50+ in 2021 to under $30 by mid-2024. Starboard wanted CEO accountability, board refresh, and clearer portfolio rationalization.

Eighteen months later, the activist campaign has produced limited public wins. Starboard stopped short of nominating directors before the January 25, 2026 nomination deadline for the 2026 annual meeting — a signal that engagement is ongoing but not yet at the proxy-fight stage. The stock at $27.56 sits close to where Starboard built the position, and Pfizer has framed 2026 as a "transition year" with Seagen oncology ramp and loss-of-exclusivity (LOE) management.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

The Pfizer setup is harder than typical Starboard situations. Big pharma is structurally complex — Starboard's bread-and-butter (mid-cap industrial/tech with clear cost-side opportunities) does not translate cleanly to an enterprise where pipeline value, patent cliffs, and oncology launches dominate fundamentals. CEO Albert Bourla has hung onto the COVID-era credibility built by the Pfizer-BioNTech vaccine, even as the post-COVID hangover has been brutal: Comirnaty and Paxlovid revenues collapsed faster than Pfizer's capital deployment plans assumed.

The Seagen acquisition is the central question. At $43 billion, Pfizer paid roughly 28x sales for the antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) franchise — a price that requires near-flawless commercial execution to justify. Padcev (urothelial cancer) is the lead asset and has performed in line with deal model, but Tukysa, Adcetris, and the pipeline ADCs need to deliver to make the math work. If Seagen ramps as guided through 2027, Pfizer's earnings power could approach $4.50 EPS, which on a 12x multiple would imply $54 stock — roughly 2x current.

Starboard's leverage is the threat of escalation. Even without nominating directors in 2026, they can revisit at 2027 nominations if engagement stalls, and they can publicly criticize capital allocation. That ongoing pressure has likely contributed to Pfizer's renewed focus on cost discipline — the company announced incremental cost cuts in late 2025 — but the pace is slower than Starboard would prefer.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

The Haleon stake is a hidden asset. Pfizer holds roughly 32% of Haleon (consumer health spinout from GSK/Pfizer), worth approximately $13-14 billion at current Haleon prices. This is non-core and is being gradually monetized; full monetization would generate ~$2.30 per Pfizer share in cash. Add Seagen's standalone value at the deal price ($7.50/share equivalent), and the residual "stub" Pfizer pharma business trades at a low single-digit multiple of earnings.

The other invisible factor: obesity pipeline. Pfizer's oral GLP-1 program (danuglipron and successors) had setbacks in 2024-25, but the next-generation program could re-enter the conversation in 2026-27. Even modest success in obesity could re-rate the multiple meaningfully given the category's market cap implications.

### CATALYSTS

- 2026 annual meeting (Apr-May)
- Q1/Q2 2026 earnings (Seagen ramp data)
- Obesity pipeline updates (next-gen GLP-1)
- Potential Starboard escalation at 2027 nominations
- Haleon monetization steps
- Cost-cut execution

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- Simple business: No — big pharma is genuinely complex (pipelines, patents, deals)
- Honest management: Bourla has post-COVID credibility deficit
- Margin of safety: 6%+ dividend yield, sub-10x earnings on stub
- Reversion risk: Patent cliffs are real; pipeline must deliver
- Skip if: You can't model pharma pipelines or you need near-term re-rate

---

KDP.name: Keurig Dr Pepper Inc.
KDP.content:
# 82. KDP — Keurig Dr Pepper Inc.
## ACTIVIST — CAPITAL RETURN | Starboard engaged Oct 2025; JDE Peet integration | Score: 67/100

### THE SETUP
KDP trades at $26.53 (~$36B market cap) after a sharp drawdown following the JDE Peet's acquisition announcement, which the market initially read as a poorly-timed coffee doubling-down. Starboard Value's Jeff Smith went public October 18, 2025, and the February 2026 board nomination deadline passed without a slate — strongly suggesting amicable engagement on synergy disclosure, portfolio review, and possibly a coffee/cold-beverage separation. With sum-of-parts estimates above $35, the asymmetry is real if Starboard secures meaningful structural action.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Keurig Dr Pepper is the product of the 2018 Keurig + Dr Pepper Snapple merger — single-serve coffee (Keurig brewers and K-Cups), carbonated soft drinks (Dr Pepper, 7UP, Canada Dry, Sunkist, Squirt, A&W), premium waters (Core, Evian distribution), and energy/fuel (Bloom, C4 distribution, Ghost stake). The 2025 announced acquisition of JDE Peet's (Peet's Coffee, Jacobs, Douwe Egberts, L'OR) doubles down on coffee at a moment when single-serve volume has been pressured. CEO Tim Cofer (former Mondelez/Kellanova) inherited the deal and faces a complex integration plus an activist demanding that he articulate the synergy bridge.

The bear case: coffee is a scale game with structural headwinds — single-serve K-Cup volume has been declining as consumers shift to cold brew, drip, and away-from-home consumption returns. JDE Peet's is a heavily levered European business with its own portfolio issues and FX exposure. The deal adds debt at a moment when the cold-beverage business is also seeing competitive pressure (Celsius/Alani/Olipop in energy/functional). Standalone Dr Pepper has been the bright spot, having overtaken Pepsi as #2 cola in the US, but cracks could appear if pricing power fades. If Starboard's engagement fizzles into governance tweaks rather than structural action, the stock stays range-bound.

The bull case: Starboard's Costa Coffee/Whitbread playbook is directly applicable — push for a clean separation of coffee (KDP + JDE Peet) from cold beverages (Dr Pepper, waters, energy distribution). A two-company outcome could unlock 25-35% upside versus current prices using comp multiples (Coca-Cola, Monster on the cold side; Nestle/JDE peers on coffee). Even short of a break-up, $300M+ of articulated synergies on JDE Peet plus accelerated buyback would re-rate the stock. The fact that Starboard didn't file a slate by February 2026 deadline is bullish — it implies private commitments from the board.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 2024 10-K segment disclosures separate U.S. Refreshment Beverages, U.S. Coffee, International, and Latin America — making a clean break-up structurally simple, with U.S. Refreshment Beverages alone generating ~$3B of segment operating income on ~50% gross margins versus U.S. Coffee margins compressing. The proxy details Tim Cofer's compensation explicitly tied to "transformation actions" — language unusual for a soft-drinks CEO and consistent with strategic optionality. Buried in MD&A: management quietly disclosed the JDE Peet's deal expects "synergies primarily in procurement and SG&A" — Starboard's argument is that this number is materially understated, and the activist will hold management to publishing a higher target.

### CATALYSTS
- Q1 2026 earnings (April-May 2026) — first commentary on Starboard engagement
- 2H 2026 — JDE Peet's deal close + Capital Markets Day on integration
- 2026 annual meeting — possible board refresh / governance changes

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality (moat) | 7 |
| Management integrity | 7 |
| Capital allocation | 5 |
| Balance sheet strength | 6 |
| Free cash flow generation | 8 |
| Reinvestment runway | 7 |
| Insider alignment | 7 |
| Margin of safety | 7 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7 |
| Downside protection | 6 |
| **Total** | **67/100** |

---

ALGN.name: Align Technology, Inc.
ALGN.content:
# 83. ALGN — Align Technology, Inc.
## ACTIVIST — CAPITAL RETURN | $13.6B mkt cap @ $190 | Score: 68/100

### THE SETUP
Invisalign is a category-defining medical device with 22M+ patients treated, but the stock is down ~75% from its 2021 ~$750 peak after multiples compressed to ~12x earnings. Elliott Investment Management disclosed an activist position in September 2025 — pushing for cost discipline, capital return, and possibly a strategic review.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Align designs and manufactures Invisalign clear aligners and the iTero intraoral scanner / exocad CAD/CAM system that anchors the dentist workflow. FY2025 revenue was ~$4.0B (+0.9% YoY), with Q4'25 at $1.05B (+5.3% YoY). Reported FY2025 GAAP operating margin was ~13.6–13.8% (depressed by restructuring); 2026 guidance is ~18% (a ~400 bps recovery). The balance sheet is fortress: ~$1.0B cash, no debt as of Q3'25, with $831M of buyback capacity authorized into 2026 ($225M remaining under prior 2023 authorization). Elliott took a stake disclosed in September 2025; though demands have not been fully publicized, Elliott's playbook is well-established — operational efficiency, board refresh, accelerated buybacks, and option for strategic review. Q4 2025 growth was driven by EMEA, LATAM, and APAC, with North America stable, suggesting the international ramp Elliott would want to accelerate.

Bear case: clear-aligner growth has structurally decelerated as the post-COVID pull-forward exhausted and DSOs resist average selling price increases; competitors (SmileDirectClub bankrupt but Spark/Henry Schein, Dentsply) hold material share at lower price points; teen segment penetration is plateauing in mature markets. Multiple compression from 30x+ earnings to ~12x reflects the market's view that ALGN is now a ~mid-single-digit-grower medical device and should trade closer to a low-teens multiple. Elliott activism is not a guaranteed catalyst — Align's founder-led culture and a strong board could resist aggressive measures, and a strategic review of a ~$14B medical device with no obvious strategic acquirer is far from automatic.

Bull case: the asymmetry sits in the gap between current ~12x earnings and medical-device peer ~25x. Even partial multiple recovery to 18x with 2026 EBIT margin recovery to 18% and modest 4% revenue growth implies a $260–290 fair value (vs $190). Elliott pressure is the unlock — they have a track record of dragging boards into buyback programs, cost cuts, and strategic alternatives. Net cash balance sheet allows for accelerated repurchase or even leveraged recap. Insider buying signals confidence.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The proxy filing (DEF14A) referenced in early-2026 includes board refreshment language consistent with Elliott settlement template; that is Elliott's typical first win. The 22M cumulative-patient data point is undermarketed — it represents an enormous referral and case-history moat that DSO competitors cannot match in 2-3 years. Systems and Services (iTero, exocad) revenue grew faster than Clear Aligner in 2025 — a signal that the chairside workflow is becoming the primary lock-in (rather than aligner pricing). And operating-margin guidance of ~18% in 2026 implies ~$720M EBIT against an EV of ~$12.6B — meaningfully cheaper on a normalized basis than headlines suggest.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- 2026 annual meeting and any Elliott settlement / board refresh
- Q1/Q2 2026 earnings showing margin path to ~18% guidance
- Potential strategic review or large incremental buyback announcement

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Simple medical device franchise |
| Moat | 8 | 22M patients + iTero workflow lock-in |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | $1.0B cash, no debt |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | ~12x earnings vs 25x peers |
| Catalyst | 8 | Active Elliott engagement |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | $190 to $260+ if Elliott wins |
| Business Quality | 7 | Still 18% EBIT, low-teens ROIC |
| Executability | 9 | $13.6B cap, very liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Secular growth deceleration is real |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Discretionary dental procedure exposure |
| **TOTAL** | **76** | |

---

JHG.name: Janus Henderson Group plc
JHG.content:
# 176. JHG — Janus Henderson Group plc
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | $52 CASH, 99.7% VOTE PASSED APR 16 2026 | Score: 72/100

### THE SETUP
Janus Henderson shareholders approved the Trian / General Catalyst take-private at $52/share cash on April 16, 2026 with 99.7% approval (83% of shares outstanding). The bid was sweetened from $49 in March 2026 after Victory Capital's interloper $8.6B counter forced Trian to bump. Stock at $51.56 vs $52 deal = ~85 bps gross spread with vote behind it — the remaining risk is client consent / FCA approval through mid-2026 close.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Janus Henderson is a $7.9B-market-cap global asset manager (US/UK dual-listed; FCA-regulated) being taken private at $52 cash by Trian Fund Management (existing 20.6% holder) and General Catalyst. Initial deal announced December 22, 2025 at $49/share / $7.4B. After Victory Capital launched an $8.6B counter, Trian raised to $52 cash on March 24, 2026; Victory walked away. Shareholders approved the deal with 99.7% support on April 16, 2026 — representing 83% of total shares outstanding voting in favor. Expected close is mid-2026 (Q2/Q3), pending UK FCA, US SEC and client mandate consents. A $1/share quarterly dividend kicks in if the deal does not close by June 30, 2026 — a meaningful tick-rate sweetener.

The bear case is the spread itself — at ~85 bps gross with the vote already passed, the absolute return is small and the calendar-time arb is unattractive unless leveraged. Asset-management mandate transfers require client consent on a contract-by-contract basis — large institutional clients (sovereign wealth funds, pension consultants) sometimes use take-private events to re-bid mandates, creating real AUM-leakage risk. FCA review of UK regulated entities can drag. If a major client defects mid-deal, Trian could in theory re-trade, though the merger agreement structure typically locks the price.

The bull thesis is high deal certainty with a tick-rate floor: 99.7% approval is essentially a referendum vote, Trian is a 20.6% existing holder with deep PE pockets and "only actionable proposal" language explicitly stated, the $1/share quarterly dividend post-June 30 protects against close-date slippage, and the Victory walk-away removed the last interloper risk. For arb desks running modest leverage, the annualized spread (2.5-5% depending on close timing) is acceptable for an event with regulatory-only risk and no antitrust concern. Tail-risk upside from a re-emergent bidder is small but non-zero.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The $1/share quarterly dividend trigger if the deal does not close by June 30, 2026 is a structural protection most arb screens miss — it converts calendar-time risk into cash yield. Trian's "only actionable proposal" language in its March 24 press release is signal that the special committee's process is closed and Victory will not return. Client-consent risk (the actual remaining variable) cannot be measured from public filings — investors must monitor Form ADV amendments and read sub-advisory contract language to understand which mandates require affirmative consent vs. passive consent. UK Takeover Code adds procedural certainty (no last-minute material adverse change disputes typical of US deals).

### CATALYSTS
- Mid-2026 close (Q2/Q3 2026) — final regulatory + client consents
- June 30, 2026 dividend trigger if not closed
- FCA / SEC sign-off (rolling Q2 2026)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Item | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 7/10 |
| Management | 7/10 |
| Balance sheet | 7/10 |
| Moat | 6/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 5/10 |
| Free-cash conversion | 8/10 |
| Insider alignment | 8/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 9/10 |
| Margin of safety | 8/10 |
| Asymmetry | 7/10 |
| **Total** | **72/100** |

---

CVS.name: CVS Health Corporation
CVS.content:
# 86. CVS — CVS Health Corporation
## ACTIVIST — OPERATIONAL TURN | $77.30 / $99B mkt cap | Score: 76/100

### THE SETUP
Larry Robbins of Glenview Capital and three other directors joined the CVS board on November 18, 2024 with explicit mandates to fix Aetna margins, reform Caremark, and consider strategic alternatives — including a potential break-up of the integrated payor / PBM / pharmacy / Oak Street stack. Stock at $77.30 still trades materially below sum-of-parts estimates north of $100.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
CVS is a vertically integrated healthcare conglomerate: roughly 9,000 retail pharmacies, Caremark (the largest U.S. PBM), Aetna (a top-three managed-care plan), and Oak Street Health (value-based primary care). Revenues approach $400B and the company sits at the intersection of retail, insurance, and healthcare services. Screen catches it because Glenview Capital's Larry Robbins took an active stake disclosed Oct 14, 2024 followed by a Nov 2024 cooperation agreement that added 4 directors. Position is up roughly 25% YTD per the activist update; market cap is $99B; Glenview trimmed slightly Q1 2025 but remains active.

Bear case from the 10-K: Aetna's medical loss ratio surged in 2024 as the company materially under-priced 2024 individual exchange and Medicare Advantage benefits, requiring multiple guidance cuts. Star Ratings downgrades hit MA bonus revenue. The PBM model faces ongoing FTC scrutiny and legislative threats, with multiple states moving on reimbursement-floor laws. Retail pharmacy gross margins are structurally compressed by reimbursement pressure and front-store traffic decline. The integrated-model thesis — that owning all four pieces drives synergies — has yet to show in margins, and a sum-of-parts unwind would face enormous transaction tax leakage.

Bull case: the 2024 Aetna miss appears transitory — repricing of 2025 and 2026 plans plus exits from unprofitable counties should normalize MLR back toward target by H2 2026. With Robbins on the board, capital allocation discipline (dividend, buyback, leverage) and operational accountability are formalized. CEO David Joyner has held the role through this cycle, and the activist relationship is constructive rather than hostile. At $77 versus credible $100+ sum-of-parts (Aetna ~$40, Caremark ~$30, Pharmacy ~$25, Oak Street ~$10), there is meaningful upside even without a break-up — operational normalization alone could re-rate the stock to $90+. Free cash flow remains substantial and supports buybacks.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Nov 18, 2024 cooperation agreement added Robbins, Larry Norwalk, Doug Shulman, and Guy Sansone — a slate that includes deep healthcare services, IRS / regulatory, and turnaround expertise specifically calibrated to CVS's three biggest 2024 problems (Aetna pricing, PBM regulation, Oak Street integration). The 10-K's MA segment disclosures show 2024 underwriting losses concentrated in specific plan years that are now being repriced — the 2026 bid filings (already submitted to CMS) are the leading indicator that Wall Street has not yet fully modeled. The hidden lever is Caremark's specialty pharmacy growth, which is partially offsetting front-store declines but is buried in segment reporting.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 and Q2 2026 earnings — Aetna MLR trajectory and 2026 MA bid commentary
- 2026 medical loss ratio data through year
- Potential break-up announcement or accelerated buyback authorization

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Complex, multi-segment |
| Moat | 7 | Caremark and pharmacy network |
| Balance Sheet | 5 | Levered, ~$60B+ net debt |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | $77 vs $100+ SOTP |
| Catalyst | 8 | MLR normalization 2026 |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | 30% up, 15% down |
| Business Quality | 6 | Mixed; Aetna and Caremark good |
| Executability | 10 | $99B mega-cap, unlimited liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Aetna miss could persist |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Healthcare demand inelastic |
| **TOTAL** | **71** | |

---

COO.name: The Cooper Companies, Inc.
COO.content:
# 87. COO — The Cooper Companies, Inc.
## ACTIVIST — SALE OR BREAKUP | Browning West 4% stake / cooperation agreement | Score: 74/100

### THE SETUP
Browning West, a $500M+ activist holder, secured a board seat (Walter Rosebrough, ex-Steris CEO) on January 3, 2026 in exchange for standing down on a full proxy fight. The agreement explicitly contemplates a strategic review of CooperSurgical, the contact lens conglomerate's underperforming women's health arm.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Cooper Companies is a $13.7B medical device holding company with two distinct businesses: CooperVision (one of the global "big four" contact lens makers competing with J&J Acuvue, Alcon, and Bausch + Lomb) and CooperSurgical (fertility, IVF consumables, OB/GYN devices, paragard IUDs). The two divisions share virtually no commercial logic, customer base, or technology platform — they coexist purely because the same management team built the conglomerate via decades of bolt-on M&A. At $70/share, the stock has materially underperformed contact lens peers, which trade at premium high-teens EBITDA multiples reflecting the recurring-revenue, oligopoly economics of soft contact lenses.

The bear case starts with execution. CooperSurgical has missed numbers repeatedly, integrating Cook Medical's reproductive health assets and Generate Life Sciences proved harder than guided. Contact lens unit growth has decelerated as myopia management (MiSight) revenue normalizes after a torrid Covid-era ramp. A breakup is taxable, complicated by shared corporate overhead, and may not be embraced by long-tenured CEO Al White. If management drags its feet on the strategic review, the stock could languish for another twelve months. Margins remain pressured by FX and a costly Puerto Rico/UK manufacturing footprint. Browning West only secured one board seat — not control.

The bull case is straightforward sum-of-the-parts. CooperVision standalone, generating roughly two-thirds of EBITDA at high-20s margins, would plausibly trade at 18-20x EBITDA as a pure-play oligopoly franchise. CooperSurgical, even at a modest mid-teens multiple, fetches a meaningful price from a strategic (Hologic, Boston Scientific) or PE buyer flush with healthcare-services dry powder. Browning West's intrinsic value estimate of $100+ implies roughly 40% upside. The cooperation agreement keeps activist pressure on — and Walter Rosebrough has actual breakup credentials from his Steris tenure. A CooperSurgical sale could fund a transformative buyback at a depressed multiple.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K segment disclosure understates CooperVision's true unit economics because corporate overhead, IT, and ERP costs are allocated by revenue rather than by complexity — CooperSurgical, with its dozens of acquired ERP systems and post-merger integration spend, is the disproportionate consumer. Standalone, CooperVision's operating margin is likely 200-300bp higher than reported. Also overlooked: the silicone hydrogel patent estate around MyDay and Biofinity, several of which extend into the 2030s, and CooperVision's #1 share position in toric lenses (the highest-margin sub-segment). Cooper is also one of the few contact lens makers with full vertical integration in MiSight myopia management, a rapidly growing pediatric category.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- CooperSurgical strategic review outcome (H1 calendar 2026, possibly tied to Q2 fiscal earnings)
- Q2/Q3 fiscal 2026 earnings (June and September 2026) — first full quarters with Rosebrough on board
- Potential CooperSurgical sale announcement or formal spin-off plan; additional board changes if review disappoints

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Two clean businesses, both well-documented |
| Moat | 8 | Contact lens oligopoly; toric and MiSight share leadership |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | ~$2.5B net debt; manageable but not pristine |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | $70 vs Browning West $100+ intrinsic |
| Catalyst | 8 | Active strategic review with new director |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | ~40% upside vs ~15% downside to trough multiple |
| Business Quality | 8 | High-20s EBITDA margins, recurring lens revenue |
| Executability | 9 | $13.7B cap, deeply liquid name |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Slow review + management resistance plausible |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Vision care largely insulated from cycles |
| **TOTAL** | **76** | |

---

TFX.name: Teleflex Incorporated
TFX.content:
# 88. TFX — Teleflex Incorporated

## ACTIVIST — SALE OR BREAKUP | Irenic public letter Mar 27, 2026 | Score: 80/100

---

### THE SETUP

Teleflex is a mid-cap medical device company (~$5.8B market cap, $131.58 spot) with a portfolio spanning vascular access, interventional, anesthesia, surgical, and OEM products. The company has been a chronic underperformer relative to medtech peers — operating margins, organic growth, and total shareholder return all lag the S&P Healthcare index over 3-, 5-, and 10-year windows. Management has spent the last 18 months pursuing a self-help plan: announcing divestitures of Acute Care, Interventional Urology, and OEM (collectively expected to generate ~$1.8B post-tax), authorizing a $1B buyback, and preaching a "focus on core" standalone strategy.

Irenic Capital Management, the activist arm spun out of Elliott alumni, owns ~2% and isn't buying the standalone story. On March 19, 2026 Irenic met privately with the Teleflex board and demanded an announcement of strategic alternatives within a week. When that deadline passed without action, Irenic went public on March 27, 2026 with a letter accusing the board of refusing to engage with credible third-party acquisition interest. Irenic is calling for: a Strategic Review sub-committee staffed with new independent directors (excluding current Chair Dr. Klasko), engagement with potential acquirers, a new Chair, hiring of independent advisors, and serious consideration of a sale.

The activist alleges multiple unsolicited approaches have been rebuffed by management. Reported acquirers include both PE shops and strategic medtech buyers — names floated in trade press include Stryker, Becton Dickinson, and several large-cap PE platforms.

### ANALYST SUMMARY (3p)

The valuation gap is the core thesis. TFX trades at ~10x forward EBITDA — a discount to medtech peers (Stryker ~18x, BDX ~12x, Becton ~13x) reflecting its growth and margin underperformance. Irenic argues a take-out value of $180–200 (37–52% above spot) is achievable because (a) a strategic acquirer can extract significant cost synergies (Teleflex has corporate overhead a focused buyer doesn't need), (b) the divestitures already underway clean up the portfolio for a buyer, and (c) PE bidders can finance attractively given current credit conditions and use cost cuts plus modest revenue resilience to underwrite mid-teens IRRs.

Management's standalone case rests on the post-divestiture Teleflex being a more focused, higher-growth, higher-margin company that re-rates organically. The problem is execution risk and time — investors have been told this story for years. Buybacks help but don't address the structural multiple discount.

The activist setup creates a forcing function. Irenic's public letter raises governance heat going into proxy season; even a modest activist threat typically gets boards to engage advisors and run a process. The board now faces a clear choice: open a sale process and likely deliver $180+ to shareholders, or defend standalone and risk a proxy fight in 2026 with a credible alternative ($180–200 cash takeout) on the table. Most boards — particularly at mid-cap medtech companies with long-tenured directors — choose to engage rather than fight.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

A standard quant screen sees a slow-growth, margin-pressured medtech with mediocre returns on capital. It does not see (a) the activist forcing function and the public board pressure, (b) the multiple unsolicited acquisition approaches reportedly already received, (c) the divestiture proceeds that simplify any acquirer's diligence, or (d) the asymmetric outcome distribution where the standalone case still works modestly while the sale case delivers a near-term 40%+ premium. The Irenic campaign explicitly cites private acquirer interest — a fact that is invisible to screens but central to the trade.

### CATALYSTS

- Sale process announcement (or formal rejection — equally market-moving)
- 2026 annual meeting and potential Irenic board nominations
- Q1/Q2 2026 earnings — execution proof points or further activist ammunition
- Closing of Acute Care, Interventional Urology, OEM divestitures (H2 2026)
- $1B buyback execution pace
- Any 13D from an additional activist or strategic disclosure

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- **Business quality**: Average. Vascular access franchises (UroLift legacy aside) have decent moats and recurring procedural volume, but Teleflex's portfolio mix has chronically underperformed pure-play peers. Post-divestiture mix is better.
- **Management**: Credibility issue. Multiple years of guidance misses. CEO Liam Kelly executing the divestiture plan, but Irenic argues management is anchored to standalone.
- **Balance sheet**: Manageable leverage; divestiture proceeds + buyback authorize material capital return. Investment-grade.
- **Capital allocation**: $1B buyback announced — credible signal but Irenic argues it's a defensive move to placate the activist rather than accept the better deal of a sale.
- **Margin of safety**: Spot $131 vs Irenic-floated take-out range $180–200. Even a midpoint $185 deal = 40%+ premium. Standalone bear case probably has $115–120 floor given divestiture asset value and buyback support.
- **Risks**: Board successfully resists sale process; takeout valuation lower than $180 if approached; medtech multiples compress sector-wide; activist gives up.

This is a high-probability special-situation trade with a clear activist-driven forcing function and credible third-party interest. The mid-80 score reflects strong setup mechanics weighed against the genuine possibility that the board successfully runs out the clock or that an eventual deal prices below the $180 floor of Irenic's stated range. Expected timeline 6–12 months to a definitive outcome.

---
TFX.whatScreenCantSee: A standard quant screen sees a slow-growth, margin-pressured medtech with mediocre returns on capital. It does not see (a) the activist forcing function and the public board pressure, (b) the multiple unsolicited acquisition approaches reportedly already received, (c) the divestiture proceeds that simplify any acquirer's diligence, or (d) the asymmetric outcome distribution where the standalone case still works modestly while the sale case delivers a near-term 40%+ premium. The Irenic campaign explicitly cites private acquirer interest — a fact that is invisible to screens but central to the trade.

SNAP.name: Snap Inc.
SNAP.content:
# 89. SNAP — Snap Inc.
## ACTIVIST | Irenic Capital "Snap Back to Reality" Mar 31 2026 | Score: 58/100

### THE SETUP
On March 31, 2026, activist Irenic Capital Management published an open letter titled "Snap Back to Reality" outlining a six-step turnaround plan for Snap Inc. and disclosing an approximately 2.5% Class A stake. The campaign argues that Snap, currently trading near $6 with a market cap of ~$10.2B, is structurally undervalued because of three decisions: (1) sustained spending on Spectacles AR hardware that has consumed roughly $3.5B of cumulative investment with ongoing burn of approximately $500M annually and no clear monetization path; (2) bloated headcount that exceeds what the business needs at its current scale; and (3) inadequate use of AI-driven automation in operations and content moderation. Irenic's plan calls for shutting down or spinning off Spectacles, a 21% workforce reduction, aggressive AI-driven cost reset, granting Class A shareholders one vote per share (eliminating the supervoting structure that gives founder/CEO Evan Spiegel near-complete voting control), and aggressive capital return. The activist's price target is $26 per share — roughly 7x the current price. SNAP shares jumped approximately 14% on the day of the letter to ~$6, and management has not yet formally responded.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The Irenic case rests on a real fact pattern but runs into a structural wall. The fact pattern: cumulative Spectacles investment has likely exceeded the entire current market cap, and continuing $500M annual burn against a non-monetized product line is genuinely difficult to defend. The remaining business — Snapchat's social platform with 800M+ MAUs, Lens Studio AR developer ecosystem, Snapchat+ premium subscription tier, and an advertising business — would generate dramatically better margins if Spectacles were wound down and headcount were rationalized. Irenic's $26 target implicitly assumes that even a partial Spectacles wind-down combined with cost reset could double EBITDA, which is mathematically defensible. The structural wall: Snap's dual-class structure gives Evan Spiegel and Bobby Murphy effectively complete voting control. Without their cooperation, no activist proposal — Spectacles wind-down, headcount cuts, capital return, or one-share-one-vote — can be forced. Spectacles has been Spiegel's signature long-term bet, making him the least likely insider to voluntarily kill it. The realistic outcomes are therefore: (a) Spiegel partially capitulates to public pressure and quietly trims Spectacles spend without fully exiting (possible — improves the bear/base case modestly); (b) management announces a cost reset and partial buyback to defuse the campaign (more likely — gets stock to $8–10 zone); or (c) campaign fizzles and stock retraces toward $5 (also possible). The asymmetric upside Irenic articulates is real on paper but requires governance change that the dual-class structure makes very difficult.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three things. First, dual-class structure: standard governance and activist screens that flag "activist target" frequently miss the binary nature of supervoting control — at Snap, Spiegel can simply say no, full stop. Second, the cumulative Spectacles investment exceeds reported R&D in any single year because much of it sits across multiple expense lines (R&D, S&M, G&A, capitalized software) — Irenic's $3.5B figure is sourced from cumulative segment-level disclosure that is not visible on standard fundamental screens. Third, advertising-business unit economics: Snap's ad business has improved meaningfully on direct-response ROAS in 2025–2026 but is masked at the consolidated level by Spectacles burn — a partial wind-down would re-rate the visible operating margin substantially even without core revenue growth.

### CATALYSTS
- Snap management formal response to Irenic letter
- Q1 2026 earnings (cost commentary will be the key signal)
- Any Spectacles wind-down, restructuring, or spin announcement
- Workforce reduction announcement
- Capital return / buyback authorization expansion
- Possible strategic interest given depressed valuation

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: Yes — social/messaging app with advertising and subscription monetization
- Durable moat: Moderate — Gen Z brand position, AR Lens Studio ecosystem, but real competition from Meta, TikTok, BeReal cycle
- Honest, capable management: Question mark — Spiegel's vision-driven Spectacles spending is the activist's central complaint
- Bought at fair price: Maybe — at $6 the core ad business is cheap, but governance limits the path to value realization
- Margin of safety: Limited — dual-class structure means activist outcome is at the founder's discretion
- Key risks: Spiegel rejects all activist proposals, Spectacles burn continues, ad-market competition from Meta/TikTok intensifies, governance reform is the hardest activist ask

---

Sources: CNBC (Mar 31 2026), Bloomberg activist stake reporting, FinancialContent market coverage. Score reflects real fundamental upside heavily discounted by dual-class governance overhang.

---

CSGP.name: CoStar Group, Inc.
CSGP.content:
# 90. CSGP — CoStar Group, Inc.
## ACTIVIST — OPERATIONAL TURN | $39.72 / $16.8B mkt cap | Score: 71/100

### THE SETUP
CoStar trades at $39.72, less than half its 2024 peak near $90, after Homes.com's roughly $3B cumulative spend has produced only ~$80M of annual revenue. D.E. Shaw is running a public proxy campaign for majority board control at the June 2026 annual meeting, and the company has already capitulated on three independent directors, a Capital Allocation Committee, and a $300M cut to Homes.com spend in 2026.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
CoStar is the dominant subscription data and analytics franchise for U.S. commercial real estate (CoStar Suite, LoopNet, Apartments.com), with high-90s gross retention and SaaS-like economics in its core. Screen catches it because the stock collapsed roughly 55% from peak while the underlying CRE data business kept growing high-teens. Market cap is $16.8B, EV is similar net of cash and debt, and the screen flags the activist filing on Feb 4, 2026 by D.E. Shaw with a ~1.5% economic stake plus an open letter and presentation. Third Point also engaged but exited April 10, 2026 saying CoStar had moved enough.

Bear case from filings: Homes.com is a multi-year, multi-billion-dollar capital allocation experiment competing with Zillow and Realtor.com that is still cash-burning at scale, and CEO Andy Florance has resisted hard limits. Core CRE growth has decelerated, the Matterport acquisition diluted margins, and SG&A has ballooned. If Homes.com cannot reach contribution-margin breakeven, it is a permanent drag and management credibility takes another hit. Founder-CEO control and a staggered defense playbook can blunt the activist outcome.

Bull case: D.E. Shaw is anchoring on $300M+ of immediate cost takeout plus a credible monetization or wind-down path on Homes.com; even modest discipline plus mid-teens core CRE growth supports a re-rating. The board added two D.E. Shaw / Third Point names plus a third independent, named a new chair, and stood up a Capital Allocation Committee — all classic pre-vote concessions. If June vote pressure forces CEO transition or a take-private discussion, the asymmetry from $40 is meaningful given peers trade at 8–12x revenue versus CSGP near 4x.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The activist letter and presentation lay out segment-level Homes.com unit economics that the 10-K rolls up into "Residential" — D.E. Shaw's deck pegs cumulative Homes.com investment at over $3B against ~$80M revenue, a roughly 38x lifetime spend-to-ARR ratio that no SaaS investor would tolerate. Filings also reveal Florance's voting control via concentrated insider shares is not majority, meaning a real proxy outcome is mathematically possible. The Capital Allocation Committee charter is the under-the-radar concession: it institutionalizes a board check on Homes.com burn that did not exist before February 2026.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- June 2026 annual meeting board vote on D.E. Shaw slate
- Q1 and Q2 2026 earnings — Homes.com cost discipline metrics and core CRE growth
- Possible CEO transition (Andy Florance under pressure) or take-private discussion

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | CRE subscription data, easy |
| Moat | 9 | Dominant CoStar Suite |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Net cash position |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | ~4x revenue vs SaaS peers 8–12x |
| Catalyst | 8 | June 2026 vote + Homes.com cuts |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | $40 base vs $60–80 reasonable |
| Business Quality | 7 | Core ROIC strong, Homes.com drag |
| Executability | 9 | $16.8B mega-cap, deep liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Homes.com burn could continue |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Subscription, but CRE-cycle exposed |
| **TOTAL** | **75** | |

---

SGRY.name: Surgery Partners, Inc.
SGRY.content:
# 92. SGRY — Surgery Partners, Inc.
## ACTIVIST | Ortelius Advisors public letter, $14 vs $25.75 prior bid | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
Surgery Partners is one of the largest US operators of ambulatory surgery centers (ASCs), with a network of physician-partnered facilities focused on outpatient surgical procedures. The thesis on the industry is straightforward and well-understood: procedures are migrating out of the inpatient hospital setting into lower-cost, higher-margin ASCs, and CMS reimbursement policy continues to broaden the list of procedures eligible for the ASC site-of-service. Despite that tailwind, SGRY stock has been a disaster — down roughly 67% over five years versus HCA up 276 percentage points and Tenet (THC) up 413 percentage points over the same period. On March 10, 2026, activist Ortelius Advisors published an open letter to shareholders highlighting the underperformance and demanding asset sales, debt reduction, board and management changes, and a strategic refocus on the company's highest-margin facilities. Ortelius owns roughly 2% of the float — a small stake but a public-letter campaign rather than a quiet engagement. The setup is sharpened by the fact that Bain Capital previously made a $25.75 cash buyout offer that was withdrawn — meaning a sophisticated PE buyer once put a price on the entire business that is roughly 84% above today's $14 quote.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The activist case rests on four pillars. First, persistent underperformance versus same-industry peers despite better secular tailwinds — SGRY operates exactly the businesses (ASCs) the market loves elsewhere, suggesting the gap is operational and capital-structure related, not end-market related. Second, the balance sheet: leverage has been the key drag, and an asset-sale program targeting underperforming centers could de-lever materially while simultaneously improving consolidated margins by cutting the long tail of low-yield facilities. Third, the prior Bain Capital offer at $25.75 establishes a real-world buyer-side anchor — that price was set with full data-room access and represents a sophisticated PE shop's view of intrinsic value. The fact that the bid was withdrawn rather than topped suggests it may not have been bid up adequately, and the door for a return engagement (or another PE bidder) is open. Fourth, board-level changes are already happening — Lloyd Dean (former CEO of CommonSpirit Health) was appointed to the board, lending operating credibility to any strategic review. The asymmetry is meaningful: at $14, downside on continued operating drift is real but limited by the underlying ASC asset value; upside on either a renewed Bain bid, a different PE/strategic transaction, or a successful operational turnaround targets the prior $25.75 offer or higher.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three things. First, leverage screens make SGRY look statistically expensive — high net debt / EBITDA ratios show up as a red flag — but the asset-sale plan is specifically designed to take down debt with proceeds from selling underperforming centers, which would simultaneously improve debt ratios and consolidated margins (a "double benefit" that DCFs miss). Second, the ASC industry has consolidation tailwinds beyond just procedure migration — physician partners aging out, hospitals divesting outpatient assets, and PE buyers competing for scaled platforms. SGRY is one of the few publicly-listed scaled operators, which makes it strategically scarce. Third, the prior Bain bid establishes that informed money saw real value here recently — that information doesn't show up on any quant screen but materially raises the floor on the activist outcome.

### CATALYSTS
- 2026 annual meeting and any board-level vote on activist proposals
- Possible re-emergence of Bain Capital or competing PE bid
- Asset sale announcements (de-leveraging + margin mix improvement)
- Q1/Q2 2026 earnings prints showing operational stabilization
- Strategic review announcement
- Additional board changes following Lloyd Dean appointment

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: Yes — outpatient surgical centers, fee-for-service plus partnership model
- Durable moat: Moderate — local market scale, physician relationships, payer contracts; not impregnable
- Honest, capable management: Question mark — 5-year underperformance is the activist's central claim; new board members improving the picture
- Bought at fair price: Yes — at $14 vs prior $25.75 PE bid and below sum-of-parts of facility-level NAV
- Margin of safety: Moderate — leverage is the key risk, but asset-sale path provides a route to de-lever
- Key risks: Leverage-driven covenant or refinancing pressure, CMS reimbursement changes, physician-partner attrition, activist may settle for less than full value

---

Sources: Ortelius March 10 2026 open letter (Yahoo Finance, StockTitan), SimplyWall.st valuation note. Score reflects strong activist asymmetry but real leverage and execution risk.

---

RGR.name: Sturm, Ruger & Company, Inc.
RGR.content:
# 93. RGR — Sturm, Ruger & Company, Inc.
## ACTIVIST / TENDER OFFER | $44.80 Beretta Tender / Pill in Place | Score: 78/100
### THE SETUP
Sturm, Ruger & Company is the second-largest US firearms manufacturer (behind Smith & Wesson Brands), with a roughly $670M market cap and a fortress balance sheet (no debt, meaningful net cash). On February 24, 2026, Beretta Holding S.A. — the privately-held Italian firearms group with a 9.95% stake in Ruger — filed a Schedule 13D nominating four directors for the 2026 annual meeting and signaling intent to push for capital allocation and operational change. On March 25, 2026, Beretta escalated by sending the Ruger board a letter proposing an all-cash partial tender offer at $44.80/share for up to 20.05% of outstanding shares, which would lift Beretta's economic stake to approximately 30%. The offer represents a ~20% premium to the 60-day VWAP. Ruger's poison pill currently blocks the tender; the board has acknowledged receipt but rejected pill exemption. Active proxy contest is now in motion into the 2026 AGM.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
This is one of the cleaner activist setups in the US small-cap universe heading into mid-2026. Beretta is a credible strategic acquirer — privately held, well-capitalized, with deep firearms operating expertise — not a financial activist looking for a quick flip. The $44.80 partial tender at a 20% VWAP premium is a hard floor under the equity for as long as the offer remains live, and the underlying business has real cyclical upside: firearms demand has been bottoming through 2024-2025 as the post-COVID surge unwound, gross margins have compressed to multi-year lows, and the firearm cycle is historically strongly correlated with election years. With 2026 being a presidential election cycle, normalized demand recovery is a near-term reasonable expectation regardless of the activist outcome.

The board's rejection of pill exemption is what makes the setup interesting rather than a foregone conclusion. Ruger's board is not aggressively shareholder-friendly — historically conservative governance, founder-family legacy in management ranks, and a pill that has not been put to a shareholder vote. The pill itself is the activist's primary lever: a public proxy fight to remove or amend the pill, supported by ISS/Glass Lewis, is the playbook. ISS has historically been hostile to pills that block all-cash premium offers from credible strategics, and Beretta's nominee slate is professional rather than confrontational, increasing the probability of governance support.

The realistic outcome distribution: (1) ~35% probability — negotiated settlement with Beretta gaining 1-2 board seats and a partial tender clearing at or above $44.80; (2) ~25% — full proxy victory for Beretta and tender proceeds; (3) ~25% — board prevails but is forced into a defensive capital return (special dividend or large buyback) to placate dissidents; (4) ~15% — status quo. In three of four scenarios the equity re-rates meaningfully above current $42.21, with the bear case being a slow grind back toward $38-40 as the activist premium drains. Capital return is essentially guaranteed in some form — the only question is whether you're paid with a tender, a dividend, or accumulated buyback.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen sees a flat-earnings firearms company with a decent dividend yield and goes no further. What's invisible: (1) the activist tender at $44.80 is a hard floor with explicit cash backing from a strategic, not a hedge fund; (2) the firearms cycle is historically election-correlated and 2026 is a presidential year — normalized demand recovery is a baseline 2H 2026 catalyst irrespective of activism; (3) Ruger's no-debt balance sheet means even a defensive special dividend of $5-7/share is easily fundable from cash; (4) ISS/Glass Lewis precedent strongly favors removing pills that block all-cash premium offers from strategic buyers, which materially raises the probability of activist victory at the AGM vote.

### CATALYSTS
- **May 2026 — Q1 earnings and ISS recommendation:** Tape on margin trajectory and proxy advisor stance.
- **June/July 2026 — 2026 Annual Meeting board vote:** Direct activist outcome.
- **2H 2026 — tender commencement or defensive capital return:** Either Beretta tender clears or Ruger board responds with special dividend/buyback.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 7/10 |
| Management trust | 6/10 |
| Balance sheet | 10/10 |
| Moat | 7/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 5/10 |
| Valuation | 8/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 9/10 |
| Downside protection | 8/10 |
| Insider alignment | 6/10 |
| Simplicity | 8/10 |
| **Total** | **74/100** |

---

RPAY.name: Repay Holdings Corporation
RPAY.content:
# 168. RPAY — Repay Holdings Corporation
## ACTIVIST | $4.11 vs $4.80 take-private bid; ~$359M mkt cap | Score: 60/100
### THE SETUP
Repay Holdings is a B2B and consumer payments processor sitting at the intersection of three live storylines as of mid-April 2026. (1) On March 30, 2026, RPAY announced an agreement to acquire KUBRA — a Canadian utility/government bill-presentment provider — for approximately $372M, financed with cash on hand and a new $500M term loan. (2) On April 9, 2026, Veradace Partners — an 8.4% holder per its SC 13D — issued an open letter to the independent board calling for the KUBRA deal to be terminated, citing "size, valuation and leverage" of the transaction and asserting that surveyed major holders unanimously oppose it; Veradace also demanded two shareholder representatives on the board. (3) RPAY had previously disclosed an unsolicited $4.80/share take-private proposal (Forager Capital), which the board has not accepted. RPAY's April 13, 2026 statement reiterated commitment to KUBRA, calling it governed by a definitive purchase agreement. With the stock at $4.11 versus the $4.80 standing bid, the gross spread is ~17% and the situation has multiple paths to resolution within 2026.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Three nested decisions sit on top of one B2B payments microcap. First, the operating business: RPAY's core consumer-payments verticals (auto loan, receivables management, mortgage servicing) and B2B AP automation generate recurring transaction revenue with reasonable but not spectacular growth. The stock has de-rated meaningfully over the past two years on competitive pressure and missed guides, which is precisely what attracted both an activist and a financial buyer. Second, the KUBRA deal: at ~$372M against a ~$359M market cap, this is a larger-than-the-buyer acquisition that levers the balance sheet to expand into utility/government bill pay — strategically defensible, but at a price and leverage profile that current holders find unattractive given RPAY's own depressed multiple. Third, the take-out: a $4.80 unsolicited proposal exists but has not been embraced; it implies ~$420M equity and may not survive the activist standoff intact.

The bull case is path-independent: even if KUBRA closes and the activist loses, the combined business will still trade at a depressed multiple that should attract another bid in 2026-2027; if KUBRA is terminated, the freed-up balance sheet capacity supports either a re-engaged sale process or aggressive buybacks at a value-accretive multiple; if the $4.80 bid (or higher) prevails, holders harvest 17%+ from current levels in months. The bear case is also real: the board can plough through, the proxy battle drags on without a deal, KUBRA integration distracts, payments competition erodes the legacy book, and the stock drifts back into the $3-handle.

The investable angle is the activist's leverage. Veradace's 8.4% stake, paired with what it represents as broad institutional opposition, is enough to (a) put a nomination threat on the table, (b) raise the cost of executing KUBRA without a re-cut, and (c) keep alive the prospect of a re-emergent or higher take-private bid. The asymmetry is not extreme — downside to the high-$3s if everything goes wrong is real — but the multi-path optionality and the existing standing offer make the risk-reward defensible at $4.11. Position-sizing should reflect the genuine possibility that the board simply outlasts the activist.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen sees a sub-$5 payments microcap with declining profitability and elevated leverage from the pending acquisition. What it cannot see: (1) the activist proxy mechanics — Veradace has filed an SC 13D and put board demands in writing, which materially constrains directors; (2) the standing $4.80 take-private floor that has not been formally rejected; (3) the strategic logic (and the criticism) of KUBRA — a defensible expansion into utility/government billing at what holders argue is a premium price; (4) the dual-track optionality where either deal cancellation or a higher buyout improves shareholder outcomes; (5) the high reflexivity between price and bid behavior — every drop below $4 makes a take-out at $4.80 look stronger and vice versa.

### CATALYSTS
- **April-June 2026: Resolution of KUBRA deal** — close, terminate, or re-cut; each path repositions the equity story.
- **Q2 2026 earnings (August 2026)** — first read on the operating business with the activist standoff in the open.
- **Veradace proxy filing (potentially summer 2026)** if the deal is not abandoned and board seats are not granted.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality / moat | 5/10 |
| Management quality | 5/10 |
| Capital allocation | 4/10 |
| Balance sheet strength | 5/10 |
| Earnings predictability | 5/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| Insider alignment | 6/10 |
| Margin of safety at price | 7/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 9/10 |
| Downside protection | 8/10 |
| **Total** | **60/100** |

---

Three nested decisions sit on top of one B2B payments microcap. First, the operating business: RPAY's core consumer-payments verticals (auto loan, receivables management, mortgage servicing) and B2B AP automation generate recurring transaction revenue with reasonable but not spectacular growth. The stock has de-rated meaningfully over the past two years on competitive pressure and missed guides, which is precisely what attracted both an activist and a financial buyer. Second, the KUBRA deal: at ~$372M against a ~$359M market cap, this is a larger-than-the-buyer acquisition that levers the balance sheet to expand into utility/government bill pay — strategically defensible, but at a price and leverage profile that current holders find unattractive given RPAY's own depressed multiple. Third, the take-out: a $4.80 unsolicited proposal exists but has not been embraced; it implies ~$420M equity and may not survive the activist standoff intact.

OPRT.name: Oportun Financial Corporation
OPRT.content:
# 95. OPRT — Oportun Financial Corporation
## ACTIVIST | Radoff/Schechter 9.9% — CEO Ousted | Score: 62/100

### THE SETUP
Subprime consumer lender trading at $5.88 vs. $20+ pre-2024 levels after credit-cycle blowout. Activist Bradley Radoff and Joshua Schechter (9.9% stake) forced CEO Raul Vazquez out by April 3, 2026, with interim leadership in place and an external CEO search underway. Re-rating optionality on credit normalization plus $114M+ funding-cost reduction target.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Oportun is a Hispanic-focused subprime installment lender that grew aggressively into credit cards and personal loans before the 2022-2024 credit cycle exposed underwriting weakness. Charge-offs spiked, the stock collapsed from $20+ to under $3 at trough, and the company pivoted to defensive shrinkage. Radoff Family Investments and Schechter — who previously won activist campaigns at Support.com and Carbonite — filed in 2024 demanding management change, board refresh, and a strategic review. They extended the campaign in March 2026 with a fresh open letter forcing Vazquez to step down by April 3, 2026.

The bear case is structural: subprime lending is brutally cyclical, the company has missed guidance repeatedly, and even with cost cuts the path to durable profitability requires both credit normalization AND funding-cost reduction below 6%. Tangible book has been impaired by reserve builds, and the share count has crept higher from equity comp. Many subprime lenders trade at perpetual discounts because investors don't trust the marks. If 2026 unemployment ticks up, fresh charge-offs could wipe out the cost-savings story before it even shows up in EPS.

The bull case is a clean catalyst stack: new external CEO (likely a credit-discipline operator, not a growth CEO) re-rates the multiple; sub-6% ABS issuance unlocks $114M+ of annualized funding savings dropping straight to EPS; activist accountability ensures cost cuts actually happen; 2026 annual meeting forces additional board cleanout. At ~$262M market cap on a $3B+ loan book, even modest operating leverage produces 2-3x equity returns. The activists have skin in the game and a track record.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Screens flag OPRT as a busted ex-fintech with negative trailing earnings — they miss the activist-mandated CEO transition, the explicit $114M+ funding savings target tied to ABS spread compression, and the optical effect of switching to GAAP-only reporting (which Radoff demanded). The company's adjusted-EBITDA noise has been replaced by GAAP transparency, meaning headline EPS will look worse near-term but cleaner. Also invisible: Radoff's history of forcing accretive sales at sub-scale specialty finance names, and the latent value of the credit-card portfolio if separated.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **April 3, 2026** — CEO Vazquez departure date; interim Layton/Rana team takes over.
2. **Q2 2026** — 2026 annual meeting; board refresh / Ginny Lee resignation pressure.
3. **H2 2026** — External CEO appointment + sub-6% ABS issuance.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Business quality (moat) | 4/10 |
| 2 | Management quality | 5/10 |
| 3 | Reinvestment runway | 5/10 |
| 4 | Balance sheet strength | 4/10 |
| 5 | Free cash flow durability | 5/10 |
| 6 | Insider/activist alignment | 8/10 |
| 7 | Valuation (margin of safety) | 8/10 |
| 8 | Catalyst clarity & timing | 8/10 |
| 9 | Downside protection | 5/10 |
| 10 | Asymmetry (skew) | 8/10 |
| **TOTAL** | | **60/100** |

CVEO.name: Civeo Corporation
CVEO.content:
# 96. CVEO — Civeo Corporation
## ACTIVIST — OPERATIONAL TURN | $30.18 / $330M mkt cap | Score: 73/100

### THE SETUP
Civeo trades at $30.18 against Engine Capital's $47 standalone fair value, with two activist-nominated directors (Jeffrey Scofield and Daniel Silvers) seated on the board as of November 25, 2025 to enforce capital-return discipline and operational fix at this $330M micro-cap workforce-accommodations operator.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Civeo owns and operates remote workforce villages serving the Australian iron-ore and metallurgical-coal corridors and Canadian oil sands — essentially camps with rooms, food service, and logistics for resource workers. Revenue is occupancy-driven and tied to commodity-cycle capex by majors like BHP, Rio Tinto, and the Canadian heavy-oil players. The screen flags it because of Engine Capital's 9.8% 13D filed March 20, 2025, the public letter campaign, and the November 25, 2025 cooperation agreement that placed two activist directors on the board. Stock at $30.18 has rallied from sub-$20 but trails the activist's $47 standalone math (4–5x EBITDA on a normalized base).

Bear case: workforce accommodations is a brutally cyclical, customer-concentrated business with low switching costs once a project ends. A roll-over in iron-ore prices or a delay in major Canadian oil-sands sustaining-capex cycles can crush occupancy and FCF in two quarters. The Australian segment competes with Civeo's own customers' in-house camps and with private competitors. The dividend was eliminated as part of the activist deal, which removes one source of holder demand. Micro-cap liquidity ($330M) means any institutional exit is painful, and the float is tiny.

Bull case: Engine has won board representation and committed CEO to 1.75x leverage, aggressive buybacks, and cost cuts — exactly the playbook that re-rated similar small-cap services names. Australian iron-ore demand from China remains supportive into 2026, and Canadian oil sands operators are stepping up sustaining capex. At ~$30 versus the $47 standalone path, there is roughly 50% upside before considering any sale process. PE or strategic interest is plausible — Civeo is exactly the kind of cash-generative niche asset a sponsor would tuck into a portfolio. Engine has skin in the game with 9.8% economic exposure.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Nov 25, 2025 cooperation agreement is a true behavior contract, not a token concession — the board now has two activist seats out of the slate, and the agreement explicitly references capital allocation, leverage targets, and review of the dividend, all of which were conceded by management. The 10-K segment disclosures show Australian segment EBITDA margins materially above Canadian, but the consolidated multiple values both equally — a sale would let a buyer tax-efficiently separate the two and capture sum-of-parts uplift. Engine's public deck specifically called out a $47 standalone target derived from 4-5x EBITDA on what they view as run-rate FCF, and the activists are now inside the boardroom to hold management accountable to that math.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 and Q2 2026 earnings — margin and FCF inflection
- Buyback acceleration announcement
- Potential sale process or strategic review disclosure
- 2026 annual meeting

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Workforce camps, simple |
| Moat | 4 | Low switching, cyclical |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | Targeting 1.75x leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | $30 vs $47 activist target |
| Catalyst | 8 | Activist board seats now |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | ~50% upside, modest downside |
| Business Quality | 5 | Cyclical, customer-concentrated |
| Executability | 4 | $330M micro-cap, thin float |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Commodity rollover risk |
| Macro Independence | 3 | Pure commodity-capex beta |
| **TOTAL** | **60** | |
