ECAT.name: BlackRock ESG Capital Allocation Term Trust
ECAT.content:
# 213. ECAT — BlackRock ESG Capital Allocation Term Trust
## CEF DISCOUNT ACTIVISM | Saba 22.57% Stake, June 9 AGM | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
Saba Capital owns 22.57% of ECAT (~$334M cost basis) and has filed a PREC14A nominating an 8-trustee slate to take board majority at the June 9, 2026 AGM. Stock at $14.63 vs. NAV $15.47 (~5–15% discount range), with a tender at NAV or open-end conversion as the bull-case crystallization event. BlackRock's prior settlement pattern (BIGZ/BMEZ tenders) underwrites the floor.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
ECAT is a $1.45B BlackRock multi-asset ESG-tilted closed-end term trust. Saba has been accumulating since 2023 and as of February 18, 2026 owns 22,451,311 shares (22.57%) at a cost basis around $334M, making this Saba's largest BlackRock CEF position. Saba has filed a PREC14A nominating eight trustees — explicitly seeking board majority — with conditional withdrawal of one nominee (Lawrence Epstein) if the board does not expand beyond nine seats. The June 9, 2026 AGM is the dispositive event. Saba's demands: NAV-based tender, conversion to an open-end fund, and (if board control achieved) potentially removing BlackRock as adviser.

The bear case is that BlackRock has greater retail-shareholder mobilization than any other CEF sponsor, has been hardening proxy-defense playbooks since the BIGZ/BMEZ campaigns, and that ISS/Glass Lewis have not consistently sided with Saba on full-slate board takeovers. ECAT is a "term trust" already (currently scheduled to terminate around 2030–2033), which BlackRock will argue obviates the need for activist intervention — the discount, sponsor will say, will close naturally as the wind-down date approaches. The current discount of ~5.4% on the spot tape is below Saba's typical 10%+ trigger, weakening the public case.

The bull case is layered. (1) Outright Saba win → board majority → NAV tender + likely open-ending = 5–15% gap closure on the entire position, plus discount compression on remaining shares. (2) Negotiated settlement (most likely) — BlackRock's pattern with BIGZ/BMEZ was a 30–40% NAV tender with standstill, capturing 60–80% of the discount-closure economics for activist holders. (3) Loss at ballot but discount narrows anyway as term-trust wind-down approaches and discount-management-program triggers (>10% avg discount tender) activate. The $14.63 entry vs. $15.47 NAV is a hard mark with multiple paths to value crystallization.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A standard CEF discount screen sees a modest ~5.4% gap and might pass. The PREC14A's structural detail is the alpha: Saba's 8-nominee slate seeks an actual board majority (not a minority slate), which legally enables — within months of a winning vote — a forced NAV tender and removal of BlackRock as adviser. The conditional Epstein withdrawal mechanism is a lawyer-crafted concession to ISS, signaling Saba's seriousness about getting institutional proxy advisor support. BlackRock's prior BIGZ tender (where the firm caved at ~32% of shares at NAV) is the explicit settlement template — the market is mispricing the probability of a similar negotiated outcome.

### CATALYSTS
- June 9, 2026 — annual meeting / proxy vote
- ISS and Glass Lewis recommendations — May 2026
- Negotiated settlement / tender offer announcement — pre-AGM possible
- Discount Management Program activation if 12-month avg discount >10%

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | CEF mechanics + activist playbook well-documented |
| Moat | 3 | None at fund level; sponsor moat is what's contested |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | $1.45B mkt cap, NAV $15.47, no leverage spiral risk |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | 5.4% spot discount; settlement could deliver 8–12% |
| Catalyst | 9 | Hard June 9 2026 AGM |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | 8–15% upside, 3–5% downside |
| Business Quality | 5 | ESG multi-asset book; mediocre alpha vs benchmarks |
| Executability | 8 | $1.45B fund; reasonable liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Saba loses + discount widens to 10%+; cushioned by yield |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Equity beta exists but activist outcome is independent |
| **TOTAL** | **68** | Settlement probability skews real risk-adjusted score to 78 |

---

BCAT.name: BlackRock Capital Allocation Term Trust
BCAT.content:
# 215. BCAT — BlackRock Capital Allocation Term Trust
## CEF DISCOUNT ACTIVISM | Saba 5.11% / Term 2032 | Score: 62/100

### THE SETUP
Multi-asset CEF where Saba Capital crossed the 5% threshold in May 2025 (~$78M cost basis) on a structure that auto-liquidates by 2032. Discount has narrowed sharply (sub-5% by 2026, briefly at premium per Q4 2025 commentary), but the term-trust mechanic is the real option — Saba doesn't need a tender to win.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
BCAT is a $1.5B BlackRock multi-asset closed-end fund (equity + credit + opportunistic) structured as a "term trust" — meaning it has a contractual dissolution date around 2032 at which point shareholders receive NAV. Saba Capital filed Schedule 13D/A May 19, 2025 disclosing 5,352,475 shares = 5.11% stake at ~$78.4M cost. Current price $14.80 vs. NAV $14.56 (research file shows -1.6% discount; BlackRock's Q4 2025 commentary says 2025 average discount was -4.8%, below the -10% Discount Management Program threshold so no 2025 tender). Per Q4 commentary: "Trust did not issue a tender offer in 2025 as its average discount was -4.8%."

Bear case: the discount has already narrowed dramatically — much of the easy alpha is captured. Saba sub-5% means no immediate proxy fight catalyst; multi-asset CEFs face compressed credit spreads in 2026 and any rate volatility hits NAV directly. The DMP threshold sits at 10% — wide gaps would have to reopen for a forced tender, which competing supply/demand from BlackRock support buying may prevent. If markets sell off, NAV drops faster than discount tightens, hurting absolute returns.

Bull case: Saba's BlackRock track record is undefeated — BIGZ/BMEZ-style 50% tenders at 99.5% of NAV. Even without a tender, the 2032 hard maturity provides pull-to-NAV with ~6 years of duration. Saba can escalate to trustee nominations at the 2026 annual meeting. Cushion: shareholders have a contractual right to NAV at term — every year that passes pulls market price toward NAV at ~mathematical certainty. BlackRock often pre-empts proxy fights with negotiated tenders.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The term-trust prospectus contains a board option to extend dissolution beyond 2032 by shareholder vote — Saba's 5.11% stake plus likely allied CEF activists creates a blocking position against extension. Per BlackRock filings: the Board "may vote to conduct a tender offer within twelve months preceding the Dissolution Date... at NAV per common share." Saba doesn't need to win a proxy fight to capture NAV — they just need to vote against any extension. This effectively converts BCAT into a 6-year zero-coupon bond on NAV with embedded equity upside.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- 2026 annual meeting: trustee nominations window (typically Q2 2026)
- DMP measurement period (Jan–Sep 2026): if average discount >10%, triggers 5% tender at 98% NAV
- Possible Saba 13D/A escalation if discount widens during equity selloff

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Plain-vanilla CEF mechanics |
| Moat | 2 | Closed-end fund — no moat |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | NAV-backed; some leverage typical |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | Discount already narrowed to 1-5% |
| Catalyst | 7 | 2032 term + DMP + Saba |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | Limited downside given pull-to-NAV |
| Business Quality | 4 | Pass-through vehicle |
| Executability | 8 | $1.5B liquid CEF |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | NAV erosion in selloff outpaces discount narrowing |
| Macro Independence | 3 | Multi-asset = market beta |
| **TOTAL** | **62** | |

This effectively converts BCAT into a 6-year zero-coupon bond on NAV with embedded equity upside.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- 2026 annual meeting: trustee nominations window (typically Q2 2026)
- DMP measurement period (Jan–Sep 2026): if average discount >10%, triggers 5% tender at 98% NAV
- Possible Saba 13D/A escalation if discount widens during equity selloff

BSTZ.name: BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust
BSTZ.content:
# 214. BSTZ — BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust
## CEF DISCOUNT ACTIVISM | 9-11% NAV Discount, 7.94% Saba Stake | Score: 71/100

### THE SETUP
A BlackRock sector CEF with 30%+ in private AI/tech holdings (Databricks, Stripe, Spectrum) trading at a persistent 9-11% discount to NAV — and Boaz Weinstein's Saba Capital just bought $93.8M to escalate from 5% to 7.94%. With BIGZ/BMEZ as a template (40-50% tender precedents), the asymmetry is short-dated and high-probability.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
BSTZ is a $1.7B closed-end fund holding listed and unlisted growth-tech equities, structured as a "term trust" scheduled to wind up in 2031. The fund pays an 8% managed distribution funded substantially from return of capital, and trades at $24.97 vs. NAV of ~$27.46 (9.1% discount). Saba Capital filed SC 13D/A on March 13, 2026 disclosing 7.94% beneficial ownership after accumulating ~5.46M shares (~$93.8M) Jan-March. Saba has already nominated trustees for the 2026 proxy season. The discount has persisted in part because the market discounts BlackRock's marks on illiquid privates such as Databricks and Stripe — and partly because rising rates strained CEF demand.

The bear case: NAV itself may be optimistic. BSTZ carries Databricks, Stripe, and other late-stage privates at quarterly fair-value marks, and a sector reset (or a Stripe down-round) would close the "discount" by lowering NAV rather than lifting price. Term-trust structure gives BlackRock until 2031 to engineer an exit, so there is no enforceable hard catalyst. Saba precedents at BIGZ/BMEZ produced sub-NAV (98%) tenders capped at 40-50% of shares — meaning the discount only partially monetizes, and residual NAV may re-discount post-tender. An 8% distribution that is partly destructive ROC also caps total return.

The bull case is settlement geometry: Saba's BIGZ playbook (52.5% tender at 99.5% NAV in 2024) is the obvious template, and BlackRock has shown willingness to settle to avoid messy contested votes. A 30-50% tender at 98-99% of NAV on a $24.97 stock with $27.46 NAV harvests roughly 10-13% on the tendered slice. Add private-IPO upside (Stripe, Databricks listing optionality 2026-27) and discount-narrowing on the residual. Annual meeting trustee vote is the gating event in Q2-Q3 2026.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The fund's N-CSR semi-annual report (filed early 2026) discloses the actual fair-value methodology for private holdings — typically last-round price plus haircuts and adjustments based on comparable public peers. Saba's accumulation pace (5.46M shares for $93.8M = ~$17.18 avg) suggests they bought in below current market, meaning their basis is well below NAV. Importantly, BSTZ has a pre-existing "Discount Management Program" that automatically triggers tender consideration if the average discount exceeds 7.5% — a board-approved governance mechanism Saba can lever in proxy materials. Watch for a settlement announcement before the 2026 record date.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- **June-July 2026:** 2026 annual meeting trustee vote (Saba slate vs. BlackRock incumbents).
- **Q2-Q3 2026:** Possible BlackRock-Saba settlement (tender modeled on BIGZ/BMEZ).
- **2026:** Discount Management Program quarterly review; possible Stripe IPO would re-rate private NAV.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | CEF mechanics straightforward; private NAV opaque |
| Moat | 3 | Wrapper, no business moat |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | No leverage; $1.7B NAV |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | 9-11% discount + tender option |
| Catalyst | 8 | Saba 7.94% + nominations |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Short-dated, capped downside |
| Business Quality | 5 | Term trust, fee drag |
| Executability | 8 | $1.7B cap, liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | NAV writedowns close discount the wrong way |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Tech beta high |
| **TOTAL** | **65** | |

BMEZ.name: BlackRock Health Sciences Term Trust
BMEZ.content:
# 220. BMEZ — BlackRock Health Sciences Term Trust
## CEF DISCOUNT ACTIVISM | 9.7% NAV discount, term expires Jan 2027 | Score: 76/100

### THE SETUP
BMEZ is a BlackRock healthcare closed-end fund with a hardwired termination date of January 31, 2027 — guaranteed liquidation at NAV. Saba Capital already extracted a 40% tender at 99.5% NAV in March 2025; the residual 9.7% discount on the remaining stub captures most of the gap with Boaz Weinstein bound by a 3-year standstill, leaving a passive sit-and-wait CEF arb with a built-in clock.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
BMEZ is a $914M market-cap closed-end fund managed by BlackRock investing in global healthcare equities — biotech, medtech, pharma, healthcare services. NAV per share ~$16.20 vs. price $14.63 = 9.7% discount. The fund is structured as a "term trust" with a contractual termination date of January 31, 2027 (extendable by one year by board action), at which point it will liquidate or convert to open-end, returning NAV to holders. In January 2025, Saba Capital reached a cooperation agreement with BlackRock that triggered a 40% tender at 99.5% of NAV (commenced March 21, 2025); Saba tendered all its shares, withdrew its proposals, and accepted a 3-year standstill through the 2027 proxy season. The residual fund is now ~$914M with the same expiration clock and a wider-than-it-should-be discount.

Bear case: a 9.7% discount at face value is not a fat margin if you have to wait ~21 months to harvest it, and healthcare CEF NAVs can drop 15-20% in a sector drawdown — biotech XBI is a volatile underlying. The board can extend termination by one year (to Jan 2028), pushing the catalyst out and dragging IRR. Saba's standstill removes the most aggressive activist from the table. Distribution rate is funded partly from return of capital, masking true performance and creating a "managed distribution" trap where investors discover post-tender that NAV has been bleeding. If the board converts to open-end instead of liquidating, redemption queues + gating rules can delay full NAV capture.

Bull case: the term-trust expiration is a contractual, near-certain catalyst — at termination, NAV is paid in cash (or in-kind transferred). Hedging the underlying with XBI/IBB or a sector-neutral basket converts the trade into a pure 9.7% discount-capture over 21 months ≈ ~5.5% annualized hedged IRR with negligible market risk. Unhedged, you get healthcare beta + the discount close. Saba's 40% tender at 99.5% NAV is precedent: when the term arrives, BlackRock will not want a messy liquidation and will likely structure another orderly exit. Possible upside scenario: BlackRock proposes a second pre-term tender at high-90s% of NAV to shrink the fund further.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The cooperation agreement (Saba-BlackRock, Jan 21, 2025) contains a "most-favored-nation" provision in some BlackRock CEF settlements that auto-extends Saba's tender economics if BlackRock offers better terms to another holder — worth diligencing in the 8-K. The board's right to extend termination by one year requires a formal vote; in BlackRock's prior term-trust history (e.g., BSL, BHK), the extensions were exercised once and then funds liquidated cleanly. BMEZ's portfolio includes a chunky private/illiquid bucket (~10-15% in Level 3 biotech) that adds NAV-mark uncertainty — at termination, those will be sold at potentially below carrying value, so the realized return may be 50-150 bps below stated NAV. Distribution policy: ~7% managed distribution rate funded by ROC creates artificial yield.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q3 2026 board decision on whether to extend termination beyond Jan 31, 2027
- Possible second-round tender announcement (Q2-Q3 2026)
- January 31, 2027 contractual termination → NAV payout

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Plain CEF discount arb with term clock |
| Moat | 4 | None — fund is sunsetting |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Modest leverage (~25%), liquid securities |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | 9.7% discount + 21-month tail |
| Catalyst | 9 | Hardwired Jan 31, 2027 termination |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | Hedged ~5.5% IRR, downside capped |
| Business Quality | 5 | CEF wrapper, healthcare beta |
| Executability | 8 | $914M mcap, decent volume |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Term extension + biotech drawdown |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Healthcare sector + rate sensitivity |
| **TOTAL** | **67** | |

---

BN.name: Brookfield Corporation
BN.content:
# 108. BN — Brookfield Corporation
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~26% discount to plan value, 13.2x DE | Score: 84/100

### THE SETUP
Brookfield Corporation owns 75% of publicly-listed Brookfield Asset Management plus on-balance-sheet stakes in real estate, insurance (BNT), and PE/infra co-investments — yet trades at $46.59 vs. the company's own $63 "plan value" target (~26% discount) and 13.2x distributable earnings vs. Apollo at 14.6x and KKR at 20x. The BAM stake alone justifies ~$37/share, leaving the rest of the empire essentially free.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Brookfield Corp (BN) is the parent holdco of the Brookfield ecosystem. Following the December 2022 spin of asset-management arm BAM as a separately listed pure-play, BN retained a 75% economic interest in BAM (which has a $80.8B market cap; BN's stake worth ~$60.6B) and consolidated the on-balance-sheet "principal investments": insurance solutions via Brookfield Wealth Solutions (BNT) including Argo, Just Group and other annuity/reinsurance blocks; real estate equity stakes (private and BPY-style), PE/infra co-investments, and accrued carried interest receivables (~$10B+ unrealized). Mcap $105B, current price $46.59. Management's published "Plan Value" framework implies fair value ~$63/share by 2026 → 26% discount. Distributable earnings (DE) ~$3.50/share; trades at 13.2x DE vs. Apollo 14.6x, KKR 20x, Blackstone ~24x.

Bear case: the holdco discount is structural — multi-tiered listed entities (BAM, BEP, BIP, BBU, BWS) plus an opaque insurance wing make sum-of-parts valuation a guesswork exercise that has historically traded at persistent 20-30% discounts. GAAP P/E of ~98x scares quant screens. Insurance leverage at BNT (~$120B AUM) carries credit-spread duration risk that could blow up in a recession; commercial real estate stub remains an underwater chapter (BPY take-private was at a meaningful discount). Brookfield's "carry" claims rest on private fund vintages whose realizations depend on a transaction-market thaw — if 2026-2027 exit windows stay closed, the carried-interest pile-up is illiquid paper. Bruce Flatt is 60+; succession isn't guaranteed.

Bull case: the math is simple — 75% of BAM at current price = ~$60B / 2.27B BN shares = ~$26.50/share, but BAM should re-rate higher given its capital-light fee model (peer multiples imply $35-40/share for the BAM stake alone). Add ~$10B accrued carry (~$4.40/share), insurance solutions earnings power (~$1.5B annual DE → ~$10/share at 15x), and real-asset principal investments worth $20-30B on balance sheet, and "Plan Value" of $63 looks conservative. Catalysts: (1) DE growth +15-20% annualized through 2027; (2) carry realizations as 2014-2017 Brookfield fund vintages exit; (3) insurance float compounding at 12%+ ROEs; (4) potential corporate simplification eliminating discount.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Brookfield's "carried interest" line on the financials (~$10B unrealized as of latest disclosure) is a mark-to-model figure that Wall Street largely ignores — but it's real cash that crystallizes when private funds sell investments. The 2014-2017 BIF/BSREP vintages are entering peak harvest years 2026-2028; Brookfield Infrastructure Fund II already returned over 2x net. The 75% BAM ownership is a tax-efficient holding structure (BN's stake is held via a Bermuda intermediary), making any attempt to collapse the structure complicated but not impossible. Insiders Bruce Flatt, Connor Teskey and other partners own ~20% of BN directly and their economics are tied to DE per share growth — the 2024 long-term plan targets $6/share DE by 2030, a 3x from today. Read the latest letter to shareholders' "Plan Value" appendix; it spells out the SOTP line by line.

### NAV BREAKDOWN
| Component | Per share | Note |
|---|---|---|
| 75% of BAM (peer-rerated) | ~$37 | At current BAM mcap; higher if re-rates |
| Accrued carried interest | ~$4-5 | $10B+ unrealized |
| Insurance Solutions (BNT) | ~$10 | $1.5B DE × 15x / share count |
| Real estate / PE / infra co-invest | ~$10-12 | On balance sheet |
| Plan Value (mgmt) | ~$63 | vs. price $46.59 |

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q4 2025 / Q1 2026 distributable earnings prints +15-20% YoY
- Carry realizations from 2014-2017 fund vintages (announced exits)
- Investor Day September 2026 — updated Plan Value disclosure

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 6 | Complex but parsable |
| Moat | 9 | Brookfield brand + scale in alts |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | Investment-grade, asset-heavy, $135B AUM |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | 26% discount + cheap on DE |
| Catalyst | 7 | DE growth + carry realizations |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | $63 plan value, downside bounded |
| Business Quality | 9 | Capital-light fee biz + float |
| Executability | 10 | $105B mcap, deep liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | CRE/insurance tail risk |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Rate-sensitive at the margin |
| **TOTAL** | **77** | |

---

LBTYK.name: Liberty Global Ltd. (Class C)
LBTYK.content:
# 109. LBTYK — Liberty Global Ltd. (Class C)
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~53% discount to NAV | Score: 76/100

### THE SETUP
Liberty Global trades at a market cap of ~$8.3B against an estimated underlying NAV of $20-25B+ — a ~50% discount that has persisted despite a ~50% share count reduction over five years and the November 2024 Sunrise (Switzerland) spin-off. Mcap of ~$8B versus 50% stakes in Virgin Media O2 (UK) + VodafoneZiggo (Netherlands) + 100% of Telenet + ~$2-3B in Liberty Ventures + cash — the math has been screaming for years and Malone keeps grinding the float.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Liberty Global is John Malone's European cable holdco. After a decade of restructuring, the asset stack today consists of: 50% of Virgin Media O2 (UK joint venture with Telefonica — fixed/mobile combination, BT competitor, mid-fiber buildout); 50% of VodafoneZiggo (Netherlands JV with Vodafone); 100% of Telenet (Belgium, taken private in 2023); a Liberty Ventures portfolio (LatAm telecom stakes, Atlas Edge, Plume, ITV stake, public/private positions); and several billion in cash and credits. Sunrise (Switzerland) was distributed to shareholders in November 2024. The dual-share structure (LBTYA/LBTYK/LBTYB) and JV-heavy asset mix makes it ungainly to model — exactly the kind of complexity Malone uses to mint long-term shareholder returns through buybacks at deep discounts.

The bear case is the persistence of the discount. JVs are notoriously hard to unlock — partners (Telefonica, Vodafone) hold ROFRs and historically use them to block crystallization events. European telecom is a brutal industry with regulatory pressure on roaming, wholesale access, and fiber. FX (USD reporting on EUR/GBP assets) creates noise. Malone is in his 80s; succession planning at the holdco level is opaque. The "discount narrows at the IPO" thesis has been pitched for half a decade with no IPO. Even successful asset sales tend to fund more buybacks rather than special distributions, which the market has stopped rewarding.

The bull case is the reflexivity of buybacks at a 50% discount. Every dollar of buyback creates ~$2 of NAV per remaining share. Liberty has shrunk shares outstanding from ~720M in 2018 to ~350M today. With Telenet now wholly-owned and clean, a re-listing is straightforward; Virgin Media O2 has been preparing IPO machinery for two years; VodafoneZiggo's recap distributions return cash to LG. Sunrise was the proof point — distribution to holders crystallized real value. One JV monetization at fair value would erase the discount overnight.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K/20-F discloses Virgin Media O2's net leverage covenants and dividend recap mechanism — VMO2 has been distributing meaningful cash to its parents (LG and Telefonica) via leveraged recaps roughly every 18 months, which is the actual operating cash return mechanism. The Liberty Ventures portfolio is marked at cost in many cases (private positions like Plume, Atlas Edge); fair value would add several dollars per LBTYK share. Class C shares (LBTYK) are non-voting and trade at a structural ~3-5% discount to Class A (LBTYA), but they're the share Liberty repurchases against — meaning all buyback accretion accrues disproportionately to LBTYK holders.

### CATALYSTS
- Virgin Media O2 IPO or partial monetization — telegraphed for 2026
- Continued aggressive buybacks (next 10-15% of float guided)
- Telenet re-listing or secondary monetization

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Factor | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 7/10 |
| Management quality | 9/10 |
| Balance sheet | 7/10 |
| Moat / pricing power | 7/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 7/10 |
| Insider alignment | 9/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 6/10 |
| Downside protection | 8/10 |
| Margin of safety | 9/10 |
| Simplicity / understandability | 5/10 |
| **Total** | **74/100** |

---

The Liberty Ventures portfolio is marked at cost in many cases (private positions like Plume, Atlas Edge); fair value would add several dollars per LBTYK share. Class C shares (LBTYK) are non-voting and trade at a structural ~3-5% discount to Class A (LBTYA), but they're the share Liberty repurchases against — meaning all buyback accretion accrues disproportionately to LBTYK holders.

LILAK.name: Liberty Latin America Ltd. (Class C)
LILAK.content:
# 110. LILAK — Liberty Latin America Ltd. (Class C)
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT 45% | SOTP $16 vs $8.78 | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
A Malone tracker stub trading at 55 cents on the dollar of indicative NAV, with management for the first time openly stating they are willing to sell pieces to close the discount. Q3 2025 inflection in rebased Adj OIBDA (+7%) marks the operational turn after three years of Puerto Rico integration pain.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Liberty Latin America is a multi-country Caribbean and Latam telecom holdco assembled by John Malone's Liberty empire. The asset base spans Cable & Wireless Caribbean (mobile + B2B in 24 markets), C&W Panama (mobile leader), VTR Chile (cable, JV with America Movil), Liberty Puerto Rico (post-Sprint integration), Cabletica (Costa Rica), and a small Liberty Networks subsea fiber JV. The Class C shares (LILAK) are non-voting and trade at the deepest discount in the complex. Market cap ~$3.48B vs management/sell-side indicative SOTP near $16 per share — a 45% discount.

The bear case is real: hurricane risk concentrates physical assets in storm corridors; Puerto Rico has been a multi-year integration drag; Chile is a hyper-competitive cable/wireless market with VTR losing share; segment-by-segment leverage is high; and Liberty trackers historically trade at structural discounts that rarely close cleanly. Reported numbers are a tangle of multi-currency, multi-segment, JV-accounted entities — most generalist investors simply skip it. Refinancing cliffs in 2027-2028 are a real concern if rates stay elevated.

The bull case rests on three things converging in 2026: (1) Puerto Rico free cash flow ramp now that the Sprint customer migration is essentially done and competitor WOM filed bankruptcy; (2) management explicitly stating they are "focused on unlocking the SOTP discount" — translation: they will sell Panama, Chile, or PR if the price is right; (3) buybacks at 45% NAV discount are extraordinarily accretive. Q3 2025 rebased Adj OIBDA +7% confirms the inflection.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
LILAK is not one company — it's six businesses in different currencies with different leverage profiles, and equity screens treat the consolidated number as one thing. The Panama segment alone, on a stand-alone EV/EBITDA basis at regional comp multiples (~6-7x), is worth more than LILAK's entire current market cap. The C&W Caribbean segment trades inside the holdco at an implied 3x EBITDA — distressed multiples for a #1 mobile operator across 24 island markets with no real competition in most of them. The Chile JV with America Movil is on Liberty's balance sheet at zero economic profit but has an obvious exit ramp (sell to Movil). None of this shows up in P/E or EV/EBITDA screens because the consolidated leverage masks segment quality.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
- **Q4 2025 / FY 2025 earnings (Feb-Mar 2026)** — confirmation of Adj OIBDA inflection and PR FCF ramp.
- **Strategic action announcement (1H 2026)** — management has signaled openness to segment sale; Chile JV reorganization is the most likely first move.
- **Q2 2026 buyback pace** — at 45% discount any meaningful repurchase is highly accretive; track authorization use rate.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Business quality (moat, ROIC) | 5 |
| 2 | Management capability & alignment | 7 |
| 3 | Balance sheet strength | 4 |
| 4 | Reinvestment runway | 5 |
| 5 | Earnings predictability | 4 |
| 6 | Margin of safety (price vs value) | 9 |
| 7 | Catalyst clarity | 7 |
| 8 | Downside protection | 5 |
| 9 | Insider behavior | 7 |
| 10 | Simplicity / understandability | 3 |
| **Total** | | **56/100** |

HHH.name: Howard Hughes Holdings Inc.
HHH.content:
# 113. HHH — Howard Hughes Holdings Inc.
## HOLDCO | 31% NAV Discount | Score: 81/100

### THE SETUP
Howard Hughes trades at $65.94 against a self-published indicative NAV of ~$95 — a 31% discount — and Boyar Value Group pegs intrinsic NAV at $128. Bill Ackman's Pershing Square just put $900M of fresh equity into HHH (May 2025) at higher prices and installed himself as Executive Chairman to convert this 120,000-acre master-planned-community land bank into a "Berkshire of real estate plus insurance," with the $2.1B Vantage Insurance acquisition closing Q2 2026.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Howard Hughes Holdings owns five elite master-planned communities — Summerlin (Las Vegas), The Woodlands and Bridgeland (Houston), Teravalis (Phoenix), and Columbia (Maryland) — totaling ~120,000 residual acres being slowly monetized over decades. Layered on top is a Class A operating portfolio (office, retail, multifamily, hospitality), the Seaport district in NYC (now substantially separated/restructured to remove the historical drag), and ~$1.2B of cash. The company's real estate segments throw off recurring NOI; the MPCs throw off periodic land sales whose timing depends on housing-cycle absorption rates and homebuilder demand. This is a 50-year asset book trapped inside a single C-corp wrapper that the public market has historically valued at a 25-40% discount to appraised value.

The bear case is the discount itself. NAV discounts persist because the public market doesn't trust holdco accounting, doesn't trust land-value-now versus land-cash-flow-30-years-out, and doesn't trust complexity. The Seaport overhang, while now mostly fixed, cost the company hundreds of millions before its restructuring. Land-sales pace depends entirely on mortgage-rate trajectory; persistent 6.5%+ mortgages strangle homebuilder absorption and stretch your monetization timeline. Add the May 2025 "takeover lite" — Ackman did not actually buy out minority holders despite an aborted January 2025 proposal — and minority holders rightly distrust the alignment story. Insurance deals (Vantage at 1.5x BV, $2.1B) can destroy capital if loss-reserve assumptions prove wrong; insurance is not a place to learn on the job.

The bull case is the Ackman conversion thesis. Pershing put in $900M at materially higher than current prices, plus up to $1B preferred to fund Vantage. The vehicle now has insurance float layered onto a self-funded land bank — a structurally rare combination. Ackman is Executive Chairman with full operational authority. If Vantage generates even modest underwriting profitability, the float becomes permanent low-cost capital to fund both internal MPC development and external acquisitions. Boyar's $128 NAV implies ~95% upside; even closing half the discount yields a 40%+ return.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K's MPC segment carries land at historical cost — Summerlin's basis dates to a 1996 Rouse-era acquisition. Comparable raw acreage in the Las Vegas valley sells today at multiples of book value the screen can't surface. The 2024 10-K disclosure on remaining sellable acres shows residual saleable land in Summerlin and Bridgeland alone supports decades of cash flow. The Vantage transaction is being funded with up to $1B of perpetual preferred from PSH at a fixed coupon — meaning HHH gets insurance float without diluting common equity at today's depressed multiple. That preferred-funded structure is the alpha screens miss: Ackman is using PSH balance sheet capacity, not HHH common, to grow.

### CATALYSTS
- Vantage Insurance close (Q2 2026)
- First insurance investment income reporting (Q3 2026)
- MPC land-sale acceleration if mortgage rates fall through 2026
- Possible Berkshire-style follow-on M&A announcement (open-ended)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 8/10 |
| Management integrity | 8/10 |
| Capital allocation | 9/10 |
| Balance sheet | 7/10 |
| Moat durability | 9/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 9/10 |
| Insider alignment | 9/10 |
| Margin of safety | 8/10 |
| Optionality | 9/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 5/10 |
| **Total** | **81/100** |

---

PSHZF.name: Pershing Square Holdings, Ltd.
PSHZF.content:
# 114. PSHZF — Pershing Square Holdings, Ltd.
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | 24% DISCOUNT | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP

Pershing Square Holdings is Bill Ackman's London/Amsterdam-listed permanent-capital vehicle holding a concentrated portfolio of 8–10 high-conviction US large-caps. PSH currently trades around $59.50 versus an indicative NAV of ~$78, a 24% discount that has been the subject of explicit shareholder attack by Ackman himself. The fund delivered 20.9% NAV growth and 33.9% total shareholder return in 2025; net assets exceed $15.5B. Repurchases totaled ~$370M in 2025 ($1.8B cumulative since the program began), narrowing the discount from a previous ~31% to today's 24%.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Pershing Square's portfolio reads like a concentrated bet on franchise quality at scale: Universal Music Group (the dominant catalog-rich music major), Restaurant Brands International (Tim Hortons / Burger King / Popeyes), Chipotle, Hilton, Howard Hughes Holdings, Brookfield, Alphabet (added recently), and a handful of others. This is not a hedge fund book — it's a long-only concentrated equity portfolio with ~5–10 names making up substantially all exposure. Returns over the 2018–2025 period have meaningfully outpaced the S&P 500 net of fees.

The discount exists because of structural friction: (1) the closed-end fund wrapper carries a perpetual stigma; (2) management fees are ~1.5% plus a 16% performance fee above hurdle, which is genuine drag; (3) US investors face ETF/ADR ineligibility — most must buy the OTC PSHZF ticker, which is illiquid; (4) Ackman key-man risk (the entire investment process is his); and (5) UMG concentration concerns (UMG alone is a top-3 position).

Ackman's response to the discount has been three-pronged. First, aggressive at-NAV buybacks — every share repurchased at 76 cents on the dollar mechanically grows NAV per share for remaining holders. Second, a planned US IPO of "Pershing Square USA" (PSUS), creating a US-listed liquid version that should cannibalize discount tourism into PSH. Third, ongoing public commentary and shareholder communications that explicitly target discount compression as a measurable goal.

The math is compelling: if you believe the underlying portfolio compounds at 10–12% annually (in line with Ackman's track record), and the discount narrows from 24% to 15% over 3 years, you get the underlying portfolio return plus a one-time ~10% re-rating gain. If the discount closes entirely (unlikely), the upside is closer to ~30% pure re-rating. The downside scenario is that the discount stays sticky — but at 24% you're essentially buying high-quality franchises at a 24% bid.

The pushback: closed-end fund discounts are notoriously persistent. Greenlight Capital Re, Third Point Reinsurance, and other Ackman-adjacent vehicles have shown that concentrated-manager wrappers tend to stay discounted regardless of performance. The PSUS IPO is now multiple years delayed.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

A screen sees a foreign ADR with high fees and concentrated equity. It does not see: the portfolio composition (which is publicly disclosed via SEC filings and reads like a quality screen), the buyback cadence, Ackman's explicit discount-compression mandate, or the math of buying $1 of UMG/RBI/Chipotle/Hilton at 76 cents. Most importantly, a screen does not see PSH's permanent-capital structure — which is what allows Ackman to run a long-duration, concentrated book without LP redemption pressure.

### CATALYSTS

- Continued at-NAV buybacks (mechanically NAV-accretive)
- PSUS US IPO (whenever it happens — should compress discount)
- Major portfolio realizations (UMG re-rating, Hilton, etc.)
- Possible re-domicile or expanded cross-listing
- Index inclusion if PSUS lists

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- Circle of competence: Concentrated equity in known consumer/media franchises. PASS.
- Durable moat: Underlying portfolio is high-moat by design (UMG catalog, Chipotle brand, Hilton scale). STRONG PASS.
- Honest, capable management: Ackman has been transparent post-Herbalife/Valeant; track record is strong. PASS.
- Margin of safety: 24% discount + quality portfolio = double margin. PASS.
- Predictable cash flows: Portfolio cash flows are genuinely durable. PASS.
- Skin in the game: Ackman's personal capital deeply invested in PSH. STRONG PASS.

Score 78/100 reflects: high-quality concentrated portfolio at meaningful discount, with explicit and active discount-compression playbook. Risk is fee drag and key-man exposure.

---

NL.name: NL Industries, Inc.
NL.content:
# 115. NL — NL Industries, Inc.
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~45% Look-Through Discount | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
NL Industries is the middle layer of the Harold Simmons / Contran three-tier holding structure (Contran -> Valhi VHI -> NL -> CompX/Kronos). NL owns 87% of CompX International (CIX) and roughly 30% of Kronos Worldwide (KRO). The look-through public-market value of those stakes alone is north of $470M against NL's ~$290M market cap — before counting net cash, the dividend, or any operating earnings. You are buying the underlying public stocks at roughly 60 cents on the dollar.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
NL Industries is a remnant of the legacy National Lead conglomerate, today a holding company whose value sits almost entirely in two publicly traded stakes plus net cash and a small residual operations footprint. The 87% stake in CompX (CIX), a security-products and marine-components maker, throws off a meaningful slug of dividend income and is the highest-quality asset in the structure. The ~30% stake in Kronos Worldwide (KRO) is a leveraged play on the global TiO2 (titanium dioxide) cycle, currently near multi-year price lows after a brutal post-COVID destocking. NL pays a $0.32/share dividend and has historically issued large special dividends when the holding-company cash pile gets uncomfortably big — this is the principal mechanism by which the discount is monetized over time.

The bear case for NL is that the discount is structural and won't close. The Contran/Valhi/NL/Kronos structure has existed for decades, the Simmons heirs control roughly 87% of NL through Contran/Valhi, and there is no realistic activist path. Liquidity is poor (average daily volume often under 100k shares), which excludes most institutional capital. Lead-paint litigation, while largely settled at this point, has periodically flared up over the past two decades and creates tail-risk that is impossible to fully underwrite. KRO is a commodity chemicals producer with limited pricing power, capital-intensive, and currently underearning — so the look-through "value" of the KRO stake assumes a cyclical recovery that may take years. The CIX stake is high quality but small in absolute dollars.

The bull case is straightforward look-through math: NL market cap of ~$290M versus stakes worth $470M+ at current public prices, plus net cash at the holdco level, plus a 5%+ dividend yield collected while you wait. Trades at 0.86x book value and roughly 5x trailing P/E. KRO is at a cyclical trough — TiO2 prices and operating rates typically mean-revert sharply, and a Kronos earnings recovery could double the look-through NAV. CompX continues to grind out high single-digit organic growth with strong margins. The Simmons family has a multi-decade history of paying out cash via specials when the holdco is liquid — patient holders have been rewarded with periodic large distributions.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
NL's 10-K balance sheet shows the stake holdings at carrying value, not market value — to compute look-through NAV you need to multiply NL's CIX share count (~10.7M shares × CIX price) and KRO share count (~30% of ~115M = ~34M shares × KRO price) and add NL's holdco cash net of liabilities. The footnotes also disclose remaining lead-paint reserves and the status of California public-nuisance litigation, which has been the single biggest swing factor over the past 15 years. Insider/Contran ownership at ~87% is disclosed in the proxy. Most importantly: the dividend history, including specials, is not in the typical screening database — NL has paid multiple large special dividends over the past decade, and these specials, not capital appreciation, are the principal historical return mechanism.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **Q1 2026 earnings — May 2026** — CompX margin trajectory; Kronos TiO2 commentary (cycle inflection check).
2. **Special dividend potential — H2 2026** — historical cadence is roughly every 2–4 years; cash build at NL holdco level disclosed in 10-Q.
3. **TiO2 price recovery — 2026–2027** — global destock completion; Kronos earnings normalization.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Business quality / moat | 5 |
| 2 | Management quality | 6 |
| 3 | Capital allocation discipline | 7 |
| 4 | Balance sheet strength | 8 |
| 5 | Free cash flow conversion | 6 |
| 6 | Reinvestment runway | 4 |
| 7 | Pricing power | 5 |
| 8 | Insider ownership / alignment | 8 |
| 9 | Margin of safety vs intrinsic value | 9 |
| 10 | Catalyst clarity / timing | 6 |
| **Total** | | **64/100** |

---

GLPG.name: Galapagos NV
GLPG.content:
# 116. GLPG — Galapagos NV
## CASH FORTRESS | Cash ~$54/sh vs $29 stock | Score: 71/100

### THE SETUP
Galapagos trades at roughly $29 against an indicative ~$54/share in net cash and financial investments at year-end 2025 — a negative enterprise value of ~$25/share for what remains of a TYK2 inhibitor in Phase 3, a CD19 CAR-T with FDA RMAT designation, and Gilead collaboration residuals. The market is paying you to take the pipeline; new management has signaled appetite for capital return after abandoning the May 2025 cell-therapy spinoff plan.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Galapagos is a Belgian biotech (Euronext + Nasdaq ADR) that received a $5B equity-and-collaboration infusion from Gilead in 2019 and has since burned a portion of it through filgotinib's commercial disappointment and a costly cell-therapy build-out. As of FY2025 results, cash and financial investments stood near EUR 3.0B (~$54/share at current FX). Headline assets are GLPG3667 (oral TYK2 inhibitor in Phase 3 for SLE and dermatomyositis, with readouts in early-to-mid 2026) and GLPG5101 (CD19 CAR-T with FDA RMAT designation in relapsed/refractory mantle cell lymphoma). Management abandoned the cell-therapy spinoff in May 2025 and is winding down that unit.

The bear case is straightforward biotech entropy: cash burn against an empty commercial pipeline. The TYK2 class faces stiff competition from BMS's Sotyktu (deucravacitinib) and follow-on entrants from Takeda and Nimbus; even a positive Phase 3 in SLE would land into a crowded autoimmune market with delayed reimbursement. Cell-therapy wind-down costs and severance erode the cash pile faster than the published runway suggests. ADR holders bear FX risk on a euro-denominated cash hoard, and Belgian dividend withholding complicates capital return mechanics. Without a credible pipeline win the stock can drift toward a perpetual cash-discount.

The bull case rests on three asymmetric outcomes. First, a positive GLPG3667 Phase 3 readout could revalue the pipeline meaningfully against today's free option. Second, an asset-sale or partnership for the cell-therapy IP (manufacturing platform plus CAR-T data) with a strategic acquirer could crystallize hundreds of millions in residual value. Third — and most undervalued — new management has explicitly signaled openness to capital return; a large tender or special dividend at any price under cash-per-share is mechanically accretive. Gilead's continuing collaboration milestones provide tail upside.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The FY2025 release confirms net cash and financial investments near EUR 3.0B and explicitly references "evaluating capital return options." Screens still treat GLPG as a clinical biotech and apply a discount-to-cash for burn risk; they miss that the cell-therapy wind-down crystallizes an end-state cash balance investors can underwrite. The Gilead 2019 framework agreement also retains residual milestone economics on legacy assets — these line items are not in screener databases. Finally, Belgian "squeeze-out" thresholds (95%) and the family-influenced board composition make a Gilead-led re-take or third-party tender at a modest premium-to-cash a real, under-modeled outcome.

### CATALYSTS
- GLPG3667 Phase 3 SLE/dermatomyositis readouts: H1-mid 2026
- Cell therapy IP/asset sale announcement: 2026 (post wind-down)
- Capital return announcement (tender/special dividend/buyback): 2026

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 5/10 |
| Management integrity | 7/10 |
| Capital allocation | 7/10 |
| Balance sheet | 10/10 |
| Moat durability | 5/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| Insider alignment | 6/10 |
| Margin of safety | 9/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 8/10 |
| Asymmetry | 8/10 |
| **Total** | **71/100** |

GLPG.analystSummary:
Galapagos is a Belgian biotech (Euronext + Nasdaq ADR) that received a $5B equity-and-collaboration infusion from Gilead in 2019 and has since burned a portion of it through filgotinib's commercial disappointment and a costly cell-therapy build-out. As of FY2025 results, cash and financial investments stood near EUR 3.0B (~$54/share at current FX). Headline assets are GLPG3667 (oral TYK2 inhibitor in Phase 3 for SLE and dermatomyositis, with readouts in early-to-mid 2026) and GLPG5101 (CD19 CAR-T with FDA RMAT designation in relapsed/refractory mantle cell lymphoma). Management abandoned the cell-therapy spinoff in May 2025 and is winding down that unit.

The bear case is straightforward biotech entropy: cash burn against an empty commercial pipeline. The TYK2 class faces stiff competition from BMS's Sotyktu (deucravacitinib) and follow-on entrants from Takeda and Nimbus; even a positive Phase 3 in SLE would land into a crowded autoimmune market with delayed reimbursement. Cell-therapy wind-down costs and severance erode the cash pile faster than the published runway suggests. ADR holders bear FX risk on a euro-denominated cash hoard, and Belgian dividend withholding complicates capital return mechanics. Without a credible pipeline win the stock can drift toward a perpetual cash-discount.

The bull case rests on three asymmetric outcomes. First, a positive GLPG3667 Phase 3 readout could revalue the pipeline meaningfully against today's free option. Second, an asset-sale or partnership for the cell-therapy IP (manufacturing platform plus CAR-T data) with a strategic acquirer could crystallize hundreds of millions in residual value. Third — and most undervalued — new management has explicitly signaled openness to capital return; a large tender or special dividend at any price under cash-per-share is mechanically accretive. Gilead's continuing collaboration milestones provide tail upside.

HUYA.name: HUYA Inc.
HUYA.content:
# 117. HUYA — HUYA Inc.
## HOLDCO | Net-Cash Stub / ~21% NAV Discount | Score: 65/100

### THE SETUP
HUYA trades at ~$3.18 with roughly $3.85/ADS in net cash (RMB 6.25B at March 2025). Tencent owns 67% economic / 95% voting. The live-streaming business is essentially a free option, and Tencent has a documented playbook of taking subsidiaries private — providing a structural floor.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
HUYA is China's #1 game live-streaming platform, controlled by Tencent following Tencent's tender offer years ago. The company sits on RMB ~6.25B of cash, short-term, and long-term deposits (about $862M, or roughly $3.85 per ADS). Against a $3.18 share price and ~$710M market cap, the operating business carries a negative enterprise value. HUYA paid a $0.66/ADS special dividend in 2024 and has signaled additional capital return programs. Float is small (only ~33% non-Tencent), and trading volume is thin, but the math is unambiguously cheap on a sum-of-parts basis.

The bear case is real and not theoretical. Chinese gaming regulation has been episodically hostile (license freezes, minor-protection rules) and the live-streaming monetization model depends on virtual-gift consumption that fell low-double-digits year over year. The VIE structure means US holders own offshore shells with contractual claims into the operating company — a structure that has survived but never been fully tested. Tencent control creates two-sided risk: they can extract value via related-party transactions, or they can take HUYA private at an unfair price (though "unfair" relative to net cash sets a hard floor). DouYu merger optionality was killed by SAMR in 2021.

The bull case is "heads I win small, tails I don't lose much." If Tencent declares another special dividend, public holders capture a high-single-digit yield directly. If Tencent takes HUYA private, the floor is net cash plus some control premium — almost certainly above current. If Chinese consumer/gaming sentiment recovers, the operating stub re-rates from zero to something. Even the Korean/SEA esports vertical Tencent is building out gives HUYA latent strategic value. With the operating business still gross-margin-positive at the platform level, and net cash covering the entire market cap, the asymmetry is genuinely attractive for sized-appropriately China exposure.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The cash quality matters: HUYA's deposits are split across Chinese onshore banks (RMB) and offshore USD accounts. Repatriation friction is real — China's SAFE rules cap dividends out of operating subs to retained earnings of the WFOE. The 2024 special dividend required Tencent's vote, signaling alignment, but also revealing the gating mechanism. The 20-F discloses related-party transactions with Tencent for game-content licensing — these are the conduit by which Tencent could either enrich or extract from minorities. Watch the "amounts due to/from related parties" line. Also note: HUYA's headline cash includes RMB time deposits earning 2-3% — not a sleepy zero-yield pile.

### CATALYSTS
- Annual special-dividend declaration (cadence established 2024)
- Tencent privatization bid (history with Sogou, Bitauto)
- Q4 2025/Q1 2026 results: capital return program update

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 4/10 |
| Management integrity | 6/10 |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Balance sheet | 10/10 |
| Free cash flow | 5/10 |
| Moat | 5/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 4/10 |
| Margin of safety | 9/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7/10 |
| Downside protection | 8/10 |
| **Total** | **65/100** |

MSGS.name: Madison Square Garden Sports Corp.
MSGS.content:
# 118. MSGS — Madison Square Garden Sports Corp.
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | NAV Discount ~31% | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
Buy 100% of the New York Knicks and the New York Rangers — the two most valuable franchises in their respective leagues — at a market cap around $8B against private comps that imply $11–13B fair value. February 2026 board approval to explore a minority stake sale is the first credible mark-to-market catalyst in years.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Madison Square Garden Sports Corp. is a single-asset stub that owns 100% of the New York Knicks (NBA) and 100% of the New York Rangers (NHL), plus their minor-league affiliates — the Hartford Wolf Pack (AHL) and Westchester Knicks (G League). Forbes and Sportico value the Knicks around $7.5B and the Rangers around $4.0B, implying an aggregate franchise value north of $11B before considering league media tailwinds. At a $322 share price and ~$8B market cap, the equity trades at roughly a 31% discount to indicative NAV — and that gap has been remarkably persistent due to the Dolan-family control overhang.

The bear case is structural. James Dolan controls the company via dual-class shares, and his public profile, history of public feuds, and reluctance to sell or distribute create a permanent governance discount. Sports franchises trade infrequently in public markets; Forbes valuations are appraisals, not realized prices. There is no dividend, and capital return has historically been minimal. NBA and NHL collective bargaining agreements introduce salary-cap and revenue-sharing changes that can compress per-team economics. MSG Networks is a separate, troubled RSN stub whose problems can spill back into perception of MSGS.

The bull case is finally activatable. In February 2026 the board unanimously approved exploring a possible spin-off or minority-stake sale of one of the franchises — the first concrete mark-to-market catalyst in MSGS's history. Recent private comps make denial untenable: the Boston Celtics sold at $6.1B in 2025, the Lakers at $10B in 2025, and the Senators at $1.3B. Citi and JPMorgan target north of $370 in early 2026; Susquehanna raised to $388 in April 2026. The new NBA media-rights deal flows through 2026–2027 and should re-base the team-level revenue assumption.

### NAV BREAKDOWN

| Asset | Indicative Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| New York Knicks (NBA) | ~$7.5B | Forbes/Sportico latest |
| New York Rangers (NHL) | ~$4.0B | Forbes/Sportico latest |
| Hartford Wolf Pack (AHL) | nominal | Player development |
| Westchester Knicks (G League) | nominal | Player development |
| Less: revolver utilization | ~($0.275B) | Net debt adjustment |
| **Indicative gross NAV** | **~$11–13B** | Implied per-share ~$470 |
| Current market cap | ~$8.0B | ~$322 / share |

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K contains the league revenue-sharing footnotes that determine how much of the new NBA media-rights deal actually flows to each franchise (and how much is shared) — that flow-through is the single biggest 2026–2027 earnings driver and is opaque to standard quant models. The proxy discloses the dual-class voting structure and Dolan-family supervoting shares; this is the discount and also the reason any sale must be Dolan-blessed. Watch the credit-agreement covenants in the 10-K exhibits — they constrain how much capital can be returned even if a minority sale closes. The MSG Networks reorganization filings (separate company) will mark RSN-related contingent recoveries.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **2026 H1–H2 minority-stake sale process** — outcome of the February 2026 board mandate.
2. **2026 NBA media-rights distribution flow-through** — first full year of new contract economics.
3. **Any spin-off / structural separation announcement** — would convert the discount mechanically.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality (moat) | 10 |
| Management track record | 5 |
| Capital allocation discipline | 5 |
| Balance sheet strength | 7 |
| Pricing power | 9 |
| Reinvestment runway | 7 |
| Margin of safety | 8 |
| Catalyst clarity | 8 |
| Insider/owner alignment | 4 |
| Downside protection | 8 |
| **TOTAL** | **71/100** |

---

NWSA.name: News Corporation (Class A)
NWSA.content:
# 119. NWSA — News Corporation (Class A)
## HOLDCO | ~34% NAV Discount, REA Stake Covers Mcap | Score: 71/100

### THE SETUP
News Corp's $14.4B market cap is essentially covered by its 61.4% stake in ASX-listed REA Group (worth ~$9.2B) plus ~$2B net cash. That implies the market values Dow Jones (WSJ, Barron's, MarketWatch, Factiva), HarperCollins, News UK (The Sun, Times of London), News Corp Australia, Foxtel, and Realtor.com at roughly $3B combined — a fraction of plausible standalone values. Persistent Murdoch dual-class control discount, but the recent succession resolution removes one overhang.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
News Corp is a multi-segment media holding company: Digital Real Estate Services (REA Group + Move/Realtor.com), Dow Jones (WSJ + financial info via Factiva), Book Publishing (HarperCollins), News Media (UK + Australia mastheads), and Subscription Video Services (Foxtel). Dow Jones in particular has transformed into a recurring high-margin digital subscription business with WSJ.com and the Factiva enterprise data feed; HarperCollins is one of the Big Five publishers; REA Group is the dominant Australian property portal with structural network effects. Realtor.com is the U.S. #2 to Zillow and runs separately under Move Inc.

The bear case: Murdoch family supervoting B-shares mean A-shareholders have no governance lever; complexity discount is structural and may not narrow without a corporate action; Foxtel is in long-term decline as Australian streaming intensifies; News UK and News Corp Australia print mastheads face the same secular headwinds as all newspapers; Realtor.com has been losing share to Zillow and is currency for any deal rather than a growth driver. AUD/USD translation introduces FX volatility into reported numbers. The post-Lachlan succession resolution removes one tail risk but doesn't itself catalyze a breakup — and the family has explicitly stated they value control over financial-engineering value.

The bull case is the look-through math. REA at 61.4% of ~$15B = ~$9.2B. Dow Jones, growing high-single-digits with ~30%+ adjusted EBITDA margins, comps to financial-info peers (Factset, Morningstar) at 15x+ EBITDA = $9-12B+. HarperCollins ~$2.5B. News UK + News Corp Australia ~$1.5-2B. Net cash ~$2B. Sum exceeds $24B vs $14.4B mcap. Active buyback program shrinks the gap mechanically. Catalysts include any spin (Dow Jones has been speculated repeatedly), a Foxtel sale (rumored multiple times), or a Realtor.com transaction. Average sell-side PT around $34 implies 35%+ upside.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K segment footnotes break out Dow Jones revenue between Professional Information Business (Factiva, Risk & Compliance — sticky enterprise contracts) and Consumer Subscriptions (WSJ, Barron's), with PIB carrying mid-teens recurring growth that screens lump into "media." REA Group is consolidated, so look-through to NWSA's economic interest requires backing out the minority interest on the balance sheet. The dual-class structure means buybacks are concentrated in NWSA (Class A) while NWS (Class B) trades at a persistent premium — pair trading possibilities. Foxtel's debt is non-recourse to News Corp parent — important for downside scenarios. Murdoch Family Trust documents and the recent court resolution (Reno, NV, 2024-2025) define the post-Rupert governance baseline.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. Foxtel sale or recap announcement (rumored, watch 1H 2026).
2. Dow Jones potential spin or minority IPO (no announced date; persistent street speculation).
3. FY2026 earnings (NWSA fiscal year ends June) — buyback pace and segment growth.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 7/10 |
| Management integrity | 6/10 |
| Capital allocation | 7/10 (buybacks) |
| Balance sheet | 8/10 (net cash) |
| Moat | 8/10 (REA, WSJ, HarperCollins) |
| Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| Insider alignment | 5/10 (Murdoch control) |
| Margin of safety | 8/10 (SOTP) |
| Catalyst clarity | 6/10 |
| Downside protection | 7/10 |
| **Total** | **71/100** |

2. Dow Jones potential spin or minority IPO (no announced date; persistent street speculation).

REINY.name: Reinet Investments S.C.A.
REINY.content:
# 120. REINY — Reinet Investments S.C.A.
## HOLDCO | NAV Discount ~35% / 80% Cash Post-PIC | Score: 76/100
### THE SETUP
Reinet Investments is the Johann Rupert family's Luxembourg-domiciled investment holding vehicle, listed primarily on the Johannesburg Stock Exchange and Euronext Amsterdam, with REINY representing the thinly-traded US ADR. Until early 2026 the bulk of NAV was tied up in Pension Insurance Corporation (PIC) — the UK pension buyout specialist — and a residual British American Tobacco (BAT) position. On March 27, 2026, Reinet completed the GBP 2.9 billion sale of its PIC stake to Athora, following the prior multi-year exit from BAT (EUR 1.6 billion proceeds across 2024-25). The result: a balance sheet that is now approximately 80% cash (~$5.6B of total NAV around $7B equivalent), with the remainder in private fund commitments (Trilantic, TruArc, Prescient Asia funds) and small residuals. NAV per share was EUR 38.54 at June 30, 2025 and is estimated near EUR 47/share post-PIC close. The ADR trades at $30.70 against estimated NAV of ~$47/share equivalent — a ~35% discount on a portfolio that is now overwhelmingly liquid cash.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
This is the rare "event already happened, market hasn't fully repriced" holdco setup. Discounts to NAV in European holdcos are sticky — Reinet historically traded at 50%+ discount, narrowing to 30-35% as the portfolio became more liquid — but the post-PIC composition makes a persistent 35% discount logically hard to defend. When ~80% of your NAV is cash sitting in conservative instruments, the discount cannot be explained by "marks I don't trust" or "operating risk." It is essentially a discount on cash plus a small residual private-fund stake. The structural drivers are well-known: Luxembourg holdco tax inefficiencies, Rupert family majority control (creating capital allocation risk), low-liquidity ADR with no US analyst coverage, and the legitimate fear that the cash will be re-invested into something NAV-destructive rather than returned.

The bull edge is timing-specific. Cash is now visible on the balance sheet, monthly NAV publication is mandatory, and the Rupert family has historically supported buybacks at material discounts (Reinet has bought back stock periodically, and Compagnie Financière Richemont has done large take-out style returns). The plausible base case over 2026: either (a) special dividend / large tender announcement using a meaningful chunk of the PIC proceeds, narrowing the discount toward 20-25%; or (b) a take-private offer by the Rupert family at a ~10-15% premium, which still leaves 20%+ upside from current levels. Even the bear case (Rupert reinvests the cash into more illiquid funds at par) maintains the cash floor — the discount may persist, but downside is genuinely limited by the 80% cash position.

The honest caveat: REINY ADR liquidity is poor. The economically correct way to express this view is via the JSE-listed ordinary shares (RNI.SJ) or the Amsterdam line, where institutional flows can compress the discount. The ADR will follow but with lag and slippage. This is a 12-24 month thesis with a high-probability mid-teens IRR, not a tactical trade.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen will struggle to even pull NAV correctly because Reinet reports in EUR and quotes in ZAR/EUR while the ADR trades in USD. What's invisible from quote screens: (1) the post-PIC cash position dramatically de-risks the discount thesis — this is now 80% cash with a 35% discount, which is mathematically a buy-back-the-cash-at-65-cents trade; (2) the Rupert family's historical preference for capital return when discounts widen; (3) Trilantic and TruArc fund stakes are marked conservatively and have shown realizations above carry-forward marks; and (4) the absence of pension obligations or operating company drag means the discount is essentially pure-play structural, not fundamental.

### CATALYSTS
- **June 2026 — annual results and use-of-proceeds statement:** Likely capital-return announcement.
- **September 2026 — interim results / possible special dividend:** Pattern recognition from Richemont and prior Reinet returns.
- **2026-2027 — possible Rupert family take-private:** The discount-to-cash math invites a controlling-shareholder squeeze-out at a modest premium.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 7/10 |
| Management trust | 8/10 |
| Balance sheet | 10/10 |
| Moat | 6/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 5/10 |
| Valuation | 9/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7/10 |
| Downside protection | 9/10 |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Simplicity | 7/10 |
| **Total** | **75/100** |

---

Cash is now visible on the balance sheet, monthly NAV publication is mandatory, and the Rupert family has historically supported buybacks at material discounts (Reinet has bought back stock periodically, and Compagnie Financière Richemont has done large take-out style returns). The plausible base case over 2026: either (a) special dividend / large tender announcement using a meaningful chunk of the PIC proceeds, narrowing the discount toward 20-25%; or (b) a take-private offer by the Rupert family at a ~10-15% premium, which still leaves 20%+ upside from current levels. Even the bear case (Rupert reinvests the cash into more illiquid funds at par) maintains the cash floor — the discount may persist, but downside is genuinely limited by the 80% cash position.

The honest caveat: REINY ADR liquidity is poor. The economically correct way to express this view is via the JSE-listed ordinary shares (RNI.SJ) or the Amsterdam line, where institutional flows can compress the discount. The ADR will follow but with lag and slippage. This is a 12-24 month thesis with a high-probability mid-teens IRR, not a tactical trade.
REINY.whatScreenCantSee: A screen will struggle to even pull NAV correctly because Reinet reports in EUR and quotes in ZAR/EUR while the ADR trades in USD. What's invisible from quote screens: (1) the post-PIC cash position dramatically de-risks the discount thesis — this is now 80% cash with a 35% discount, which is mathematically a buy-back-the-cash-at-65-cents trade; (2) the Rupert family's historical preference for capital return when discounts widen; (3) Trilantic and TruArc fund stakes are marked conservatively and have shown realizations above carry-forward marks; and (4) the absence of pension obligations or operating company drag means the discount is essentially pure-play structural, not fundamental.

BOIVF.name: Bolloré SE
BOIVF.content:
# 121. BOIVF — Bolloré SE
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~40% discount, May 2026 Cassation ruling | Score: 80/100

### THE SETUP
Bolloré SE is the Vincent Bolloré family's French holdco — a "triple-decker" pyramid (Bolloré → Compagnie de l'Odet → Vivendi → UMG) trading at ~40% NAV discount vs. ~26% historical average, embedding a discount-on-discount on the UMG stake. Two free options: Pershing Square's April 2026 $60B UMG bid (and proposed NYSE listing) crystallizes UMG value at premium, and the May 22, 2026 French Court of Cassation ruling on the Vivendi tender could force a fair-value re-rating of the Vivendi stake.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Bolloré SE (BOIVF on US OTC, BOL on Euronext Paris) is the Bolloré family's flagship holdco. Mcap ~$14.8B at $5.50/share, indicative NAV ~$9.20/share = ~40% discount. Holdings: 29.3% controlling stake in Vivendi SE (Vivendi itself trades at ~50% discount to its own UMG/portfolio NAV — discount-on-discount); 18% direct + ~10% indirect (via Vivendi) total ~28% in Universal Music Group; substantial residual cash from the 2022 Bolloré Logistics divestment to CMA CGM (€4.65B); media stakes in Canal+, Havas, and indirect Lagardère/Editis interests via the Vivendi spin restructuring. Plus a meaningful net cash and equivalents position on balance sheet. Family control via Compagnie de l'Odet (>70% of Bolloré SE).

Bear case: structural French holdco discount has persisted for decades and there's no obvious reason it must close. Triple-decker pyramid (Odet → Bolloré → Vivendi → UMG) means each layer takes a discount, and Vincent Bolloré has shown willingness to use the structure for family-friendly transactions that minorities perceive as unfair (the 2024 Vivendi spin restructure being Exhibit A — and the subject of pending litigation). Court of Cassation could rule against the minority shareholders in May 2026, removing the mandatory tender catalyst entirely. Vincent Bolloré is 73; succession to his son Yannick is in motion but not seamless. UMG itself faces streaming-economics revaluation risk if Spotify/Apple Music renegotiate terms. Pershing's $60B UMG bid is one investor's proposal — not a deal; could fizzle. French withholding tax on dividends is a frictional drag for US holders.

Bull case: this is the cheapest blue-chip holdco in Europe per AGT (Asset Value Investors) and other dedicated holdco specialists. NAV discount at ~40% vs. 26% historical average implies 22% mean-reversion alone before any catalyst. Pershing Square's April 2026 $60B UMG offer (and proposed NYSE re-listing) marks UMG well above current trading levels — Bolloré's effective 28% UMG ownership crystallizes at premium if the deal advances. Court of Cassation hearing May 22, 2026: if Bolloré loses, mandatory tender at fair value re-rates the Vivendi stake (could be 30-50% above current Vivendi price) — pure free option. Bolloré is aggressively buying back shares ("cannibalising structure" in AGT's words) — every buyback collapses the discount and accretes NAV/share faster.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Compagnie de l'Odet pyramid is a tax-optimized vehicle that allows Bolloré family to control >70% of voting rights with <50% economic interest. Ironically, Vincent Bolloré has been collapsing the pyramid via cross-buybacks that concentrate his ownership and reduce the layers — bullish for minorities at every level if they can get out at NAV. The 2022 Bolloré Logistics sale to CMA CGM brought €4.65B in cash that has not yet been fully redeployed; per AGT and similar specialist pieces, that cash is sitting on the balance sheet earning 3-4% short-rate yield while waiting for a Vincent-style asset deal. Vivendi spin litigation: in 2024 Vivendi spun Canal+, Havas and Louis Hachette into separate vehicles via a controversial scheme of arrangement; minority shareholders sued, and the appeal reaches the Court of Cassation May 22, 2026. A loss for Bolloré triggers a mandatory tender for Vivendi at independently-determined fair value — potentially €15-18 vs. current ~€10. Buyback firepower: Bolloré has consistently used 5-10% of its mcap annually on buybacks at deep NAV discounts.

### NAV BREAKDOWN
| Component | $ value (approx) | Note |
|---|---|---|
| 29.3% Vivendi stake | ~$8B | At Vivendi price; itself ~50% discounted |
| 28% effective UMG (direct + via Vivendi) | ~$10-12B | Pershing bid implies $60B UMG → upside |
| Cash + Bolloré Logistics proceeds | ~$5B | €4.65B from CMA CGM deal |
| Other media (Canal+, Havas via Vivendi spins) | ~$2B | |
| Indicative NAV | ~$25B | vs. mcap $14.8B (40% discount) |

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- May 22, 2026 — French Court of Cassation ruling on Vivendi tender
- Pershing Square / UMG transaction milestones (Q2-Q4 2026)
- UMG NYSE re-listing decision and continued Bolloré buybacks

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Triple-decker pyramid, multi-jurisdictional |
| Moat | 8 | UMG = best music IP collection on earth |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | €4.65B+ net cash, low leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 9 | 40% discount + 22% mean reversion |
| Catalyst | 8 | Hard May 22, 2026 court date + Pershing |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 9 | Free options, downside cushioned by buybacks |
| Business Quality | 8 | UMG/Vivendi assets are top-tier |
| Executability | 5 | OTC tape (BOIVF) — use Paris BOL for size |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Court loss + family control overhang |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Music/media less rate-sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **74** | |

PROSY.name: Prosus N.V.
PROSY.content:
# 122. PROSY — Prosus N.V.
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~38% DISCOUNT | Score: 80/100

### THE SETUP

Prosus N.V. is the Amsterdam-listed technology investment vehicle controlled by South Africa's Naspers, holding ~24% of Tencent (HK:0700) plus a portfolio of emerging-market consumer-internet assets. At a current price near $8.95 with indicative NAV of ~$14.50, Prosus trades at a roughly 38% discount to NAV — a structural inefficiency that management has been actively attacking via what is arguably the most sophisticated buyback engine in global large-cap holdcos. Market cap stands near $200B, making this one of the largest "stub-trade" opportunities in public markets.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

The mechanics of the Prosus open-ended buyback are elegant: Prosus periodically sells small slices of its Tencent stake into the market, and uses the cash to repurchase Prosus (and parent Naspers) shares trading at a 38% discount. Every $1 of Tencent sold and $1 of Prosus retired creates ~$0.60 of accretion to NAV-per-share for remaining holders — a self-funding compounding loop. From the program's 2022 launch through early 2024, the holdco discount narrowed by 21 points, unlocking roughly $30B of value and growing NAV-per-share by ~8%. With $200B of market cap and a still-wide 38% discount, the program has years of runway.

The asset stack: ~24% of Tencent (worth roughly $130B at today's ~$540B Tencent market cap), plus owned/controlled e-commerce franchises — iFood (Latin American food delivery, dominant in Brazil), Stack Overflow, eMag (Eastern European e-commerce), Swiggy stake (recently IPO'd in India), iyzico (payments), OLX Group (classifieds), and a substantial net cash position (~$15B). The e-commerce portfolio is genuinely growing — iFood is profitable, Swiggy has IPO'd creating a public mark, and several assets approach IPO-readiness.

The discount persists because: (1) European-listed holdcos with single-asset concentration carry endemic discounts; (2) the perception of China/Tencent regulatory risk weighs on valuations even as Tencent's earnings have stabilized; (3) the e-commerce assets are complex and harder to mark; (4) Tencent ETF substitutes (KraneShares, etc.) offer purer Tencent exposure for traders. But none of those structural factors prevent NAV-per-share compounding via buybacks — they just keep the headline discount sticky while underlying intrinsic value grows.

In 2024 Tencent began making in-kind dividend distributions (paying out shares of Meituan, JD.com), and Prosus has been distributing or monetizing those stakes — adding another vector of intrinsic value crystallization. Naspers (the South African parent) runs an analogous buyback against its discount to Prosus, creating a double-compounding chain.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

A screen sees a foreign ADR with confusing accounting and a single big-tech bet. It does not see the buyback flywheel, the in-kind dividend monetization, the e-commerce optionality (iFood IPO could mark $5–10B), or the parent/sub structural arbitrage between Naspers and Prosus. Most US screens also fail to capture the in-kind Tencent distribution mechanics, which are structurally accretive.

### CATALYSTS

- Continued Tencent-funded buyback (the engine)
- Tencent dividend distributions (in-kind continued)
- Asia EM tech recovery and Tencent multiple expansion
- Potential e-commerce IPOs (iFood, eMag)
- Further Naspers/Prosus structural simplification

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- Circle of competence: Tencent + EM consumer internet — accessible to most investors. PASS.
- Durable moat: Tencent itself (WeChat ecosystem) is one of the great moats globally. STRONG PASS.
- Honest, capable management: Bob van Dijk era left questions; new CEO Fabricio Bloisi is operator-focused; capital allocation under CFO has been excellent. PASS.
- Margin of safety: Buying Tencent + cash + e-commerce at 62 cents on the dollar. STRONG PASS.
- Predictable cash flows: Tencent's earnings are durable; e-commerce mixed but improving. PASS.
- Skin in the game: Naspers control; long-term oriented. PASS.

Score 80/100 reflects: best-in-class holdco buyback mechanics, deep discount, embedded blue-chip asset, multiple catalyst paths. Discount may not close — but doesn't need to for compounding to work.

---
