GBLBF.name: Groupe Bruxelles Lambert SA
GBLBF.content:
# 123. GBLBF — Groupe Bruxelles Lambert SA
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~31% DISCOUNT TO NAV | Score: 74/100

### THE SETUP
Belgian Frère/Desmarais family holdco trading at roughly a 31% discount to a transparent, mostly-listed NAV — while management has accelerated portfolio rotation (EUR 1.7B sold to Carlyle AlpInvest at only a 9% discount, well inside GBL's own discount) and is buying back stock. Classic European holdco discount with self-help finally arriving.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Groupe Bruxelles Lambert (Euronext Brussels: GBLB; OTC ADR: GBLBF) is the Belgian listed vehicle of the Frère family (consolidated with the Desmarais Power Corp side via the legacy Pargesa structure). It owns a portfolio of European blue-chip listed minorities — Pernod Ricard, Adidas, Imerys, SGS, Holcim — and a growing private/alternative bucket including Affidea (private healthcare), Canyon Bicycles, Sanoptis (dental), and the Sienna Investment Managers alt-asset platform. NAV per share was reported at roughly EUR 113-121 against a current share price near EUR 80 (USD 84 ADR), implying a ~31% discount that is wide even by holdco standards.

The bear case is gravitational: family-controlled European holdcos almost never close their NAV discount, and several of GBL's largest listed positions are themselves under pressure. Adidas has been volatile post-Yeezy. Pernod Ricard is in a multi-year premium-spirits down-cycle as Chinese demand weakens. Holcim is cyclical. Imerys is structurally challenged in industrial minerals. The private bucket is opaque and arguably marked optimistically. And the Sienna alt-platform is a multi-year build that consumes capital before producing it. The discount has been wide for two decades; expecting it to close is the kind of mean-reversion bet that has cost value investors a lot of money in European holdcos (c.f. Pargesa, Investor AB, Industrivärden).

The bull case is that this iteration of GBL is acting differently. CEO Sophie Lambin has accelerated divestments — the Carlyle AlpInvest secondary in late 2025/early 2026 sold roughly EUR 1.7B of NAV at only a ~9% discount, dramatically inside GBL's own 31% trading discount, and recycled proceeds into share buybacks. Buying back your own stock at 0.69x NAV with cash raised at 0.91x NAV is one of the cleanest accretion trades in capital allocation. Continued private monetization plus Sienna IPO/sale optionality plus an Adidas exit on any rally would compound NAV per share even if the discount never narrows.

### NAV BREAKDOWN (approx., late 2025 / early 2026)
| Holding | Stake | Approx. EUR Value | % of NAV |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pernod Ricard | ~7% | ~2.0B | ~10% |
| Adidas | ~3.5% | ~1.5B | ~8% |
| SGS | ~19% | ~3.5B | ~18% |
| Holcim | ~7% | ~3.5B | ~18% |
| Imerys | ~54% | ~1.5B | ~8% |
| Webhelp/Concentrix residual | — | ~0.5B | ~3% |
| Private (Affidea, Canyon, Sanoptis, etc.) | various | ~4.5B | ~23% |
| Sienna IM (alt platform) | 100% | ~1.5B | ~8% |
| Net cash / treasury / other | — | ~0.5B | ~4% |
| **Gross NAV** | | **~19B** | **100%** |
| Share count (~134M post-buyback) → NAV/share | | | **~EUR 113-121** |

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
GBL's accelerated treasury share program is structurally accretive but doesn't show up in a P/E screen because the holdco doesn't earn an operating P/E. The Carlyle AlpInvest secondary deserves attention from the November 2025 Capital Markets presentation — selling EUR 1.7B at a 9% discount and using proceeds to buy back stock at a 31% discount is per-share NAV-accretive on the order of 3-4% in a single transaction. Also note that the family holding (via Power Corp / Desmarais and Frère Bourgeois) is over 50% — there is no takeover scenario, only self-driven discount narrowing.

### CATALYSTS
- 1H 2026 — continued private portfolio monetization
- Quarterly buyback updates (treasury share authorization)
- Possible Sienna IM IPO or strategic sale (multi-year)
- Adidas exit on any sustained rally
- Portfolio simplification announcements

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Listed-portfolio NAV is transparent |
| Moat | 5 | Family scale + access to private deals |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Net cash, ~EUR 0.5B liquidity |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | 31% NAV discount, 0.69x P/NAV |
| Catalyst | 7 | Buyback + monetization arc |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Look-through to listed equities at discount |
| Business Quality | 7 | Diversified blue-chip economics |
| Executability | 5 | OTC ADR — wider spread, low US volume |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | Discount may not close, but earnings still flow |
| Macro Independence | 6 | European cyclical exposure (Holcim, Imerys, Adidas) |
| **TOTAL** | **74** | Best long-duration name in batch |

---

SFTBF.name: Sofina S.A.
SFTBF.content:
# 124. SFTBF — Sofina S.A.
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~25% discount to indicative NAV | Score: 74/100

### THE SETUP
Sofina is a 130+ year-old Belgian listed holding company controlled by the Boël family — one of the older industrial dynasties in Belgium, with a track record of compounding NAV at roughly 13% annually over three decades. Unlike a traditional European holdco that owns one or two industrial flagships, Sofina runs a diversified late-stage venture and growth-equity book organized in three pillars: Long-term Minority Investments (~50% of NAV — direct minority stakes in private and listed growth companies), Sofina Growth (~28% — late-stage tech), and Sofina Private Funds Investment (~22% — LP positions in top-decile VC/PE managers including Sequoia, Insight Partners, and a roster of Asian growth funds). Indicative NAV per share at H2 2025 sat around EUR 297–298. Today's price (~EUR 240 / ~$240 USD on the SFTBF pink sheet) implies roughly a 25% discount to NAV — versus only 6% in mid-2025 and 17% at year-end 2025. This is one of the wider NAV discounts Sofina has traded at in the last decade and reflects a confluence of overhangs rather than any change in management quality.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
What you're buying at today's discount is a high-quality, relationship-driven late-stage venture book at roughly 75 cents on the euro, with a Belgian family operator that has decades of disciplined compounding behind it. The portfolio includes residual exposure to Byju's (already heavily written down), Stripe (multi-year IPO candidate), ByteDance (held indirectly via several top-tier VC funds), Coursera, Mango, Aleph Farms, plus a diversified LP book in Sequoia, Insight, and other premier managers. The widening of the discount from 6% to 25% over nine months is not driven by NAV deterioration — H2 2025 NAV actually rose ~1% — but by sentiment: skepticism on private-company marks generally, China-internet exposure (ByteDance), Byju's writedown headlines, Belgian small-cap illiquidity, and family-control discount. None of those reasons is permanent. Sofina is actively repurchasing shares below NAV (a direct per-share NAV accretion), and even modestly conservative private marks would still leave the implied discount at >20%. The asymmetric bet is straightforward: at 13% historical NAV CAGR plus a 25% discount that could partially close on any single liquidity event (Stripe IPO, ByteDance secondary), total returns of 20%+ annualized are mathematically plausible without heroic assumptions.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three things US screens miss entirely. First, SFTBF on US pink sheets is a thin echo of the much more liquid Brussels SOF.BR listing — you're buying the same security but US screens may show stale quotes and zero institutional ownership, falsely flagging it as "uninvestable." Second, the NAV calculation is published semi-annually with a lag, so the headline "discount" you see is calculated against a stale NAV; in periods when private valuations have likely moved (Stripe last secondary at $91B+, Insight Partners' marks tightening), the look-through discount is even wider than the headline. Third, Sofina runs a permanent-capital model — no LP redemption pressure means they can be patient through full venture cycles, the way Berkshire is patient through equity cycles. Most peer European holdcos (Investor AB, Wendel) own listed industrial blue chips; Sofina's edge is its access to top-decile private growth managers.

### CATALYSTS
- Stripe IPO (long expected; would mark up Sofina's indirect stake materially)
- ByteDance partial monetization, secondary, or eventual IPO
- Continued at-discount buybacks (every one is per-share NAV-accretive)
- H1 2026 NAV update with stable or rising private marks
- NAV-discount mean reversion toward the historic 10–15% range
- Possible upgrade to MSCI Belgium or other index inclusion as float dynamics normalize

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: Mostly — it's a permanent-capital growth-equity book, but the underlying portfolio requires ongoing diligence on private marks
- Durable moat: Yes — multi-decade relationships with top VC/PE managers and direct deal access are not easily replicated
- Honest, capable management: Yes — Boël family has compounded NAV at ~13% over 30+ years; disciplined buyback below NAV
- Bought at fair price: Yes — ~75 cents on the euro for a quality late-stage portfolio is one of the wider discounts in a decade
- Margin of safety: Strong — discount provides cushion; even modest private-mark write-downs leave look-through value above current price
- Key risks: Further private-mark erosion, prolonged closure of IPO window, Belgian holdco discount may stay wide longer than expected, USD/EUR FX

---

Sources: Sofina H2 2025 NAV update (Investing.com), GuruFocus discount analysis, Stock Analysis EBR:SOF page. Score reflects high quality of underlying portfolio and operator, balanced against well-known holdco-discount stickiness.

---
SFTBF.analystSummary: What you're buying at today's discount is a high-quality, relationship-driven late-stage venture book at roughly 75 cents on the euro, with a Belgian family operator that has decades of disciplined compounding behind it. The portfolio includes residual exposure to Byju's (already heavily written down), Stripe (multi-year IPO candidate), ByteDance (held indirectly via several top-tier VC funds), Coursera, Mango, Aleph Farms, plus a diversified LP book in Sequoia, Insight, and other premier managers. The widening of the discount from 6% to 25% over nine months is not driven by NAV deterioration — H2 2025 NAV actually rose ~1% — but by sentiment: skepticism on private-company marks generally, China-internet exposure (ByteDance), Byju's writedown headlines, Belgian small-cap illiquidity, and family-control discount. None of those reasons is permanent. Sofina is actively repurchasing shares below NAV (a direct per-share NAV accretion), and even modestly conservative private marks would still leave the implied discount at >20%. The asymmetric bet is straightforward: at 13% historical NAV CAGR plus a 25% discount that could partially close on any single liquidity event (Stripe IPO, ByteDance secondary), total returns of 20%+ annualized are mathematically plausible without heroic assumptions.
SFTBF.whatScreenCantSee: Three things US screens miss entirely. First, SFTBF on US pink sheets is a thin echo of the much more liquid Brussels SOF.BR listing — you're buying the same security but US screens may show stale quotes and zero institutional ownership, falsely flagging it as "uninvestable." Second, the NAV calculation is published semi-annually with a lag, so the headline "discount" you see is calculated against a stale NAV; in periods when private valuations have likely moved (Stripe last secondary at $91B+, Insight Partners' marks tightening), the look-through discount is even wider than the headline. Third, Sofina runs a permanent-capital model — no LP redemption pressure means they can be patient through full venture cycles, the way Berkshire is patient through equity cycles. Most peer European holdcos (Investor AB, Wendel) own listed industrial blue chips; Sofina's edge is its access to top-decile private growth managers.

CODI.name: Compass Diversified Holdings
CODI.content:
# 125. CODI — Compass Diversified Holdings
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~53% Discount, Lugano Restatement Overhang | Score: 75/100

### THE SETUP
Compass Diversified is a Berkshire-style operating holdco that owns roughly a dozen mid-market niche-leader subsidiaries. After accounting irregularities at the Lugano Diamonds subsidiary in 2025 forced a restatement, CEO replacement, and SEC inquiry, the equity collapsed and now trades at $10.40 versus a stated indicative NAV of ~$22 — a 53% discount even after writing Lugano down to zero.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
CODI owns a portfolio of branded-consumer and industrial businesses with collective adjusted EBITDA of roughly $430M+. Branded-consumer holdings include BOA Technology (the Boa lacing system used in athletic and outdoor footwear), 5.11 Tactical (tactical apparel and gear), Marucci Sports (premium baseball bats), Velocity Outdoor (just sold), Ergobaby (baby carriers), PrimaLoft (synthetic insulation), and Lugano Diamonds (under restatement). Industrials include Altor Solutions (cold-chain packaging), Sterno, Arnold Magnetics, and Honey Pot Co. The model is to buy private mid-market businesses in the $200M-$2B range, hold them, and pay a quarterly distribution to shareholders.

The bear case is the overhang. Lugano was the highest-growth, highest-margin subsidiary by reported numbers — and those numbers turned out to be inflated. SEC investigation is ongoing and could result in fines, restated financials going further back, or executive accountability actions. The distribution was suspended/cut, removing the income-buyer base. HoldCo-level debt sits around $1.8B, and the conversion from a partnership (K-1) to a C-corp added tax friction. New leadership is unproven. Even excluding Lugano, the remaining EBITDA contribution depends on consumer health for several of the largest pieces (5.11, Marucci, Ergobaby) — all sensitive to discretionary spend.

The bull case is the math. Strip Lugano entirely from the model, assume ~$330M of clean EBITDA from the rest, apply 8x — a defensible multiple for a basket of branded niche leaders — and you get ~$2.6B EV. Net of HoldCo debt, equity value clears the current $781M cap with room to spare. BOA, 5.11, and Marucci alone arguably justify the entire market cap. The Velocity divestiture (announced March 2026) demonstrates active portfolio rationalization, generates cash to delever, and proves the SOTP exercise works in real life. New CEO + refreshed board have signaled intent to crystallize value through additional sales. Insider buying has appeared at recent prices.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen can't see segment-level economics that aren't disclosed. CODI reports adjusted EBITDA by segment in the 10-Q, but valuable detail — like BOA's licensing-vs-product mix, 5.11's government-channel revenue, or Marucci's gross margin progression — sits in MD&A footnotes and earnings call transcripts. The Velocity sale price (when disclosed) becomes a real-money read on what mid-market private buyers will pay for CODI's portfolio companies, calibrating the SOTP. Also: HoldCo debt covenants and any Lugano-related representation/warranty exposure — buried in credit-agreement amendments — could constrain capital allocation if the restatement worsens.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Lugano restated financials and SEC resolution — expected H2 2026
- Additional subsidiary divestitures following Velocity sale (March 2026)
- Reinstatement of distribution following balance-sheet clean-up

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Diversified holdco; complex but tractable |
| Moat | 6 | Individual subsidiaries have brand/IP moats |
| Balance Sheet | 4 | $1.8B HoldCo debt, distribution cut |
| Margin of Safety | 9 | 53% NAV discount per management mark |
| Catalyst | 7 | Divestitures, SEC resolution, distribution restoration |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | $10 down to $7 / up to $18 |
| Business Quality | 6 | $430M EBITDA across niche leaders |
| Executability | 7 | $781M mcap; reasonable liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | More accounting issues, debt covenant trip |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Consumer-discretionary heavy |
| **TOTAL** | **64** | Classic post-blowup NAV discount; selective conviction |

---

BOC.name: Boston Omaha Corporation
BOC.content:
# 126. BOC — Boston Omaha Corporation
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~30% discount to tangible book, $388M mcap | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
Boston Omaha is Adam Peterson's (Buffett's grand-nephew via Magnolia Capital) micro-cap holdco trading at $12.53 vs. ~$18 indicative tangible book — a ~30% discount to a portfolio of cash-generative billboard assets, rural fiber broadband, surety insurance, an asset-management platform and a stake in Sky Harbour. New buyback authorized; closely held; sentiment bombed-out after co-CEO departure. The kind of small, ugly, ignored compounder Munger said you find by sitting alone in a room.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Boston Omaha (BOC) is a $388M-mcap holdco operating four segments: (1) Link Media Outdoor — the fourth-largest US billboard operator behind Lamar/Outfront/Clear Channel with ~3,500 billboards; (2) AireBeam + FibAir rural fiber broadband (>$30M revenue, growing); (3) General Indemnity Group — surety insurance growing 20%+ annually; (4) Boston Omaha Asset Management (BOAM), managing third-party LP capital across real estate, sports and a minority interest in Sky Harbour (private aviation hangars). Cash and securities ~$200M+. ~30.7M shares outstanding. Indicative NAV/share ~$18 (per the bull thesis circulating in Q1 2026 Nasdaq/Motley Fool coverage), price $12.53 → ~30% discount. Operating segments combined produce ~$50M EBITDA, valued at 10x = $500M equity vs. $388M mcap before adjusting for cash and Sky Harbour stake.

Bear case: governance scar tissue. Co-CEO Alex Rozek departed April 2024, leaving Adam Peterson as sole CEO — the market has not forgiven the resulting ambiguity around capital allocation and segment focus. March 2026 Motley Fool piece labeled it a "value trap" citing weak management execution and slow capital deployment. Sky Harbour stake creates equity-mark volatility (it's a public stock; quarterly mark-to-market hits BOC's earnings). Billboards are a slow-growth, ad-cyclical business; rural fiber is capex-heavy and faces BEAD subsidy delays. Surety insurance is sub-scale vs. The Hartford/Travelers. ~$1M daily volume = small-cap illiquidity that punishes any forced exit. The Berkshire-junior comparisons set unrealistic expectations.

Bull case: tangible book of ~$18/share is mostly hard assets (billboard structures with permanent permits, fiber buried in the ground, regulated insurance capital, public-mark Sky Harbour shares, T-bills). At $12.53, you're paying 70 cents on the dollar for a portfolio that compounds at mid-teens. New buyback authorization is meaningful — at $12.53 with ~30.7M shares and $200M+ cash, management can shrink the share count materially if Peterson chooses to. Median analyst PT $18 = 44% upside to fair value before any operating beat. Catalysts: broadband EBITDA inflection (negative → positive as subscriber base scales), Sky Harbour value crystallization, BOAM AUM growth providing high-margin third-party fees, and accretive M&A using Peterson's underwriting discipline.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K segment disclosures show Link Media's billboard portfolio is about 70% digital-convertible — meaning the 3,500 boards have an embedded "digital upgrade" optionality that triples revenue per face when converted. Roughly 20% has already been converted; the remaining runway is 5-7 years of organic capex-funded growth at 15-20% IRRs. Sky Harbour stake (BOC owns ~10-15% via SHG) is marked at quoted price, but the underlying private-aviation hangar business has 50%+ EBITDA margins and a 20-year lease book — likely undervalued at quoted price. Adam Peterson's Magnolia Capital owns ~17% of BOC personally — every dollar of buyback shrinks his denominator while he keeps absolute holdings. Boston Omaha Asset Management (BOAM) is a hidden compounder: it earns 1-2% management fees on outside LP capital with near-zero incremental cost, and AUM disclosure has been climbing.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Buyback execution announcements in Q1-Q2 2026
- Broadband segment EBITDA crossover (negative → positive) by year-end 2026
- BOAM AUM milestone disclosure / new fund close

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Four-segment holdco, traceable |
| Moat | 6 | Billboard permits + fiber routes |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | $200M+ cash, low leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | 30% discount to tangible book |
| Catalyst | 5 | Slow-burn; no hard date |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | $18 PT vs. ~$10 downside |
| Business Quality | 6 | Mixed; broadband still negative |
| Executability | 4 | $388M mcap, ~$1M ADV |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Value trap risk, slow deployment |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Mostly insulated from rates |
| **TOTAL** | **64** | |

---

TRC.name: Tejon Ranch Co.
TRC.content:
# 129. TRC — Tejon Ranch Co.
## HOLDCO | NAV DISCOUNT | Score: 74/100

### THE SETUP

Tejon Ranch Co. is one of the most unusual real-estate holdcos on a US exchange: a single, contiguous 270,000-acre ranch sixty miles north of Los Angeles, straddling the Grapevine on Interstate 5. The land bank has been in the same corporate hands since the 1840s and remains overwhelmingly raw, but it sits in the path of California's housing-starved southern megalopolis. Stock trades around $19.36 versus an indicative NAV closer to $32, a roughly 39% discount. Market cap is ~$510M; book is ~0.75x. There is modest debt, a cash cushion north of $80M, and four named development projects in various stages: Mountain Village (residential, entitled), Centennial (a 19,333-home community whose entitlements were partially reset by a June 2025 California Court of Appeal ruling), Grapevine, and the Tejon Ranch Commerce Center (an industrial park with multi-million-square-foot build-out potential along I-5). Add agriculture, mineral and water royalties, and a Hard Rock Hotel & Casino joint-venture opening in 2026, and the equity is essentially a long-dated, partially-monetizing California land option.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

The bull case is geometric, not arithmetic. Even at a deliberately conservative $4-5K/acre, 270,000 raw California acres alone implies more than $1B of land value — twice the equity market cap. Entitled or pre-entitled acres in the Mountain Village/Centennial footprint trade in published comps at 10-20x raw, which is where the $32 NAV print comes from and where the optionality lives. The Hard Rock Casino opening in 2026 and Terra Vista's lease-up are real, datable catalysts that demonstrate Tejon can actually move land from "raw" to "cash-flowing." Bulldog Investors has been in the boardroom and pushed a strategic-review process that concluded mid-2025; the new direction explicitly contemplates faster monetization. Risks are equally clear: Centennial litigation reset the timeline by years, California land politics is famously hostile, and TRC has worn a "value trap" label for a decade because corporate G&A absorbs operating cash while the land slowly appreciates. The casino JV economics are modest in the near term. At ~$510M cap, a take-private bid by an infrastructure or real-estate platform is plausible but not necessary — even an orderly monetization at the activist's pace closes most of the discount. This is a sleep-well-at-night option on California demographics with a well-credentialed activist as catalyst.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

Quant screens hate Tejon. Reported earnings are negligible to negative because operating losses on the development side run through the income statement while land appreciation never does — the carrying value of the ranch on the books is essentially historical cost from the 19th century. That makes P/E and ROE meaningless and triggers automatic "value trap" exclusions. A discount-to-NAV screen catches it, but the NAV inputs are themselves judgmental (Centennial entitlement risk, water-rights valuation, and Commerce Center build-out are all soft numbers). The hidden asset most screens miss is the water portfolio: state-water-project allocations and groundwater rights in a thirsty state are increasingly the constraint on California development and are carried at trivial book. The other hidden asset is contiguity — 270,000 unbroken acres sixty miles from LA cannot be reassembled. Finally, the casino JV is structured as a royalty-and-rent stream that won't fully show up until 2027 run-rate.

### CATALYSTS

- Hard Rock Hotel & Casino opening 2026 (royalty/rent stream begins)
- Centennial re-entitlement decision late 2026
- Continued Commerce Center build-to-suit announcements
- Activist-driven asset sale, JV, or special distribution
- Possible take-private by an infrastructure or real-estate platform

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- Circle of competence: Yes — California land economics are knowable.
- Durable advantage: Yes — irreplaceable contiguous footprint at the I-5 chokepoint.
- Honest, able management: Mixed historically; activist board refresh improves the read.
- Margin of safety: Yes — ~39% discount with real-asset floor, not earnings extrapolation.
- Avoid stupidity: Litigation overhang is real; sizing must respect a multi-year holding period.

Verdict: a high-quality, low-multiple-of-NAV way to own a piece of Southern California's growth path with an activist catalyst. Score reflects discount and asset quality, offset by slow monetization clock and entitlement risk.

---

ITOCY.name: ITOCHU Corporation (ADR)
ITOCY.content:
# 130. ITOCY — ITOCHU Corporation (ADR)
## HOLDCO | ~25-30% NAV DISCOUNT + BERKSHIRE 10.07% | Score: 74/100

### THE SETUP
ITOCHU is the highest-quality of the five Japanese sogo shosha Berkshire Hathaway has been quietly accumulating since 2020. Berkshire crossed 10% in March 2026 (10.07%, up from 9.54% — disclosed via a National Indemnity vehicle) and Buffett has signaled he is "still buying." Morningstar's fair value implies 23–36% upside across the trading houses, with ITOCHU best-in-class on ROE and most insulated from commodity cycles thanks to FamilyMart and a consumer-tilted portfolio.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
ITOCHU operates eight divisions — Textile, Machinery, Metals & Minerals, Energy & Chemicals, Food (FamilyMart 50%+, Dole), General Products & Realty, ICT & Financial, and "The 8th Company" (consumer focus). It is the only top-tier sogo shosha with under-indexed resource exposure, generating earnings stability that peers Mitsubishi/Mitsui/Sumitomo/Marubeni cannot match. Berkshire's stake is now 10.07% (up from 9.54% — disclosed in a March 2026 ITOCHU release) and the five-house aggregate Berkshire holding has topped $30B. Total shareholder yield (dividend + buyback) is roughly 4.32% with a 1.4 trillion-yen buyback authorization across 2024-25.

The bear narrative: trading houses are opaque conglomerates, Japanese governance lags Western standards even after the Tokyo Stock Exchange "PBR<1" reform campaign, JPY/USD volatility introduces FX risk for ADR holders, and the SOTP discount has persisted for years — there is no obvious catalyst to close it. ADR liquidity (ITOCY) is thinner than the Tokyo line. Resource exposure, though under-indexed, is still material — a global commodity downturn would compress earnings. Management's payout ratio is around 26% TTM; capital return is meaningful but not aggressive.

The bull thesis stacks: (1) Berkshire endorsement at 10%+ with stated intent to keep buying; (2) Morningstar fair value implying 23-36% upside; (3) FamilyMart take-private (2020) showed management's willingness to monetize SOTP; (4) Dole and other consumer carve-outs are plausible follow-ons; (5) the TSE PBR-under-1 campaign is forcing larger buybacks across the sector; (6) yen normalization would mechanically lift the ADR. ITOCHU is a quality compounder at a HoldCo discount with a Hall-of-Fame anchor shareholder.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
ADR investors miss the disclosure that Berkshire holds via National Indemnity — the March 2, 2026 ITOCHU press release confirms the 10.07% threshold breach and the regulatory filing path. Most US screens map ITOCY to "diversified financials" or "trading conglomerate," missing that FamilyMart (Japan's #2 convenience chain at 16,000+ stores) is consolidated inside the Food division and would re-rate dramatically as a standalone retail comparable. The sogo-shosha business model — earning from minority stakes, captive financing, off-take rights, and trading margin — does not appear cleanly in standard ROIC / ROE screens, which under-state economic returns by capitalizing the wrong base.

### CATALYSTS
- Continued Berkshire accumulation (next disclosure if Berkshire crosses 11%)
- FY26 results May 2026 — buyback pace + dividend hike
- Possible consumer carve-out (Dole IPO / FamilyMart partial)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Item | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 8/10 |
| Management | 8/10 |
| Balance sheet | 7/10 |
| Moat | 7/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 7/10 |
| Free-cash conversion | 7/10 |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7/10 |
| Margin of safety | 8/10 |
| Asymmetry | 8/10 |
| **Total** | **74/100** |

---

ITOCHU is a quality compounder at a HoldCo discount with a Hall-of-Fame anchor shareholder.

Most US screens map ITOCY to "diversified financials" or "trading conglomerate," missing that FamilyMart (Japan's #2 convenience chain at 16,000+ stores) is consolidated inside the Food division and would re-rate dramatically as a standalone retail comparable. The sogo-shosha business model — earning from minority stakes, captive financing, off-take rights, and trading margin — does not appear cleanly in standard ROIC / ROE screens, which under-state economic returns by capitalizing the wrong base.

MSBHF.name: Mitsubishi Corporation
MSBHF.content:
# 131. MSBHF — Mitsubishi Corporation
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | NAV Discount ~25% | Score: 80/100

### THE SETUP
Buffett's largest sogo shosha holding (Berkshire just lifted to 10.23%) trades at roughly 25% discount to Morningstar fair-value SOTP. Aggressive buybacks, the $5.2B Aethon shale acquisition, and the TSE "PBR<1" reform initiative are converging — Western analysts still misprice the model.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Mitsubishi Corporation is the largest of Japan's five sogo shosha (general trading houses) and Buffett's largest sogo shosha holding inside Berkshire Hathaway. Through ten business groups, MC owns or operates positions across natural gas / LNG, mineral resources (notably copper via the Anglo American JV), industrial materials, automotive (50% of Mitsubishi Motors), food, infrastructure, and consumer/urban development (including 50% of Lawson convenience-store chain alongside KDDI). At ~$35.10 per ADR (8058.T in Tokyo) versus indicative NAV near $47, the discount is roughly 25%, with Berkshire's recent step-up to 10.23% providing institutional validation.

The bear case rests on commodity cyclicality and structural Japan-discount stickiness. MC's resource-heavy mix means earnings re-rate down with copper, met coal, and LNG cycles. Western analysts struggle with the sogo shosha model — equity-method investments dominate the income statement, segment disclosure is dense, and JPY translation drags reported USD figures. Regulatory and political risk in resource-extraction geographies (Chile, Australia, Russia historically) is real. The Japan listing premium may not close as fast as the bull case implies; the TSE "PBR<1" campaign is moral suasion, not a binding requirement.

The bull case is conviction-grade. Berkshire's ongoing accumulation past 10% — and Buffett's repeated public admiration for the sogo shosha model — is the strongest possible third-party validation. The Aethon shale acquisition (~$5.2B, closing 2026) demonstrates the capital-deployment muscle Western generalists discount. Management's MC Shared Value 2024 mid-term plan commits to 9–10% ROE and aggressive buybacks (1T+ JPY year-to-date). Lawson's 2024 take-private with KDDI cleaned up a noisy quasi-listed asset. Catalysts are stacked through 2026.

### NAV BREAKDOWN

| Segment | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Gas / LNG | Long-position LNG, upstream | Cash flow anchor |
| Mineral Resources | Copper (Anglo JV), met coal, iron ore | Most cyclical |
| Industrial Materials & Infrastructure | Steel, chemicals, plant EPC | Steady |
| Petroleum & Chemicals | Refining/trading | Margin-sensitive |
| Automotive & Mobility | 50% Mitsubishi Motors JV | Equity-method |
| Food Industry | Norway salmon (Cermaq), grains, food trading | Defensive |
| Consumer Industry | 50% Lawson (with KDDI), retail | Now private |
| Power Solution | IPP, renewables | Growth |
| Urban Development | Real estate | Embedded value |
| Aethon shale (2026) | $5.2B US gas acquisition | New US exposure |

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Annual Securities Report (Yuho) breaks out segment equity-method earnings and the unrealized gains on the marketable equity portfolio in a way the consolidated headlines hide; reported book value materially understates fair value because resource concessions and equity stakes are carried at cost. The 1T+ JPY YTD buyback announcement and the explicit MC Shared Value 2024 commitment to "progressive dividends" are footnoted commitments, not headline numbers. The Aethon transaction prospectus, when filed, will show standalone shale economics that Western screens will ignore for at least one full reporting cycle.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **Berkshire 13F filings 2026** — continued accumulation past 10.23%.
2. **2026 H2 Aethon close** — first quarter of consolidated US shale earnings.
3. **2027 MC Shared Value mid-term plan refresh** — likely raised buyback authorization.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality (moat / scale) | 8 |
| Management track record | 9 |
| Capital allocation discipline | 9 |
| Balance sheet strength | 9 |
| Pricing power | 6 |
| Reinvestment runway | 8 |
| Margin of safety | 8 |
| Catalyst clarity | 8 |
| Insider/owner alignment | 8 |
| Downside protection | 8 |
| **TOTAL** | **81/100** |

---

POW.TO.name: Power Corporation of Canada
POW.TO.content:
# 132. POW.TO — Power Corporation of Canada
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~20% DISCOUNT | Score: 76/100

### THE SETUP

Power Corporation is the Desmarais family's Montreal-based holding company, structured as a multi-decade compounder built around insurance, asset management, and a now-emerging digital-wealth franchise. Trading near C$73, with NAV indicatively around C$92, POW currently trades at roughly a 20% discount to NAV — meaningfully tighter than the historical 30% but still wide enough to offer a margin-of-safety entry into a portfolio of high-quality regulated financials. Market cap is ~US$31.7B. The asset stack: 66.9% of Great-West Lifeco (TSX:GWO), 62.1% of IGM Financial (TSX:IGM), 83.6% of privately-held Wealthsimple, and indirect stakes in Europe's GBL, Pargesa, Sagard alternative-asset platform, and Power Sustainable.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

The Desmarais family's prior "triple-decker" structure (Power Financial wrapped inside Power Corp) was simplified in 2020, removing one layer of holdco discount. Yet the discount has remained sticky for three reasons: (1) Great-West Life is a conservative, lower-multiple insurer that drags blended valuations; (2) family voting control via multiple voting shares limits any forced break-up optionality; and (3) the most valuable hidden asset — Wealthsimple — is private and conservatively marked. Wealthsimple is the embedded growth call option: Canada's leading digital broker and wealth manager, profitable in 2024, with a private valuation of ~C$15B (POW's 83.6% look-through ~C$12.5B). Comparable public fintechs (Robinhood at ~25x earnings, Interactive Brokers at premium multiples) suggest Wealthsimple alone could be worth C$15–25B in an IPO window.

POW's capital allocation under Jeffrey Orr (and now successor leadership) has tilted aggressively toward buybacks at NAV-discounted prices, which mechanically grows look-through NAV per share. The dividend yields ~5%+, the holdco simplification continues, and Sagard's alt-asset AUM is scaling — adding a fee-stream that didn't exist a decade ago. The combination of (a) buying $1 of NAV for $0.80, (b) a 5% dividend, (c) underlying GWL/IGM earnings growth, and (d) an embedded Wealthsimple monetization catalyst, makes POW one of the cleaner risk-adjusted compounders in Canadian large-cap.

The pushback: Canadian holdco discounts are stubbornly persistent (Onex, Fairfax, Brookfield holdcos all carry them), the family will never relinquish control, and Great-West Life's exposure to UK/Irish/Putnam/Empower competes in a brutal asset-management margin environment. Wealthsimple monetization could be years away — or never (the family may prefer to compound it privately).

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

A screen sees a Canadian financial holdco yielding ~5%, with mid-single-digit earnings growth and a P/E around 10x. It does not see: (a) Wealthsimple — invisible at book value but worth multiples of carrying cost; (b) the buyback cadence that mechanically narrows discount; (c) Sagard as a future fee-engine; (d) the Desmarais family's century-long capital allocation track record (Paul Desmarais Sr. built one of the great Canadian compounder franchises). The look-through quality of the underlying assets is materially better than the SOTP arithmetic suggests.

### CATALYSTS

- Wealthsimple IPO or partial monetization (the big one)
- GWL/IGM earnings growth and dividend hikes
- Sagard alt-asset AUM scaling past C$25B
- Continued buyback acceleration at discount
- Further holdco simplification announcements

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- Circle of competence: Insurance, wealth management, asset management — well-documented. PASS.
- Durable moat: Scale, regulatory licenses, distribution, brand (GWL is one of the world's largest insurers). PASS.
- Honest, capable management: Desmarais family stewardship spans 60+ years; capital allocation track record is excellent. STRONG PASS.
- Margin of safety: 20% discount to NAV plus underlying conservative marks; double-margin. PASS.
- Predictable cash flows: GWL, IGM, dividends — yes. PASS.
- Skin in the game: Family controls; aligned with long-duration shareholders. PASS.

Score 76/100 reflects: high-quality holdco at attractive discount, embedded Wealthsimple optionality, family stewardship, and durable cash flows. Discount may be sticky but downside is well-protected.

---

ONEX.TO.name: Onex Corporation
ONEX.TO.content:
# 133. ONEX.TO — Onex Corporation
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | ~27% Discount to NAV | Score: 76/100

### THE SETUP
Canadian alternative-asset / private-equity holdco trading at ~$105.72 vs. an indicative $145 NAV — a 27% discount that management itself has called "anomalous." Aggressive buybacks at the discount, a CEO transition completed (Bobby Le Blanc replaced founder Gerry Schwartz), and $50B+ AUM platform with multiple monetization catalysts pending.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Onex is one of Canada's two flagship alternative-asset platforms (alongside Brookfield-adjacent vehicles), founded by Gerry Schwartz in 1984 and now managed by Bobby Le Blanc (CEO since 2024). The business is a hybrid: a fee-bearing asset-management franchise (Onex Partners private equity funds I-VI, ~$25B Onex Credit CLO/structured-credit platform, Gluskin Sheff high-net-worth wealth management) plus a $5-6B+ balance-sheet portfolio of legacy PE positions (Parkdean Resorts, Save-A-Lot, Convex Insurance, Wealth Enhancement Group, residual Ryan Specialty exposure). Total AUM exceeds $50B. The company explicitly publishes an "indicative NAV per share" each quarter.

The bear case is structural: Canadian holdcos persistently trade at 20-40% discounts to NAV due to founder-controlled multiple-voting share structures, illiquidity vs. U.S. peers, currency translation friction for U.S. investors, and the perennial PE-discount-skepticism — "if you mark your private positions to the public market, the discount disappears." Gluskin Sheff has had write-downs and AUM pressure in recent cycles. Onex Credit's CLO equity tranches are sensitive to credit-cycle stress. The PE realization machine is working but slow — $8B of realizations across funds in 2025 ($800M to Onex's balance sheet) is impressive but doesn't immediately re-rate the holdco multiple. Schwartz still controls voting, so the discount has structural floor.

The bull case is the buyback math: at a 27% discount, every share repurchased creates ~37 cents of NAV-per-share accretion for every dollar spent. Management has publicly identified the discount as unjustified and is sizing buybacks accordingly. Convex Insurance (specialty re/insurance startup founded with Onex backing) is approaching IPO/sale-window — a successful monetization at a 5-7x return on invested capital would be a defining catalyst. Onex Partners VI has fresh dry powder. Bobby Le Blanc as CEO removes the "founder overhang" narrative; rumors of a U.S. dual-listing would meaningfully expand the investor base. ADR (ONEXF) exists but is illiquid; the Canadian ONEX.TO listing is the cleaner vehicle.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Onex's quarterly "Investing Performance" supplement (filed on SEDAR) breaks out segment-level NAV: balance-sheet PE positions are marked at recent transaction values where available, and the wedge between management's indicative NAV ($145/share) and a conservatively-marked third-party NAV is roughly $15-20/share — meaning even on stress-marked NAV, the discount is still 15-20%. Convex Insurance's carrying value is disclosed separately and represents one of the largest single positions; its potential IPO is the single largest event-driven catalyst. Also missed by screens: Gerry Schwartz's voting control diminishes meaningfully under the post-transition governance — multiple-voting shares are subject to sunset triggers, which removes one of the structural discount factors over a multi-year horizon.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **2026** — Convex Insurance monetization (IPO or strategic sale).
2. **Quarterly through 2026** — Continued aggressive buybacks at deep discount; NAV/share compounding.
3. **2026-2027** — Possible U.S. dual-listing announcement (rumored); would expand investor base materially.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 7 |
| Management integrity | 8 |
| Capital allocation | 9 |
| Balance sheet | 7 |
| Moat | 6 |
| Reinvestment runway | 7 |
| Insider alignment | 9 |
| Valuation | 9 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7 |
| Downside protection | 7 |
| **Total** | **76/100** |

---

SEB.name: Seaboard Corporation
SEB.content:
# 134. SEB — Seaboard Corporation
## HOLDCO | ~48% discount to indicative NAV | Score: 67/100

### THE SETUP
Seaboard Corporation is a Bresky-family-controlled (>70% economic and voting) Kansas-based diversified agribusiness conglomerate operating across six unrelated segments: Pork (Seaboard Foods, #3 US producer at ~4M+ hogs/year), Marine (Seaboard Marine, ~50-vessel containerized cargo carrier focused on Caribbean and Latin America), Commodity Trading & Milling (Seaboard Overseas Trading Group, with flour mills across Africa, Caribbean, and Latin America), Sugar & Ethanol (Argentine cane operations), Power (Dominican Republic generation), and a 14% stake in Butterball, the US turkey JV with Smithfield/Maxwell Farms. The stock trades around $2,350 with a market cap near $2.7B against a per-share book value approaching $4,500 — implying roughly a 48% discount to tangible book — the largest such discount in roughly 40 years per published Seeking Alpha analysis.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The discount is real, large, and the product of an unusually unfortunate confluence. Pork producer margins have been compressed for two-plus years on a combination of weak hog pricing, elevated feed costs, and Prop 12-related Western US sow-housing capex. Argentine sugar/ethanol has been pressured by both global sugar prices and Argentine policy dysfunction. Latin American containerized shipping, while a duopoly-leaning structure, saw freight rate normalization post-COVID. Each segment is in a cyclical trough simultaneously — a statistical rarity that plus the family control plus the >$2,000 share-price retail-deterrent has produced a decade-low valuation. Meanwhile, the holdco itself sits on a substantial cash and securities portfolio (commonly cited at ~$700M) that anchors downside.

The Bresky family's stewardship is the historical edge. Steven Bresky and Ellen Bresky have managed the company across cycles with a patient-capital, reinvest-internally philosophy, occasional special dividends in flush years, and modest opportunistic buybacks. They do not sell. The family's reluctance to do an M&A-driven event makes a "value unlock" catalyst unlikely from the inside — but it also makes the discount a feature, not a trap, for patient capital willing to underwrite cycle normalization.

The honest risks: (1) the discount has been wide before and stayed wide for years — there is no forcing mechanism; (2) Argentine assets carry both currency and expropriation risk under shifting administrations; (3) Marine operates in a freight market subject to Houthi disruption / Panama Canal drought / Latin political dysfunction; (4) Butterball has limited transparency given its private JV status, and the recent Smithfield take-private chatter could redefine that stake's optionality (positive or negative); (5) the family could elect to permanently underdistribute capital, in which case the discount is permanent and the "NAV" is academic.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A standard screen sees the headline P/E (often negative or distorted in trough years), the high share price, the controlled-company governance score, and the agribusiness sector tag — and screens it out. It cannot see that the holdco's marketable securities portfolio alone backstops a meaningful share of the market cap, that pork prices began recovering in late 2025 / early 2026, or that the Butterball stake — historically valued at zero in many models — has potential mark-to-market through any Smithfield-related corporate action. It also cannot see Bresky family buyback history at trough valuations (small but meaningful signal).

### CATALYSTS
- **Pork cycle recovery through 2026-2027:** the single largest earnings lever; Seaboard Foods swings to meaningful profitability.
- **Possible Butterball strategic action (12-24 month tail post-Smithfield take-private):** mark-to-market or distribution of the 14% stake.
- **Latin shipping demand normalization + special dividend (history of 2018, 2021):** the Bresky family's preferred capital-return mechanism.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Business quality (durable moat) | 6/10 |
| 2 | Management & alignment | 8/10 |
| 3 | Balance sheet strength | 8/10 |
| 4 | Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| 5 | Margin of safety to base case | 8/10 |
| 6 | Catalyst clarity / timing | 4/10 |
| 7 | Downside scenario quantified | 7/10 |
| 8 | Insider / sponsor skin in game | 9/10 |
| 9 | Simplicity of the thesis | 4/10 |
| 10 | Asymmetry (heads-tails payoff) | 7/10 |
| **Total** | | **67/100** |

---

DNDCF.name: Dundee Corporation
DNDCF.content:
# 135. DNDCF — Dundee Corporation
## HOLDCO | 65% NAV Discount | Score: 73/100

### THE SETUP
Goodman family Canadian mining holdco trades at a ~65% discount to indicative NAV (~$3.50/sh vs $1.20 price). $200M+ of crystallized cash from 2025-2026 monetizations (New Found Gold unit sale + Borborema royalty sale to Gold Royalty Corp Jan 2026) sits inside a $95M market cap.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Dundee Corporation is a Toronto-listed family holding company controlled by the Goodman family. Founder Ned Goodman built and then nearly destroyed it through a series of failed mining and energy bets in the 2010s. Son Jonathan Goodman has been executing a pivot toward a "scalable mining platform" — monetizing legacy positions, returning capital, and positioning to attract third-party LP capital. The portfolio includes residual public mining stakes (New Found Gold, Reunion Gold), the Westhaven Gold Shovelnose JV/earn-in (~C$85M staged), private positions (United Hydrocarbon, AgriMarine), and the recently sold Borborema gold royalty (US$45M cash to Gold Royalty Corp, closed January 2026).

The bear case is the canonical Canadian small-cap holdco trap: tiny float (~$95M USD market cap), illiquid junior mining positions whose marks are speculative, multiple-voting shares that cement Goodman family control and prevent shareholder-led restructuring, and a long history of value destruction that justifies investor skepticism. Even with $200M+ of crystallized cash, the family may reinvest it into the "mining platform" rather than returning it. NAV-discount holdcos can stay discounted for decades — Loews, Pargesa, and many others have shown the pattern. Liquidity risk is real for any institutional position.

The bull case is mathematical: $200M of cash inside a $95M shell is a 2x asymmetric setup before any other asset moves. Jonathan Goodman has demonstrably executed multiple monetizations (this is not promise — it is realized cash). Catalysts include continued public-stake monetization, Westhaven Shovelnose milestone payments, possible Goodman-family take-private (the cleanest exit), large buyback or special dividend, and launch of a third-party-funded mining platform that would crystallize a management-fee multiple on the parent. Even at a permanent 50% discount, current price implies meaningful upside.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Dundee's MD&A discloses public-market investment positions at fair value, but the private positions (United Hydrocarbon Chad oil concession, AgriMarine, various junior mining warrants) are carried at cost or stale marks. The "Subordinate Voting Shares" structure — Class A subordinate vs Class B common with 100:1 voting — is detailed only in the AIF and prevents any non-Goodman-family change of control. The January 2026 8.6% press release noted the Borborema royalty sale closing; the proceeds are not yet visible in any consensus financial database.

### CATALYSTS
- Westhaven Shovelnose earn-in milestone payments (2026)
- Capital return announcement (special dividend / SIB / buyback)
- Possible Goodman-family take-private bid

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 6 | Holdco math is clear, asset valuations less so |
| Moat | 4 | None — it's a holdco |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | $200M+ crystallized cash vs $95M mcap |
| Margin of Safety | 9 | 65% NAV discount |
| Catalyst | 7 | Multiple in flight, none dated |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 9 | 2-3x upside, limited downside from cash |
| Business Quality | 5 | History of value destruction |
| Executability | 4 | Tiny float, OTC US ticker |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Discount can persist forever |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Some gold/commodity beta |
| **TOTAL** | **73** | |

---
DNDCF.analystSummary:
Dundee Corporation is a Toronto-listed family holding company controlled by the Goodman family. Founder Ned Goodman built and then nearly destroyed it through a series of failed mining and energy bets in the 2010s. Son Jonathan Goodman has been executing a pivot toward a "scalable mining platform" — monetizing legacy positions, returning capital, and positioning to attract third-party LP capital. The portfolio includes residual public mining stakes (New Found Gold, Reunion Gold), the Westhaven Gold Shovelnose JV/earn-in (~C$85M staged), private positions (United Hydrocarbon, AgriMarine), and the recently sold Borborema gold royalty (US$45M cash to Gold Royalty Corp, closed January 2026).

The bear case is the canonical Canadian small-cap holdco trap: tiny float (~$95M USD market cap), illiquid junior mining positions whose marks are speculative, multiple-voting shares that cement Goodman family control and prevent shareholder-led restructuring, and a long history of value destruction that justifies investor skepticism. Even with $200M+ of crystallized cash, the family may reinvest it into the "mining platform" rather than returning it. NAV-discount holdcos can stay discounted for decades — Loews, Pargesa, and many others have shown the pattern. Liquidity risk is real for any institutional position.

The bull case is mathematical: $200M of cash inside a $95M shell is a 2x asymmetric setup before any other asset moves. Jonathan Goodman has demonstrably executed multiple monetizations (this is not promise — it is realized cash). Catalysts include continued public-stake monetization, Westhaven Shovelnose milestone payments, possible Goodman-family take-private (the cleanest exit), large buyback or special dividend, and launch of a third-party-funded mining platform that would crystallize a management-fee multiple on the parent. Even at a permanent 50% discount, current price implies meaningful upside.

The "Subordinate Voting Shares" structure — Class A subordinate vs Class B common with 100:1 voting — is detailed only in the AIF and prevents any non-Goodman-family change of control. The January 2026 8.6% press release noted the Borborema royalty sale closing; the proceeds are not yet visible in any consensus financial database.

WED.TO.name: Westaim Corporation
WED.TO.content:
# 136. WED.TO — Westaim Corporation
## HOLDCO (NAV DISCOUNT) | Mkt Cap ~C$530M / ~30% NAV discount | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
Westaim is a TSX Venture-listed Canadian financial-services holding company with two principal assets and one notable shareholder. The principal assets are (1) a residual stake in Skyward Specialty Insurance Group (NASDAQ: SKWD), a US specialty P&C insurer that IPO'd in January 2023 and has compounded ~70%+ since, and (2) a 51% interest in Arena FINCO, a private-credit investment platform managed in partnership with Arena Investors LP, which has scaled to roughly $3B+ AUM. The notable shareholder is HG Vora Capital (Parag Vora), a respected event-driven hedge fund that has been involved in the name for years. At C$4.40 per share against an indicative NAV of approximately C$6.30, the stock trades at a ~30% discount to its sum-of-the-parts.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The Westaim setup is one of the cleanest holdco-discount stories on either Canadian exchange right now, and the discount mechanics are doing the work for shareholders even if nothing dramatic happens. Skyward Specialty has been a genuine compounder since IPO, and Westaim has methodically monetized its position through secondary offerings — most notably the May 2024 trim of 4.4 million shares at $36.50 — and recycled proceeds into share buybacks at a steep discount to NAV. That dynamic is mathematically accretive: every dollar of Skyward stock sold near intrinsic value and used to repurchase Westaim stock at 70 cents on the dollar of NAV creates roughly 30 cents of incremental per-share value. Management has executed this playbook consistently, and there is still a multi-million share Skyward position remaining. The Arena FINCO stake is harder to value — private-credit valuations are opaque and the platform's underlying loan book is not transparent to outside investors — but Arena has scaled AUM steadily and the management-fee economics alone are meaningful.

The discount persists because Westaim is structurally hard to own: TSX Venture listing limits institutional ownership; Canadian holdco discounts are sticky; the float is small; the Skyward overhang of "more secondary sales coming" weighs on the perceived NAV; and the Arena valuation is genuinely uncertain. None of these reasons are fundamental — they are technical and disclosure issues that can resolve over time. The asymmetric outcome here is a substantial issuer bid (SIB) that would buy back a meaningful chunk of the float at a premium-to-market but discount-to-NAV, or a full take-private by HG Vora. Either action collapses the discount in a single event.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Most quant screens cannot value a Skyward residual stake (which is publicly listed but inside a holdco structure), let alone a private-credit JV stake. The mechanical NAV-discount narrowing from each Skyward sale + Westaim buyback is invisible to factor models that look only at trailing earnings or book value. HG Vora's involvement signals an aligned, sophisticated owner who is patient but transactional — exactly the type of shareholder that engineers structural value events. The TSX Venture listing also depresses passive demand to nearly zero, which inflates the discount.

### CATALYSTS
- Continued Skyward Specialty share sales at higher prices, recycled into buybacks
- Arena FINCO valuation step-ups as AUM grows
- Substantial Issuer Bid (SIB) announcement
- Possible take-private led by HG Vora
- Reorganization separating Arena into its own listed vehicle
- Migration to TSX main board (would unlock institutional demand)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: mostly — Skyward is straightforward, Arena less so
- Durable advantage: yes — repeatable buyback/NAV-narrow mechanic
- Honest, capable management: strong; HG Vora alignment
- Margin of safety: yes — ~30% NAV discount
- Reinvestment runway: clear via accretive buybacks

Sources: westaim.com; Business Wire May 2024 secondary pricing; Skyward IR.

---

FWONK.name: Liberty Media Corp - Formula One Group (Series C)
FWONK.content:
# 137. FWONK — Liberty Media Corp - Formula One Group (Series C)
## HOLDCO — NAV DISCOUNT | F1 + 86% MotoGP tracker | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
Tracker stock holding 100% of Formula One plus 86% of MotoGP (Dorna closed Nov 2024) trading at ~24% discount to indicative NAV of $115. Liberty Media has a multi-decade track record of collapsing tracker stocks into operating companies (SiriusXM, Live Nation, Atlanta Braves) — collapsing the FWONK tracker is the multi-year catalyst.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
FWONK tracks Liberty's premium global motorsports portfolio. Formula One generates ~$4.5B revenue (2025, +23% YoY) from race-promotion fees, broadcasting rights (Sky, ESPN, F1+ streaming), sponsorships and Paddock Club hospitality. The Concorde Agreement with the 10 F1 teams runs through 2025 and is being renegotiated. MotoGP — acquired November 2024 from Bridgepoint/CPP for EUR 4.2B — adds the world's premier motorcycle racing series with ~$500M revenue and similar broadcast-rights flywheel. Together: pure-play global motorsports rights at $87.86 versus $115 sum-of-parts.

Bear case: Tracker stocks structurally trade at discounts because the holders own no claim on F1's actual cash — the parent (Liberty Media) controls capital allocation. Wells Fargo cut PT to $89 in April 2026 citing race-calendar saturation (24 races) and Las Vegas GP attendance softness. The "Drive to Survive" Netflix-driven US fan growth has plateaued. Andretti/Cadillac entry as 11th team dilutes prize-money pool. MotoGP integration is unproven and was acquired at a full price (~17x EBITDA). Concorde Agreement renewal could shift profit share toward teams, compressing F1 EBITDA margin from ~35% toward 28-30%.

Bull/catalyst: Sell-side consensus values F1 alone at $20-22B (vs Liberty's 2017 acquisition price of $4.4B) — this is real compounding. Liberty's stated intent to eventually convert tracker into operating-company shares is well-telegraphed; the SiriusXM precedent (collapsed in 2024) closed a similar 15-20% discount. Goldman maintains Buy. F1+ direct-to-consumer streaming launches monetize the long-tail US/India/Brazil markets. With LLYV split-off complete and SIRI simplified, F1 group is the next obvious Liberty cleanup. A tracker-collapse announcement is the catalyst that closes 15-20% discount in days.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Liberty's 10-K segment disclosures show F1's "Other F1 revenue" line (Paddock Club, sponsorship outside the title sponsor pool) growing 30%+ — this is the highest-margin revenue and rarely modeled by sell-side. The Concorde Agreement clause on prize-money pool (currently 50% of EBITDA goes to teams) is up for renegotiation; Liberty's leverage is the F1+ streaming product, which it can use to argue for a higher revenue base before splitting. Quint hospitality (acquired 2022) cross-sells into MotoGP and PGA events — a hidden vertical-integration play not in any DCF. Lastly, 86% of MotoGP means Liberty has a path to 100% via Dorna minority buyouts at conservative multiples, further consolidating IP rights.

### CATALYSTS
- 2026 Concorde Agreement renewal (terms with teams)
- F1+ streaming ARPU and subscriber disclosure (H2 2026)
- Possible tracker-collapse announcement (any time; SiriusXM precedent)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | F1 is one of the world's great brands |
| Moat | 9 | Singular global motorsport monopoly |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | Tracker debt manageable; $4.2B Dorna funded |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | $87.86 vs $115 NAV; 24% disc |
| Catalyst | 7 | Tracker collapse; Concorde renewal |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | +30-50% on collapse vs limited downside |
| Business Quality | 9 | F1 ~35% EBITDA margin, recurring rights |
| Executability | 9 | $23B cap, fully liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Concorde squeeze + MotoGP integration |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Live sports somewhat insulated |
| **TOTAL** | **77** | Highest-quality compounder in batch |

---

EA.name: Electronic Arts Inc.
EA.content:
# 178. EA — Electronic Arts Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | $210 PIF/Silver Lake LBO | Score: 81/100

### THE SETUP
The largest leveraged buyout in history is on the glide path: $55B all-cash at $210/share by PIF, Silver Lake, and Affinity Partners. UK and China cleared in early March 2026, the shareholder vote has passed, and a June 2026 close puts ~3.0% gross spread (~18% annualized) on offer with regulatory tail risk effectively retired.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
On September 29, 2025 EA agreed to be acquired by a consortium of Saudi Arabia's PIF (rolling its existing ~9.9% stake), Silver Lake, and Jared Kushner's Affinity Partners for $210 per share in cash, valuing equity at $55B. JPMorgan committed $20B of debt financing — the largest single-bank LBO debt package on record — with PIF supplying ~$36B of equity. The shareholder vote passed; the UK CMA and Chinese SAMR cleared in early March 2026. CFIUS is the residual consideration but PIF's pre-existing 9.9% stake suggests a familiar pattern. At $203.83 the gross spread is 3.03% with expected close in June 2026 (EA's fiscal Q1 FY27), implying ~18% annualized — a chunky M&A arb spread for a deal this far along the regulatory path.

The bear case for the spread is binary-but-low-probability: (1) political backlash to Saudi sovereign-wealth ownership of a marquee US gaming asset triggers fresh CFIUS scrutiny or congressional pressure — possible but management would have flagged any agency objections by now; (2) the LBO debt package — the largest ever underwritten by a single bank — wobbles if syndication coincides with credit market dislocation, though JPM has commitment letters and bridge facilities in place; (3) closing-mechanics delay pushes settlement past June 2026 and elongates the annualized math but does not impair the $210 outcome. None of these realistically threaten the take-out price.

The bull case is plain merger arb: the deal is fully financed, blessed by the largest regulator (UK), cleared by the most-feared Asian regulator (China), and the shareholder gating event has passed. PIF's 9.9% rollover meaningfully de-risks CFIUS optics. Three percent gross over ~3 months at this stage of a fully financed deal is among the highest absolute spreads in an arb book today. Position sizing should reflect the binary downside if the deal somehow re-traded (likely 30%+ drawdown to standalone ~$140 EA fair value), but the probability-weighted return is highly attractive.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A standard arb screen captures the 3% spread but can't price the asymmetry of the residual risks. The PIF rollover is the key feature: by agreeing to roll its existing 9.9% stake, the Saudi sovereign wealth fund has materially de-risked CFIUS's foreign-control review — what would otherwise be a brand-new Saudi acquisition of a US gaming asset is, structurally, a partial roll-up of an already-disclosed foreign holder. CFIUS rarely blocks transactions that simply move an existing minority owner to majority. Equally invisible: the JPM bridge has been "in market" since announcement with no syndication snags, signaling debt markets are absorbing the package smoothly.

### CATALYSTS
- Closing — June 2026 (EA fiscal Q1 FY27)
- CFIUS clearance disclosure — pre-close
- Final closing-mechanics 8-K — May/June 2026

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 10 | Cash deal, fully disclosed merger agreement |
| Moat | 9 | EA owns Madden, FIFA/EA Sports FC, Apex, Sims — durable IP |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Pre-deal net cash; post-deal irrelevant |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | 3% spread; ~30% downside if deal breaks |
| Catalyst | 10 | Hard June 2026 close |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | 3% up, 30% down if break — but break prob now <5% |
| Business Quality | 9 | High-margin software; mid-teens ROIC |
| Executability | 10 | Mega-cap; deep liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 7 | CFIUS surprise / financing wobble — both unlikely |
| Macro Independence | 9 | Deal-specific; macro not driver |
| **TOTAL** | **85** | Pure arb with residual political risk; high-conviction sleeve trade |

---

PEN.name: Penumbra, Inc.
PEN.content:
# 140. PEN — Penumbra, Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | 12.6% Gross Spread / 22.9% Annualized | Score: 62/100

### THE SETUP

On January 15, 2026, Boston Scientific (BSX) announced an agreement to acquire Penumbra for $374 per share in a mixed cash-and-stock deal valued at approximately $14.5 billion. Holders may elect either $374 cash or 3.8721 BSX shares per Penumbra share, subject to a 73% cash / 27% stock proration. The current market price of $332.13 implies a 12.61% gross spread and roughly 22.88% annualized assuming a Q4 2026 close.

This is a strategic re-entry for Boston Scientific into neurovascular — a market BSX exited in 2017 when it sold its neurovascular franchise to Stryker. Penumbra brings the dominant U.S. mechanical thrombectomy platform (the JET 7, Indigo, Lightning) plus a peripheral embolization business that fits BSX's interventional cardiology and peripheral playbook. There is essentially no overlap in current product portfolios, which is why FTC review is expected to be straightforward — though the deal has not yet cleared HSR.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

The 12.6% spread is meaningfully wider than typical strategic medtech deals, which usually trade at 3-6% gross. The widening reflects three things: (1) the stock-portion exposure to BSX share price drift between signing and close, (2) HSR/FTC timing uncertainty given the political environment around healthcare M&A in 2026, and (3) the proration mechanic — anyone electing all-cash gets prorated down, so the practical realized value depends on election dynamics across the holder base.

Penumbra's standalone value is also a real consideration. The company put up strong thrombectomy growth in 2025 and has a pipeline of vacuum-aspiration thrombectomy expansions that could justify a higher standalone multiple if the deal breaks. That said, Penumbra had its own execution missteps in prior years (the JET 7 Reperfusion Catheter recall in 2020-21) and the strategic logic for Boston Scientific is real — neurovascular margins are 75%+ gross and the call-point overlaps with BSX's existing neuromodulation rep base.

The deal economics are clean enough that this is a "rate of return" arb: the question is not whether the deal closes, but when. A Q3 2026 close would push annualized returns above 30%; a slip to Q1 2027 brings it to roughly 16%. Risk-adjusted, this looks like a high-teens IRR opportunity in a deal with very high probability of close.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

Stock-deal arb spreads embed a hedging cost that pure ratio screens miss: the 27% stock portion needs to be hedged by shorting 3.8721 × 0.27 = 1.045 BSX shares per PEN share. Anyone running this without the BSX hedge is taking a directional bet on Boston Scientific. The proration also means the realized cash/stock mix depends on what other holders elect — index funds and event-driven funds tend to elect different ways.

There's also a quiet "second leg" — Penumbra's immersive healthcare (REAL System VR rehabilitation) business is being divested as part of the deal terms, so any post-close stub remainder for Penumbra holders is essentially zero.

### CATALYSTS

- HSR clearance (expected Q2-Q3 2026)
- Penumbra shareholder vote (pending)
- BSX share price stability through close
- Election deadline announcements

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- Simple business: Yes — strategic medtech roll-up
- Honest management: BSX track record on Edwards (CRM), Bard divestments — credible acquirer
- Margin of safety: 12.6% gross / 22.9% annualized provides cushion for 6-month delay
- Reversion risk: If deal breaks, PEN likely trades to $250-280 (pre-deal levels were $260s) — call it 20% downside
- Skip if: You can't hedge the BSX leg, or you need certainty of timing

---

AES.name: The AES Corporation
AES.content:
# 180. AES — The AES Corporation
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | $14.47 vs $15.00 cash | Score: 67/100

### THE SETUP
GIP (BlackRock) + EQT Infrastructure VI + CalPERS + QIA agreed March 1, 2026 to take AES private at $15.00/share all-cash, 100% equity-financed (no LBO debt risk), $10.7B equity / $33.4B EV. At $14.47, you're capturing 3.7% gross spread / ~4.4% annualized over 9–12 months until late-2026/early-2027 close. The deal is real, the financing is solid; the spread is the price of regulatory complexity across 14 countries.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

The AES Corporation (NYSE: AES) is a global utility/IPP with 2025 total revenue of $12.23B, four reportable segments: Renewables ($2.9B revenue, $503M operating margin), Utilities ($4.1B / $635M), Energy Infrastructure ($5.4B / $901M), and New Energy Technologies ($1M / -$11M). US footprint anchored by AES Indiana (regulated utility, $5.5B rate base, 9.75% allowed ROE per partial settlement), AES Ohio (T&D-only utility), plus 4 utilities in El Salvador. The 10-K (filed March 2, 2026) discloses the merger agreement entered March 1, 2026 with Horizon Parent, L.P. — a Delaware LP controlled by GIP and EQT Infrastructure VI funds. Total operating margin was $2,211M in 2025, down from $2,314M in 2024, with the Energy Infrastructure SBU declining 27% (-$332M) due to portfolio rationalization (Chile Renovables sale to GIP partner, AES Renewable Holdings tax-equity sales).

The bear case is regulatory complexity. The deal requires: FERC approval (US wholesale energy markets); state PUC approvals in Indiana (IURC) and Ohio (PUCO); HSR antitrust review; and crucially, CFIUS review given Qatar Investment Authority (sovereign wealth) and presumably a national-security review of foreign control over US critical infrastructure. International approvals span El Salvador, Chile, Brazil, Colombia, Dominican Republic, Mexico, Argentina, Panama, Puerto Rico — each with separate energy ministries and competition authorities. Any single jurisdiction can demand divestitures or impose conditions. Beyond regulatory: energy-policy uncertainty under the Trump 2.0 administration (renewables tax credit repeal/clawback risk via OBBBA) impacts AES's Renewables SBU economics post-close, but the deal price is fixed at $15. No financing risk because the consortium pre-funded 100% equity.

The bull case is the deal closes near-as-promised at $15.00 cash. Consortium economics: GIP paid $12.5B for 49% of Texas-power-IPP Vistra in 2024; QIA invested $10B+ in US energy infrastructure historically; CalPERS as LP de-risks the deal politically (pension fund alignment with US infrastructure). 100% equity financing eliminates debt-market disruption risk that killed several 2022–2023 take-privates. The 30-day VWAP premium pre-rumor was 40.3%, signaling strong board endorsement and likely shareholder approval. At 4.4% annualized return through Q1 2027 close, this is a respectable spread for a deal with low completion risk and well-capitalized buyers — equivalent yield to investment-grade corporate bonds with potential for upside if close accelerates.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K Subsequent Events note details that "Parent is controlled by investment vehicles affiliated with one or more funds, accounts or other entities managed or advised by Global Infrastructure Management, LLC and the EQT Infrastructure VI fund" — meaning the formal control entity excludes CalPERS/QIA, who are LPs in the GIP/EQT vehicles rather than direct co-investors at the legal-control level. This may help on CFIUS framing (sovereign-LP investment via fund-of-funds is more digestible than direct sovereign acquisition). AES's existing partnership with GIP at AES Pacifico/Chile Renovables (49% sold to GIP August 2025 for $77M plus prior tranches) created the strategic relationship that led to this deal. AES Indiana's June 3, 2025 rate case settlement (return on common equity 9.75%, $5.5B rate base, no further rate increases until next case) provides locked-in cash flow visibility for the post-close LBO economics. The 6.50% convertible senior notes were settled in 2025 for $1.0B cash, simplifying the capital stack ahead of the deal.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- **Mid-2026** — AES shareholder vote (proxy expected Q2/Q3 2026)
- **Q3-Q4 2026** — FERC, HSR, IURC, PUCO approvals; ongoing CFIUS review
- **Q4 2026 / Q1 2027** — Targeted close per merger agreement; potential interim ticking-fee/dividend rights

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Multi-segment, multi-country complexity |
| Moat | 6 | Regulated utility cash flows + IPP portfolio |
| Balance Sheet | 5 | $33.4B EV / $10.7B equity = significant leverage stays |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | $15 floor; 3.7% gross spread |
| Catalyst | 8 | Definitive deal, dated close |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | 3.7% upside vs 15-25% downside on break |
| Business Quality | 6 | Mixed; regulated utility good, IPP cyclical |
| Executability | 9 | Mega-cap, deeply liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | CFIUS QIA risk; 14-country approvals |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Mostly insulated from macro until close |
| **TOTAL** | **66** | Quality merger arb with regulatory tail risk |

AES.analystSummary:
The AES Corporation (NYSE: AES) is a global utility/IPP with 2025 total revenue of $12.23B, four reportable segments: Renewables ($2.9B revenue, $503M operating margin), Utilities ($4.1B / $635M), Energy Infrastructure ($5.4B / $901M), and New Energy Technologies ($1M / -$11M). US footprint anchored by AES Indiana (regulated utility, $5.5B rate base, 9.75% allowed ROE per partial settlement), AES Ohio (T&D-only utility), plus 4 utilities in El Salvador. The 10-K (filed March 2, 2026) discloses the merger agreement entered March 1, 2026 with Horizon Parent, L.P. — a Delaware LP controlled by GIP and EQT Infrastructure VI funds. TXNM.name: TXNM Energy, Inc.
TXNM.content:
# 177. TXNM — TXNM Energy, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | $61.25 cash / 3.87% gross spread | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
TXNM Energy (formerly PNM Resources) is the parent of Public Service Company of New Mexico (PNM) and Texas-New Mexico Power (TNMP), a regulated electric utility serving roughly 800,000 customers across New Mexico and Texas. On May 19, 2025, Blackstone Infrastructure Partners agreed to acquire TXNM in an all-cash take-private at $61.25/share, valuing the company at approximately $11.5 billion including assumed debt. Shareholders approved with a reported 99.6% vote. With the stock at $58.97 against a $61.25 deal price, the gross spread is 3.87% and the implied annualized return for a Q3 2026 close is around 9%. This is the second take-private attempt for the company — Avangrid's bid was blocked by the New Mexico PRC in 2021 — which is the entire reason this spread exists.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The deal has cleared FERC, HSR, FCC, the NRC for the Palo Verde nuclear stake transfer is pending, and the Texas PUCT has signed off. The single binary risk is the New Mexico Public Regulation Commission (PRC). The PRC famously rejected the Iberdrola/Avangrid acquisition in 2021 on consumer-protection grounds despite a settlement, and the New Mexico Attorney General and Office of Consumer Advocate have already raised objections to the Blackstone transaction. Blackstone's commitments — rate freezes, capex commitments, jobs commitments — are designed to address the prior rejection, and the regulatory architecture (passive infrastructure capital, no operational control, ring-fenced subsidiaries) is specifically structured to be more palatable than a foreign strategic acquirer.

The risk is not that the deal closes at a lower price; merger agreements of this type rarely re-cut at the regulatory stage. The risk is that the PRC denies, Blackstone walks, and TXNM trades back to its standalone level (likely $40-45 based on pre-deal trading and peer multiples). That would represent a 25-30% drawdown against a 4-9% upside on close — a roughly 1:4 reward-to-risk ratio. Position sizing should account for this asymmetry.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three things a quote screen misses. First, the New Mexico political backdrop: the state has a Democratic trifecta and an unusually activist consumer advocate office; private equity ownership of utilities is a national flashpoint after Hawaii Electric. Second, Blackstone's commitments package — they are reportedly offering a multi-year rate freeze, $2B+ in committed grid investment, and headquarters retention in Albuquerque — is materially richer than what Avangrid offered. Third, TXNM's regulated rate base growth (~9% annually driven by data center load in TX) gives Blackstone enough cushion to pay $61.25 even with concessions, which means an absolute walk is less likely than concession-driven re-trade attempts.

### CATALYSTS
- New Mexico PRC procedural schedule and hearing examiner recommendation (key data point in 2026)
- NRC approval of Palo Verde nuclear plant interest transfer
- Final PRC order — yes/no binary
- Blackstone concession package amendments in response to OCA testimony

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Simple business: Yes — regulated electric utility
- Pricing power: Yes, regulated returns on equity around 9.5%
- Honest, capable management: Going away — terminal trade
- Reasonable price: At $58.97, you are paying for spread arbitrage, not the underlying business
- Within circle of competence: Merger arb requires regulatory legal judgment
- Margin of safety: 3.87% gross spread provides minimal cushion against a PRC denial; downside is materially larger than upside

The trade is a regulatory binary, not a business analysis. Sizing matters more than thesis quality. A 1-2% position with a stop above $52 (below the likely standalone print) creates a defined-risk merger arb. The 9% annualized IRR is rich for a utility take-private precisely because the market remembers 2021.

---
