GTLS.name: Chart Industries, Inc.
GTLS.content:
# 144. GTLS — Chart Industries, Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | 4.9% Annualized Spread | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
Baker Hughes is acquiring Chart Industries for $210 cash per share, a $13.6B deal that already cleared a 98.9% shareholder vote in October 2025 and most material regulatory hurdles. With GTLS at $208.30, you collect a tight ~0.8% gross spread for what looks like a near-certain Q2 2026 close — a rates-plus risk-free income substitute, not a fat-pitch arb.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Chart Industries is a global engineered cryogenic process technology provider — heat exchangers, storage tanks, regasification, hydrogen liquefaction, and after-market services across LNG, industrial gas, and clean-energy markets. The company is the dominant pure-play in cryogenic equipment after its 2023 Howden acquisition built it into a $4B+ revenue process-technology platform. On July 28, 2025 Baker Hughes announced an all-cash $210/share offer, totaling $13.6B including assumed debt. The strategic logic: BKR's Industrial & Energy Technology segment swallows a complementary, recurring-revenue cryogenic franchise to round out its non-oilfield re-rating story.

The bear case here is mathematical, not fundamental. At a 0.82% gross spread to a $210 deal price, you are underwriting maybe a 4-5% IRR if Q2 2026 close hits, and zero or negative if the antitrust dominoes (China SAMR most notably) require divestitures or stretch into Q3. China is the swing variable; Baker Hughes has substantial Chinese LNG exposure that regulators may use as leverage. Standalone GTLS without the deal would likely re-rate down to $160-170 on a busted-deal scenario as Howden integration angst returns. Asymmetry is not in your favor at this spread.

The bull case is duration-of-cash-equivalents, not equity upside. Shareholder approval is in the bag (98.9%); US, EU, UK, Brazil, and most major reviews are cleared per BKR disclosures. The remaining holdouts are administrative timelines rather than substantive challenges given limited horizontal overlap (oilfield services vs. cryogenic process equipment). For a fund running an event book, GTLS is a high-confidence "park money" sleeve into Q2 2026 close, generating ~5% annualized with low correlation to broad equities.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
GTLS's merger proxy reveals the deal blew through one prior unsolicited approach in early 2025 at lower implied price; the board ran a 22-day go-shop that produced no superior bid, hardening the floor. Termination fee is $475M payable to BKR if Chart breaks; reverse termination fee is materially larger if BKR walks for antitrust. The proxy also discloses Howden earn-out payments still flowing — those crystallize on close, slightly reducing net cash. Most overlooked: BKR's Q3 2025 10-Q disclosed it had pre-funded $7B of acquisition financing via senior notes, removing financing risk that arb desks were modeling at 50bps of spread.

### CATALYSTS
- China SAMR antitrust decision (expected Q1 2026)
- Final ROFR / minor jurisdiction clearances Q2 2026
- Deal close and cash settlement (estimated April-June 2026)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 8/10 |
| Management integrity | 8/10 |
| Capital allocation | 7/10 |
| Balance sheet | 7/10 |
| Moat durability | 8/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 7/10 |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Margin of safety | 6/10 |
| Optionality | 9/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 11/10 (capped 10) |
| **Total** | **78/100** |

---

CTRA.name: Coterra Energy Inc.
CTRA.content:
# 145. CTRA — Coterra Energy Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | $30.89 / 0.70 DVN exchange ratio | Score: 60/100

### THE SETUP
Coterra agreed Feb 2, 2026 to be acquired by Devon Energy in an all-stock deal at a fixed 0.70 DVN per CTRA share, implying $30.96 versus a current $30.89 — a 23 bps gross spread but with full exposure to DVN's stock and oil-price beta. HSR cleared April 7, 2026 and shareholder votes are pending in Q2.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Coterra is a diversified U.S. independent E&P with assets in the Permian Delaware, Anadarko, and Marcellus, formed via the Cabot–Cimarex merger. It generates substantial free cash flow at mid-cycle commodity prices and pays a fixed-plus-variable dividend. The screen catches it because of the announced merger with Devon Energy, a $26B all-stock deal at 0.70 DVN per CTRA share. At a $30.89 spot price the gross spread is just 0.23%, annualizing to roughly 1.36% — essentially priced as a near-certain close with the only meaningful risk being DVN's share-price volatility between now and Q2 close.

Bear case: this is not a true arbitrage — it is a synthetic long DVN. If WTI rolls back to the mid-$60s or natural gas weakens, DVN's stock can drop 15–25% and the implied CTRA value follows, easily wiping out years of expected merger return. Stock-deal arbs have no collar protection here. There is also integration risk: combining two large E&Ps with overlapping basins always produces synergy slippage and asset-sale leakage. If proxy advisors flag governance or social issues at one of the two shareholder votes, the deal could face an embarrassing close vote even with HSR done.

Bull case: HSR clearance on April 7, 2026 removes the only material regulatory obstacle, and the basins are complementary not horizontally overlapping. Devon and Coterra both have aligned management visions on shareholder returns, and the pro forma entity will be one of the largest U.S. independents with scale benefits in midstream contracts and service costs. For investors who want DVN exposure anyway, owning CTRA captures the 0.70 ratio plus collects any narrowing of the spread before close. The best risk-adjusted play is a paired short of DVN against long CTRA to lock the 23 bps and clip the dividend differential.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 0.70 fixed exchange ratio means CTRA holders are fully exposed to DVN's commodity beta with no walk-away or collar. CTRA's Marcellus dry-gas position is the hidden gem the merger model under-credits — Devon historically had no gas exposure, and at $4+ Henry Hub the Marcellus throws off cash flow that materially de-risks DVN's oil-heavy book. The merger proxy will quantify run-rate synergies (likely $400–600M) that the current CTRA price has not fully discounted. Watch the proxy for the special-committee process — if any board member dissented or asked for a higher ratio, that is a signal of upside negotiating leverage that could come back if DVN trades up materially before vote.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- CTRA shareholder vote (Q2 2026)
- DVN shareholder vote (Q2 2026)
- Deal close expected Q2 2026 with synergy reveal

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | E&P merger, straightforward |
| Moat | 5 | Commodity, no moat |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | Investment-grade, low leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | 23 bps gross spread |
| Catalyst | 8 | Q2 vote + close |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 4 | Synthetic long DVN, no asymmetry |
| Business Quality | 6 | Mid-quality E&P, good basins |
| Executability | 9 | Mega-cap, deep liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | DVN stock or oil drop sinks return |
| Macro Independence | 2 | Pure commodity exposure |
| **TOTAL** | **57** | |

---

WBS.name: Webster Financial Corporation
WBS.content:
# 146. WBS — Webster Financial Corporation
## M&A ARB | Deal Value $75.59/sh ($12.3B) | Score: 62/100

### THE SETUP
On February 3, 2026, Banco Santander, S.A. (SAN) announced a definitive agreement to acquire Webster Financial Corporation for approximately $12.3 billion, valuing WBS at $75.59 per share. Consideration is mixed: $48.75 cash plus 2.0548 Santander ADS per WBS share, putting the deal at roughly 65% cash / 35% stock. The combination expands Santander's US commercial-banking footprint materially, layering Webster's Northeast middle-market book and HSA Bank deposit franchise onto Santander's existing US platform. With WBS trading at $73.37, the gross spread is ~3.0%; on an expected Q4 2026 close, that annualizes to roughly 5.4% — a workable risk-arb spread, though not the easy money the headline implies.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
This is fundamentally a regulatory-risk arb, not a financing or shareholder-vote arb. Bank M&A approval pipelines have shortened under the current regulatory regime, but a foreign-bank acquisition of a top-25 US commercial bank brings additional friction: the Federal Reserve and OCC must bless the transaction, CFIUS will review the foreign-acquirer angle, and Santander itself must clear EU regulatory and shareholder hurdles. None of these are blocking in our base case — Santander already operates a sizable US bank (Santander Bank N.A.) and has a clean US regulatory record — but each adds time, and time is what compresses the IRR. The stock-portion exposure is also non-trivial: 2.0548 SAN ADS per WBS share means a 10% drop in Santander stock between announcement and close would reduce WBS's deal value by roughly $1.20-1.30 per share, narrowing the spread or pushing it negative depending on entry. Sophisticated arbs typically hedge this by shorting SAN against the long WBS, which is straightforward but adds borrow cost.

The break risk is asymmetric: an outright block is unlikely given the strategic rationale and Santander's existing US operations, but a regulatory-driven "extract concessions" delay (additional capital requirements, divestiture demands, community-benefits commitments) is plausible and would push close into early-to-mid 2027. The fundamental downside if the deal breaks is the stand-alone WBS franchise value — a quality regional bank with a strong HSA deposit book — which is reasonably valued in the high $50s to low $60s, implying ~15-20% downside in a break scenario versus ~3% upside if it closes on schedule. Risk/reward is acceptable but not exceptional.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Bank-merger arb is not a quantitative game — the spread is set efficiently within hours of announcement. The edge here is qualitative reads on regulatory disposition (Santander's existing relationship with US regulators is strong, which is underappreciated), interest-rate path (a steep cut cycle would re-rate the stock portion), and Santander's strategic commitment (the company has explicitly identified US commercial banking as a core growth pillar, making walk-away unlikely). HSA Bank deposit value as a strategic asset to Santander is also under-modeled by most arb desks.

### CATALYSTS
- Both shareholder votes (WBS and SAN) — pending
- Federal Reserve approval (BHC Act application)
- OCC merger approval
- CFIUS clearance
- EU regulatory clearance for Santander
- Final close (Q4 2026 target)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: yes — pure event-driven arb
- Durable advantage: n/a — short-duration trade
- Honest, capable management: Santander credible acquirer with US track record
- Margin of safety: thin — 3% spread, 15-20% break downside
- Reinvestment runway: n/a

Sources: Webster Bank investor relations Feb 3 2026 announcement; Banking Dive coverage.

---

AVNS.name: Avanos Medical, Inc.
AVNS.content:
# 147. AVNS — Avanos Medical, Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | $25.00 cash, ~1.5% gross spread, 2H26 close | Score: 58/100

### THE SETUP
All-cash take-private announced April 14, 2026: American Industrial Partners agreed to acquire Avanos at $25.00/share, EV ~$1.272B, a 72% premium to the prior close. Stock at $24.62 = ~1.5% gross spread, ~3% annualized to expected H2 2026 close. Standard PE merger arb: low antitrust risk (small medtech, no horizontal overlap), no financing condition, board-unanimous approval. Pure spread harvest with limited deal break downside given the depressed pre-bid price.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Avanos is a focused medical device company spun from Kimberly-Clark Healthcare in 2014, now operating two segments: Specialty Nutrition Systems (enteral feeding, neonatal — the legacy MIC-KEY/CORPAK brands) and Pain Management & Recovery (ON-Q surgical pain pumps, COOLIEF radiofrequency ablation, game-ready cold therapy). FY2025 net sales $701.2M (+1.9% YoY), with ~6% organic growth in "strategic" sub-segments offset by the divested respiratory health business. The deal: AIP pays $25.00/share cash, $1.272B enterprise value, premium 72.1% to April 13, 2026 close and 82.8% to 30-day VWAP. No financing contingency. Closing expected H2 2026, subject to AVNS shareholder vote and HSR. At $24.62, the math is gross spread $0.38 / $24.62 = 1.54%, annualized ~3% across the ~6-month horizon.

Bear case (deal break): the standard list. Shareholder vote could go sideways if a topping bidder doesn't emerge and a holder argues $25 underprices the COOLIEF/ablation franchise — but the 72% premium makes that unlikely. PE financing isn't a stated condition, but AIP's debt commitments could tighten if credit spreads widen. Antitrust: low risk (PE buyer, no overlap), but DOJ has been more activist in PE roll-ups; an extended HSR pull-and-refile could push closing into Q1 2027 and crush the IRR. The 10-K flagged that the company faces "intense competition" and "pricing pressure from group purchasing organizations" — if the deal breaks, the standalone re-rate to the pre-deal ~$14.50 level would be a ~40% downside hit.

Bull case: 8-month timeline is generous; closing could pull forward to Q3 2026, doubling the IRR. Overwhelming premium, board-unanimous, no financing condition, and the natural acquirer pool (other PE sponsors who passed) is large. A topping bid is unlikely but not zero — strategic medtech (Stryker, Teleflex, Becton Dickinson) could opportunistically interlope on the pain franchise. Cushion: even if the deal slips a quarter, the spread accretes; Avanos has $124M+ of cash on the balance sheet, no covenant pressure, and the standalone business produces low-double-digit FCF margins. Engaged Capital and other activist holders had been pushing for strategic alternatives — sale resolution validates that thesis.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Two structural items the screen can't price: (1) Engaged Capital was reportedly the activist behind the strategic-review process — their cost basis is well below $25, so they are aligned to vote yes and unlikely to hold out for more, sealing vote outcome. (2) The HSR clock is the binding constraint: AIP is a financial sponsor with no horizontal medical-device overlap, so a "pull-and-refile" is not the base case. Antitrust review timeline is closer to 30–45 days than the 6 months commonly modeled. The 10-K disclosed pending product-liability exposure on Game Ready cold-therapy products and historic Hernia mesh — these are reps-and-warranties items that matter for the deal close mechanics; the merger agreement should be read for any MAC carve-outs around legal reserves.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Definitive proxy filing (~May–June 2026) and special meeting date for shareholder vote.
- HSR expiration / second-request decision (~June 2026).
- Deal close H2 2026; final cash distribution to holders.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Cash deal at $25, simple math |
| Moat | 4 | Mid-tier medtech, decent niche brands |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | $124M cash, modest leverage |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | Spread is the entire MoS, ~1.5% |
| Catalyst | 9 | Specific dated deal close |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 4 | +1.5% / –40% break risk |
| Business Quality | 5 | Low-growth medtech, ~8% op margin |
| Executability | 8 | $1.2B mcap, liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Deal break = $14.50 standalone print |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Closing not macro-sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **64** | |

---

TWO.name: Two Harbors Investment Corp.
TWO.content:
# 148. TWO — Two Harbors Investment Corp.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | BUMP EXPECTED | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP

Two Harbors Investment is an Agency MBS-focused mortgage REIT with a mortgage-servicing-rights overlay — a structure designed to balance interest-rate sensitivity by offsetting MBS duration risk with the negative-duration profile of MSRs. The deal at issue: on March 26, 2026, CrossCountry Mortgage (private) announced an all-cash bid of $10.80 per share, valuing the company at ~$1.13B. Notably, this bid replaced an earlier stock-based deal from United Wholesale Mortgage (UWMC); CrossCountry paid a $25.4M termination fee to break that prior arrangement. TWO last traded at $11.12, which means the spread is negative — gross spread of -2.88%, annualized -4.8% — i.e., the market is pricing in a topping bid or a bump from CrossCountry rather than the announced terms. Expected close is Q4 2026; shareholder vote pending. Antitrust risk is low — a mortgage REIT acquisition by a private mortgage originator is a well-trod regulatory path. Litigation risk from UWMC and the standard plaintiff-bar lockup challenges are the main wrinkles.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

The trade isn't a classic "lock in the spread" arbitrage; it's the inverse — the market has already moved past the announced price and is paying for a bump. There are reasonable underpinnings to that pricing. First, CrossCountry's willingness to pay UWMC a $25.4M termination fee implies strong strategic conviction in the asset; bidders that pay a break fee to displace a prior deal often retain bidding-war upside. Second, UWMC, having been topped, has the option to come back over the top — and the all-stock-versus-all-cash dynamic from the prior deal suggests a re-engineered cash-or-mixed bid is plausible. Third, mortgage REITs with MSR overlays are scarce strategic assets to private mortgage originators that need a portfolio counterpart to their origination engine, which creates a small but real bidder universe. The downside, if no bump materializes, is well-defined: the price reverts toward $10.80 at close, implying ~3% downside from current levels, against an unspecified but non-trivial upside if a competing bid emerges. Because the spread is negative, the trade is a long-volatility positioning around a competitive process, not a yield-extraction arb. Sizing should reflect the asymmetry: limited downside if the deal closes at announced terms, meaningful upside if a bidding war materializes, but the timeline (Q4 2026 close) means several months of carry and event risk.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

Standard merger-arbitrage screens flag negative spreads as "unattractive" and route capital elsewhere — they miss that a negative spread on a strategic-buyer cash deal where a prior bidder was just bought out is a legitimate competitive-process signal, not a dislocation. Other things screens miss: (1) the $25.4M termination fee paid to UWMC sets a partial floor on the next topping bid's seriousness — a casual second look from UWMC would imply that fee was wasted; (2) Two Harbors' MSR portfolio carries fair-value marks that move with rates and prepayment speeds, which means the underlying NAV changes between announcement and close in ways that can pressure either side to renegotiate; (3) plaintiff-bar suits on inadequate-disclosure or inadequate-process grounds are essentially automatic in REIT M&A and rarely block deals but occasionally produce supplemental disclosures that delay timing.

### CATALYSTS

- Topping bid from UWMC or third party (the negative-spread trade)
- Definitive proxy filing and proxy advisory recommendations (ISS/Glass Lewis)
- Shareholder vote
- Regulatory clearances (low risk)
- Final close in Q4 2026

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- Circle of competence: Yes — mortgage REIT M&A mechanics are well-documented.
- Durable advantage: N/A — this is event-driven, not a compounder.
- Honest, able management: Board ran a competitive process and accepted higher cash bid.
- Margin of safety: Limited at current price — small downside if no bump, modest upside if bump.
- Avoid stupidity: Don't size as if announced-deal arb; size for the bidding-war option.

Verdict: a negative-spread M&A situation that the market is correctly identifying as bidding-war optionality rather than mispricing. Score reflects clean structural setup and credible bump path, offset by negative entry spread, multi-quarter timeline, and binary path-dependence.

---

OGN.name: Organon & Co.
OGN.content:
# 149. OGN — Organon & Co.
## TAKE-PRIVATE | Sun Pharma + Grünenthal Bidding War | Score: 72/100

### THE SETUP
Bloomberg reported April 16, 2026 that Sun Pharmaceutical and Grünenthal are submitting binding bids for Organon at a reported ~$12B / ~$45-per-share level — implying a 22.95% gross spread to the current $9.76 price. Board has not yet accepted; this is bid-on-bid speculation, not a signed deal.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Organon is the women's-health and established-brands spinoff Merck completed in June 2021. The portfolio is Nexplanon (long-acting reversible contraceptive — the crown jewel, growing double digits and patent-protected to 2027 with formulation extensions through 2030+), a biosimilars portfolio (Hadlima, Renflexis, Brenzys, Aybintio), and a long tail of established off-patent brands (Singulair, Nasonex, Cozaar, Zetia, Vytorin) that throw off cash but decline 3-7% annually. The company has been a serial value-trap since the spin: leverage was excessive at separation (~4x), the dividend was cut in 2024, and Nexplanon LOE (loss of exclusivity) hangs over the model.

The bear case for the deal: there is no signed merger agreement. "Binding bids" reported in Bloomberg can collapse — boards reject offers, financing falls through, due diligence finds skeletons. Sun Pharma is Indian-domiciled and Grünenthal is German private — both face CFIUS / cross-border review, and women's-health products may attract political sensitivity. The reported $12B price is 8x EBITDA, which is rich for a declining-asset pharma roll-up. If the deal falls apart, OGN reverts to its pre-rumor trading range ($7-9), implying ~20% downside vs. ~36% upside if it closes at $45 — symmetric, not asymmetric. Nexplanon LOE in 2027 is the elephant: any acquirer is buying a wasting asset.

The bull case: bidding wars typically settle higher than initial bids — Sun and Grünenthal both want the women's-health franchise, and a third bidder (rumored: a sovereign wealth-backed consortium, possibly EQT or KKR) could enter. Nexplanon generates $1B+ in revenue at 80%+ gross margin — even with LOE, follow-on next-gen formulations (Nexplanon NXT in late-stage trials) extend the franchise. The biosimilars business is structurally undervalued in the standalone trading multiple. If a deal closes anywhere from $42-50, the IRR over a 9-12 month timeline annualizes to 25-35%.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Organon's 10-K discloses Nexplanon revenue concentration explicitly — it is the single largest product and the asset any acquirer is really buying. Hidden in segment disclosure: the Established Brands portfolio still throws off ~$3B in highly-cash-generative revenue at minimal R&D reinvestment, which is exactly what a PE-style sponsor or generics consolidator wants. Sun Pharma specifically has built its U.S. franchise via established-brands acquisitions (Taro, Ranbaxy assets) and knows how to run wind-down portfolios. The spread to the rumored bid (~23%) is unusually wide for a confirmed-bidders situation — the market is pricing meaningful (~50%) probability of no deal, which screens as "M&A arb" but is really event-probability arbitrage on whether a binding bid converts to a signed agreement.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **Q2 2026** — Definitive merger agreement signed (or board rejection / no-deal announcement).
2. **2H 2026 / Q1 2027** — Antitrust + CFIUS clearances; expected close.
3. **2027** — Nexplanon LOE date — relevant whether deal closes (acquirer integration) or fails (standalone re-rating).

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 6 |
| Management integrity | 6 |
| Capital allocation | 5 |
| Balance sheet | 4 |
| Moat | 6 |
| Reinvestment runway | 5 |
| Insider alignment | 6 |
| Valuation | 8 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7 |
| Downside protection | 5 |
| **Total** | **58/100** |

---

SBAC.name: SBA Communications Corporation
SBAC.content:
# 150. SBAC — SBA Communications Corporation
## TAKE-PRIVATE | Rumored $250/sh KKR/Brookfield bid vs. $223 | Score: 52/100

### THE SETUP
SBA Communications is the #3 US tower REIT (behind American Tower and Crown Castle), owning roughly 40,000 macro towers across the Americas and Africa. On April 2, 2026, Bloomberg reported SBAC was working with advisors to explore a sale after preliminary interest from KKR and Brookfield Infrastructure. No definitive agreement has been announced, no premium is on the table, and the company itself has not confirmed a formal process. Shares popped from the high-$190s to ~$223 on the leak and have hovered there ever since, implying the market gives roughly 60-65% probability to a deal getting done somewhere near the rumored $250 indication. This is "narrative arb," not a true merger arb — there is no signed deal, no walk price, no break fee, and no shareholder vote teed up. It belongs in the speculation bucket.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The strategic logic is real. Tower REITs are the platonic ideal of private infrastructure: 5-10 year contracted MLAs with auto-escalators, ~70% incremental margins on co-location, and underwriting that resembles a triple-net lease against investment-grade tenants (T-Mobile, Verizon, AT&T, Vodafone). KKR has been an aggressive buyer of telecom infra (Vantage Towers, Telecom Italia's NetCo), and Brookfield owns GD Towers in Germany via its infrastructure platform. SBAC's higher-leverage / higher-IRR posture (net debt to EBITDA in the high-6x range) is exactly the kind of asset PE can re-equitize and lever back up post-rate-cuts.

But the math is hard. At $250, the equity check is ~$27B; assume a takeout at 22-23x AFFO and EV pushes toward $52-55B — among the largest infrastructure LBOs ever. Financing such a deal requires a club, REIT-conversion mechanics, and cooperation from key tenants whose MLAs may have change-of-control language. American Tower and Crown Castle are out as strategic buyers (antitrust). The most realistic path is a consortium of KKR + Brookfield + a sovereign wealth co-investor.

The 12% gross spread reflects three live risks: (1) the talks fall apart and shares retrace to a pre-leak ~$195 print (~12-15% downside), (2) any signed deal closes at a price closer to $230-$240 rather than $250, and (3) a 12-15 month close timeline given FCC license transfers and country-by-country tower regulators in Brazil, South Africa, etc. Position sizing should reflect that this is an unconfirmed rumor, not a definitive merger agreement.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen will show "12% spread, 11% annualized" and rank this attractive. What it misses: there is no signed merger agreement. The "deal" is one Bloomberg sourced article. SBAC's board has acknowledged nothing publicly. There is no walk-away price, no go-shop, no fiduciary out — because there is no deal. The screen also misses the optionality the other way: if AFFO continues to compound at 6-8% with rate cuts in 2026, SBAC may walk away from any bid below $260-$270 and the stock re-rates organically.

### CATALYSTS
- **Q2 2026 earnings (early August 2026):** management commentary on strategic review, even a "no comment / process ongoing" formalization.
- **Definitive merger announcement (target: late Q2 / early Q3 2026):** the binary event — confirms or kills the trade.
- **FCC consent + sovereign reviews (12-15 months post-signing):** the real long-tail to close, modeling toward Q2 2027.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Business quality (durable moat) | 9/10 |
| 2 | Management & alignment | 6/10 |
| 3 | Balance sheet strength | 5/10 |
| 4 | Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| 5 | Margin of safety to base case | 4/10 |
| 6 | Catalyst clarity / timing | 4/10 |
| 7 | Downside scenario quantified | 5/10 |
| 8 | Insider / sponsor skin in game | 4/10 |
| 9 | Simplicity of the thesis | 5/10 |
| 10 | Asymmetry (heads-tails payoff) | 5/10 |
| **Total** | | **53/100** |

---

CWAN.name: Clearwater Analytics Holdings, Inc.
CWAN.content:
# 179. CWAN — Clearwater Analytics Holdings, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE | $24.11 / $24.55 cash | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
Clearwater agreed Dec 21, 2025 to be taken private by a Permira / Warburg Pincus consortium (with Francisco Partners and Temasek participating) at $24.55 cash per share, an $8.4B deal at a 47% premium. Go-shop ended Jan 23, 2026 with no superior bid; shareholder vote is May 6, 2026; spot at $24.11 implies a 1.83% gross spread or about 9.1% annualized.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Clearwater Analytics is a SaaS platform for institutional investment accounting, performance, compliance, and risk reporting — used by insurers, asset managers, corporates, and government entities to handle multi-asset portfolio data. The model is high-retention, multi-tenant, and produces SaaS-grade economics. Screen catches it because of the announced LBO take-private at $24.55 cash. Spread of 1.83% gross / 9.1% annualized reflects a low-friction, financing-secured deal with a defined May 6, 2026 vote and Q2 2026 expected close. PE consortium includes a sovereign co-invest (Temasek) which is positive for financing certainty.

Bear case: even an essentially-clean PE LBO can stall on foreign regulatory clearances (EU, UK, possibly Asian) or on shareholder class-action objections that delay the close into late Q3 or Q4. CWAN's go-shop produced no superior bidder — bears can argue the sale price under-values the franchise given peer SaaS multiples have re-rated since announcement. If the deal breaks for any reason, the unaffected price was likely closer to $17 (pre-announcement low to mid teens), so downside is meaningful — call it 25-30%. Sponsor LBO break risk is low but not zero in a turbulent rates environment if debt financing has to be re-cut.

Bull case: this is a textbook clean PE take-private. Sponsor consortium with deep equity backing, sovereign co-invest, committed financing, no antitrust overlap (multiple PE firms, no horizontal concern), board-recommended deal, completed go-shop, shareholder vote scheduled. The 13E-3 amendments in April 2026 indicate process is on rails. At a 1.83% gross spread and 9.1% annualized to Q2 close, this is one of the lower-risk arb spreads available — especially attractive when paired against a portfolio of higher-risk merger arbs to balance downside. For a non-arb investor, this is essentially a cash equivalent maturing in late Q2.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 13E-3 filings disclose the special-committee process, financial advisor fairness opinion, and projection set used to justify the $24.55 price — which gives a window into management's own forward case for organic growth and margin trajectory that public guidance had been more conservative on. The go-shop window closing Jan 23, 2026 with zero superior offers is informative about strategic alternatives — strategic acquirers (FactSet, S&P, MSCI) chose not to bid, which tells you the price is at or above what synergy buyers would pay. The Permira/Warburg consortium structure with Temasek co-invest indicates pre-arranged equity certainty — financing risk is essentially zero.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Shareholder vote May 6, 2026
- Final foreign regulatory clearances (HSR cleared, foreign pending)
- Deal close H1 / Q2 2026

| Business Quality | 9 | High-quality SaaS |
| Executability | 9 | $8.4B deal, deep liquidity |

CWAN.analystSummary:
Clearwater Analytics is a SaaS platform for institutional investment accounting, performance, compliance, and risk reporting — used by insurers, asset managers, corporates, and government entities to handle multi-asset portfolio data. The model is high-retention, multi-tenant, and produces SaaS-grade economics. Screen catches it because of the announced LBO take-private at $24.55 cash. Spread of 1.83% gross / 9.1% annualized reflects a low-friction, financing-secured deal with a defined May 6, 2026 vote and Q2 2026 expected close. PE consortium includes a sovereign co-invest (Temasek) which is positive for financing certainty.

Bear case: even an essentially-clean PE LBO can stall on foreign regulatory clearances (EU, UK, possibly Asian) or on shareholder class-action objections that delay the close into late Q3 or Q4. CWAN's go-shop produced no superior bidder — bears can argue the sale price under-values the franchise given peer SaaS multiples have re-rated since announcement. If the deal breaks for any reason, the unaffected price was likely closer to $17 (pre-announcement low to mid teens), so downside is meaningful — call it 25-30%. Sponsor LBO break risk is low but not zero in a turbulent rates environment if debt financing has to be re-cut.

Bull case: this is a textbook clean PE take-private. Sponsor consortium with deep equity backing, sovereign co-invest, committed financing, no antitrust overlap (multiple PE firms, no horizontal concern), board-recommended deal, completed go-shop, shareholder vote scheduled. The 13E-3 amendments in April 2026 indicate process is on rails. At a 1.83% gross spread and 9.1% annualized to Q2 close, this is one of the lower-risk arb spreads available — especially attractive when paired against a portfolio of higher-risk merger arbs to balance downside. For a non-arb investor, this is essentially a cash equivalent maturing in late Q2.

BHF.name: Brighthouse Financial, Inc.
BHF.content:
# 153. BHF — Brighthouse Financial, Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | $70/sh Cash Deal — Shareholders Approved | Score: 71/100

### THE SETUP
Aquarian Capital signed a definitive $70/share all-cash take-private of Brighthouse on Nov 6, 2025. Shareholders approved Feb 12, 2026. Stock at $62.22 = 12.5% gross spread to a $70 cash terminal value, with only multi-state insurance regulatory approvals (NY DFS being the gating item) standing between today and a ~$7.78/share payoff. Annualized ~22.7% if it closes Q4 2026.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Brighthouse is one of the largest U.S. annuity and life insurance providers — over 2.0 million policies in force, ~$206B AUM at year-end 2025, spun out of MetLife in 2017. Per the 10-K (Feb 2026): combined RBC ratio 456%, total adjusted statutory capital $5.4B, both above the 400-450% target range. Aquarian Capital (a private insurance-focused holding/PE platform) agreed Nov 6, 2025 to acquire BHF for $70/share = $4.1B equity value, all cash. Aquarian stated the deal is fully committed financing — not subject to a financing condition — and there will be no incremental debt at Brighthouse or Aquarian's existing insurance businesses. BHF special meeting Feb 12, 2026 approved the merger.

Bear case: U.S. life insurance deals require approval from every state where the insurer is domiciled or licensed. New York DFS in particular has historically imposed structural conditions (capital maintenance agreements, dividend restrictions, additional surplus contributions) that have killed or delayed deals — most notably the failed Apollo/Athene-PHL Variable arbitrage and prior PE-into-insurance battles. Aquarian is a relatively new sponsor with limited public regulatory track record. State commissioners may demand a "Form A" Holding Company Act filing review of 6-12 months minimum. Variable annuity book sensitivity to equity markets means a 2026 selloff could prompt regulators to demand additional capital — possibly forcing Aquarian to renegotiate.

Bull case: shareholder vote already passed (Feb 12, 2026) — that risk is gone. RBC at 456% gives Aquarian a massive capital cushion to absorb regulatory demands without renegotiating price. The deal is committed-financed with no MAC tied to capital markets. Brighthouse is not a stranger to insurance regulators — it has been a standalone public insurer since 2017 with established Form A precedents in its key states. Spread of 12.5% gross / 22.7% annualized is generous relative to the residual risk. Cushion: at $62.22, downside on deal break is probably $50-55 (pre-deal trading range), so risk/reward is roughly $7.78 up vs. $7-12 down — slightly skewed favorably.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K (per Stocktitan summary) explicitly addresses how the merger limits the company's flexibility: covenants restrict capital actions, M&A, and material commitments while the deal pends. More importantly, BHF's variable annuity hedging program (Shield Annuities, the largest registered index-linked annuity book in the U.S.) has historically caused statutory capital volatility — Aquarian and the NY DFS will both want to confirm hedging assumptions hold up under the Form A review's stress tests. The 456% RBC is well above any reasonable regulatory ask, but a sharp Q2/Q3 2026 equity drawdown could compress the cushion fast and slow the timeline.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Form A regulatory filings (NY DFS, Delaware, Massachusetts) decisions Q2-Q3 2026
- Possible Q4 2026 closing payment of $70 cash
- Q1 2026 earnings (any RBC weakness flagged by regulators)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Annuity hedging is complex but deal is simple |
| Moat | 4 | Distribution scale; commoditizing |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | RBC 456%, $5.4B adj. capital |
| Margin of Safety | 7 | 12.5% gross to terminal $70 |
| Catalyst | 9 | Defined cash takeout |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 6 | $7.78 up / ~$10 down on break |
| Business Quality | 5 | Spread biz, ROE volatile |
| Executability | 9 | Mega-cap liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | NY DFS historical blocker |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Equity-linked annuity sensitivity |
| **TOTAL** | **66** | |

CCO.name: Clear Channel Outdoor Holdings, Inc.
CCO.content:
# 155. CCO — Clear Channel Outdoor Holdings, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE | $2.43 Cash, ~2% Spread | Score: 67/100

### THE SETUP
Mubadala Capital + TWG Global agreed Feb 9, 2026 to take Clear Channel Outdoor private at $2.43/share all-cash ($6.2B EV, 71% premium to unaffected). Stock at $2.38 = 2.1% gross spread / ~5% annualized to expected close. The only material gating item is CFIUS for the UAE acquirer.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Clear Channel Outdoor is a global out-of-home advertising operator with U.S. (Americas + Airports) and historically European footprints. The Feb 9, 2026 transaction values CCO at $6.2B EV with shareholders receiving $2.43/share cash. Equity from Mubadala/TWG; preferred from Apollo; debt led by JPMorgan + Apollo. Go-shop expired late March 2026 with no superior bid (per InsideArbitrage), narrowing risk to regulatory closure. Expected close window: late 2026 to early 2027 pending CFIUS, FCC license transfers, and stockholder vote. Spread at $2.38 = ~2.1% gross / mid-single-digit annualized — typical for a deal with a foreign-buyer/CFIUS gating factor.

The bear case turns on three risks. First, CFIUS: Mubadala is an Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund and CCO owns U.S. airport outdoor advertising assets — those airport contracts touch passenger movement data and are physical infrastructure inside secured federal facilities. CFIUS could impose mitigation (data security, U.S.-citizen-only board members) or, in a worst case, block. Second, financing: Apollo preferred + bank debt is committed but conditioned on no MAC; any 2026 advertising recession (CCO is highly cyclical) gives lenders an exit ramp. Third, the stock is sub-$3 and trades wide bid/ask — execution friction is meaningful, and a deal break sends shares back to ~$1.40 unaffected (-40%).

The bull case is short-dated arbitrage with limited downside catalysts already removed. Go-shop expired without a topping bid — eliminating one source of uncertainty (and confirming the strike price is fair). The 71% premium provides political cover for board approval and reduces stockholder-vote risk to near-zero. Mubadala has prior CFIUS experience and would have pre-cleared the deal structure with advisors before signing. Annualized 5% spread on a deal with a defined close is acceptable for a special-sit allocator alongside other small-cap merger arb. The fact that no shareholder activist or Delaware appraisal action has materialized since Feb 9 is a positive tell.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The DEFM14A merger proxy (filed by CCO post-announcement) discloses the "fair value" board projections that supported the $2.43 price — typically containing a base case AOI/EBITDA forecast, plus management's standalone DCF which would imply >$3+ in success scenarios. This becomes material in the rare event of a Delaware appraisal challenge. Additionally, the credit agreements at the holdco and OpCo level have change-of-control puts that must be tendered or refinanced — buyer financing structure addresses this, but the timing of bond refi is a closing condition. Watch for CCO's Q1 2026 earnings (any meaningful AOI miss strengthens the buyer's MAC argument) and FCC filings for license transfer at airport assets.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- **Q2 2026:** Stockholder vote (proxy mailed and meeting scheduled).
- **Q2-Q3 2026:** CFIUS notice review (typical 90-day clock + extensions).
- **Q3-Q4 2026:** Closing target window (deal expected late 2026/early 2027).

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Take-private merger arb |
| Moat | 4 | Outdoor ad concessions |
| Balance Sheet | 3 | Highly levered ($6.2B EV vs $0.6B equity) |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | 2% spread; deal-break -40% |
| Catalyst | 8 | Defined close, no go-shop |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Asymmetric the wrong way (small upside, large break risk) |
| Business Quality | 4 | Cyclical, low-margin OOH |
| Executability | 6 | Sub-$3, wide bid/ask |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | CFIUS conditions/block |
| Macro Independence | 5 | Ad cycle dependent |
| **TOTAL** | **52** | |

---

APLS.name: Apellis Pharmaceuticals, Inc.
APLS.content:
# 189. APLS — Apellis Pharmaceuticals, Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | $41 cash + up to $4 CVR @ $40.90 | Score: 72/100

### THE SETUP
Biogen agreed March 31, 2026 to acquire Apellis for $41/share cash plus a non-transferable CVR worth up to $4 (Syfovre 2027–31 sales triggers). Tender offer (SC TO-T filed April 14, 2026) initially expires May 13, 2026, with a Section 251(h) back-end merger — no shareholder vote required. At $40.90 the gross spread on the cash piece is ~0.24%; the CVR is the optionality.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Apellis is a commercial-stage biotech with two approved C3-targeted complement therapies: Syfovre (intravitreal pegcetacoplan) for geographic atrophy secondary to AMD, and Empaveli (subcutaneous pegcetacoplan) for PNH and now C3G/IC-MPGN. FY2025 results: net product revenue $689M (Syfovre $587M + Empaveli $102M); total revenue $1.0B including a one-time $275M Sobi (ex-US partner) milestone payment; net income $22.4M — first profitable year. Cash $466.2M at year-end. A $94M convertible matures September 2026. Syfovre delivered ~102,000 doses in 2025 and held ~60% GA market share; Empaveli ended with 267 cumulative C3G/IC-MPGN patient starts and >5% US market penetration. On March 31, 2026 Biogen announced the $5.6B acquisition; the SC TO-T was filed April 14, 2026 with a May 13, 2026 initial expiration. Morningside and certain insiders (~14%) signed support agreements. The Apellis Board unanimously recommends the offer.

Bear case: at $40.90 vs. $41.00 cash, the spread is essentially zero — risk/reward is asymmetric to the downside. Deal-break would re-rate APLS back to standalone fundamentals (probably mid-$20s given the convertible maturity and post-deal investor exodus), implying ~30%+ downside vs. ~0.3% upside on the cash piece. The CVR ($2 if 2027-30 sales hit $1.5B; another $2 if 2027-31 sales hit $2.0B) is "non-transferable" — it cannot be monetized in the secondary market and may be valued at zero by many institutional holders. Hitting $1.5B annual Syfovre + Empaveli sales by 2030 requires meaningful growth from $689M base, while complement competition (Iveric Bio's Izervay, Roche's pipeline) is intensifying.

Bull case: deal-break risk is genuinely low — Biogen and Apellis have zero marketed-product overlap (Biogen's neuro/MS franchise vs. Apellis's complement); HSR is routine; tender mechanics avoid shareholder vote and Section 251(h) back-end merger ensures swift close (Q2/Q3 2026). The 14% support stockholder pact provides a head-start toward the majority tender threshold. The CVR is real optionality — at $689M base with 17% YoY dose growth, a path to $1.5B by 2027–30 is plausible if Empaveli's nephrology launch executes. CVR worth ~$1–2 in present-value terms is the difference between a 0.3% deal arb and a 5–10% total return.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The CVR structure is the alpha. Syfovre LTM dose growth was ~17% and Empaveli's C3G launch is materially ahead of street models — a screener computes the deal IRR on the cash piece alone and concludes the trade is uninteresting, but the CVR's $2 + $2 ladder is back-end-loaded and matches a plausible product trajectory under Biogen's broader commercial reach. Also unappreciated: the $94M convertible maturing September 2026 was an overhang that the merger neatly resolves. The 14% support stockholder commitment effectively de-risks the tender minimum. Finally, no Biogen/Apellis product overlap means antitrust review is genuinely routine — closing-risk skew is far smaller than typical pharma deals.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- May 13, 2026 initial tender expiration (extension routine if tender minimum not yet met)
- HSR clearance during Q2 2026
- Section 251(h) back-end merger and cash settlement (Q2/Q3 2026)
- CVR effective issuance and Syfovre 2027 sales trajectory updates

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Tender mechanics + CVR are knowable |
| Moat | 6 | Syfovre ~60% GA share; complement IP |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | $466M cash, $94M convert resolved by deal |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | 0.24% spread on cash; CVR is the cushion |
| Catalyst | 9 | Hard-dated tender / close timeline |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Limited upside, ~30% break-risk downside |
| Business Quality | 7 | Newly profitable, growing franchise |
| Executability | 9 | Large-cap biotech, deep liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Deal-break or zero-CVR scenario |
| Macro Independence | 9 | Pure deal-event, market-uncorrelated |
| **TOTAL** | **70** | |

---

CNTA.name: Centessa Pharmaceuticals plc
CNTA.content:
# 157. CNTA — Centessa Pharmaceuticals plc
## M&A ARBITRAGE — CVR | Lilly Take-Private $38 + $9 CVR | Score: 76/100

### THE SETUP
Eli Lilly announced the acquisition of Centessa on March 31, 2026 at $38.00 cash plus a $9.00 contingent value right tied to three regulatory/commercial milestones. CNTA trades at $39.66 — a -4% gross spread to the cash floor — meaning the market is pricing roughly $1.66 of CVR value, or ~18% probability-weighted. This is a CVR pricing trade, not a deal-break trade.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Centessa is a UK-incorporated clinical-stage biotech whose lead asset is an orexin-receptor agonist for narcolepsy and other sleep-wake disorders. Lilly is paying ~$6.3B headline at the cash floor for a pre-revenue biotech because the orexin program has shown highly differentiated efficacy in narcolepsy type 1 — a category Lilly does not currently play in but where it sees long-term commercial fit alongside its neuroscience and metabolic franchises. The structure (UK scheme of arrangement, cash + CVR) is standard for cross-border biotech take-privates and provides high deal certainty on the cash component.

The bear case centers on the CVR. If the FDA approval, label, or sales milestones slip, the $9 CVR pays $0 and CNTA holders end up with $38 (down ~4% from current price, plus opportunity cost over a 6-month wait). UK scheme-of-arrangement timelines can stretch — court hearings, shareholder meetings, regulatory clearance from CMA and HSR. While Lilly/Centessa overlap is essentially zero, antitrust delay risk is non-zero in the current environment. A hostile FDA outcome on a pivotal Phase 3 readout between signing and close could give Lilly a MAC argument, though the deal value is small relative to Lilly's market cap.

The bull case is that the CVR milestones are well-defined and the orexin Phase 2 data is strong. If you believe the program is genuinely best-in-class for narcolepsy, two of the three milestones are likely achievable on a 24-36 month horizon, implying a probability-weighted CVR closer to $4-6, not $1.66. The cash floor caps downside at $38, the deal certainty is high (LLY balance sheet, no overlap), and the asymmetry favors CVR believers. A typical biotech CVR trades for 15-25% of headline value at signing; reaching that range on this deal would imply $40.5-40.25, putting current price near fair-CVR consensus.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The screen flags a -4% spread and looks past it. The actual trade is the CVR composition — which the proxy/scheme document (filed within 30-60 days of announcement) will detail with milestone definitions, payment triggers, and time-bar dates. Reading the CVR agreement reveals whether the $9 is one $9 payment or three $3 tranches (the latter is much more valuable on probability-weighted basis because partial wins are still wins). Also hidden: Lilly's typical CVR payment record on prior biotech acquisitions — a track record screen wouldn't pick up. Finally, the orexin field has multiple competing programs (Takeda, Alkermes); the CVR's commercial milestone may be the hardest to hit if competitive launches compress the addressable market.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- UK scheme document and CVR agreement filing — expected May/June 2026
- CMA + HSR clearance milestones — summer 2026
- Court hearing and scheme effective date — expected Q3 2026

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | UK scheme + cash + CVR — well-trodden structure |
| Moat | N/A | Pre-revenue; deal-driven |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Lilly is acquirer; LLY is investment-grade |
| Margin of Safety | 6 | $38 floor vs $39.66 = -4% gross |
| Catalyst | 9 | Definitive deal signed; clear timeline |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | $38 down / $47 up if all CVR hits |
| Business Quality | N/A | Clinical-stage biotech |
| Executability | 8 | $6.3B cap; liquid trading |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | UK scheme delays; CVR worth $0 |
| Macro Independence | 9 | Idiosyncratic; deal-driven |
| **TOTAL** | **59** | Cash floor solid; CVR pricing requires diligence |

---
CNTA.analystSummary:
Centessa is a UK-incorporated clinical-stage biotech whose lead asset is an orexin-receptor agonist for narcolepsy and other sleep-wake disorders. Lilly is paying ~$6.3B headline at the cash floor for a pre-revenue biotech because the orexin program has shown highly differentiated efficacy in narcolepsy type 1 — a category Lilly does not currently play in but where it sees long-term commercial fit alongside its neuroscience and metabolic franchises. The structure (UK scheme of arrangement, cash + CVR) is standard for cross-border biotech take-privates and provides high deal certainty on the cash component.

The bear case centers on the CVR. If the FDA approval, label, or sales milestones slip, the $9 CVR pays $0 and CNTA holders end up with $38 (down ~4% from current price, plus opportunity cost over a 6-month wait). UK scheme-of-arrangement timelines can stretch — court hearings, shareholder meetings, regulatory clearance from CMA and HSR. While Lilly/Centessa overlap is essentially zero, antitrust delay risk is non-zero in the current environment. A hostile FDA outcome on a pivotal Phase 3 readout between signing and close could give Lilly a MAC argument, though the deal value is small relative to Lilly's market cap.

The bull case is that the CVR milestones are well-defined and the orexin Phase 2 data is strong. If you believe the program is genuinely best-in-class for narcolepsy, two of the three milestones are likely achievable on a 24-36 month horizon, implying a probability-weighted CVR closer to $4-6, not $1.66. The cash floor caps downside at $38, the deal certainty is high (LLY balance sheet, no overlap), and the asymmetry favors CVR believers. A typical biotech CVR trades for 15-25% of headline value at signing; reaching that range on this deal would imply $40.5-40.25, putting current price near fair-CVR consensus.
CNTA.whatScreenCantSee:
The screen flags a -4% spread and looks past it. The actual trade is the CVR composition — which the proxy/scheme document (filed within 30-60 days of announcement) will detail with milestone definitions, payment triggers, and time-bar dates. Reading the CVR agreement reveals whether the $9 is one $9 payment or three $3 tranches (the latter is much more valuable on probability-weighted basis because partial wins are still wins). Also hidden: Lilly's typical CVR payment record on prior biotech acquisitions — a track record screen wouldn't pick up. Finally, the orexin field has multiple competing programs (Takeda, Alkermes); the CVR's commercial milestone may be the hardest to hit if competitive launches compress the addressable market.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- UK scheme document and CVR agreement filing — expected May/June 2026
- CMA + HSR clearance milestones — summer 2026
- Court hearing and scheme effective date — expected Q3 2026

BLX.TO.name: Boralex Inc.
BLX.TO.content:
# 159. BLX.TO — Boralex Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE | C$37.25 cash deal, 1.66% gross spread | Score: 74/100

### THE SETUP
Brookfield Asset Management and La Caisse (CDPQ) signed a definitive agreement on March 25, 2026 to take Boralex private at C$37.25/share all-cash, valuing the Canadian renewable IPP at roughly US$9 billion. Stock at C$36.64 offers a clean 1.66% gross spread / ~2.94% annualized to a Q4 2026 close — a low-risk M&A arb anchored by a domestic acquirer (no Investment Canada Act blow-up risk).

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Boralex is a Quebec-headquartered renewable independent power producer with wind, solar, hydro and storage assets in Canada, France and the US, ~3 GW operating capacity plus a multi-GW development pipeline. The deal: Brookfield acquires the equity at C$37.25 cash with La Caisse rolling/contributing to retain a 30% partner stake in the privatized vehicle. Total enterprise value ~US$9B. Current price C$36.64 vs. C$37.25 deal price = 1.66% gross spread; annualized ~2.94% assuming a Q4 2026 close (roughly 7 months). Boralex's last public trading multiple was ~10x EV/EBITDA, in line with peer renewable IPPs. The transaction is structured as a plan of arrangement requiring 66.67% Boralex shareholder approval and Quebec Superior Court sanction.

Bear case: the spread is thin — 1.66% gross translates to single-digit annualized returns and offers little cushion if the deal slips two quarters or breaks. Investment Canada Act review, while less fraught for a Canadian acquirer, can still take 6-9 months for a strategic energy asset of this size. La Caisse already owns a stake which simplifies optics but means CDPQ governance must be re-papered. Multi-jurisdictional regulatory consents (FERC for US wind farms, French CRE/préfectoral consents for European wind/solar, Quebec's energy ministry) introduce schedule risk. A mild rate-cut surprise that re-rates IPPs could see Boralex shareholders agitating for a price bump à la Crown Castle / Innergex precedents. And a 1.66% spread offers no upside — if it widens to 4% on a delay, the IRR collapses.

Bull case: this is a domestic acquirer (Brookfield BAM is Canadian-incorporated; La Caisse is the Quebec pension), eliminating the Investment Canada "net benefit" hostility seen in foreign infrastructure takeouts. La Caisse rolling 30% means a key existing shareholder is signed-off — institutional vote support is already substantially de-risked. The C$37.25 price represents ~28% premium to undisturbed and was recommended unanimously by Boralex's special committee. Arbitrage upside: closes Q3 instead of Q4 → annualized return jumps to 4-5%; deal sweetener if a topping bid emerges (TotalEnergies, Engie or Iberdrola have all looked at Canadian wind assets).

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The deal documents disclose La Caisse's reinvestment as a "significant minority" rolled at deal price — meaning CDPQ is effectively underwriting the valuation with its own balance sheet. That signals Brookfield is not stretching. The merger agreement also reportedly includes a no-shop with a customary fiduciary out and a break fee in the ~3% range, which discourages a bidding war but doesn't preclude one. Cross-border arb angle: Boralex is Canadian-listed only (no NYSE listing), so US arbs need to clear FX exposure — most run a CAD/USD forward into the close date, locking in the spread in USD terms. Insurance arbs note the deal financing is internally funded at Brookfield (no external bridge), which removes the financing-out contingency that has tripped up several 2024-25 take-privates.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Boralex shareholder vote (special meeting expected June-July 2026)
- Investment Canada Act and Competition Act clearance (target Q3 2026)
- FERC and French regulatory consents → expected close Q4 2026

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Plain-vanilla cash deal arb |
| Moat | 6 | Renewable IPP — long-dated PPAs |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | ~4x net debt / EBITDA, project-financed |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | 1.66% gross spread is thin |
| Catalyst | 9 | Defined close date Q4 2026 |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Capped upside, real break-risk to ~C$30 |
| Business Quality | 7 | Contracted cashflows, 3 GW operating |
| Executability | 9 | C$9B mcap, deep liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | Break = ~17% downside to undisturbed |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Spread insensitive to rates short-term |
| **TOTAL** | **70** | |

---

- Investment Canada Act and Competition Act clearance (target Q3 2026)
- FERC and French regulatory consents → expected close Q4 2026

KW.name: Kennedy Wilson Holdings, Inc.
KW.content:
# 160. KW — Kennedy Wilson Holdings, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE | 0.1% gross spread | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
Kennedy Wilson, the global real estate operator and investment manager, agreed on November 24, 2025 to be taken private by founder/CEO Bill McMorrow's management group with Fairfax Financial financing at $10.90/share all-cash ($1.65B). With shares effectively pegged at the deal price (gross spread ~0.1%), this is one of the tightest pending arbs on the tape — but it's a true MBO, with all the conflict-of-interest and litigation surface that implies.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Kennedy Wilson is a global real estate platform with two major segments: a $30B+ AUM investment management business (multifamily, industrial, debt platform with Fairfax) and a balance-sheet portfolio of multifamily and commercial properties primarily in the western US, UK, and Ireland. The post-pandemic period has been brutal — book value compressed by interest rate hikes on its long-duration property holdings, the dividend was cut, and the stock spent two years drifting in the $7-11 range. The November 2025 buyout values KW at about 0.85x reported book and ~$10.90 cash per share, which insiders argue reflects the realizable mark — though minority holders argue it's a lowball given the franchise value of the credit JV with Fairfax.

The bear case is straightforward MBO conflict. Bill McMorrow founded KW in 1988 and runs the take-private alongside Fairfax, a long-standing capital partner. The same insiders who priced the deal also negotiated the deal. Multiple law firms have announced investigations. Standard PE-style downside risks apply: financing contingencies, debt market shifts, Fairfax board approval flow, and a slow shareholder vote calendar pushing close into late Q2 or Q3 2026. With essentially zero spread there is no compensation for any of this risk — adverse outcomes (litigation drag, vote delay, MFW remediation) only hurt.

The bull case for buying here is opportunistic — if the spread widens to 1.5-2% on litigation noise without changing the underlying close probability, it becomes a pure low-rate yield-pickup arb backed by Fairfax's balance sheet. Fairfax (Prem Watsa) has reputational incentive not to walk; the company is operationally in better shape after 2024 dispositions; and the path to close looks routine for a real estate MBO. Today, however, the spread compensates nobody.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
This deal has a "go-shop" period and a special committee process — minority shareholder lawsuits typically force either a small bump or expanded disclosure (proxy supplements). Watch the preliminary proxy for: (1) the special committee's projections vs. management's projections (gaps signal undervaluation arguments), (2) bankers' DCF range vs. $10.90 deal price, (3) any second-bidder names in the background section. Fairfax already owns ~9% of KW common stock and has a deep credit-platform JV — meaning the buyer arguably already controls economic exposure regardless of vote outcome. The CEO and senior executives are rolling significant equity, which both confirms thesis and creates the conflict.

### CATALYSTS
- Definitive proxy filing — expected Q1 2026
- Shareholder vote — expected Q2 2026
- Deal close — guided Q2 2026

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Factor | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 6/10 |
| Management quality | 5/10 |
| Balance sheet | 5/10 |
| Moat / pricing power | 5/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 5/10 |
| Insider alignment | 4/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 9/10 |
| Downside protection | 8/10 |
| Margin of safety | 4/10 |
| Simplicity / understandability | 8/10 |
| **Total** | **64/100** |

---

FFIC.name: Flushing Financial Corporation
FFIC.content:
# 161. FFIC — Flushing Financial Corporation
## M&A ARBITRAGE | 0.85 OCFC stock-for-stock | Score: 62/100

### THE SETUP
Stock-for-stock bank merger with all approvals received and shareholder vote passed; sub-2% annualized spread reflects near-certainty of Q2 2026 close. The "asymmetry" here is not the spread — it is whether to use FFIC as a cheaper proxy for OCFC ahead of integration synergies and the Warburg Pincus $225M strategic capital injection.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Flushing Financial is a $9B-asset NY metro community bank with deep multifamily and Asian-American commercial customer concentration in Queens, Manhattan and Long Island. OceanFirst (NJ-based, ~$13B assets) announced the merger 12/29/2025 at an exchange ratio of 0.85 OCFC per FFIC share, valuing the deal at ~$579M. The Warburg Pincus $225M concurrent PIPE into the combined company de-risked capital and signaled institutional comfort with credit marks on FFIC's CRE book. Shareholders voted yes; Fed/OCC/NY DFS approvals were granted by April 2026.

Bear case: FFIC trades essentially at deal value — there is no spread to speak of and downside reopens if the OCFC share price slides between now and close. FFIC's underlying multifamily/CRE concentration in NYC remains pressured by the 2019 NY rent law, and OceanFirst inherits ~$3B of that book. If credit deterioration accelerates post-close, the merged entity could be forced to mark down further, swallowing synergies. Liquidity in FFIC also collapses into the close window — exits become harder.

Bull/catalyst: The Warburg PIPE adds a private-equity validator and ~10% pro-forma capital cushion, allowing OceanFirst to absorb credit marks without dilution. Cost synergies of ~30%+ on FFIC's expense base were guided. For shareholders willing to accept OCFC stock, the combined entity becomes a $22B mid-Atlantic franchise with NY metro reach — a logical re-rating candidate at 1.2-1.4x tangible book vs current ~1.0x. Closing in Q2 2026 unlocks that.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The DEFM14A reveals OceanFirst negotiated a 4.5% breakup fee and a "double trigger" walk right tied to MUB (material adverse change) defined to specifically exclude credit-quality deterioration on the multifamily book — meaning OCFC accepted the rent-stabilized exposure with eyes open. Flushing's chairman John Buran rolled into the merged board, suggesting management continuity. The 0.85 ratio was set against a 30-day VWAP collar that has since eroded as OCFC traded down ~6% post-announcement on integration concerns; FFIC holders effectively own a discounted call on OCFC's recovery.

### CATALYSTS
- Q2 2026 deal close (May-June expected based on regulatory clock)
- First combined-entity earnings release Q3 2026 with synergy roadmap
- Warburg Pincus PIPE funding and board representation finalization

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Vanilla bank stock merger |
| Moat | 5 | NY ethnic-deposit niche; eroding |
| Balance Sheet | 5 | $9B assets; CRE/multifamily concentrated |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | $16.29 vs $16.34 deal — 0.31% spread |
| Catalyst | 8 | Q2 2026 close highly probable |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 4 | Limited upside, real OCFC tape risk |
| Business Quality | 5 | ROIC ~8%; NIM compressed |
| Executability | 7 | $580M deal; ample liquidity |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | OCFC weak, NYC CRE shock |
| Macro Independence | 3 | Bank tied to rates + NYC RE |
| **TOTAL** | **54** | Sub-2% annualized — pass for arb specialists |

---

EQH.name: Equitable Holdings, Inc.
EQH.content:
# 162. EQH — Equitable Holdings, Inc.
## M&A ARB | CRBG All-Stock MOE — Negative Spread Implies Bump | Score: 58/100

### THE SETUP
On March 26, 2026, Equitable Holdings (EQH) and Corebridge Financial (CRBG) announced an all-stock merger of equals creating a ~$22B insurance and wealth firm. EQH receives 1.55516 NewCo shares per EQH share; CRBG receives 1.000. The market is trading EQH at $41.73 vs. an implied $41.61 deal value — a slight negative spread suggesting market expectation of a price bump or competing bid.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Equitable Holdings is the former AXA Equitable U.S. life insurance and retirement business, spun out of AXA via IPO in 2018. Its core franchises include Equitable Financial (variable annuities and life insurance), AllianceBernstein (publicly-traded asset manager AB), and Equitable Advisors (wealth management distribution). The CRBG combination — announced as a true MOE rather than an acquisition — would create a top-5 U.S. life and retirement player with combined scale in annuities, group retirement, individual life, and asset management. AIG remains a meaningful CRBG holder; the deal would help AIG ultimately monetize its CRBG stake into a more diversified vehicle.

The bear case starts with the negative spread itself: trading through deal price means downside if the deal closes at announced terms or breaks. Insurance MOEs carry meaningful execution risk — multi-state insurance regulator approvals (NY, CT, TX, IA), Fed/OCC for the holding combination, antitrust review, and shareholder votes from both sides. The all-stock structure exposes EQH holders to CRBG share-price drift before close. Variable annuity hedging programs, RILA reserves, and reinsurance side-car structures create complexity that integration teams underestimate. Activist agitation for a higher exchange ratio is possible but typically fails in agreed MOEs. Long timeline (Q4 2026 expected close) extends regulatory and market-condition risk.

The bull case is twofold: (1) a competing bidder for either side could emerge — life insurance consolidation has been steady (Brookfield, KKR, Apollo, Sixth Street all active), and EQH's wealth/AB stake has alternative-strategic value; (2) negotiated bumps are common when targets trade through, especially in MOEs where each side's board can renegotiate exchange ratios pre-vote. AB stake monetization, scale-driven cost synergies, and a re-rating of the combined entity to a higher ROE multiple are plausible.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 1.55516 exchange ratio implies a specific reference price relationship between EQH and CRBG that is not fixed-value — meaning the spread will move with both stocks. Read the merger agreement (when filed) for any walk-rights, top-up mechanics, and break-fee structure. Equitable's 61% ownership of AllianceBernstein (AB) is the embedded optionality the screen ignores: AB could be sold or spun separately, and the combined NewCo will need to address that strategic question. Insurance regulator approval timing in NY (DFS) and IA is the binding constraint, not antitrust. Watch for proxy advisor recommendations (ISS, Glass Lewis) — in MOEs these often determine whether one side's shareholders force a bump.

### CATALYSTS
- Joint proxy/S-4 filing expected Q2-Q3 2026 with detailed exchange-ratio analysis
- Shareholder votes from EQH and CRBG (likely Q3-Q4 2026)
- Insurance regulator approvals (NY DFS, multi-state) through Q4 2026

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Life insurance accounting is genuinely opaque |
| Moat | 6 | Distribution scale, AB stake, and regulatory capital franchise |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | Insurance leverage normal; combined ~$22B equity value |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | Trading through deal price; no margin in arb |
| Catalyst | 7 | Defined catalysts with proxy timing |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Bump optionality offset by deal-break/CRBG drift risk |
| Business Quality | 6 | Mid-quality; ROE improving but VA hedging is volatile |
| Executability | 9 | Highly liquid large-cap |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Deal break or terms unchanged means flat-to-negative return |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Highly rate- and equity-market sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **56** | Pure bump-speculation trade; size accordingly |

---

CRBG.name: Corebridge Financial, Inc.
CRBG.content:
# 163. CRBG — Corebridge Financial, Inc.
## ACTIVIST — SALE OR BREAKUP | EQH all-stock merger of equals (Mar 26, 2026) | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
On March 26, 2026, Corebridge Financial (the AIG life & retirement spin) and Equitable Holdings announced a $22B all-stock merger of equals — combined entity will be 51% CRBG / 49% EQH. Stock trades right at implied combined value ($26.75 vs $26.76 deal value), so the trade is no longer arbitrage but a directional bet on the pro forma franchise.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Corebridge Financial is the former AIG Life & Retirement business, IPO'd by AIG in September 2022 and progressively distributed to AIG shareholders and to anchor investor Blackstone (which also took a stake in the asset management arm and provided private credit origination). The business spans individual retirement (annuities, mutual funds), group retirement (recordkeeping for K-12, higher ed, healthcare), life insurance, and institutional markets. Equitable Holdings is similarly composed: variable annuities, group retirement (former AXA Equitable), and a 65% stake in AllianceBernstein. The combined entity would be one of the largest US life and retirement platforms with substantial scale in annuity manufacturing and distribution.

The bear case starts with the deal itself. All-stock merger of equals deals historically underperform, integration of two life insurance balance sheets is genuinely complex (capital regimes, hedge programs, asset/liability matching across two general accounts), and there is meaningful regulatory risk in life insurance (state insurance commissioners across multiple states must approve). Combined entity will have significant variable annuity exposure — a product line with hedging and equity market sensitivity. Synergy estimates from these deals are typically optimistic. With essentially zero arb spread, holders take all the deal break risk for very little reward. Blackstone's role as anchor investor and asset manager creates governance complexity.

The bull case requires looking past the deal arithmetic to the pro forma franchise. The combined entity would have differentiated scale in retirement, particularly in tax-exempt markets (K-12, healthcare). Cost synergies from duplicative corporate functions are real, and revenue synergies from cross-selling Corebridge's individual annuities into Equitable's group retirement footprint could be material. The Blackstone relationship continues to provide private credit yield enhancement on the general account — a genuine structural advantage versus mutual life insurers. If the deal closes and synergies materialize, the combined entity should command a higher multiple than either standalone.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Corebridge 10-K's general account asset disclosures detail the Blackstone-originated private credit allocation, which is rapidly growing as a percentage of investment portfolio and earning meaningfully higher yields than legacy public corporate bonds — providing a structural NIM advantage that takes time to compound. Also: the proxy filings for the merger will detail break fees, "go-shop" provisions (or absence thereof), and which board seats Blackstone retains in the combined entity — these governance details often determine whether minority holders get fair treatment in subsequent transactions.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Definitive merger proxy filing (likely Q2 2026); shareholder vote (Q3 2026)
- State insurance regulatory approvals across multiple jurisdictions (rolling through Q3-Q4 2026)
- Expected close Q4 2026; first combined-entity guidance issued

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 6 | Life insurance balance sheets are complex |
| Moat | 6 | Scale + Blackstone yield enhancement |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | Insurance liabilities; capital ratios adequate |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | Trades at deal value, no buffer |
| Catalyst | 7 | Defined merger close path |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 4 | All deal-break risk, no spread |
| Business Quality | 6 | Annuity manufacturing, decent ROE |
| Executability | 9 | Mega-cap, very liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | MOE deals often underperform |
| Macro Independence | 4 | VA exposure to equity markets and rates |
| **TOTAL** | **58** | |

HCBN.name: HCB Financial Corp.
HCBN.content:
# 164. HCBN — HCB Financial Corp.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | 6.8% Annualized Spread | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
HCB Financial Corp., a small Michigan community bank holding company, agreed on March 18, 2026 to be acquired by Independent Bank Corporation (IBCP) for ~$72.81 per share in mixed cash and stock (1.590 IBCP shares + $17.51 cash). HCBN trades at $71.00 — a ~2.55% gross spread to a Q3 2026 close, juicy for a vanilla bank tuck-in but constrained by OTC illiquidity and IBCP stock-price drift risk on the unhedged equity portion.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
HCB Financial is the holding company for Hillsdale County Bank, a small Michigan community bank with a few branches concentrated in south-central Michigan. The franchise is exactly the type of sub-$1B-asset community bank that has seen consolidation pressure intensify with rate-cycle stress and rising compliance costs. Independent Bank Corporation (IBCP, ~$5B assets) is a rational consolidator — heavy in Michigan, runs cleaner cost ratios, and can take out HCB's core deposits while wringing duplicative back-office and IT spend. The deal headline of $72.81 per HCB share, totaling $70.2M, prices the bank at a modest premium to tangible book.

The bear case starts with the structure: HCBN holders receive 1.590 IBCP shares per HCB share plus $17.51 cash. That means roughly 76% of the consideration is exposed to IBCP's stock. If IBCP drops 10%, your implied take-out value drops to roughly $67.50 — wiping out the spread. There is no collar disclosed in the public release, which is unusual for community-bank stock deals and concentrates spot-price risk on holders. Add OTC liquidity (HCBN is thinly traded), a small absolute float, and the practical inability to short IBCP at retail-friendly borrow costs, and this is structurally hard to professionalize. Regulatory risk is genuinely low — Fed/OCC/state community-bank approvals are nearly automatic for a deal this size — but execution timing can drift one quarter on examiner scheduling.

The bull case is for a small fund or family-office investor willing to ratio-hedge IBCP. At 1.590:1 short-IBCP / long-HCBN, you isolate the cash component and the spread to deal close, generating a low-teens annualized IRR if Q3 2026 close holds. IBCP itself trades at reasonable bank metrics (~10x earnings, 1.2x TBV); the post-merger combined entity should accrete EPS by mid-single digits as cost synergies (likely 30-40% of HCB's expense base) realize through 2027.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Community-bank merger arbs of this size almost never appear in screener-driven datasets because HCBN reports through OTC Markets rather than EDGAR's clean structured-data pipeline. The 8-K filings on the IBCP side disclose a customary $2.8M termination fee and a fiduciary out — both standard for $70M deals. What's not in the screen: the proxy will likely reveal director and 10% holders signed voting agreements covering 30%+ of shares, making the shareholder vote a formality. Hillsdale County Bank's loan book is concentrated in agricultural and small-business lending in Michigan — an area where IBCP already has scale and underwriting models, supporting fast integration.

### CATALYSTS
- Proxy / S-4 effectiveness (expected May-June 2026)
- HCBN shareholder vote (estimated July 2026)
- Regulatory close + cash/stock distribution Q3 2026

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 6/10 |
| Management integrity | 7/10 |
| Capital allocation | 7/10 |
| Balance sheet | 7/10 |
| Moat durability | 5/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 5/10 |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Margin of safety | 6/10 |
| Optionality | 5/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 9/10 |
| **Total** | **64/100** |

---
