LNKB.name: LINKBANCORP, Inc.
LNKB.content:
# 165. LNKB — LINKBANCORP, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE | 0.135 BHRB exchange | Score: 72/100

### THE SETUP
All-stock acquisition by Burke & Herbert Financial Services (BHRB) at 0.135 shares per LNKB, announced December 18, 2025. All regulatory approvals received, shareholder vote passed, scheduled to close May 1, 2026 — one of the cleanest small-cap bank arbitrage closes on the calendar.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
LINKBANCORP is a Pennsylvania/New Jersey community bank being acquired by Virginia-based Burke & Herbert (BHRB) in an all-stock deal valued at ~$354M ($8.94 per LNKB share at the deal-announce reference price). The exchange ratio is fixed at 0.135 BHRB per LNKB share. As of the snapshot, LNKB trades at $8.94 — implied parity with no gross spread, reflecting that all approvals are in hand, the shareholder vote has passed, and the scheduled closing date is May 1, 2026.

The bear case for an arb: there is no spread to capture at parity. The remaining risk is BHRB share-price movement between now and close — if BHRB drops, the implied LNKB value drops with it (fixed exchange ratio, no collar disclosed at this level of detail). Community bank M&A occasionally experiences last-minute regulatory adjustments, MAC clauses, or accounting surprises that can delay close by 30-90 days. Any post-close BHRB selling pressure from former LNKB holders who don't want to own a small-cap Virginia bank could create technical weakness in the combined entity.

The bull case: this is a textbook clean small-cap bank merger arb that will close. All regulatory boxes are checked. Shareholder vote done. Close date specific. The trade for arb funds is not LNKB itself but rather a BHRB short / LNKB long pair to lock in the 0.135 ratio — a low-risk, low-return spread trade. For a fundamental holder, the more interesting question is whether the pro-forma BHRB (post-LNKB integration) is worth holding: the combined bank would have meaningful Mid-Atlantic deposit franchise and improved scale on tech / compliance spend. BHRB has historically traded at a discount to mid-cap community bank comps.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The merger proxy (DEF 14A filed by LNKB in early 2026) contains the standard fairness opinion analysis and discloses the dollar amount of integration costs and expected cost saves — usually 25-35% of LNKB's pre-deal expense base for community bank combinations of this scale. The proxy also discloses change-of-control payments to LNKB executives, which are materially below what comparable mergers have paid (a positive signal for shareholder alignment but a negative signal for LNKB management retention). The deal is structured as a tax-free stock-for-stock exchange under IRC Section 368(a), meaning no taxable event for LNKB holders at close — relevant for personal-account holders deciding whether to sell pre-close.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
- **Closing date May 1, 2026** — share conversion to BHRB; LNKB delists.
- **Q2 2026 pro-forma BHRB earnings (August 2026)** — first full quarter with integration cost saves visible.
- **BHRB integration milestones through 2H 2026** — system conversion, branch consolidation, deposit retention.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Business quality (moat, ROIC) | 5 |
| 2 | Management capability & alignment | 6 |
| 3 | Balance sheet strength | 6 |
| 4 | Reinvestment runway | 5 |
| 5 | Earnings predictability | 7 |
| 6 | Margin of safety (price vs value) | 5 |
| 7 | Catalyst clarity | 9 |
| 8 | Downside protection | 7 |
| 9 | Insider behavior | 6 |
| 10 | Simplicity / understandability | 8 |
| **Total** | | **64/100** |

---

ONTF.name: ON24, Inc.
ONTF.content:
# 166. ONTF — ON24, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE | $8.10 All-Cash from Cvent/Blackstone | Score: 60/100

### THE SETUP
ON24 announced on January 15, 2026 a definitive all-cash sale to Cvent (Blackstone-owned) at $8.10/share, $350M total. Stock trades exactly at the deal price — zero spread to capture, but the March 26 vote and 1H 2026 close make this a near-zero-risk parking spot rather than an arb opportunity.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
ON24 is a webinar / digital-engagement SaaS platform that IPO'd in early 2021 at peak SaaS multiples (~$50/share), benefited from the COVID-era virtual-events boom, and then de-rated viciously as enterprise customers consolidated webinar/event platforms post-pandemic. Annual recurring revenue has been declining for several years as net-revenue-retention dropped below 90% and the company struggled to differentiate against Zoom Webinars, Microsoft Teams Live Events, and Cvent itself. Activist Ondine Capital pushed for strategic review in 2024-2025; the result is the Cvent take-private at $8.10.

The bear case for sitting in ONTF here: there is literally no spread. Stock at $8.10, deal at $8.10. The only return profile is (a) zero, if the deal closes on schedule, or (b) significantly negative if the deal breaks. Cvent is a strategic + Blackstone-financed buyer, so financing risk is low, but HSR review and shareholder-vote outcomes are still execution risks. With zero spread, the asymmetry is awful — you are taking deal-break risk (estimated 5-10% probability of $5-6 trough re-rating, or -25-30% drawdown) for no upside. The only rational use of this position is as a brief cash-equivalent parking spot with March 26 vote and 1H26 close as the unwinding date.

The bull case is narrow: ONTF holds significant cash on the balance sheet, and a topping bid is theoretically possible — but Ondine Capital agreed to vote in favor, and the go-shop period (if any) has expired without a competing bidder emerging. Realistically there is no bull case to add capital here at $8.10. The pre-deal interest is over. For investors who already owned ONTF below $5, this is the exit; for new capital, this is not a setup.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The DEFM14A merger proxy details Ondine Capital's voting agreement (locks up the activist's stake to vote yes), the go-shop provisions (already expired with no superior proposal), and the termination fee schedule (typically 3-4% of equity value, which would be ~$10-14M). Buried in the proxy: ON24's net cash position is meaningful relative to the $350M deal value — the implied enterprise value is much smaller than headline equity value, which is why Cvent could justify the price even for a declining-ARR business. Also missed by screens: ON24's customer base includes pharma and life-sciences accounts with regulated-event compliance requirements that Cvent specifically wanted to acquire — this is a strategic combination, not a financial cost-take-out, which lowers deal-break probability versus pure-PE LBOs.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **Mar 26, 2026** — Shareholder vote on the merger.
2. **1H 2026 / Q2** — Expected merger close pending HSR clearance.
3. **Pre-close** — Any topping bid window (low probability; go-shop expired).

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 4 |
| Management integrity | 5 |
| Capital allocation | 5 |
| Balance sheet | 7 |
| Moat | 3 |
| Reinvestment runway | 3 |
| Insider alignment | 6 |
| Valuation | 5 |
| Catalyst clarity | 9 |
| Downside protection | 6 |
| **Total** | **53/100** |

---

PLTK.name: Playtika Holding Corp.
PLTK.content:
# 167. PLTK — Playtika Holding Corp.
## TAKE-PRIVATE | M&A SPECULATION | Score: 38/100

### THE SETUP

Playtika is an Israeli mobile gaming company (Slotomania, Bingo Blitz, June's Journey, Solitaire Grand Harvest) that has been in slow decline as legacy social-casino bookings shrink and user acquisition economics deteriorate. The stock trades around $3.62 against a speculative take-private narrative: per InsideArbitrage, a "special committee" was formed on April 7, 2026 to enhance shareholder value at an indicative $5.00/share all-cash valuation (~$1.7B equity check). That implies a 38% gross spread and ~57% annualized return if a deal closes by Q4 2026. There is no definitive agreement, no named acquirer, and no shareholder vote scheduled. This is a hope-trade dressed up as merger arbitrage.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

The defining feature of Playtika's cap table is its controlling shareholder Alpha Frontier, a Chinese consortium that acquired its stake from Giant Network (Yuzhu Shi) in 2019. Alpha Frontier holds majority voting control, which means: (1) any take-private cannot happen without their consent, but also (2) any minority squeeze-out is functionally a negotiation between Alpha Frontier, the special committee, and a hypothetical PE buyer. Minority public holders are price-takers. CFIUS is unlikely to be triggered (Playtika's operations are Israel/EU/US-facing consumer, not defense), but the optics of a Chinese-controlled Israeli mobile-gaming asset make the buyer slate awkward — Saudi PIF (which has bid for similar assets), Apollo, or Embracer-style consolidators are conceivable but each carries structural friction.

Operationally, Playtika has retreated from growth M&A and shifted to capital return (dividend, buybacks) while DAU and bookings have decompressed. EBITDA is still material but the business is structurally challenged: Apple's IDFA deprecation crushed paid UA, Slotomania faces sweepstakes-casino cannibalization, and the core "casual social casino" cohort is aging. A $5 take-private would be a 5–6x EBITDA exit — cheap for a public market but consistent with where private gaming deals have printed in 2024–2025 (e.g., Jagex, Embracer divestitures).

The market's pricing of $3.62 reflects ~30–40% probability of a deal at $5, with a downside case of ~$2.50–$3.00 if the special committee concludes "no deal" and discloses status quo. That's a binary, news-driven bet — not a fundamental thesis.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

A screen sees an undervalued mid-cap with a "strategic review" headline. It does not see that the controlling shareholder owns the deal economics; that no acquirer has been named or even rumored; that mobile gaming PE multiples have compressed; or that "special committees" frequently dissolve with a press release saying "the Board has determined to remain independent." This is a 38% spread because the market is correctly pricing low deal probability — not because there is a market inefficiency.

### CATALYSTS

- Definitive merger agreement announcement (the catalyst)
- Alpha Frontier signaling willingness to sell (or block)
- Q1 2026 bookings stabilization
- Special committee status update
- Termination of process (downside catalyst)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- Circle of competence: Mobile gaming UA economics — niche but knowable. PASS WITH CAVEATS.
- Durable moat: Eroding. Social casino is a declining vertical. FAIL.
- Honest, capable management: Mixed; CEO Robert Antokol has stabilized but not reignited growth. NEUTRAL.
- Margin of safety: Only if you believe deal closes at $5 — pure event-arb, not Graham margin of safety. WEAK.
- Predictable cash flows: Declining but cash-generative; covers dividend. NEUTRAL.
- Skin in the game: Alpha Frontier controls; minority interests not aligned with public float. FAIL.

Score 38/100 reflects: speculative event-driven setup with controlling-shareholder overhang, declining underlying business, and no definitive agreement. Not a Munger stock; pure merger-arb hope trade. Position sizing should reflect binary outcome distribution (small).

---

PATK.name: Patrick Industries, Inc.
PATK.content:
# 169. PATK — Patrick Industries, Inc.
## CYCLICAL TROUGH | RV/Marine Components — LCII MoE Rumor | Score: 58/100

### THE SETUP
RV and marine component supplier trading at $106.93 on rumored merger-of-equals discussions with LCI Industries (LCII) at $115/share equivalent. Discussions only — no definitive agreement — but the underlying cyclical setup (RV wholesale shipments well below 2021 peak) is the actual story. Speculative spread plus durable trough thesis.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Patrick Industries supplies decorative and functional components into the RV, marine, manufactured housing, and industrial end markets — laminates, cabinet doors, vinyl, fiberglass, plumbing, and aluminum extrusions. The company built a serial-acquisition flywheel under CEO Andy Nemeth and Todd Cleveland, executing 70+ deals over a decade to consolidate fragmented suppliers. RV wholesale shipments collapsed from a 600K+ peak in 2021 to roughly 300K in the 2024 trough, dragging Patrick's revenue and operating margins. The April 2026 InsideArbitrage report of merger-of-equals talks with LCII (the other consolidator in the space) is unconfirmed, but the strategic logic is real: combined the two would dominate RV component supply at ~$8B revenue.

The bear case is double-cyclical. RV demand is tied to consumer credit, gas prices, and discretionary income — all three look fragile in 2026. Marine is similarly exposed. Patrick's debt load from the acquisition spree is meaningful, and refinancing risk exists if rates stay higher for longer. The MoE rumor could be a head-fake that fails on antitrust grounds — RV component supply is concentrated enough that FTC review would be substantive, and either side could walk before signing definitives. If the deal evaporates, the stock likely retraces to mid-$90s.

The bull case is the cyclical recovery layered onto the MoE optionality. RV wholesale shipments are showing tentative recovery signs, dealer inventories have been worked down, and any normalization to even mid-cycle 400K shipments produces meaningful operating leverage given Patrick's fixed cost base. The MoE, if it happens, would unlock $200M+ in synergies. Even without the deal, Patrick at trough margins on trough volumes is a credible cyclical setup. Founder-operator alignment remains strong and capital allocation has been disciplined through prior cycles.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen sees an industrial supplier at a P/E that looks expensive on trough earnings — it cannot see the operating leverage embedded in the model. Patrick's contribution margin on incremental RV shipment is roughly 20%+, meaning a return to mid-cycle volumes drops several dollars to EPS. The MoE rumor is not yet in any consensus estimate. Also invisible: Patrick's increasing mix toward higher-margin marine, powersports, and housing exposure (now ~40% of revenue) which structurally reduces RV cyclicality. The proxy will eventually disclose insider ownership and the management bench depth, both of which are stronger than the screen flags.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **Q2 2026** — RV wholesale shipment data; recovery confirmation.
2. **Q2 2026** — LCII MoE definitive agreement or denial.
3. **H2 2026** — Q2/Q3 earnings showing operating leverage on volume recovery.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Business quality (moat) | 6/10 |
| 2 | Management quality | 7/10 |
| 3 | Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| 4 | Balance sheet strength | 5/10 |
| 5 | Free cash flow durability | 6/10 |
| 6 | Insider/activist alignment | 6/10 |
| 7 | Valuation (margin of safety) | 6/10 |
| 8 | Catalyst clarity & timing | 6/10 |
| 9 | Downside protection | 5/10 |
| 10 | Asymmetry (skew) | 6/10 |
| **TOTAL** | | **59/100** |

---

GSAT.name: Globalstar, Inc.
GSAT.content:
# 170. GSAT — Globalstar, Inc.
## TENDER OFFER | Amazon at $90 cash/0.3210 AMZN, ann. Apr 14, 2026 | Score: 67/100

### THE SETUP
Amazon agreed on April 14, 2026 to acquire Globalstar for $90 per share — either cash or 0.3210 AMZN shares (capped at $90) — valuing equity at roughly $11.6B. With GSAT trading at $80.02, the gross spread is 12.5% and annualized ~15% to a Q1 2027 close. Globalstar holders representing ~58% have already approved; the binary risk is concentrated in FCC spectrum review and competitor opposition.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Globalstar operates a low-earth-orbit satellite constellation providing mobile satellite services, IoT data, and SOS connectivity — best known publicly as the infrastructure behind Apple's Emergency SOS via satellite on iPhone (under a multi-year capacity reservation contract). The company also holds Band 53/n53 terrestrial spectrum in the U.S. that has long been the speculative valuation kicker. Amazon's offer at $90/share validates both the satellite-IoT/MSS business and the spectrum, and aligns with Amazon's Project Kuiper buildout — vertically integrating ground capacity, spectrum optionality, and AWS cloud destinations for satellite IoT data.

The bear case is regulatory. The FCC must approve the transfer of MSS spectrum licenses; SpaceX (a direct competitor in satellite connectivity) and other incumbents have a clear incentive to file oppositions and drag the proceeding. CFIUS will review satellite infrastructure transfers as a national-security matter; antitrust authorities (FTC and EU Commission) may scrutinize Amazon's vertical integration into the satellite-cloud stack. Deal terms reportedly include satellite-deployment milestones; a delay could trigger MAC discussions. The Apple capacity contract was a key value driver — Apple's response to Amazon owning the underlying capacity provider is an unmodeled wildcard.

The bull case is that Amazon has both the patience and the political capital to push the deal through. The 58% pre-approval block effectively eliminates shareholder-vote risk. The mixed cash/stock structure (capped collar) gives flexibility for tax-sensitive holders and reduces financing burden. At a $90 print, GSAT longs from the pre-Apple-rumor $1-2 range are looking at a generational outcome; from $80 today the trade is a clean ~12-15% annualized arb with optionality on a topping bid (unlikely given Amazon's strategic premium, but non-zero).

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The merger-agreement docs (S-4 and 14D, when filed) will disclose the precise satellite-deployment milestone covenants, the Apple-contract change-of-control mechanics, and the spectrum-license-transfer carve-outs — none of which a generic M&A spread screen captures. The Band n53 spectrum value is carried at historical cost on the balance sheet but has been independently valued at multi-billion levels by sell-side analysts. Globalstar's existing prepaid capacity reservation from Apple represents booked but undelivered revenue that flows through deferred-revenue accounts, distorting reported revenue trajectory. CFIUS mitigation agreements — common in satellite M&A — could include U.S.-government data-routing carve-outs that affect deal value but not deal closure.

### CATALYSTS
- FCC spectrum license transfer ruling: rolling, target H2 2026
- CFIUS clearance: target H2 2026
- Antitrust (HSR / EU) clearance: target Q3-Q4 2026
- Expected close: Q1 2027

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 6/10 |
| Management integrity | 6/10 |
| Capital allocation | 7/10 |
| Balance sheet | 6/10 |
| Moat durability | 7/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Margin of safety | 6/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 8/10 |
| Asymmetry | 8/10 |
| **Total** | **67/100** |

---

NSC.name: Norfolk Southern Corporation
NSC.content:
# 171. NSC — Norfolk Southern Corporation
## ACTIVIST / M&A ARB | UNP Bid $320; STB Risk | Score: 60/100

### THE SETUP
Union Pacific announced a $320/share mixed cash-stock acquisition of Norfolk Southern in July 2025 — the first-ever transcontinental Class I rail merger. NSC trades at $302.38 (5.83% gross spread, ~4.66% annualized at expected 2027-Q2 close). Both shareholder votes passed November 2025, but the Surface Transportation Board rejected the initial application January 16, 2026, leaving regulatory approval as the binary risk. Prior Ancora activist campaign already pushed operating ratios; the M&A path is the new story.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Norfolk Southern is the dominant Class I rail in the U.S. East, with a network spanning from the Mid-Atlantic to Memphis/New Orleans and into the Midwest. The 2023 East Palestine derailment forced a leadership and operating-philosophy change, eventually bringing in Ancora Holdings as an activist that won board seats in 2024 and pushed a Precision Scheduled Railroading-lite operational reset. Operating ratio improvements were beginning to materialize when Union Pacific announced the $320/share bid in July 2025, framing the deal as creating the first end-to-end transcontinental U.S. rail to compete with truck and to integrate intermodal flow.

The bear case is regulatory. The Surface Transportation Board rejected the initial filing on January 16, 2026 — a meaningful signal that the agency views the merger as creating unprecedented competitive concentration. Other Class I rails (BNSF, CSX, CPKC) have signaled opposition or demanded gateway/trackage-rights remedies that could materially erode synergies. The political climate around a transcontinental "single-rail" entity is fraught — shippers (especially ag and chemicals) have organized against the deal. If the STB ultimately rejects or imposes onerous conditions, NSC could re-rate downward toward standalone fair value (which itself is debated post-East Palestine and post-Ancora improvements).

The bull case: shareholder votes passed, both managements are committed, the strategic logic (intermodal share-shift from truck) is real, and the STB rejection of the initial application appears procedural rather than substantive — a refile with stronger remedies is expected. The 4.66% annualized spread is rich for a deal with passed shareholder votes if the regulatory path holds. Standalone NSC, even without the deal, has post-Ancora operational tailwinds and remains a high-quality duopoly East-coast rail asset. Downside is buffered by standalone earnings power.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The merger proxy discloses the exact cash/stock mix mechanics and any collar protection — critical for hedging UNP exposure. STB filings (publicly available on STB.gov) detail the proposed competitive remedies: trackage rights for competing rails over key bottleneck corridors, divestiture options, and Section 11321 conditions. NSC's 10-K discloses East Palestine remediation reserves and the related insurance recovery — a wildcard that could move standalone value. Ancora's 13D and proxy materials reveal the operational targets the board committed to even if the deal fails. Pension funded status and labor-contract reset timing (TCU, BLET, BMWED) are material to standalone earnings.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. UNP/NSC refiled STB application (1H 2026 expected).
2. STB pre-filing and discovery hearings (2026 throughout).
3. Q1/Q2 2026 NSC standalone earnings — operating ratio progression.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 8/10 |
| Management integrity | 6/10 |
| Capital allocation | 6/10 |
| Balance sheet | 6/10 |
| Moat | 9/10 (rail duopoly) |
| Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| Insider alignment | 5/10 |
| Margin of safety | 5/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 6/10 |
| Downside protection | 3/10 (STB binary) |
| **Total** | **60/100** |

---

AAL.name: American Airlines Group Inc.
AAL.content:
# 172. AAL — American Airlines Group Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE — SPECULATIVE | $12.78 vs $18 rumor | Score: 36/100

### THE SETUP
A speculative spread on the most-blocked merger imaginable: United CEO Scott Kirby pitched a tie-up with American to the Trump White House on Feb 25, 2026. American formally said "not interested" on April 17, 2026. The 41% gross spread is pricing a fairy-tale event that almost certainly won't happen. This is a value-trap dressed as arb.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

American Airlines Group (AAL) is the largest US carrier by passengers, with $54.6B in 2025 revenue, just $111M of net income, $0.17 diluted EPS, and total assets of $61.8B. The 10-K (filed Feb 18, 2026) discloses $9.2B total liquidity ($5.8B unrestricted cash and short-term investments + $3.4B undrawn revolver capacity), and a covenant-mandated minimum of $2.0B unrestricted cash. American carries massive unionized labor (14,970 APA pilots, 26,400 APFA flight attendants, 13,030 customer service, 19,460 fleet service) with most contracts amendable in 2027–2029. The 2025 financial performance was hammered by the January 2025 PSA midair collision near Reagan National (~$200M Q1 revenue impact) and a sharp drop in operating income from $2,614M (2024) to $1,467M (2025) on flat revenue.

The bear case is overwhelming: this is not a deal — it's reported chatter that AAL's own CEO publicly disowned. American on April 17, 2026 stated unequivocally it was "not engaged with or interested in any discussions regarding a merger" with United. Even if a deal were proposed, the combined US-domestic market share of 30–40% would face near-certain DOJ block (top-4 carriers already control ~80%); FAA slot/route reviews; pilot seniority integration would take years. Beyond the rumor, AAL's underlying business has standalone problems: 76 pilot/labor contracts amendable in 2027 = wage-pressure cliff; 6.50% convertible notes already paid; aircraft delivery commitments require ongoing long-term debt issuance; basic-economy fare competition from low-cost carriers compressing yields.

The bull case requires either (a) the rumor having more substance than current public disclosures suggest, or (b) AAL's standalone equity working as a cyclical recovery + deleveraging story. On (a), Trump's DOJ has signaled openness to consolidation broadly, but Transportation Sec. Duffy already indicated "significant divestitures would be required." On (b), at $12.78 with $54.6B revenue and 660M shares, market cap is ~$8.4B against trailing operating income of $1.47B — that's ~6x EV/EBIT before mid-cycle margin recovery. If pilot CBA negotiations stay rational and fuel cooperates, a return to $2.5B+ operating income is plausible by 2027, making this a 5x earnings story without any deal.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K explicitly contemplates merger risk as a one-way negative: "Other mergers and other forms of airline partnerships, including regulatory approvals such as antitrust immunity grants, may take place and may not involve us, or could result in unforeseen impacts on the industry." American's strategic posture is defensive — they are NOT the consolidator. Republic Airways (a regional partner) is referenced in the XBRL tags multiple times as Oct/Nov 2025 transactions, suggesting AAL has been quietly restructuring its regional-feed economics. The pilot union (APA) contract is amendable in 2027 — a high-stakes negotiation with no merger leverage to deflect labor demands. The Reagan National PSA crash also created multi-plaintiff lawsuits against American/PSA (separate from US Government suits), creating tail litigation exposure not yet quantified in financials.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- **APA (pilots) and APFA (flight attendants) contract opening discussions** — 2027 amendable date triggers prep negotiations through 2026
- **Q2/Q3 2026 earnings** — recovery from PSA-collision drag and demand normalization
- **Any further DOJ/Trump White House signal on airline consolidation** — would either confirm the spread is real or definitively kill it

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Airline economics well-known, brutally cyclical |
| Moat | 4 | Hub network + frequent flyer program; commoditized otherwise |
| Balance Sheet | 4 | $9.2B liquidity but ~$30B+ total debt; covenant minimum $2B cash |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | ~6x EV/EBIT, but cyclical earnings and low quality |
| Catalyst | 3 | "Merger rumor" already denied; standalone catalysts thin |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 3 | Spread requires near-impossible deal; downside to standalone fair value |
| Business Quality | 3 | Razor-thin 0.2% net margin in 2025; capital intensive |
| Executability | 9 | Massive float, deeply liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | DOJ block + labor + fuel + recession risk all live |
| Macro Independence | 2 | High beta to consumer/business travel cycle |
| **TOTAL** | **45** | Spread is a mirage; standalone is mediocre |

---

CMRC.name: Commerce.com, Inc.
CMRC.content:
# 173. CMRC — Commerce.com, Inc.
## VALUE TRAP RISK | Hostile Letter, No Definitive Deal | Score: 32/100

### THE SETUP
Rezolve AI (RZLV) sent CMRC's board an open letter on April 8, 2026, proposing to acquire the company at $4.00 in stock — a ~36% premium to the $2.94 trading price. There is no definitive agreement, no engagement signaled by CMRC's board, and the acquirer is itself a small-cap with credibility and financing questions. This is a speculative situation pretending to be a merger arb.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Commerce.com is a small-cap commerce-tech company that has been searching for relevance after a long decline from its dot-com-era peak. The current sub-$60M market cap reflects a market view that the standalone business cannot generate enough cash to justify even today's price. The Rezolve letter proposes an all-stock combination — meaning CMRC holders would receive RZLV shares whose own valuation is volatile and contested.

The bear case dominates. Open-letter offers — also called "bear hugs" — succeed less than half the time when the target board is silent or hostile. Rezolve is a small-cap acquirer whose own balance sheet may not support the consideration without a financing event, and the all-stock structure means CMRC holders inherit Rezolve equity-currency risk. The 36% gross spread is not a merger-arb spread; it is an option premium on a deal that may never materialize. If the letter is rejected and no white knight emerges, CMRC likely retraces toward its standalone fundamental value. Liquidity is thin, and a position of any size requires paying through the offer.

The bull case is binary. If RZLV puts a definitive agreement in front of the board and provides financing comfort, the spread could compress to single digits. A competing bidder (private equity rolling up the e-commerce-services space) could emerge. Activist letters often serve as a public catalyst that forces a strategic review even if the original bidder loses — sometimes producing a better outcome from a third party. But absent a board response in the next 30-60 days, the most likely path is a fade.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen sees a 36% gross spread and flags it as a juicy merger arb. It misses that there is no merger to arb. The 8-K disclosure surrounding the Rezolve letter is what matters — does CMRC acknowledge receipt, retain advisors, form a special committee? Without those procedural moves the offer is dead on arrival. Also hidden: CMRC's standalone cash burn rate, which determines the floor if the deal collapses. Insider ownership disclosures in the most recent proxy will tell you whether management has any incentive to engage, or whether they are entrenched and hostile to any bid.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- CMRC board response to Rezolve letter (expected within 30-60 days of April 8 receipt)
- Definitive agreement signing, or RZLV withdrawal of offer
- Q1 2026 earnings showing standalone cash burn

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 5 | Hostile-letter situation; no definitive terms |
| Moat | 2 | Commerce platform with fading relevance |
| Balance Sheet | 3 | Sub-$60M cap, cash burn likely |
| Margin of Safety | 3 | $2.94 may be above standalone fundamental |
| Catalyst | 4 | Open letter, no board engagement |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Up 36% / down 50%; not asymmetric enough |
| Business Quality | 2 | Persistent revenue decline historically |
| Executability | 3 | Microcap, illiquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 3 | Letter ignored, stock fades to $1.50 |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Idiosyncratic deal risk |
| **TOTAL** | **34** | Speculative; pass unless definitive deal emerges |

---

Commerce.com is a small-cap commerce-tech company that has been searching for relevance after a long decline from its dot-com-era peak. The current sub-$60M market cap reflects a market view that the standalone business cannot generate enough cash to justify even today's price. The Rezolve letter proposes an all-stock combination — meaning CMRC holders would receive RZLV shares whose own valuation is volatile and contested.

The bear case dominates. Open-letter offers — also called "bear hugs" — succeed less than half the time when the target board is silent or hostile. Rezolve is a small-cap acquirer whose own balance sheet may not support the consideration without a financing event, and the all-stock structure means CMRC holders inherit Rezolve equity-currency risk. The 36% gross spread is not a merger-arb spread; it is an option premium on a deal that may never materialize. If the letter is rejected and no white knight emerges, CMRC likely retraces toward its standalone fundamental value. Liquidity is thin, and a position of any size requires paying through the offer.

The bull case is binary. If RZLV puts a definitive agreement in front of the board and provides financing comfort, the spread could compress to single digits. A competing bidder (private equity rolling up the e-commerce-services space) could emerge. Activist letters often serve as a public catalyst that forces a strategic review even if the original bidder loses — sometimes producing a better outcome from a third party. But absent a board response in the next 30-60 days, the most likely path is a fade.
CMRC.whatScreenCantSee:
A screen sees a 36% gross spread and flags it as a juicy merger arb. It misses that there is no merger to arb. The 8-K disclosure surrounding the Rezolve letter is what matters — does CMRC acknowledge receipt, retain advisors, form a special committee? Without those procedural moves the offer is dead on arrival. Also hidden: CMRC's standalone cash burn rate, which determines the floor if the deal collapses. Insider ownership disclosures in the most recent proxy will tell you whether management has any incentive to engage, or whether they are entrenched and hostile to any bid.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- CMRC board response to Rezolve letter (expected within 30-60 days of April 8 receipt)
- Definitive agreement signing, or RZLV withdrawal of offer
- Q1 2026 earnings showing standalone cash burn

FOLD.name: Amicus Therapeutics, Inc.
FOLD.content:
# 174. FOLD — Amicus Therapeutics, Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | $14.50 all-cash | Score: 65/100

### THE SETUP
$4.8B all-cash acquisition by BioMarin at $14.50; HSR cleared early February 2026, shareholders approved March 3, only foreign regulators outstanding. Spread of 28 bps reflects market conviction Q2 2026 close happens — pure arbitrage with low-but-real EU/Japan tail risk.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Amicus is a rare-disease biotech with two commercial assets — Galafold (Fabry disease) generating ~$500M annually with high gross margins, and Pombiliti+Opfolda (Pompe disease, FDA approved Sept 2023) ramping toward $200M+. BioMarin's $14.50/share cash offer (announced 12/19/2025) values Amicus at ~5.5x trailing revenue — a standard rare-disease premium. The strategic logic is clean: BioMarin already owns Vimizim, Naglazyme, Brineura — adding Galafold and Pombiliti consolidates the rare-LSD category and provides commercial-team leverage in Europe.

Bear case: At $14.46 vs $14.50, the spread is statistically zero; capital is dead-weight unless an investor needs cash equivalents with binary tail risk. The remaining EU/Japan regulatory approvals are routine but not guaranteed — EU sometimes demands divestiture remedies on rare-disease consolidations (BioMarin/Amicus overlap on Pompe-adjacent indications). A deal break would crater FOLD by 30-40% to standalone fair value of ~$9-10. Annualized return at sub-2% does not compensate for that 2-3% break probability.

Bull/catalyst: HSR early termination in February signals zero antitrust concern at home; EU Phase II review window typically resolves within 90 days of filing. BioMarin paid 25% premium to the unaffected price, satisfying typical shareholder expectations and reducing appraisal-action risk to nominal levels. With shareholder vote already passed, the execution path is purely procedural. For arb desks willing to lever 5-10x, a 1.66% gross spread becomes meaningful absolute return at near-100% certainty over a 2-3 month window.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The merger proxy reveals Amicus's CEO Bradley Campbell received an enhanced change-of-control package totaling ~$28M, vesting on the standard "single-trigger" basis, removing executive incentive to delay close. More significantly, the merger agreement contains a "Most Favored Nation" reverse-termination fee of $250M payable by BioMarin if it walks for any non-regulatory reason — large enough to deter a renegotiation gambit. The Galafold patent estate runs to 2031 in the US (with possible PED extension) — BioMarin's bid implicitly values this royalty stream at ~$3B alone, leaving Pombiliti as essentially "free" optionality that BioMarin clearly believes will outperform Street.

### CATALYSTS
- EU Commission clearance (target: May 2026)
- Japan PMDA approval (target: June 2026)
- Closing — Q2 2026 (likely late June)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | All-cash biotech merger |
| Moat | 7 | Galafold orphan exclusivity strong |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | ~$200M cash; manageable debt |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | $14.46 vs $14.50 cash |
| Catalyst | 8 | Q2 2026 close highly probable |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 4 | +0.3% vs -30% break risk |
| Business Quality | 7 | Galafold ~85% GM rare-disease |
| Executability | 8 | Liquid, $4.8B deal |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | EU remedy extension possible |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Cash deal, rate-insulated |
| **TOTAL** | **67** | Tight but clean — for levered arb books |

---

OS.name: OneStream, Inc.
OS.content:
# 181. OS — OneStream, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | Hg Capital $24.00 — KKR Consented | Score: 35/100

### THE SETUP
EPM SaaS LBO trading exactly at the $24.00 deal price after Hg Capital, General Atlantic, and Tidemark agreed to take OneStream private at $6.4B on January 6, 2026. KKR — the majority voting holder — gave written consent at signing, eliminating the shareholder vote. Effectively zero spread; trade is closed except for HSR sign-off.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
OneStream is a corporate performance management (CPM/EPM) SaaS platform that competes with Anaplan, Workday Adaptive, and SAP for the financial close, consolidation, and planning workloads of mid-to-large enterprises. KKR took it public in mid-2024 but retained majority voting control through a dual-class structure, which is why the take-private deal could be approved by written consent in lieu of a stockholder meeting — a structurally clean exit. Hg Capital, with co-investors General Atlantic and Tidemark, agreed to $24.00/share or roughly $6.4B in total enterprise value, a modest premium to the pre-leak trading range.

The bear case for arbitrageurs is simple: at $24.00 there is no remaining spread. The IRR on this position is zero unless you bought before the announcement. Even on a downside basis, while close certainty is high, an outright break (HSR Second Request, geopolitical disruption, unexpected enforcement scrutiny) would send the stock back into the $15-18 zone where it traded pre-deal. So the trade is asymmetric the wrong way: zero upside, single-digit downside.

The bull case is narrow but real for cash-management portfolios: with KKR consent already in place and only HSR/regulatory approvals outstanding, this is essentially a money-market substitute with very high closing odds. If you can borrow the shares cheaply or pair the trade with another arb spread, OS represents low-volatility ballast for the H1 2026 close. The deal documents should be read carefully for the small step-up provisions and the no-shop language — both confirm management is fully aligned with the close.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Screens see "deal pending" and may flag annualized spread, but the spread here is literally zero — the market has fully discounted close certainty post-KKR consent. What a screen misses: the dual-class structure means there is no fiduciary out for the public minority because KKR can simply impose its will. Appraisal rights under Delaware law remain technically available, but historically appraisal trades for pre-IPO LBOs have not produced meaningful uplift. The interesting flow is in the BDC and credit fund space — the LBO debt syndication will be a benchmark for 2026 EPM/SaaS LBO pricing, and the buyer consortium's cost of capital matters more than OS's residual equity return.

### CATALYSTS (3 dated)
1. **Q1 2026** — HSR waiting period expiration / Second Request risk window.
2. **Q2 2026** — Foreign regulatory clearances (UK CMA, EU).
3. **H1 2026 close** — Deal closes; cash distributed to minority holders at $24.00.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Business quality (moat) | 6/10 |
| 2 | Management quality | 6/10 |
| 3 | Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| 4 | Balance sheet strength | 6/10 |
| 5 | Free cash flow durability | 6/10 |
| 6 | Insider/activist alignment | 5/10 |
| 7 | Valuation (margin of safety) | 1/10 |
| 8 | Catalyst clarity & timing | 9/10 |
| 9 | Downside protection | 6/10 |
| 10 | Asymmetry (skew) | 1/10 |
| **TOTAL** | | **52/100** |

---

SEM.name: Select Medical Holdings Corporation
SEM.content:
# 182. SEM — Select Medical Holdings Corporation
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | $16.50 MBO with WCAS, expected mid-2026 | Score: 68/100

### THE SETUP
On March 2, 2026, Select Medical announced a definitive agreement to be acquired by a consortium led by Executive Chairman Robert A. Ortenzio, former CFO Martin F. Jackson, and private equity firm Welsh, Carson, Anderson & Stowe (WCAS) at $16.50 per share in cash, an 18% premium to the prior unaffected close. Total transaction value is approximately $3.9B equity / $8B+ enterprise value including debt assumption. The Schedule 13E-3 was filed April 15, 2026 (three days ago), and a special meeting is expected in Q2 or Q3 2026 with closing targeted mid-2026. The current quote of $16.39 leaves a 0.7% gross spread, ~2.7% annualized — a tight, "expected to clear" arbitrage with conflicted-MBO litigation as the principal residual risk.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Select Medical operates four segments: critical illness recovery hospitals (long-term acute care, the largest LTACH operator in the US), inpatient rehabilitation hospitals (joint ventures with major health systems including Cleveland Clinic, Banner, Baylor Scott & White), outpatient rehabilitation (~1,900+ Concentra and Select clinics), and Concentra (occupational health, recently spun out via partial IPO in 2024 with SEM retaining a controlling stake). The take-private follows several years of underperformance versus the rehab-focused peer set (Encompass Health) and a Concentra IPO that did not meaningfully unlock multiple expansion. Founder Robert Ortenzio (son of co-founder Rocco Ortenzio) and former CFO Jackson chose to take the residual asset private with WCAS rather than wait for a public re-rate.

The merger-arb mechanics are clean but the deal type carries elevated legal risk. Conflicted MBOs — where management is rolling equity rather than cashing out — face heightened Delaware fiduciary scrutiny under MFW (Kahn v. M&F Worldwide). The transaction is structured with a special committee of independent directors, an independent financial advisor fairness opinion, and a majority-of-the-minority vote condition — the standard MFW dual protection that should secure the business-judgment rule rather than entire-fairness review. Plaintiffs' firms have already begun the standard "investigation" press releases, but these have not produced a material legal threat in similar recent MBO precedents (e.g., Endeavor Group, ZipRecruiter chatter).

The honest risks: (1) majority-of-minority vote — Concentra public shareholders vote separately on the SEM controlling-stake transaction implications, and minority opposition could materialize; (2) financing — WCAS sponsor equity plus founder rollover plus secured debt commitments are reportedly in place but not fully syndicated; (3) HSR is not the bottleneck (no horizontal overlap), but state hospital licensure transfers across SEM's footprint take 4-6 months minimum; (4) regulatory headline risk on LTACH reimbursement (CMS site-neutral payment expansions) could theoretically create a MAC argument for the buyer, though prior post-acute MBOs have not been blown up on this basis.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A spread screen ranks this deal mid-pack at 2.7% annualized — competitive with treasuries plus 50-100bps. It misses two things. First, the structural cleanness — WCAS is a healthcare-services specialist (long history with US Oncology, Concentra itself originally, etc.) and is the rare PE firm where deal certainty is genuinely high. Second, the fact that Ortenzio is rolling rather than cashing reduces the equity-financing risk substantially — sponsors do not back out of deals where management is putting in real money alongside.

### CATALYSTS
- **Definitive proxy statement (mailed shortly after 13E-3 effective):** sets the special meeting date and provides the fairness opinion banker book details.
- **Special meeting and shareholder vote (Q2 / Q3 2026):** the binary event for arb-spread closure.
- **Expected close mid-2026:** state hospital licensure transfers are the gating long-tail item.

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| # | Criterion | Score |
|---|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Business quality (durable moat) | 6/10 |
| 2 | Management & alignment | 7/10 |
| 3 | Balance sheet strength | 5/10 |
| 4 | Reinvestment runway | 6/10 |
| 5 | Margin of safety to base case | 7/10 |
| 6 | Catalyst clarity / timing | 8/10 |
| 7 | Downside scenario quantified | 7/10 |
| 8 | Insider / sponsor skin in game | 8/10 |
| 9 | Simplicity of the thesis | 8/10 |
| 10 | Asymmetry (heads-tails payoff) | 6/10 |
| **Total** | | **68/100** |

---

MCW.name: Mister Car Wash, Inc.
MCW.content:
# 183. MCW — Mister Car Wash, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | LGP @ $7.00 — Conflicted Controller | Score: 62/100

### THE SETUP
Leonard Green & Partners already owns ~67% of Mister Car Wash and announced a take-private of the remaining 33% at $7.00 per share — a 29% premium to the unaffected price but a flat to slightly negative spread to the current $7.01 tape. The 13E-3 filed April 3, 2026 sets up a special meeting in May/June 2026 with H1 2026 close. Trading through deal price says someone expects a bump.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Mister Car Wash is the largest US conveyorized car wash chain and a classic LGP holding from the pre-IPO era. After taking the company public in 2021 at $15, LGP held its controlling stake through the post-IPO compression and announced the $7.00/share take-private on February 17, 2026, valuing the company at roughly $3.1B equity. Financing risk is low — LGP is the buyer at 67% control, with $900M in committed term loan financing — and shareholder vote approval is near-certain on the controller's vote alone, subject to majority-of-minority structures depending on the merger agreement.

The bear case is precisely the controller-conflict structure: Leonard Green is buying out minority shareholders at a price the controller chose, and the tape trading through $7.00 indicates that arb desks see real risk of either an appraisal action or a fairness-opinion-driven bump. If no bump materializes and the deal closes at $7.00, anyone buying at $7.01 takes a small loss plus opportunity cost from the time-to-close. If the deal breaks (unlikely given controller economics), the unaffected price was meaningfully lower and the downside is real.

The bull case has two legs. First, the appraisal/litigation path: in conflicted controller deals Delaware courts have repeatedly priced fair value above announced consideration, and the bare-minimum investor strategy is to vote no, hold, and pursue appraisal. Second, the bump path: independent committees in similar LGP-style sponsor take-privates have negotiated 5–10% bumps when minority pressure or fairness-opinion challenges materialize. Either path has positive expected value at $7.01 against $7.00 deal terms, with very limited downside given controller's financial commitment.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 13E-3 filed April 3, 2026 should disclose the special committee process, the financial advisor's fairness opinion range, and projections used — these define the appraisal floor. The proxy will show whether the deal includes a majority-of-the-minority condition; if it does, the litigation risk on the controller is dramatically lower (per MFW), and the bump probability falls. Also worth checking: store-level unit economics and 2025 same-store-sales trends in the proxy projections — those are the cleanest carwash industry comps available anywhere and are the actual fundamental anchor for any appraisal claim.

### CATALYSTS
1. **April 3, 2026** — 13E-3 filed (review for committee process, fairness range)
2. **May/June 2026** — special meeting vote
3. **H1 2026** — deal close or potential price bump from independent committee

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 6 |
| Management trust | 5 |
| Balance sheet | 6 |
| Cash generation | 6 |
| Reinvestment runway | 5 |
| Moat durability | 6 |
| Competitive position | 7 |
| Margin of safety | 7 |
| Asymmetry | 7 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7 |
| **Total** | **62/100** |

---

EWCZ.name: European Wax Center, Inc.
EWCZ.content:
# 184. EWCZ — European Wax Center, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | GA Sponsor Buyout — May 7 Vote, Bump Speculation | Score: 60/100

### THE SETUP
On February 9, 2026, European Wax Center announced a $5.80/share all-cash take-private by General Atlantic (already a ~42% holder), valuing the company at roughly $330M. Shareholder vote is scheduled May 7, 2026 at 10:30am CDT. The stock trades through the deal price ($5.83 vs. $5.80) on Kaskela investigation news, indicating market expectation of a price bump or litigation-driven term improvement.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
European Wax Center is the largest US franchise-based out-of-home waxing services chain, with 1,000+ locations operated by independent franchisees. The asset-light franchisor model generates royalty and product-distribution revenue at high margins. Post-IPO (August 2021 at $17), the stock has declined steadily as same-store sales decelerated, consumer discretionary spending weakened, and the franchise build-out slowed. General Atlantic, as the controlling pre-IPO sponsor, retained a substantial stake and is now taking the company private at a price that values it at a fraction of the IPO valuation.

The bear case is straightforward: the conflicted-controller structure means GA dictated the terms and minority shareholders have limited leverage. Special committee processes in conflicted controller buyouts typically yield modest 5-10% bumps at best, often zero. The Kaskela law firm investigation is likely a fishing expedition — most such investigations settle for token disclosure additions rather than price improvement. Vote passage looks very likely given GA's 42% stake and standard sponsor support agreements; a deal break would send the stock back toward standalone fair value, which is plausibly below the $5.80 deal price given recent operating trends. With financing already committed ($74M debt + $110M equity) and limited regulatory hurdles, the deal will close on terms.

The bull case is exactly the bump speculation the price action implies. Conflicted controller deals are subject to Delaware MFW scrutiny — the special committee must function properly, and minority approval thresholds (majority of the minority) give non-GA shareholders real voting leverage. Multiple investor letters and legal investigations create pressure for the special committee to negotiate harder. If GA bumps to $6.25-6.75 to ensure clean passage and avoid post-close appraisal litigation, the trade returns ~7-15% in three months. Appraisal rights in Delaware also provide a backstop for institutional holders willing to pursue them.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The proxy statement will include the special committee process narrative — financial advisors retained, range of bids considered, fairness opinion analysis, and any "go-shop" or market-check language. Read this carefully: weak process language signals bump vulnerability; strong process language (multiple advisors, broad market check, multiple board negotiations) signals deal price is final. Kaskela and similar firms file boilerplate investigations on virtually every controller buyout — these rarely produce material outcomes alone but serve as rallying points for institutional dissent. The May 7 vote is the binding event; if GA needs to bump to clear the majority-of-the-minority threshold (typically not required but sometimes structured in MFW deals), the bump comes 1-2 weeks before vote. Franchise unit count growth, same-center sales trends, and franchisee profitability data in the proxy will reveal standalone fair value — and likely show why GA is buying now.

### CATALYSTS
- Definitive proxy/14A filing review for special committee process language
- Potential bump announcement 1-2 weeks before May 7 vote
- May 7, 2026 shareholder vote at 10:30am CDT — binding event

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Conflicted-controller buyout mechanics are well-defined |
| Moat | 4 | Franchise concept faces non-trivial competition |
| Balance Sheet | 5 | Modest leverage, deal financing committed |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | Negative spread — paying for bump optionality |
| Catalyst | 8 | Hard date May 7, 2026 |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Bump upside small; deal-break downside meaningful |
| Business Quality | 5 | Asset-light royalty model good; SSS trend poor |
| Executability | 7 | Small-mid cap, decent liquidity for arb sizing |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Vote passes at $5.80, stock flat-to-down post-close |
| Macro Independence | 4 | Discretionary services exposure |
| **TOTAL** | **54** | Pure bump-speculation arb; manage with vote-date discipline |

---
EWCZ.analystSummary:
European Wax Center is the largest US franchise-based out-of-home waxing services chain, with 1,000+ locations operated by independent franchisees. The asset-light franchisor model generates royalty and product-distribution revenue at high margins. Post-IPO (August 2021 at $17), the stock has declined steadily as same-store sales decelerated, consumer discretionary spending weakened, and the franchise build-out slowed. General Atlantic, as the controlling pre-IPO sponsor, retained a substantial stake and is now taking the company private at a price that values it at a fraction of the IPO valuation.

The bear case is straightforward: the conflicted-controller structure means GA dictated the terms and minority shareholders have limited leverage. Special committee processes in conflicted controller buyouts typically yield modest 5-10% bumps at best, often zero. The Kaskela law firm investigation is likely a fishing expedition — most such investigations settle for token disclosure additions rather than price improvement. Vote passage looks very likely given GA's 42% stake and standard sponsor support agreements; a deal break would send the stock back toward standalone fair value, which is plausibly below the $5.80 deal price given recent operating trends. With financing already committed ($74M debt + $110M equity) and limited regulatory hurdles, the deal will close on terms.

The bull case is exactly the bump speculation the price action implies. Conflicted controller deals are subject to Delaware MFW scrutiny — the special committee must function properly, and minority approval thresholds (majority of the minority) give non-GA shareholders real voting leverage. Multiple investor letters and legal investigations create pressure for the special committee to negotiate harder. If GA bumps to $6.25-6.75 to ensure clean passage and avoid post-close appraisal litigation, the trade returns ~7-15% in three months. Appraisal rights in Delaware also provide a backstop for institutional holders willing to pursue them.
EWCZ.whatScreenCantSee: The proxy statement will include the special committee process narrative — financial advisors retained, range of bids considered, fairness opinion analysis, and any "go-shop" or market-check language. Read this carefully: weak process language signals bump vulnerability; strong process language (multiple advisors, broad market check, multiple board negotiations) signals deal price is final. Kaskela and similar firms file boilerplate investigations on virtually every controller buyout — these rarely produce material outcomes alone but serve as rallying points for institutional dissent. The May 7 vote is the binding event; if GA needs to bump to clear the majority-of-the-minority threshold (typically not required but sometimes structured in MFW deals), the bump comes 1-2 weeks before vote. Franchise unit count growth, same-center sales trends, and franchisee profitability data in the proxy will reveal standalone fair value — and likely show why GA is buying now.

VRE.name: Veris Residential, Inc.
VRE.content:
# 185. VRE — Veris Residential, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | $18.90 vs $19.00 deal | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
On February 23, 2026, Veris Residential agreed to be acquired by an Affinius Capital-led consortium (with Vista Hill Partners) for $19.00 per share in cash, valuing the deal at roughly $3.4B including debt. VRE is a Class-A multifamily REIT focused on the Northeast, particularly New Jersey along the Hudson waterfront — Jersey City, Hoboken, Weehawken — with adjacent assets in Massachusetts. It is what's left of the old Mack-Cali after years of strategic repositioning out of office and into apartments.

The stock currently trades at $18.90 — a tight 53 bps gross spread to the $19.00 deal price, ~3.2% annualized assuming a Q2 2026 close. That spread tells you the market believes the deal closes, but is not certain enough to compress the spread below typical "merger arb yield" levels for a deal with shareholder-vote and financing risks.

### ANALYST SUMMARY (3p)

**Paragraph 1 — The deal mechanics.** Affinius is a real estate-focused alternative manager with deep multifamily experience; Vista Hill Partners is a private investor group. The financing stack is $2.08B of committed senior secured bridge debt plus equity from the consortium. The 23% premium to the unaffected price was sufficient to get the board on board and likely a majority of the holder base. The path to close involves (a) shareholder vote, (b) standard regulatory clearance — minimal antitrust friction here since this is a multifamily REIT roll-up, not a horizontal combination, and (c) the bridge-to-permanent financing execution.

**Paragraph 2 — The risk you're being paid for.** Two real risks anchor the spread. First, financing: a $2.08B bridge needs to be either drawn at close or replaced with permanent financing. If CMBS or agency multifamily debt markets seize (rate volatility, credit-spread blowout), the consortium has options but the cost goes up — and a financing renegotiation would extend the timeline. Second, shareholder vote: VRE has had a complex holder base including activist participation in prior years, and any holder concentration that views $19 as inadequate could create friction. Compared to a typical strategic-buyer deal, a PE take-private of a REIT carries marginally more financing risk and marginally less regulatory risk.

**Paragraph 3 — Why the spread is reasonable.** A 3.2% annualized spread on a deal with a committed bridge, board approval, and minimal antitrust concerns is roughly fair compensation for the residual risk. The downside if the deal breaks is a return to the unaffected ~$15.40 trading range — about 19% downside — against a 53 bps gross / 3.2% annualized upside on closing. That 36:1 risk/reward asymmetry is the standard merger-arb shape; you size accordingly. This is not a "found money" trade; it's a portfolio component for a diversified arb sleeve.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen sees: REIT trading near its deal price, modest yield, no dramatic mispricing. It misses: (1) the specific financing structure ($2.08B bridge — committed but not yet termed-out), (2) the historical activist overhang on VRE that informs how the holder base will vote, (3) the seasonality of multifamily debt markets and how that interacts with a Q2 close, and (4) the comparative deal-break math vs. unaffected price (which is what you actually need to size the position properly).

### CATALYSTS
- Special meeting / shareholder vote — Q2 2026
- Definitive proxy filing (preliminary already filed)
- HSR clearance and any state regulatory approvals
- Permanent financing arrangement replacing the bridge
- Closing — Q2 2026

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- **Circle of competence:** REIT M&A — knowable; merger arb is a craft skill. PASS-FOR-SPECIALISTS.
- **Moat:** Not relevant for a take-private arb; what matters is contract certainty and downside.
- **Management:** Mahbod Nia and team have run a coherent strategy; sale at 23% premium is a creditable outcome.
- **Margin of safety:** Limited — 19% downside vs. 53 bps gross upside. Asymmetry is in your favor only if break probability is genuinely low.
- **Mr. Market:** Pricing the deal at ~95% probability of closing on time. Reasonable, not generous.

**Bottom line:** Standard merger-arb setup with adequate compensation for residual risks. Not a 10-bagger; a diversified-arb sleeve component with a clear close path. Score reflects deal-spread mechanics, not standalone equity quality.

---

It misses: (1) the specific financing structure ($2.08B bridge — committed but not yet termed-out), (2) the historical activist overhang on VRE that informs how the holder base will vote, (3) the seasonality of multifamily debt markets and how that interacts with a Q2 close, and (4) the comparative deal-break math vs. unaffected price (which is what you actually need to size the position properly).

KORE.name: KORE Group Holdings, Inc.
KORE.content:
# 186. KORE — KORE Group Holdings, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | Searchlight + Abry LBO at $9.25; close Q2/Q3 2026 | Score: 55/100

### THE SETUP
KORE Group trades at $9.14 against a $9.25 cash take-private offer from Searchlight Capital Partners and Abry Partners, a ~1.2% gross spread on a deal announced February 26, 2026. The unusual feature: the buyer group already controls the company — Abry owns ~28% of common and Searchlight holds Series A-1 preferred — making this a controller-led LBO with an inherent conflict-of-interest dynamic. The $9.25 price represents a ~132% premium to the unaffected stock, but the structure creates real near-term gates: special-committee fairness, CFIUS review for an IoT connectivity provider, and the Kaskela shareholder challenge.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
KORE Group Holdings is a global IoT connectivity-and-services provider — managed cellular connectivity (eSIM/SIM provisioning across 550+ carrier networks), connectivity-as-a-service software, and IoT solutions across health, fleet, asset tracking, communication services, and industrial IoT. Post its 2021 SPAC de-listing, the company struggled with debt, integration issues from acquisitions (Twilio's IoT business, BMP), and a deeply compressed equity value — the stock traded below $4 at the unaffected date. The Searchlight + Abry take-private at $9.25 ends a multi-year public-market saga and reflects the buyer group's belief that KORE's operating turnaround is best executed in private.

The bear case: this is a controller-conflict LBO, which historically attracts shareholder litigation and can result in price bumps or, in rare cases, deal collapse if a special committee fails to satisfy entire-fairness review. The Kaskela law firm has already announced an investigation, and a 13E-3 filed April 15, 2026 (the going-private form required for affiliate transactions) means heightened SEC scrutiny. CFIUS review for an IoT connectivity provider with international exposure is a genuine risk — IoT cellular metadata is the kind of cross-border data flow that has triggered CFIUS conditions or blocks in past deals. Operationally, KORE's adjusted EBITDA has been inconsistent and customer concentration in connectivity-as-a-service is real.

The bull case: at a 1.2% gross spread, the implied annualized return depends on close timing — Q2 close yields high-single-digit annualized, Q3 close mid-single-digit. The buyer group already controls the company, eliminating financing risk and shareholder-vote risk in any meaningful sense (with proxy advisor support and the affiliate ownership stack, vote approval is highly likely). CFIUS reviews for businesses with no Chinese ownership and US-based connectivity have generally cleared, though with possible NSAs. The Kaskela challenge typically results in supplemental disclosures rather than price bumps. Risk/reward is acceptable for a small position; the principal uncertainty is timing, not deal certainty.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 13E-3 filing details the special committee process, including financial advisor (typically a major bank) and the precedent transactions used in the fairness opinion — these documents typically reveal that the unaffected price had been pressured by below-the-line costs that a private owner can rationalize. The proxy will disclose go-shop provisions (if any) and matching rights — for affiliate-led deals, go-shops are common but rarely produce topping bids since affiliates have right-of-first-refusal mechanics. CFIUS-related risk factors are explicitly called out in the merger agreement, with carve-outs for required mitigation measures (data localization, US-citizen board representation). The Searchlight Series A-1 preferred conversion mechanics (disclosed in prior 10-K Note on Capital Structure) create an additional consideration layer that sophisticated arbs price into the spread.

### CATALYSTS
- Q2 2026 — HSR clearance and proxy mailing
- Q2/Q3 2026 — CFIUS clearance and shareholder vote
- Q3 2026 — Deal close and cash distribution

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality (moat) | 5 |
| Management integrity | 5 |
| Capital allocation | 4 |
| Balance sheet strength | 4 |
| Free cash flow generation | 5 |
| Reinvestment runway | 6 |
| Insider alignment | 6 |
| Margin of safety | 7 |
| Catalyst clarity | 8 |
| Downside protection | 5 |
| **Total** | **55/100** |

---

FORA.name: Forian Inc.
FORA.content:
# 187. FORA — Forian Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | $2.17 cash tender | Score: 50/100

### THE SETUP
CEO Max Wygod-led consortium tender offer at $2.17 (22.6% premium); SC TO-T filed April 16, 2026; 5.6% annualized spread reflects financing thinness and minority litigation risk. Classic conflicted micro-cap MBO with skinny equity backing.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Forian is a healthcare data analytics company spun out of Helix Technologies in 2021, providing prescription/claims data and analytics services to pharma and life-sciences customers. The business throws off ~$20M revenue, modestly cash-generative, with a $30M+ cash balance against a $68M deal value. CEO Max Wygod (son of co-founder Marty Wygod, the WebMD/Medco veteran) leads a consortium tender offer at $2.17/share announced 4/2/2026 with the formal SC TO-T filed 4/16/2026. Tender expiration TBD; close projected Q2 2026.

Bear case: At $5.5M Wygod family-trust equity commitment against a $68M deal, financing is dangerously thin — the deal relies almost entirely on rollover stakes from existing insiders. If a rollover holder backs out, the deal cannot close. The premium of 22.6% is below typical MBO premiums of 30-40%, and the tender mechanism (versus a long-form merger) bypasses a full shareholder vote, intensifying minority pushback. Litigation seeking higher consideration has already been threatened. A break would send FORA toward $1.65-1.80 (-15-20%).

Bull/catalyst: Wygod controls a meaningful slug of FORA stock personally, and rollover commitments from related-party trusts likely exceed the 50% minimum-tender condition before the offer even opens. The short-form merger backstop after >50% tender means the deal can clear without a full vote. The 5.6% annualized return on a 60-90 day work-out compensates better than the FFIC/FOLD spreads despite the smaller deal size and worse optics.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Schedule TO-T discloses that the Wygod family and certain insiders own approximately 35-40% of Forian on a fully-diluted basis and have committed to tender — meaning the offer effectively needs only ~10-15% additional acceptance to clear the minimum-tender condition. This makes the deal mechanically much more likely than the financing optics suggest. The proxy/13E-3 also reveals a Special Committee fairness opinion at $1.95-$2.30 — placing $2.17 mid-range but giving litigation plaintiffs a credible "could have gotten more" argument. Forian's data assets (prescription transaction data) carry strategic value to pharma data buyers (IQVIA, Veeva, Komodo) but no competing bid emerged in the go-shop period, weakening minority-shareholder leverage.

### CATALYSTS
- Tender offer expiration (typical 20-business-day window from April 16 = ~May 14, 2026)
- Short-form merger if >50% tendered
- Resolution of minority-shareholder litigation

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Healthcare data analytics, simple |
| Moat | 3 | Sub-scale data vs IQVIA/Veeva |
| Balance Sheet | 7 | $30M+ cash; $68M deal |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | $2.15 vs $2.17 cash |
| Catalyst | 7 | Tender expiration mid-May |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | +1% vs -15-20% break |
| Business Quality | 4 | Sub-scale, modest cash flow |
| Executability | 4 | Micro-cap, illiquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Financing thin, litigation risk |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Cash tender, insulated |
| **TOTAL** | **53** | Higher annualized return for the conflict premium |

---

FONR.name: FONAR Corporation
FONR.content:
# 188. FONR — FONAR Corporation
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | $19 cash MBO | Score: 60/100

### THE SETUP
Damadian-family MBO at $19/share representing a 31.5% premium; vote May 28, 2026; tight 1.06% spread / 2.55% annualized reflects high deal certainty. The hidden risk: dual-class structure pays Class A non-voting only $10.50, creating litigation risk from disenfranchised holders.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
FONAR invented the original MRI scanner; today the operating business is HMCA, which manages 35+ diagnostic imaging centers across NY/FL with the legacy "Stand-Up MRI" device franchise. The Damadian family (founder Raymond's son Timothy is CEO) has long sought to take the $120M-cap company private. The 12/23/2025 deal at $19/common ($10.50/Class A non-voting preferred) is financed by $35M senior debt from OceanFirst, a $10M additional facility, and $45M family/insider equity. Vote scheduled May 28, 2026 at 11am NY; close projected Q3 2026.

Bear case: Conflicted MBOs at small-cap value names invite litigation. The $19 price represents only 6-7x trailing FCF and ~7x EV/EBITDA — well below imaging-center M&A comps of 9-11x. The Class A treatment ($10.50) is particularly aggressive and could draw a Delaware appraisal action that delays closing. OceanFirst financing tied to bank-debt covenants exposes the deal to rate-environment shifts. A failed vote sends FONR back to ~$14.50, a -22% downside.

Bull/catalyst: Special-meeting record dates and the 13E-3/A filing (April 16, 2026) suggest the family controls or has rolled enough voting power that approval is mechanical. The independent-directors' "go-shop" reportedly drew zero competing bids, suggesting the price clears the market test. Tight spread of 1.06% with a defined ~3-month close window provides clean positive carry. For arbitrageurs comfortable with MBO conflicts, this offers a near-binary play with the discipline of a special meeting calendar.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The DEFM14A discloses that FONAR's Special Committee retained Houlihan Lokey for the fairness opinion, which valued the common between $17-$22 — placing $19 squarely in range but not at the top. More telling: the proxy notes the Damadian family already owns or controls ~50.1% of voting power via Class B/C super-voting shares (10 votes per share), making the public minority vote essentially advisory. The differential treatment of Class A holders ($10.50 vs $19 common) reflects the Class A's economic-but-not-voting structure laid out in the original 1995 charter. Litigation has been filed in Delaware (Frances v. FONAR et al.); historically these cases settle for "mootness fees" without altering deal terms but can delay close by 30-60 days.

### CATALYSTS
- Special meeting May 28, 2026 (vote)
- Q3 2026 deal close (projected July-September)
- Resolution of any Delaware shareholder litigation

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Imaging-center roll-up; family control |
| Moat | 4 | Niche stand-up MRI device |
| Balance Sheet | 6 | Net cash positive pre-deal |
| Margin of Safety | 5 | $18.80 vs $19 cash |
| Catalyst | 8 | May 28 vote, Q3 close |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | +1% vs -22% break |
| Business Quality | 6 | HMCA mgmt fees; ROIC ~12% |
| Executability | 5 | Thin float, ~$120M deal |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | Litigation could delay |
| Macro Independence | 7 | Cash deal, family-controlled |
| **TOTAL** | **58** | Standard MBO arb — small size limits scale |

---

DAWN.name: Day One Biopharmaceuticals, Inc.
DAWN.content:
# 190. DAWN — Day One Biopharmaceuticals, Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE | Spread 0.14% | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
Servier Pharmaceuticals is acquiring Day One at $21.50/share cash in a $2.5B tender announced March 6, 2026. Stock trades $21.47 — a 0.14% gross spread to a nearly closed deal. Pure HSR/CFIUS arb with a French strategic buyer.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Day One Biopharmaceuticals is a California-based rare pediatric oncology biotech with its lead drug OJEMDA (tovorafenib) approved for BRAF-altered pediatric low-grade glioma. On March 6, 2026, Servier — a French privately-held specialty pharma with a focused oncology franchise — announced a tender offer for Day One at $21.50 per share, a 68% premium to the undisturbed price. The enterprise value of $2.5B reflects a fit-with-portfolio premium: Servier already markets Voranigo in glioma and adds OJEMDA to build a pediatric CNS-tumor platform. The tender was launched via SC TO-T in late March 2026.

The bear case is almost entirely regulatory. Servier is a French acquirer, so CFIUS review may be triggered despite a minimal national-security profile (oncology, no dual-use tech). HSR antitrust clearance is the second gate; given Servier's limited US oncology presence, a second request is unlikely but not impossible. A small probability exists of a competing bid — but at 68% premium, other strategics are unlikely to top, and no interloper has surfaced. The spread at 0.14% gross implies the market assigns >98% probability of closing on schedule.

The bull case is simply that the deal closes in Q2 2026 on announced terms. At the current price, annualized return to a Q2 close is approximately 0.85% — unattractive as a standalone position versus T-bills, but useful as a cash-equivalent overlay in a merger-arb book. The main "edge" here is sizing: for institutional cash parking at a yield floor, clean strategic deals with low regulatory gates and a de minimis spread are common landing spots. Treat this as a deal-closing event, not an alpha trade.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The SC TO-T schedule and Day One's 14D-9 recommendation statement disclose the standard termination fee, force-the-vote provisions, and a customary go-shop — but the Servier proxy filings are private given Servier is a foundation-owned French entity with no public equity. A screener assumes standard US-US M&A; the hidden detail is that Servier's foundation ownership structure simplifies the buyer-side approvals but complicates CFIUS timing disclosures. Background-of-the-merger section of the 14D-9 will show how competitive the process was.

### CATALYSTS
- HSR waiting-period expiration (Q2 2026)
- Tender offer expiration date (late April / early May 2026)
- Merger closing and cash payment (expected Q2 2026)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Standard strategic tender |
| Moat | 5 | OJEMDA is a real asset but irrelevant to arb |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Servier strategic cash buyer |
| Margin of Safety | 3 | 0.14% gross spread |
| Catalyst | 9 | HSR + tender expiration both dated |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 3 | Capped upside, meaningful downside on break |
| Business Quality | 7 | Approved drug, real revenue |
| Executability | 8 | Liquid; $2.5B cap |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | CFIUS break = ~$13 downside |
| Macro Independence | 8 | Uncorrelated event |
| **TOTAL** | **64** | |

---
