The spread at 0.14% gross implies the market assigns >98% probability of closing on schedule.

The bull case is simply that the deal closes in Q2 2026 on announced terms. At the current price, annualized return to a Q2 close is approximately 0.85% — unattractive as a standalone position versus T-bills, but useful as a cash-equivalent overlay in a merger-arb book. The main "edge" here is sizing: for institutional cash parking at a yield floor, clean strategic deals with low regulatory gates and a de minimis spread are common landing spots. Treat this as a deal-closing event, not an alpha trade.
DAWN.whatScreenCantSee: The SC TO-T schedule and Day One's 14D-9 recommendation statement disclose the standard termination fee, force-the-vote provisions, and a customary go-shop — but the Servier proxy filings are private given Servier is a foundation-owned French entity with no public equity. A screener assumes standard US-US M&A; the hidden detail is that Servier's foundation ownership structure simplifies the buyer-side approvals but complicates CFIUS timing disclosures. Background-of-the-merger section of the 14D-9 will show how competitive the process was.

KZR.name: Kezar Life Sciences, Inc.
KZR.content:
# 191. KZR — Kezar Life Sciences, Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | -5.1% gross spread (CVR play) | Score: 58/100

### THE SETUP
Aurinia Pharmaceuticals announced March 30, 2026 it would acquire Kezar Life Sciences for $6.955 cash + one Contingent Value Right per share (~$51M headline). Kezar trades at $7.33 — 5% above the cash component — meaning the market is implicitly pricing the CVR at ~$0.40. With Tang Capital's 9% stake locked up supportive and the SC TO-T already filed April 13, 2026, this is a pure CVR optionality bet.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Kezar is a clinical-stage biotech that effectively wound down its lupus nephritis (LN) program for zetomipzomib in late 2024 after Phase 2 PALIZADE was halted on safety. After exploring strategic alternatives through 2025, the company landed on a sale to Aurinia (which sells Lupkynis for LN and would gain zetomipzomib as a pipeline option, plus Kezar's residual cash). The deal: $6.955 cash up front + a CVR tied to (1) net proceeds from any disposition of zetomipzomib, (2) Everest Medicines milestone payments, and (3) excess cash above a defined $50M closing threshold. Tender close is targeted for Q2 2026.

The bear case centers on the CVR tail. Zetomipzomib's safety signal makes any out-license a charity case — most acquirers will pay nominal upfront with milestones. Everest milestones depend on regulatory progress in China that may not materialize. And the cash threshold is tight — Kezar's actual closing balance after wind-down costs, severance, D&O tail insurance, and banker fees may dip below $50M, meaning the cash-excess CVR component pays zero. Buying KZR at $7.33 means you've already paid $0.375 above the guaranteed cash for these CVR lottery tickets — a negative arb in the base case if the CVR pays under $0.30.

The bull case is that the CVR has real option value. Zetomipzomib retains a Phase 2 dataset in some indications, Everest paid Kezar $7M up front for China rights and milestones could exceed $200M aggregate, and the wind-down was planned with the cash threshold in mind. If even one CVR component clears, total deliverable easily exceeds $7.30. Tang Capital — sophisticated biotech wind-down specialists — supporting the deal at 9% suggests the CVR was priced reasonably. Annualized economics improve materially if closing is faster than guided.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Read the SC TO-T's CVR Agreement carefully. The $50M cash threshold is calculated net of "permitted expenses" — a defined term that includes deal expenses, severance, tail insurance, accrued litigation reserves, and "wind-down expenses" managed by Aurinia. That gives the buyer significant discretion to depress the cash component of the CVR. Tang Capital's tender support agreement (likely pegged to the $6.955 + CVR package) is the clearest signal: Kevin Tang specializes in late-stage biotech wind-downs and reportedly invested in Kezar specifically for this kind of structured exit. The CVR's expiration date and Aurinia's obligation to "use commercially reasonable efforts" on zetomipzomib disposition are the two material legal provisions to confirm.

### CATALYSTS
- Tender offer expiration — May 2026 per SC TO-T
- Deal close — Q2 2026
- First CVR payment trigger (zetomipzomib disposition or Everest milestone) — H2 2026 to 2027

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Factor | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 3/10 |
| Management quality | 5/10 |
| Balance sheet | 6/10 |
| Moat / pricing power | 2/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 2/10 |
| Insider alignment | 7/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 9/10 |
| Downside protection | 7/10 |
| Margin of safety | 5/10 |
| Simplicity / understandability | 6/10 |
| **Total** | **52/100** |

---

TERN.name: Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc.
TERN.content:
# 192. TERN — Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc.

## TENDER OFFER | Merck $53 cash, May 4 expiry | Score: 88/100

---

### THE SETUP

On March 25, 2026, Merck & Co. announced a definitive agreement to acquire Terns Pharmaceuticals for $53.00 per share in cash, valuing the deal at approximately $6.7 billion. Merck filed the SC TO-T tender offer documents on April 7, 2026; the tender expires May 4, 2026 with a Q2 2026 expected close. As of mid-April 2026, TERN trades at $52.65 — a gross spread of $0.35, or ~0.66%, against a deal certain to close within ~3 weeks. Annualized that's ~4% — a low-risk merger arbitrage with strategic acquirer financing certainty.

The strategic rationale is squarely about TERN-701, Terns' lead asset — a novel allosteric BCR-ABL inhibitor in development for chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). TERN-701 has differentiated potential against the standard-of-care TKIs (imatinib, dasatinib, nilotinib, asciminib), including activity against resistance mutations. Merck has been aggressively rebuilding its hematology pipeline as Keytruda's loss-of-exclusivity countdown continues, and TERN-701 fits that strategic gap. The 31% premium to the unaffected price reflects competitive bidding dynamics (multiple acquirers reportedly engaged before Merck won).

### ANALYST SUMMARY (3p)

This is about as clean a merger-arb setup as biotech offers. Merck is investment-grade, has explicitly committed cash from balance sheet (no financing condition), and the deal is a tender-offer-plus-back-end-merger structure that closes mechanically once the tender condition (>50% of shares) is met. Pre-revenue oncology biotechs almost never face HSR/antitrust complications because there's no overlapping commercial product or competitive market share at issue. Merck's lack of a directly competing CML asset further reduces antitrust risk to near zero.

The shareholder base — biotech specialists who own TERN for the TERN-701 binary, plus event-driven funds who arrived after announcement — is highly likely to tender. Tender conditions of 50%+ of outstanding shares are routinely met on day one of acceptance for deals at premium prices to strategic acquirers. There is no go-shop period concern; the price is locked.

The remaining spread — $0.35 — pays for the residual tail risk that something genuinely surprising emerges in HSR review (extremely unlikely for a pre-revenue oncology asset), a material adverse change occurs at TERN before close (no obvious vector — the TERN-701 dataset is the asset, and trial data won't move materially in three weeks), or Merck somehow walks (essentially zero probability given strategic rationale, signed deal, and reputational cost). The 4% annualized return is below the broader merger-arb market average for risk reasons but reflects the unusually clean setup.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

A screen sees a biotech trading near a fixed price — flat momentum, no obvious story. It misses (a) the deal mechanics (tender expiration date, expected close), (b) the strategic-buyer credit quality eliminating financing risk, (c) the antitrust simplicity of pre-revenue oncology M&A, and (d) the position-sizing implication: with a 3-week timeline and ~zero downside risk if the deal breaks (TERN's unaffected price was ~$40, but Merck's cash certainty plus alternative bidders would likely floor any break-price near $45–50). Effectively this is a cash-equivalent with a small return enhancement.

### CATALYSTS

- **May 4, 2026 tender expiration** — primary catalyst
- HSR clearance (already filed; standard 30-day review unless Second Request)
- Tender condition met (>50% of shares)
- Back-end merger close, Q2 2026
- Cash distributed to tendering shareholders shortly after acceptance

### MUNGER CHECKLIST

- **Business quality**: Irrelevant for arb purposes — what matters is deal certainty. TERN as an operating company is a pre-revenue clinical-stage biotech, value driven entirely by TERN-701 dataset.
- **Acquirer**: Merck — investment-grade pharma, cash-rich, strategic rationale is clear hematology pipeline build.
- **Deal structure**: Tender offer + back-end merger; cleanest possible structure. No financing condition, no shareholder vote on the front end (just the >50% tender condition).
- **Spread analysis**: $0.35 / $52.65 = 0.66% gross over ~3 weeks = ~4% annualized. Below historical biotech-strategic averages (typically 5–8%), reflecting unusual cleanliness.
- **Margin of safety**: Downside if deal breaks: TERN trades back toward $40s, so ~$8–10 per share at risk against $0.35 reward. Risk-reward only works because the probability of break is genuinely de minimis (estimate <2%).
- **Risks**: HSR Second Request (very unlikely); MAC at TERN (unlikely in 3 weeks); some unforeseen disclosure issue. The asymmetry is unfavorable in nominal terms but favorable on probability-weighted basis.

This is a position-sizing question, not a stock-picking question. For arb funds, this is a put-it-on-and-collect trade. For generalists, it's a useful cash-plus alternative for portfolio cash awaiting redeployment over the next month. Score is high because the probability-adjusted IRR is attractive and the downside is bounded — but this is not a wealth-creation idea, it's a cash-management idea.

---

GLDD.name: Great Lakes Dredge & Dock Corporation
GLDD.content:
# 193. GLDD — Great Lakes Dredge & Dock Corporation
## TENDER OFFER | $17.00 cash, tender expired Mar 31, 2026 | Score: 62/100

### THE SETUP
Saltchuk Resources, the family-owned Seattle transportation conglomerate, agreed in February 2026 to take Great Lakes private at $17/share — a ~25% premium. The tender offer has already expired (March 31, 2026); GLDD now sits at deal price awaiting back-end merger close in Q2 2026. This is a closed-end stub: no spread, only the close mechanic and the ~60-day weighted cost of capital separating arb holders from cash.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Great Lakes Dredge & Dock is the largest U.S. provider of dredging services and the only domestic player with the Jones Act-eligible fleet scale to bid on most U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and port-deepening contracts. Saltchuk — a private holding company that already owns TOTE, Foss Maritime, Tropical Shipping, and Northern Air Cargo — agreed on February 11, 2026 to acquire GLDD for $17 per share in cash, valuing equity at roughly $1.2B and enterprise value near $1.5B. The SC TO-T was filed March 4, 2026; the tender expired March 31, 2026 with conditions met; back-end merger and delisting expected Q2 2026.

The bear case at $17 is essentially zero return. Spread is 0%; if the back-end merger slips into Q3 due to a MARAD or CFIUS-style review of the Jones Act vessel transfer, the dollarized opportunity cost grows. There is also a thin tail risk that Saltchuk discovers a material adverse change in the offshore wind backlog — Great Lakes' subsea-rock installation vessel program for offshore wind has been an execution-risk story for years and a single dispute could create grounds for renegotiation, though the merger agreement has tight outs.

The bull case is mostly a no-loss waiting trade: hold for ~60 days, collect $17, redeploy. Optional upside would require a topping bid, which is highly unlikely given (a) Saltchuk's strategic fit and patient capital, (b) Jones Act limitations narrowing the universe of credible bidders to U.S. flag operators, and (c) shareholder approval already crystallized via tender. The realistic IRR is the risk-free rate equivalent over the close window.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 14D-9 disclosed the Saltchuk negotiation history and reveals the board ran a limited process — Saltchuk's diligence and superior-strategic-fit value (Jones Act fleet consolidation, dredging cross-sell into TOTE/Foss) means a topping bid would have to overcome both price and a meaningful break fee. The merger agreement's reverse termination fee structure and Saltchuk's all-cash, family-balance-sheet financing (no syndicated debt commitment) mean financing risk is essentially nil. Screens flagging GLDD as "M&A target" miss that the optionality has already been monetized; this is a cash-equivalent until close.

### CATALYSTS
- Back-end merger closing: Q2 2026 (NYSE delisting + cash payment)
- Final regulatory clearance (MARAD/Jones Act consents): expected April-May 2026
- Final SEC Form 15 deregistration filing post-close

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 6/10 |
| Management integrity | 7/10 |
| Capital allocation | 7/10 |
| Balance sheet | 6/10 |
| Moat durability | 6/10 |
| Reinvestment runway | 5/10 |
| Insider alignment | 6/10 |
| Margin of safety | 5/10 |
| Catalyst clarity | 9/10 |
| Asymmetry | 5/10 |
| **Total** | **62/100** |

---

ULY.name: Urgent.ly Inc.
ULY.content:
# 194. ULY — Urgent.ly Inc.
## TENDER OFFER | $5.50 cash / 2.23% gross spread | Score: 62/100

### THE SETUP
Urgent.ly is a connected-mobility and roadside-assistance platform that dispatches service providers (towing, lockouts, jump-starts, tire changes) on behalf of OEMs, insurance carriers, and auto clubs. The company went public via a 2023 SPAC merger and has struggled with covenant pressure on its debt facility throughout 2024-2025. On March 13, 2026, Agero — a privately held, much larger roadside-assistance competitor backed by Cross Country Group — agreed to acquire Urgent.ly through subsidiary Medford Hawk for $5.50 per share in cash, valuing the equity at approximately $156 million. Agero filed its SC TO-T tender offer March 30, 2026, and amended it April 17, 2026. The current price of $5.38 implies a 2.23% gross spread and roughly 22% annualized to a late-May 2026 close.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
This is a small-cap strategic roll-up where a thinly-traded, levered target is being absorbed by its larger private competitor. The strategic logic is straightforward: Agero is the dominant U.S. roadside dispatcher, and acquiring Urgent.ly removes its primary tech-enabled competitor while picking up OEM contracts and a software stack that complements Agero's call-center heritage. The price — $5.50 — reflects ULY's distressed posture; the stock traded as high as $20+ post-SPAC and has been a chronic disappointment.

The 22% annualized spread on a small deal is doing real work. Three live risks. First, ULY's credit facility has been amended multiple times and a covenant trip during the tender period would likely give Agero a walkaway. Second, Agero is private and its financing sources are not transparently disclosed in deal documents the way a public acquirer's would be. Third, the minimum tender condition on a thinly-held name with active short-side activity could fail or extend the tender. None of these are showstoppers, but cumulatively they justify a wider spread than a typical strategic deal.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The strategic backdrop: the U.S. roadside-assistance market is consolidating rapidly. Agero/Cross Country had been circling Urgent.ly informally for at least 18 months, and the $5.50 print is not an opening bid — it follows the company's own strategic review. The deal is best understood as a rescue: ULY's standalone alternative was a debt restructuring that would have wiped equity. From the seller's side, the board likely had no realistic counterparty because the credit facility's restrictions limited go-shop dynamics. From the buyer's side, Agero gets the asset cheaply but inherits OEM-contract assignment risk. Customer consents on enterprise SaaS-type roadside contracts are real and unmodeled.

### CATALYSTS
- Tender offer expiration (currently set for late May 2026)
- HSR clearance — likely a non-issue given size
- Minimum tender threshold disclosure
- Any further TO/A amendments or extensions
- Credit facility covenant compliance through close

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Simple business: Yes — dispatch software with a call center
- Pricing power: No, ULY is a price-taker against carriers and OEMs
- Honest, capable management: Going away
- Reasonable price: At $5.38 you are buying $0.12 of spread for binary close risk
- Within circle of competence: Standard tender mechanics with small-cap distress overlay
- Margin of safety: Minimal — if the deal breaks, ULY likely trades to $1-2 on debt fears

This is a "fast nickel" trade. The 22% annualized headline is misleading because the asymmetry is brutal: roughly $0.12 upside versus $3-4 of downside if Agero walks on a covenant trip. Position size accordingly. The right way to play this, if at all, is small and only after the minimum tender condition is met and the offer goes effective — at which point the spread compresses but the binary risk vanishes.

---

ACLX.name: Arcellx, Inc.
ACLX.content:
# 195. ACLX — Arcellx, Inc.
## M&A ARBITRAGE | $115.05 vs $115 cash + $5 CVR | Score: 70/100

### THE SETUP
Gilead's $115/share cash tender offer for Arcellx expires April 27, 2026, 5:00 p.m. ET. ALL regulatory approvals (HSR, ACCC, Austrian FCA) cleared as of mid-April 2026. 17.5% of shares already tendered. Trading at $115.05 means you're capturing nominal cash + free optionality on a $5 CVR tied to anito-cel cumulative sales >$6B by Dec 31, 2029. This is a near-certain close in 1–2 weeks.

### ANALYST SUMMARY

Arcellx (ACLX) is a clinical/early-commercial cell-therapy company being acquired by Gilead Sciences via Ravens Sub, Inc. (a Delaware merger sub) for $115.00 cash per share PLUS one CVR worth $5.00 contingent on cumulative worldwide sales of anitocabtagene autoleucel (anito-cel) exceeding $6.0 billion on or before December 31, 2029, payable March 31, 2030. Total enterprise value approximately $4.5B. The SC 14D9/A filed April 17, 2026 confirms the offer was extended to April 27, 2026 (from April 24) "to allow additional time for the satisfaction of the remaining condition" — essentially the ACCC 14-day waiting period that runs to 10:00 a.m. ET April 27. As of April 16, 2026, 10,271,823 shares had been tendered (17.5% of outstanding). The Minimum Tender Condition is a majority of outstanding shares; given the deal premium and clean approvals, the back-end DGCL §251(h) short-form merger should follow promptly after the offer closes.

The bear case is narrow but real. CVR realization probability: anito-cel's $6B cumulative-sales hurdle by end-2029 requires the product to ramp from current pre-/early-launch revenue to a multi-billion-dollar annual run rate within ~3 years. CAR-T BCMA competition (Bristol-Myers Squibb's Abecma, J&J/Legend's Carvykti) is fierce; Carvykti alone did >$1B in 2024. Arcellx/Gilead's anito-cel must capture meaningful share in relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma to clear $6B cumulative through 2029 — possible but not free money. CVR holders also bear the risk that Gilead chooses commercial strategies (pricing, geographic launches) that maximize long-term NPV but suppress near-term sales below the threshold. Tender-offer mechanics: even with all approvals, a small chance exists that minimum tender condition isn't met, requiring extensions.

The bull case is the dominant case here: at $115.05 vs $115.00 cash floor, you're paying a $0.05 premium for a CVR that an internal probability-weighted expected value of $0.50–$2.00 per share (10–40% probability of hitting $6B sales hurdle). Annualized return on the cash leg is essentially negative carry (you're paying through par by $0.05 for ~10 days = ~ -1.5% annualized), but the CVR optionality more than compensates. All regulatory hurdles cleared (HSR cleared earlier; FCA Austria cleared April 14, 2026; ACCC published positive determination April 13, 2026, with 14-day waiting period expiring at 10:00 a.m. ET April 27, 2026 — the EXACT timing aligns with the tender expiration). Squeeze-out merger at $115 cash for non-tendering holders follows under DGCL §251(h).

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The CVR is structured with a single bullet payment date (March 31, 2030) tied to a cumulative-sales (not annual-sales) threshold. This means EVERY dollar of anito-cel revenue from launch through Dec 31, 2029 counts — including any 2026/2027 launch revenue, prior-period US sales, and ex-US launches Gilead pursues. The "subject to cumulative worldwide Sales (as defined in the CVR Agreement) of the Company's anitocabtagene autoleucel (anito-cel) product exceeding $6.0 billion" wording is critical — "Sales" definitions in CVR agreements often net out rebates/chargebacks/returns, so the gross revenue threshold is effectively higher than $6B. The CVR Agreement is custodied by Computershare. Gilead has motivation to drive sales (it's their product post-close) but no obligation to maximize the CVR — there's no "commercially reasonable efforts" enforcement teeth visible in the SC 14D9 disclosures.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- **April 27, 2026, 5:00 p.m. ET** — Tender offer expiration; all regulatory conditions satisfied
- **Late April / Early May 2026** — DGCL §251(h) short-form merger close; cash distribution to non-tendering holders; CVR issuance
- **2026–2029** — Anito-cel commercial launch ramp dictating CVR realization probability; Gilead Q4 2026 / 2027 earnings will start showing anito-cel run rate

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 9 | Clean tender mechanics, simple CVR |
| Moat | 6 | Anito-cel is differentiated CAR-T; Gilead exec capability |
| Balance Sheet | 9 | Gilead funded; cash leg locked |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | $115 cash floor; CVR free option |
| Catalyst | 10 | 10 days to close, all conditions met |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Tiny downside, $5 CVR upside |
| Business Quality | N/A | Acquired co; not a standalone business question |
| Executability | 8 | Liquid, definite tender mechanic |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | CVR may go to zero if anito-cel underperforms |
| Macro Independence | 9 | Hard-cash deal independent of macro |
| **TOTAL** | **72** | Cash-equivalent + free lottery ticket |

---
ACLX.analystSummary:
Arcellx (ACLX) is a clinical/early-commercial cell-therapy company being acquired by Gilead Sciences via Ravens Sub, Inc. (a Delaware merger sub) for $115.00 cash per share PLUS one CVR worth $5.00 contingent on cumulative worldwide sales of anitocabtagene autoleucel (anito-cel) exceeding $6.0 billion on or before December 31, 2029, payable March 31, 2030. Total enterprise value approximately $4.5B. The SC 14D9/A filed April 17, 2026 confirms the offer was extended to April 27, 2026 (from April 24) "to allow additional time for the satisfaction of the remaining condition" — essentially the ACCC 14-day waiting period that runs to 10:00 a.m. ET April 27. As of April 16, 2026, 10,271,823 shares had been tendered (17.5% of outstanding). The Minimum Tender Condition is a majority of outstanding shares; given the deal premium and clean approvals, the back-end DGCL §251(h) short-form merger should follow promptly after the offer closes.

The bear case is narrow but real. CVR realization probability: anito-cel's $6B cumulative-sales hurdle by end-2029 requires the product to ramp from current pre-/early-launch revenue to a multi-billion-dollar annual run rate within ~3 years. CAR-T BCMA competition (Bristol-Myers Squibb's Abecma, J&J/Legend's Carvykti) is fierce; Carvykti alone did >$1B in 2024. Arcellx/Gilead's anito-cel must capture meaningful share in relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma to clear $6B cumulative through 2029 — possible but not free money. CVR holders also bear the risk that Gilead chooses commercial strategies (pricing, geographic launches) that maximize long-term NPV but suppress near-term sales below the threshold. Tender-offer mechanics: even with all approvals, a small chance exists that minimum tender condition isn't met, requiring extensions.

The bull case is the dominant case here: at $115.05 vs $115.00 cash floor, you're paying a $0.05 premium for a CVR that an internal probability-weighted expected value of $0.50–$2.00 per share (10–40% probability of hitting $6B sales hurdle). Annualized return on the cash leg is essentially negative carry (you're paying through par by $0.05 for ~10 days = ~ -1.5% annualized), but the CVR optionality more than compensates. All regulatory hurdles cleared (HSR cleared earlier; FCA Austria cleared April 14, 2026; ACCC published positive determination April 13, 2026, with 14-day waiting period expiring at 10:00 a.m. ET April 27, 2026 — the EXACT timing aligns with the tender expiration). Squeeze-out merger at $115 cash for non-tendering holders follows under DGCL §251(h).
ACLX.whatScreenCantSee:
The CVR is structured with a single bullet payment date (March 31, 2030) tied to a cumulative-sales (not annual-sales) threshold. This means EVERY dollar of anito-cel revenue from launch through Dec 31, 2029 counts — including any 2026/2027 launch revenue, prior-period US sales, and ex-US launches Gilead pursues. The "subject to cumulative worldwide Sales (as defined in the CVR Agreement) of the Company's anitocabtagene autoleucel (anito-cel) product exceeding $6.0 billion" wording is critical — "Sales" definitions in CVR agreements often net out rebates/chargebacks/returns, so the gross revenue threshold is effectively higher than $6B. The CVR Agreement is custodied by Computershare. Gilead has motivation to drive sales (it's their product post-close) but no obligation to maximize the CVR — there's no "commercially reasonable efforts" enforcement teeth visible in the SC 14D9 disclosures.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- **April 27, 2026, 5:00 p.m. ET** — Tender offer expiration; all regulatory conditions satisfied
- **Late April / Early May 2026** — DGCL §251(h) short-form merger close; cash distribution to non-tendering holders; CVR issuance
- **2026–2029** — Anito-cel commercial launch ramp dictating CVR realization probability; Gilead Q4 2026 / 2027 earnings will start showing anito-cel run rate

SLNO.name: Soleno Therapeutics, Inc.
SLNO.content:
# 196. SLNO — Soleno Therapeutics, Inc.
## TENDER OFFER | $53 cash, ~0.4% gross spread, low risk | Score: 78/100

### THE SETUP
On April 6, 2026, Neurocrine Biosciences announced a definitive agreement to acquire Soleno Therapeutics for $53.00 per share in cash via a tender offer plus second-step merger structure, valuing the deal at approximately $2.9B. Soleno is a small-cap rare-disease biotech whose lead asset is VYKAT XR, a once-daily extended-release diazoxide choline therapy for Prader-Willi syndrome — a rare genetic disorder causing chronic hyperphagia. The transaction was announced at a 34% premium to Soleno's prior unaffected price. SLNO traded almost immediately into a tight gross spread: at the current price of $52.78 versus the $53.00 deal price, the gross spread is approximately 0.42%, implying an annualized return of roughly 2.5% assuming a Q2 2026 close. The SC TO-C was filed in early April 2026, with the formal tender expected to commence by mid-April and HSR review pending. This is a clean, low-complexity strategic-buyer rare-disease deal — exactly the profile that historically prints with high probability of close.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The merger-arbitrage case here is "high-probability close at modest annualized return," not a wide-spread special situation. The pieces line up cleanly. (1) Strategic buyer: Neurocrine is an established, cash-rich endocrinology and rare-disease specialist — VYKAT XR fits squarely into its existing therapeutic franchise (INGREZZA, CRENESSITY) and there is essentially no product overlap that would create antitrust concern. (2) Financing risk: Effectively zero. Neurocrine is funding the deal from balance-sheet cash and has no contingent financing condition. (3) Antitrust: Rare-disease deals between non-overlapping franchises typically clear HSR without a second request. The 30-day HSR clock should run cleanly. (4) Shareholder approval: Tender offer requires only >50% of shares tendered — far easier to clear than a vote, and large institutional holders typically tender into a 34% premium without resistance. (5) Interloper risk: Possible but low — the 34% premium is reasonable relative to comparable rare-disease takeouts, and Soleno would almost certainly have run a process before signing. The principal risk to the spread is timing slippage rather than deal break: if HSR drags from "Q2" into "early Q3," the annualized return compresses but the absolute return is preserved. At current levels, the trade is a low-IRR, high-probability bond-like position appropriate for arbitrage capital with a defined holding window.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three things. First, the rare-disease structure means the true diligence question isn't "will the deal close" but "will VYKAT XR's launch trajectory hold up between sign and close" — which matters for any potential MAC clause but in practice rarely triggers given the orphan-drug economics. Second, Neurocrine's strategic logic — building scale in endocrinology after CRENESSITY's launch — makes this acquisition more important to Neurocrine than the headline price suggests, reducing walk-away risk. Third, tender-offer mechanics mean the deal can close meaningfully faster than a proxy-vote merger: once minimum tender condition is met and HSR clears, settlement happens within days, not weeks — a structural feature that screens treating all M&A spreads identically miss.

### CATALYSTS
- SC TO-T formal tender commencement (mid-April 2026)
- HSR clearance
- Minimum tender condition satisfaction
- Closing in Q2 2026 per management guidance
- Any second-request or extended HSR review (downside catalyst)

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: Yes — rare disease biotech with single-product cash flow tied to VYKAT XR launch
- Durable moat: Yes (irrelevant for arb but supports floor) — orphan exclusivity for Prader-Willi syndrome
- Honest, capable management: Yes — sold at a clean premium to a strategic with adequate process
- Bought at fair price: Yes for arb purposes — risk-adjusted spread of ~2.5% annualized with very high close probability
- Margin of safety: Strong — strategic cash-rich buyer, no overlap, tender structure
- Key risks: HSR timing slippage, undisclosed pre-close VYKAT XR launch issues, very low probability of a competing bid

---

Sources: CNBC announcement (Apr 6 2026), Neurocrine PRNewswire press release. Score reflects high deal-close probability with modest absolute return profile.

---

APTOF.name: Aptose Biosciences Inc.
APTOF.content:
# 197. APTOF — Aptose Biosciences Inc.
## TAKE-PRIVATE PENDING | 0.6% Gross Spread to C$2.41 | Score: 62/100

### THE SETUP
Korean strategic Hanmi Pharmaceutical is acquiring Aptose at C$2.41/share (~US$1.74) via Alberta plan of arrangement. Shareholder vote passed March 31, 2026, final court order received, expected close end of April 2026. At ~US$1.73, the residual spread is days of merger-arb yield with binary downside if the cross-border close hiccups.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Aptose is a clinical-stage oncology biotech whose lead asset is tuspetinib (TUS), an oral kinase inhibitor for newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia (AML). The TUS+venetoclax+azacitidine triplet (TUSCANY study) reportedly delivered 90% CR/CRh across 40-120 mg doses with 78% MRD-negative remissions. The company has run on going-concern fumes for years, and Hanmi (already a strategic partner and ~17% holder) stepped in with a take-private at C$2.41/share — a 28% premium to the 30-day VWAP of C$1.88. Total deal value is approximately US$25M plus an US$11.1M bridge loan that Hanmi has already extended to keep the lights on through close.

Bear case: this is a clinical biotech with negative cash flow, going-concern doubts disclosed in the 10-K, and a single asset that has not generated revenue. If the cross-border arrangement hits a snag — Alberta court appeal window, residual SEC 13E-3 conditions, TSX final approval, or any dissenting-shareholder action — the stock has no fundamental floor. Standalone, APTOF was trading at C$1.88 pre-deal and would likely revisit a far lower number absent Hanmi capital. Cross-border plan-of-arrangement deals carry timing risk even after vote/court approval; the 13E-3/A was just amended April 1, 2026.

Bull case: this is a near-fully-cooked deal. ISS recommended FOR, no superior proposal emerged, vote passed, court order in hand, and Hanmi has the balance sheet and the strategic logic (Korean pharma extending into US AML pipeline). The annualized spread on ~14 days to close at 0.6% gross is roughly 8-15% IRR depending on settlement timing. Cushion is the bridge loan, Hanmi's existing equity stake, and the absence of any organized opposition. Risk/reward is binary-tight: collect 1-2% in two weeks, or take a 20-40% mark-down if the deal cracks late.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 13E-3/A filed April 1, 2026 reveals two things screens miss: (1) Hanmi already advanced an US$11.1M bridge loan, which means walking away costs Hanmi the bridge, not just the C$25M equity check — it's now economically committed. (2) The plan of arrangement is under Alberta law (Court of King's Bench), which is a fast, well-trodden path with predictable timing once the final order issues. Combined with no organized dissent and ISS support, residual deal risk is mostly settlement mechanics, not fundamental break risk. Standalone NAV is effectively zero — this is a pure deal-or-no-deal.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Plan of arrangement closing — late April 2026 per company guidance
- Final TSX delisting and cash distribution to APTOF holders
- Outside date June 30, 2026 — hard backstop if mechanics slip

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 8 | Simple cash arb at known price |
| Moat | 2 | None standalone — clinical biotech |
| Balance Sheet | 3 | Going-concern; bridge loan from acquirer |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | 0.6% spread; downside is 30-50% break risk |
| Catalyst | 9 | Court-approved, vote passed, days to close |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 5 | Tight binary; small upside, real downside |
| Business Quality | 3 | Pre-revenue oncology |
| Executability | 4 | OTC US listing thin; better in Canada |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 5 | If deal breaks, no floor |
| Macro Independence | 9 | Cash deal indifferent to markets |
| **TOTAL** | **52** | |

---

BLX.name: Banco Latinoamericano de Comercio Exterior, S.A. (Bladex)
BLX.content:
# 198. BLX — Banco Latinoamericano de Comercio Exterior, S.A. (Bladex)
## HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | ROE 16-18% / 7%+ Yield | Score: 73/100

### THE SETUP
NOTE: The screener research file mistakenly mapped BLX to "Boralex Inc." — Boralex trades as BLX.TO on the TSX, not on NYSE. The U.S.-listed BLX is Bladex, the Panama-domiciled supranational trade-finance bank owned in part by Latin American central banks. There is no Brookfield take-private here. BLX is a quietly compounding LatAm trade-finance lender printing record profits and raising the dividend.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Bladex is a Panama-headquartered specialized trade-finance bank serving Latin American corporates and financial institutions. Class A shares are owned by Latin American central banks; Class E shares trade on NYSE as BLX. Per FY24 results (filed in 2025 20-F): record annual net income $205.9M (+24% YoY) = $5.60 EPS; ROE 16.2% (+153 bps YoY); credit portfolio all-time high $11.2B (+18%); Tier 1 Basel III ratio 15.5%; regulatory capital adequacy ratio 13.6%; NIM 2.47% FY24 (2.44% in 4Q24); approx. $12B total assets. Quarterly dividend raised 25% to $0.625 = $2.50 annual. 2025 momentum continued: 2Q25 net profit $64.2M / EPS $1.73 / annualized ROE 18.5%; FY25 net income $226.9M = $6.11 EPS; raised dividend again toward $1.50 declared 4Q25.

Bear case: Latin American sovereign credit risk is the elephant — Bladex's portfolio spans Brazil, Mexico, Colombia, Chile, Argentina, Central America. Any regional crisis (Argentina default, Venezuelan-style sanctions spillover, Brazil fiscal blow-up) hits the loan book directly. NIM of 2.47% is thin and rate-sensitive — Fed cuts compress spreads. The bank's funding base depends on confidence-sensitive interbank and capital markets borrowing — a single confidence shock can trigger funding stress. Class structure (central bank Class A holders) means minority shareholders have limited governance influence.

Bull case: ROE inflection from ~14% in 2023 to 16-18% in 2024-2025 with capital adequacy intact = the rare profitable EM bank trading at modest multiples. At ~$36-42/share area in 2025-2026 with $6.11 FY25 EPS = ~6-7x earnings. Dividend yield 6-7% backed by $200M+ net income and growing. Bladex is the natural hedge to USD-funded LatAm trade — its niche is too narrow for global G-SIBs to compete, too sophisticated for purely domestic LatAm banks. Cushion: 15.5% Tier 1 ratio is well above regulatory minimum, Basel III compliant, and trade finance assets are short-duration (90-180 day rollovers) so credit shocks resolve faster than mortgage book stress.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Bladex has a structurally lower funding cost than commercial-bank peers because Class A central-bank shareholders provide implicit credibility — central banks of member countries are unlikely to allow a default. This is explicitly disclosed in the 20-F as a competitive advantage. The 2030 strategic plan (filed via 6-K) targets continued ROE expansion and credit portfolio growth, with management explicitly committing to disciplined capital allocation and progressive dividend hikes (now 25% raise + special dividend pattern). Trade finance margins also expand when interest rate volatility creates dislocations between LatAm and USD funding curves — exactly the regime since 2022.

### CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
- Q1 2026 earnings: continued ROE >16% would re-rate the multiple
- Possible additional dividend hike (board pattern is annual increases)
- 2026 20-F filing with updated 2030 plan progress

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Trade finance is straightforward |
| Moat | 7 | Central bank shareholder backstop |
| Balance Sheet | 8 | Tier 1 15.5%, NPL low |
| Margin of Safety | 8 | ~6-7x earnings, 6-7% yield |
| Catalyst | 6 | Earnings momentum + dividend |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 8 | Yield + EPS growth + re-rating optionality |
| Business Quality | 7 | 16-18% ROE, growing |
| Executability | 7 | $1.5B+ market cap, NYSE liquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 6 | LatAm sovereign tail risk |
| Macro Independence | 4 | EM/rate sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **68** | |

GBCS.name: Selectis Health, Inc.
GBCS.content:
# 199. GBCS — Selectis Health, Inc.
## HOSTILE TENDER | $5.05 OFFER / $4.40 SPOT | Score: 41/100

### THE SETUP
Black Pearl Equities filed an SC TO-T in March 2026 to take Selectis private at $5.05 per share — a 45% premium to the pre-announcement price — but the board has not endorsed it, the financing comes from a single thinly-known lender, and the 51% minimum-tender condition is the kind of trip-wire that kills micro-cap hostile bids.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Selectis Health (OTCQB: GBCS) operates skilled-nursing and assisted-living facilities across the southeastern U.S. — small fleet, long-lived real estate, tight Medicaid economics. Like many sub-$30M micro-cap nursing operators, it trades by appointment, has minimal float in unaffiliated hands, and almost no sell-side coverage. Black Pearl Equities' all-cash hostile tender at $5.05 totals roughly $15.5M of equity and is being financed via Milrose Capital LLC. The bid expires May 11, 2026 at 5pm New York time. Current price of $4.40 implies a 14.8% gross spread — extraordinarily wide for a tender supposedly closing in three weeks, which tells you the market is pricing meaningful execution risk, not skepticism about Black Pearl's intent.

The bear case dominates the spread. First, the board has not recommended the offer. In a hostile tender for a controlled or insider-heavy micro-cap, board hostility is often dispositive — management can deploy a poison pill, dilutive issuance, or simply refuse to release diligence to push the bid back. Second, the 51% minimum-tender condition is brutal in OTC stocks where shares are scattered across cost-basis-anchored retail holders who don't read tenders. Third, Milrose Capital LLC is not a household name; financing risk on a $15.5M micro-cap deal where the lender controls the closing condition is real. Fourth, even if the tender succeeds in reaching majority, going-private squeeze-out litigation in micro-cap SNF deals is endemic.

The bull case is the asymmetry: $5.05 vs $4.40, with a hard May 11 stop. If the tender fails the stock probably round-trips to the pre-bid level near $3.50, implying ~20% downside vs ~15% upside — but the catalyst window is 3 weeks, so on annualized terms the bet is mathematically attractive for someone who can size it small. A friendly counter-bid or board capitulation at a token bump would close the gap quickly. Real-estate-rich SNF operators have attracted multiple PE bids in the past two years.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K filed April 15, 2026 should be read for two things only: lease structure (master leases or facility-by-facility) and Medicaid receivables aging. Master-lease structures with cross-default provisions can torpedo a take-private if the lessor has change-of-control consents — a frequent deal-breaker in nursing home M&A that is invisible from screens but lives in lease exhibits. Also check for any shareholder agreements or voting trusts; Selectis has a concentrated insider register, and a single non-tendering 10%+ holder can sink the 51% condition single-handedly.

### CATALYSTS
- May 11, 2026 — tender offer expiration (5pm NY)
- Schedule 14D-9 board response (10 business days from commencement)
- Any superior-proposal announcement or financing-condition update from Black Pearl

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 7 | Simple SNF operator, clear deal mechanics |
| Moat | 2 | None |
| Balance Sheet | 3 | Highly levered SNF model |
| Margin of Safety | 4 | $5.05 vs $4.40 — small cushion |
| Catalyst | 8 | Hard May 11 date |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 4 | ~15% up vs ~20% down |
| Business Quality | 3 | Medicaid-dependent, low margin |
| Executability | 2 | OTC, micro-cap, illiquid |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 4 | Tender fails — break risk severe |
| Macro Independence | 6 | Reimbursement-driven, not cyclical |
| **TOTAL** | **43** | Speculation, not investment |

---

A friendly counter-bid or board capitulation at a token bump would close the gap quickly. Real-estate-rich SNF operators have attracted multiple PE bids in the past two years.

Master-lease structures with cross-default provisions can torpedo a take-private if the lessor has change-of-control consents — a frequent deal-breaker in nursing home M&A that is invisible from screens but lives in lease exhibits. Also check for any shareholder agreements or voting trusts; Selectis has a concentrated insider register, and a single non-tendering 10%+ holder can sink the 51% condition single-handedly.

WIX.name: Wix.com Ltd.
WIX.content:
# 271. WIX — Wix.com Ltd.
## SELF-TENDER (RECENTLY COMPLETED) | $92 tender / Mkt Cap ~$3.7B | Score: 66/100

### THE SETUP
On April 1, 2026, Wix.com completed a modified Dutch-auction self-tender offer that repurchased approximately 17.6–18.7 million shares at $92.00 per share for total consideration of roughly $1.62–1.72 billion. The transaction represented a retirement of roughly 29-30% of the pre-tender share count — an unusually aggressive capital-return action, far larger than what most SaaS peers have been willing to do. Shares jumped 9.9% on announcement. Post-tender, the stock has re-rated lower, trading around $68-70 versus the $92 tender price, implying roughly 30% upside back to the level at which the company itself was willing to retire stock.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Self-tenders at a premium to the market are among the most reliable positive signals in event-driven investing because they combine three things: (1) explicit management conviction on intrinsic value, (2) a structural reduction in share count that mechanically raises per-share metrics, and (3) a temporary overhang from arbitrageurs and short-term holders who tender and exit. The post-tender drift lower is the textbook outcome of this third dynamic — the holders who were in the stock specifically to tender are out, and the stock now has to find new marginal buyers at a lower base rate of demand. This creates a window.

The more interesting question is whether $92 actually reflects intrinsic value or whether it was simply an opportunistic use of Wix's balance sheet at a moment when management had conviction but the broader SaaS tape was weak. The company's 2025 results have been mixed: ARR growth has moderated into the mid-teens, the AI agent / "Wixel" monetization story is still early, and competitive pressure from Shopify, Squarespace and Canva remains intense. However, Wix's free-cash-flow generation has improved materially, the subscription base is sticky, and the ~30% float reduction is immediately accretive to per-share FCF. At the current ~$70 level against a management-blessed $92 reference point, there is an implicit put underwritten by the fact that the company has both the cash and the willingness to buy back stock. If Q1 2026 earnings (May 2026) confirm that FCF trajectory is intact, the stock should mechanically re-rate toward the tender level. If growth decelerates further, the $92 signal becomes less authoritative and the thesis weakens.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Screens see the share-count reduction but not the post-tender arb-exit drift that creates the entry window. Screens also don't distinguish between a self-tender at market (defensive) and a self-tender at a premium (offensive conviction signal) — Wix's was explicitly the latter. The AI agent monetization layer is not yet in consensus numbers but is reflected in management's willingness to lever the balance sheet for buyback. Israel-domicile geopolitical discount has also weighed on the multiple versus US-domiciled SaaS peers at similar growth/FCF profiles.

### CATALYSTS
- Q1 2026 earnings (May 2026) — first print reflecting new share count
- Additional capital-return authorization (ordinary-course buyback)
- ARR reacceleration / AI agent monetization disclosure
- Geopolitical risk-premium compression

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: yes — subscription website builder
- Durable advantage: moderate — sticky SMB base, AI moat uncertain
- Honest, capable management: founder-led, aligned, demonstrated capital discipline
- Margin of safety: yes — ~30% discount to management-blessed price
- Reinvestment runway: ARR growth slowing; more capital return likely

Sources: GlobeNewswire Apr 3 2026 final results; Stocktitan.

---

YEXT.name: Yext, Inc.
YEXT.content:
# 273. YEXT — Yext, Inc.
## SELF-TENDER (COMPLETED) | $140M DUTCH AUCTION @ $5.75-$6.50 | Score: 42/100

### THE SETUP
Yext ran a $140M modified Dutch auction self-tender that expired March 18, 2026 in a $5.75-$6.50 range. The tender was originally announced at $180M but was reduced and extended (amendment filed February 23, 2026) — a subtle tell that the prior pricing band didn't fully clear. Post-tender, the stock has cratered to $3.71, roughly 36% below the tender floor price and 43% below the ceiling. That gap between tender price and current market is the single most important fact in this writeup.

Yext is a digital presence / knowledge graph SaaS company — the tech that makes sure your business hours and address show up correctly across Google, Apple Maps, Yelp, Alexa, and hundreds of other endpoints. It acquired Hearsay Systems in 2024 to expand into regulated-industry social selling. Revenue sits in the ~$400-420M range with mid-single-digit growth at best and frequent quarters of decline. The AI-search-eating-SEO narrative has become both its opportunity and its existential threat.

### ANALYST SUMMARY (3p)
**(1) The tender was a signal, not a bottom.** Boards run self-tenders when they believe the stock is undervalued and they have excess cash. Yext's board paid up to $6.50 with the full knowledge of what Q4 FY26 looked like internally. Six weeks later the stock is at $3.71. That is a brutal signal: either (a) the post-tender quarter came in materially worse than management telegraphed, (b) the float reduction exposed thin buyer base and the stock fell through its natural clearing level, or (c) both. All three interpretations are bearish.

**(2) The "AI search will kill SEO, ergo Yext wins as the structured-data provider" thesis is not obviously working.** The bull case for Yext has always been that as LLMs and AI search agents replace the ten blue links, those agents need authoritative structured data about businesses — and Yext is the plumbing. That's logically coherent. The problem is Google, Apple, and OpenAI have every incentive to scrape or compel the data directly rather than pay Yext a subscription. Four years into the AI search era, Yext is not growing meaningfully. The thesis is getting harder to defend with each quarter of flat results.

**(3) The valuation math depends heavily on FCF, not revenue.** At $3.71 and a post-tender share count meaningfully reduced (roughly 22-25M shares retired at $6.125 midpoint = ~$140M/avg price), market cap is in the $400-450M range. Yext generates $50-70M of FCF in a decent year. That's 7-9x FCF — cheap on paper. The catch: FCF quality is poor (heavy stock-based comp, long-dated deferred revenue cushion), and a decelerating SaaS business re-rates to lower multiples, not higher ones. Private equity could theoretically take this out at $5-6 given the FCF profile, but no such buyer has materialized despite years of depressed pricing.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Screens see "recent self-tender closed at $6.50" and assume that's a floor. It's not. A tender sets a reservation price for *shareholders who tendered*. Everyone who held through it is marked to the new market, which is $3.71. The screen also can't see the float dynamics: post-tender liquidity is thinner, so selling pressure from a disappointed holder disproportionately moves the stock.

The other invisible layer: Hearsay acquisition integration. The financial services vertical is the part of Yext that could plausibly get sold to a strategic (Salesforce, HubSpot, or a compliance-focused private equity rollup). If management splits the company — sell Hearsay, keep the core listings business — there may be a sum-of-parts case at $5-6. No public indication of that path today.

### CATALYSTS
- **Q1 FY27 earnings (May 2026):** First post-tender print; confirms or denies the "tender masked weak quarter" thesis
- **AI search partnership / licensing deal:** Would validate the structural bull case; absence continues to erode it
- **Activist emergence:** At $400M market cap with depressed multiple and completed tender, a 13D filer demanding sale or split is plausible
- **Strategic review announcement:** Low probability near-term but rising if the stock stays below $4

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- **Circle of competence:** B2B SaaS with AI disruption overlay — not simple. The "is AI search a tailwind or existential threat" question is genuinely hard to answer confidently.
- **Moat:** Modest and eroding. Network of listing endpoints has some stickiness; switching costs are real but not enormous. Hearsay has a compliance moat in regulated verticals.
- **Management:** Michael Walrath (CEO) is a competent operator with public-markets pedigree. The decision to run a tender at $6.50 with the stock now at $3.71 is either unlucky timing or capital-allocation miss.
- **Margin of safety:** $3.71 price with ~$50M+ FCF and net cash gives a decent floor, but melting-ice-cube risk is real. PARTIAL.
- **Price:** Optically cheap, fundamentally uncertain. The tender-gap is a red flag, not a green one.

**Bottom line:** The tender is done and was not a bottom. This is now a fundamental call on whether Yext can grow again in the AI era — and the evidence does not yet support that. Pass unless Q1 FY27 print surprises meaningfully to the upside or an activist/strategic catalyst emerges.

---

Screens see "recent self-tender closed at $6.50" and assume that's a floor. It's not. A tender sets a reservation price for *shareholders who tendered*. Everyone who held through it is marked to the new market, which is $3.71. The screen also can't see the float dynamics: post-tender liquidity is thinner, so selling pressure from a disappointed holder disproportionately moves the stock.

The other invisible layer: Hearsay acquisition integration. If management splits the company — sell Hearsay, keep the core listings business — there may be a sum-of-parts case at $5-6. No public indication of that path today.

WW.name: WW International, Inc. (WeightWatchers)
WW.content:
# 203. WW — WW International, Inc. (WeightWatchers)
## POST-BANKRUPTCY | $109.8M MARKET CAP, ~10M FLOAT | Score: 58/100

### THE SETUP
WW International filed prepackaged Chapter 11 on May 6, 2025 and emerged just 49 days later on June 24, 2025 — a record-fast restructuring that wiped out $1.15 billion of debt in exchange for converting noteholders into equity owners. Pre-petition common stock was cancelled (now trades as WGHTQ on OTC, holders received warrants only). Reorganized common continues on Nasdaq under the same WW ticker, with roughly 10 million shares outstanding and ~91% owned by former noteholders (Goldman Sachs, Permira, Atlas Merchant, and a syndicate of credit funds).

Post-emergence capital structure: ~$469M total debt and ~$160M cash. Market cap at $10.98 sits at roughly $110M, against a brand still generating $750M+ of revenue and a clinical/GLP-1 telehealth business (the Sequence acquisition) that finally gives the company a structurally relevant product in the Ozempic era.

### ANALYST SUMMARY (3p)
**(1) Fresh start accounting + tiny float = pricing dislocation.** With only 10M shares outstanding and 91% locked up with creditor-owners who want cash exits, true float is somewhere south of 1 million shares. Sell-side coverage is gone. Index funds don't own it. Most fundamental investors won't touch a name fresh out of its second bankruptcy in a decade. This is exactly the structural setup that has historically produced 3-5x returns in post-BK equity (HTZ, Avaya, GTT pattern) — though it has also produced zeros when the operational thesis fails.

**(2) The clinical pivot is the entire thesis.** Legacy WeightWatchers (in-person workshops, the app, the points system) is in secular decline — GLP-1 drugs are eating its lunch. The Sequence telehealth acquisition flipped the script: WW now writes GLP-1 prescriptions to its members. If clinical revenue is inflecting at 50%+ y/y and reaching, say, $200-300M by FY26, the multiple compresses fast. If clinical stalls at sub-$150M while legacy revenue collapses 15-20%/year, this is a melting ice cube and the equity prints zero.

**(3) Creditor overhang is the dominant near-term variable.** Goldman, Permira, Atlas don't run pizza joints — they're financial owners with an exit clock. Lockups expire in 2026; expect a registered secondary or strategic sale within 12-18 months. The path of least resistance: sell the clinical business to a pharma-aligned acquirer (Hims & Hers, Noom, even a GLP-1 manufacturer building a direct-to-consumer wedge) at a strategic multiple. Optimistic scenario: $300-500M clinical sale price = ~$30-50/share back to equity. Pessimistic: creditors dump into a thin tape and crush the stock.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Screens see "post-bankruptcy weight loss company" and recoil. What they miss:
- The **Sequence (now WeightWatchers Clinic) prescriber network** is one of the few moats in DTC obesity care — state-by-state telehealth licensure is non-trivial to replicate. A pharma player paying $400M for that infrastructure is not a stretch.
- The **brand still has 70%+ aided awareness** with overweight US adults — pharma marketers value that distribution.
- **Lockup expiry is a known catalyst date**, not a surprise. Smart money sometimes accumulates *into* lockup expiry to absorb the supply.

### CATALYSTS
- **Q1 2026 print (first full post-emergence quarter):** Clinical revenue trajectory, member-net-add inflection
- **Mid-2026:** Lockup expirations — overhang clearing event (positive or negative depending on absorption)
- **2026-2027:** Strategic sale of WeightWatchers Clinic to pharma/DTC acquirer
- **Sell-side coverage initiation:** likely 6-12 months post-emergence as creditors prepare exits

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- **Circle of competence:** Consumer health + telehealth + post-BK creditor dynamics. Three layers, none of them simple. NEUTRAL.
- **Moat:** Brand awareness (yes) + telehealth prescriber network (modest) + recurring subscription base (eroding). Mixed.
- **Management:** Tara Comonte (CEO since 2024) is operationally credible; the noteholder board is finance-driven, not consumer-driven. The latter is actually a positive for an exit-focused thesis.
- **Margin of safety:** $110M market cap on $750M+ revenue = 0.15x sales. If clinical works, even a 1x sales multiple = 6-7x from here. If it doesn't, equity is a zero. Asymmetric, but truly binary. PARTIAL.
- **Price:** Cheap on absolute basis, fair-to-expensive if clinical disappoints. Munger would note that "cheap" in a structurally challenged business is often a trap.

**Bottom line:** Free option on a clinical pivot working, with creditor-driven catalysts on a 12-18 month clock. Sized as a 1-2% position, not a core long. Position sizing matters more than thesis precision here — this either works in a hurry or it doesn't.

---

- The **brand still has 70%+ aided awareness** with overweight US adults — pharma marketers value that distribution.
- **Lockup expiry is a known catalyst date**, not a surprise. Smart money sometimes accumulates *into* lockup expiry to absorb the supply.

VRM.name: Vroom, Inc.
VRM.content:
# 204. VRM — Vroom, Inc.
## POST-BANKRUPTCY | $15.43 / ~$80M mkt cap | Score: 68/100

### THE SETUP
Vroom filed a prepackaged Chapter 11 on November 13, 2024, and emerged on January 14, 2025, after wiping out legacy equity, discharging roughly $290M of unsecured convertible notes, and issuing approximately 5.16M new common shares plus 364K warrants to creditors. Stock relisted on Nasdaq Global Market on February 20, 2025 under the same VRM ticker; warrants trade OTCQX as VRMWW. Approximately $780M of debt remains, almost entirely securitization warehouse debt at the UACC subsidiary — non-recourse to the parent. Fresh-start accounting was adopted at emergence.

What's left of "Vroom" is no longer an online used-car dealer. The ecommerce business was wound down. What you actually own is:
1. **UACC** — United Auto Credit, a subprime auto-loan platform, in run-off mode generating positive cash flow as the existing portfolio amortizes.
2. **CarStory** — an AI/data analytics platform for automotive retailers; small but with potential strategic value to a larger industry buyer.
3. **NOLs** — approximately $1.5B in net operating loss carryforwards, preserved through the Section 382 carve-out for bankruptcy emergences (the L5(h) safe harbor / G(1)(b) treatment), meaning they should be largely usable rather than wiped out by the ownership change.

### ANALYST SUMMARY (3p)

**Paragraph 1 — Why the float is invisible.** Five-million-and-change shares is below the radar of nearly every institutional buyer; index funds can't own it; mutual funds with size mandates can't own it; ETFs ignore it. The shares are concentrated with creditors who took them in the plan — names like Mudrick, Soros Fund Management, and other distressed-debt funds — and who face restricted-stock distribution mechanics until S-1 registration clears. That creates a pure technical overhang: known supply, no natural buyers, no sell-side coverage. Classic post-BK micro-cap setup.

**Paragraph 2 — What you're paying for at $15.43.** At ~$80M market cap, you're paying for: (a) the run-off cash flow of UACC — a subprime portfolio amortizing predictably, throwing off cash that returns to the parent; (b) CarStory — small-but-real AI/data asset that could be sold to AutoNation, Cars.com, CarGurus, or a private buyer; (c) the NOL shield, which has theoretical value of hundreds of millions if the company ever generates taxable income or merges into a profitable acquirer; and (d) optionality on net cash returns to shareholders as UACC winds down. The thesis is not "Vroom revives ecommerce"; it's "creditor-shareholders eventually rotate, and what's left is a cash-generative wind-down with embedded NOL optionality".

**Paragraph 3 — The path to value realization.** Three things need to happen, in any order, to monetize: (1) S-1 registration of creditor shares clears, allowing legitimate distribution and price discovery; (2) UACC continues to throw cash to the parent and management decides to return it — either via tender, special dividend, or buyback at a premium to current price; (3) NOL valuation allowance gets revisited, either through actual taxable-income generation or a strategic transaction that uses the shield. None of these has a fixed date, which is exactly why the stock is cheap. The risk is that the wind-down is messier than expected — UACC subprime credit losses spike, or capital is consumed by securitization make-whole mechanics. Non-recourse debt limits the downside to the parent.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
A screen sees: micro-cap, post-BK, no revenue trajectory, "melting ice cube" optics. It misses: (1) the NOL value preserved through bankruptcy under Section 382's safe harbor, (2) the non-recourse nature of the $780M securitization debt — it does NOT threaten the parent equity, (3) the creditor-shareholder distribution mechanics that are creating temporary supply pressure, (4) the strategic optionality of CarStory as an asset sale, and (5) the run-off math of UACC, which is a known-shape cash flow rather than an operating uncertainty.

### CATALYSTS
- Resolution of NOL valuation allowance disclosure
- Sale or strategic monetization of CarStory data/AI assets
- UACC portfolio wind-down returning capital to parent
- S-1 registration of creditor shares clearing technical overhang
- Potential reverse merger or use of NOL shell

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- **Circle of competence:** Post-BK micro-cap with subprime-auto runoff — only knowable with real reps in the category. CAUTION.
- **Moat:** None at the operating company; the moat is the NOL and float scarcity. NON-OPERATING.
- **Management:** New post-emergence team; track record is short. UNPROVEN.
- **Margin of safety:** Trades below stated tangible book per company disclosure; non-recourse debt limits downside. ADEQUATE-IF-DISCLOSURES-ARE-CLEAN.
- **Mr. Market:** Five-million-share float ensures mispricing in either direction. EXPLOITABLE.

**Bottom line:** Pure special situation. NOL + run-off + technical overhang is a real combination, but only sized as a small position in a portfolio that can wait for any of the three catalysts. Score reflects asymmetric setup with execution risk.

---
VRM.whatScreenCantSee: A screen sees: micro-cap, post-BK, no revenue trajectory, "melting ice cube" optics. It misses: (1) the NOL value preserved through bankruptcy under Section 382's safe harbor, (2) the non-recourse nature of the $780M securitization debt — it does NOT threaten the parent equity, (3) the creditor-shareholder distribution mechanics that are creating temporary supply pressure, (4) the strategic optionality of CarStory as an asset sale, and (5) the run-off math of UACC, which is a known-shape cash flow rather than an operating uncertainty.

WOLF.name: Wolfspeed, Inc.
WOLF.content:
# 205. WOLF — Wolfspeed, Inc.
## POST-BANKRUPTCY | Mkt Cap ~$1.24B | Score: 67/100

### THE SETUP
Wolfspeed filed a prepackaged Chapter 11 on June 30, 2025 and emerged on September 29, 2025 as a recapitalized silicon-carbide pure-play. The reorganization cut roughly $4.6 billion of debt — about 70% of the prior obligations — pushed remaining maturities out to 2030, and reduced cash interest expense by approximately 60%. The legacy common was cancelled. Roughly 48 million new shares were issued, down dramatically from the ~156 million pre-BK float, with old equity holders receiving only ~1.3 million shares at a 0.0083 exchange ratio. Senior noteholders and backstop investors — including Apollo Global Management — own essentially all of the new equity. Post-emergence, the company carries about $2.07 billion of remaining debt against roughly $1.29 billion of cash. After a spectacular post-emergence pop (intraday gains widely reported at ~1,700%), the stock has settled around $25.56 with fresh-start accounting in place.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
The post-bankruptcy WOLF is a structurally different company than the one that filed. The death-spiral thesis that dragged the prior equity to zero — too much debt, too much capex, no FCF visibility, dilution risk — has been surgically removed. What remains is the only US-domiciled silicon-carbide pure-play, with the Mohawk Valley 200mm SiC fab operational, approximately $2 billion of CHIPS Act funding intact, and a clean cap table. The fresh-start accounting restates the asset base, which makes prior-period comparisons largely meaningless and contributes to the screening blackout that typically follows post-BK emergences for 6-12 months.

The bull case rests on the SiC EV-adoption inflection in 2026-2028. Silicon carbide power electronics are increasingly the standard for 800V EV traction inverters, EV onboard chargers, fast-charging infrastructure and industrial power applications. Wolfspeed's competitive position is real — Mohawk Valley is the world's largest 200mm SiC device fab and the company controls upstream substrate supply through its Durham operations. The bear case is twofold: (1) Chinese SiC suppliers (BYD, SICC, TankeBlue) have flooded the market and compressed pricing, and (2) the post-BK shareholders (Apollo et al.) are not natural long-term holders and will distribute / sell over the next 12-18 months, creating overhang. That overhang is the central reason the stock is mispriced — it is a forced-seller dynamic, not a fundamental quality issue.

The setup follows the classic Greenblatt post-BK pattern: clean balance sheet, hated by fundamental investors who remember the prior wipeout, ignored by quants because of fresh-start accounting, and held by creditors who have to sell. As the distribution overhang clears and the SiC adoption cycle accelerates, the equity has a clear path to re-rating.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Fresh-start accounting makes nearly every quant ratio (P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/B) either nonsensical or misleading for at least 12 months. Most screens exclude post-emergence equities for 6-9 months until clean periods exist. Sell-side coverage has not re-initiated meaningfully. The CHIPS Act funding ($2B intact) is not always reflected in screener cash positions. Apollo's known distribution timeline is invisible to non-credit investors. Russell index reconstitution will eventually add passive demand once the float seasons.

### CATALYSTS
- Sequential gross-margin expansion as Mohawk Valley utilization ramps
- EV OEM SiC design-win disclosures (Tesla, Ford, GM, Hyundai)
- Strategic JV / equity investment announcement
- Russell index reconstitution (June 2026)
- Apollo distribution / overhang clearing
- China SiC pricing stabilization

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
- Understandable business: yes — SiC power semis, single product family
- Durable advantage: yes — vertically integrated US fab, hard to replicate
- Honest, capable management: post-BK board refresh; new CEO discipline
- Margin of safety: moderate — clean balance sheet but cyclical demand
- Reinvestment runway: very large; SiC TAM expanding through 2030

Sources: Wolfspeed IR Sep 29 2025 emergence release; Latham & Watkins; Benzinga; Financial Content.

---

FLYYQ.name: Spirit Aviation Holdings, Inc. (Spirit Airlines)
FLYYQ.content:
# 206. FLYYQ — Spirit Aviation Holdings, Inc. (Spirit Airlines)
## POST-BANKRUPTCY | Chapter 22 stub | Score: 28/100

### THE SETUP
$6M market cap stub equity on an airline that emerged from Chapter 11 in March 2025 and re-filed five months later. CNBC reports liquidation possible "as early as this week" (April 15, 2026). Pure lottery ticket: liquidation ($0) versus a Frontier/Allegiant scoop-up of slot portfolio (multi-bagger).

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Spirit's first Chapter 11 (Nov 2024-March 2025) wiped out $795M of senior secured debt; bondholders took the equity, the stock relisted on NYSE American as FLYY in April 2025, and management promised a "right-sized" 200-aircraft fleet ULCC. Five months later, an August 2025 fuel spike combined with weak leisure demand re-broke the model. The company filed Chapter 22 on 8/29/2025, the stock was de-listed and now trades OTC as FLYYQ at ~$0.215 — implying creditors recover ~$273M cash against ~$1.19B of debt with the stub holding the residual.

Bear case: Chapter 22s historically wipe equity. The April 15, 2026 CNBC piece sourced from advisors warns liquidation could be triggered within days as DIP lenders lose patience. Spirit's brand has been gutted — 2025 traffic data shows -22% YoY; pilot attrition to UAL/DAL eroded the operational base. The 28.3M share count means even a constructive plan (debt-for-equity at lower haircut) likely cancels old equity per Section 1129's absolute priority rule. Realistic probability of zero recovery: 70-80%.

Bull/catalyst: Frontier (ULCC) twice tried to merge with Spirit (2022 ULCC deal blocked) and reportedly approached during the first Ch11. With JetBlue prohibited (DOJ), Frontier remains the only logical acquirer of Spirit's slot portfolio at FLL/MCO/LAS — slots that have $200-400M in standalone secondary-market value. A 363 sale to Frontier could include a "consent fee" to common, or a plan that retains stub. At $0.215, even a $1-2 takeout is a 5-10x. Allegiant has also been mentioned. Catalyst window: weeks, not months.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Chapter 11 docket reveals the U.S. Trustee filed objections to the proposed DIP financing structure on grounds it impairs unsecured creditors and equity inappropriately — this is unusual and could force Spirit's advisors to consider a 363 sale instead of a straight cramdown plan, which historically preserves more value for the stub. Additionally, Spirit's Latin/Caribbean route authorities (Bogota, San Pedro Sula, Lima) are scarce and not freely transferable in liquidation; an acquirer must take them in a 363 sale, providing slight strategic value to the remaining estate. The fuel hedge book was unwound at the first emergence — Spirit is fully exposed to spot crude at $90.

### CATALYSTS
- Imminent (April 2026) DIP financing decision / liquidation determination
- Potential Frontier or Allegiant 363 bid
- Plan of reorganization filing with stub treatment specified

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Understanding | 6 | Airline, but Ch22 mechanics complex |
| Moat | 2 | Brand impaired; ULCC model under attack |
| Balance Sheet | 1 | $1.19B debt vs $273M cash, burning |
| Margin of Safety | 3 | $6M cap on $5B revenue history |
| Catalyst | 7 | Binary, weeks-away |
| Risk/Reward Asymmetry | 7 | -100% vs +500-1000% |
| Business Quality | 1 | Negative EBITDA, structurally impaired |
| Executability | 6 | OTC, thin but tradeable |
| Inversion (Bear Case) | 1 | Liquidation extremely likely |
| Macro Independence | 2 | Fuel and consumer demand sensitive |
| **TOTAL** | **36** | Lottery ticket — size accordingly |

MCKPF.name: Mallinckrodt plc / Keenova Therapeutics
MCKPF.content:
# 207. MCKPF — Mallinckrodt plc / Keenova Therapeutics
## POST-BANKRUPTCY | OTC Stranded — NYSE Relist H2 2026 | Score: 64/100

### THE SETUP
Two consecutive bankruptcies wiped legacy holders entirely. The current cap table is sophisticated distressed funds with cost basis near zero. The August 2025 Endo merger and November 2025 Par Health spin created Keenova Therapeutics — pro-forma 2026 revenue $1.94–2.0B and adj EBITDA $730–760M — yet it trades OTC on pink sheets with institutional ownership effectively prohibited until NYSE relisting in H2 2026.

### ANALYST SUMMARY
Mallinckrodt emerged from its second Chapter 11 in November 2023, cutting ~$1.9B of funded debt and restructuring the opioid trust. On August 1, 2025 it merged with Endo Inc (also a 2024 post-BK company), and on November 10, 2025 it spun off Par Health (generics and sterile injectables). The reorganized branded specialty pharma was renamed Keenova Therapeutics and management has guided to NYSE relisting in the back half of 2026. The OTC tickers (MCKPF/MNKKQ/MNKTQ) currently price the equity at a tiny implied market cap relative to the underlying EBITDA, reflecting the institutional ownership ban on pink sheets, opioid stigma, and post-BK distribution-driven tax-loss selling.

The bear case is that "stranded security" theses are notoriously slow to resolve, the NYSE relisting timing can slip easily, and the pro-forma EBITDA guidance includes Endo merger synergies that have execution risk. The branded portfolio includes Acthar, Inomax, and Therakos — each with its own competitive and patent-litigation pressure — and the combined company is still a low-growth, asset-heavy specialty pharma with limited pipeline. Two BKs in five years suggests the asset base itself is impaired.

The bull case is pure mechanical: relisting flips the buyer base from retail-only to long-only institutional, which historically compresses post-BK stranded-security discounts substantially. With ~$700M+ EBITDA, a clean cap structure post second emergence, and a sponsor-class shareholder base aligned on relisting, the re-rating event is well-defined. Par Health spin establishment provides a separate equity for value to surface. Acthar revenue stabilization post the long-running litigation removes a key overhang.

### WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Standard data feeds get this name wrong on every dimension: market cap reads as ~$4.5M because the OTC float is tiny, share counts are stale across MNKKQ/MNKTQ/MCKPF tickers post-emergence and post-spin, and the August 2025 Endo merger plus November 2025 Par Health spin mean any trailing financial print on a screen is meaningless. The actual Keenova entity needs to be analyzed off the merger 8-Ks, the spin Form 10, and management's own pro-forma guidance — none of which a screen ingests. This is the textbook "look at the documents, not the data" situation.

### CATALYSTS
1. **H2 2026** — NYSE listing of Keenova Therapeutics
2. **2026 quarterly prints** — first clean quarters of pro-forma combined Endo+Mallinckrodt P&L
3. **2026** — Par Health independent trading establishment, Acthar litigation resolution

### MUNGER CHECKLIST
| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| Business quality | 5 |
| Management trust | 5 |
| Balance sheet | 7 |
| Cash generation | 8 |
| Reinvestment runway | 5 |
| Moat durability | 5 |
| Competitive position | 5 |
| Margin of safety | 9 |
| Asymmetry | 8 |
| Catalyst clarity | 7 |
| **Total** | **64/100** |

---
